⏳ INITIALIZING BOOK ENGINE (PLEASE WAIT)...

Antirevolutionary Politics

II
Daniel Metcalf

Preface to Volume II

This second volume, titled The Application, moves from the theoretical foundations laid in Volume I to the concrete political program. Here Kuyper addresses the practical machinery of the State: Representation, Justice, Army, and Social Policy.

Rights, Attribution, and Fair Use

Copyright © 2026 by Daniel Metcalf. All rights reserved.

This scholarly edition is provided for educational, research, and personal use. Attribution is required for any public citation or reuse of this material. Commercial reproduction, distribution, or monetization of this text—in whole or in significant part—is strictly prohibited without the express written permission of the Translator (Daniel Metcalf). Fair Dealing/Fair Use for scholarly critique and citation is encouraged.

Technical Note & AI Disclosure

Translation Methodology: This English edition was produced using advanced Large Language Model (LLM) artificial intelligence technology to translate the original Dutch text of Abraham Kuyper's Antirevolutionaire Staatkunde (1916-1917). The AI system was prompted to preserve Kuyper's distinctive theological and political vocabulary, maintain his complex sentence structures where possible, and render 19th-century Dutch idioms into readable modern English while respecting the historical register.

The translator (Daniel Metcalf) provided:

  • Human oversight and verification of all translated passages for theological accuracy and stylistic consistency
  • Editorial decisions on rendering key Neo-Calvinist terminology (e.g., "Sphere Sovereignty," "Common Grace")
  • Structural organization and formatting of the three-volume edition
  • Quality assurance through spot-checking against the Dutch source PDFs

Scholarly Apparatus: In addition to the translation, AI algorithms were utilized to generate the following supplementary materials:

  • Synthesis of the Analytical Register (from the original back matter) into section headnotes
  • Thematic clustering via Natural Language Processing (NLP) to enhance the General Index with conceptual groupings
  • Generation of the "Synopticon" cross-reference index linking Kuyper's arguments to Mortimer Adler's Great Ideas of the Western World
  • Compilation of the Encyclopedic Glossary and Biographical Register based on text analysis and historical context

Limitations: While every effort has been made to ensure accuracy, users should be aware that this is an AI-assisted translation and may contain subtle errors in theological nuance or historical context. Scholars engaged in critical work are encouraged to consult the original Dutch text. This edition is intended as a scholarly resource to make Kuyper's political theology accessible to English-speaking audiences, not as a replacement for the authoritative 1916 Dutch original.

Source Texts: The translation is based on the digitized versions of the original Antirevolutionaire Staatkunde (J.H. Kok, Kampen, 1916-1917), available via Internet Archive and other public domain repositories.

The permanence has failed. 537.—§ 4. The former militarism. 538.—§ 5. Combating it. 539.—§ 6. Our religious life. 539.—§ 7. Our Navy. 541.—§ 8. Magical progress. 542.—§ 9. Coastal defense. 543.—§ 10. The small states in distress. 544. § 11. Cruiser squadron. 545.—§ 12. The strength of our Army. 547.—§ 13. One-tenth. 548.—§ 14. Still uncertain. 549. § 15. Everything changed since 1870. 550.—§ 16. A permanent volunteer corps. 551.—§ 17. Two possibilities. 551.—§ 18. General conscription. 552. § 19. The difficulties not insurmountable. 553.—§ 20. Extension of years of service. 554. § 21. Wartime. 555.—§ 22. Exercise years. 556.—§ 23. Cadre. 558.—§ 24. The officers and refresher courses. 559.—§ 25. Concentrated defense. 561.—§ 26. The concentration decreases. 562.—§ 27. The border guard. 563.—§ 28. Fortified places. 564.—§ 29. Entirely new conditions. 566. § 30. Impeded imports. 566.—§ 31. Our
  • General Index

# Volume Application

[7]

Antirevolutionary POLITICS WITH FURTHER ELUCIDATION ON OUR PROGRAM

BY DR. A. KUYPER

“Calvinism is the origin and guarantee of our constitutional freedoms.” - GROEN VAN PRINSTERER

SECOND PART: THE APPLICATION

J.H. KOK - 1917 - KAMPEN

[9]

1.5. This second part, which offers the Application, does not strictly adhere to the order of our Program. There are two reasons for this. First, the all-governing principle-based questions that were prominent in our Program had already been addressed, to some extent, in the first part of this work. And secondly, while this second part was in preparation, a revision of our Party Program was undertaken, which had to be taken into account. One may assume that by the time this second part is completed, the revision will have reached a satisfactory conclusion. There was no need to wait for this, as the revision will leave the core of our Party Program entirely untouched, and will mainly focus on improving some unfortunate expressions, and on maintaining electoral, social, colonial, and military connections with the significant changes in the factual circumstances that have occurred since 1878. In its fundamental character, however, our Program will not undergo the slightest modification, as the framers of it deemed this unnecessary, and no one has yet voiced a demand for it. It is true that the necessity of making considerable changes to the organization of the Antirevolutionary Party was argued by me at the extraordinary Deputies’ meeting in 1907, and a commission was appointed at the time to take up this task; but this affects the Statutes, not the Program. While a simultaneous and more radical revision of the organization, and thus of the Statutes, may be anticipated along with the revision of the Program, this remains a separate action, the desired outcome of which we await with confidence. It is likely that, as an appendix to this second part, not only the Program of 1878 with the revision thereof, but also the statutorily sanctioned new organization, will be included. Before this second part is fully delivered, it will already be late in 1917, and of course the Deputies’ meeting that will decide on the Program and Statutes must...

[10]

We will come together again in the course of 1916. There can be no question of postponing it until the regular Deputies’ meeting for the 1917 elections. Indeed, one will have to be prepared to make the extraordinary Deputies’ meeting, which comes together for the revision of the Program and Statutes, last more than one day. The organization of our official party life must be radically revised. One will therefore have to take the time for it. This is the reason why the discussion of the draft revision in our Deputies’ meeting, as well as in the Central Committee and in the local Electoral Associations, will have to be properly prepared. All this will have to be completed before the end of 1916, and the outcome of it will, before the expected publication of the last installment of this second part in March 1917, lend itself to the inclusion of the Appendices. Whether this revision of the Program and Statutes will, in the foreseeable future, lead to the reunification of the two now separated parties, remains to be seen. One should not despair of such a reunification, and in more than one respect it would be desirable. Yet this must be left to the course of political life. In itself, the division into a more progressive and a more conservative group, as will be argued later, is not unnatural, either for us or for any other political group. It may even be said that no political party is conceivable, or has ever appeared, in which a certain difference of more progressive and more conservative intentions did not arise. The question is merely whether the personal and material powers at our disposal are sufficient to allow us the luxury of a division into a more progressive and a more conservative circle. Yet it cannot be denied that the three parties on the Right together are more numerous than the political groups that refuse to accept our Christian starting point, each of our own parliamentary groups is still too small in number of members to allow us a firm and united political stance. Yet it is precisely the smallness of the number of members that allows the individual to come to the fore too much. This in itself brings the not to be underestimated danger that one of our three groups, in order to strengthen itself against the other two, seeks support from the opposing party, as has already happened more than once. This then naturally leads to the fact that we are increasingly being pushed into the conservative flank; which means that, while we profess our Christian principle in our Program, when it comes to the crunch, time and again we end up in the conservative flank of the revolutionary groups; precisely the dangerously threatening danger against which Groen van Prinsterer has so earnestly and so penetratingly warned us until his death. Perhaps a younger generation will succeed in what for the time being has seemed unattainable. Only let me repeat my firm statement, before this second part is also distributed among the voters, that the division that separated us in 1894 was not an absolute necessity of the situation, and that whoever can reunite the separated parts in the future will promote the fulfillment of the prayer that never died in me.

KUYPER.

The Hague, June 1916.

[11]

VII

[13]

Antirevolutionary POLITICS PART II THE APPLICATION

CHAPTER I
THE Antirevolutionary PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT

THE Antirevolutionary PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT

§1. Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[14]

THE Antirevolutionary PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT

§2. The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[15]

THE OPENING OF OUR PROGRAM - DIFFERENCES ACCORDING TO COUNTRY. Yet one feels how this club, which would be completely outside the picture to exert any significant influence on Russian politics, would have to act entirely differently than our Central Committee here at home. The same applies to the Dutch Colony in Smyrna, which, like the one in St. Petersburg, dates back to the 17th century and likewise has its own Church, even with its own churchyard, but just as little, even if it came to professing its political principles, could even think of appearing as a party in Asia Minor. Quite different again is the position of our various groups of emigrants in Michigan, Iowa, Illinois, New Jersey, etc. in the United States, or of our emigrants in Canada and Argentina. Here they soon appeared with their own press and their own club connections, but of course could only do so by joining the major party movements in those countries, albeit with reservations. In Brussels alone, there was at the time a Dutch Colony of no less than 20,000 people, and in part one also found groups of Dutch emigrants in Ghent and Antwerp. Insofar as there were principled Calvinists among them, it went without saying that they too were intent on exerting political influence; but their position was still quite different from ours, and what they did was therefore no direct consequence of what we do here at home. Yes, even in our own country, the Anti-Revolutionary Party cannot present a uniform image in all provinces. In Utrecht and Limburg, to mention only these two provinces, the numerical ratio in which the Calvinists stand to the rest of the population is so completely divergent that there can be no question of a consistently similar appearance in both regions. This must be emphasized more, because we are quite certain that we also have spiritual kindred in many other countries who are nevertheless unable to appear as an independent political party of their own. Just think of the Presbyterians in America, in Great Britain and Ireland, as well as the Reformed in Germany, the Calvinists in Hungary or the Reformed in France. The political milieu in which one finds oneself, the strong relationship to the other state parties, and likewise the past that historically continues to have an effect, often diverge so strongly that there can be no general rule for the public appearance of the

[16]

THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A PEOPLE’S FACTION. To establish Calvinists or their kindred spirits in all lands, and one is always forced to seek for one’s own country an organization suited to that country, and that therefore the Program which has to serve for that organization will have to bear a quite peculiar character. What Burke, Von Haller, Hamilton, Bonald, Le Maistre or Stahl have best given in England, America, Switzerland, France, England or Germany, can therefore never serve as a fixed guiding thread for our conduct. Above all, Groen van Prinsterer saw this from the outset, and therefore set a premium, not on general political ideas, but specifically on bringing Dutch thoughts to the people. The difference intended here depends on many things—on the national character, on the nature of the country, on the past, and on the particular circumstances of the time. The revolutionary principles that penetrated Europe from Paris at the end of the 18th century, and since the first half of the 19th century, are one for all lands and peoples, precisely because they lacked any historical stamp. Hence a constitution almost identical for all countries was readily made ready. In the historical field, on the other hand, diversity maintains itself, which is directly connected with the variety of life.

§4. THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.
[17]

THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. The consequence of this is that almost all our candidates are elected only with the help of other parties, but then also in turn give their votes in other districts to the candidates of our electoral allies. The calculation that the population faction of our party now, as in the 16th century, estimates at one-tenth, is therefore not derived from the results of the ballot box, but is the result of entirely different data that stand at our service. The Central Committee succeeded in extending its organization over the whole country in such a web of Electoral Associations that we always counted between 500 and 600, and as the ballot box approaches, we often even exceed this last figure. Secondly, one can go here on the organization of our private school system. And in the third place, on the ecclesiastical division. From this last, however, it appears that the Reformed Churches alone already count nearly 500,000 members, so that an estimate that adds up all the denominations of the Calvinists certainly does not lead us to an overestimation of our own strength, but rather points to the stubborn group that clings inexorably to the Calvinistic principles, gathered from all the Churches, as in the days of the Gueux, to one-tenth of the entire population. Two things are now conceivable here. The state of affairs will either present itself in such a way that this one-tenth remains entirely by itself, or there will be an interest at stake for the whole country and the whole people that will of itself lead the guidance of this one-tenth. The latter was the case in the 16th century; the consequence of which was that at that time the Calvinists had the decision in their hands; in the first-mentioned case we are now. In the 16th century, the word went out from our group, in which public opinion expressed itself; now public opinion can turn against us with overwhelming force. If the opponents on our side had also not had the imprudence to bring the School Question to the fore in 1857, our influence on the course of the country’s affairs would probably not have led to the Crown entrusting the composition of a Cabinet to one of our statesmen even three times.

[18]

THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT.

§5. Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[19]

ALLIES. - KUYPER AND DR. BRONSVELD.

§6. Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.
[20]

8 THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A PEOPLE’S PARTY. He never saw any danger in this, he repeatedly asked himself what the future might possibly bring. Never, on the other hand, has this tempted Kuyper to descend into the conservative side paths. The impulse, the inspiration for his political action always came to him from Calvinism. That is why the Calvinist people have praised a man like Kuyper to the end, while they felt that Dr. Bronsveld had never really been one of us. This in no way diminishes the personal esteem in which this ever-ready champion is held. Only, he unfolded in a way quite different from what was first suspected. He seemed at first to promise an acquisition for Calvinism, and he grew into a conservative-liberal, with, in a spiritual sense, a generous, full confession of the Christ.

§7. The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[21]

THE ELECTORAL LAW BRANCH. Bergsius fell as a victim. In my view, the decisive moment was here alone to wait, so that an expansion of the right to vote would bring a broader class of citizens to the fore, and that was precisely what the electoral law of Tak van Poortvliet promised to give us. From 1870 onwards, I had always pushed for an expansion of the right to vote. It grieved me deeply that the “little people,” among whom Calvinism had found its school, had to stand behind the self-satisfied bourgeoisie. Personally, I was always offended by the slackness, the indifference, and the unprincipled nature of the Conservatives. And when, after twenty years, my first call for an expansion of the right to vote, Tak van Poortvliet finally came up with a proposal that at least did not oppose what the Anti-Revolutionary party desired, the idea occurred to me to, with the help of the other side, finally put an end to the misery that had lasted since 1848, and if necessary, to do in two steps what apparently could not be done in one. I cannot have respect for the opposition to Tak’s intentions; and anyone who looks at the proposals that are now on the agenda, and what those same conservative elements of 1894 are now willing to concede, immediately feels that there could be no question of an opposition on principle at the time. It was nothing more than a turning against the “little people” in order to “remain master in the land.” Here, therefore, in my opinion, a ready opportunity presented itself to let the more radical element bring in the rescue, as an exception. This would push the little people more to the fore, and in particular also among the Roman Catholics, the Christian-political element, following the example of the German Center, to take over the leadership, which was still too much in the hands of the Conservatives. Yet this swing missed its goal. It appears that the radical circles were so filled with aversion to anything called “fine” that they almost everywhere left our candidates in the lurch and went over to the Conservative-Liberal in the run-off. Thus it became clear that the more mature political development that had been achieved in our party - for our circles went over as one man to the radical candidates - was still completely lacking in them. I had not expected this. And in this lay a fault which I confessed as guilt. I should have known that a transition to a candidate of the “fine” ones, out of fear of clerical over

[22]

10. THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT. The domination of the Clericals was excluded. Whether this Clerical nightmare will grow remains to be seen. For now, the Anti-Revolutionary party must be very much on guard against the radical elements; especially in the social question, the Anti-Revolutionary party must remain free from any Socialist infiltration.

§8. The Schism of 1894.

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.

In the secession of those who now call themselves the “Christian-Historical Union,” a phenomenon of a very different nature appears. In the elections of 1913, it came to light how all independence fails in this Union. One must be very careful in judging this Union, lest one pass an unjust verdict. In general, it stands firm that those who gather in this Union are fervent believers in the Gospel, mostly inspired by the Revival and the associated Methodism. As a rule, the School Question arouses their warm interest, and they do take a real interest in the ethical-political issues. Questions such as Sunday observance, prostitution, temperance, hygiene, poor relief, the penitentiary system, and many more, tend to inspire particular interest in the men and women of this Union. Often, they also have a love for Missions. Hence, as long as political action revolves around these issues, the Union is truly a Union, and often exerts an influence that we can also applaud. But—and this is what must be particularly noted—when the proper political constitution of the State comes to the fore, then there is no more motley jumble of political ideas imaginable than what comes to the surface in the solidarity of this Union. Not that it lacks eminent personalities. It has always been a source of regret to us that in our good Netherlands, one so often falls short in appreciating the personal conduct of one’s political opponent. It always seemed wonderful to us that on the other side, men like Thorbecke, van der Putte, and Kappeyne van de Coppello had emerged; and even now, the conduct of Cort van der Linden and Treub did us good.

[23]

THE SCHISM OF 1894. Why should we now conceal this, and what could now move us to besmirch their good name in public? I have consistently opposed Kuyper in the Chamber and outside the Chamber on principle, but I still remained on friendly terms with him until his untimely death, just as with Franssen van de Putte. I have never understood, therefore, what it was that moved not only the press of the opposing party, but even the leaders of the opposing party in the House of Representatives, to constantly denigrate statesmen of a more Christian character, as they were rightly called. The caricature has never bothered me, and I have even contributed to making it monumental, through my preface to the publication of a collection of Over twenty-five years; and even the Kölnische Zeitung applauded this. But what has always annoyed and grieved me was the vehemence with which one has persecuted, as a mocker and scoffer, first Groen van Prinsterer until 1876, when he died, and, after him, especially me. One knows what broke loose in 1909 on the occasion of a lecture for a knighthood; an uproar so high that a Commission of Inquiry had to intervene before it could be silenced. This vehement, literally carving of his political opponents is something specifically Dutch. Abroad, it is not much better either, but in any case, they did not go as far as with us here. And what was even more to be deplored, even the press of the Christian-Historical Union did not manage to keep itself free from this. Gather together all that the Dutchman, before and after the period of 1901–1905, has displayed in vehemence against me personally, and you will involuntarily ask yourself how it was possible among Christians that this stream of bitter waters could only keep dripping; and this all the more, since the newspaper that served me as an interpreter almost completely abstained from such polemics. Perhaps a single arrow may have slipped from my pen, but as a rule I always confined myself to a positive exposition of my feelings, and there is no one among the members of the Christian-Historical Union who can accuse me of a personal persecution. Only once did I have to distance myself, but then it went tit for tat, and that was the end of it. It never caused any ill will with me. That this was not always reciprocated from the Christian-Historical side is no other to be explained than from the complete misunderstanding in the Christian-Historical Union of a

[24]

12THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A PEOPLE’S PARTY. THE LEADING POLITICAL IDEA. As I already remarked, among those who rallied under the Union, there was a praiseworthy busyness on almost every domain of Christian life; only, not in the political domain. In the political realm, a few gifted thinkers and speakers emerged in the Union, but each with their own monopoly. There was a vigorous individualism, but little trace of concerted cooperation; and of that unity in feeling, views, and convictions, which is indispensable, little was often to be discovered, emanating from the leadership of a political party among the people.

§9. MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[25]

MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. - PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. And nothing is more thoughtless than the one-sidedness of some of ours, to still want to bind our party to the tactics followed by Groen van Prinsterer and also by me at first, even after this entire change in our position in the country. In our first period, there was no thought of grasping the reins of government; I even considered at my first appearance that even dreaming of it would lead to a denial of our principles. Our Program, which I designed in 1878, was therefore still entirely in the style of that first period. After 1889, however, completely different relationships entered in, and this explains why since that year a certain change in our tactics had to take place, which influenced our programmatic appearance. Repeatedly, especially in 1888, 1891 and 1894, attempts have been made to arrive at a more precise formulation. There was no question here of a denial of principles; it was merely seen that not everything could henceforth be demanded at once, and that for the sake of the allies, one had to modify the formulation of one’s demands at times. What nevertheless helped to preserve unity amidst these tactical shifts was the School Question; in this issue, one usually drew the same line by itself; as soon as differences of opinion arose on this issue, unity of purpose was not regained until it was, and even now it still binds us together in the Education Commission of Minister Cort v.d. Linden.

§10. PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.
[26]

14. The Anti-Revolutionary Party as a Popular Movement. They have taken up the leadership and guidance; but already with a Twelve-year Truce, the situation changed, faith receded into the background, and trade and shipping came to the fore. On a small scale, a shift in public opinion could be observed, but in the opposite direction, when the anxious pangs of war and the blow to our trade broke us, and the need of the moment again called more broadly to God. Then so many turned again to the religion that they had hitherto only nominally honored. Yet this cannot give us the position of the 16th century, when the religious awakening was not the fruit of necessity, but the mighty national struggle drew its initiative mainly from faith, and not from anything else. We stand midway between these two possibilities. It may be that the Lord our God has humbled us again, as a punishment for our lack of unity, our re-emerging individualism, and the half-compliance of many with the men of Unbelief and Revolution, banishing us once more from the sphere of Government and assigning us again the role of a protest party, a da capo of the period 1857–1878; something that would likely purify our political blood and strengthen our willpower. Then we will naturally go back to a Program of Principles and regain our integrity. On the other hand, if the struggle on the official terrain must be continued on our part as well, so that participation in that terrain remains an inescapable duty, then, even with the most uncompromising confession of our principles, it will still come down to reckoning with our allies in terms of their application; and it will remain the highest political art, never to consent to a tactical action that would mortally wound our principle, but conversely to safeguard ourselves from all obstinacy that would cut off all effectiveness of our principle. It will always depend here on what Groen van Prinsterer meant when he raised the slogan: “In our isolation lies our strength!” This applied in full meaning when Groen uttered it as a slogan, but lost that meaning with the appearance of Mackay’s ministry. For its appearance had only been possible as the fruit of electoral cooperation, and could only bear fruit through cooperation in the Second Chamber. It was therefore nothing but political self-delusion when some also in 1890, in 1901 and in 1908 declared the Anti-Revolutionaries to be a party of isolation.

[27]

ELECTORAL PROGRAM. Those upon whom the responsibility for the administration rested attempted to muzzle this age-old slogan. In our first period from 1857–1889, this isolation was precisely what was needed to make a later appearance outside of isolation possible; and if, now or later, a state of affairs were to return in which the Anti-Revolutionary party would count for almost nothing in the official affairs of the country, the order to retire in order to jump higher would immediately be given again. In the still ongoing second period, which began in 1889, on the other hand, the indispensable alliance cut off that isolation and kept the isolation no other meaning than that it remained in our program, i.e. in the principles professed by us, what is called pure on the crest. That even among us more than one of our best men stood too close to the Liberals in the oath question, in the conditional condemnation, and so much more, and left a gap on our side, was a denial of the isolation on the principled field, against which I have always warned and from which we have never reaped anything but self-weakening.

§11. Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[28]

16 THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A POPULAR PART. delijk aangevend, dat hij zich door geen overeenkomst gebonden wenschte te zien. Dat dit voor de eenheid der Antirevolutionaire partij in de Tweede Kamer noodlottig moest werken, ligt voor de hand. Immers, de heer De Savornin Lohman was feitelijk de leider der Antirevolutionaire Kamergroep, en door zijn eigenzinnig optreden werd het onmogelijk, dat de Antirevolutionaire partij als zoodanig met een gemeenschappelijk program voor den dag kwam. Wel werd er dan ook telkens geklaagd over gebrek aan eenheid in de Antirevolutionaire Kamergroep, maar de oorzaak lag in de eigenzinnigheid van den heer De Savornin Lohman, die zich door geen afspraak gebonden achtte. Eerst toen hij in 1905 uit de Kamer verdween, kwam er meer eenheid in de Antirevolutionaire Kamergroep. Maar ook toen nog bleef het moeilijk, om tot een gemeenschappelijk Stembus-program te geraken, omdat de Christelijk-Historischen, die zich nu sterker voelden, ook hun eigen eischen stelden. Eerst in 1918 is het gelukt, om tot een gemeenschappelijk Stembus-program te komen, dat door alle Antirevolutionaire en Christelijk-Historische candidaten kon worden onderschreven.

[29]

ONCE AGAIN, MR. LOHMAN. - FUNDAMENTAL STARTING POINT.

It is necessary to clearly state that he had no intention of reckoning with the agreement that had been made. The very idea of such an agreement was completely at odds with his entire personal existence. His strength lay in strict individualistic action; the quintessence of rigid individualism shone through in him. Such individualism has always seemed highly questionable to us, and without distinction, it has been rejected as utterly untenable by all practical writers on party organization. But this does not help when it is opposed to what is desired, embodied in a man of such high significance. Whoever rejected the concert in this case could not have spoken otherwise than he did. He could not but remain true to himself; something that often caused serious inconvenience, because it was often absolutely impossible to conclude, from his attitude in one case, to his attitude in a similar case. Personality too is a mystery, and in that mystery, often there is no place for a self-consistent stance. This is often wrongly condemned. Blindness to what lies hidden in the mystery of personality often leads to an unfair judgment. Political cooperation, therefore, is safest when it respects this mystery in such personalities and knows how to arrange itself in such a way that the damage caused by such individualism can be averted, and the full power hidden in it can be utilized at a given moment.

§13. Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[30]

THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT. The unfolding is different. Socialism has sought such a starting point in life rules that it prescribed for the future in social coexistence. And so our Anti-Revolutionary party cannot shirk from firmly stipulating such a starting point. Of course, a confession had to come to the fore, in which its general Anti-Revolutionary character was evident, but this could not suffice. As was extensively set forth in the First Part, one can stand as one man against the principles of unbelief and revolution, and yet be extremely diverse in one’s State architecture. Russia is strongly opposed to unbelief and revolution, the same can be said of Turkey, but now lay the Constitutions of these two Empires alongside the constitution that Belgium gave itself in 1830, and the distance that yawns on constitutional terrain between Belgium and these two autocratic Empires is not too harsh a word. To say that you are Anti-Revolutionary does not say enough. You know well what you do not want, but not what you do intend. Against Unbelief and Revolution!—it is excellent, but where are you then for? The insufficiency that lay in this negative duality was also felt, and hence one already spoke in the thirties of the previous century, in addition to this, of Christian-Historical, as also in article one of our Program both are joined together as a unity of purpose, and as complementing each other. Over against Unbelief stood then the Christian Confession. Not every faith can serve to oppose unbelief. Buddhism too stood over against unbelief. Likewise Confucianism. And in an even stronger sense, Islam. But over against the action of 1789—’95 there was no salvation to be sought with these three. The cry of unbelief in Paris was very specifically hurled against the Church of Christ. It was the Christian faith that one abjured and banned. In rising up against this cry of unbelief, it was therefore to be determined whether one would then take up the abjured Christian faith again, or would propagate a new faith discovery. What not long thereafter was recommended as Modernist in such broad circles, was the new discovery, which, when closely examined, had already undertaken a reaction against sheer unbelief under Robespierre. Very rightly, one has therefore immediately opposed the cry of Unbelief and Revolution, the slogan of the

[31]

THE ONE-SIDED VIEW OF “CHRISTIAN”

The Christian element opposed. Christian versus Unbelief, historical versus Revolution.

§14. The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”
[32]

20 THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT.

Only in the political field have we overcome this one-sidedness. Whoever speaks of Christian parties must also include the Roman Catholic State parties; and so the expression “Christian-Historical” is to be understood as encompassing the movement that in many European countries had risen up against the Christ-denying and the historically disregarding movement in the action of Unbelief and Revolution.

§15. Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[33]

NOT LIMITED TO OUR LAND - EMERGING FROM THE PAST. The political party here in this country still bears the Roman Catholic name; that the dissenters of 1894 prefer to call themselves Christian-Historical, and that at the mere mention of the Anti-Revolutionary name, the entire country automatically thinks of the party whose founder was Groen van Prinsterer. Yet we do not let go of the positive name of Christian-Historical. Whoever undertakes the political struggle must not only know what he is fighting against, but just as decisively what he has bound the struggle for.

§16. Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.
[34]

22 ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS PART OF THE PEOPLE. Emerging Absolutism. And upon closer inspection, only Groen van Prinsterer can be said to have been brought to the task of publishing the “Archives de la maison d’Orange,” which led him to immediately go back from the French Revolution to our revolution against Spain and Philip; something that also brought him into unintended contact with Calvinism.

§17. Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil.
