The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the
[35]

DRIFTING—A HAPPY TURN.23 The power in the land had passed out of the hands of the Calvinists into those of their political opponents. The university, placed outside the Church under State control, filed down the principles and smoothed them out. So the generation of the Gueux receded into the background. They were pushed into a corner. There they gradually became moldy. And the ultimate outcome was that they withdrew almost entirely from public life, left the national cause to others, and found themselves gathered together partly in mystical, partly in dogmatic societies. So a void emerged at the end of the 18th century among us. The old Calvinists were not asleep, but had then withdrawn into their tents. This is why the Sans-Culottes could almost without resistance occupy our land and establish their revolutionary principles here, and why, in the face of this invasion from outside, resistance lived in many hearts, but without this resistance being able to organize and mobilize its forces, and therefore not knowing better than to let God’s water run over God’s acre.

§18. A Happy Turn.

Analytical Summary: A Happy Turn.

It was only in the so-called “Secession” and shortly thereafter in the appearance of the “Friends of the Truth” that a desired turn occurred, which was later confirmed and expanded upon by the so-called Doleantie. Reformed theology dawned again. Calvin was read again. One lived in what had once been our glory. And in this, the remarkable phenomenon occurred that the historical study at our Royal Universities finally drew back the veil that Wagenaar in particular had drawn over our national history, and showed the whole people again the old Calvinistic life as it had actually been in the days of its glory. Robert Fruin in particular came up with the true history. Campbell in America supported him in this, insofar as America’s history was connected with ours. And although Groen van Prinsterer felt compelled to oppose Motley in his Maurits, he cannot deny that Motley too brought our past again in a highly attractive picture. All this has now worked together

[36]

24 ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS PART OF THE PEOPLE. to make the false image of our past, which had been introduced into the country especially by the Nut van ‘t Algemeen, fade, and to make way for a painting in fresher tones that could attract and inspire. As a final, not unimportant phenomenon, it may be added here that the rich edition of Calvin’s works undertaken by Reuss, Cunitz and Baum in 1863, the many biographies that came to light from both Reformed and Roman Catholic and Lutheran sides about Calvin, the publication of many documents that enhanced his significance, arranged by Bonnet and Herminjard, and not least the publications on the occasion of the fourth centenary of his birth, drew general attention to the fact that Calvin was by no means only a theologian, but was truly also a statesman, and even an economist, of high historical significance. So it may be said that what is now more generally honored as the Calvinistic movement here in this country is neither artificial nor imposed, but has arisen from the life of the population group that continued to bear the old stamp, and has been brought about by a series of phenomena of a completely independent character. Troeltsch’s studies provide the proof for this. What Bungener wrote about Calvin did not touch the core of the matter. Bungener was not up to date with the history and wrote just before the new, more impartial studies on Calvin appeared for the public. Even Dr. Allard Pierson, among us, although himself rather anti-Calvinist, also paid tribute to Calvin’s significance in a comprehensive work.

§19. Three Groups in the People.

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.

Although what had been so forgotten and faded had come to life again in fresh colors, yet no moment could be cherished that the whole of our people would greet the re-unfurling of Calvin’s ennobled banner with the sympathies and a clinging love. At most, an expression of reverence could be spoken of. It was generally seen that with the revival of Calvinism, one could reckon on a richer energy manifesting itself within the Calvinistic circle itself, yet it stood firm from the outset that

[37]

THE THREE GROUPS IN THE PEOPLE. 25 It would be a mistake to think that the inspired and convinced Calvinists are now no more than a group in the masses. Three kinds of historical fruit presented themselves to the people’s affection when our latest historical period was ushered in, in 1848. From the Middle Ages lived on what inspired our Roman Catholic fellow-countrymen; from the 18th century spoke to us the revolutionary group; and between these two stood the group that had once derived its high inspiration from Calvin. These three historical periods had succeeded one another—the Roman Catholic, the Calvinistic, and the Revolutionary—and each of these three types is still represented in our great popular groups. This must not be misunderstood. It is not claimed that our Roman Catholic fellow-countrymen are still half-medieval offshoots, nor that the Calvinists of the 20th century are copying the Gueux of the 16th century, nor even that the now almost predominant element that celebrates its birthday in 1789 may be confused with the Jacobins and the Septembriseurs. One by one, each of these three groups has developed according to the demands of the time, and become fit to coexist with the other two groups. Hence a coexistence in national coherence became possible for these three groups. All that can be stated with certainty is that three kinds of political types present themselves in our people, of which the oldest received its stamp in the Middle Ages, the second in the days of the Reformation, and the third in the turbulent years that settled accounts in 1789. As for the territorial aspect, it need only be noted that North Brabant and Limburg and the eastern part of Dutch Flanders did not go along with the historical movement of our national life. The Generality Lands were naturally in a dependent position, and it is much to be regretted that the movement which at the time seemed to have already achieved its full triumph even in Ghent, was brought to a standstill and even declined by a new success of the Spanish arms. It cannot be denied, therefore, that these Generality Lands in the 16th, 17th and 18th centuries have only very partially shared in our historical life. They have been oppressed and suppressed by the Seven Provinces. Violence has been done to the free spirit. This is certainly much to be regretted, even if it may not be viewed otherwise than in the light of the history of the time. Where the Roman Catholic clergy was in power, an even harder fate befell us, and the sharp balance position in which we found ourselves after the

[38]

26THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A POPULAR FACTION. After the Peace of Münster, the two great European powers stood opposed to one another, our government allowed us to adopt no other course of action. That we also spoke of our country above and below the Moerdijk even after 1815, and even after 1848, may therefore by no means be regarded as an attempt at new oppression. Rather, such language was intended in part for a section of our people, with a very different past, to create for them a more fitting political position. Yet the outcome has shown that the initial so strong separation between the country above and below the Moerdijk would of itself lead to unity; and without exaggeration it may be said that people on both sides increasingly feel themselves to be one people. It is otherwise with the two other groups, which territorially flow completely into one another. There may also be, as regards these two groups, a territorial distinction between the northern provinces and the central provinces of the country observable, so that the radical elements feel most at home in North Holland, Friesland, Groningen and Drenthe, and on the other hand the more tempered factors in Utrecht, Gelderland, South Holland and Zeeland, but to point out a separation in the provinces does not yield this. Liberals and Radicals are found alongside Calvinists and Roman Catholics in all the other provinces. The Veluwe may from of old bear a somewhat more distinct character, but a separation like that of the Roman Catholic element in Limburg and North Brabant is not to be found higher up. Such a separation was not even to be established in the days of the Reformation. To mention only the names of Oldenbarnevelt and Erasmus, the circles in which the spirit of each found entrance, though certainly not to be mistaken for identity, were to be found throughout the land. In origin and tribal affiliation the motive will often have lain, from which the later opposition between Roman Catholics, Erasmians, Calvinists and Anabaptists arose, but here stands opposed that, for example, with the two types of Frisian origin, which even in a greater or lesser bodily stature still come out, no trace of any political distinction presents itself. The national character of the people revealed itself in its natural shadings and the manifold given facts, in order to be able to bring each of these three main groups to bloom, according to difference of time, and according to difference of occasion.

[39]

THE THREE TYPES NOT EQUALLY CHARACTERISTIC.27

§20. The three types not equally characteristic. But this does not mean that, in the course of time, each of these three types has in turn dominated the national condition: the Roman Catholics in the Middle Ages, the Calvinists in the 16th century, and the French Revolutionaries after 1789. It does not at all follow from this that they are equal in national significance. Undoubtedly, the Seven Provinces, once living a common life with Flanders and other southern regions, have enjoyed a high and flourishing position and have gradually taken an increasingly important position in Northern Europe; but it must not be forgotten here that the national energy was at the time much more strongly developed in what is now called Belgium than in the northern provinces. Neither Amsterdam nor Rotterdam could in those days be placed on a par with Ghent and Bruges. The wealth of medieval life had more stamped its imprint on the national character in the South than in the North. The reference to the rich medieval type therefore has much less significance for the present Kingdom of the Netherlands than for the western part of Belgium. And in the second place, it must be added that at that time the unity of our national life was much less consolidated. Under the Burgundians, the Dutch provinces did not occupy an independent position, but were united with what lay outside our later borders; and under Charles V and Philip, we even came, though only in a personal way, into a Union with entirely different states from Central and Southern Europe—something which could not but be detrimental to the confirmation of our own national character. Whoever investigates the historical significance of our lower lands may well find the period we lived through in the Middle Ages highly important, but nevertheless hesitates for a moment to acknowledge without reservation that our glorious period dates from April 1, 1572. Not in the Middle Ages, but in the period of the Reformation, lies the high point of our national history. It cannot be emphasized enough how much scrupulous fairness is required in such a comparison, and we shall beware of the assertion that from the Calvinist side the correct toll of reverence has always been paid to the medieval period in our history, to which they undoubtedly had a right. But it stands to reason that from the Roman Catholic side, too, we have not always been done justice. It cannot be valued highly enough that especially in the second half of the 19th century a more impartial judgment gained influence. Just with a view to this more unanimous judgment, it may now at least be established that not only the foreign historians, one may well say without any exception of importance, honor the period after 1572 as the highest and most glorious, but that also within the country the best interpreters of each of the three types increasingly come to the unanimous conclusion that the world significance of our small country came out most richly in the heyday of Calvinism. This in no way denies that our national life in those days of our glory also showed dark sides. The resistance to the monarchical state form, and even against the House of Orange, has almost become a source of our unhappy discord until 1789, and it is impossible to say how much higher our country would have soared if immediately after the separation from Philip II the crown had been offered to Prince William. It had to come to this in 1813; but who calculates what national suffering would have been spared us if we had taken this important step two centuries earlier. But even if one plays open cards and takes into account all the suffering, oppression and restriction of rights that the temporarily dominant group has inflicted on the other two groups, it is historically established that we were never before 1572 what our national life was in the period between those two dates, and never again after 1789. To top it off, the fact that we offered a refuge to the politically and religiously persecuted from all countries is proof that the actual life here was a life in freedom, and that the publications and ordinances that claimed to infringe on this freedom had more the effect of satisfying formal demands than of inflicting suffering on those who deviated.

Analytical Summary: The three types not equally characteristic. But this does not mean that, in the course of time, each of these three types has in turn dominated the national condition: the Roman Catholics in the Middle Ages, the Calvinists in the 16th century, and the French Revolutionaries after 1789. It does not at all follow from this that they are equal in national significance. Undoubtedly, the Seven Provinces, once living a common life with Flanders and other southern regions, have enjoyed a high and flourishing position and have gradually taken an increasingly important position in Northern Europe; but it must not be forgotten here that the national energy was at the time much more strongly developed in what is now called Belgium than in the northern provinces. Neither Amsterdam nor Rotterdam could in those days be placed on a par with Ghent and Bruges. The wealth of medieval life had more stamped its imprint on the national character in the South than in the North. The reference to the rich medieval type therefore has much less significance for the present Kingdom of the Netherlands than for the western part of Belgium. And in the second place, it must be added that at that time the unity of our national life was much less consolidated. Under the Burgundians, the Dutch provinces did not occupy an independent position, but were united with what lay outside our later borders; and under Charles V and Philip, we even came, though only in a personal way, into a Union with entirely different states from Central and Southern Europe—something which could not but be detrimental to the confirmation of our own national character. Whoever investigates the historical significance of our lower lands may well find the period we lived through in the Middle Ages highly important, but nevertheless hesitates for a moment to acknowledge without reservation that our glorious period dates from April 1, 1572. Not in the Middle Ages, but in the period of the Reformation, lies the high point of our national history. It cannot be emphasized enough how much scrupulous fairness is required in such a comparison, and we shall beware of the assertion that from the Calvinist side the correct toll of reverence has always been paid to the medieval period in our history, to which they undoubtedly had a right. But it stands to reason that from the Roman Catholic side, too, we have not always been done justice. It cannot be valued highly enough that especially in the second half of the 19th century a more impartial judgment gained influence. Just with a view to this more unanimous judgment, it may now at least be established that not only the foreign historians, one may well say without any exception of importance, honor the period after 1572 as the highest and most glorious, but that also within the country the best interpreters of each of the three types increasingly come to the unanimous conclusion that the world significance of our small country came out most richly in the heyday of Calvinism. This in no way denies that our national life in those days of our glory also showed dark sides. The resistance to the monarchical state form, and even against the House of Orange, has almost become a source of our unhappy discord until 1789, and it is impossible to say how much higher our country would have soared if immediately after the separation from Philip II the crown had been offered to Prince William. It had to come to this in 1813; but who calculates what national suffering would have been spared us if we had taken this important step two centuries earlier. But even if one plays open cards and takes into account all the suffering, oppression and restriction of rights that the temporarily dominant group has inflicted on the other two groups, it is historically established that we were never before 1572 what our national life was in the period between those two dates, and never again after 1789. To top it off, the fact that we offered a refuge to the politically and religiously persecuted from all countries is proof that the actual life here was a life in freedom, and that the publications and ordinances that claimed to infringe on this freedom had more the effect of satisfying formal demands than of inflicting suffering on those who deviated.
[40]

28 THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A NATIONAL FACTION.

§21. The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over
[41]

THE THIRD TYPE IS THE WEAKEST. The dominant national type, of itself, gives what has been experienced since 1795. The fame of our country stood far above the highest in the Calvinistic period, and it even seemed a miracle that a land so small and with so little populous a population had risen to such world significance; and may, though in a much lesser degree, be said of these lowlands even in the Middle Ages, that they drew all eyes to themselves by the splendor of their life—of the third period in which the French-revolutionary type here took the lead, it cannot be testified otherwise than that it began to bring us little other than political misery until 1813; that from 1813 to 1848 it caused us to sink into insignificance, at least compared to the days of De Ruyter and Tromp; and that only in 1848 a revival began, in which soon the two historical types of our nationality also awoke from their slumber and helped call forth a fresher national development. The attempt, hatched in Vienna, to restore the unity of the seventeen Dutch provinces, broke our strength from the outset; and the fatal attempt of the counselors of our first King to bind the Roman Catholic Church, especially in Belgium, to an unnatural bond, excluded once and for all the possibility of our growing together again with the Southern Netherlands. For ten years then the fermentation that arose from this division undermined our strength. Finally, forced to use military force, the entire government of our country became increasingly unbalanced, and there could be no talk of a revival of our ancient stem. Not long after, we were hardly counted among the powers of Europe anymore, and not the old Netherlands, but the newly arisen Belgium was commonly called into the European consultation. Even after 1848, the slackness of our position still had an effect. The April movement of 1853 even set us back, and it can be freely said that not much more than a quarter of a century dates the happier turn of affairs that has economically strengthened us and restored some political prestige; not least through the Peace Conference. Politically, the moving force in this, as far as domestic affairs are concerned, mainly came from the School Act of 1857. It may not be forgotten that the Secession, and Thorbecke’s approval of the dragonnades against that Secession, also spoke here; but the most powerful elements of the Separation

[42]

The Antirevolutionary Party as a Segment of the People. Even though the Separatists emigrated to America, the Separation lost its political significance. And without fear of contradiction, it may also be said that it has essentially been the imposition on our people of the neutral school or the “sectarian school of the moderns” that has stirred up the national waters and completely altered the situation. This may be stated with certainty, for it was the School Question that caused the Roman Catholic inhabitants, who had previously, under Thorbecke’s leadership in 1853, sided with the Liberals, to now choose an almost diametrically opposed course. That it was above all Groen van Prinsterer from whom the leadership in the School Question emanated, hardly needs to be recalled, and in this respect it may also be said that the new situation has been heralded by the noble duo of Thorbecke and Groen. Thorbecke, to deliver us from the narrow-minded Conservatism of those days, and Groen, to revive something of the spirit of the fathers in the new situation. While we readily acknowledge that the third period, in which the French Revolutionary type gained the upper hand, has ultimately freed us from much of our former misery and has brought about not a little for which we too must pay our tribute, the fact remains that the first entry of the new type among us, from 1789 onward, ended in a lamentable failure; that it has now already lost its sole dominance, and has again to reckon with the Antirevolutionary element, both from the Calvinist side and from the Roman Catholic side, and can thus be said to have secured for our national people only a certain degree of existence, which casts a shadow over the glory of the Calvinist era. Our final conclusion can therefore be no other than to establish that the Medieval period has shone with glory in many respects; that, however, it was only the Calvinist period that granted our country a kind of hegemony in Europe; and that what it lost through the Sansculottes, the Liberalism that came after 1789 has not returned to our country.

§22. The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the
[43]

THE PSYCHOLOGICAL NATIONAL MYSTERY. The national character is determined by many factors. It would require a delicate scale to determine precisely which distinct elements are blended in our national character and in what proportion this blending took place. The repelling and attracting forces that govern the formation of such a national character often disappear into a psychological mystery. The West Frisian, the Limburger, the South Gröninger differ so much from one another that the unity in the variations is not always immediately grasped. And when one further considers the differences that time and circumstance bring, and above all the religious influences that come into play, the precise features of the common character do not always come into clear view. If, however, one inquires into the probability, it will hardly be disputed that the most fixed folds in the national character are given, not in days of languishing weakness, but in a development of power that dominates the entire life of the people. Now, to make the comparison, if one places the three periods that our people have lived through in historical light, it may be said that the three types that were predominant in each period differed so greatly in the display of energy and in the success of their efforts that the type that came to the fore in the period of 1572–1789 not only offered the richest data of the three, but also, through the hazardous struggles it endured, left the deepest impression on the national character. It was not the first nor the third period of our national existence, but the middle period that brought our people to the highest tension, that aroused the most heroism in our people, that inspired them to the highest sacrifices, and aimed at the highest target. In each of these respects, what entered as a new phase of life with the capture of Briel rose so far above what came after it in loftiness of tone that, as far as character-forming power is concerned, the middle period left the other two far behind. For character formation, high moral power is needed. It is in strife and struggle that high moral power comes to its own formation. And since the difference in character manifestation was so conspicuous in every way between the middle period and the other two, there can be no doubt that of these three periods, the middle one was the most character-forming.

[44]

The variation must have been the dominant force in the formation of our national character. Undoubtedly, the spiritual type that prevailed in the Middle Ages still lingers among us, and there is no doubt that Modernism has now exerted the most influential influence. But if one goes back to the deepest incisions in the character portrait of our people, then whoever places Thorbecke, Groen van Prinsterer, and Schaepman side by side, feels that the historical character of the Dutch people only came to the fore in Thorbecke, who in almost everything felt attracted by foreign models; that Schaepman was indeed the inspiring incarnation of what a neglected, very numerous part of our people chose as its target; but that only of Groen van Prinsterer can it be testified that the historical Dutch thought from the glorious period of our formation as a distinct people spoke from him and worked through him.

§23. The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,

Analytical Summary: The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,
[45]

THE FUNDAMENTAL TONE. The struggle to establish this has been fierce. But we do not derive any rights from it. A national type is not defined by name or designation in the laws. What we have fought for in the second half of the previous century and now in this twentieth century has never been anything other than equality of rights. The tendency of the dominant type has been strongly to curtail our equal rights. They excluded us from public office. A well-known Minister of the Interior openly stated of his applicants for mayoral positions that he could not use a “Kuyperian”; and this was the very same statesman who at first gave the appearance of not feeling any aversion to our aspirations. This was Mr. S. van Houten. Neither Bilderdijk nor Groen van Prinsterer was tolerated at the University. Naturally, no application for a professorship ever came to me. Our influence had to be broken, soon to be shattered. It may well be said that I myself gave occasion for this by my unsparing polemics, but this does not take away the fact that Groen van Prinsterer, who in this respect was more restrained, was almost more bitterly loaded with abuse and pushed into a corner than I myself. Through our own faults and lukewarmness, we were trampled underfoot. Or where was the group that stood up for Bilderdijk to protest against his banishment? And when nevertheless the “Night School” and the circles of the Societies and the Church of the Separation remained inflexible and stubborn in their resistance, so that they persevered in their opposition, there seemed to be but one remedy to completely eradicate us from the national whole, and that remedy would then be the neutral school. They had also tried to put the Reformed Churches of the Fathers in 1815 into a governmental straitjacket that would deprive them of all free breathing space, but the Separation movement proved that progress was not to be made in the religious domain; a fact later more firmly confirmed by the Doleantie. But the School gave hope. In that school one could await the youth, the yet unplastered youth. Blank paper on which to write at will. This would bring us to consumption and make us die away. Against this, our resistance has gone forth; and this resistance would never have led to a decisive result, if the direction in which we pleaded our case had not struck so deep into the fundamental traits of our national character. We were absolutely not allowed to consent even to a partial blurring of this fundamental trait of our national character.

[46]

The mistaken view that led the Reformed to withdraw in silence towards the end of the 18th century would have made us guilty of a glaring dereliction of duty. Our national character has now, from 1572 onwards, sharply expressed and firmly established its fundamental traits. If these fundamental traits were to wear away, the very life and energy of our entire national character would suffer. Hence the calling that goes out to everyone who, out of sincere conviction, still professes the principles of our forefathers—not only for their own sake, but equally for the strengthening of the national character—to restore the direction that, as Article I confesses, “under the leadership of Orange, received its stamp under the influence of the Reformation around 1572.”

§24. Our People.

Analytical Summary: Our People.

Let there be no misunderstanding. What we are working for is not that our people underwent a similar transformation through the Reformation and the national struggle it necessitated, as unfortunately so many were driven into a strongly divergent and now revolutionary direction at the turning point of the 18th into the 19th century. Whoever conceives of it in this way loses sight of two things that must be emphasized. The first is that what we call our people was not formed until 1572; and the second points out that the deepest incision, from which the fundamental traits of our national character emerged, was made in the struggle that followed 1572 and resulted from it. What was found in the Low Countries before 1572 was a much more extensive group of kindred tribes, mixed with Celtic elements in the Walloon region. What we now call the Dutch people, on the other hand, is merely a part of this, which was previously not yet bound into a tighter national unity in the North, but was only then first united. It was only from this tighter union of what was established in the northern provinces that what we now call the Dutch people emerged, in the struggle against Spain. And secondly, this incorrect conception of what we call our people, and the identification of our people with the entire population of the Low Countries in the Middle Ages, leads to a misunderstanding of the inherent national character. That there was originally a strong resemblance, and in

[47]

OUR PEOPLE. The very clear kinship that existed between the Gelderland man and the Fleming, between the Zeelander and the Brabanter, is not disputed, but a split has nevertheless occurred here. What lived together in the North possessed traits that were not found among the people in Flanders and Brabant, and conversely, it lacked not a little that had shone in the South. However related they may be, the character of the people diverged between the Scheldt and the Rhine. This did not come out so strongly as long as both elements lived partly the same life. But scarcely had the political separation occurred, or the two variations in the one national character that had previously existed began to develop ever more independently—and the result was that there were now two distinct peoples lying side by side, certainly related in origin, but yet in that very kinship each displaying its own characteristic traits; and as for the people in the North of the Low Countries, this northern part derived its own variation and its powerful national impulse from the Reformation. Now it must not be said that our national character is a product of the Reformation. Such a statement would betray a lack of historical sense. Here there was a collaboration of two factors. There was in the people inhabiting the northern provinces a disposition that spoke to the Reformation, and there was in the Reformation a spiritual driving force that sympathized with our national character. It was not by chance that in what later became Belgium, Romanism, and in what is now called the Netherlands, Calvinism, continued to dominate the spiritual condition. What inwardly stirred and tingled on the banks of the Scheldt proved in the long run to fit better with the Roman Catholic view of life, while conversely on the banks of the Rhine the Reformed view of life developed richer power. But alongside this Romanist and this Reformational element, the Revolutionary element had long stood in both North and South, though this only came to the surface in 1795, and so in assessing what lay behind 1572, and continued from then until 1789, it is not yet to be reckoned with. The main thing is simply to clearly recognize that what we now call the people of the Netherlands received its stamp not in the Middle Ages, but only in the years of the Reformation, and how, in connection with this, the direction in which our fathers moved in their glorious period also retains serious significance for the future of our people.

[48]

36THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A PEOPLE’S PARTY.

§25. Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit

Analytical Summary: Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit
[49]

LASTING INFLUENCE. CALVINISM. - GUIDED BY ORANGE. This drove us to continually seek out avenues that could lead to a compromise between the Christian confession and modern philosophy. One need only peruse, on the one hand, the works of the old Chantepie de la Saussaye and his kindred spirit Gunning, and on the other hand, those of Van Oosterzee, to be convinced that it is still the German scholarship and the German formula that determines the direction of thought here. What was placed alongside and against this by the admirers of the Reveil from Swiss, French, and English theology, stood too weak in terms of principles to provide sufficient counterweight to these German influences. Thus, neither what the Reveil brought us, nor what this new orthodoxy offered us, could speak to the posterity of our Calvinistic people. One first had to reject this posterity, as Beets did in his talk of the “Night School.” And it was not until around 1879, when Calvinism was restored to honor both on the scientific terrain by Fruin et al. on the modern side, and by the Free University on the orthodox side, that a revived spirit of action once again stirred at the very root of our national existence. For only then did we reconnect with what Article I of our Program calls “the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp around 1572.”

§26. Guided by Orange. To this has been added: “guided by Orange”; an addition that must not be omitted. For it cannot be denied that in the characterized Calvinism, as it also penetrated our country from Geneva and France at that time, there was a danger not to be underestimated, the aftereffects of which are still discernible. In the last chapter of the First Part of this work, I have extensively explained how from the outset there was a decisive element in Calvinism that could only develop and mature later, and that, politically speaking, only came to full effect in the United States; but it was also then added that in Geneva (one need only think of the burning of Servetus) an intolerant dogmatism still prevailed, which, though indispensable for the church, could only result in schism and oppression in its political consequences.

Analytical Summary: Guided by Orange. To this has been added: “guided by Orange”; an addition that must not be omitted. For it cannot be denied that in the characterized Calvinism, as it also penetrated our country from Geneva and France at that time, there was a danger not to be underestimated, the aftereffects of which are still discernible. In the last chapter of the First Part of this work, I have extensively explained how from the outset there was a decisive element in Calvinism that could only develop and mature later, and that, politically speaking, only came to full effect in the United States; but it was also then added that in Geneva (one need only think of the burning of Servetus) an intolerant dogmatism still prevailed, which, though indispensable for the church, could only result in schism and oppression in its political consequences.
[50]

38. THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A PEOPLE’S PARTY. One must distinguish these two well. It is one thing what the Confession demands in the ecclesiastical domain, and quite another what can be derived as a consequence from the ecclesiastical system in the social and political sphere. Not for what concerned Geneva, but certainly for what pertained to France and Scotland, Calvin himself, as I showed in the First Part, emphatically pointed out this difference—and Neo-Calvinism is indeed a genuine offshoot of Calvin’s spirit, but a shoot that could only typify the further progress of history. In Geneva, Church and State were still too interwoven and intermingled, and it is from this that not only the burning of Michael Servetus, but in general all the persecution that has gone out from Calvinistic lands on account of the faith, is to be explained. The unity of State and Church in Geneva has also seduced many of our Calvinistic preachers in the 16th and 17th centuries to also claim authority in the political domain. Even that does not completely exculpate them. And if history nevertheless tells us that in no country in the world was there more spiritual freedom of movement, even for declared heretics, than in Calvinistic Holland, then this must be seen not so much as thanks to the right insight of our theologians of that time, who almost all still had a fondness for the old Roman heresy hunt, but much more to the Prince of Orange, who himself felt warmly for spiritual freedom, and had not only the courage, but also the policy, to on the one hand pay the toll of his reverence to Calvinism, but on the other hand to anticipate the more mature development to which that Calvinism, if rightly understood and according to its fundamental principles, had to lead of itself. One need not therefore idealize the Prince of Orange. Just as with him, so also with Marnix of St. Aldegonde, the desire to detach themselves politically from the supremacy of clericalism undoubtedly also played a role. It can even be conceded that the prospect of confirming and improving their own position also spoke. It all too often goes through human factors in people that only too often also show blemishes. But even as we take this human element into account with Prince William, it still remains historically established that he saw the consequences of Calvinism for the life of the state earlier and better than the theologians of that time, and that it was thanks to him, and not to them, that in the Calvinistic Netherlands there reigned a richer spiritual freedom of movement than anywhere else in the world.

[51]

THE WRONG DONE TO US.39 For this, above all, we must be thankful—that the screws of the Roman model, which many a Calvinist theologian of those days would have most liked to see applied inquisitorially, have fallen into disuse. The addition in Art. I of the words: “Led by Orange” could therefore not be omitted. The Anti-Revolutionary party must, after all, make as clear and loud as possible in its program, to make known to the people with what aversion this craving for spiritual tyranny fills it, as it has so often resounded from the history of the 16th and 17th centuries, and how its intention is—leaving the ecclesiastical life aside (for about this it does not speak as the Anti-Revolutionary State party)—to bring the principles to dominance in the political domain, just as they were first grasped in their purity in the later development of Calvinism, especially in the land across the sea, and in the days of old were put into practice by none other than the Prince of Orange. The Church the Church, but also the State the State. And it is precisely in this that the honor of Calvinism is sought—that from the outset it carried within itself what would lead, through gradual development, to the confession of Common Grace, and thereby to the right relationship between theology and political science.

§27. The wrong done to us. Our party has set up a price in 1878, in its own starting point, to point to this happy influence of the Prince of Orange. Let no confusion creep in here. In matters of ecclesiastical life, Calvin’s insight has also been his own. His dispute with Dathenus does not touch the very life of the church, but the influence of the Church on the life of the people in the political domain. As a theologian, especially Johannes a Lasco deserves appreciation on this point, but on a broader scale it has above all been the influence of the Prince that has safely guided us. Meanwhile, one should not lose sight of the fact that this pointing with preference to the Prince is foreign to the intention, to praise anything that has later emerged from the House of Orange on this delicate terrain of the Princes. Prince Maurice aligned himself much more closely with the Calvinists. The then-emerging conflict with Arminianism necessitated this. But his life history would stand in a more glorious light for us if Oldenbarnevelt had died a natural death. Under Prince Frederick

Analytical Summary: The wrong done to us. Our party has set up a price in 1878, in its own starting point, to point to this happy influence of the Prince of Orange. Let no confusion creep in here. In matters of ecclesiastical life, Calvin’s insight has also been his own. His dispute with Dathenus does not touch the very life of the church, but the influence of the Church on the life of the people in the political domain. As a theologian, especially Johannes a Lasco deserves appreciation on this point, but on a broader scale it has above all been the influence of the Prince that has safely guided us. Meanwhile, one should not lose sight of the fact that this pointing with preference to the Prince is foreign to the intention, to praise anything that has later emerged from the House of Orange on this delicate terrain of the Princes. Prince Maurice aligned himself much more closely with the Calvinists. The then-emerging conflict with Arminianism necessitated this. But his life history would stand in a more glorious light for us if Oldenbarnevelt had died a natural death. Under Prince Frederick
[52]

THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT. Hendrik came to be estranged in a completely different sense from the Calvinist core of our people and the Stadtholder. And later, too, complaint upon complaint often arose. The lamentable thing was not that the love for the House of Orange cooled among us, but that we so often sought in vain the reciprocal love that had come to us so abundantly under the Silent One and Prince Maurice. Even after the return of the House of Orange in 1813, the descendants of the Moderates and Datheenians belonged to the warmest supporters of the monarchy. “The Netherlands and Orange” was even the slogan that was adopted by almost all our electoral associations; and throughout the 19th century, they were fully assured of the loyalty of the Reformed. They knew that they could count on us; and when in 1847/’49 the Stork in Flushing and the radical Courant in Arnhem dared to set up their exploits against the House of Orange so boldly and brutally that the King himself was threatened with the scaffold, it was in our circle that not only prayers were offered at the altar, but also for the throne. In no circle more than in ours did enthusiasm for Orange reign. But precisely because this is established and not for a moment open to doubt, our predecessors have never concealed how painful it was to us that this love was so coldly reciprocated. The violence done to the Reformed Churches of the Fathers by the Royal Decree of January 1815 continues to cry out for redress and restoration. Our first King should not have allowed himself to be burdened with this ordinance. By issuing this Decree, he committed clear injustice and transgressed against a sacred past of ours. The same must be said of the treatment meted out to the Secessionists in 1834. There, too, nothing was spared, and no violence was withheld, in order to impose exotic, foreign ideas and theories on the ecclesiastical domain. The later withdrawal of the fateful decree of 1815 did not improve the situation, but even aggravated it. Only under King William III did one sometimes hear friendly words again in audiences. And when Baron Mackay was entrusted with cabinet formation in 1889, the sting of the breach seemed at least healed, and it could also be gratefully acknowledged thereafter that our Queen often made us forget the sad lack of Orange’s reciprocal love from earlier years. The relationship has now become quite different. As Calvinists, we do not ask for any privilege, any privilege over our fellow citizens.

[53]

ENDURING BONDS.41 of a different spirit. All we desire is that we will neither be relegated to the status of zealots, nor used as blind followers. It must remain the Netherlands and Orange, and we too must belong to that Netherlands. Grateful for the happy turn of events that also occurred in this case, the Anti-Revolutionary party could therefore in 1878 not present a principled program without explicitly pointing to the bond that binds Orange and the Netherlands. Flattery is not at all involved here. We kneel before God, and never before the bearer of the Crown.

§28. Enduring Bond. No Calvinist, in any country, has ever thought of whitewashing any injustice for the benefit of a princely ruler. But apart from what criticism may later have become necessary, we still always honor in the appearance of Prince William the Silent a gift of God to our people in their then so distressed condition; we thank God for what the time has bestowed on our fatherland in the generation of the Prince; we now still refresh ourselves in the national glory that has emanated from this House of Princes over our people as a nation; and we now still take sides, not on ecclesiastical, but on political terrain, for what the Silent One, the first among all princes in Europe, as the consequence of the Reformation, brought us in the bestowal of full spiritual freedom. Precisely for this reason, protest is constantly lodged by us against any attempt to discredit Orange as an ornament, as Mr. S. van Houten has dared to do. Not, we hasten to add here, as if this Statesman wanted to detract from the glory of our House of Orange. What he spoke of as an “ornament” was only the formulation in a single word of his constitutional theory. But here lies the difference, that such a declining expression of “ornament” was more dear to him, while it strikes us as offensive to our love for Orange. Especially after the shameful treatment that the House of Orange has suffered through its expulsion and forced flight to England in 1797, and after the more than shameful threat with which in 1848 in the liberal press one even dared to speak of the scaffold to unsettle Orange itself, it betrayed a stiff lack of tenderness and self-control in a man who ultimately became an advisor to the Crown, to hear him speak of “ornament.” Not daring to criticize a Prince openly is a cringing cowardice that Orange demands less than it requires, and which is unthinkable especially in the Calvinist. But this does not detract from the firm demand that we remain thankful for honoring what our God has bestowed on us in Orange through his great Princes. A glory in the past, the happy echo of which was to be heard in our revered Queen, as the last scion of the ancient House of Orange.

Analytical Summary: Enduring Bond. No Calvinist, in any country, has ever thought of whitewashing any injustice for the benefit of a princely ruler. But apart from what criticism may later have become necessary, we still always honor in the appearance of Prince William the Silent a gift of God to our people in their then so distressed condition; we thank God for what the time has bestowed on our fatherland in the generation of the Prince; we now still refresh ourselves in the national glory that has emanated from this House of Princes over our people as a nation; and we now still take sides, not on ecclesiastical, but on political terrain, for what the Silent One, the first among all princes in Europe, as the consequence of the Reformation, brought us in the bestowal of full spiritual freedom. Precisely for this reason, protest is constantly lodged by us against any attempt to discredit Orange as an ornament, as Mr. S. van Houten has dared to do. Not, we hasten to add here, as if this Statesman wanted to detract from the glory of our House of Orange. What he spoke of as an “ornament” was only the formulation in a single word of his constitutional theory. But here lies the difference, that such a declining expression of “ornament” was more dear to him, while it strikes us as offensive to our love for Orange. Especially after the shameful treatment that the House of Orange has suffered through its expulsion and forced flight to England in 1797, and after the more than shameful threat with which in 1848 in the liberal press one even dared to speak of the scaffold to unsettle Orange itself, it betrayed a stiff lack of tenderness and self-control in a man who ultimately became an advisor to the Crown, to hear him speak of “ornament.” Not daring to criticize a Prince openly is a cringing cowardice that Orange demands less than it requires, and which is unthinkable especially in the Calvinist. But this does not detract from the firm demand that we remain thankful for honoring what our God has bestowed on us in Orange through his great Princes. A glory in the past, the happy echo of which was to be heard in our revered Queen, as the last scion of the ancient House of Orange.
[54]

§29. According to the needs of our time.

Analytical Summary: According to the needs of our time.

Finally, our Program adds to the thus discussed the following so highly necessary further specification, that the Anti-Revolutionary party does not aim to provide in our days a strict copy of the past, but that our striving must rather be “to allow the character of the people to develop into a form that meets the needs of our time, and thus corresponds to the modified state of the people.” Not to have seen this firm demand of history has always been the deadly error of all reactionary politics. A professed principle is not bound to one single form of application and elaboration. Rather, the same principle of statecraft can bring out many different forms of government; and this not out of a constant longing for change, but because other conditions and circumstances have arisen, new questions demanded a solution, and new discoveries brought forth entirely different movements in the people’s life. Whoever does not understand this, and does not take it into account, does not increase the power of the principle he professes, but puts it out of use and dooms the confessor to powerlessness. It was therefore a dereliction of duty, when in the first half of the 19th century the Calvinists among us felt no need for a literature that took into account the modified state of the people, and constantly immersed themselves in nothing but the reading of what they called “the old writers.” The less they could be satisfied with this, because these “old writers” were mainly not countrymen, but Englishmen, who could not fully understand our national character and our national needs.

[55]

THE CHURCH AND THE STATE IN MUTUAL RELATION.43 And the Scots had been those who, insofar as they also touched on political issues, could not reckon with our national data and almost exclusively chose as their guiding principle what was pleaded for in Great Britain. This placed our so numerous Calvinists, who remained adrift here, outside their time. What was discussed among parties in the country did not arouse their interest, and what moved them stood outside the connection with the actual political dispute. Even Da Costa was thereby tempted to temporarily favor a Monarchical Absolutism that would ultimately not even be bound by an oath to the constitution; while Bilderdijk, on the other hand, in a completely different way, pushed away what Beets called the Night School, but, as compensation for what was thus lost, turned to a gleaning from medieval life, which at least in these seven northern provinces spoke to almost no one. If what the Fathers confessed is no longer applicable to the degenerate condition, then the principle from which they lived and from which they derived their heroic magic power may undoubtedly not be abandoned for a single moment, much less surrendered; but it is an equally inexorable demand that a way of thinking and acting be derived from this principle that can offer a way out for the present. It must therefore be fought with inexorable determination, on the one hand, that not a splinter of one’s principle be allowed to break off, but on the other hand also that the confessed principle not be stifled by binding it unchangeably to its first life expression. A principle is not a dry formula that merely asks to be written down; what is to be a principle must carry life within it and from this inherent life constantly shoot forth new shoots.

§30. The Church and the State in Mutual Relation. One immediately feels this in the relationship between Church and State. In the 16th century, the struggle that the Reformation undertook against Rome’s overpowering authority naturally brought the ecclesiastical question to the fore in all political domains. The ecclesiastical question dominated the entire political sphere, both domestically and with a view to foreign relations, and here the foreign relations mostly stood...

Analytical Summary: The Church and the State in Mutual Relation. One immediately feels this in the relationship between Church and State. In the 16th century, the struggle that the Reformation undertook against Rome’s overpowering authority naturally brought the ecclesiastical question to the fore in all political domains. The ecclesiastical question dominated the entire political sphere, both domestically and with a view to foreign relations, and here the foreign relations mostly stood...
[56]

44THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT. The foreground, while the war, as we may express it, was not then in the air. As a matter of course, the Roman Catholic influence at the beginning of the mighty struggle could not have been directed to anything other than the maintenance of the position which had been acquired as the outcome of a centuries-long struggle with the Ghibellines in Germany and with the Clergy in England. State and Church had in the Middle Ages grown together to such a degree that a radical change in the ecclesiastical domain would of itself also strike at the root of the civic life, and call into being alliances between the various Powers which were intended primarily to offer resistance to the Reformation movement. Now, since in not a few lands the Reformation had indeed penetrated, but had only won over a very small part of the population, it could not be otherwise but that, upon the outbreak of the war, the constant danger threatened that the ecclesiastical minority would be tempted to collude with the enemy. This, too, was felt by us—how the Roman Catholic part of the population could not always be counted upon firmly to uphold our resistance against the Spanish might. At least, one feared here, as elsewhere, a constant working in the hand of what the foreign enemy aimed at, on the part of the minority. In France, one was constantly afraid that the Huguenots would side with England and with the apostates in Germany, and just so here at home, that Spain and France would receive indirect support from the Roman Catholic leaders. To what extent this was rightly seen, is here left undecided, but the fear existed; and it was this apprehension which of itself led to the effect that, not only in the administration of the country, a watchful eye was kept upon the differently-minded minority, but that their freedom of movement was taken from them, and as much as possible their influence on the history of the land was restricted. It could therefore almost never be otherwise, or everywhere the government identified itself with the Church of the majority of the people, and, by binding the higher officials to the confession of that Church, excluded the dissenters as much as possible from all participation in the government. If one now asks whether this exclusion, through the assumption of civil disabilities, was contained in the Calvinistic principle, or at least flowed so directly therefrom that the Calvinistic conviction could not tolerate any other political policy than one bound to a particular Church, then the statesman, for whom the history, as over England to America,

[57]

THE CHURCH AND THE STATE IN MUTUAL RELATION.45

If one looks at the historical development, a clear picture emerges that cannot be denied. A political system that binds the exercise of authority to the confession of a particular Church is not of Calvinistic origin, but of medieval provenance; this is Roman Catholic. Not that the Roman Catholic Church cannot thrive without this system being in force. The United States and our own country show this differently. Overseas and in our own land, the Roman Catholic Church has even come to a richer bloom, without the state system being bound in any way to the Roman Catholic confession. This, however, does not take away that where the Roman Catholic Church may unite almost the entire population of the land under its scepter, and has no apostasy or revolution to lament among the members of its body, the Roman Catholic system will always call for a state church, and will place no small obstacles in the way of the freedom of movement of those who think differently. The idea of the coherence between Church and State, developed by Rome, was so ingrained in the 16th century that neither the Reformed nor the Lutheran movement initially paid any attention to abolishing it. Even Calvin initially acted under the prevailing power of this idea, and Geneva was its prime example. And it was only the religious wars in France and then in England that have necessarily led, not to Lutheranism, but to Calvinism—not in conflict with, but entirely in conformity with what was inspired in the principles of Calvin—to the recognition of Common Grace and thereby to the liberation of conscience and the separation of the State from the Church. Now, since the international situation has changed completely, and the influence of the Church on the life of the people has taken on a completely different character, to still want to cling to the theory of Dort regarding the relationship between State and Church is, of course, to prefer the grave air above the free breath of life. Hence our plea for the Free Church in the free State as the highest consequence of the Calvinistic principle. Even in Germany, it is now recognized that Calvin, and not Luther, pointed the way here, and the Reformed Church has itself to blame for having created a situation that let go of its positively Christian character as an indispensable condition of life, by swearing by what still belongs at home in the Lutheran and Roman Catholic lands, but from which we have outgrown.

[58]

46THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT.

§31. The People’s Representation. It is no different with the People’s Representation. Under the republican form of government, our Commonwealth has not known representation as we understand it today. There were estates, but no People’s Representation; the cities and manorial landowners held the power; the deputies were in no way representatives of the nation before the Government, but rather the installers and maintainers of the Government itself. There was certainly popular choice in the election of the power-holders, but the right to participate in that choice was extremely limited. As shown in the last chapter of the First Part, Calvin did not praise such a system, and ultimately he himself went over to advising in a democratic sense. Whoever now tries to apply the completely antiquated representation ideas of the 16th century to the present state of affairs would thereby show themselves to be anything but faithful to the pure principle, but rather to blatantly disown it. To now still advocate a State form that does not fully do justice to the representative principle, would brand one as backward and out of one’s time. The question of a proper People’s Representation has now itself become the main question for the future development of the State life. And the Calvinist direction, which has always proceeded from the people, and even found its strength in the “common folk,” would lose its inner driving force and let itself be written off, if it did not take the lead rather than follow on this point of our constitutional development in the spirit of freedom and love of the people. To be a Christian Democrat remains the title of honor for every Calvinist. §32. Navy and Army. As a third example, let the National Defense be pointed out. In the days of the Reformation, there was hardly any mention of a national army, as we know it today. The Condottieri had indeed lost ground, but the army with which one went to war still consisted mostly of volunteers offering themselves, and even of mercenary troops from foreign countries, just as we still used to employ, and partly still do, not a few Swiss, Germans and Belgians for our East Indian army. Only of the army of Gustavus Vasa and of the army of Cromwell can it be said that they were driven and urged by a higher and holier interest. For the rest, however, the armies were mostly composed of rough men, and the holy enthusiasm for the cause of the country was sometimes all too little to be noticed. That the same regiment, after the lapse of ten years, drew the sword against a higher national interest, which it had first helped to defend at the price of its blood, occurred more than once. All the more the custom came into vogue with us to assemble the power for the defense of the country’s interests from hired manpower, inasmuch as the navy with us, more than the army, stood in importance, and the manning of the navy, both here and still in England, was constantly recruited by free choice. The civic guard had a more national character, and knew no wages as a motive; but the militia, as it is now in force, first appeared under Napoleon’s domination, and has since also been maintained in our Kingdom. Meanwhile, our people have been far from eager for the new military idea. To become a soldier was not considered an honor, but a plague, and the system of substitutes soon came up to free oneself from this plague by money. It has therefore lasted until the very end of the 19th century before we really got a national army, and the idea of universal military service, which now imposes itself of its own accord, is still not realized with us. Now, because in the glorious days of our fathers the condition prevailed in navy and army that excluded a national army, shall one identify the system then in force with Calvinism, and therefore, as ultra-conservative, set it against the military force that is now so absolutely indispensable? Certainly not. The principle of Calvinism and this is, to highly esteem the significance of the national life; and the future of the national life is now inexorably, at least for small nations, in danger, if not the whole people finally girds the sword as one man for the country’s independence.

Analytical Summary: The People’s Representation. It is no different with the People’s Representation. Under the republican form of government, our Commonwealth has not known representation as we understand it today. There were estates, but no People’s Representation; the cities and manorial landowners held the power; the deputies were in no way representatives of the nation before the Government, but rather the installers and maintainers of the Government itself. There was certainly popular choice in the election of the power-holders, but the right to participate in that choice was extremely limited. As shown in the last chapter of the First Part, Calvin did not praise such a system, and ultimately he himself went over to advising in a democratic sense. Whoever now tries to apply the completely antiquated representation ideas of the 16th century to the present state of affairs would thereby show themselves to be anything but faithful to the pure principle, but rather to blatantly disown it. To now still advocate a State form that does not fully do justice to the representative principle, would brand one as backward and out of one’s time. The question of a proper People’s Representation has now itself become the main question for the future development of the State life. And the Calvinist direction, which has always proceeded from the people, and even found its strength in the “common folk,” would lose its inner driving force and let itself be written off, if it did not take the lead rather than follow on this point of our constitutional development in the spirit of freedom and love of the people. To be a Christian Democrat remains the title of honor for every Calvinist. §32. Navy and Army. As a third example, let the National Defense be pointed out. In the days of the Reformation, there was hardly any mention of a national army, as we know it today. The Condottieri had indeed lost ground, but the army with which one went to war still consisted mostly of volunteers offering themselves, and even of mercenary troops from foreign countries, just as we still used to employ, and partly still do, not a few Swiss, Germans and Belgians for our East Indian army. Only of the army of Gustavus Vasa and of the army of Cromwell can it be said that they were driven and urged by a higher and holier interest. For the rest, however, the armies were mostly composed of rough men, and the holy enthusiasm for the cause of the country was sometimes all too little to be noticed. That the same regiment, after the lapse of ten years, drew the sword against a higher national interest, which it had first helped to defend at the price of its blood, occurred more than once. All the more the custom came into vogue with us to assemble the power for the defense of the country’s interests from hired manpower, inasmuch as the navy with us, more than the army, stood in importance, and the manning of the navy, both here and still in England, was constantly recruited by free choice. The civic guard had a more national character, and knew no wages as a motive; but the militia, as it is now in force, first appeared under Napoleon’s domination, and has since also been maintained in our Kingdom. Meanwhile, our people have been far from eager for the new military idea. To become a soldier was not considered an honor, but a plague, and the system of substitutes soon came up to free oneself from this plague by money. It has therefore lasted until the very end of the 19th century before we really got a national army, and the idea of universal military service, which now imposes itself of its own accord, is still not realized with us. Now, because in the glorious days of our fathers the condition prevailed in navy and army that excluded a national army, shall one identify the system then in force with Calvinism, and therefore, as ultra-conservative, set it against the military force that is now so absolutely indispensable? Certainly not. The principle of Calvinism and this is, to highly esteem the significance of the national life; and the future of the national life is now inexorably, at least for small nations, in danger, if not the whole people finally girds the sword as one man for the country’s independence.
[59]

Page 60—-

48THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT.

§33. The Social Question. With a brief mention of the social question, let us refer to these examples and conclude. In the 16th century, there was no social question. The Peasants’ Revolt in Germany seemed to point in that direction, but it arose from a completely different motive. What called the social question into a very peculiar form of life in the 19th century was twofold. Firstly, the fact that the increased technology made it possible for factories to arise, in which hundreds of workers were set to work simultaneously; something which naturally caused the close and cordial relationship between employer and worker to be entirely lost. And secondly, the more mature development of the working class, thanks to the high flight that popular education also took in connection with vocational education. This higher position of the worker demanded more abundant satisfaction of the needs of social community life, and the heaping up of hundreds of workers outside of all higher and more intimate connection with the employer, brought into existence conditions that our fathers had never known. Not that our fathers had no care for the workers. The Guild system certainly promoted an excellent understanding to no small degree. The needs of the working class spoke less loudly; but the needs, as far as they spoke, were then as a rule well and not too narrowly satisfied. What only characterized the then existing condition in a less desirable way, was the excessive dependence in which the worker found himself on his employer. Slavery had come to an end, but the conviction had not yet gained entrance that the worker, as a free man, stood not under, but alongside his employer, in civil rights and in social life. But if one now asks whether this then existing condition could be logically deduced from the principle we profess, in the sense that the introduction of an entirely changed condition would be a denial of that principle, then it must be answered with absolute decisiveness, that a denial of principle can only come into question with him who, where such a totally different condition has entered, refuses to reveal the power of his principle also in the solution of the questions now presenting themselves.

Analytical Summary: The Social Question. With a brief mention of the social question, let us refer to these examples and conclude. In the 16th century, there was no social question. The Peasants’ Revolt in Germany seemed to point in that direction, but it arose from a completely different motive. What called the social question into a very peculiar form of life in the 19th century was twofold. Firstly, the fact that the increased technology made it possible for factories to arise, in which hundreds of workers were set to work simultaneously; something which naturally caused the close and cordial relationship between employer and worker to be entirely lost.
