And secondly, the more mature development of the working class, thanks to the high flight that popular education also took in connection with vocational education. This higher position of the worker demanded more abundant satisfaction of the needs of social community life, and the heaping up of hundreds of workers outside of all higher and more intimate connection with the employer, brought into existence conditions that our fathers had never known. Not that our fathers had no care for the workers. The Guild system certainly promoted an excellent understanding to no small degree. The needs of the working class spoke less loudly; but the needs, as far as they spoke, were then as a rule well and not too narrowly satisfied. What only characterized the then existing condition in a less desirable way, was the excessive dependence in which the worker found himself on his employer. Slavery had come to an end, but the conviction had not yet gained entrance that the worker, as a free man, stood not under, but alongside his employer, in civil rights and in social life. But if one now asks whether this then existing condition could be logically deduced from the principle we profess, in the sense that the introduction of an entirely changed condition would be a denial of that principle, then it must be answered with absolute decisiveness, that a denial of principle can only come into question with him who, where such a totally different condition has entered, refuses to reveal the power of his principle also in the solution of the questions now presenting themselves.
[61]

THE SOCIAL QUESTION - KEEPING PACE WITH OUR TIME.49Just Calvinism, which has always placed the common people entirely on the same line with the more powerful middle class in Christ’s Church, and in its consideration of the mutual relationship in civil society, has strictly adhered to Holy Scripture, could not for a moment fail to keep step with the development of the social principle. And it would have been not a maintenance, but rather a culpable denial of our principle, if we had resisted the democratic suction of our century. The lower class of the population also belongs in every way to our people, and therefore no social condition may rest, which does not also provide satisfaction for the primary needs of social community life for the common people.

§34. Keeping Pace with Our Time.

Analytical Summary: Keeping Pace with Our Time.

The four given examples may suffice to make it clear what we mean by a “development of our political life in a form that meets the needs of our time.” In the relationship of Church and State, in the significance of the Representative Assembly, in the defense of our national existence, and no less in the social question, we now find ourselves confronted with entirely different data than the policy of our fathers in the 16th and 17th centuries governed. Now to want to maintain not only the principle, but also the practice of our Calvinistic fathers against these new conditions, would be tantamount to writing our principle to death. Whoever does not clearly see that the boy grown into a young man has put off the child’s shoes, forfeits all right to have a say in what the cause of the fatherland demands. In three respects, therefore, the Calvinist of today must be prepared to adapt his principle to a change in the form of life. Left to himself, the Anti-Revolutionary group is too small in number to let its influence on the affairs of the country penetrate under the vigor of the representative system. From this it follows that it must seek affiliation with like-minded groups, and thus also that, with a view to the necessity of such cooperation, it must push into the background what would otherwise repel.

[62]

50THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT. to come forward, and in turn to bring to the fore the problems on which unanimous agreement is to be obtained. For whatever differences and even conflicts may still arise, one must strive for a compromise. And apart from that, what separates us from our other, more distant kindred spirits should wound their form as little as possible. Formerly, this was different. In the 16th century, the antithesis that holds here could neither be veiled nor softened. Now, since the struggle is against an entirely different enemy, in case of difference, a more cautious approach is not only permissible, but a demand of statesmanship. In what has been handed down to us from the age of our glory, it is not the then-current form, but only the essence that must bind us; and it must, as Da Costa sang to us, increasingly become “in its form of this time.” Secondly, the problems that occupied our Fathers were often entirely different from the problems that demand our attention. If one now nevertheless constantly tried to go back to the problems of the 16th century, this would not result in anything other than a repristination, and all our political efforts on our part would be in vain. Nor can nor may our party shirk the obligation to help find a solution for the so complex issues that now demand a solution in the political arena, in accordance with its own peculiar principles.

§35. Dogmatic and Political.

Analytical Summary: Dogmatic and Political.

And here comes in the third place, that our principles are not only to be confessed, but always require clearer historical elucidation. This last point manifests itself by itself in the exchange of the name Puritan, as explained in §8 of Our Program, and the name Calvinist, or Neo-Calvinist, which we placed at the beginning of the first part of this work. In 1878 there was still an objection to choosing the rigid, taut name of Calvinist as a starting point, inasmuch as at that time, both here at home and abroad (with the exception of Hungary and a part of southern France), a Calvinist meant nothing else...

[63]

DOGMATIC POLITICS. He was a zealous advocate of the doctrine of election, preferably in an infralapsarian sense. This, and nothing else, was what was understood, if now and then in England, here at home or in Germany, someone spoke of a Calvinist. The meaning of the word “Calvinist” had become exclusively dogmatic. Its historical significance had been lost. And the designation of a political system was hardly seen by anyone anymore. It would therefore have led to an incomprehensible qualification in 1878 if we had already insisted on the Calvinist baptism of our party. On the other hand, the political character still lay well in the name of Puritan, as it is still quite common, especially in Scotland, but also in England. That is why at the time the name of Puritan had to be preferred by us over that of Calvinist. Since then, however, this necessity has lapsed. The study, which has been devoted to Calvinism in its various manifestations almost everywhere since 1880, gives us the right, and even the duty, to return to the generic name of Calvinist. For now we are so far that the unfolding which Calvinism underwent in its transition from Geneva to France, from France to England, and from England to America, is clearly visible before our eyes. The distinction between ecclesiastical and dogmatic Calvinism on the one hand and political and economic Calvinism on the other has now been historically established, and all uncertainty has been removed about the metamorphosis that political Calvinism has undergone in the course of the centuries. What is now often called neo-Calvinism is in fact nothing other than the result to which the natural unfolding of Calvin’s ideas has led. In our First Part this has been extensively pointed out. Now, therefore, it suffices to state that this further study of the historical course of Calvinism gives us the right, indeed the duty, to abandon the kind of name Puritan that was still used in 1878 and to restore the generic name of Calvinist in its political honor. Of dogmatic Calvinism we do not speak here at all. Here the ecclesiastical and dogmatic element that is hidden in the name of Calvinism remains completely excluded. Even whoever wishes to join our party as a member is not tested on his dogmatic confession. For a political party, a profession of agreement with the political program suffices.

[64]

52THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS A PEOPLE’S MOVEMENT.

§36. The Shift in England. In the nearly forty years that have elapsed since the first publication of Our Program, yet another complete transformation has taken place, which must equally not be passed over in silence, namely, in regard to our connection with the Anti-revolutionary groups or circles in other countries. Since 1878, a wholly different relationship has also emerged outside our own circle, as far as the foreign countries are concerned, particularly in Great Britain. Gladstone’s death on May 19, 1898, and Chamberlain’s attack in 1900 on the South African Boer Republics are the two historical monuments that have been decisive here. Those who were Calvinists in the democratic sense had often turned their political sympathies towards England, and I too have allowed myself to do so. I did not forget how England had often done us highly unjust competition in the maritime and colonial sphere in the 17th century, nor how England had taken not only Ceylon but even the Cape Colony from us at the beginning of the 19th century. Yet our sympathies still seemed to have to lean towards England. In 1890, Gladstone had published his well-known work: The Impregnable Rock of the Holy Scripture, in which he had advocated more clearly than ever before his steadfast agreement with the Statesmanship that rests on God’s Word. He felt a deep aversion to the Radicalism that was then already creeping into England. He moved unconditionally in the line of the Old Whigs. And without hesitation it must be stated that all that was of Calvinistic origin, both in England and especially in Scotland, supported him. It is true that it already raised some doubts that a man like John Morley, who was Gladstone’s political confidant, moved entirely outside that rock of the holy Scripture, but here too Groen van Prinsterer found support in Allard Pierson, and Calvin was also rehabilitated here in our country by Allard Pierson. Forster’s school policy could serve as an example to us here, and the encounters I had with Forster and other English statesmen, through the mediation of my academic contemporary Lord Reay from Leiden, always warmed my sympathies anew. I felt again and again that, as an English citizen, I would be a Gladstonian, and how, conversely, Gladstone, whatever his ultimate fate may have been on our terrain, was undoubtedly a Groenian.

Analytical Summary: The Shift in England. In the nearly forty years that have elapsed since the first publication of Our Program, yet another complete transformation has taken place, which must equally not be passed over in silence, namely, in regard to our connection with the Anti-revolutionary groups or circles in other countries. Since 1878, a wholly different relationship has also emerged outside our own circle, as far as the foreign countries are concerned, particularly in Great Britain. Gladstone’s death on May 19, 1898, and Chamberlain’s attack in 1900 on the South African Boer Republics are the two historical monuments that have been decisive here. Those who were Calvinists in the democratic sense had often turned their political sympathies towards England, and I too have allowed myself to do so. I did not forget how England had often done us highly unjust competition in the maritime and colonial sphere in the 17th century, nor how England had taken not only Ceylon but even the Cape Colony from us at the beginning of the 19th century. Yet our sympathies still seemed to have to lean towards England. In 1890, Gladstone had published his well-known work: The Impregnable Rock of the Holy Scripture, in which he had advocated more clearly than ever before his steadfast agreement with the Statesmanship that rests on God’s Word. He felt a deep aversion to the Radicalism that was then already creeping into England. He moved unconditionally in the line of the Old Whigs. And without hesitation it must be stated that all that was of Calvinistic origin, both in England and especially in Scotland, supported him. It is true that it already raised some doubts that a man like John Morley, who was Gladstone’s political confidant, moved entirely outside that rock of the holy Scripture, but here too Groen van Prinsterer found support in Allard Pierson, and Calvin was also rehabilitated here in our country by Allard Pierson. Forster’s school policy could serve as an example to us here, and the encounters I had with Forster and other English statesmen, through the mediation of my academic contemporary Lord Reay from Leiden, always warmed my sympathies anew. I felt again and again that, as an English citizen, I would be a Gladstonian, and how, conversely, Gladstone, whatever his ultimate fate may have been on our terrain, was undoubtedly a Groenian.
[65]

THE REVERSAL IN ENGLAND.53 This is how it would have been. To further confirm this, I was reinforced when I received the invitation from Pretoria to come to England and support the Transvaal Deputation of Kruger and Du Toit. With Beelaerts van Blokland, I then went to London, and for weeks on end I was able to plead the almost sacred cause of the Boers. Even then, Gladstone was the man of my full sympathy. One remembers how the overrunning of Spitskop in the Transvaal had unexpectedly strengthened the Boers in their resistance against England. Gladstone, though naturally called upon to watch over England’s interests first and foremost, felt warmly for this, and in the end the Deputation was allowed to return home to Pretoria, enriched by what Gladstone had granted them. I used to go to England almost every year. My research on La Saco in the Guildhall and in the Walloon Church of St. Martin le Grand necessitated this, and Lord Reay’s gracious introduction always made my stay in London, Oxford, and Cambridge richer in political and literary enjoyment. All the more so since Stahl, von Gerlach, and other Anti-Revolutionaries in Germany were so strongly inclined towards the Conservative groups, it attracted me increasingly that in England I found political sympathies that, like my own, had their origin in the Calvinistic-Presbyterian activism of the 16th and 17th centuries. It has been deeply moving for me, then, that such a serious change has taken place here after Gladstone’s death. The Conservatives came to power, and the determination to undermine and not to do justice to the Boer republics soon became apparent. Even during my stay in America in 1898, I heard that Chamberlain had been to Washington to secure Mc Kinley’s neutrality, as if for war, and then it broke out. The two states of Dutch origin in South Africa were destroyed, and with our whole people I deeply felt the cutting injustice that was thereby also done to the Dutch stem. And it was then, finally, that Chamberlain’s Cabinet was overthrown, but not replaced by a Cabinet in the direction of Gladstone, but by a government like the one that still continues under Asquith, which threw Gladstone’s memories overboard, even in Ulster threatened the re-emerging Presbyterianism with armed force, and found in Botha the man to carry on Chamberlain’s undertakings in South Africa. Against this, there is no

[66]

54THE Antirevolutionary PARTY AS A PEOPLE’S MOVEMENT. A wave of resistance has emanated from England or Scotland. It now became clear how Presbyterianism in Great Britain had lost all independent political significance; and the mighty impulse of a higher principle, which still drew so powerfully towards England under Gladstone, was overshadowed in less than a decade. It is under a government that calls itself liberal, but is supported by all that is still Presbyterian or Puritan, that the hero of the Boer uprising, Christian de Wet, has been imprisoned in the church, after La Rey had already been killed. Thus, the turnaround in Great Britain has become a complete antithesis, and for our Antirevolutionary party, unless we want to disown our principles and our past, there is no longer any support or sympathy to be found across the sea in Great Britain. It is French radicalism that triumphs in England.

§37. The Antirevolutionaries in Germany. In Germany, there was no compensation to be found for this. Stöcker’s appearance did have my warm sympathy, but as I have told Stöcker himself more than once, he failed to take a political stance in a general sense. In the social sphere, he acted energetically and decisively, but his social policy hung too much in the air and lacked a general political foundation. That is why joining his efforts was not possible for us. The Junker parties, the Agrarians, who made up the core of the Conservatives, had increasingly disowned Stahl’s political testament. Von Bismarck’s political conduct moved almost entirely in the sphere of strengthening Germany’s global significance, and although he openly professed, “We Germans fear God and no one else,” the religious vein running through his politics was still strongly tinged. In the May Laws, he even dared to attack the freedom of the Church. With all the admiration for the mighty results he achieved, he could still not elicit any political affiliation from us Antirevolutionaries. The “Reformists,” who were reviving in Germany, did try to approach me once or twice to join their ranks, but their school tactics, their avoidance of all political involvement, and their sober Calvinism seemed to me to promise too little for an attempt at affiliation. In Barmen and Elberfeld, there certainly would have been favorable prospects; but even there, the conditions under which the action took place were too specifically German, and too little principled and comprehensive in a general sense, for me to engage in a closer understanding without suffering harm myself. The Protestant element in general, and the Reformed in particular, lacked entirely what made the Roman Catholic Center in Germany so strong, in that it did not have the courage to act as an independent party. It was therefore understandable that some more decisive men like Kleist-Retzow and Von Gerlach sought to be incorporated into the Center. But this too failed on its own. And so, alas, it must be admitted that the rich prospect that opened up for the powerful formation of an Antirevolutionary party in Germany after Stahl’s appearance has not been realized. Personally, there are thousands and thousands in Germany who are kindred spirits to us and envy our independent party position; but it has not come to an independent formation, and there is hardly any prospect of it happening in the future. Switzerland offers no hope for the future either. In the French cantons, what is outlined in France is repeated in a bioscopic way. The Italian cantons are too small in population to take an independent position. And in the German cantons, it is the Roman Catholic ones where the most emphasis is placed on the honor of one’s own principles, while in the Protestant ones, German theology often marries French political ideas, and thus here too a revival of Calvinism is excluded. One saw it at Calvin’s centenary. Glorification of a name in which part of Switzerland spoke of ancient glories, but of spiritual kinship with the traditions of the man of Geneva, not a trace in all that was manifested and celebrated. Religion and politics here too almost hermetically sealed off from each other. Not even the faintest indication of political action in Calvin’s footsteps. §38. In Roman Catholic lands. In predominantly Roman Catholic lands, there could naturally be no revival of the Antirevolutionary movement. The Roman Catholic Church, with its hierarchical structure and its centralized authority, does not allow the formation of independent political parties within its fold. The Ultramontane movement, which has spread from Rome, has indeed brought about a certain political mobilization, but this has been entirely in the service of the Roman Catholic Church and its interests. The Antirevolutionary principle, which seeks to maintain the independence of the various spheres of life, is diametrically opposed to this. The Roman Catholic Church, with its claim to sovereignty over all of life, cannot tolerate any independent political movement that would limit its power. And so, in Roman Catholic lands, the Antirevolutionary movement has found no foothold. The political activity of Roman Catholics has been entirely absorbed by the struggle for the interests of their Church. The Antirevolutionary principle, which seeks to maintain the independence of the various spheres of life, has found no echo there.

Analytical Summary: The Antirevolutionaries in Germany. In Germany, there was no compensation to be found for this. Stöcker’s appearance did have my warm sympathy, but as I have told Stöcker himself more than once, he failed to take a political stance in a general sense. In the social sphere, he acted energetically and decisively, but his social policy hung too much in the air and lacked a general political foundation. That is why joining his efforts was not possible for us. The Junker parties, the Agrarians, who made up the core of the Conservatives, had increasingly disowned Stahl’s political testament. Von Bismarck’s political conduct moved almost entirely in the sphere of strengthening Germany’s global significance, and although he openly professed, “We Germans fear God and no one else,” the religious vein running through his politics was still strongly tinged. In the May Laws, he even dared to attack the freedom of the Church. With all the admiration for the mighty results he achieved, he could still not elicit any political affiliation from us Antirevolutionaries. The “Reformists,” who were reviving in Germany, did try to approach me once or twice to join their ranks, but their school tactics, their avoidance of all political involvement, and their sober Calvinism seemed to me to promise too little for an attempt at affiliation. In Barmen and Elberfeld, there certainly would have been favorable prospects; but even there, the conditions under which the action took place were too specifically German, and too little principled and comprehensive in a general sense, for me to engage in a closer understanding without suffering harm myself. The Protestant element in general, and the Reformed in particular, lacked entirely what made the Roman Catholic Center in Germany so strong, in that it did not have the courage to act as an independent party. It was therefore understandable that some more decisive men like Kleist-Retzow and Von Gerlach sought to be incorporated into the Center. But this too failed on its own. And so, alas, it must be admitted that the rich prospect that opened up for the powerful formation of an Antirevolutionary party in Germany after Stahl’s appearance has not been realized. Personally, there are thousands and thousands in Germany who are kindred spirits to us and envy our independent party position; but it has not come to an independent formation, and there is hardly any prospect of it happening in the future. Switzerland offers no hope for the future either. In the French cantons, what is outlined in France is repeated in a bioscopic way. The Italian cantons are too small in population to take an independent position. And in the German cantons, it is the Roman Catholic ones where the most emphasis is placed on the honor of one’s own principles, while in the Protestant ones, German theology often marries French political ideas, and thus here too a revival of Calvinism is excluded. One saw it at Calvin’s centenary. Glorification of a name in which part of Switzerland spoke of ancient glories, but of spiritual kinship with the traditions of the man of Geneva, not a trace in all that was manifested and celebrated. Religion and politics here too almost hermetically sealed off from each other. Not even the faintest indication of political action in Calvin’s footsteps. §38. In Roman Catholic lands. In predominantly Roman Catholic lands, there could naturally be no revival of the Antirevolutionary movement. The Roman Catholic Church, with its hierarchical structure and its centralized authority, does not allow the formation of independent political parties within its fold. The Ultramontane movement, which has spread from Rome, has indeed brought about a certain political mobilization, but this has been entirely in the service of the Roman Catholic Church and its interests. The Antirevolutionary principle, which seeks to maintain the independence of the various spheres of life, is diametrically opposed to this. The Roman Catholic Church, with its claim to sovereignty over all of life, cannot tolerate any independent political movement that would limit its power. And so, in Roman Catholic lands, the Antirevolutionary movement has found no foothold. The political activity of Roman Catholics has been entirely absorbed by the struggle for the interests of their Church. The Antirevolutionary principle, which seeks to maintain the independence of the various spheres of life, has found no echo there.
[67]

THE ANTIREVOLUTIONARIES IN GERMANY. - IN ROMAN CATHOLIC LANDS. 55 bemoeiïng, en hun sober Calvinistische scheen mij bij proefneming tot aansluiting toch te weinig te beloven. In Barmen en Elberfeld ware zeker gunstige uitzicht geweest; maar toch waren de verhoudingen waaronder zelfs in het Rijnland de actie zich voordeed, te speciaal Duitsch, en te weinig principieel en alomvattend in generalen zin, om mij, zonder zelf schade te lijden, op een naurere verstandhouding in doen te gaan. Het Protestantsche element in ‘t gemeen, en het Reformirt in ‘t bijzonder, miste zoo geheel wat het Roomsche Centrum in Duitschland zoo sterk maakte, doordien het den moed niet had om als zelfstandige partij op te treden. Het was dan ook te verstaan, dat enkele meer besliste mannen als Kleist-Retzow en Von Gerlach, aanzoek deden om bij het Centrum te worden opgenomen. Doch ook dit liep van zelf spaak. En zoo moet, helaas, beleden, dat het rijke uitzicht dat zich na Stahl’s optreden voor het krachtig zich vormen van een Antirevolutionaire partij in Duitschland opende, niet verwezenlijkt is. Persoonlijk zijn er in Duitschland duizenden en nogmaals duizenden, die ons geest-verwant zijn en onze zelfstandige partij-positie benijden; maar tot zelfstandige formatie kwam het dus verre niet, en zelfs van een uitzicht dat het er toe komen zal, kan ter nauwer nood gesproken worden. Zwitserland biedt even min hoop voor de toekomst. In de Fransche kantons herhaalt zich bioscopisc h, wat in Frankrijk zich afteekent. De Italiaansche kantons zijn te klein in bevolking om een eigen positie in te nemen. En in de Duitsche kantons zijn het de Roomsche, waarin nog het meest op ‘t eeren van eigen beginselen wordt aangedrongen, terwijl in de Protestantsche de Duitsche theologie zich veelal huwtaan de Fransche staatkundige denkbeelden, en aldus ook hier een weer op-leven van het Calvinisme uitsluit. Men zag het op Calvijn’s eeuwfeest. Verheerlijking van een naam waarin voor een deel van Zwitserland al oude glories spraken, maar van geestelijke verwantschap aan de tradities van den man van Genève zelfs geen sprake in al wat gemanifesteerd en geëerd werd. Religie en politiek ook hier bijkans hermetisch van elkander afgesloten. Van politieke actie in Calvijn’s spoor geen flauwe aanduiding zelfs.

§38. In Roomsche landen. In overwegend Roomsche landen kon natuurlijk van een weer op-bloeien

Analytical Summary: In Roomsche landen. In overwegend Roomsche landen kon natuurlijk van een weer op-bloeien
[68]

ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY AS PART OF THE PEOPLE. There could be no talk of Calvinism as an action in the sense of the German Center, but it would certainly have been very conceivable. What the Center aims at is not merely a specific German or exclusively Papal movement, but very certainly also a descent from this special to the general Christian principles, which, as with us, could lead to the cooperation of Protestants and Roman Catholics. However, one finds no indication of this, neither in Italy nor in Spain and Portugal, nor even in Belgium. In Italy, the old Waldensians, as well as the few Protestants in Spain and Portugal, swear by the Radicals, while for the Roman Catholics the decision hangs on the restoration of the ecclesiastical state to the Pope. And when one examines the state of affairs in Belgium, then the remarkable phenomenon certainly presents itself here, that the small circle of Protestants does not unanimously join the Radicalism, but even partly goes along with the Clericals; but it stands firm that the Clericals, who have now been in power for nearly thirty years, have not gone over to a principled anti-revolutionary statesmanship. As is well known, the constitution that Belgium gave itself in 1831 still remains the model for the statesmen of Liberalism. Even if it is now that the Clericals have been in power for three decades, the attempt to transform this radical-revolutionary state law according to the requirements of a higher principle has never even been thought of. In my many conversations with Belgian clericals, it was not even possible to discover that they were familiar with the writings of the German Center. They hardly even recognized the name Groen van Prinsterer, and his writings were unknown to them. For Belgium, therefore, there could be no talk of a self-conscious anti-revolutionary stance on the principles of state law. Practically, one often moved, as in the school question, on the desired line, but one did not penetrate to the fundamentals of Christian state construction. In Belgium, they really never understood what we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, above the Moerdijk were aiming for.

§39. Russia.

Analytical Summary: Russia.

In the first half of the previous century, there were in the Netherlands quite strong sympathies for the Czar’s empire. The Cossacks came to our aid at our first liberation. Grand Duchess Anna Paulowna won the sympathy of many. Interesting memories still lived on of Czar Peter. Our Dutch colony in Petrograd still maintained a lively connection with our northern provinces. The Russian securities were always considered one of the best funds here, and they also made a great impression at that time. It was known that the principles of the French Revolution hardly encountered any fiercer resistance anywhere than in the Czar’s empire. Even ecclesiastically, it aroused sympathy that the Greek Orthodox Church, even though it was Caesaro-papistic, could still offer resistance to Papal supremacy. One was not detached from Russia in our circles. In this relationship, however, a very noticeable change has now gradually occurred. It is true that the Czar’s initiative for the Peace Conference attracted attention for a moment, but the necessary gave way too quickly, and thus saw his great plans completely thwarted. Furthermore, the way in which Finland was treated and time and again against the freedom-thirsting elements was acted upon, hindered many sympathies, and however much the prematurely deceased Mr. De Martens drew the connection with the Netherlands again, it has not yet become between the Netherlands and Russia what it was in the thirties of the previous century.

[69]

RUSSIA. - THE UNITED STATES. The strongest sympathies of our Anti-Revolutionary Party still go out to the United States of North America. For there the Calvinism has reached its full development, the Common Grace has come into full honor, state and church have been disentangled, everything that is church flourishes precisely through complete independence, combined with complete financial responsibility, and in no country are the sympathies of the Christian, ecclesiastical and non-ecclesiastical life so honored and warm on both sides as precisely in the Union. There is no need to go into further explanation of this, since in the First Part all the necessary light has already been shed on this. It is only in the nature of the matter that in the Union there could be no talk of the founding of a separate anti-revolutionary party. The Republicans, after all, have long since taken over the task of defending the Christian foundations of the state, and the Democrats, who are now in power, also do not show any tendency to abandon these foundations. The Anti-Revolutionaries therefore have no reason to form a separate party in the United States, but can fully join the existing parties, as long as they remain true to their Christian-historical foundations.

[70]

The Antirevolutionary Party as a People’s Party. Originally, they gave precisely what the Antirevolutionary intends. And while we openly acknowledge that the Republicans of today have in many ways strayed from their original spiritual starting point towards the economically enriching, yet we still cannot but reckon ourselves as historically akin to the Republicans. Although this brief overview of the situation abroad shows many significant changes since 1878 when Our Program saw the light, the fact remains that even now, only in the Netherlands is there an organized Protestant Antirevolutionary Party against the principles of Unbelief and Revolution. Our opposition to Unbelief and Revolution stands in many ways on par with what emanates from the Roman Catholic side against the spirit of the age. And many in all lands are the believers in the Gospel who, even in the political realm, are one with us. What is unique to us is this: that we, thanks to our past and the Calvinistic origin of our national existence, see ourselves as a solitary Protestant political party, which neither petrifies the people in conservatism, nor wanders off into the false democracy of 1789.

§41. Radicalism in France. With France, our relationship could not be other than strained. Not because the Monarchy gave way to the Republic. We ourselves have been a Commonwealth, and our country has been able to fulfill the most glorious role in Europe’s history precisely in that Commonwealth form of government. The form of government decides nothing here in the least. It is precisely with the United States, who also chose the republican form of government, that we feel the closest political kinship. A Calvinist can never forget that in Geneva there was no talk of a Monarchy, and that Calvin himself lived in a Commonwealth, so to speak. What makes reconciliation with the French form of government unthinkable for us is solely this, that France still moves along the straight line for which Rousseau first indicated the two fixed points, and that line is precisely the line of Unbelief and Revolution. It is perfectly true that first Napoleon, and after him both the returned Bourbons and what came after them, have in many ways deviated from that line. But the fact remains that the basic direction of France’s political development has been and still is the direction of Unbelief and Revolution.

Analytical Summary: Radicalism in France. With France, our relationship could not be other than strained. Not because the Monarchy gave way to the Republic. We ourselves have been a Commonwealth, and our country has been able to fulfill the most glorious role in Europe’s history precisely in that Commonwealth form of government. The form of government decides nothing here in the least. It is precisely with the United States, who also chose the republican form of government, that we feel the closest political kinship. A Calvinist can never forget that in Geneva there was no talk of a Monarchy, and that Calvin himself lived in a Commonwealth, so to speak. What makes reconciliation with the French form of government unthinkable for us is solely this, that France still moves along the straight line for which Rousseau first indicated the two fixed points, and that line is precisely the line of Unbelief and Revolution. It is perfectly true that first Napoleon, and after him both the returned Bourbons and what came after them, have in many ways deviated from that line. But the fact remains that the basic direction of France’s political development has been and still is the direction of Unbelief and Revolution.
[71]

THE RADICALISM IN FRANCE.59 The House of Orleans, shuddering at Danton and Robespierre, yet this applied to their excesses. Both Napoleon and the Bourbons and the Orleanists sought to temper, to moderate, to be on their guard against the overwhelming power of the plebs; but what they neglected was to establish a positive starting point, in opposition to the principle that in 1789 and 1795 gained the upper hand. Napoleon got into less trouble in the realm of principles, because he gave the popular spirit a restless distraction in his wars. The Bourbons were much more preoccupied with reaction, but concentrated that reaction in the honor of their House and in erecting a fence around that House, so that the popular power could not penetrate; but of building up a State theory against the revolutionary theory, and that with their own foundation, there was no question. Hence the Bourbons already in 1830 collided with the influence of the people, and had to give way to the more accommodating Orleanists. These, however, led France even further astray. They wanted to be masters in the economic field. They built on gold, instead of on principle. Thus they gained the well-to-do middle class and the rich capitalists on their side, but alienated the believers and the democratic groups from themselves. They did not pursue a dynastic policy, like the Bourbons, but a policy that only benefited the material side of life. Already after a rule of only eighteen years, they too succumbed. And when now the republican form of government returned, it proved to be so weak in its joints, that Napoleon III could already after four years call back the Empire. Only when this Empire was overthrown in 1870, did the revolutionary republic under Gambetta return, and this is the one that, after first having moderated its steps under Thiers and Mac Mahon, soon let the reins slip more loosely, and increasingly came out in a more radical course. Not without seriousness has the Conservative element for a time sought to dispute this Radicalism the field; but since this Conservatism also remained attached to the principles of the Revolution, it could not but be, that with each new election for Parliament a tithe of Conservatism went over, joining the Radicalism, and soon leading this to an undisputed triumph; and since then this Radicalism in France has had nothing more to fear. It broke with the Churches, it abolished all stipends...

[72]

THE Antirevolutionary PARTY AS A POPULAR MOVEMENT. The clergy were not taught, and even arbitrarily disposed of church buildings, suppressed the Christian school, deprived the Free University of all authority, and, after the complete rejection of Clericalism, elevated the truly revolutionary element to that long-delayed triumph. Da Costa’s poetic judgment of Paris as a second Babylon proved, as regards the principle prevailing there, to be less and less open to contradiction. What now came was not the reckless wildness of Danton, nor the fantasy of Robespierre; it was more closely knit together, it was more practically worked out; but as regards Unbelief and Revolution, to adhere to Groen’s maxim, it is undoubtedly the spirit of 1789 that still maintains spiritual leadership in France, yes, even attained undisputed dominance. Even Ribot took a seat in Poincaré’s cabinet together with Viviani, yes, under Viviani.

§42. Disappointment. The disappointment brought upon us by France was thus complete. As followers of Calvin, nothing could have been more congenial and dear to us than to incorporate, in tempering the one-sided Germanic element in our national character, the French variation as well. Already under the Burgundians, the first germination of this had manifested itself, and even in our language the admixture of a French element is unmistakable. They are not all Gallicisms that one wants to banish from our language as such. Words like: pleasure, umbrella, province, fruit and the like have, by introducing variations in meaning, enriched our language. The affinity between the Swiss-German and our Dutch also points to the fact that, in contrast to High German, Low Dutch prefers to maintain itself through this French admixture. Through Calvinism, had it been able to gain a foothold in France, this intrusion of a Romance variation into our people would likely have truly enriched us. Yet it was not to be. The Bartholomew’s Night has given us the Huguenots, which of itself brought a breach with France.

Analytical Summary: Disappointment. The disappointment brought upon us by France was thus complete. As followers of Calvin, nothing could have been more congenial and dear to us than to incorporate, in tempering the one-sided Germanic element in our national character, the French variation as well. Already under the Burgundians, the first germination of this had manifested itself, and even in our language the admixture of a French element is unmistakable. They are not all Gallicisms that one wants to banish from our language as such. Words like: pleasure, umbrella, province, fruit and the like have, by introducing variations in meaning, enriched our language. The affinity between the Swiss-German and our Dutch also points to the fact that, in contrast to High German, Low Dutch prefers to maintain itself through this French admixture. Through Calvinism, had it been able to gain a foothold in France, this intrusion of a Romance variation into our people would likely have truly enriched us. Yet it was not to be. The Bartholomew’s Night has given us the Huguenots, which of itself brought a breach with France.
[73]

DISAPPOINTMENT. - THE QUESTION OF SOVEREIGNTY. When, at the end of the 18th century, a more desirable understanding could have arisen, since the lust for conquest of Louis XIV and XV had come to a standstill, the French Revolution has even widened the chasm between our Calvinism and French politics. With all appreciation for the excellent forces that still continue to attract us in France in the non-political sphere, and with all high esteem for many personal gifts and talents, an Antirevolutionary here at home can still not stand otherwise than principally opposed to the tendencies in French political spirit. The fundamental contrast between what we Calvinists intend and what is leading in French political thought has not been blunted since 1795, but rather sharpened even more. Calvinism and the French Radicalism that still triumphs in the Seine city are two. Chapter II.

THE SOVEREIGNTY OF ORANGE.

§1. The Question of Sovereignty.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.

In Article 2 of Our Program, the Antirevolutionary Party confesses twofold. Firstly, that the sovereignty over all peoples and all States rests in God; and secondly, that the highest authority derived from this sovereignty resides in the House of Orange in the Netherlands. The first part has been sufficiently dealt with in Part I; therefore, here only a brief word about the lodging of the derived sovereignty in Orange. Since outside of God Himself absolutely no one can be conceived who could dispose of sovereignty, it could only be granted by the Almighty. This could take place immediately or mediately.

[74]

The sovereignty that Adam possessed over Paradise was conferred upon him directly. Without any intermediary, it was directly declared to Adam: “Fill the earth and subdue it, and have dominion over all that is in it.” To a certain extent, this can be placed on the same level as the calling of Moses at the Burning Bush, and in a certain sense also the calling of David, even though Samuel was the intermediary conveying God’s decree. The mandate of the Crown in the Kingdom of God to the Christ also took place, according to Psalm II:6, directly. In the course of history, on the other hand, and generally speaking, the mandate did not come about except indirectly. Providentially, a person, a house, a college, or even the people themselves, can actually be in possession of the supreme authority, without there having been any direct intervention from God’s side. There may have been a conquest of the supreme authority through some act of violence. The supreme authority may have been inherited or transferred. It may also have been transferred by the people, and thus seemingly granted by the people. But in whatever way human arrangement or human action may have intervened, the source from which the supreme authority flows is and remains always God Himself, and man here does nothing other than serve instrumentally. The sealed document by which a King delegates the supreme authority over an outgoing naval fleet to one of his admirals can, as a rule, be drawn up by an official servant and handed over to the admiral by a subordinate, so that the King merely signs; but the fact remains that this official and this messenger are merely performing instrumental service, and that it is exclusively from the King’s will and decision that the power and authority which is the King’s is, within the designated sphere, transferred to this admiral. Now the course of events here may have been strictly regulated and formally interconnected, so that the mandate and transfer of the supreme authority took place without the slightest disturbance; but it may also have been that irregularities occurred here.

§2. Transition to Orange. And this latter was evidently the case when in 1813 the then Prince of Orange appeared as Sovereign Prince, or, according to the expression...

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[75]

THE TRANSITION PERIOD.63 After the Constitution of 1815, the Crown placed itself at the head. A brief period preceded 1813, during which the supreme authority over these lands rested with the Emperor of France. Before Napoleon acquired this supreme authority, it had been exercised for a certain number of years by King Louis Napoleon. This exercise of supreme authority was preceded by the revolutionary period, in conformity with the Constitution of 1789. And after the one who had taken up the supreme authority in 1795, we come rightly to the provincial States, who were jealous of their sovereignty, flanked by the General States, and supported by the hereditary Stadtholdership, which since 1747 had been occupied by William IV, Prince of Orange, and his successor Prince William V. Now, when on 17 November 1813 the imperial authority of Napoleon sank, there was in itself neither a person nor any college that could be pointed out as now having the sovereignty to offer and transfer it to the Prince of Orange. Formally, the historical line was broken. But although the regulation of the supreme authority had thus become unready, there was not the slightest doubt about the real situation. There worked in the people, as a result of what had happened since 1789 and 1795, a tendency towards submission, a need for a vigorous exercise of supreme authority throughout the land, and for the historical consciousness of almost everyone, the Prince of Orange was the person to whom one desired to submit. Alongside this stood the Prince, who hastened back from England and himself felt the same calling not to leave the embarrassed country helpless. And in Hogendorp, Van der Duyn van Limburg Stirum, the heroic element emerged, where their own movements under an irresistible impulse took the initiative and gave the impetus to make the embarrassed people find in Orange what everyone felt had to come. The ingenuity with which one later unraveled the threads from a legal point of view, which had almost woven into an enchantment what had been so cruelly torn apart over the past 15 years, may have been legally meritorious, but could not suffice as proof. In the hereditary Stadtholdership there was formally no sovereign authority. Just as the landowner entrusts the care of one of his estates to the farmer who lives on the land, with the proviso that after him his son and then his grandson will cultivate the same estate, there is in the least no transfer of ownership.

[76]

64. The Sovereignty of Orange. When the revolt broke out in 1572, the supreme authority over these lands rested with the King of Spain, and his stadholders had no other calling than to exercise this supreme authority in his name, under responsibility to their principal. Even in the “Wilhelmus” it is confessed: “The King of Spain I have always honored.” The sense of duty to honor the Prince against Religion, and thus against God, has then provoked the revolting and the abjuration of Philip. And from that hour the unhappy struggle has arisen between the stubborn oversight of the States-Provincial over their own sovereignty and the popular impulse that, through the elevation of the States-General, pressed towards national unity, and herein found its inspired symbol in Orange.

§3. The States and Orange. Yet this did not take away that formally the States-Provincial maintained themselves in their relative independence, and that of a formal abdication of their sovereignty even to the hereditary stadholder there has never been any question. So it remained a struggle between two currents. The formally dead-ending current continued to ascribe the sovereignty to the coterie of the Regents, the national life-current went against this. At the call of the Sans-Culottes one lost oneself in the contrat social. But in order, when the bitter fruit thereof began to taste too bitter, to throw off the French yoke and the French garb, and now, under Orange, to face a better future, as one hoped. The outcome was, therefore, that all high authority slipped away from the former power-holders, and that this, not by formal deduction, but by the very facts of history, passed over to Orange, and was accepted by Orange. Hence every laboriously constructed proof for the transition of the supreme authority to Orange remains weak, but on the other hand, for him who understands the speech of God in history, not only the sovereignty of the House of Orange, but also the “by the grace of God” that enhances its honor, no longer allows any contradiction in the national popular consciousness. Naturally, a formula also had to be found then for the transition of the form of government, and this has been designed in the formula, first in 1814, that “the sovereignty of the United Netherlands is and remains entrusted,” and in 1815, that “the Crown of the Netherlands” is and remains entrusted; and further by going back to 1747 to establish who would count as the Progenitor of the royal family; but the compelling force for the legal argument lay just as much in the one as in the other. The Great Assembly of Notables could no more in its own authority give the bond with the past. Whatever ingenuity has been applied to provide this remarkable change in the state of affairs with a legally stamped birth certificate, it is not thereby that the then Prince of Orange won the hearts of the people. §4. Whence the enthusiasm? The enthusiasm with which the people greeted his appearance, he owed to the memories and to the after-feeling of the misery and distress that our people, in all its ranks and classes, had suffered under the French yoke, and to the memory, so sharply contrasting with this, of the glory that in the 16th century had been bestowed on the Netherlands by the great Princes of Orange. The contrast between the then exceptional greatness of our country, when the small Netherlands had become for more than half a century the leader of the world market and the ruler of the seas, and the smallness to which we had sunk in 1813, worked enchantingly on the popular spirit. Bilderdijk in his “Holland grows again, Holland blooms again, Holland’s name is restored” - expresses with perfect accuracy what was going on in the hearts at the time, especially when soon after the Flemish land came under the Dutch crown and the political splendor of the 17th century seemed to revive. This has then been turned against. The expectation that the Netherlands would see the glory of its past renewed could, given the distorted power relations in the European state system, not but end in disappointment, and so, of itself, after the failure of Belgium’s annexation, led to the resentment against Orange that crept into many hearts in 1848. But the fact remained that in 1813 the opposition of the Regents, which even then still crept on, out of sheer shame fell silent, and that, on the contrary, the sympathetic tone of the State...

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[77]

... prevailed.

[78]

66 THE SOVEREIGNTY OF ORANGE. The part of the people who, as if with one breath, welcomed Orange upon his return to the fatherland and on the coast of Scheveningen.

§5. The Kingship of David. It was also here as it was in the days of David. Even then, between the pretender-Kings, such a spirit of solidarity was born among the people, not least through David’s heroic deeds, that in the end first only the men of Judah and then all the tribes of Israel proclaimed David as King and crowned him. In 2 Samuel 5:1–3, it is also so clearly expressed that the tribes of Israel came to David at Hebron, and the elders of Israel made a covenant with him that he should be king over them. And these were the men who anointed David as King. First the anointing by Samuel, and now at Hebron the anointing by the elders of Israel. The King felt that he was called by God, and all the people having entered into covenant with him, felt that David was given to them by God as King. The only indefiniteness lay in the fact that the expression of the people’s desire and the people’s willingness had a somewhat floating character. What in 2 Samuel 2:4 are called the “men of Judah” were not enfranchised by virtue of a legally introduced general right of suffrage, but presumably the “elders” of Judah, just as in 2 Sam. 5:3 express mention is made of the “elders” of Israel. Up to a certain height, at any rate, the organic tribal bond of that time still stood, and thus in each tribe, in each clan, and in each family, the men were designated who had the right to act representatively.

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[79]

THE KINGSHIP OF DAVID. - JUST SO HERE. - THE NOTABLES. 67

§6. Just so here.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.

Entirely the same was seen in 1813-’14. Prince William of Orange was aware that, by virtue of the past, no one other than he, with his House, could be King, that he was called to that Kingly dignity. There could be no talk of a rival, of a co-pretender. Nor was any mentioned. But this did not mean that he did not have to be accepted by the people. And since at that time there was no legal representation of the people that could act in the name of the people, it was quite natural that here it depended on a freely emerging initiative, and this then came from the three eminent men who, without being authorized or appointed to do so, boldly took the initiative, in the name of the whole people, to act as political heralds. It was with it as with the speaking at a grand demonstration, if there is a prominent jubilee. Then everyone’s feeling demands that the word be spoken in the name of all. And if there is then one who presents himself as the spokesman of everyone’s feelings, takes the word and loudly expresses what he knows is in everyone’s heart, then the applause follows of itself and seals what he said. Whoever then takes the word is not appointed to do so by legal vote, but felt instinctively that the word was expected of him. This is precisely what also took place here in a political sense. The old organization was no longer there, the new one was still lacking. Yet there was a current through the heart of the people that called for action and desired Orange. Whoever then stepped forward did not do too much. It could also have been three others. But these three could, on the basis of their own initiative, accomplish what was to be done, and they did.

§7. The Notables. Nor was it otherwise with the notables who solemnly sealed what had been done in the assembly. Such a representative college was necessary. For the composition of this college, the most obvious form was chosen. From these, good or bad, emerged, as it were, the most suitable representatives.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[80]

68 THE SOVEREIGNTY OF ORANGE. The opposing factions were not breathing the national spirit, but rather the dying spirit of a clique. It mattered little, therefore, how the assembly of notables was constituted. However composed, this assembly had the people behind it. It did not depend on the letter of their mandate, but on the fact that they were animated by the same spirit that inspired the entire people, save for that small clique. The rich and glorious past of Orange cast its spell, now that the sad outcome of what the heroes of 1795 had undertaken had tormented the national heart. And therefore it mattered little how everything was formally arranged. A divine ordinance was felt. That divine ordinance spoke in the popular consciousness, and it spoke in the Prince of Orange. And the fact that the sovereign Prince would soon write the title of King, that he, whatever he was, was by the grace of God, lived at the time as an unshakable conviction in all political awareness. What Tollens sang in his “Who has Netherland’s Blood” about the “Deity on the heavenly throne” did not convey the warmth of the “Wilhelmus van Nassauwen.” In the “Who has Netherland’s Blood” the poetry is lacking, and this lack of poetry Tollens has tried to make up for by high-flown, resounding terms, like those that still today hinder everyone in that boastful: “Free from foreign stains.” But this does not take away that in March 1813 the Napoleonic screw was wrenched off the tripartite of our national existence, not only the Prince, but also the people in its broader ranks experienced a moment in which the grace of God lifted us out of our misery. Precisely the unhistorical and completely at odds with our entire past character of the revolutionary movement, which was embodied in the Constitution of 1798, and which had led not only to the expulsion of Orange, but even to the driving out of a man like Bilderdijk, for his unwillingness to let go of Orange, made itself so powerfully felt in 1813. What, after all, was what these revolutionaries had achieved, compared to the imperishable glory that still echoed from the 16th and 17th centuries?

§8. The error of the 16th century. So something stirred in the popular consciousness, reproaching the national...

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.
[81]

THE FAULT OF THE 16TH CENTURY. The conscience cried out against the ungrateful injustice done to Orange. In such a historic moment, this sense of guilt naturally arose in the national consciousness, and from this in 1813 came the impulse of the entire nation (with the exception of the Kezen-clubs in the larger cities) to make amends for the guilt of the past and to make up, through warm enthusiasm, for what had been done to Orange at the end of the 18th century. The unforgivable political error committed in the 16th century could not be undone, but at the return of the Prince to Amsterdam, the anxious feeling still washed over what had been neglected at the time by the broad stream of the people. The reader feels what errors, what neglect is referred to here. One should have, when uniting in the Union of Utrecht in 1579 and when renouncing the monarchical authority of the King of Spain in 1581, not thought of a “Stadtholder” anymore, but should have immediately placed Prince William in Philip’s place; whether one wanted to hail him as Count or Duke, or immediately under the title of King. The United Netherlands were formed out of a certain number of small Statelets, which were monarchically governed and had been subject from of old to their Count or Duke. That the monarchical rights which applied in each of these Statelets had gradually accrued to the same House, and that ultimately Charles and Philip, in their own person, had become rulers over all these provinces together, did not in itself change the form of government. That form was and remained monarchical. That in 1568 one rose up against Philip did not mean the replacement of the monarchical form of government by the republican, but drew its inspiration from the resistance to religious tyranny, especially as it manifested itself in the Inquisition at the time. There was therefore not the slightest reason or occasion why one should now have to switch to the republican form of government. The natural course would much rather have been if each of the liberated States had then appointed a new Count or Duke, especially with the proviso that the new Prince would now no longer be at the same time Prince over a completely foreign country, as had previously been the case with the Burgundians and later especially with Charles and Philip. What our country needed at that moment was a national Crown, and that Crown borne by Princes from a House that was indigenous here.

[82]

70 THE SOVEREIGNTY OF ORANGE. It was, and his own blood was mingled with the blood of the nation. The logic of history had thus demanded that William of Orange be placed in the stead of Philip. But this is precisely what they did not want. The States of the various provinces, now that Philip had fallen away, wished at the same time to break the monarchical principle, and henceforth the supreme power would rest with the States themselves. This is what Oldenbarneveldt, this is what later the De Witts always aimed at. Through this misstep, the struggle then broke out between the people in their broader ranks, who had taken up the fight against Philip for the sake of religious freedom, and the Statists, who had almost exclusively been bent on the increase of power. So it could not be otherwise, or the Calvinists remained the champions of the Reformation element and continually cried out for Orange, while on the other hand the Erasmians and Remonstrants chose against Orange and for the States; and hence the root of the political bitterness that has poisoned our political relations since 1581. That Orange himself is not free from blame in this, must not be concealed. The Prince should not have acquiesced in the falsified state of affairs that now entered. This later compelled Maurits to a more decisive intervention. And with Prince Frederick Henry, the relationship had already bent so far that it now came to a provisional comparison between the Prince and the States, of which the Reformed were the victims. Whoever now thinks how entirely different the history of our fatherland in the 17th century would have run, if we had not been plagued with a bastardized stadtholdership, but from the outset had been covered by the Crown of Orange, cannot deplore deeply enough that the unforgivable fault committed in 1581, and cannot but imagine with sorrow in his heart how entirely different the course of our history would have been, if at the moment when the fatal decision fell, one had known how to guard against this misstep. Something that is not said, as if the monarchical form of government were the only conceivable or usable one. Even a republican form of government; the Union of America shows it; can produce a brilliant result. Only—just as the relations stood here at the time, after the abdication of Philip, it was self-evident that the Statist party had to come more and more to stand over against the Party of Orange and of the Reformation, and from this irreconcilable twofold struggle all the misery that first broke out in 1795 and later in 1848 was born.

[83]

71 THE DISILLUSIONMENT OF 1813.

§9. The disillusionment of 1813. Also in 1813, alas, but all too soon a disquieting disillusionment has followed. In order to prevent the old feud from reviving, our first King has tried to place himself above the parties, which of course had to result in the principles of 1789, with only half strength, it is true, but still working. The fatal Royal Decree of January 1815 regulating the Churches completely disorganized the then Reformed Church, and was the harbinger of the persecution to which the Secessionists were soon subjected. The manner of proceeding in the Belgian provinces could have no other consequence than that these provinces were only waiting for the first best revolutionary reaction in Europe, to tear themselves loose from us again with French help. The battles of Hasselt and Leuven were a military success, but brought us the least national glory. The King’s second marriage brought estrangement. And so it was far from natural that we soon came to stand before a disordered condition, which, alas, contrasted all too darkly with the enthusiasm of 1813. Whoever, like the writer of this, did not experience the evil days of 1848, can also now form no conception of the misrelations between Crown and People that had then entered. The revision of the Constitution of 1848 was the one that, with all the defects that clung to it, still brought rescue from the then prevailing malaise. And now that we have entered the twentieth century, we may rejoice in the rule of the latest scion of the House of Orange, it cannot be thanked warmly enough that the administration of our revered Queen has been so surprisingly happy, and that it was indeed as if in 1913 the inspired echo was heard of the enthusiasm with which in 1815 the first King had been cheered. It may now be confessed without any appearance of exaggeration that, with few exceptions, those who will always remain under every monarchical rule, the Netherlands and Orange are again one in soul.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[84]

72 THE SOVEREIGNTY OF ORANGE.

§10. Our “Netherlands and Orange.”

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.

The Anti-Revolutionaries also chose, from the very beginning of their political appearance, this “Netherlands and Orange” as the motto for their Electoral Associations. Not that they have ever or at any time been able to enjoy any particular grace from the Bearer of the Crown or from his Court. We have already pointed out the fatal blow that the Royal Decree of January 1815, concerning the Churches, and later the fierce action against the men of 1834, brought us. Groen van Prinsterer’s actual appointment as Court Archivist has just as little led to his advice in State affairs being of decisive influence on the three Kings under whom he participated in politics, or even on one of them. At the Public Universities, our men have been shunned throughout the first three-quarters of a century. In offices and positions, they were granted but a sparse place, except in the rarest of exceptions. Beets’ naming of the “Night School” also applied here. Only in 1853 and 1878 did one get the impression that a change was coming, and since then it may gratefully be acknowledged that the three Cabinets of Mackay, Kuyper and Heemskerk could always rejoice in the impartial constitutional cooperation from higher quarters.
