One may therefore say that we see more and more of the setbacks that occurred repeatedly in the past disappearing, and that we are now almost on a par with the other political parties in the Palace. We do not ask for more, of course. Never any privilege, but also never any exclusion. But however this may be, as Anti-Revolutionaries we remain, by virtue of our bond with the past, to keep the enthusiasm for the House of Orange alive in all our circles, in all our schools. “Netherlands and Orange” remains our watchword. And now do not say that such a bold speaking about the House of Orange, since it bears the Crown, is not to be reconciled with our attachment to Orange. The opposite is true. Even our Calvinistic Fathers have always understood their calling to see that not in the crawling of the courtier, but only in manly courage combined with loyalty, the historical love for a dynastic house proves itself as a power in the life of the people. Crawling and flattering has never been the nature of the Calvinists, and precisely for this reason their love for Orange contains an unconditionally powerful, mystical force.

[85]

OUR “NETHERLANDS AND ORANGE” - INSTINCTS AND INTUITION. 73

§11. Instincts and Intuition.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.

There also works in peoples and dynasties, who under God’s high ordinance, as we may express it with a Scriptural image, are married, a historical instinct and an intuition. The historical instinct makes one feel and honor the bond that was not woven by human choice, nor by human regulation, but by the entire concurrence of circumstances, fiber by fiber, and that clings as of itself around peoples and dynasties. And where historically such an irresistible instinct to belong together draws, as it were, sucks, this lays such a claim on the emotional life that an intuitive conviction is born of itself, which, without much argumentation, seeks connection of itself, and that this connection can only be realized in a covenant. The idea of the covenant is from the beginning sanctified in Holy Scripture by application to the relationship between people and prince from the marriage thought, and much disturbance in political life would have been prevented if this Scriptural thought had also found more acceptance among the teachers of constitutional law. Although it must be acknowledged that in very many cases even violence has more than mutual desire bound the dynasty with the people, or also where violence was absent, genealogical kinship decided the fate of the people, the result is nevertheless in the end almost always that people and prince feel they must belong together, and therefore desire nothing else than to continue the established understanding. Even where originally no fiber bound people and prince together, and the resistance and unwillingness of all sides disputed the prince’s power, the history has not seldom unrolled its captivating story of an ever more intimate connection, which was the fruit of the intuitive awareness that any change would be a deterioration and that only mutual affection could radiate the warmth of life. There have been many dynasties that failed to cultivate this instinctive and intuitive feeling of sympathy, but this often led to the sad outcome that the bond, which had never really become firm, was torn apart by the first storm.

[86]

74 THE SOVEREIGNTY OF ORANGE. Finally, it was either completely severed or torn apart. That is precisely why such emphasis must be placed on the fact that the bond that binds the Netherlands to Orange belongs to the most instinctive in history and the most intuitive in thought. Here, indeed, a covenant had been established. Orange was not a dynasty that had imposed itself, but one that had itself aroused the people’s desire to be one with the House of Orange. Naturally, the ultimate decision as to whether the people would submit themselves in matrimonial loyalty to the dynasty of Orange lay with the people, but from Orange as the pretender, enticements and attractions had gone forth, and that to such a strong degree that we in the Netherlands can hardly think of any other dynasty than that of the House of Orange.

§12. Formal Regulation. Naturally, this national marriage, if I may use this expression, also had its formal aspect; but whatever has been attempted to date the rise of the monarchy back before 1813, nothing can set aside the fact that it was only in 1813 that the sovereignty became hereditary in the House of Orange. Yet this does not prevent us from appreciating the attempts that have led to an ante-dating of the transition; something that came out most strongly by having William IV presented as the true progenitor of the dynasty. This did not do anything to the people; but not without reason, it was foreseen from the outset that conflicts of succession between the members of the House of Orange could arise; and that, if this illustrious House were to depart early or late, foreign pretenders could interfere in our national affairs, and thus disturb the rest and peace of our domestic economy. Elsewhere, history had taught clearly enough how, in such dynastic conflicts, division can arise within the people itself, so that one part of the people sets itself against one pretender, and another part against another; and it was precisely for this reason that an attempt was made to prevent such a catastrophe by a sharp formulation of the succession in the Constitution. How difficult this often was, is evident when one compares the arrangements made in 1813, in 1848 and in 1887, and takes note of the fact that even now further amendments seem to be in order, if, after the conclusion of the present war, a more radical revision of the Constitution should once again be undertaken. One should therefore not make the mistake of seeing only the quarrelsomeness in the efforts expended here. The statesmen who have to watch over the future would be failing in their high duty if they were to carelessly leave even a single letter in our Constitution unattended or unwritten. For indeed, it can depend on a single omission whose cock will crow when the time comes.

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[87]

75 FORMAL REGULATION - THE DARK SHADOW.

§13. The Dark Shadow. Also in another respect, the formal regulation is of such serious significance, especially now that the long-cherished hope that the House of Orange would still be granted length of days in the Netherlands even after the death of the Prince of Orange in Paris and of Prince Alexander in The Hague, alas, has not been fulfilled. To the second wife of our late King no son was born; and however rich the possession the King has made of us, yet Princess Juliana, if she, though after long days, may have the crown pressed upon her head, will no longer belong to the House of Orange, but to the House of Mecklenburg, to perhaps transfer the crown once more to a House as yet unknown to us. Euphemism does not help here. One can also try here what has been dared with the Habsburgs in Vienna; but since it is not the title, but the blood that indicates membership of the family, any attempt of this kind could only result in a mere play on words. And even this play would be hampered by Article 29 of the Constitution, which states that only the eldest of the King’s sons, or further male descendants, who are the presumptive heirs to the Crown, shall henceforth bear the title of Prince of Orange. This provision connects with Art. 17 of the Constitution of 1814, where it was stated: “As hereditary prince, he (the eldest son of the sovereign prince) is given the title of Royal Highness.” The other Princes and Princesses then bore the title of Serene Highness. But as early as 1815, this was replaced by what is now

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[88]

76 THE SOVEREIGNTY OF ORANGE. In Art. 29 it stands. The continuous thought was, therefore, that a very clear distinction is made between the eldest son and the eldest daughter of the bearer of the Crown. If it is a princess, then she does not receive the title of Prince of Orange. Even Princess Juliana, to this day, does not bear the title of Princess of Orange; which is certainly not without significance. But even if it were otherwise, here there would never be a question of anything other than a mere title; and the continuation or transition of the lineage is regulated not by a title, but only by the blood.

§14. Precision of regulation. We are therefore, though still far away, yet ultimately threatened by the tragic painful outcome, that there comes a break in the history; that the glorious House of Orange dies out in the blood; and that the Netherlands thereby stands to lose what could never be compensated for us by any other House, however excellent. In connection with this, we stood before a highly important choice. For in connection with this extinction, one can do one of two things: one can either take the position that, after the fall of the House of Orange, the people’s covenant right should be restored, so that it could itself, through its constitutional organs, make the choice of a new dynasty; or one can weave the question of how, after the extinction of the House of Orange, to provide for the succession, into the dynastic inheritance. The former will only come into play if, according to Article 21, “upon the death of the King, there is no competent successor according to the Constitution.” Then, says the article, this is directly appointed by the States-General, summoned in a double number for this purpose. On the other hand, the second course of action remains, so that a completely foreign prince can see the crown come to him here in this country. In itself, there is nothing unsatisfactory about this. It even makes more or less the impression that the Crown of the Netherlands is in particular possession of one or another family, which, even at its extinction, would still have a decisive say in the course that this matter would take. The regression of the right of appointment to the people would therefore laugh at the extinction of the direct blood relationship in the male line.

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”
[89]

PRECISION OF REGULATION. - DANGERS. 77 Yet the reasons that advise against this are not devoid of weight. Now that in the new state life there are constantly fundamentally different political parties in the States-General opposed to each other, the election of a Prince by such a States-General in double number would of course give rise to a tremendous struggle at the ballot box. It could hardly be otherwise, or each party would already have chosen a candidate in advance who seems to be devoted to its principles. It would therefore most likely become a struggle at the ballot box between Prince A, B or C. Something in which, moreover, it would only too easily be imagined that foreign Powers, in the interest of their own princely house or political position, would try to intervene in the election; not directly, of course, but then sideways; and the election of the States-General would thereby be in danger of not being a national affair. Nor should we forget that in the heyday of our history the Netherlands was a Commonwealth, and that there are still elements among us who have little feeling for the monarchical principle. This historical element could also embitter the struggle, all the more so as the Socialists do not conceal that the republican form of government remains the ideal for them, and then of course not in remembrance of the Commonwealth of the Union of Utrecht, but of what created the new state here in 1795. Perhaps completeness itself would even require adding that a completely open choice might well lead to a war conflict and endanger our independence. Many therefore said that in the Constitution, as long as possible, the dynastic ramifications should be held on to, and that, as long as there is still a reigning Head, he should receive the power to make proposals for the succession, and during his lifetime should not see an unwanted succession forced upon him. For the orderly progress of affairs, it is and remains desirable that the new direct popular choice be preceded by the States-General.

[90]

78 THE SOVEREIGNTY OF ORANGE. It would be unwise to shift the general so far as it can be done only slightly. It may indeed be objected that this would lead to the succession of a Crown bearer who could never win the sympathy of the people, but against this it must be said that not only can the people make a very sad mistake in a new choice, but moreover the chosen Prince can never guarantee for his dynasty. The descendants of a less desired successor may also disappoint, as those of a self-chosen sovereign. What Orange has been for us, and thank God, still is, can never be compensated; and as the moment approaches when we have a dynastic dark future to face, the hope of the people then rests in that same God who once gave us Orange and has enriched our history for four centuries. Whether it would not be desirable to also grant the Crown Princess the title of Princess of Orange, could be considered at the next general constitutional revision. If it were more than a title, serious objections could certainly arise against it. Now, however, since it is expressly declared in the Constitution itself to be nothing more than a title, it remains an open question why only the son, but not the daughter of the bearer of the Crown, will bear this title. The kinship does not see this title in the least. This speaks in what the Bearer or the Bearer of the Crown testifies in his name and designation of origin to present himself as Prince or Princess of Orange-Nassau. This, however, is something quite different. The King of Prussia, as is well known, also calls himself Prince of Orange-Nassau in his surname. Here, on the other hand, there is nothing but a title. Suppose, for example, that a male scion of our princely house were now born, he would be a Duke of Mecklenburg as far as his gender is concerned, but by virtue of Article 34 he would still be a Prince of Orange in title. This honor could now also be granted to a female member of the House. Yes, even if a completely foreign dynasty were to rule here at the end of the 20th century, this titulature would still continue by virtue of the Constitution. This is now to be determined at the next general constitutional revision. But there is more that needs to be considered, at least in the Constitution. Never too sharply can it be watched that the articles on succession are examined in every way with a magnifying glass and weighed as on a golden scale.

[91]

DANGERS. - NEVER THOUGHT OF A CONSTITUTION BEFORE. 79 In the explanation of the Anti-Revolutionary Program, on the other hand, there is no need to go further into this. For in this further question, it is legal issues without a party character, in which jurists from different parties can be equally inclined. Not without reason has it been pointed out several times during the recent war that our Empire could be threatened from the side of Pan-Germanism; and there is no doubt that the advocates of this unjust system would not fail, if a difference of opinion on the succession ever arose, to oppose us at the slightest flaw or mistake in the chapter of our Constitution that deals with the succession, even if it were only the narrowest crack left open to slip through. The acumen of the gentlemen jurists who try to prevent such danger is therefore appreciated. For our people in their various ranks and classes, on the other hand, who do not usually engage in such formal questions, the main thing remains the instinctive and intuitive bond that it continues to feel for Orange. It may even be said that, just as for a widow her deceased husband continues to live on in intuition more than once, so also for no small part of the Dutch people Orange will remain Orange in intuition, even if it were ultimately laid to rest in the crypt at Delft. Chapter III

THE CONSTITUTION

§1. Never Thought of a Constitution Before. The very idea of inserting a Constitution into the organization of the State is of very recent origin. Our first

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[92]

The Constitution dates only from 1798. Much older than even the word “Constitution” is our own. What is now called the Constitution was still known in 1798 as the “Regulation of the State,” or also as the “Act of State Regulation.” This name held until 1801, with only this difference: that the act of 1798 was entitled “Regulation of State for the Batavian People,” while in 1801 this title was changed to “Regulation of the Batavian People.” The modifications to this regulation brought about by the popular vote of April 9–16, 1805, left the title unchanged. It was not until 1806, when Louis Napoleon ascended the throne as King, that this underwent change. The regulation then issued under the royal signature acquired the name of Constitution. The Treaty of May 24, 1806 introduced this French term to our country, in imitation of France. Article 7 of this Treaty, however, still refers to “the Constitutional Statute of France.” The term “Constitution” as such was not spoken of until the proclamation of the Sovereign Prince on March 29, 1814. And since then, the expression “Constitution” has been adhered to, in 1815 as well as in 1840, 1848, and 1887. Even outside the official framework, the word “Constitution” was almost entirely unknown to us. In the curious Middle Dutch Dictionary of Professor Dr. J. Verdam, the word does not occur at all, and in the Dutch Dictionary of De Vries and Te Winkel, only this sentence from Hooft is cited: “The King had the holy Covenant sworn anew, declaring thereby that he wished to persist in it, and to have it maintained as a Constitution of the Realm”—but even here it is not the name of any state document. After Hooft, Borger is the first to include the word and then use it figuratively: “Frugality is the constitution of his administration.” And thereafter, it is only after 1814/15 that the expression comes into vogue, not least with Conscience, who as a Fleming translated the French word “Constitution.” The idea of basing the structure of the State on a Constitution or Fundamental Law had thus not yet found entry. In England, as is well known, there is even now no Constitution at all, and if we go back to the Roman Empire, the Greek Kingdoms, including Egypt, the attempt to give the government a Constitution as a foundation had never been thought of. To infer from this that there had been no agreement, no mutual declaration between people and government, would be a mistake.

[93]

FAMILY OF STATES.81 For what concerns our country, it is only necessary to refer to the Union of Utrecht to see the opposite embodied. Regulation of the mutual relationship between Government and People cannot be lacking. Without regulation, that relationship would be disrupted of itself. But it by no means follows from this that this relationship needs to be put in writing and cast into legal form in all its parts.

§2. Family of States. Compare here merely the household of the State with that of the private citizen. When in such a private family, man, wife, children, and servants live together, it goes without saying that there exist certain relationships between the head and the other members of the family, and also between those members among themselves, and that these relationships are governed by certain rules. Yet for this no constitutional family document is drawn up. The rule for those relationships is partly found in civil law and labor laws, but for the far greater part rests in the tacit agreement, like the custom of the country, and more specifically the local usage of city or village. One knows that usage from the parental home and from what one hears or sees with neighbors. There is in all these relationships all kinds of things that one does of oneself and as if by intuition, and there are things that one does not do on equal grounds. So there is a certain regularity; but what is to be valid here is not described and agreed upon; it applies of itself. And so also it is in the social sphere in a broader sense. The social life too is well bound to rules, but those rules operate as an unwritten that, i.e. by virtue of usage and of themselves. But this did not go so customarily in the political sphere. Continually there was conflict. There was a struggle over rights and power. This led to stipulations and agreements. Just think of the charters of the cities. But it did not come to an academic exposition of the political relationships between the Government and the distinct parts of the people. In fact, it was the French Revolution that first gave the impetus to the indispensable declaration of a Constitution or Basic Law, as the standardized architectural plan for the construction of the government and the state life; and in itself this was more to be praised than to be condemned. The earlier charters, and in particular the Magna Carta in England, often bore a one-sided character and fitted the condition that gave the aristocracy the upper hand. Of the people in their broad, more democratic conception there was at that time still no mention.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[94]

82 THE CONSTITUTION.

§3. The participation of the people.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.

But time, and in that time the development of the people, advanced; and when gradually not only the collective whole of each class, but also the individual existence of the individual citizen, began to be taken into account, it could not be otherwise, or the political party system had to come to the fore; not for the defense of economic or class interests, but by virtue of difference in insight into the political principle from which the government of the state arose. This now had the necessary consequence, that not only for the relationship between Government and People certain regulations had to apply, but that, even more urgently still, a need arose to provide each of the independent parties that were struggling with one another in the one and the same people a guarantee against the overwhelming domination of the rival parties. From now on the dispute was no longer entirely and exclusively between People and Government, but there came in addition, no less threateningly, the conflict between the distinct parts of the people themselves. The time was past when the magnates among the people could be regarded as representing the whole people. Especially after the experience here in the country with the Regent clique, a far more violent conflict was to be reckoned with between those magnates and the ordinary citizenry than between the People and the Government. If the form of government was monarchical, the suppressed party could still always count on support and protection from a higher hand, if not actually reckon on it, then at least hope for it. In Republics, on the other hand, the dominant party threatened to entrench itself in the government itself, to appropriate the governmental power, and thus not only to keep the parties standing next to it from the party weapons of organization, press and suffrage, but almost to decapitate them. Whoever investigates the state regulation of 1798 will see with what ruthless tyranny all who held with Orange were pushed into a corner,

[95]

THE PEOPLE’S VOICE.83 So much so that even a man like Bilderdijk had to seek refuge in flight to Germany, immediately feels how radically the situation has changed compared to earlier times. Hence, from that time on, the need—nay, the necessity—to come to a more stable regulation of the mutual relations could no longer be denied. The Calvinists in France had already felt this under the leadership of Hotman and Languet; and when the Pilgrim Fathers in America wanted to put their colonial life on a firmer footing, it immediately became clear to them that an agreement would no longer suffice and that a written accord had to be resorted to. Especially when it came to the uprising in America against the English rule, the idea of enshrining the foundations for the new government to be established in a Constitution presented itself so naturally that they proceeded to do so, state by state, even without prior agreement. Although it must always be remembered that the very idea of the constitution first broke through in the triumphant year of the French Revolution, it may never be inferred from this that the Anti-Revolutionary party would be hostile to the idea of the constitution as such. It had already come to the fore in Calvinist circles, both here and in America, even before the Paris conclusions were drawn; and with ideas such as those advocated by Da Costa here and Martensen in Denmark, the Anti-Revolutionary party can never unite. To be satisfied with a constitution that would be granted as a royal favor, unilaterally by the Sovereign to the people, and could also be taken back by the Sovereign at the moment it pleased him, would be a betrayal of our principles. What could be foregone in previous centuries, because the political development of the people was still so backward at the time, would no longer be tolerable now that this development has made such gigantic strides in an individualistic and thus democratic direction. Safeguards against abuse of power must now not only possess the people against the Sovereign, but also one part of the people against the preponderance of another part living by different principles.

§4. The letter powerless against life. Emphasis is placed on this, not as if an act, a written...

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.
[96]

The Constitution is a document, signed as required, and provided with all necessary seals, which could never raise an immovable dam against the very stream of life itself. Any provision in whatever constitution, which is no longer sustained by the people’s consciousness, is already partially paralyzed and doomed to uselessness. What was determined and re-granted in the constitution is couched in words. Words themselves, however, indicate something abstract in nature—they do not fully express the matter itself. A meaning must be attached to those words. That meaning can be twofold, threefold. The choice between these determines the interpretation; and where it then stands between many interpretations, the wording of the article, just as it stands in the constitution, will be interpreted in a changing sense according to the spirit of the times and the convictions arising from that spirit of the times. The fate that befell Article 194 regarding education is clear proof of this. That in 1848 the mere remote thought was not even entertained that, just as is now the case, a contribution of many millions of guilders from the state treasury would be granted to Private Education, no one asserts. What was most graciously intended to be granted to particularism in education in 1848 was the permissibility of this free education. In fact, however, the members of the States-General who gave their vote to the draft of Article 194 then under consideration, meant nothing else than that the Public School would remain the solemn anchor of the State, and that for this neutral Public School the necessary funds would always be lavishly provided, but never even a single guilder for the colored and private education. This idea lay so far below the horizon of Dutch thought at the time, that on our part it was scarcely pointed out, and we already felt excessively enriched and honored with the freedom acquired to do at our own expense what was in itself hardly desired. It stood then with our Primary Education, just as in 1815 and in 1874 with our Higher Education. Our Higher Education, by virtue of its first regulation, knew by no means any free play. It would all emanate solely from the Government. The free initiative was not even tolerated. That was then changed in 1876, but only in the sense that whoever chose to do so could establish it. Permission in general, nothing more, was given, and granted with the thought that it would nevertheless remain unused.

[97]

POWERLESS AGAINST LIFE.85 remain. And while in 1904/’5 a change was made here, and the Free University was also legally recognized, even then there was still no thought of granting it, just as with Primary and Secondary Education, subsidies from the state treasury for its professorships. This might be allowed for the Gymnasia, and this might be in the form of scholarships, but at that time one could by no means reach the point of actual subsidy. The intention, it was maintained, of what was legally valid did not permit this. On both occasions, one thus stood before the same closed sluice, but in the matter of Primary Education, this sluice has now at least not dared to be opened wide, and the position was even defended by Professor Buys that Article 194, if properly interpreted, undoubtedly allowed for subsidy. Now I do not say that violence has been done to the wording of the article, but it remains undoubtedly true that the later interpretation was the result of a changed popular conviction. Even though Thorbecke himself had recognized that he could not think of a decent public education system with Free Schools alone, Thorbecke could never bring himself, in what he regulated and tried to order, to do justice to the idea of freedom to the extent that he blew the trumpet of freedom. In theory, Thorbecke sometimes sang with an ideal voice, but in practice he remained intent on curbing the influence that he constantly feared from the orthodox and especially from the Reformed camp. Yet it did not help, whether one tried to fix this exclusivism through sharply formulated wording in the constitutional article. The popular conviction broke the power of the letter. That letter soon appeared in the eyes of many to be less tightly stretched, and in the end it was Thorbecke’s own spiritual children who considered the subsidizing of the Free School very well compatible with the rule formulated in Art. 194. And although we now admit that in the Primary School question the popular conviction had a spontaneity that does not easily come out so strongly on other issues of a constitutional nature, since they cover a smaller area, yet it can generally be said that the people in their broader circles consult the articles of the Constitution almost as little as ever, but live and develop in a legal conviction that is mainly determined by the facts and the explanation given to them.

[98]

The Constitution, which governs the press and its facts, is increasingly setting the tone in the States General, so that it not only announces itself at every ballot box, but effectively dictates the law. It does not follow from this, however, that we too, in the English manner, could get along just as well without a constitution.

§5. Yet the written document is indispensable. For our constitutional order was too suddenly and violently overthrown at the end of the 18th century. In 1813, one could not continue living in the old house, even if it was newly furnished. What stood there was no longer a house, but mere ruin. A completely new situation was ushered in, and to seal this, a written and sworn constitution was indispensable. The Constitution then laid down and bilaterally sealed acquired the form of a covenant from the outset. It was the people of the Netherlands who, after having been mortally wounded, saw their wound unexpectedly healed, and now felt the will arise within them to, by renouncing past follies, make use of the solid historical core of their past and, invoking God’s blessing, face an entirely new, as yet unknown future; and at that future, our people at the time knew no other desire than to seek the guidance of Orange. However, precisely with a view to the manifold evil that had arisen from the unpreparedness of the relationship between the people and Orange in the 16th, 17th and 18th centuries, the urge was all the stronger in 1813 to now come to a firm accord, and it is this accord that has been embodied in the Constitution of 1813/1815. Monarchical self-conceit and all Orangist deification must be excluded here. Above all, the people remained the main thing, and everything that governed the state order was secondary. However majestic the tradition of the House of Orange might be, and however perfectly one had been cured of the republican predilection, even in 1813 it was and remained: Orange for the people, and not the people for Orange. However, precisely with a view to what lack of proper regulation of the relationship between the two had spoiled in the past, one now felt the compelling need

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[99]

NOT TO BE MECHANICALLY CONNECTED. 87 and the imperative demand was recognized on both sides not to rush things through overnight, and it was precisely the written accord that could prevent the repetition of former misery. Hence our lex scripta, which precisely because it was an accord, could not be replaced by a new Constitution in 1848. The Constitution of 1813/1815 is and remains the foundation on which our state edifice, as far as the accord is concerned, still rests. Amended, expanded, embellished, supplemented, it has been in 1815, 1840, 1848, 1887, and soon it will be amended again, but the document as a document remained one, and by virtue of the agreement indicates what Orange undertook towards the Netherlands and the Netherlands towards Orange, and further, according to which rules the various constituent parts of the people as a whole would be maintained in their rights and freedoms by both.

§6. Not mechanically connected. What was fortunately avoided here is any mechanical interconnection between the Netherlands and Orange. The Belgian Constitution is of a mechanical character. If the King who wore the Crown in Belgium dies, and there is a Prince who is to succeed him according to constitutional rule, then the succession does not take place organically and automatically. For some days there is then no King, and the government rests with the ministers. It is they who summon the heir to the throne to take the required oaths. And only after this has happened and the accord with the people has been entered into by him, is the crown placed on his head and his kingship begins. But something like this was precisely what was not wanted in 1813 with us. With us it is: “The king is dead, long live the king.” At the very moment the King breathes his last, his successor steps up as King. It happens organically and automatically. And only then does the newly enthroned King himself summon the States General to seal the bond between people and prince before the eyes of God. One even felt something for having this laying of the bond between people and prince take place not in a meeting of the Estates, but in a palace of divine worship.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.
[100]

88 THE CONSTITUTION. It naturally follows from the accord entered into in 1813. This accord does not lapse with the death of the Prince, but continues of itself between people and prince. Not only the Prince, but the people also change. Though there may still be a few here and there who are over a hundred years old and were already there when the accord was entered into, it cannot be established with certainty that the people as a whole are the same as those who made up the people in 1813, for all those persons have since died. It is not the case that only the Prince of that time has fallen away by death; the very same applies equally to the people themselves. But just as the people do not present themselves at a certain point in time as new or renewed, in order to enter into a new accord, but rather continue to live and exist organically under the old accord, so approximately the same goes for the House of Orange. Even if William I, II, and III died one after the other, yet the House still continued on in our revered Queen, and the strictly organic connection continues of itself, excluding any mechanical connection. All that was necessary was that the newly ascended Prince should personally swear the accord, so that the people would retain the very same guarantee against him that they had obtained from the first King who ascended the throne.

§7. Continuous Accord.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how

Something that needs to be emphasized all the more, since with Dynasties that endure for centuries, a sense so easily creeps in as if these Dynasties were the subject and the people merely came to seal their power. The Bourbons in France especially gave currency to this rising conception by the anointing from the church. For so it seemed as if the French Kings, quite apart from the people, received the kingship over Gaul by special revelation from God, and as if the people had only to present themselves at the coronation. The “I am the state” of Louis XIV was the expression of this. It cannot be deplored enough that the phrase “By the Grace of God” in the royal title has thereby been so perverted that many now refuse to recognize this addition as valid even for our land. Article 72 of the constitution gives as the opening of the formula of proclamation only the words: “We, etc., King of the Netherlands, etc.” The attempt made in the revision of 1887 to insert the expression “By the Grace of God” failed. The resistance that arose from the liberal side against this attempt was even violent. From that side, the notion still persisted that the “I am the state” was trying to revive itself in this. Especially the unbelieving statesmen continue to maintain that the making of a King is a human work, in which there can and may be no talk of a doing of God or a disposition of God. Let them, even if they are ministers themselves, allow that the words “By the Grace of God” still figure above the laws, but this is then merely titular and devoid of all further meaning. What they absolutely will not tolerate is that the Constitution itself should represent these formulae as indispensable for the validity of the proclamation. The abuse of the formula by princes has made it intolerable in the eyes of atheism, and it is also recognized on the other side that the resistance that has arisen from this abuse is partly understandable. But here too the rule applies that abuse does not abolish use. In the Crown there is an authority that can never be granted to a human by other humans. Among humans, one cannot and may not dispose over the life, freedom, and good of another. That disposition belongs to God alone, and therefore a Prince as a human can never possess this threefold right of disposition over other humans, except by the grace of God. And this being so, it is not without significance that this firm starting point comes to expression above every science, above every resolution. Now if the supreme authority passes by extinction and birth into the same or a related line, then there is indeed in this a disposition of God in whose hand are death and life. If the son succeeds the father as bearer of the crown, then it is the God who caused that son to be born from that father, to whom that son owes the crown now coming to him. And although the legal regulation in the accord has been established from the human side, yet here too it was God’s guidance in history that brought this rule into being. It is therefore no mere play on words, but the real validity of the historically organic connection, so that the bearer of the Crown himself comes forward, that he owes his sovereign right to God’s ordinance.

[101]

CONTINUOUS ACCORD. 89 All successive Princes in the Netherlands have therefore personally sworn the accord, and in this way the people have retained the same guarantee against them as they had obtained from the first King. This personal swearing of the accord by each new Prince is not a mere formality, but has real significance. For in this way the people are assured that the Prince does not stand above the accord, but is bound to it, and that the accord is not a mere human work, but rests on a divine foundation. The people do not make the Prince, but the Prince makes the people. The people do not give the Prince his authority, but the Prince gives the people their rights. The people do not create the accord, but the accord creates the people. And this accord, which binds both Prince and people, is not a human work, but rests on the ordinance of God. This is the reason why the Constitution does not speak of a “social contract,” but of an “accord” - a term that indicates the divine foundation. The Constitution does not derive the authority of the Prince from the people, but from God. The people do not make the Prince, but God makes the Prince, and the people only recognize this divine appointment. This is the reason why the Constitution does not speak of the “sovereignty of the people,” but of the “sovereignty in its own sphere” - a term that indicates the divine origin of authority. The Constitution does not make the people sovereign over the Prince, but makes both Prince and people subject to a higher sovereignty, which is God’s. This is the reason why the Constitution does not speak of “popular morality” as the foundation of the state, but of “custom” and “propriety” - terms that indicate the divine origin of the moral order. The Constitution does not make the state a product of the people’s will, but makes the state rest on the ordinance of God, which precedes the people and binds both Prince and people. This is the reason why the Constitution does not speak of the “social contract,” but of the “continuous accord” - a term that indicates the divine foundation of the state, which is not subject to human caprice, but rests on the eternal ordinances of God.

[102]

The Constitution must primarily be reckoned from what happened in 1813. There lies the accord, and what took place at the demise of the Princes was nothing other than the application of what was rightfully established in 1813. This also hangs together with the oft-disputed rule that the supreme authority is not to be measured by the detailed provisions that appear in the Constitution for it. Those who advocate this erroneous interpretation of the constitutional accord believe that the Constitution presents the full catalog of all the particular rights that belong to the Bearer of the Crown. The Prince may, according to this rule, allow himself anything that is granted to him by the specific provisions of the Constitution, but conversely, he also remains prohibited from anything that is not expressly included in that catalog.

§8. This Sixth Division. This Sixth Division of the Second Chapter of our Constitution does indeed somewhat create the impression as if it were intended to be so and no other way. In the twenty articles that deal separately with the Power of the King, even the smallest particulars are enumerated of what is thought to belong to the King. Even powers of a subordinate nature, such as, for example, the granting of nobility, the right of the mint, in the sense that the Sovereign “is able to have his effigy placed” on the coin, the right to award orders of merit, etc., are separately included and acknowledged as the highest monarchical rights of the Crown-bearer. A descent into details that, upon closer inspection, are beneath the dignity of a constitution. Why may a learned man not wear the honorary titles of foreign doctorates without permission, and must the Sovereign grant him this permission by royal decree as if it were a knighthood? It is said that a knighthood creates a relationship in life between the Sovereign who grants the cross and the person who wears it, but who still attaches significance to such mere forms of propriety in the 20th century? By now descending into such, for all intents and purposes, insignificant trifles, our Constitution has from the outset weakened its own power in the details, and precisely thereby created the impression as if these twenty articles now fully and precisely stated what the King was allowed to do, so that everything not included therein was then also prohibited to him. Bureaucratic mannerisms, but more harmful than beneficial to correctly portraying what the King’s power should be. When the authority over a people is entrusted to a princely person or a princely House by common accord, the general concept of this authority comes to the fore. So it is with every appointment. Whoever is appointed as a minister and has taken the oath, does not receive a catalog of ministerial duties and rights that precisely stipulate what he may and must do henceforth, but finds three things to regulate his conduct: first, the existing laws, decrees and ordinances; secondly, the long-standing usages that are said to apply in his department; and thirdly, the specific discussions that have led to his appointment on the part of the Sovereign. But besides this detailed [guidance], there also applies for him the duty and the competence to do what, according to the general concept of the ministerial office, flows forth from his office itself. A minister who, in a critical moment, would excuse himself from taking the necessary measures by saying that the obligation they wanted to impose on him was not in his little book, would show himself to be beneath his office. The virtuosity of a general, a minister, and even of a father and mother in their own household, must precisely consist in this, that the responsible person, in unplanned and irregular situations, accomplishes by his head and will what no set of regulations imposes on him. And so it is also here. A King who violates the accord, forfeits his Crown, but also a King who would govern solely according to what is in the little book, and had no sense of what the general character of the Crown demands of him, would also show that he cannot uphold the honor of his Crown. Even where regulation is lacking, the powerful emergence and the inexorable demand of the situation can still be; and woe to the Sovereign who would then fall short in princely initiative. Only here is it always to be seen that the people’s interest, and only in the second place, and thus in a secondary position, the dynastic interest, governs the princely initiative.

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.
[103]

Page 104—-

92 THE CONSTITUTION.

§9. Unilaterally Promulgated. The objection that the constitutional accord is still not promulgated by the two parties, but, as some would have it, unilaterally by the King, does not detract from the matter at hand. The King promulgates the Constitution “with the common deliberation of the States-General,” and it is these States-General, in whose assembly the people are represented. One could have chosen a very different form here, so that the existence and joint action of the two parties would have been more clearly expressed. Then the people, as the main party concerned, would naturally have had to be mentioned first, and only after the oaths had been taken by both sides, would the further arrangements for the mode of government have had to be established by the Prince in common deliberation with the States-General. What is now united in the Constitution in a formally not quite correct manner, would then have been divided into two distinct actions. In the first place, one would have obtained the accord between People and Prince, in which nothing else would have been discussed than the terms of the actual accord regarding the confirmation of the Throne, the succession, the income, the Regency, etc., with the main provisions regulating the mutual relations between what the King would be able to do and what the people reserved for themselves. On the basis of this accord, the Prince would then have received his Crown. And after the monarchical person had thus received the Crown, the Prince, no longer by accord but solely in common deliberation with the People’s Representatives, would have established the further stipulations for the administration.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[105]

PRINCIPLED ACCORD AND FURTHER REGULATION. 93

What was properly to be the Constitution would then have been much shorter; and thereafter, as the first organic law, the detailed regulation of the general administration would have been established. As required, it could have been determined in the common accord that not only any amendment of the fundamental accord, but also the primary organic law of the general administration, would have been bound to special conditions of common deliberation—whether by binding the common deliberation to a United Session of the two Chambers, or by introducing a special provision for the required number of votes, or by making the adoption of a definitive resolution by the States-General dependent on a prior dissolution. This form, however, was not chosen in 1813. Preference was given to allowing the appearance to prevail, as if the Sovereign Prince acted by virtue of the past of His House, and it was now made to appear as if it was this self-acting Prince who, out of good will, granted rights and freedoms to his people.

§10. Principled Accord and Further Regulation.

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.

This incorrect standpoint naturally obliterated the distinction between the principled accord and the further regulation of the administration. Hence, in this case, accords and regulations were brought together in one and the same state document, and the Constitution which served for this purpose was burdened with a troublesome load of detailed regulations. This was, in the meantime, nothing but a mere appearance, unless one grants the Prince the right to set aside the Constitution at his own discretion—a thought which, as we have already remarked, even Da Costa for a moment smiled upon, and which was also advocated by the renowned ethicist Professor Martensen. But precisely for this reason, one may also not endorse the absence of the actual accord between People and Prince, which is hidden in the Constitution. Whoever imagines that in 1813 the then heir of the House of Orange, by his own power by virtue of his descent from William IV, took the government into his own hands, and now, as hereditary ruler with the acceptance of the sovereign title, issued a regulation to please his people, actually surrenders the rights of the people; something that has often been desired from the Conservative side, but which on our side can never be accepted. Naturally, whoever takes this standpoint can lament and insist that the Prince who, out of sheer favor, granted a constitution to his people, can also take back what he had placed at the disposal of his people out of his own free initiative. No sworn oath can then give firmness to what found its origin solely in the Prince’s discretionary power. Those oaths, back and forth, would then only apply as long as the princely disposition on which they were based remained in force. But precisely from this it also appears that here a starting point is valid which...

[106]

94 THE CONSTITUTION cannot and must not be accepted. It is not to honor a princely house dynasty, but the dynasty is accepted and invoked by the people to bless and prosper the people according to an agreement. It is never the Prince who creates his people as a father, but it is the people who call upon the help of the Prince to obtain a firm accord and good, orderly governance. That the power which either the people possess, or which belongs to the Prince, flows from the majesty of God and not from human authority, hardly needs to be repeated here; but with the exception of the military conquest of the land by a mighty potentate, it is always the people in whom the starting point lies, and it is the Prince or the Dynasty who comes to the people and does not act except with the approval of the people. Always according to the rule to which we have always adhered, and which in America led to the confession at the head of the Constitution: “As God has given us the power to choose our own magistrates.” Precisely the condition in which our people also found themselves in 1813; and what the Triumvirate then realized was in fact nothing other than to make it possible for the people, who could not yet speak for themselves because the organ for it was still lacking, to express their desire and their will. All conservative tinkering on this point is therefore entirely devoid of any real value. The false pretense that the people would be there to honor the Dynasty must give way to our uncompromising conviction that the Princes and the Dynasty are there to govern the people by the grace of God and to make them happy through this regime.

§11. The form also demands correctness. But after having placed this with full determination in the foreground, it also follows from this that the princely authority, after having been accepted by the people, must be respected by the people, and thus also in the Constitution. The form is also very important here. Compared especially with the Belgian Constitution, it can be said that in our Constitution the only good form remained. For in our Constitution, the power of the King is first fully dealt with, and everything that relates to the royal power; and only then, in the second place, do the States-General come into play. The first chapter of our Constitution deals with the Kingdom and its inhabitants; immediately afterwards comes the second chapter On the King; and only with article 78 does the third chapter On the States-General come into play. The difficulty for our Constitution was first the establishment of the provisions of the Legislative Power; and in this case they then followed the strange path of not bringing this under the chapter on the King, but under the States-General, and this in its full extent, including the section on the Budget. This cannot be recognized as correct, all the more so since in this sixth section of the Second Chapter there is no specific provision on the legislative power. Only the executive power is declared in Art. 55 to rest with the King, while the legislative power is completely silent. It is only determined, firstly, that the King submits draft laws to the States-General and can approve or disapprove of proposals for laws sent to him by the States-General; while further only the manner of the promulgation of the laws that will apply is determined in Art. 72. Here one thus encounters a confusion of two principles. On the one hand, the Constitution wants to honor the power of the people to bring about a law only through the cooperation of its representatives, and on the other hand, the majesty of the bearer of the crown, whose signature alone can make a draft into a law. Thus the appearance was maintained as if the legislation could only come about through the King, and yet the legislation found no place in the chapter On the King, but in the third chapter of the States-General. §12. Two ways out. There were only two ways to escape this confusion. Not by, as was done in Belgium, placing the legislation before the chapter On the King, and with a view to this, letting the whole regulation of Parliament precede that of the royal power. This does injustice to the majesty of the royal authority.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[108]

THE CONSTITUTION.
