If there is a government with a title of majesty, as is the case in every monarchically organized country, then the Crown must precede in the Constitution, and the States-General must follow. And to prevent confusion and corruption of good order, only two ways are open. One can either place a separate chapter on the legislative power before the chapter on the King, or one can let such a separate chapter follow the two chapters on the King and the States-General. The legislative power is the product of two factors, both of the Crown and the States-General. One cannot, therefore, deal with the legislative power in the chapter on the King. For that would give the appearance of an absolute monarchical act, and the legislative power in the constitutional state can never be that. One remembers how Calvin constantly insisted that the law as a whole should be held in high esteem for its own inherent worth. But it is just as little permissible to do what has been done in our Constitution, and to place it under the States-General. Certainly the States-General cooperate in matters of legislation, and may even take the initiative; the King is in every act of legislation absolutely dependent on their cooperation and thus also on the approval of the States-General, i.e., of the people. But just as little as legislation is the one-sided act of the King, just as little can or may it be the one-sided act of the people, if there is monarchical authority in the country. It would therefore have been most natural to begin the Constitution with the accord between People and Dynasty, in order to lay down in the provisions of this accord the regulation of the legislative power, and to determine what share in the legislative power belongs to the Crown and what to the People. Then the King and finally the States-General could have been dealt with. And only after the completion of these two would there have been room, not for the legislative power, but for the regulation of the manner in which a law comes into being. Now, however, the accord is in our Constitution in a way that cannot be excused completely overshadowed, so that in our opinion one could also suffice with first dealing with the King, then with the States-General, and after dealing with these two in a separate chapter, with the legislative power. Our objection to the present arrangement in our Constitution

[109]

NO PEACEFUL DEVELOPMENT. 97 The accepted arrangement in no way arises from a desire to surrender the land and people to princely caprice. On the contrary, from the Calvinist side, we would resolutely resist any attempt in that direction. What we do not wish to accept is that Article 109 openly places the legislative power under the States-General in the Constitution, and that under the chapter dealing with the States-General, the Prince is also granted a certain co-determination. Hence our insistence that the legislative power either act in the form of an accord before the chapters on the King and the States-General, or, if this is no longer deemed necessary, appear in a new, separate chapter after the treatment of the King and the States-General. Only in this way can both come to their right—on the one hand, the majesty of the Crown Bearer, and on the other, the primordial legal standpoint of the People. And this for the entire legislation, and in a special sense for any possible amendment of the Constitution once in force.

§13. NO PEACEFUL DEVELOPMENT. Whether it would still be possible to make such a far-reaching change, at least in the form, in the entire structure of our Constitution, may be doubted. So far, it has only come to a fundamental change in 1848, and this under the impression of the then generally prevailing revolutionary state of affairs. As long as the calm weather condition continued to prevail in the political sky, there was little inclination on the King’s side for any significant change; and it was only when the events in Paris, Germany, and Austria made the rulers in all countries fear a repetition of what had been experienced in 1789 and the following years in France, and when, as a result of the French Revolution, much was already being turned upside down in other countries, that concessions were made from the King’s side. Such a juncture could return. Fear even arose from more than one quarter that the great world war of 1914 in its consequences could lead to an outbreak of general discontent and thereby to very serious social unrest. If this were the case, then of course the significant change that would then be awaited would have only one goal, namely to further significantly expand the popular influence of the most numerous class among the voters. Another outcome of such turmoil can also be imagined. A new Napoleonic figure could emerge who, either for all of Europe or for each country separately, would crush the revolutionary reaction coming from below with far-reaching violence and give the state system a more dictatorial organization. What would happen to the Constitution in such a case cannot be predicted, even though the Napoleonic period continues to prophesy analogies. If, on the other hand, any dictatorial intervention were to be avoided, and the revolutionary element were to succeed in removing what had so far prevented it from the Constitution and in enforcing a one-sided shift of power to the side of the lower classes of the people, then there could naturally be no question of doing justice to the accord in the Constitution, nor of putting it in a better formal order. It is therefore to be feared that the fundamental flaw committed in 1814/’15 will not be easily removed from our Constitution, and that the mixed and therefore confused character it still exhibits, of wanting to be at the same time an accord between People and Dynasty and a basic regulation of the form of government, will not be easily purified. §14. AMBIGUOUS. The difference of opinion without end that arose after 1814 over the way in which changes should be made to the Constitution once introduced is connected with this ambiguous character of this state document. If the changes had only to do with the terms of the accord between People and Dynasty, then the form of the change, to be determined by both parties, would have had to establish equal conditions and the aim would have been to make such a change difficult. The Dynasty would have had a right to this. Now, on the other hand, the mistake has been made of incorporating the accord or the covenant together with the detailed regulation of all kinds of rights in the rest of the state organization into one document, one stands, where the Constitution is concerned, before the question of whether one should proceed according to the rules of the accord or according to the rules of the detailed regulation of the state organization.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[111]

AMBIGUOUS.99Whenever a change in the law is proposed, there is always this contradiction—that with regard to the agreement, the modification must be bound to the strictest possible rule, while in terms of the detailed regulations, it should be made as smoothly as possible. For what does not concern the dynastic Crown rights, but the administration and its many particulars, one faces an entirely different struggle, namely the struggle carried on among the various classes and parties in the people, not with the Crown, but among themselves; and it may safely be said that, now that the more monarchical form of government has become firmly established in our country, constitutional amendments will increasingly result in a struggle between the classes in the nation and the parties in the people, rather than in a conflict between the people and the sovereign. It is not in the matter of the agreement, but in what concerns the struggle between the constituent parts of the national life, that the two conflicting tendencies always present themselves—on the one hand, the desire to hold on to what one has, and on the other, the determination to strive for the acquisition of much that one does not yet possess. Preservation of the existing or transformation into new forms becomes the two-sided slogan. Now, since the outcome of the ensuing struggle naturally depends to a large extent on the easier or more difficult way in which the amendment of any constitutional article is to be carried through, it follows that the question of what conditions the amendment is to be bound to is of such high importance that it will often dominate the entire issue of amendment. In most countries with a constitutional form of government, the basic principle here is that in the matter of constitutional amendment, the ordinary legislative procedure will not be followed. Ordinary repair, addition, or decoration of the state edifice could largely be left free, but the foundation would have to remain essentially what it was. The ordinary legislation could not be applied here. The course of events had to be set in motion on a slow, gradual progression. Many different ways of proceeding came into consideration here. One sought to increase the durability by requiring a higher qualified majority, most often of two-thirds, sometimes even of three-fourths of the votes. To emphasize the greater weight of the action, one would double the number of parliamentary members the people would have to choose for this act.

[112]

Where Parliament was divided into two Chambers, which as a rule met and decided separately, there was often a desire, in the matter of amending the Constitution, to have the two Chambers meet jointly and decide by a combined vote. In order to ensure due deliberation, it was often proposed as a rule that the ordinary legislative procedure should first be followed, to indicate the points on which and the form in which amendment was desired; and that only then should a newly elected Parliament decide on the final adoption or rejection of the proposals. Proposals for amendment could, according to more than one constitution, originate not only from the Government, but if necessary also from Parliament; while on the other hand the Crown remained free to refuse its assent. In England, which has no written Constitution, they have even gone further in the case of Home Rule, and have determined that, if the Upper House rejects a bill passed by the Lower House, this bill may be introduced again, but with the proviso that, if the Lower House adheres to it a second time, and it is then again rejected by the Upper House, the Lower House, without further cooperation of the Upper House, and covered by the Crown, can of its own authority and in spite of the resistance of the Lords, declare the bill to be valid as law. So the ingenuity on this point seems inexhaustible. What was proposed was never satisfactory. One was always seeking a more fruitful system for one’s own wishes. And only this much can be stated, that in the long run a facilitation of the amendment of the Constitution is being sought and will be carried through in most countries.

§15. From Van Hogendorp. This also applies to the Netherlands. We owed the basic form of our Constitution to Gijsbert Karel van Hogendorp, and already from this Statesman, in his so-called Sketch, the first idea went out concerning the way in which a revision of the Constitution to be established shortly could be brought about. “As other times and circumstances may require changes and new institutions,” he wrote in his Sketch, “so at all times a law

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[113]

It may be declared that such a case is at hand. The matter itself will have to be indicated and expressed clearly, and the law submitted to the Provincial States in the usual manner. These Provincial States, who had to send members to the Parliament, would then do so in this particular case in a double capacity, and the double assembly of the States-General would then decide on the proposal in the usual way, as is the case with other laws. Van Hogendorp probably presented it in this way in order to make his idea as acceptable as possible to the Provincial States and later the General States, where there were still some regents’ sensibilities at work. It also became clear that he himself preferred a somewhat tighter tightening of the cord. At least in the end, he did not go unwillingly with the idea of some more decisive members, and at the final decision, a majority of ⅔ was also demanded by him, as this was also recorded as the final result of the deliberations in the Constitution of 1814. For Article 144 stipulated that everything would go as with the ordinary laws, but “with the exception that there must be a majority of votes, at least two-thirds of the members present.” It was also noteworthy that Article 146 gave the Commission appointed to draft the Constitution authentic authority, at least in the first three years, to give the official interpretation in case of doubt about the interpretation of what was stipulated; something that almost reminds one of what is called the invocation of the judge in the United States in case of a constitutional conflict. However, the wagon of the State did not initially deviate from the track laid down, so that in the revision of this, which became necessary in 1815 due to the annexation of the Belgian provinces to our country, the provision of Article 142 was quite violated. For in this article it was prescribed that the ordinary legislator should not only declare revision necessary, but also indicate word for word the text that the new or revised article would contain. This second provision was not complied with in 1815, and it was limited to the ordinary legislator merely declaring that revision on this and that point was necessary, without the new wording being legally established beforehand. Something in which this second deviation also occurred,

[114]

102 THE CONSTITUTION.that the final decision would be taken in North-Netherlands by the double chamber, but that in the Belgian provinces the Notables would receive the decision. Something that is all the more striking, since in the constitutional text of 1815 the provision also remained that the ordinary law had to establish the wording of the amendment. For Article 229 expressly stated that not only the necessity of any change or addition had to be expressed, but also that “the change or addition itself must be clearly indicated and expressed.” As for the composition of the college in which the decision would be made, and the way in which that decision would be made, Article 232 stipulated that at least two-thirds of the members making up the assembly had to be present, and that, moreover, three-quarters of the members present had to agree with the proposal for it to be passed. There is no mention here yet of the participation of the First Chamber in the amendment. It is true that the First Chamber was established in 1815 under Articles 78 and 80, but in the articles regulating the revision of the Constitution, this First Chamber was not yet taken into account. There is only mention in Articles 232 et seq. of the Second Chamber. The Nine Men did want to involve the members of the First Chamber in the revision, but not in double numbers. The members of both Chambers would then decide in a joint meeting, but in this mixed meeting the members of the Second Chamber, doubled, would be able to cast 220 votes, and those of the First Chamber at most 40, later even fewer.

§16. Later ideas.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.

In 1848, the result in Article 196 of the Constitution revised at that time was that the ordinary legislator, without even declaring the “necessity” of revision, would still expressly indicate the change desired. According to Article 197, both Chambers were then dissolved, and in the newly elected Chambers, each separately, and without doubling, “by a two-thirds majority of the votes cast, decided on the proposed revision. What

[115]

LATER IDEAS - TWO KINDS OF REVISION

It was with great difficulty that in 1887 people tried to bring about changes here, but this attempt was thwarted. Even now, it still remains as it was established in 1848. And it was only through the Constitutional Commission, which the Heemskerk Ministry managed to appoint, that a completely different modification was then proposed. The De Meester Cabinet had also already made revision of the articles of the same Chapter possible. But neither the proposals of the State Commission of the De Meester Cabinet, nor those of the State Commission of the Heemskerk Cabinet, have come up for further consideration. What is to be expected from the Van der Linden Cabinet in this regard cannot yet be calculated. It even seems probable that, if any revision comes about as a result of this initiative, it will not even deal with the same Chapter. It therefore seems superfluous to discuss the commissorial attempts of 1906 and 1912 to revise the same Chapter separately. Only for the course of the ideas and not for the constitutional process, these two unborn children are of importance. The proposals for amendment that came from the Social Democratic parties and the Liberal Democratic side can be passed over here in silence. Mr. A.J. Fokker brought together the revision proposals of the State Commission, which were ready in 1906, in a fine edition that saw the light of day in Leiden with Doesburgh. As far as necessary, reference is made to that. We are now left with nothing else than to set forth the manner of proceeding, and to explain as far as necessary, which seems to us the most recommendable.

§17. Two Kinds of Revision

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the

In this connection, it is now emphasized that above all it would be desirable not to bind all the articles of the Constitution to the same mode of revision. If it is once established that the Constitution on the one hand establishes the bond between People and Dynasty, and on the other hand lays down the conditions for the regulation of the government, then it cannot but be questionable to prescribe a completely equal manner of acting for the modification or revision of two such utterly divergent groups of provisions. Amendments to...

[116]

104 THE CONSTITUTION.

To bring into accord the relationship between the People and the Princely House is of an entirely different character than the revision of more specific provisions regulating the affairs of state. In the accord or covenant, and thus also in the revision thereof, two independent magnitudes stand opposed to one another. The People on the one side and the Prince with his Dynasty on the other. Here it is so essential that the regulation of their mutual relationship in itself be a definitive one and not subject to change without mutual consent. It is true that the reigning Prince always retains the right to withhold his assent from any revision; but without further provision, this right could become very precarious. If, in accordance with the outcome of a general election, the Second Chamber is composed in such a way that the parties wishing to replace the monarchical principle with the republican one form the majority, then the Prince is anything but entirely free in the choice of his Cabinet. Likely then, in order to avoid an all too serious conflict, the desire will arise to, through revision, grant such an expansion to the rights of the People that the monarchical authority can only be maintained in form, and dissolves into what even a man like Mr. van Houten calls an ornament. Now if from such an action a proposal for revision emerges that in a very serious way threatens the existence of the Monarchy, and the Prince then refuses to enact this draft into law, then, as history here and elsewhere has taught, street tumult soon awaits, just as it proceeded from Leiden to the Residence in 1848, and the Prince is ultimately likely to be subjected to such threatening violence that he either yields or abdicates. With a view to this, it is now desirable not to make the revision of the constitutional accord too easy. Revision itself must never be definitively cut off, but it is in every way desirable to establish such provisions for the revision of the covenant between People and Prince that such a revision can only arise from a well-considered popular conviction. One must not proceed here in a reckless and hasty manner. Without a doubt, it may also be assumed that the many provisions by which one sought to make the revision of the Constitution difficult from 1814 onward, had virtually no other purpose than to grant sufficient firmness to the Dynasty; not in itself, but always in connection with the rights and freedoms that the Dynasty was to enjoy.

[117]

REVISION PREVENTED THEREBY. 105 The dynasty had to be respected. The accord was also two-sided in the nature of the matter. There had to be something unshakable established for the benefit of the people on the side of the dynasty, but then also the reverse, for the benefit of the dynasty on the side of the people. For this reason, it would have been most desirable that the chapters of the Constitution regulating this mutual relationship could not be revised except under burdensome provisions. However, there is absolutely no reason whatsoever to bind the revision of all other articles of the Constitution to such a burdensome regulation. It is evident how much modification is desired from all sides in the regulation of education, poor relief, the place that the churches will occupy in the life of the nation, the means required for the defense of the independence of the country and its colonies, or if it were a matter of abolishing the Council of State in its present form—then there is no conceivable reason why modifications of such a nature should be made subject to such an onerous procedure.

[118]

106 THE CONSTITUTION. The Belgian Constitution does not even contain an article on Education, and it does not know a Council of State. Whether this is preferable or not is left undecided; but it is then clearly evident how little such regulations govern the accord or covenant between people and sovereign. § 18. Revision thereby prevented. Nothing has so seriously hindered the development of our state as the fact that very strict provisions were indispensable for the accord between people and sovereign, and that now, since accords and more detailed state regulations were included in the same Constitution, the articles of more detailed regulation have also been subjected to the same burdensome provisions for revision. Now there is an increasing tendency to weaken the rules of revision in order to avoid the inconvenience of our political life; but against this the opposite objection now arises of its own accord, that in this way the required firmness of the accord or covenant is threatened to be taken away. If now the revision is governed by the definite requirement that the revision of all articles and chapters may only be carried out in one and the same way, then it will have to remain a give and take, and the revision of one part will be made easy, and of the other part difficult. A contradiction that is pointed out at every revision, but which appears not to be easily smoothed out. Such a requirement, however, that the revision of both main elements in our Constitution should have to take place in one and the same way always, cannot be justified by anything. Nothing prevents, in the Constitution itself, to determine that the revision of the chapters that give the accord or covenant can only take place in a special way, and that on the other hand for the revision of the other chapters, since they are of a different nature, a different, simpler way of revision will apply. If a desire also carries a certain general and lasting character to bring some modification in the regulation of the accord, no bearer of the Crown will stubbornly resist it; but this general and lasting character of such a desire for modification must then also have been evident in a pre-defined form. Something that has been tried and succeeded both in 1814/’15 and in 1848 and in 1887. But against this, the completely different desire always came up to facilitate the revision of articles that were of a factual nature. In the revision of such articles, interests of a completely different nature were at stake, and it has not a little hindered the normal and healthy development of our state life, that in order to give the desired firmness to the accord, every other revision has now also been bound to an equally rigid regulation. If one now distinguishes in our Constitution the two kinds of provisions, and establishes more lenient provisions for what in no way requires such rigidity in revision, then the difficulty that one constantly encounters is resolved, and it will be satisfied both with what the dynasty has a right to demand and with what the normal development of our state life requires. § 19. Much can be removed from the Constitution. Secondly, it would be desirable to extract from our Constitution all those regulations that lose themselves too much in the details and do not belong in a Constitution, but rather in laws or royal decrees.

[119]

MUCH CAN BE REMOVED FROM THE CONSTITUTION - TWO-THIRDS? 107 Therefore, they could very well be left to the ordinary legislator. A provision like that of Article 111, which requires a preliminary investigation for every legislative proposal submitted to the States-General, and that the Chamber itself regulates this investigation, is not only superfluous, but even detrimental to the proper course of affairs, and often causes entirely unnecessary loss of time for the members of the States-General. That, as Article 173 now establishes, the intervention of the Government is not required in the correspondence with the heads of the various religious denominations, is superfluous. That according to Art. 177 the weight, fineness and value of the coin species are regulated by law, is a too detailed provision for a Constitution. Already the comparison of our constitution with that of other states shows in every way how unnecessary it is to have gone over to a constitutional regulation of all kinds of particulars, which elsewhere are not governed by any constitutional article at all. And yet it is precisely these kinds of articles that constantly give rise to the desire for revision, in order to leave the revision of the Constitution itself almost entirely free. Yet, if one once includes in the Constitution all kinds of such regulations that constantly require small modifications, then one of two things happens: either one, hardly taking the text of the Constitution into account, goes quietly on one’s way and effectively ignores the Constitution, or, in order not to get stuck on such detailed regulations, one makes the revision of the Constitution as such too free.

§20. Two-Thirds? Among the means that are indicated to maintain the distinct character of Constitution and ordinary law in a revision, the provision of the size of the required majority stands out. This majority was set at two-thirds from the outset in 1814/’15. In 1848 this remained the same. And in 1887 too, one did not depart from the requirement of the two-thirds. The two-thirds were then calculated on the present members. One did not get involved with the absolute number of members that could come up. In the State Commission of 1906, on the other hand, this two-thirds was abandoned, and the figure of “at least 76 members” was set as the requirement in the joint session.

Analytical Summary: The three types not equally characteristic. But this does not mean that, in the course of time, each of these three types has in turn dominated the national condition: the Roman Catholics in the Middle Ages, the Calvinists in the 16th century, and the French Revolutionaries after 1789. It does not at all follow from this that they are equal in national significance. Undoubtedly, the Seven Provinces, once living a common life with Flanders and other southern regions, have enjoyed a high and flourishing position and have gradually taken an increasingly important position in Northern Europe; but it must not be forgotten here that the national energy was at the time much more strongly developed in what is now called Belgium than in the northern provinces. Neither Amsterdam nor Rotterdam could in those days be placed on a par with Ghent and Bruges. The wealth of medieval life had more stamped its imprint on the national character in the South than in the North. The reference to the rich medieval type therefore has much less significance for the present Kingdom of the Netherlands than for the western part of Belgium. And in the second place, it must be added that at that time the unity of our national life was much less consolidated. Under the Burgundians, the Dutch provinces did not occupy an independent position, but were united with what lay outside our later borders; and under Charles V and Philip, we even came, though only in a personal way, into a Union with entirely different states from Central and Southern Europe—something which could not but be detrimental to the confirmation of our own national character. Whoever investigates the historical significance of our lower lands may well find the period we lived through in the Middle Ages highly important, but nevertheless hesitates for a moment to acknowledge without reservation that our glorious period dates from April 1, 1572. Not in the Middle Ages, but in the period of the Reformation, lies the high point of our national history. It cannot be emphasized enough how much scrupulous fairness is required in such a comparison, and we shall beware of the assertion that from the Calvinist side the correct toll of reverence has always been paid to the medieval period in our history, to which they undoubtedly had a right. But it stands to reason that from the Roman Catholic side, too, we have not always been done justice. It cannot be valued highly enough that especially in the second half of the 19th century a more impartial judgment gained influence. Just with a view to this more unanimous judgment, it may now at least be established that not only the foreign historians, one may well say without any exception of importance, honor the period after 1572 as the highest and most glorious, but that also within the country the best interpreters of each of the three types increasingly come to the unanimous conclusion that the world significance of our small country came out most richly in the heyday of Calvinism. This in no way denies that our national life in those days of our glory also showed dark sides. The resistance to the monarchical state form, and even against the House of Orange, has almost become a source of our unhappy discord until 1789, and it is impossible to say how much higher our country would have soared if immediately after the separation from Philip II the crown had been offered to Prince William. It had to come to this in 1813; but who calculates what national suffering would have been spared us if we had taken this important step two centuries earlier. But even if one plays open cards and takes into account all the suffering, oppression and restriction of rights that the temporarily dominant group has inflicted on the other two groups, it is historically established that we were never before 1572 what our national life was in the period between those two dates, and never again after 1789. To top it off, the fact that we offered a refuge to the politically and religiously persecuted from all countries is proof that the actual life here was a life in freedom, and that the publications and ordinances that claimed to infringe on this freedom had more the effect of satisfying formal demands than of inflicting suffering on those who deviated.
[120]

THE CONSTITUTION. To this number were added the 100 members of the Second Chamber, plus the 50 members of the First Chamber, which made 150; and when this was divided in half, it gave 75. So to gain a majority, one only had to add one more. And so they reached 76. Even the most recent Constitutional Commission went down this path. They thus arrived at the ordinary majority, not of the members who had appeared, but of the total number of members who made up both Chambers. Of a previously desired limitation, which the majority wished to set at three-fourths, one no longer hears anything. The effect of such a regulation is naturally entirely governed by the question of whether the two Chambers of the States-General are homogeneously composed. This has, however, not always been the case. The period has not been so short that the liberal statesmen, though with some nuance, had not managed to secure a majority in both the Second and the First Chambers. This changed in 1889 with the advent of the Mackay Cabinet, and again under the Kuyper Cabinet. But then the liberal composition of the majority in the First Chamber, combined with the rightist composition of the Second Chamber, led to an open conflict, which the dissolution of the First Chamber in 1904 temporarily resolved. In 1905, on the other hand, and again in 1913, the conflict returned, though in reverse order. Thus the situation arose twice over that the Second Chamber was liberal and the First Chamber rightist in its majority. This shows how the colors and composition of the two Chambers can govern and modify the significance of the figure required for a majority in a constitutional revision. If the political color of the First and Second Chambers is homogeneous, then the requirement of a majority of 76 members for a constitutional revision means nothing other than to prevent the nuances of one color from leading to an excessively dominant position of one of those nuances. If, on the other hand, the political base color of the majority in both Chambers is divergent, then the figure of 76 can lead to surprising results. At this moment, the First Chamber counts 19 members of the Left and 31 of the Right, while the Second Chamber, on the other hand, has 45 of the Right against 55 of the Left. Now, if one combines both Chambers in a joint session, this gives 76 of the Right against 74 of the Left. How much the Left predominates in the Second Chamber

[121]

THE MOST DESIRABLE METHOD.109

If the large majority had the power, then in the joint session the majority of the V2 + 1 would still remain on the Right. If, upon the death of Mr. W. Hovy, a second liberal member had been elected by the States of Zeeland next to Mr. Fokker, it would stand at 75 against 75, and action would remain impossible; while with the loss of even just one more seat for the First or Second Chamber, the majority in the joint session would be on the Left. The provincial elections of 1916 have made this precarious situation even more acute. And this precarious state of affairs could even take on a more serious character, if even in the current composition of the First and Second Chambers, a temporary serious illness were to prevent one or two members of the same side from appearing in the assembly. Even an unexpected death could now make the outcome critical, since the intention was to permanently set the number at 76, while it appears time and again how extremely seldom it succeeds, when 150 members are candidates, to have them all present.

§21. The Most Desirable Method. The most desirable course in revising the Constitution would therefore come down to this, that the entire fundamental State document be split into two main parts, of which the first would embody the accord between People and Sovereign, and the second would establish the further regulation of the State. For each of these two main parts, a separate mode of revision would then have to be determined; for the first on a more solid, for the second on a less difficult footing. For the more solid regulation of the revision of the first Main Part, one would then have to remain with what was determined in 1814/15 and in part in 1848. This would then come down to 1° that the States-General would have to declare and clearly formulate the necessity of revision and what the desired new reading would be; 2° that the States-General would then be dissolved and doubled in number of members; and 3° that the two Chambers would have to decide with not less than two-thirds of the cast votes. Then follows 4° the signing and 5° the promulgation by the King. For the revision of the second Main Part of the Constitution, on the other hand, a simpler mode of action could suffice.

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over
[122]

110 THE CONSTITUTION.

Here it concerns often changes in regulations of a subordinate nature, which must be able to come about without much disturbance of the ordinary order; something which, even with the omission from the Constitution of too many details, must not encounter too great an obstacle. Here it would be most desirable, so without a prior declaration of necessity, first in the ordinary session of the two Chambers separately the desirability and text were established, and then in the joint meeting of the two Chambers with 80 votes the decision fell. Dissolution of the Chambers after the first vote would in itself be undoubtedly desirable and would be formally more correct, but has too much against it and cannot be deemed indispensable. The objection that arises against this comes down to this, that a States-General, elected solely with a view to a detail regulation regarding constitutional revision, would lack any guiding principle for the further conduct of government policy. Suppose a proposal came up to order parts of the Army, also without personal consent of the soldiers, in case of need to the Colonies, and Art. 184 underwent a change in this sense, then of course a Second Chamber, emerging from an electoral struggle over this one point, would lack any guiding principle for the further treatment of the affairs of the State in the four-year period that followed the dissolution. Doubling is equally an undesirable expedient. Persons who are not at all accustomed to move in the parliamentary circle, and know little of the past, would then have to cooperate on an equal footing with the seasoned members. As a rule, such a mixing of the dissimilar leads to daring outcomes, which it is better to avoid.

§22. As for the relationship of the parties. The question could only arise whether regulation of the revision on a more solid footing would not be required, where it concerned a constitutional issue that touched on the relationship between the various political parties or classes; especially in the matter of the franchise. Often it has been presented as if, with too easy a mode of revision, a danger would threaten from the side of the Left.

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the
[123]

THE RELATIONSHIP OF THE PARTIES - GENERAL OR PARTIAL.

A temporary majority could too easily abuse its position to twist the entire constitutional order to suit its own ends. That there would be a tendency towards this is undisputable. But one must not lose sight of the fact that, except in revolutionary times, a somewhat significant revision of our Constitution does not just fall from the sky, but generally comes up for discussion years in advance and under public scrutiny, and is often already prepared by the introduction of revision bills by members of the Chamber. Hence, it is soon noticeable at the ballot box whether revision is on the agenda or not. If it is, it is usually already put forward by the parties at the ballot box, and the vote that the people cast in the general election is often already a declaration of their desire. This generally leads, after the Cabinet has taken office, in which the outcome of the ballot box is reflected, to the appointment of a State Commission; as has repeatedly been the case, even after the revision of 1887. In the reports of these commissions, the representatives of the people then naturally obtain a more definite formulation. And in this way, what was otherwise aimed at by the dissolution of the Chamber, can now of itself fall within the parliamentary fold.

§23. General or Partial. The not unimportant question of whether general or partial constitutional revision is preferable is also connected with this. If the revision proceeds too slowly, and too many extraordinary measures are required, one is reluctant to gird oneself for a constitutional revision when only matters of subordinate importance are involved. One then prefers to interpret the letter of the existing articles in a somewhat different way, and to achieve, without revisions, by means of modified interpretation what would require too much effort in revision. Just think of the article on education. This then has the consequence that a certain number of desired revisions accumulate, and that eventually, at a more general revision, a clean slate is made. Groen van Prinsterer foresaw from the outset where this would lead. It then gives the appearance as if, time and again, say after half a century or a quarter century, a completely new order of things is established by the people, just as many still imagine that the constitutional order of things here was only established in 1848. This undermines the popular sense that the Constitution has remained one and the same from the outset until now, and that what has changed were merely modifications made to the one permanent State document. Much healthier is the view that one makes the necessary changes by a simpler way of revision on a few detail points at a time. But then it also goes without saying that the revision must be able to come about more smoothly, on a simpler footing and without too much effort. Otherwise, the partial change is constantly postponed, is even partly played off against the constitutional precept in practice, and this then naturally leads to taking all the postponed matters together after a certain period, and thus seeking salvation again in a general revision. If it were the case that one had only gone slightly beyond the just measure in such a rapid partial constitutional revision, then in this more expeditious handling of matters the means would also be provided to correct a mistake made and to go back the required step. §17. The Attitude of the Sovereign. Above all, one must not lose sight of the fact that the States General can never achieve their goal otherwise than through the intermediary of the King; and precisely now, where it is a struggle between two parts or groups of the people, the King is designated to watch over the common right of the people. A King who strongly opposes where an accord is at stake, fights for dynastic rights and acts, as one will, in an egoistic sense; something that never makes a happy impression and easily nourishes anti-dynastic sentiments in some group of the people. The bearer of the Crown will therefore always act more wisely by taking a neutral position on such points

Analytical Summary: The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,
[124]

112 THE CONSTITUTION. and by not allowing himself to be drawn into the party struggle, but by standing above the parties as the guardian of the Constitution and the common good. This is all the more necessary now that the parties have become so sharply opposed to each other. For the King, the Constitution is not a party program, but the permanent foundation of the State, which he has sworn to maintain. And precisely because the parties are so sharply opposed, the King must be all the more the unifying power that holds the State together. This does not mean that the King should not have his own political views and convictions, but that he should not allow himself to be drawn into the party struggle, but should stand above the parties as the guardian of the Constitution and the common good.

[125]

THE ATTITUDE OF THE SOVEREIGN - THE THREAT OF POPULAR UNREST.

If it comes to a revision of the Constitution, and the issue lies not between the people and the Sovereign, but between groups, parties, and classes within the people, then there is a case to be made for justice - that the Sovereign should defend the group that is too severely disadvantaged against the encroachment of competing groups. This could lead to a strengthening of a one-sided preponderance, and thus a partisan choice in the most unhealthy sense; but whereas such intervention by the Sovereign was not uncommon in earlier periods, since the Sovereigns then knew how to make one of the parties subservient to their own interests, there is no longer any fear of this in the current constitutional life. A Sovereign who now steps forward for the rights of the various groups and parties is not diminished in general esteem, but rather gives the impression of defending a higher right. As late as 1878, this was evident in the deputation under Elout van Soeterwoude at Het Loo. King William III then made it clear and unambiguous that he was not insensitive to what was lacking in justice in our Elementary Education Act, and the outcome crowned this expression of a sovereign sentiment by already leading public opinion in a different direction ten years later. And if one finally points to the revolutionary turmoil that can seek salvation in street riots in order to enforce the will of the common people, one must not lose sight of the fact that nothing can better curb this ever-recurring danger than a more flexible revision of the constitutional provisions.

§25. The Threat of Popular Unrest. If the revision of the Constitution is made too difficult, and it is thus postponed from year to year, and there is almost no redress in the ordinary way, this is precisely what so easily sets the popular passions in motion and provokes violence. To hold fast to the two distinct conditions - the one for the maintenance of the accord between people and Sovereign, but which must then also have a character of firmness, and the other for the maintenance of the proper connection between political life and the constitutional order - is the task that the Sovereign has to fulfill.

Analytical Summary: Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit
[126]

The constitutional form in which this finds expression determines, in my opinion, the standpoint from which one must approach the revision of the Constitution. If the first requirement is rigidity, and the second flexibility and suppleness, it already follows from this how almost impossible it is to unite both under the same point of view. If for the accord between People and Princes regarding the regulation of the rights of the people the same manner of revision applies, then the fateful consequence is that either the revision of the Constitution is made too difficult in order to nail down the accord, or the accord is too much weakened in order to better carry out the revision of the Constitution. The Conservatives were most guilty of the former evil. This gave rise to an ebb and flow, under which the dignity of the constitutional life was indeed damaged. The walls of the building must be firm, and only the doors and windows movable. Nothing has done as much damage to the constitutional certainty as the fact that accords and detailed regulations have been decided under the same law. In Great Britain, where the written document is lacking, but the mutual right is rooted in the spirit of the people, the constitutional life still stands the firmest and strongest. Small oscillations and shakings have certainly also occurred in England, but at least the building itself has not yet been shaken. On the European continent, on the other hand, and also with us, the general satisfaction over our constitutional coexistence has noticeably declined in the last quarter century. Complaints of an irreparable fiasco have already come from more than one side. Those who could now be blamed for this were not, as was previously very common, the princely abuse of power. Precisely over this one-sided evil, however, complaints have become increasingly rare. From the side of the Crown, a spirit has rather penetrated the land that testified to a warm interest in the fate of the people, to an effort to win the love of the people, and to an ever more scrupulous respect for the rights of the people. The dissatisfaction with the existing order has also turned less and less against the Crown. This was still partly the case in 1848. Not anymore now. Rather, the stains are now increasingly visible on the side of the people in the legal community. There is a tendency towards anarchy, almost every bond begins to hinder. Free as the little bird in the air we want to become. Chaotic phenomena are already emerging. Precisely for this reason, it seemed important to place more emphasis on the character of the Constitution, and at least to bring up a way out that could lead to reconciling and uniting the firmness and suppleness in the revision, which are now constantly damaging each other, in such a way that a healthy relationship between the two can finally discard all crutches. That this is possible is not in doubt, provided that the entire Constitution is stripped of all superfluous detailed regulations, i.e. the side lines are erased with the sponge, and one goes back to the main and fundamental lines, in order to express them in such simple, clear and logically thought-out language that the cacography disappears of itself. In this respect, too, our Constitution does not belong to the most fortunate. Although it can least of all unite us with the political principles from which the Belgian Constitution of 1830 proceeds, yet it may not be denied the praise that it remains a model in its language and style. Only by the way it may be noted here that in a division of the Constitution into two main parts, of which the first would contain the accord between People and Prince, and the second would lay down the details of the Constitution, the dividing line need not necessarily fall at Article 79 of our present Constitution. The distribution of the content over the different parts of our current Constitution would of course have to undergo modification.

[127]

HISTORY OF THE COUNCIL. 115

The Council of State, as it exists with us, dates from the Constitution of 1815. It is true that already in the Constitution of the Batavian Republic of 1798 a Council of State was provided for, but this was of a different nature. The Council of State, as it was then conceived, was intended to be a kind of executive committee, which would assist the Executive Directory in the administration of the country. The Constitution of 1815, on the other hand, gave the Council of State a completely different character. It was no longer an executive committee, but a purely advisory body, which had to assist the King in the exercise of his royal prerogatives. The Constitution of 1848 did not change this character of the Council of State, but only expanded its competence somewhat. The Constitution of 1815 had given the Council of State the task of advising the King on all matters of legislation and administration, as well as on the granting of pardons. The Constitution of 1848 added to this the task of advising on the appointment of the highest officials. The Council of State thus became a body that had to assist the King in the exercise of his royal prerogatives, both in the legislative and in the administrative field. This character of the Council of State has remained unchanged to this day.

[128]

CHAPTER IV
THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE

THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE. A republic in 1805, and even already under Charles V, had a nearly identically named body established, but these previously existing colleges had a different character, because they saw themselves as partly entrusted with the task that has since fallen to the Departmental Ministers. In 1805, therefore, alongside the Council of State, not ministers but Secretaries of State were appointed. See Articles 47 and 48. Article 47 provided for the appointment of a General Secretary of State, and Article 48 the functions of five Secretaries of State, for Foreign Affairs, for the Navy, for War, for the Interior, and for Finance. The Council of State, on the other hand, which was regulated by Articles 44, 45 and 46, seemed more like the Conseil d’État of the Burgundian Princes and the creation of similar colleges by Napoleon. In Articles 71—74 of the Constitution of 1815, a Council of State of a different character emerged, which was appointed by the King, would not have more than twenty-four members, preferably from the different Provinces, whose members the King could appoint and dismiss at will. The King himself would be the chairman of this Council, the Prince of Orange a member by right, and the other Princes of the Royal House could also be granted a seat in this high State body by the Sovereign. All legislative proposals came up for consideration in this Council, and the King sought the advice of this Council on all measures of domestic administration, both here and for the Colonies of the Kingdom. Furthermore, the King could ask this Council for advice on all State affairs that he considered desirable to have such advice on. However, the decisions were made by the King alone. The Council merely received notification of this decision. Only after this had been so regulated in Articles 71—74, did the establishment of the Ministerial Departments follow in Article 75; and in the second paragraph here it was stipulated that the King could call one or more Ministers into the Council of State. The three articles for the more detailed regulation of the ministerial powers and obligations were only inserted in 1840. It cannot be denied, therefore, that in 1815 the Council of State was very much superior to the Departmental Ministers. Even in the Constitution of 1848, the establishment of the Council of State in Section VII of Chapter II still preceded the establishment of the Departmental Ministers. Two articles (Articles 71 and 72) were even devoted to the establishment of the Council of State here, and only one to the establishment of the Ministers. The Princes now fell out, and only the Prince of Orange remained. The task of the college was now to advise on all proposals for laws and general administrative measures, both here and in the Colonies and possessions. The King could also elicit advice on other State affairs. It remained the case that the King alone decided and merely communicated his decision to the Council. Just as in 1815, it was stipulated that the “having heard the Council of State” should appear in the title of every law. Furthermore, the law was also to regulate the entire constitution of the Council of State. In 1887 all this remained the same, but now a new addition was made in Article 76, that a law could entrust the decision on disputes to the Council of State or to a particular section of this Body. The more detailed regulation of one and the other has been effected by the Acts of 21 December 1861, 28 June 1881, 11 July 1884, and 8 December 1906, and by the Royal Decrees of 4 September 1862, 29 March 1875, 8 December 1877, 16 November 1881, 13 October 1905 and 28 March 1907 pursuant to these Acts. A separate Section was now established for Administrative Disputes, so that in the end the Administrative Jurisdiction also found its regulation.

§2. The three extra cases. The entirely separate task assigned to the Council of State for the three cases described in Article 45 lies entirely outside of this and is included in Chapter II, Section 4, dealing with the Regency. Article 38 regulates what must be done by the heads of the Ministerial Departments in the matter of the Regency; but when the exercise of the Royal Authority becomes unavoidable in the absence of the Bearer, it is not the Cabinet, but the Council of State that steps in, and it is not the least of this assignment that has stamped a seal of very high quality on the college. Just as throughout the past, so also from this assignment to the eventual exercise of the Royal Authority, it appears that the Council of State stands in very close connection with the Crown. Something that also applies elsewhere.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[129]

THE THREE EXTRA CASES. 117 Professor Bluntschli in his Allgemeines Staatsrecht rightly points out that the Council of State in the Netherlands has a special position compared to the Councils of State in other countries. This special position is mainly due to the three extra cases mentioned in Article 45, in which the Council of State is called upon to act. These three cases are: 1. When the King is a minor; 2. When the King is incapacitated; 3. When the throne is vacant. In these three cases, the Council of State does not merely advise, but actually exercises the Royal Authority. This is a very special task, which gives the Council of State a completely different character than the usual advisory body. It is not just a consultative body, but in these three cases it is the governing body. This is why the Council of State in the Netherlands has a much more prominent position than the Councils of State in other countries. The fact that the Council of State is called upon to exercise the Royal Authority in these three cases shows how closely it is connected to the Crown. This close connection with the Crown is also evident from the fact that the King himself is the chairman of the Council of State, and that the Princes of the Royal House have a seat in it by right. All this gives the Council of State in the Netherlands a very special character, which sets it apart from the Councils of State in other countries.

[130]

THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE. The State Law, 6th edition, page 267, even goes so far as to say: “The Council of State is thus an institution for free reflection, for... the wise deliberation around the King, just as the Chambers have to represent the interests and opinions of the people”; and on page 270: “The Council of State is the examining and advising intellect of the head of state.”

§3. Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[131]

WEAKENING OF SIGNIFICANCE - CABINET AND COUNCIL. Only in a later period did this high college also include statesmen from Anti-Revolutionary or non-liberal Roman Catholic backgrounds. The nature of the college entailed that it possessed a more lasting character than could be attributed to most Cabinets, and that it thereby represented more the general course of political life. The ministers came and went, continually varying in their efforts and intentions. In contrast to this variability in Cabinet life, the Crown Bearer represented the permanent and as much as possible unchanging, and only in this permanent could the Council of State provide support. Now, on the other hand, as the composition of the Council of State began to suffer under ministerial pressure along with the constant change inseparable from the spirit of the Cabinets, the Council increasingly ceased to possess a character of its own, and, though at a certain distance, continually followed the ebb and flow in Cabinet life. The original intention behind the Council’s actions, to uphold the permanent, characteristic nature of the royal authority against the ever-changing in Cabinet life, was thus increasingly lost.

§4. Cabinet and Council.*

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.

The original independence diminished. And since the advice of the Council was not made public, nor the response of the Ministers to the Council’s criticism, it could not but happen that in the ministerial bureaus less and less attention was paid to the advice received from the Council. By virtue of its one-sided composition, the Council was generally unable to issue other than broadly liberal advice, with the peculiar mark that almost always, when the drafts tended in a more radical direction, the criticism of the Council pushed them back towards a more conservative line. When such a tempering opinion came from the King’s side to the Department, the Department’s immediate effort was to set it aside as very little to the point and as insignificant in a briefly written counter-opinion.

[132]

THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE.

to set aside. This does not mean that the Council did not repeatedly point out technical errors or omissions, and that corrections were not often recommended that were gratefully adopted, but the political character of the advice remained without effect. Whether the majority of the Council, or of the Section under which the bill in question fell, differed from the minister who had prepared the bill, was known in advance. It was also known in advance whose judgment the advice would be drawn up. These members of the Council were well known in the Department, and their political views were therefore by no means in the dark. So the Department knew just as certainly in advance in what spirit the advice would turn out, what would be approved and what would be rejected, and what they would wish to put in place of what was rejected. The Department was also fully conversant with the arguments for and against that would be brought forward. The consequence of this was that the incoming advice could not surprise in any respect; that one could be almost certain in advance of what would be noted; and that one was immediately ready with one’s own criticism, in order to refute and invalidate the criticism of the Council. When the advice arrived, an official was immediately appointed to disarm it. Often in a couple of weeks the counter-criticism was ready as an apology for the bill. This apology was then sent to the Crown, and brought to the knowledge of the Council of State, and all the purposeless debate died away here. Of course, the Council of State was fully aware of all this. To know in advance that the study devoted to such a bill will almost certainly be entirely fruitless labor, naturally does not stimulate to special exertion. He who is so certain in advance that his counter-advice will be given little value; that it will only come under the eyes of very few; and further, after being rejected, will move to the archives just as unused and unread by the public, does what he does out of a sense of duty, but does not sharpen his talents. The sad truth remained, that the obtaining of the advice of the Council of State both forced this Council and then the Department into a critical debate in the dark, of which the real fruit often failed to appear, and in which

[133]

LOSS OF DISTINCTIVE CHARACTER.121 The complaint about the wasteful loss of time cannot always be avoided. An already somewhat unpleasant relationship, which often took on an even more unpleasant character, if it later turned out that the members of the Council who were called upon to give advice, despite all their other excellence, were not experts on the particular subject that was the subject of the legislative proposal submitted to them. In the past, this caused less difficulty, insofar as the draft laws submitted for advice dealt, if not exclusively, then at least primarily, with subjects of constitutional and financial administration. But since the administration has had to increasingly delve into highly technical subjects of a highly specialized nature, it has not infrequently happened that among the members of the Council the special expert who could provide the necessary enlightenment on that special point was lacking. Just think of the insurance system, which of course requires a completely special study, and imagine how strange it must have been for a member of the Council to find himself confronted with a draft of two hundred or more articles, regulating a matter that was almost foreign to him, and lying before him in a draft drawn up by experts. One cannot reproach the thus embarrassed Council member for this. For the composition of the Council is not geared to such highly specialized subjects; and the effort that many a member of the Council has nevertheless made to make up for the deficiency in the composition of his college by redoubling his efforts cannot be praised highly enough.

§5. Loss of Distinctive Character. The Council of State, as it now functions among us, lacks a distinctive character. In the task entrusted to the Council, there is a lack of unity of thought. On the one hand, it gives the impression of superfluity, and on the other hand, of excessive meddling. What Napoleon intended in his Conseil d’État was to provide an improved copy of what had arisen under the Burgundian Princes and reached its highest significance under Charles V. Alongside, and to some extent even above the departmental ministers, Napoleon sought in

Analytical Summary: Allies.
