This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[134]

THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE. The Council of State was the thinking body that advised it, and to whose counsel the departmental ministers added the deed. In our view, in 1814/15 they set themselves a completely different goal, and sought in the Council of State a shield for the Dynasty. Not in an unjust form, as if to bargain away the rights of the people, but still to ensure for the Sovereign Prince, soon the King, an advisory college as a bulwark against the dubious stirrings from below; something not entirely unlike the Privy Council in England. From what the Batavian Republic had dared, they very well understood that the departmental ministers must increasingly come under popular influence, and that it was therefore of the highest importance for the Prince to be able to dispose of a College of acknowledged eminent Statesmen, who could watch over the preservation of the balance between the two powers in the State. But precisely this has led to increasingly limiting the significance of the Council of State, to virtually undermine the independent position of this Council, and thus of itself to raise the question of what it was really for. Compared to the all-controlling position of the Departmental Ministers, the position of the Council could not but decline, and this naturally gave rise to the creation of an entirely new function for the Council in administrative jurisdiction, in order to make the significance of the Council perceptible from that side. The original intention to be a shield for the King was increasingly lost, so that it became a ministerial crisis convention, not that the Crown sought the advice and counsel of the Council of State, but that it called upon the chairmen of the two Houses to give unofficial advice. Even the originally so correct idea, that the King, who in his abiding person represented the unity of government against the ever-changing ministries, should have at his disposal a college of high order of experts, which would also have a permanent character against the changes of the Cabinet, was completely lost. As a college of advice on matters of state, the Council of State has hardly ever been of service; its influence on legislation has become increasingly insignificant; and undoubtedly administrative jurisdiction and the temporary bearing of royal authority under Article 45 are now its main significance.

[135]

ADMINISTRATIVE JURISDICTION. 123

§6. Administrative Jurisdiction. Now it is granted that this last, entirely exceptional function fits in a very peculiar way with a Council such as the one dealt with here. Under normal circumstances, the Council would never be called upon to act in this matter. If there is a successor to the throne, if it is necessary to entrust a Regent, and if at the moment of the Prince’s demise, in the first case the Prince or Princess, and in the other case the Regent or Regentess, are present on the spot, the Council of State sees itself called to no act or deed. Only in abnormal circumstances, if there is no successor to the throne, or if such a Regent is absent, would the Council of State have to intervene. Such intervention now forms an essential part of the task that has been laid upon the President of the Council, but in the ordinary course of events the cases to which this task refers hardly ever occur. It would never have been thought of to call such a college into being solely for this purpose. Elsewhere, in more than one country where no Council of State has been established, the possibility has nevertheless been reckoned with that such a case of embarrassment might arise, and it has then been entrusted to the Council of Ministers what with us fell to the Council of State. Now this is not quite the same. It must be admitted that the Council of State, which from the outset had the purpose of raising a shield for the Crown, leans more peculiarly towards exercising the royal authority than the Council of Ministers; but even if we are willing to emphasize this, it would not do to maintain such a costly college mainly with a view to such a case, which is almost never likely to occur. It calls for much more definite and extensive work; but for such a certainly highly necessary jurisdiction, a Council of State such as we possess does not seem to be the most peculiar and self-evident college.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.
[136]

THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE.

§7. How it is organized elsewhere

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how

In Switzerland, a state even smaller than the Netherlands, a separate college has been established for this jurisdiction in the Tribunal fédéral (see Articles 106–114 of the Constitution). This Tribunal rules on disputes between the Confederation and the Cantons, between the Cantons themselves, and also on disputes between the Confederation or Cantons with corporations or individuals. Furthermore, this Tribunal can also adjudicate in any other type of dispute if both parties request it to do so. It is also entrusted with jurisdiction over “conflicts of competence” between national and cantonal authorities; over public-law disputes between Cantons; and over complaints by individuals regarding the violation of their public and constitutional rights. Even the federal legislation, by virtue of Articles 110 and 113 of the Constitution, is empowered to assign other conflicts to this Tribunal for adjudication. Although the scope of this task does not quite match what falls under our administrative jurisdiction, in principle and in its starting point one finds here as well a regulation of administrative jurisdiction, but one that, departing from an independent college specifically established for this purpose, is added to a college of a completely different origin and character; and even then, not assigned to the entire college, but only to a specific department of it. So in other countries as well, recourse has been sought in the establishment of an independent college. Consider, for example, the Reichsgericht in Austria, which was called into being by the Constitutional Law of December 21, 1867 and established in Vienna. Article 2 of this Law stipulates that the jurisdiction of this Reichsgericht includes competence disputes between judicial and administrative authorities, between the representation of the People and the magistrates in the various provinces, between the local authorities, and also over disputes between municipalities, corporations, or individuals with the authorities of various ranks. Now certainly the example of Austria as one of the Great Powers would not allow a conclusion for a country as much smaller as ours; but precisely to dispel this objection...

[137]

ADMINISTRATIVE JURISDICTION AND AUTONOMY. To cut this off, one first points to Switzerland, where the population is still far behind ours. Add to this Belgium, right on our border, where even the Council of State is lacking, and further argument can be spared to show that an administrative court or another college could also act for us. Even the Beroepswet [Appeals Act] already offers a certain analogy.

§7. Administrative Jurisdiction and Autonomy. What speaks even more strongly in this regard is that the Council of State, by virtue of its origin, can least of all be regarded as the designated college for administrative jurisdiction. Between the authorities of higher and lower rank, between those authorities and both public and private associations and private individuals, disputes and conflicts constantly arise, in which the question always arises as to what extent the municipalities, provinces, polders and so many others have or do not have a certain independent position. The autonomy of local and regional character is a matter of the utmost delicacy, and in the care of which two different opinions have long stood opposed to each other here at home. On the one hand, the excessive fragmentation of the state, and on the other hand, a strong centralization spirit. The former has been at home here for a long time, the latter gained the upper hand in France, especially during the Revolution, and has also come to us from Paris and gained dominance in the days of the Batavian Republic. And although this all-solving spirit of centralization was thwarted here after 1814, it cannot be denied that our Liberals have always shown a poorly concealed preference for that centralization, and that the autonomous life, even in our polder administrations, has always been curtailed by them. That the professor Oppenheim took up the cause of municipal autonomy again with love and faithfulness may elicit our gratitude, but it can hardly be denied that, to avoid difficulties, one has always been more inclined here to let the autonomous character of municipality and province suffer, if that could facilitate the orderly course of administration.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[138]

126 THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE. Appointments of mayors, which from of old, even now in Austria, belonged to the municipalities, but have been assigned here to the central authority, and for a very long period were the means and instrument of the liberal leaders to strengthen their influence in the countryside. Even the complete abolition of the independence of the municipalities has almost become the rule here. While even in Brussels the suburbs retained their independent character, so that there are still separate municipal councils not only for the center, but also for the suburbs, it has become almost the rule here that as soon as a large city demands the annexation of entire villages under its administration, this desire is granted. Insofar as this tendency of the Central Administration can of itself lead to conflicts with that Administration with the local and provincial authorities and between these and other corporations and persons, it is of course highly desirable that in the college that will judge these conflicts, not only the centralizing, but also the autonomy-minded element in the popular spirit should come to its right. This has also been taken into account in the establishment of the Reichsgericht in Austria as well as in the establishment of the Tribunal fédéral in Switzerland, and in the composition of that Court and Tribunal, one-sidedness has also been avoided.

§9. The Council is unfit for this. One might therefore have expected that the administration of justice in conflicts of this nature would be entrusted either to a college dominated by the autonomous, or to a college in which both the centralizing and the autonomy-minded element were equally represented. But the current regulation among us does not meet this requirement. The inherent character of the Council of State, by virtue of its origins and past, has always leaned one-sidedly towards the side of the Crown, whose shield is its vocation. And although the later regulation of the Constitution and the law has not entirely disowned this original character, but has considerably weakened it, in no case can it be claimed that the Council of State is a college in which attachment to the autonomous has the same weight as attachment to the centralizing.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[139]

THESE COUNCILS ARE UNSUITABLE.127 In our local, provincial, and corporate life, they were naturally represented. The modern State idea, which does not aim to allow a national life that has arisen from within to ripen in a more organic connection, but rather imagines that the power lies within it to create a political form for the national life, is increasingly gliding over the earlier antithesis between autonomous local life and its central integration. A separate administrative and legal college, established specifically for this purpose, even if it came into being with the cooperation of the autonomous life, would at least have intended to provide a certain counterweight. As it is now regulated with us, however, it cannot be otherwise—the preference for the spirit of centralization will rather continue to push back the autonomous life even further. In fact, there are now only a very small number of gentlemen and jurists, who are often deliberately appointed with a view to the Division of Disputes, who have the entire swell of the autonomous against the central principle in their power. The purpose for which the Council of State was originally called into being among us, the further regulation that this Council received in 1861, 1881 and 1884, gave the Council a composition and a character that would have provided full guarantee if the autonomy were to run wild and try to do damage to the central character of the supreme authority, but in which, under the present regulation, almost every guarantee is lacking for maintaining in our state law what the principle of autonomy demands. In this there is not the slightest suspicion that there would be an intentional bias in the Division of Disputes to disturb the proper balance between the central and the autonomous principle. Deliberate and intended partiality need not be thought of at all in this Division; rather, one is assured that the arrival of Professor Oppenheim in this Council offers the autonomy even more chances; what was meant to be shown is only that the Council of State, according to its origin in 1815, and according to the further regulation it received as a result of the constitutional form of 1848, does not give us what first and foremost comes to the fore in a college of administrative jurisdiction.

[140]

THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE.

§10. The second point demands no less. The second task assigned to the Council of State, to oversee the legislation and the executive power, leads to an equally unsatisfactory conclusion. The regulations for this task are given in Articles 20–26 of the Act of 1861, with the later amendments made therein. Art. 20 merely states the commission given to the Council by Art. 45 of the Constitution, and the subsequent articles stipulate: 1° that all legislative proposals, whether originating from the Crown or the States-General, are to be submitted to the Council for consideration; 2° that all general measures of domestic administration for the State and its Colonies and possessions in other parts of the world are to be submitted to the Council; 3° that the Council is to be heard by the Crown regarding the annulment of decisions of the Provincial and Deputed States, as well as of local ordinances; 4° that the Council is to be heard by the Crown in the cases where the Law commands it; 5° that the Council is to be heard on all matters of general or particular importance which the Crown deems necessary; 6° that the Council can make recommendations to the Crown regarding subjects of legislation or administration, insofar as it deems the issuance of general measures of domestic administration desirable; and 7° that the Sections of the Council are to provide information, when requested, regarding administration and legislation to the Ministers. In short, this amounts to a certain control over the departmental activities, both in what they accomplish and in what they are deemed to devise. That this is intended to fulfill a very real need cannot be disputed. There must be, in legislation and administration, a certain connection between what is accomplished in successive periods of government. Now that the Cabinets change and not infrequently one appears to undo the work of its predecessor, the danger undoubtedly arises that the Cabinet changes disrupt the coherence of the administration. Since a Minister can take office who is less familiar with the secrets of administration, what emanates from the Ministers can become unmistakably lacking in solidity and coherence.

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.
[141]

COUNCIL OF MINISTERS. The influence of the Ministers has steadily increased in significance, and yet they are often regarded in the Departments as lodges that come and go without often leaving many traces of their ministerial tenure. More than once it has been pointed out how this has sometimes even given some departmental officials an influence on the course of the country’s affairs that does not rightfully belong to them. Since such officials often stand at the head of an important division for a long series of years, their gradual accumulation of knowledge of the matters handled, at least in the details, far exceeds the familiarity of the Ministers with this subject matter. This can cause a Minister, who in his past has been completely outside the administration, to readily go along with proposals, ideas, and expressions of his officials, which are in themselves far from impeccable. The Secretary-General does then exercise some oversight, but even he cannot fully master the scope of the departmental output.

§11. The Council of Ministers. And the Council of Ministers can also exercise some control, but over a busy Department the production of documents is often so extensive that the control of this Council of Ministers can only be very superficial. A Minister whose Department has an excessive workload and who is therefore constantly having to appear before the States-General cannot read and assess with the required accuracy everything that comes under his eye from the other Departments. This is especially impossible in this country, because the custom persists of providing all legislative proposals with such extensive Memoranda that keeping up with the reading of all the documents that accumulate year after year becomes absolutely impossible. Whoever examines the stacks of documents with any attentiveness, which pile up anew each year, immediately understands that a Minister who would want to process this entire stack would cut off all possibility of own production for himself. And in addition to this, the Council of Ministers cannot meet so frequently, as this would become too time-consuming, and furthermore, in the Council, which is mostly silent...

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[142]

The Council of State and the Ministers of State—It is a serious defect that the meetings of the Council of State, which last only two hours, bring up so many new topics that it becomes utterly impossible to discuss any incoming document in the Council in detail and to investigate its particulars.

§12. Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[143]

The terms have been neglected in our Constitution. This has been shown in particular in the First Part, regarding the use of the words: “Realm,” “Country,” and “People” in our Constitution. We have therefore the least reason to boast about our official literature. French lends itself to official language in a surprisingly flexible way, German always folds where French offers rigid, even material, and English gives legal style in solid chunks. Our language, on the other hand, often lends itself in no other way than in a very clumsy manner to express the desired thought concisely. Hence, in so many legal documents, an administrative terminology and a way of speaking that seems to be minted on the unintelligible. Just listen to the taxpayers constantly being perplexed by the unintelligible language of the tax assessments. Without reservation, it must be confessed that no country as much as ours is in need of a factual and formal control and enlightenment of all legislative, executive, and administrative documents. The criticism we have allowed ourselves on what the Council of State provides in the matter of Legislation and Administrative Measures is by no means intended to plead the idea that control could well be missed here; but, on the contrary, to come out for the fact that the control now existing does not suffice. Whoever, like the writer of these lines, has had the opportunity in recent years to closely examine the documents in which this control was offered, cannot for a moment hesitate about this. Not as if in the intended Control Memoranda there was not more than once an effective indication of committed mistakes, even of direct errors, and as if, with each comprehensive draft, what the coherence of the articles and the connection with other laws demanded, was not often pointed out in a sufficient manner; but the criticism of the document gave no other control than that such a draft of a law also encounters in the sections of the States General, and is then inserted in the Preliminary Report. What an expert member of the States General brings up in the general considerations on such drafts or on individual articles, seems like two drops of water to what the Council of State offers, without the former being able to read his own speech literally in the States General, without any particular merit.

[144]

THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE. Another person might also think of the illegitimate birth of this piece. If the draft laws as they have passed the Council of Ministers go straight to the Chambers, then, grosso modo, almost everything that now comes to him from the Council of State will be found in the Chamber report for the Minister. It is a very small number of members of the Council of State who draw up the draft advisory opinion for each bill, and in bills of political importance, it is almost always to be expected that the majority in the Section or Investigative Commission are of the same political persuasion. Although it is therefore that the Council of State puts the Ministers in a position to correct some formal errors, over which they would otherwise have been hard hit in the States General, and although the advice can give him occasion to complete a less clear argument,—the advice is generally not of decisive importance. The Minister is not made wiser by it, and the bill does not gain in perfection through it.

§13. Waste of time without purpose.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.

One is therefore confronted with the undeniable fact that a high college of state has often spent too much time on investigating and issuing an opinion that hardly yields any positive gain, and that, on the other hand, the careful control that should have come, fails to materialize. Legislative work is not everyone’s work. The style of laws and the language of the law make quite peculiar demands. The theory of the method for the drafting of laws is not known to those who have not studied or learned it. A busy Minister lacks the time at first to draw up important draft laws himself. He must receive the material for this from the official, but the official gives the elaboration of the draft. And when the elaborated piece comes to the Minister, but in such a way that it must be completely reworked as regards form, wording, style and language, there is no question of the Minister being able to bring this into readiness himself. For this, all time is lacking. Whoever imagines this, does not know the ministerial life. In Thorbecke’s days, something like this was still partly possible, because the departmental pressure at the Ministry of the Interior at the time was not a third of what it is now. In Belgium, they have therefore adopted the custom of appointing two senior officials at each Department who are solely concerned with the proper drafting of the drafts; and that this already helps to some extent in the right direction, is evident from the production. However, this aid is not sufficient. As far as the entire legislation of a country is concerned, the supreme authority of a few leading spirits in matter, style and form must here intervene; and these must, for the whole of their service, have no other task than to maintain the majesty of legislation even in its form. They must have auxiliary personnel at their disposal for all kinds of detailed research. And they must even have the authority to consult experts of the first rank on subjects of a very special nature. Only in this way can it be prevented that the unity of legislation would become uncertain; that the connection with the legislation of the past would become loose; that the conception of the draft laws would mock a good architectural style; that through careless speech in the law, the terminology in our legislation would approach chaos; and that the legal style would be deprived of its soul through arbitrariness in usage. In England, they have followed this rule from of old, and they have found it good. To come out for or against this or that political tendency would of course have to lie entirely outside the vocation of such a College. The members of such a College therefore need not be politically neutral personalities at all. Such neutral-minded people generally have little genius. Only, they should have strictly refrained from anything related to the party struggle in their investigations and in the drafting of their advisory opinions; their task would be to watch over the legislative forms for that form alone.

§14. Yet give advice, but freely.

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”

Should it now follow from this that the Council of State could be relieved of giving advice on the outgoing documents? Not in the least. Even if a Conseil d’État in the old sense is unthinkable in our country, the Council of State must nevertheless retain the right to give advice on all important bills and royal decrees. This advice, however, must be completely free, without any regard for the political tendencies of the day. The Council of State must be able to say what it considers to be good or bad, useful or harmful, in the draft, without any consideration for the political color of the Cabinet. And the Minister, in turn, must be able to accept or reject this advice without regard to the political complexion of the Council of State. Only in this way can the Council of State fulfill its true task, which is to be the guardian of the majesty of the law and the unity of legislation. The Council of State must be able to speak freely, without fear or favor, and the Minister must be able to listen to it freely, without fear or favor. Only then will the Council of State be able to fulfill its true function.

[145]

WASTE OF TIME WITHOUT PURPOSE. - YET GIVE ADVICE, BUT FREELY. 133

in our country, the Council of State must nevertheless retain the right to give advice on all important bills and royal decrees. This advice, however, must be completely free, without any regard for the political tendencies of the day. The Council of State must be able to say what it considers to be good or bad, useful or harmful, in the draft, without any consideration for the political color of the Cabinet. And the Minister, in turn, must be able to accept or reject this advice without regard to the political complexion of the Council of State. Only in this way can the Council of State fulfill its true task, which is to be the guardian of the majesty of the law and the unity of legislation. The Council of State must be able to speak freely, without fear or favor, and the Minister must be able to listen to it freely, without fear or favor. Only then will the Council of State be able to fulfill its true function.

[146]

The Council of State and the Ministers of State. This in no way diminished the necessity for the King to ensure that he receives the necessary guidance in the decisions he must make. What must be abandoned is not the giving of advice per se, but the formal exercise of control over all outgoing documents, however insignificant, as is still required by Article 72 of the Constitution. A purely formal chamber of control, in the genre of our Court of Audit, that examines the legislative text, the style of legislation, and the form of the law, even in connection with existing laws, and scrutinizes the form, is certainly desirable. Even in the smallest and most insignificant draft, the required form may be lacking; and how important each point of form and expression can be, was evident only recently, when the system of cremation, contrary to the undoubted intention of the law, was in fact admitted by the Supreme Court, solely on the ground of a defect in the form of the law. There can never be any question of neglect of form in the legislative domain. Any omission here can cause the most serious confusion. But it does not at all follow from this that a body as high-ranking as the Council of State has to watch over this. More than once a completely insignificant legislative text has sounded: “The Council of State having been heard,” as if in mockery, and it is undoubtedly beneath the dignity of the college. What must remain, however, is that all draft bills and measures of general administration by the Council of Ministers are sent to the States General for their cognizance; and then it is the Council of State itself that has to decide whether or not to advise on the matter. It could then be obliged to communicate to the Departmental heads within an expressly determined period whether or not the draft can be submitted to the States General without further delay; and only if the draft submitted to the Council gave rise to advice to the King would the Departmental heads need to await further communication. This in itself would not at all imply that the Council would have to insist on substantive or formal modification of the draft. Often it would be very good if the Council, after careful consideration, decided to refrain from all pressure for criticism of the draft. The first communication to the Department would then also

[147]

THE PRESIDENCY OF THE KING.135 Whether this means that the draft was allowed to pass undisturbed, or that it was held for further deliberation. The outcome of this further deliberation could then have led to the conclusion that amendments were urged on one or more points, or that the further deliberation led to refraining from any criticism of the proposal. The holding in deliberation would then have had the sole purpose of examining whether, and if so, to what extent, it seemed advisable to point out to the King the scope or significance of the draft, in connection with the continuity of government, and also in connection with the King’s national responsibility.

§15. The Presidency of the King. Understood in this way, the composition of the Council brings the presidency of the King and the participation of Princes of the Royal House to its right. The King is not the Chairman of the Council of Ministers, but appoints one of them to preside over the Council. Nor does the King preside over the Chambers of the States-General. Both Chambers of the States-General have a chairman who is directly appointed by the King for the First Chamber, but for the Second Chamber is effectively chosen by the members themselves. A regulation that provides for presidential assistance in the event of the temporary absence of the appointed Chairman, as the rules of order provide for this. A further designation that may result in a meeting in which a very important decision is made, completely helping itself and acting entirely outside the direct connection with the Crown. It is therefore very carefully guarded that the chairmanship in the Second Chamber does not lean too much towards the side of the Crown, and formally emanates from the Crown, but still has its actual origin and sometimes even acts entirely outside the Crown. With the Council of State, on the other hand, this is quite different. This Council itself makes no proposal regarding the presidency. The presidency here comes about entirely outside the Council. It is self-evident. Inaugurated, the King is here in person the Chairman. And he can appoint and does appoint

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[148]

THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE.

He is a vice-president, but this Vice-President remains an ordinary member, whose chairmanship is only in the absence of the Chairman, entirely dependent on whether this absence is the result of the death of the King, while the heir to the Crown, who comes after him, has not yet acted, or only the absence is to be noted, because the King did not appear in the Council, nor summoned him to his palace. So and not otherwise is the official appearance of the Body in its connection to its Head. There is absolutely no question of the appointment of the Head here. The Head is there spontaneously. But precisely with a view to this completely exceptional relationship, the contrast now strikes all the more, that officially or unofficially no one counts this Royal presidency, and passes it by as merely nominal. In so decisive a sense has this chairmanship of the King become purely nominal, that the Vice-President does not even think of obtaining the King’s pleasure before a meeting is held, or whether He Himself will appear, or leave the presidency to him. This leaving of the chairmanship to the Vice-President has become such a fixed custom and rule, that, the vicein his title now set aside, the Vice-President always behaves as if he were the actual chairman. In other areas, there sometimes exists such a looser relationship between the Chairman and an Honorary Chairman, to whom the President can now and then offer the gavel. But even that looser relationship does not exist here. With the Council of State, one has to do with the undeniable fact that the genuine, the real Chairman never appears, never takes the gavel in hand, and without any further notice or communication, leaves it to the Vice-Chairman to function as the actual Chairman; not once or a few times, but as a rule, and one can say, always. Not as if a new bearer of the Crown would not have the fuller right to appear in person and take the gavel in his own hand. Formally, the chairmanship of the King is here absolutely absolute, and on not a single point could it be contested to him. But this is precisely the striking contrast that one encounters here, that to the King in the most absolute sense a function accrues, which is never fulfilled by himself, but always by another in his name.

[149]

LOSING ITS ORIGINAL CHARACTER.

§16. Losing its original character.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.

This can only be explained by the fact that the Council of State has gradually been made something that is constantly losing its original character; and this special character consisted in the fact that the Council of State was something separate for the King and for his benefit, not in a personal sense, but for his benefit as the reigning Sovereign. The Council of State is therefore accountable to no one but the Sovereign. No public is made of the deliberations in the Council of State. The documents that emanate from the Council of State have a private character. Of course, it is assumed here that neither administrative jurisdiction nor legislative control will belong to its task, just as both have never been counted among them before. If the Council of State gives no other advice than to the King, then the documents that are drawn up by the Council of State also remain documents of a confidential nature between the King and him. In itself, the King would not even be obliged to bring the drawn-up documents to the knowledge of the ministers. This does not mean that the King would not usually do this, but in itself it would be very conceivable that the King himself, as chairman, would consult the Council around him, also with a view to the attitude of one or more of his ministers. Now it has been more directed that the King holds no personal consultation other than with his ministers, but conversely it would be very conceivable that the King would feel the need to receive the opinion of expert, high-ranking statesmen about the attitude of his ministers and about what was to be done in this regard. And although it is undisputed that the King is at all times in a position to consult one or more of these statesmen in confidence, it is in the nature of the matter that a college specially chosen with care would be much better able to provide the King with all the desired information.

[150]

THE PRIVY COUNCIL AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE.

§17. Consistently Confidential Character. This college or this Council also continued to have a confidential character, and could even become intimate. This is most clearly expressed in the appointment of the Privy Council in England, as well as in the title of the “Geheimräthe” in Germany, or in what is called the “secret council” in various German states. It is not the ministers who need this Council, nor even the States-General;—the need for such a Council, if properly constructed, exists only for the King, and partly for the Princes of his House. Only with this conception does the Royal Chairmanship come into its own right. In Austria they spoke of a “Hofrat,” in Brandenburg of a “geheimer Staatsrat,” in smaller states of a “Geheim Kabinet”; and the functioning of these secret colleges always came out strongest where the Ruler was intent on strengthening his own power and pushing back the significance of Parliament or the Estates in general. Hence it became necessary to call into being, alongside and in opposition to these secret councils or conseils d’états, the departmental ministers, whose responsibility to both Prince and Parliament ultimately first held back and then completely prevented any overgrowth of princely power over the liberties and rights of the people. But what could not and should not fall away was a council of a secret or, if one wishes, of a private character, which advised the King on the attitude he should adopt, on all kinds of occasions, for the benefit of his people and for the safeguarding of his royal authority. Even in 1879 a “Staatsrat” was still established for Alsace-Lorraine as a Reich province. In Prussia, Bavaria and Saxony too one finds a “Staatsrat,” while in Württemberg the name “Geheimer Rat” was retained. However, the true character of these colleges was gradually disappearing there as well. The responsible ministers, the Parliaments with decisive powers, and the entire constitutional system that was increasingly asserting itself, deprived these Secret Councils or State Councils of all significance in the field of legislation and administration, over which they had previously disposed. The course of events in the Privy Council in England was not much different, although it had also acquired some judicial powers.

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the
[151]

EXTRAORDINARY STATE COUNCILS.

§18. Extraordinary State Councils. The attempt to strengthen the College by appointing extraordinary state councillors, however well-intentioned, in order to obtain the advice of a special expert on matters of a special character, proved equally ineffective. Such an appointment was increasingly becoming a mere honorary title. The complete abolition of the Councils of State was the entirely natural consequence of this constant decline in significance, as for example in the Belgian constitution. Where there was fear of abolition, work has since been found for such a Council, and it has been assigned tasks that it could or could not fulfill, or in which it wasted its efforts in vain. The honorary titulature that was made of the German “Geheimrat” and our “Staatsraden in buitengewonen dienst” stood in the end completely on a par with the higher-ranking knight’s crosses; something that applies to our “Ministers of State” as well.

Analytical Summary: A Happy Turn.
[152]

140 THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE.

§19. Ministers of State. A Minister of State is now to be understood as nothing more than the honoring of a prominent man as an expert in the general affairs of the State. There are three kinds of ministers: first, the active departmental ministers, appointed for a specific group of state affairs; secondly, the non-active ministers, honored as experts in state affairs in a general sense; and thirdly, plenipotentiary Ministers to act on behalf of the Crown with the government of a foreign country. Thorbecke now incorrectly combined the title of Minister in the first two meanings, when in official documents he called himself “Minister of State and of the Interior”; a combination that completely obliterated the distinction between activity and non-activity. When someone is called a “Minister of State,” this contains nothing more than a declaration by the King, on the advice of his Cabinet, that this honorary title is due to him on account of his significance in the general politics of the country. There lies a declaration, no calling to any service or task. The title of Minister of the Interior, on the other hand, is not a declaration nor the granting of an honorary title, but indicates the calling to fulfill an assigned task. The word “Minister” in the first case is a mere appearance, for a “Minister of State” performs nothing. No task is assigned to him. Nor does he receive any honorarium or pension. On the other hand, “Minister” for the man of the Interior is no mere appearance, but the real expression of the relationship in which he sees himself placed towards the King. He is truly the King’s servant, as the Latin word minister also means nothing but servant. In the Old Testament, our translators have used the expression: chosen servant, which is also the most common meaning of the Hebrew word ‘ebed. Now a Minister in Latin had exactly the same meaning. It was used for him who was invested with a high state office, but also for the household servant or for him who was appointed as an attendant to a young boy. A more precise distinction could lead to the conclusion that in a revision of the Authorized Version the very significant difference between “servant” and “minister” should be applied. In no case, however, can one speak of a “Minister of State and of Foreign Affairs” in one breath. Appearances cannot be equated. It should therefore have been “the Minister of State Thorbecke, Minister of the Interior,” in the same sense as the honorary titles of Knight, Esquire, Baron and Count are placed before the name.

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.
[153]

MINISTERS OF STATE - HONORARY TITLES. 141 But leaving this aside, the distinction conferred on someone by an appointment as Minister of State can, upon closer inspection, be no other meaning than that of a mere honorary title. An extraordinary Councilor of State can still be called upon in a special case, but a Minister of State does nothing in his capacity; and nothing would be lost to the country if this entire title were to disappear. It therefore depends solely on how one judges distinctions and honorary titles in general. The Union has not introduced them in America, and Switzerland has remained just as foreign to them. And in our old Republic there was only the bestowal of a golden chain, which a renowned admiral or general received to wear around his neck. But then the honored general or statesman was not included in an order or rank, but only his personal deed or merit was honored. In general, it can therefore be said that the republican state constitution provides no place for such distinctions of rank, even though in France, however revolutionary it may be in politics, not only the Legion of Honor, but also various lesser decorations are distributed. Yet these decorations do not belong to the Republic, and it may be said in particular that the Calvinist current has at least not encouraged the emergence of such distinctions. In a monarchical state, on the other hand, such means of distinction are constantly being introduced, and then it is not unnatural that these means are of two kinds, consisting partly in the bestowal of knightly crosses and ribbons, and partly in the granting of titles, the latter of which stands higher.

[154]

The Council of State and the Ministers of State. Just as universities create honorary doctorates to honor someone’s scholarly merits, so too can the title of Privy Councillor or Minister of State serve to express that the government has also granted honor to someone’s political conduct. Thus, the once so highly esteemed meaning of being a member of a Privy Council in Germany has now been reduced to a mere tribute from the King to someone’s merits. However, this is by no means a reason to let the entire concept of the Council of State in our country be absorbed into such an honorary distinction. That would amount to abolishing the Council of State as a college and retaining only the political grandees under the title of Privy Councillor. But we cannot applaud this. Everywhere, the task of being an administrative judge is entrusted to a separate college that is specifically established for this purpose and whose composition is related to the various and often very distinct cases that come before this jurisdiction; and even if one were to go further and create a Chamber of Control over legislation, which would be called upon to watch over the legal form, the text of the law, and the mutual connection of the laws, the abolition of the Council of State would still be most inadvisable. A Council of State remains necessary, not to interpose itself between the King and Parliament. Every attempt to weaken the grip of the purely constitutional system will always find unrelenting opposition in Calvinism. The King is for the people, and not the people for the King. That people speaks in its representation, and in that representation the government can act either through responsible ministers, as with us, or through commissions from Parliament, as in the United States, where no responsible ministers are to be found. Between the government and the people represented in Parliament or the States-General, no other power may be inserted, and the interaction of these two powers on each other must remain absolutely normal, if necessary with the help of the plebiscite.

§21. The King remaining a factor. What is necessary, however, and can never be missed without harm, is...

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over
[155]

THE KING REMAINING A FACTOR. To become a permanent body that can advise the King is a college of a permanent nature. The need for this arises for the King, first of all, from the fact that the King is the permanent factor in the Administration, and that his Departmental Ministers change. A reign of forty years is not unusual for a Sovereign of sound bodily constitution. He may have to take office already as an 18-year-old prince, and remain below the usual age limit of 58. In those forty years, he would then have had some ten ministries under him. Yet a Cabinet still does its work with us about as well as ever. Those ten ministries will now represent, one after the other, a modified, divergent, or even mutually opposing political thought; and even if the leading political thought remains the same, the difference in persons can still bring about very important changes. Just compare men like the former Minister Kappeyne van de Coppello with the Minister of the Establishment, Cort van der Linden, and you immediately feel the contrast in the persons as such, which leaves a completely different historical impression behind. Now, if one wants that the same King, successively served by such utterly divergent ministers, should not be anything other than their echo, then naturally one gets the ornament. Mr. S. van Houten may not be too harshly judged for disputing all independent spontaneity to the King; the criticism exercised on his saying applied only to the choice of the word “ornament.” A King who can do nothing else, nor do anything else, than successively hum the political ditty presented to him by ten different flutes, is not an ornament, that is, not an adornment, but a figure that inspires aversion. Being an echo can still express a pure sound; but the same person, who in succession lets ten completely divergent echoes glide from his lips, is no man. Of course, with “ornament” was meant the splendor and brilliance of the Crown, which adorned the government, but precisely this did injustice to the seriousness that was required here. A King may not for a moment get the idea that the real splendor of his position lies in his palaces, his stables, and his parks. That would be the Mammonistic Kingship. And what, quite differently, a serious people seeks in its King, is a man of character who intends the welfare of his people, and who seeks self-satisfaction not through the state robe of his marshal, but through what he himself does for his country and people. The period of 1848,

[156]

The Council of State and the Ministers of State, who were actually aiming to make the King a mere figurehead, are now a thing of the past. That period brought the historically necessary critique of what the Kings in general had made of the kingship. The Kings were then the great sinners, and in the 17th and 18th centuries had not a little corrupted their position by yielding to sinful desires. A reaction against this could not fail to come, and to prevent the return of such a woeful period, the necessary safeguards are now provided in the more principled consequences of constitutional life. It may even be said that exaggeration has also done harm here, and that the democratic expressions have brought harm to the democrats themselves, so much so that the significance of the Monarchical element is now again gaining the upper hand. But while this may sway back and forth again, it remains firm that the King embodies the element of the durable and the lasting unity, as distinct from the changing element in the political currents of the ballot box. The Ministers come and go, but the King remains. For this reason, the claim does not hold that a King would be obliged to consult only with his Ministers. This demand is made from a radical side, but it is the fruit of superficial thinking. Even formally, this claim cannot be admitted, because the required hearing of the Council of State itself already commands the invocation of advice from elsewhere on the fruit of the Ministerial labor. The way in which the invocation of this advice now takes place may have greatly diminished its significance, but there still lies clearly expressed in it that the King is not only not bound in an absolute sense to the proposal of his Ministers, but even under the obligation not to rely on them exclusively, but on the contrary, to always have a fixed, durable and lasting college to hear. This now accords with the durable character of the King’s administration. Just as his royal authority stands as durable and lasting over against the change of Ministerial persons, so also the nature and composition of the Council of State corresponds to this, which outlives Minister after Minister, and finds itself called upon to judge the doings and omissions of the successive Cabinets. However the majorities in the States-General may alternate, and in connection with this the one Cabinet may succeed the other,

[157]

STATISTICS OF THE MINISTRY. 145 replaces, it is and remains the administration over the same people in the same country; and whatever fluctuations may occur in that administration, there must remain a connection between the different parts of the legislation.

§22. Statistics of the Ministry. One should also reckon with the fact that a Ministry is, after all, a very inconstant magnitude in our time. Since Mr. Thorbecke, on November 7, 1849, one can say, at the beginning of the second half of the previous century, not less than twenty-seven Ministers of the Interior had taken office, from old liberals, moderately advanced, more radical, and from the Anti-Revolutionary camp. Rest did not come. The political swell kept restlessly on. And with 24 Ministers over the 50 years, from 1848 to 1901, reckoned on average, this gives a political wave every other year. Now it is so, that there were also two transitions in the Kingship, as regards the persons, in this half-century, but these two changes compared to the twenty-four changes in the Department of the Interior make then yet a strongly speaking impression of unity, firmness and coherence, all the more because the Queen, who succeeded the late King William III, may still rejoice in almost youthful vitality, and, if God permits us, can still very well, by human calculation, cross the first half of this 20th century. Born in 1880, the Queen would then have reached the age of 70 only. If now already Moses sang in Psalm 90:10: “As for the days of our years, in them are seventy years, or even by reason of strength eighty years,” then it should yet belong to the all-possible, that the life granted to our Sovereign may be extended to us until 1960. From 1850 to 1950, thus in a hundred years, there would then be only two changes in the Bearer of the Crown, while in the Cabinet the change came to the dozen in the half-century. Calculated to 1960, one would thus get about forty Cabinets together, even if the change had taken place only every four years from now on. Forty Cabinet changes now against only two changes on the Throne give a sense of durability against instability, which shows how completely right it was to assert that in the Bearer of the Crown a political life-element must come to its right, which in a non-monarchical country, and even in the Cabinet life on monarchical soil, is almost unthinkable.

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the
[158]

146 THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE. changes only every four years. Forty Cabinet changes now against only two changes on the Throne give a sense of durability against instability, which shows how completely right it was to assert that in the Bearer of the Crown a political life-element must come to its right, which in a non-monarchical country, and even in the Cabinet life on monarchical soil, is almost unthinkable.

§23. Independent Policy.

Analytical Summary: The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,

With a view to this, a certain independent policy is now demanded in a Bearer of the Crown, which remains foreign to the successive Cabinets. Even less so, if there is only a gradual difference in political direction between the successive Cabinets; but very decidedly if Cabinets succeed each other that are fundamentally opposed to each other in political starting point. Then, indeed, in a Cabinet that takes office to replace a fundamentally different Cabinet, there is usually a certain tendency not to continue the thread that the preceding Cabinet had spun, but rather to break the thread and replace it with a thread of a different fabric. It has indeed always appeared that in this moderation was observed, and that the reckless tearing up of just-issued laws, whose enactment one had opposed as a Member of Parliament, was extremely rare. Only reckless arrogance would go so far as to think first of demolishing the just-built, instead of one’s own construction, in the event of a Cabinet change. But however far that sobriety may go, the fact remains nevertheless that Cabinet changes, partly already in the case of gradually differing, but especially in the case of fundamentally opposed Cabinets, must almost of themselves lead to the unraveling of the unity, at least endanger the firm interconnection of the two legislative fragments. One knows what Treub undertook against the Talma law.
