Hence one comes to the result that the unity and coherence of the legislation cannot rest in the Cabinets, which come and go, but must rest in the Crown, which remains under the constant Cabinet changes. Herein now lies a very peculiar vocation for the Crown.

[159]

INDEPENDENT POLICY - CABINET CHANGE.

A constitutional King cannot impose his personal political convictions against the convictions of the people. This would be a conception of the Kingship that would be completely unacceptable to our people. The King aligns himself in the exercise of power with the clearly emerging popular conviction. Whoever cannot acquire this for himself lacks the personal suitability for the constitutional Kingship. But even if the constitutional line must be followed through, there are still nuances. A Prince who, by experience, knows how the ultra-element is constantly wavering in the popular sentiment, has it constantly in his power to maintain a certain unity in the course of legislation. This element of maintaining unity can already make itself felt in the choice of persons for the portfolios.

§24. Cabinet Change. Yet for this very purpose it is necessary that the King, at each change in the Cabinet and at each important measure that comes up, can give a clear account of the situation and of what the coherence of the coming years with the past demands. Now that the Sovereign can consult the chairmen of the two Chambers at the change itself can work well, but it is still something that goes completely outside the Constitution. The chairmen of the two Chambers are not chosen with this in mind. They may be excellent for the leadership of affairs in Parliament, but it does not at all follow from this that their political insight has the clarity necessary to advise on Cabinet changes. It also very rarely happens that the majority in both Chambers differs, so that then the Chairmen often also belong to very divergent political directions. Or if, against the vote in both Chambers, the Crown has still appointed a Chairman of the First Chamber who differs in his political direction from its majority, then such an elected one would still express the sentiment of the body he presides over the least. Therefore, the custom that has now arisen of consulting the Presidents need not be given up. Even if the Constitution does not demand it, it can certainly be useful. Only

Analytical Summary: Our People.
[160]

148 THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE.

it remains highly doubtful whether this consultation of the Presidents offers the Crown bearer what he needs in such moments. Certainly it seems strange that there is no consultation with the body that is specifically intended to advise the Crown. If the Council of State is to be the advisor to the Crown, not the least to maintain the unity of the administration in the country in the face of the changing ministers, then it is hardly to be contradicted that advice in this regard is even more serious at Cabinet changes than at a standalone legislative proposal of importance. Now this naturally depends not a little on the personal receptivity of the Monarch.

§25. The Sovereign needs counsel. In all countries, there have sometimes been Sovereigns of such genius, of such superior endowment, and of such comprehensive mastery, that the decisions to be taken could be left entirely to themselves. We do not say that a truly superior Prince would disdain seeking advice. Just he who stands very high knows how often, in many respects, further clarification of details and circumstances may be necessary. But in any case, such a personally superior Prince would know how to choose his own advisers, and the care for the unity of the administration could very well be left to him. But such highly elevated personalities are also rare exceptions in the House of Orange. What has been experienced in the House of Orange for centuries, that such a series of genius heroes emerged one after the other, remains a high exception, and the course of affairs cannot depend on this exception. Especially where an hereditary prince can already assume the government at the age of 18, no one can want to establish as a rule that even a young man under 20 would always possess the sufficient maturity of insight and judgment to always make the right choice. Hence the constantly felt need to provide the Bearer of the Crown with a college that can serve him with clear information, and this entirely in confidence. The multitude of the matters that await the King’s cooperation also makes having such a college almost indispensable. Even

Analytical Summary: Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit
[161]

A COUNCIL OF STATE IS NECESSARY. - ONE DOES NOT OVERSEE THE WHOLE.

The most skilled statesmen are not equal to each Department. Someone can be an excellent Minister of Public Works or of War, without being sufficiently at home in the financial field, and knowing what the financial future of the State permits. And so also, one can be a highly esteemed Minister of Justice, without being equally at home in the affairs of agriculture, industry, and commerce. In the Cabinet, each one stands for their own Department, and in the Council of Ministers one can only indirectly, under the leadership of the Chairman, concern themselves with the particulars of another Department. One leaves what one is not at home in to others for decision; or where one is called to participate in the decision, as in the Council of Ministers, one follows the advice of one’s colleague. Here about the same applies as can never be denied in the House of Representatives. Only very few Members of Parliament are at all at home in all matters of State. This is why they unite in clubs. In these clubs, the bills introduced are studied in the hands of those few members who are considered to be more particularly versed in the subjects dealt with. On their lead, a common advice is then arrived at, and this advice the members of the club then proceed to follow, even though they themselves are very well aware that they are not up to par on such a subject.

[162]

150 THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE.

In other countries, e.g. in France, another way of doing is still followed, by officially appointing commissions in the Chamber itself for various categories of affairs; in which commissions one then, as much as possible, pro rata to their numerical strength, allows the various parties to cooperate. But whichever way of doing one may prefer, in all such measures the prevailing thought expresses itself, that not except in very rare exceptions can the same Member of Parliament form an independent judgment on everything.

§26. One does not oversee the whole.

Analytical Summary: Guided by Orange. To this has been added: “guided by Orange”; an addition that must not be omitted. For it cannot be denied that in the characterized Calvinism, as it also penetrated our country from Geneva and France at that time, there was a danger not to be underestimated, the aftereffects of which are still discernible. In the last chapter of the First Part of this work, I have extensively explained how from the outset there was a decisive element in Calvinism that could only develop and mature later, and that, politically speaking, only came to full effect in the United States; but it was also then added that in Geneva (one need only think of the burning of Servetus) an intolerant dogmatism still prevailed, which, though indispensable for the church, could only result in schism and oppression in its political consequences.

One therefore does not in the least lower or diminish a Sovereign, if one also takes into account with his person the fact, recognized everywhere else, that not except in very rare exceptions would any one person, without assistance, be able to oversee the entire domain of the State, and to come to a just conclusion on every matter of higher or lower politics, solely from his own perspective. The Sovereign too remains a person of finite comprehension. With him too, therefore, one must not count on an all-encompassing insight that would make all helpful advice superfluous. Enlightenment is therefore indispensable for the Sovereign, if he is to be able to pass a mature judgment on what his Ministers ask of him and propose to him. And it is precisely from this that one feels how incorrect it is to say that the King must rely solely and exclusively on his Ministers. For then one excludes all independent judgment of the Sovereign and reduces his decision to an echo of the Ministerial word. The King must draw an independent conclusion from the proposals of his Ministers, and for this no Sovereign can be deemed capable unless a college stands at his service that clearly shows him what is for and what is against, outlines it clearly for him, and makes him attentive to what might escape the King’s attention and yet prove to be of unavoidable importance for the future of the country or for the future of the Princely House. The conclusion can therefore be no other than that in the Constitutional Monarchy the reigning Sovereign needs a trusted college that can inform him on a confidential footing, and thus prevent that under the strong pressure of the Ministers, in thoughtlessness a decision would be taken that would actually inflict irreparable damage on the interest of the country with a view to the future, or on the position of the Crown. Undoubtedly the King is obliged to keep himself informed of what is recommended or contested in the Press on political matters, and likewise of what is brought up at political meetings or assemblies of all kinds. Even what appears in magazines can be important for him. The pamphlet literature too can influence the mood of the people. To take personal cognizance of all this would be a titanic task for the Sovereign.

§27. Private assistance is not enough.

Analytical Summary: The wrong done to us. Our party has set up a price in 1878, in its own starting point, to point to this happy influence of the Prince of Orange. Let no confusion creep in here. In matters of ecclesiastical life, Calvin’s insight has also been his own. His dispute with Dathenus does not touch the very life of the church, but the influence of the Church on the life of the people in the political domain. As a theologian, especially Johannes a Lasco deserves appreciation on this point, but on a broader scale it has above all been the influence of the Prince that has safely guided us. Meanwhile, one should not lose sight of the fact that this pointing with preference to the Prince is foreign to the intention, to praise anything that has later emerged from the House of Orange on this delicate terrain of the Princes. Prince Maurice aligned himself much more closely with the Calvinists. The then-emerging conflict with Arminianism necessitated this. But his life history would stand in a more glorious light for us if Oldenbarnevelt had died a natural death. Under Prince Frederick

And if it is so that even a private secretary is helpful

[163]

PARTICULAR ASSISTANCE IS NOT ENOUGH. It may be possible to keep the King informed of all this, but this cannot be sufficient. For the whole range of these expressions from the popular sphere must also be weighed from a political point of view, and this cannot be entrusted to a private secretary, since he has no political significance, and it is precisely the political significance that is at stake here. However much it may be obvious that a Sovereign should provide himself in his Cabinet with subordinate personnel who furnish information by category, information is not yet guidance. Guidance can sift the politically important from the insignificant, and also draw the King’s attention to what is truly of political importance to him; and therefore, in this respect also, only a very confidential college, composed of the most eminent men, can provide the King with what he needs. Such a college need not trouble the King with all sorts of reports that get lost in details; it can select and choose, and focus the Sovereign’s attention mainly on what is of weighty importance. The task of guidance should not be limited to the college alone. There is no reason why the Sovereign should not, on appropriate occasions, personally consult a single expert from the college on detailed points. The Ministers must be able to discern in everything that they are serving a Sovereign who is too high to be merely a signer of documents. They must perceive that they are dealing with thoughtful minds with a prepared will in the Sovereign. They are not only to be the thinkers, so that the King would merely be the seal that authenticates the products of their thinking. However important the ministerial office may be, and however serious the responsibility of the Minister, so that it is not only permitted to him, but even his duty and calling, in a certain sense to exert political pressure on the Sovereign, they must nevertheless always be able to discern that they are serving in the King a thoughtful Sovereign, and that a deliberate policy, and not mere arbitrary will, must lead to the conclusion. If a King wants to be a mere figurehead, one must acquiesce in this, but it must never be aimed at, never be presented, as if the role of a figurehead were the ideal of the Constitutional Sovereign. On the contrary, from the outset the princely education of the King must be directed towards a sense of duty of the highest order.

[164]

THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE. A higher order calls him to greater vigilance. Not even in the most extreme case must the King engage in a struggle for his royal or dynastic rights. The relationship between him and his advisory college must be such that the King prefers to champion the rights and interests of the people, and that the college, which enjoys his confidence, lets its warning be heard when the right balance between King and Chamber is in danger of being disturbed. The King embraces all Departments, i.e. the entire State, in his deliberations, and likewise the King remains, even when the Ministers have gone. Not in them, but in the Sovereign, is the unity, the coherence of past, present and future represented. What the ministerial council can only do very partially, namely gather the products of all Departments into one bouquet, is the task of the Council of State, because this college has to advise the Sovereign, and the peculiar character of the monarchical position itself entails that the King has to watch over the State and not directly over any single Department.

§28. Foreign Affairs. This now applies to the whole affair of the State, but even more so in particular to Foreign Affairs, National Defense and the Colonies. The relationship of the Sovereign to other foreign Sovereigns has a completely peculiar character. Sovereigns often exist in blood or family relationship to each other. They correspond with each other. They pay each other visits, and on these visits important conversations often take place, which may not go beyond the Minister of Foreign Affairs, but are then nevertheless conducted by the Sovereign himself, even in his presence. The King receives envoys from other Powers, and even ambassadors, as was also the case in The Hague in the past, but has since come to an end. Ambassadors now have this peculiarity that they can visit the King directly, bypassing the Minister of Foreign Affairs, and discuss important matters with him personally. While it is rightly emphasized that in such visits the Minister of Foreign Affairs remains the responsible person, it is nevertheless impossible to prevent the King himself from leading the encounter. One hears that even a toast that the Sovereign will propose is communicated in advance to the Minister of Foreign Affairs, but one knows how often the Sovereign disregards this. Abroad, the custom is that when the Sovereign pays an official visit to another government, the Minister of Foreign Affairs always accompanies him and is present at all discussions. In our country, on the other hand, this only happens as a rare exception, which can be fraught with danger. When receiving foreign envoys at the Court, the Minister of Foreign Affairs is indeed accustomed to participate in the introduction of the Envoy, but this does not take away the fact that there are then very well opportunities where the Sovereign comes into discussion with such an envoy, entirely at his own risk. Enough to draw attention to the fact that the Department of Foreign Affairs can pose very serious difficulties in this respect, and that it is therefore not only desirable but necessary that the King, also as Sovereign among Sovereigns, can dispose of the not only desirable but often highly necessary information. In 1914 and thereafter, everyone felt this. Article 57 of the Constitution clearly states: “The King has the supreme administration of Foreign Affairs,” and, even more strongly, Article 58 stipulates that the King declares war. Likewise, Article 58 stipulates that the King concludes and ratifies all treaties with foreign Powers, and need only give the States General further information on the content to the extent that he deems the interest of the State to allow it. A rather ticklish point, since the conclusion of the treaty usually precedes the communication of it to the States General. §29. Navy and Army. Up to a certain point at least, the same applies to the navy and army. Not only does the King conclude the treaties and declare war, but Article 60 also assigns him “the supreme command of the naval and military forces.” The King can himself take the field at the head of his army, and there has always been a peculiar relationship between the Sovereign and the officer corps, which is already expressed in the Orange cockade.

Analytical Summary: Enduring Bond. No Calvinist, in any country, has ever thought of whitewashing any injustice for the benefit of a princely ruler. But apart from what criticism may later have become necessary, we still always honor in the appearance of Prince William the Silent a gift of God to our people in their then so distressed condition; we thank God for what the time has bestowed on our fatherland in the generation of the Prince; we now still refresh ourselves in the national glory that has emanated from this House of Princes over our people as a nation; and we now still take sides, not on ecclesiastical, but on political terrain, for what the Silent One, the first among all princes in Europe, as the consequence of the Reformation, brought us in the bestowal of full spiritual freedom. Precisely for this reason, protest is constantly lodged by us against any attempt to discredit Orange as an ornament, as Mr. S. van Houten has dared to do. Not, we hasten to add here, as if this Statesman wanted to detract from the glory of our House of Orange. What he spoke of as an “ornament” was only the formulation in a single word of his constitutional theory. But here lies the difference, that such a declining expression of “ornament” was more dear to him, while it strikes us as offensive to our love for Orange. Especially after the shameful treatment that the House of Orange has suffered through its expulsion and forced flight to England in 1797, and after the more than shameful threat with which in 1848 in the liberal press one even dared to speak of the scaffold to unsettle Orange itself, it betrayed a stiff lack of tenderness and self-control in a man who ultimately became an advisor to the Crown, to hear him speak of “ornament.” Not daring to criticize a Prince openly is a cringing cowardice that Orange demands less than it requires, and which is unthinkable especially in the Calvinist. But this does not detract from the firm demand that we remain thankful for honoring what our God has bestowed on us in Orange through his great Princes. A glory in the past, the happy echo of which was to be heard in our revered Queen, as the last scion of the ancient House of Orange.
[165]

FOREIGN AFFAIRS - NAVY AND ARMY. 153

not, that the King himself leads the encounter. One hears that even a toast that the Sovereign will propose is communicated in advance to the Minister of Foreign Affairs, but one knows how often the Sovereign disregards this. Abroad, the custom is that when the Sovereign pays an official visit to another government, the Minister of Foreign Affairs always accompanies him and is present at all discussions. In our country, on the other hand, this only happens as a rare exception, which can be fraught with danger. When receiving foreign envoys at the Court, the Minister of Foreign Affairs is indeed accustomed to participate in the introduction of the Envoy, but this does not take away the fact that there are then very well opportunities where the Sovereign comes into discussion with such an envoy, entirely at his own risk. Enough to draw attention to the fact that the Department of Foreign Affairs can pose very serious difficulties in this respect, and that it is therefore not only desirable but necessary that the King, also as Sovereign among Sovereigns, can dispose of the not only desirable but often highly necessary information. In 1914 and thereafter, everyone felt this. Article 57 of the Constitution clearly states: “The King has the supreme administration of Foreign Affairs,” and, even more strongly, Article 58 stipulates that the King declares war. Likewise, Article 58 stipulates that the King concludes and ratifies all treaties with foreign Powers, and need only give the States General further information on the content to the extent that he deems the interest of the State to allow it. A rather ticklish point, since the conclusion of the treaty usually precedes the communication of it to the States General.

§29. Navy and Army. Up to a certain point at least, the same applies to the navy and army. Not only does the King conclude the treaties and declare war, but Article 60 also assigns him “the supreme command of the naval and military forces.” The King can himself take the field at the head of his army, and there has always been a peculiar relationship between the Sovereign and the officer corps, which is already expressed in the Orange cockade.

Analytical Summary: According to the needs of our time.
[166]

154THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE. And on this basis it does not distinguish itself from any other corps of officials. Now the Ministers take turns replacing each other, and a passing Cabinet is strongly driven to significantly weaken the position of the Navy and Army—in such a way that their restoration to strength would later be unattainable under another Cabinet—then undoubtedly the obligation would rest on the King to stand up for the energetic significance of national defense. In the period that has elapsed since 1848, this has not always been done. Highly partisan driving has led to the neglect of our Defense, and when the war broke out in 1914, it was felt by almost all parties how much the Navy and Army had been somewhat neglected; so much so that the very parties who had most insisted on the solidity of our Defense then raised the cry for universal conscription. It is therefore so desirable for this State interest that the King, as representing the permanent and enduring, should exert such pressure on the Cabinets that are only passing, that the State interest is attended to as required. Also with a view to this, it is therefore so necessary that the King should always have a college of advisers at his service to watch over the interests of the country.

§30. The Colonies. The third point on which emphasis should be laid here is the administration of our Possessions and Colonies. Here too, after all, Article 61 of the Constitution states: “The King has the Supreme Administration of the Colonies and Possessions of the Realm in other parts of the world.” That addition: “in other parts of the world” could have been omitted. This is not the main point here. The main point is that these Possessions and Colonies do not form a part of the Realm, but belong to the Realm. It is true that Article I of the Constitution was amended in 1887 so that now the Colonies and Possessions are also considered to form part of the Kingdom of the Netherlands, but this was certainly not an improvement. In the 1815 wording, Article I spoke exclusively of the Kingdom of the Netherlands, referring to the separate Provinces. But as early as 1848, a change was made in this respect, so that it then came to stand that the Kingdom of the Netherlands in Europe was considered to consist of our eleven provinces. This already tended to consider the Colonies as well as a part of the Realm. And in 1887 they went even further and included them in the Realm. That this is incorrect is already evident from the fact that Java, etc. do not belong to the King, but to the Realm, and that the King of the Netherlands is not also King of Java. Java, etc. belong to the Realm, and the Supreme Administration rests with the Bearer of the Crown, because he is the ruling Sovereign of the Realm. But leaving this aside, the relationship of the Crown to Sumatra and to Gelderland certainly differed in every respect, and from this too arises the necessity that a competent college should stand at the King’s service to advise him on matters of administration over large tracts of land, which the King himself usually does not even know by sight. I consider it particularly necessary to point this out, because the Constitution itself in Articles 55, 58 and 59 makes exceptions here. For the rest, the need for advice is just as much the case with regard to the other Departments.

Analytical Summary: The Church and the State in Mutual Relation. One immediately feels this in the relationship between Church and State. In the 16th century, the struggle that the Reformation undertook against Rome’s overpowering authority naturally brought the ecclesiastical question to the fore in all political domains. The ecclesiastical question dominated the entire political sphere, both domestically and with a view to foreign relations, and here the foreign relations mostly stood...
[167]

THE COLONIES. - CONCLUSION. 155 A King needs a clear insight into the conditions and needs of the State. In himself, whether young or old, sufficient knowledge of State affairs cannot be presumed. It may be there, but it may also not be. And in this respect, it can least of all be counted on with any certainty. Relying on the certain is a requirement here.

§31. Conclusion. Briefly summarized, the conclusion comes down to this: Firstly, that the Council of State, or more understandably expressed, the Council of State, must be relieved of the task of controlling laws, administrative measures and decisions, and that this should then be formally entrusted to a separate Chamber of Control, which, without political significance, takes care of the proper form and proper coherence of laws and ordinances. Secondly, that administrative jurisdiction does not belong to a department of the Council of State, but requires an independent college, and indeed a college that is not one-sidedly composed, but in which the autonomous element also has a voice.

Analytical Summary: The People’s Representation. It is no different with the People’s Representation. Under the republican form of government, our Commonwealth has not known representation as we understand it today. There were estates, but no People’s Representation; the cities and manorial landowners held the power; the deputies were in no way representatives of the nation before the Government, but rather the installers and maintainers of the Government itself. There was certainly popular choice in the election of the power-holders, but the right to participate in that choice was extremely limited. As shown in the last chapter of the First Part, Calvin did not praise such a system, and ultimately he himself went over to advising in a democratic sense. Whoever now tries to apply the completely antiquated representation ideas of the 16th century to the present state of affairs would thereby show themselves to be anything but faithful to the pure principle, but rather to blatantly disown it. To now still advocate a State form that does not fully do justice to the representative principle, would brand one as backward and out of one’s time. The question of a proper People’s Representation has now itself become the main question for the future development of the State life. And the Calvinist direction, which has always proceeded from the people, and even found its strength in the “common folk,” would lose its inner driving force and let itself be written off, if it did not take the lead rather than follow on this point of our constitutional development in the spirit of freedom and love of the people. To be a Christian Democrat remains the title of honor for every Calvinist. §32. Navy and Army. As a third example, let the National Defense be pointed out. In the days of the Reformation, there was hardly any mention of a national army, as we know it today. The Condottieri had indeed lost ground, but the army with which one went to war still consisted mostly of volunteers offering themselves, and even of mercenary troops from foreign countries, just as we still used to employ, and partly still do, not a few Swiss, Germans and Belgians for our East Indian army. Only of the army of Gustavus Vasa and of the army of Cromwell can it be said that they were driven and urged by a higher and holier interest. For the rest, however, the armies were mostly composed of rough men, and the holy enthusiasm for the cause of the country was sometimes all too little to be noticed. That the same regiment, after the lapse of ten years, drew the sword against a higher national interest, which it had first helped to defend at the price of its blood, occurred more than once. All the more the custom came into vogue with us to assemble the power for the defense of the country’s interests from hired manpower, inasmuch as the navy with us, more than the army, stood in importance, and the manning of the navy, both here and still in England, was constantly recruited by free choice. The civic guard had a more national character, and knew no wages as a motive; but the militia, as it is now in force, first appeared under Napoleon’s domination, and has since also been maintained in our Kingdom. Meanwhile, our people have been far from eager for the new military idea. To become a soldier was not considered an honor, but a plague, and the system of substitutes soon came up to free oneself from this plague by money. It has therefore lasted until the very end of the 19th century before we really got a national army, and the idea of universal military service, which now imposes itself of its own accord, is still not realized with us. Now, because in the glorious days of our fathers the condition prevailed in navy and army that excluded a national army, shall one identify the system then in force with Calvinism, and therefore, as ultra-conservative, set it against the military force that is now so absolutely indispensable? Certainly not. The principle of Calvinism and this is, to highly esteem the significance of the national life; and the future of the national life is now inexorably, at least for small nations, in danger, if not the whole people finally girds the sword as one man for the country’s independence.
[168]

156THE COUNCIL OF STATE AND THE MINISTERS OF STATE. The element of our municipal and provincial administrations is represented. And understand that the Council of State or of State is a trusted college for the enlightenment of the Bearer of the Crown, of which he himself is the natural Chairman, to whose advice he has recourse as often as he feels the need for it, and that he offers his advice as often as the College by majority decides that it must resolve to do so. And this with the tendency to represent the unity of the State against the division of the Departments: to represent the lasting interests of the people against the passing and coming Cabinets; and to keep the relationship between Country and People on the one hand and King and Dynasty on the other in the right balance. Extraordinary Councils of State ought to become an active element, and to be appointed with a view to special interests. The Ministers of State, finally, are nothing else than bearers of an honorary title, of nobility or knighthood, only differing in this, that other honors from the Crown have personal peculiarities or particular deeds as their object, while the honorary title of Minister of State wishes to express how the one thus appointed is esteemed by the Crown in regard to the general interest and the whole State to have performed or to be able to perform something meritorious. The conferring of knighthoods and honorary titles of that nature is connected with the institution of the Monarchy. This innocent means of binding a few persons to itself, the Monarchy may be left to, however little effect it may have in itself.

[169]

DIVISION OF THE STATE TASK. - ELSEWHERE ANDERS. SEE AMERICA. 157Originally, something more than an honorary title was intended with the Ministership of State, as appears, among other things, from the provision in Art. 9 of the Decree of March 31, 1842, Stbl. n°. 9. For this article provided that the King could temporarily include such Ministers of State in the Council of Ministers. Yet this idea did not hold. The reason why it seemed advisable to us to speak of these Ministers of State not among the Departmental Ministers, but in one chapter with the Council of State, is that the Ministers bear the Departmental character and do not derive their position from the State as a whole. Since this broader character, which the Ministers lack, does belong to the Council of State and the Ministers of State, it seemed more correct to combine these two. The Council of Ministers remains the sum of the parts, and lacks the organic unity concept. Only political power has ever been granted to the Council of State, with the exception of the exercise of government. Chapter V THE DEPARTMENTAL MINISTRIES

§1. Division of the State Task. With the exception of a very few miniature states, such as San Marino or Hohenzollern-Segmaringen, the task of the State is too extensive in a country of any significance to be placed in the hands of a single person. Hence the division of the administration into sections, commonly called Departments or Ministries. Yet this institution by no means implies only a division of labor. It also establishes the indispensable link between the Bearer of the Crown and the Representation of the People. §2. Elsewhere anders. See America. The indispensable link to connect Government and Parliament could also be established in another way, as is actually the case in the United States of North America. However, what can be done there, because the form of the State is republican, cannot well be thought of in the Monarchy. True, as history teaches, in the English way of connecting Government and Parliament, but not what came about in America. In America, the Ministers are nothing else than Secretaries of the President. His task alone is too great, and therefore he surrounds himself with helpers, who bear the title of Secretary of State, of War, of the Navy, etc.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[170]

THE DEPARTMENTAL MINISTRIES.

He himself appoints them entirely at his own discretion, and they alone deal with him and are accountable to him alone. The political character of these Ministers is also entirely different from the significance they have in our country or in England. They do not appear before Parliament at all in America. They are the hands of the President, and nothing more. Yet responsible persons also appear in the American Union, but they are not appointed by the President, but by the People’s Representatives. It is Congress that appoints standing committees from among its members for the administration of the various parts of the government. These are the Committees that come into contact with the President, and through the President with the Ministers. But in Congress, the President sends his proposals directly, and it is Committees that advocate for them in Congress. One feels how here nothing else is given than a sharpening of popular government. It is the people who, with universal suffrage, appoint Congress, not state-wise, but once and for all for the entire Union. The individual States also have a say, but only in the Senate. The delegation to Congress, on the other hand, is a direct one, through mixed voters. For Congress, the entire Union forms one all-encompassing state; the reason why there can be no question of real sovereignty in the individual States. In the thus elected Congress, therefore, the entire people is represented, not in a French-revolutionary sense, as if the plenitude of power originally rested in man, and thus in the constituent parts of the people, but with the understanding that one confesses: “As God has given us the power to choose our own magistrates.” The origin of all authority and power therefore remains honored in Almighty God; but by God and on behalf of God, this authority and this power have been placed in the people, and it is the people of the United States, in whose representation the actual government resides. Yet the appointment of the President is not entrusted to this Congress, but remains reserved for the people themselves. The people thus call, as it were, two things into being. On the one hand, a head in the President, and on the other hand, a representation in Congress, plus the Senate. These two: President and Congress, now marry, as we may express it, and a twofold action now takes place: first, the President appoints assistants to himself,

[171]

MINISTERS AS MEMBERS OF PARLIAMENT.159 On the one hand, he multiplies them, and these are his ministers; while on the other hand, Congress elects from its midst the various commissions for State affairs, which, together with the President, bring forward the proposals that are then submitted to the judgment and approval of Congress, and subsequently of the Senate. This way of proceeding is thus entirely different from the arrangement that has come about in most European states. Yet even the way of proceeding that is followed in Europe is not uniform. England in particular takes a distinct place in this. True, the King in England also appoints his ministers for the various parts of the State’s task, but as ministers they can only act in their capacity as members of Parliament. It is not the case that a minister, as with us, can also be a member of Parliament, and not even that he must be re-elected as a member of the House after his appointment. This latter is also indispensable, but in acting as a minister, the appointee does so in his dual capacity, on the one hand as invested with the confidence of the Electors, and on the other hand as invested with the confidence of the Crown. It is not a Parliament that stands on its own, and into which the appointee enters as chosen by the King to take a seat at the ministerial table, which is entirely distinct from the parliamentary benches. Ministerial benches and members’ benches form one whole. Those who act as ministers sit in Parliament first and foremost as members of Parliament, but then as such members who have been honored with the confidence of the Crown. In England too, the ministers are a link between Sovereign and People, but not in the sense that the King, as it were, hooks this link from the outside onto the People’s representation; no, it must be understood quite differently in England, namely that the ministerial members are members of Parliament, and that the King, by appointing these members as Ministers, attaches the link himself, which has its first firmness in the House. Neither the above-sketched American nor this English way of proceeding applies among us.

§3. Ministers as Members of Parliament. With us, it is the Bearer of the Crown who, as the possessor

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[172]

The Departmental Ministries possess an independent power that comes to the People’s Representatives to lead the course of affairs in association with them, according to the manner agreed upon with them in the Constitution. Hence, even the permitted choice of ministers from among the members of the States-General found no entry with us. In the Pierson-Borgesius Cabinet, an attempt was made to try this, as the Messrs. Borgesius and Lely both sought and obtained a mandate, but it did not suffice and has not been followed since. Especially the circumstance that not all, but only two Ministers, were in possession of the dual quality, proved detrimental in practice. It then became all too conceivable that one of the two minister-Chamber members would vote against a bill of one of his colleagues submitted to the judgment of the States-General. Speaking as a Chamber member proved difficult for them. Even attending the meetings of the States-General did not go very smoothly. In short, both from the side of the Chamber and from the side of these Ministers, it was soon devalued that the connection of these two qualities did not run smoothly. At times, one even got the impression that these elected officials would rather have returned their mandate to the Electors. In the past, one had not gone to this idea in practice. When the experiment was taken, it was on a small scale. And on that test, the whole undertaking soon collapsed. With 1901, the experiment fell away of itself, and in the Cabinets that have since taken office, the newly emerged experiment has immediately fallen completely out of practice; as one may well predict, to remain completely outside of practice. One should never have had to create the possibility of such a dual employment in the Constitution. Art. 94 also only determines sideways that a Minister can also be a Chamber member. It says: “The Heads of the Ministerial Departments have seats in both Chambers,” and it is then added: “They have only an advisory vote, unless they may have been appointed as members of the assembly.” The possibility of the dual employment is thus opened up; it is not excluded; but one feels from the choice of words that there is more a matter of toleration than of something that would be normal and desirable. If one asks why then this addition occurs in Art. 94, and why such a dual employment was not rather cut off, it lies at hand that the existing custom in the Constitutional country allowed something of a modified meaning.

[173]

THE CROWN AND THE STATES-GENERAL.161 that it was innocent to oppose it so sharply against us; in the matter, on the other hand, they disapproved of the position of the Crown as too independent in its origin, so as not to lead to a somewhat divergent result in the relationship of the ministers to the States-General.

§4. The Crown and the States-General. Both, the Crown and the States-General, saw their powers granted and mutually recognized; but the Ministers of the Crown were the agents of the Crown, and not of the States-General or the people. It was the Bearer of the Crown who appointed them and went about it entirely freely. Only, the agents appointed by him were struck with complete powerlessness if the States-General refused them the indispensable cooperation for the execution of their office. With the introduction of ministerial responsibility, a bond was thus laid, which the servants of the Crown had to conform to the spirit and sense of the people of the Netherlands as revealed at the ballot box. In and through that ballot box, the people spoke, sometimes in this sense, sometimes in that. The contrast could be antithetical, the difference could also be merely gradual, but the Crown could not go further and would hamper itself in the execution of the governing task if the Ministers who acted as its servants had missed all conformity with the pronouncement of the ballot box. The connection did not fail in that, but the duality remained unimpaired. There remained the People who spoke through their representatives, and there remained the King who bore the Crown as the dynastic heir of historical rights. Of a blending of the parliamentary and the royal character of the authority, there was no question with us. Formally, both remained sister-like separated from each other, and the Constitution regulated their mutual relationship. It is true that for a few decades the conservative-aristocratic side has tried to push the Crown forward, as if the people, for the glorification of the Crown, were merely an appendage to the Crown, and even Da Costa was for a moment confused in this relationship of authority. The Anti-Revolutionary party, on the other hand, never strayed in this. For us, the people always stood in the foreground, and it was to this people that God, for the good of this people, gave the dynasty of Orange. That this relationship has all too often led to a lowering of the high significance of the Kingship is not denied here, and we do not dispute that an unfortunate choice of Ministers has sometimes promoted this evil. Everything depends so much on the personal here. Even in England it was repeatedly shown how rich the influence of the Crown can still be, if the Prince who wears the Crown is rich in a genius for the constitution. No less was it evident how a genius-constitutional Minister can, even under strong democratic pressure, still succeed in upholding the honor of the Crown. And even the way in which the democratic enthusiasts and drivers were personally treated proved to be of no small influence on the course of affairs. Just think of the attitude of many Social Democrats when the entire European household was shaken by the war in 1914. On the point of authority, however, there can and may never be given in. No higher honor can exist for a Prince than to bless his people; and the Minister who is not deeply imbued with this is spoiling, even if he intends it in the least, the only good relationship that, according to a Higher purpose, must exist between Prince and People.

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.
[174]

§5. Ballot box and new Cabinet. That is why, however, the Bearer of the Crown threatens to spoil his own position if he tries, in the appointment of a new Cabinet, not to reckon very closely with the outcome of the preceding ballot box. He can be authorized, even compelled, to deviate from what that outcome seemed to demand, if, as in 1913, the parties that secured the majority in the Second Chamber at the ballot box could not reach an agreement among themselves. Yet the Queen had then also followed the electoral demand and had begun the Cabinet formation by entrusting it to Dr. Bos; and only when he proved unable to fulfill the task he had taken upon himself and resigned his mandate, was Mr. Cort v.d. Linden called. Something like this, however, does not come about without...

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[175]

RESISTANCE TO ANY HESITATION IN DUTY.163 This occurs very rarely, and in the ordinary course it is therefore always desirable that the Crown should promptly give heed to the electoral indication and call upon the person whom it can be regarded as best expressing the desire of the majority of the voters to form a Cabinet. Not everyone agrees with this, and especially from the Conservative side it has been repeatedly argued that following this line of conduct would be detrimental to the dignity of the Crown. The choice of Ministers, it is then said, is an absolute prerogative of the Crown, and one promotes popular sovereignty at the expense of the Royal Majesty if one fosters the idea that the ballot box indicates which kind of statesman should be entrusted with the formation of the Cabinet. Formally, this is also completely correct. If the bearer of the Crown, after a decisive liberal majority emerged from the ballot box, were to call upon a statesman of the Right to form the Cabinet, this would not be in conflict with the Constitution, and yet the Sovereign would thereby undermine their own prestige. The consequence of such a commission would immediately give rise to a violent clash between the Cabinet and the States General.

§6. Resistance to any hesitation in duty.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.

Even the constitutionally-minded members of the Right would have to take a decisive part in the opposition to such an appointment. Nothing could come of it. Before long, the newly appointed Cabinet would have to request its dismissal. And if then a liberal Cabinet took its place, the People would have gained influence and the Crown would have lost prestige. One is reminded of the days of the Proclamations with the orange borders. Also at the resignation of the Cabinet-Mackay, there was a moment when the King did not immediately submit to the pronouncement of the ballot box; but this protest against the necessity of yielding to the pronouncement of the ballot box died away within a very short time, and with the advent of the Cabinet-Tienhoven-Tak we moved again into the normal line. Therefore, precisely in the interest of the Crown itself, one cannot be warned enough against any provocation of a struggle between the choice of the Cabinet-formateur and the result of the ballot.

[176]

164 THE DEPARTMENTAL MINISTRIES. One must never for a moment hesitate on this critical point. The weakest attempt to bargain with the result of the ballot box must never be risked. In the case of a doubtful ballot box result, uncertainty may arise, and even about the choice of the person who will be called upon as Cabinet-formateur, differences of opinion may arise; even about the candidates for the other Departments the Crown can exercise criticism. On the other hand, with regard to the Cabinet-formateur as such, the Sovereign has the most brilliant position, if they, without the slightest attempt at resistance, immediately and straightforwardly follow the pronouncement of the majority of the voters. Just then, their Majesty remains unscathed, and the perverse consequences of a wrongly guided electoral action do not fall on the King’s account, but on the account of the defective guidance that had been given to the people in the electoral field. Not only the guidance in the ballot box, but the Electoral Law itself may have caused the opinion of the people to be expressed incorrectly, and it may well be that later a legislative measure will be necessary to remedy this. But if the ballot box has been active, and as a result of that action a majority of a certain direction has appeared in the Second Chamber, then it is that direction that must come out in the new Cabinet, and the King stands unblemished and unharmed in their honor, if they, without a trace of resistance, allow that pronouncement of the people of voters to take effect. The Crown is for the people, never the people for the Crown.

§7. Recourse to dissolution. It has been objected to this that the Crown, if it chooses otherwise, and thereby the opposition in the States General becomes too strong, possesses the power to dissolve the States General; and this too has been tested in the days of the Proclamation; but even taking this way out led to bitter disappointment. Not the electoral power of the voters, but precisely the royal prestige had then suffered very considerable damage, and has never been fully restored. Even after the pronouncement of the ballot box, there must be no conflict. Such a

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[177]

RECOURSE TO DISSOLUTION - THIS STRUGGLE IS NOW OVER.

The Crown always loses in a struggle, unless a higher principle is at stake, so that it is not about the supremacy of the royal authority, but about a sacred interest. The right of dissolution may seem to be, but is not in reality, a power granted to the Crown for self-defense. It may have seemed originally, or even that the right of dissolution itself entrusted a highly questionable power to the Bearer of the Crown, but in fact it turned out quite differently. Historically, it is a fact that every attempt at dissolution, at least as a rule, has failed miserably, and has resulted in the opposite of what was intended. It may therefore be safely established that any resistance to the express will of the ballot box has always been detrimental to the dignity of the Crown, and that the position of the Crown has been strengthened, not weakened, whenever immediate and full effect was given to the pronouncement of the ballot box. Not for a moment even may the impression take root that the Crown represents an interest of its own, so that the people would have to lean on it to serve this independent interest. The very awareness of this must not arise. Above all, at such a moment of Cabinet change, the whole people must feel that the tide of public opinion is truly decisive even for the Cabinet. Neither in the retirement of the Kuyper Cabinet in 1905 nor in that of the Heemskerk Cabinet in 1913 has the hesitation that caused it to waver in 1891 been repeated.

§8. This struggle is now over.

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.

One may say that we have now moved beyond the theory of the opposite. The forced maneuvers of the old Mr. Heemskerk have made us definitively abandon all resistance to the ordinary rule. The ballot box, not the Crown, determines the political direction in which the country will be governed; and not on the Crown that appoints, but on the Statesmen who follow the call to take a seat in the new Cabinet, rests the responsibility for what will then come to pass. Especially from the circle of Da Costa were ideas propagated among us that were connected with his incorrect conception of the authority of the Constitution; but Groen van Prinsterer soon saw through this and was quick to attack it. Only a coup d’état can make a breach here, but for a coup d’état no argument can be made in theory. Whoever resorts to such an attack on the state order acknowledges for himself that he is resorting to a completely arbitrary act. Not until afterwards can the necessity of such an attack be argued, and this argument must then show that the country was indeed in political danger of life. This threat can come from two sides. There can be a danger to the life of the country in the international relations, which, if there is to be any rescue, necessitates immediate and unconditional decisive action. Or also, there can be a threat to the established order of things by Jacobin unrest and by an unscrupulous Fronde. But it cannot be claimed that a coup d’état would always in itself be justified; as a rule, this is too dangerous an undertaking to be resorted to. In the theory of constitutional law, there is no place for such an attack on the established order of things. The rule must remain that the Ministers are the intermediaries who connect the Crown and the People with each other, and the less theorized is made of their mutual relationship, and the more calmly and quietly the Crown allows the course of electoral life to take its course, the more firmly the Majesty of the Crown will take root. Just as one knows from a child who constantly pulls up a young plant to examine the just emerging roots, how he thereby ruins the regular growth of the plant and ultimately causes it to die completely. And so also of the monarchical plant it can be said, that it thrives all the better the more quietly and normally one allows its roots to work through. Nothing weakens its vital force as much as precisely this constant meddling with the roots of the monarchical being. Only letting it grow quietly promises bloom here as well.

§9. A Council of Ministers.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.

Not devoid of importance is further the question of whether or not there is a Council of Ministers, just as there is a Council of State. In the new period of our political life, which began in 1848, the idea of a Council of Ministers began to emerge...

[178]

166 THE DEPARTMENTAL MINISTRIES.... but it was not until the Thorbecke Constitution of 1848 that it received its first legal recognition. This Council of Ministers, however, is not a body with independent authority, but merely an advisory body to the Crown. The Ministers are not the representatives of the people, but the confidants of the Crown. They are not the leaders of the people, but the intermediaries between Crown and People. Their task is not to carry out the will of the people, but to advise the Crown on how to govern the country. The Crown remains the sovereign, the Ministers are merely the servants of the Crown. This is the theory, but in practice it has turned out quite differently. In practice, the Ministers have become the leaders of the people, and the Crown has become the servant of the Ministers. The people no longer look to the Crown, but to the Ministers, for the direction of the country’s policy. And the Crown, in order to maintain its position, has had to submit to the will of the Ministers. This is the reality of the situation, and it is a reality that cannot be ignored.

[179]

A COUNCIL OF MINISTERS It is true that people have slowly come to recognize the need for such a Council; but even Fransen van de Putte resisted this idea in 1901, which Dr. Kuyper defended. And although it is acknowledged that one can better reckon with such a Council, it cannot be denied that our Constitution, except in Article 38, knows nothing of the establishment of such a Council. The seventh division of the Second Chapter of the Constitution has the title: “Of the Council of State and the Ministerial Departments.” It does not say: “Of the Council of State and the Council of Ministers,” but: “and of the Ministerial Departments”—each on its own. Article 77 speaks just as little of a Cabinet, but stipulates: “The King establishes the Ministerial Departments and appoints and dismisses their heads as he sees fit.” Even stranger is that in the second paragraph of Article 72 we read: “The heads of the Ministerial Departments provide for the execution of the Constitution and the other laws.” Now it is self-evident that all kinds of things are determined in our Constitution that can only be firmly established through sufficient cooperation of the Ministers, and yet no Council is mentioned, and the heads of the Departments are each named individually, as if the government of the State were thus divided into pieces and fragments. Only in Article 38 does the Constitution discuss the case where the King is unable to govern, and then, it states, “the heads of the Ministerial Departments shall act in consultation.” This somewhat peculiar expression shows that whoever formulated it did not think of an official body called the Cabinet. The guiding idea remained that the unity of government resided solely in the person of the King, and that this unity of power, as it were, had its hands and arms in the Ministers to exert the necessary outward action. Nor does our Constitution state that the King, just as he is president of the Council of State, would also be the chairman of the Cabinet. On the contrary, one of the Ministers always acts as president. The Ministers are together just as much the organ through which the royal power over the country is exercised. With this in mind, it is unthinkable that these seven or eight separate organs would not have to cooperate, and they can only cooperate under presidential leadership. And also

[180]

168 DEPARTMENTAL MINISTRIES. For every bill that goes to the States-General, all the Ministers together bear ministerial responsibility. When a regulation for the meetings of the heads of the Departments was then issued, it could not be otherwise, or the Ministers did not appear in it as chance-met officials, but as servants of the Crown who had to come together regularly and therefore needed a president. In fact, the Council was thus. Often this Council even met twice a week.

§10. A Fixed Chairman? The only thing that remained limping was the question whether the Cabinet Formateur would be the permanent fixed Chairman, or whether the various Ministers, in turn, would be entrusted with the leadership of the whole. Initially, they did not want to allow a fixed presidium. In a fixed sequence, each Minister then received the gavel in turn, and only the duration of each period still differed. The presidium could change by the month, by the two months, and so on. This was relatively indifferent, but a continuous chairmanship was in any case excluded. And this is precisely what has now been changed in the 1901 Regulations, so that the Queen appoints the Chairman, and the Minister chosen by her presides as long as it seems desirable to her. If one asks what preference was given earlier to a presidium that alternated, this is to be sought in two things. On the one hand, each Minister wished to remain free, to be on an equal footing with his colleagues, not to be under collegial pressure, and, as far as some leadership was indispensable, to be able to share in giving that leadership with his fellow officials. It should not be a Council that came together; it had to be a loose gathering of equal officials. And it was precisely this equality that was threatened if one of them held the gavel throughout the entire four-year period. It was precisely against the appearance and the corps that objections were raised. The system should not become a college. The Ministers had to be completely independent persons who only came together now and then as having a common interest, to discuss and decide on that common interest.

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.
[181]

A PERMANENT CHAIRMAN?

The discussion of common interests was the first task of the Ministers. And while thus the first rank grew out of the Ministers’ desire for personal independence in their administration, it was also natural that the Crown would rather deal with independent agents than with a firmly united Council of Ministers. Yet it was in the nature of things that this view had to give way to the unifying conception of the ministerial office. Already in the formation of a new Cabinet, the idea of unity was at work. It was not the King who, according to his own insights and good pleasure, successively called six, seven, or eight statesmen to himself and appointed them as Ministers, as would have been proper under the old system, but the Sovereign confined himself to summoning one prominent statesman to the fore and entrusting him with the formation of the entire Cabinet. From this first and only summoned one, the nomination then proceeded, and it was to his nomination that the King affixed his seal, often without even, however briefly, exchanging thoughts with the other Ministers. Not infrequently did the King first become personally acquainted with the newly appointed Ministers at the swearing-in ceremony. In various ways the King could gather information about the person most suitable for the formation of the Cabinet; but once the King’s choice had settled on a particular statesman, the further selection of persons was often left to him alone and entrusted to him. For all further steps that would lead to this end, the initiative remained with him and him alone. He sought, he chose, he discussed the conditions. He was the one who brought the various statesmen, on whom he had fixed his attention and who were still strangers to each other, into connection with one another. He discussed with those summoned the course of conduct the new Cabinet would follow. In short, he and he alone was in fact the factotum in the formation. The determination of the governing program to be offered proceeded from him, and only the consent of those he had summoned together ratified what he had devised. To be sure, after this nomination, the program belonging to that nomination had to be presented to the Crown, and the formation, as far as necessary, had to be discussed with the Crown. It also happened sometimes that the King, before the final appointment, still wanted to hear the opinion of the President of the Council or of another Minister.

[182]

170 THE DEPARTMENTAL MINISTRIES. That the Crown demanded certain modifications, and that these were negotiated by the Cabinet-maker with his future colleagues—how could it be otherwise, for only the Crown could give its approval? But amidst all reverence for this royal prerogative, it had nonetheless become an increasingly fixed convention that the Cabinet-maker, and not the Crown, actually brought the formation to fruition. It was from him that the connection emanated, by which these now eight new Ministers were assembled into a cohesive whole and empowered to conduct a homogeneous administration.

§11. Homogeneity. One knows how Groen van Prinsterer constantly insisted on this homogeneous character of the administration. A Cabinet composed of Ministers each pursuing their own path and each seeking to advance their own political direction would soon become the plaything of the States-General. No “unified” force could emanate from it, because it was not one. Even in the ancient name of “Cabinet” lay the idea of a unity of firm cohesion. The more the involvement of the Government in the life of the people expanded, the more urgent the need to ensure that one Minister did not work against another.
