Even the Tinhoven-Tak Cabinet, may its memory be cursed, has taught us how fatal a lack of homogeneity can ultimately become for the entire Cabinet. But it follows from this that it was not enough to secure this homogeneity in the Cabinet’s program at the time of its formation; it was equally necessary, indeed even more necessary, to maintain that homogeneity throughout the further conduct of the administration. Even the order in which subjects were to be addressed had to be firmly assured, lest one Minister cause another to stumble. But all of this was absolutely unattainable if first one, then another Minister in the college presided. Whoever presides not only holds the gavel, but also always wields no small influence over the course of affairs. It is therefore of the utmost importance for all the Ministers together that this influence emanating from the leadership should always move in a uniform direction, remain in conformity with the program, and proceed without resistance or delay.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[183]

HOMOGENEITY.

Is it not already the case that more than one Cabinet has failed because the driving force was hidden away? Now, with the situation as it stands, how does one intend to have each of the Ministers preside in turn? Then, one after the other, Interior Affairs, Navy, War, Public Works, Colonies, and now also Agriculture, would come into play. Is it not obvious that someone who has served as Minister of Foreign Affairs can often appear, and will often need to appear, who as a diplomat has learned many countries more intimately, but precisely because of that is less well-versed in the political conditions of his own country? Does it not go the same way that a capable, clear-thinking, thoroughly knowledgeable naval officer can be appointed to the Department of the Navy, but has more experience of the dangers than he has explored the political situation of his own country? Is it not often the case that the same applies to the General who maintains the national defense on the Department of War as desired? Is it not entirely conceivable that an engineer appears for Public Works and for Agriculture, who is unparalleled in his specialty, yet is not sufficiently versed in the general legislation to lead the general administration? And may it not even be said of the Colonies that a statesman who is at home in Java often stands somewhat as a stranger to the political situation in the mother country? You understand me well. I do not in the least dispute the possibility that from the ranks of our diplomats, admirals and generals, engineers and colonial specialists, a man may arise who is a born statesman and will not easily be surpassed by anyone in the correctness of his policies and the many-sidedness of his insight for the conduct of affairs. But here too the exception confirms the rule, and the rule is and remains that statesmen like Thorbecke, the elder Heemskerk Azn., Kappeyne van de Coppello and Van Houten did not wear the uniform, were not technical specialists, and had not been guests in the East for long periods. Now, suppose the Cabinet-maker is a seasoned man for the presidency, but sitting with him in the council of Ministers is a brave admiral, a skilled diplomat or a capable policy engineer - does one not then feel that these specialists cannot be the chosen ones for the chairmanship, and that they are usually far too little involved in general politics to be able to take the lead in affairs? In addition, the States-General may also feel the need for...

[184]

THE DEPARTMENTAL MINISTRIES. It is not merely a matter of consulting the first Minister or feeling his pulse, but it seems urgently necessary to provoke a debate on the general policy of the country. In connection with the Colonies, it is also not better to think that, as it is now, the answer to the States-General is given in the name of the entire Cabinet by the first-called, to whose choice all colleagues owe their ministerial life. In not a single, truly Constitutionally-governed country is it otherwise understood, and the dissatisfaction of the States-General would not be dampened if, in such a matter, the Cabinet-former were silent and a military or technical Minister were put forward. The course of this matter can therefore be no other than it has actually been. The former of the Cabinet must be the designated chairman and remain so until the end, and under his presidency and leadership the corps of Ministers must naturally come together as a Cabinet Council.

§12. Number of Departments. The number of Departments remained at six in our country. There was a Department of Foreign Affairs, one for the Interior, one for Justice, the Navy, War and the Colonies. As a seventh Department, a new Department was added on November 6, 1877 under the name of Public Works, Trade and Industry, for which Tak van Poortvliet took office for the first time. And under the leadership of Minister De Meester, this newly established Department was again divided into two, and we received as the eighth Department that of Agriculture, Trade and Industry. In this way, the Interior was greatly relieved, since both Public Works and Agriculture, Trade and Industry had previously fallen under the Interior.

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[185]

Also in 1901–1905, a part of this new Department, namely the Labor division, had been included in the Interior, but after the crisis of 1905 this division went back to Public Works, and then to Agriculture, Trade and Industry. This number of Departments is not large for our country. The population has doubled compared to earlier, and what speaks most for this, the involvement of the Government in economic affairs, which could hardly be mentioned before, has been greatly expanded since then. Of more than one Department, it can be said that the work that now comes under the responsibility of more than one Minister amounts to at least double what it was in earlier days. At the political revolution in 1848, the ministerial burden was still so small that even a very busy man like Thorbecke did not stand for half of what later came under the responsibility of the Minister of the Interior. It is to this very serious increase in the second half of the previous century that it must be attributed that the Department of the Interior, as it existed under Thorbecke, soon split into three Departments. This increase of the Departments by two may therefore also be welcomed. It was necessary. In Belgium, the number stood at eleven in 1914. There had gradually been a separate Department for Arts and Sciences, one for Railways, one for Agriculture and Public Works, one for Industry and Labor added to the former regular Departments. So there is no question that the number of eight for the Netherlands would be excessive. The only question that remained was whether the separation of the Navy and War was still justified. Already in 1901, Dr. Kuyper had urged a merger of these two, and in 1912 Mr. Colijn was the head of both Departments, and there was the intention, after the election campaign, to unite them if the Cabinet remained in office. This idea of merging the two has not taken effect. In 1913, the gentlemen Rambonnet and Bosboom again appeared as separate Ministers, and the chance of a merger has certainly not improved. A final decision on this may only be expected when the Colonial Navy has obtained its definitive regulation. It is to be expected that then the necessity will be seen to separate the Colonial Navy from that in the Netherlands. And if one proceeds to this, the Dutch Navy will shrink accordingly, and will naturally merge with the National Defense, so that the merger of the Navy with War will immediately follow; which can also offer the chance that the name of War, which fits a country like ours so little, will pass into that of National Defense, as was already proposed in 1901 and tried again in 1912.

[186]

THE DEPARTMENTAL MINISTRIES.

§13. An Equal Voice for All. No less important a question is whether all Ministers in the Cabinet should have an equal voice in voting. At least when it comes to making serious political decisions, this can be a weighty matter. Without falling into one-sidedness, it may be asserted that the technical ministers as a rule were not the leading predecessors in the political arena. Now, among the eight Ministers currently in office, no fewer than four are of a more technical background. This applies not only to Public Works, Trade, Agriculture and Industry, but also to the Navy and War. Yet may not the Ministers who now sit in the Cabinet for these four national interests be regarded as experts in their own Departmental affairs, and have engineers and officers not so deeply ventured into politics that they can be considered competent to help decide the course of general politics? And yet, their present position in the Cabinet is no less than to give the decision. For when a weighty issue is being discussed in the Cabinet, on which opinions are divided, then a single vote can be the decisive factor, depending on which opinion that single vote joins. Suppose one is faced with the decision whether the franchise shall be general, or bound to the peasant class—it may well be that the Ministers of the Interior, Foreign Affairs and Justice would choose the peasant franchise, while the Minister of Finance would prefer a general franchise, and that the four technical Ministers would side with him. Then, of course, the decision given by the four technical Ministers, supported by the one of the four political Ministers who is placed at the head of Finance, and who may well have had more of a technical than a political career behind him, as was indeed the case with Minister Treub in the Cabinet of Cort van der Linden, and also with Minister Leyy after his retirement, would carry the day. Now, even those technical Ministers will have formed an opinion on such a political issue as, for example, the franchise, just like almost everyone else who is involved, but it must be admitted that they do not have the moral right to override the three more political Ministers with their view.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[187]

AN EQUAL VOICE FOR ALL—THE SALARIES OF MINISTERS. 175

It often happens in the Cabinet that such a technical Minister would prefer to abstain from voting, just as one of the political Ministers not infrequently abstains from voting on technical bills; but although the water cannot be made entirely pure here, one still feels that in completely equating the technical with the political Ministers, at a serious moment, there could lie a real danger. It may be assumed that as a rule the technical Ministers will go along with the President, but there is absolutely no guarantee for this, and the question therefore cannot be suppressed, whether a more specific regulation for the casting of the single vote in the Cabinet might not be advisable. In England, the rule is not the same for all Ministers, but for other reasons. With us, a solution to this difficulty might best be found in leaving the decision on political matters to the political Ministers, with the understanding that advice would be given by all, but that the decisive vote would remain with the four political gentlemen.

§14. The Salaries of Ministers. No less seriously must the question be raised whether the ministerial remuneration with us is not too low. A Minister with us receives little more than 11,000 guilders per year. It is true, it is called 12,000 guilders, but for the pension contribution almost a thousand guilders per year is deducted, so that it amounts to little more than 11,000 guilders. The judgment now on the question of whether this is sufficient or not, appears at once from the fact that even a century ago an amount of 12,000 guilders was fixed. Now, reckoning with the circumstance that the value of money in this full century has certainly declined by 50%, it follows that in 1815 a remuneration was considered necessary which would now be equivalent to almost 18,000 guilders. Add to this that we emerged in 1815 from the period of Napoleonic distress,

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”
[188]

THE DEPARTMENTAL MINISTRIES, which had even simplified life in the highest circles, and one immediately feels that a remuneration of 50% less than before can no longer suffice in any respect. It is indeed an undeniable fact that the Ministers, with the remuneration that now comes to them, cannot live in the Residence on the footing that their office, by its dignity, would demand. It has repeatedly occurred that Ministers who were entirely without means had to seek a cheap rented house in a remote neighborhood; had to bear the not inconsiderable costs of moving and furnishing from afar; and as Ministers had to participate in all kinds of subscriptions that recur every year. The possibility of official receptions was of course completely excluded for such persons. And without exaggeration, it can be said that the position these Ministers often have to take as citizens in The Hague is not in keeping with their high position. The complaint about this weighs all the more heavily, because more than one, when he became a Minister, lost 6,000 guilders a year by leaving a previous office, of which he only got 4,000 guilders back in his pension. If then an initial life without office followed for him, the actual loss was indeed not insignificant. Now it is true that the Minister of Foreign Affairs receives an extra allowance for the reception of diplomats with their ladies, but this did not help, for example, the Chairman of the Council of Ministers, who, by virtue of his special position, is constantly invited by foreign diplomats to their dinners and is almost obliged to give counter-dinners in return for maintaining his position. Such a situation must not be allowed to continue. The difficulty is now that a Minister in power does not readily go about begging the States General for higher salaries. It has therefore been considered to establish the ministerial remuneration constitutionally in the revision of the Constitution. This too could achieve the goal. Otherwise, the only ready way would be to seek an amendment. An amendment, introduced by a combination of members from all parties, would be the most delicate form to provide for the existing state of need, and the amount would then be set at 20,000 guilders, with a pension increase to an amount of 5,000 guilders, but without retroactive effect. The allowance of the Minister of Foreign Affairs would also then be increased from 8,000 to 10,000 guilders.

[189]

DEPARTMENTAL BUILDINGS - SALARIES OF CIVIL SERVANTS. So first a financial position would be granted to the Ministers that would be approximately equal to what had been allotted to them from 1813 onwards. Permanent ministerial residences, as one finds them in Belgium and elsewhere, are not to be recommended. Here the size of the family also plays a role, and one also has to reckon with the furnishings.

§15. Departmental buildings. More care has been taken for the departmental buildings in recent times. For many years, the condition for more than one Department was no longer tenable. Not only had the buildings gradually become too small to accommodate the ever-growing staff, but in more than one Department it was even found necessary to rent three, four separate houses in different parts of the city to accommodate a department or part of a department for which no accommodation could be found in the main building. Naturally, this was very inconvenient for the Minister, who, if he wished to see one of his subordinate officials, had to summon him by telephone from a great distance. Gradually, this is now improving, and this deserves praise. Our simple Residence does not call for monumental buildings. But even Switzerland then gives the example of how the dignity of the higher officials and the good communication between the Ministers and their officials can be provided for without falling into architectural extravagance. §16. Salaries of civil servants. That the civil servants too are concerned with improving their position lies in the spirit of our time, and that they created an association with that purpose is understandable. Only the question arises whether there might not be an improvement to be obtained by limiting the offices and somewhat reducing the number of officials. As it is now, one has too much to do, so that he has to try to make up in the evening at home what the State has neglected during the day.

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[190]

The departmental ministries remained unfinished, while the other often did not even know for half a day what he would undertake. It would be advisable to have a Commission examine the situation abroad and then design a complete reorganization of this service. This was also desirable in two other respects.

§17. Better organization of the offices. In the first place, the present organization of the departmental offices and the Minister requires much more than necessary to get involved in all kinds of small details. It goes without saying that this would be impractical on a large scale in great States. If one thinks of England, France, Germany and Russia, it needs no argument how in these countries a Minister, who had to get involved in everything that goes through his hands and waits for his decision and signature, would simply never be ready, even if he worked day and night. It follows from this that, if we knew how to follow the organization that is the rule in such large countries, the Minister would be saved a lot more time for his own study and consultation, as well as for granting hearings to associations and individuals. With us, this has all been conceived on a small scale and extremely economical, especially after 1848. Small modifications do not help either. Only a completely changed organization could bring us where we need to be with our Ministers. As for the division of labor, the Belgian custom pleads not a little, to appoint, if necessary, two different classes of higher-ranking officials in a ministerial office, of whom one class takes care of the administrative work, while the other devotes itself exclusively to legislative work. With us, both parts of the office task are mostly mixed together, and it is the same Referendary or Administrator who on the one hand has to oversee the entire administrative work, and on the other hand has to prepare the drafting of decisions or legislative proposals. This has the advantage on the one hand that the draftsman of the legislative documents knows how to connect them precisely with the existing practice, but on the other hand it cannot be denied that a spirit of routine easily creeps in here, which is not always favorable to the development of new ideas.

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the
[191]

BETTER ORGANIZATION OF BUREAUS. 179 Those who are born for administrative work, as a rule, cannot well adapt to what the legislative labor demands. Splitting could make up for what is currently lacking here. Something to which more value should be attached, which is now being prepared in the same Department by four or five Referendaries, each in a different style and manner of expression; which is not right. Even if later it might be met what was recommended in the previous chapter, and a Commission of formal control would come to maintain the unity in our legislation, it would still be desirable that in each bureau also for the first preparation of legislative proposals two or three special officials were appointed, who would be more naturally designated for such work. Finally, a word is still needed about the titles of the officials. There has gradually arisen a tendency to grant certain honorary titles to the more privileged, in order to distinguish who stands higher or lower. It was first a Referendary for each division of the Bureau, and further there were Head Clerks, Clerks or Assistant Clerks, while at the head of the entire personnel stood the Secretary-General. Gradually, however, the new title of Administrator has emerged, and it is now becoming more customary for a very meritorious Referendary, or even a Referendary with more years of service, to have himself announced as an Administrator. Elsewhere one finds a Councilor-Advisor, a Treasurer-General, etc. Of course, this multiplication of official titles is not, and in any case the title of Administrator for the head of a Division is not very happily chosen. An Administrator is someone who is more specifically charged with the administration, and although the chief of a Division also carries out certain administration, it can hardly be claimed that this is his main occupation. The administrative part of his task is often very subordinate, and least of all expresses the high quality of his official position. There would therefore be little lost if we were relieved of this title of Administrator for the Chief of a Division. If two or more Referendaries are working in the same division, the title of Councilor-Advisor for the chief would be much more appropriate. The Referendary refers to the Minister; one who not only referred, but also advised, would after all stand higher by nature.

[192]

180 THE GENERAL ACCOUNTING OFFICE.

CHAPTER VI
THE GENERAL ACCOUNTING OFFICE.

§1. The Two Interested Parties. The Accounting Office did not attract much attention in our circles. Only since the appointment to that Chamber of one of our most popular Statesmen was attention also more focused on this College among us. A brief discussion of this governing body may therefore find its place here. In an ever-increasing measure, Finance plays a role of high importance on the government terrain. The state budget keeps climbing in figures. In the Provincial States, ever greater amounts are also entrusted to the authorities. And even the Municipal Councils, at least in the larger cities, have gradually come to dispose of such colossal annual resources that at this lowest level of government too, finance is speaking louder and louder. Here, two kinds of questions naturally come to the fore, one of a political, the other of an administrative nature. From a political point of view, the struggle between the Government and the People arises here, and from an administrative consideration one stands before the question whether the country’s monies are being handled with strict honesty. Yet, however much these two questions may diverge, as far as the accounting is concerned, they have been brought under the protection of one and the same college, and this one college is the Accounting Office. Two interested parties are involved here. The Crown-bearer as Head of the Government must obtain certainty that the organs of his government are conducting the financial management in the most punctual manner, and on the other hand the People must be able to control whether the monies of the People, which have been ceded by the Representatives for the Government, are indeed being spent in conformity with the agreement made. The agreement thus intended between Sovereign and People comes about through the parliamentary approval of the Budget Laws, both as regards receipts and expenditures. Hence, to the Accounting Office is entrusted the task of controlling whether the agreement thus made is actually being observed.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[193]

THE TWO INTERESTED PARTIES. 181 The States-General must remain in control, to investigate in detail what has been collected, and for what purposes and in what manner the collected funds have been spent. This control also has an administrative character, but it is primarily of a political nature. Suppose for a moment that the Government had at its disposal, from mines, funds, or any other source whatsoever, sufficient means so that there would be no question of levying taxes, then the People would effectively be at the mercy of the Government’s discretion, and their rights would be seen to dwindle and perish. The rights that the people now possess, and in which they are strongest, they have successively conquered to a richer degree by the fact that the administration was impoverished, had no private means, and was therefore almost entirely dependent on the States-General, who were empowered on behalf of the People to grant or refuse the necessary means to the Government. This very right to grant or refuse the necessary means now naturally demands, as the key to the lock, that the States-General can investigate whether the collection and expenditure of the granted means takes place in accordance with the agreements made. The indispensable control thus has a completely different character than the central control the Crown-bearer needs regarding the conduct of his organs or officials. Nominally it is the King who collects, and the King who spends the money. But the piecemeal involvement is much too extensive for him to be able to administer it himself. He is therefore compelled to investigate and examine whether his administrative organs have acted honestly. From history, one knows, unfortunately only too well, how shamefully this has been defrauded, if not here, then elsewhere. In order to be personally responsible, the Bearer of the Crown is therefore obliged to closely monitor his organs or officials in their housekeeping. He must know that he is not being deceived. In itself, it would therefore have been very conceivable that two control colleges had appeared; one to control the officials on behalf of the King, and the other to exercise control over the administration itself on behalf of the Representatives. The first would then have been a purely administrative control college; while the second control college would have had to provide a guarantee that the administration was carried out in accordance with the agreements made.

[194]

THE GENERAL AUDIT OFFICE. 182 It was evident that the Government had adhered to the agreement made.

§2. The two controls united. Nothing, however, prevents these two, principally distinct controls, from being entrusted to the management of one and the same college, as has indeed happened almost everywhere. The means by which this double and in its nature distinct control can nevertheless be entrusted to one and the same college, has been found among us in this, that the Second Chamber would offer the candidates for membership of the college to the Crown, and that the Crown would make the choice from these candidates presented by the Second Chamber. Hereby two goals were simultaneously achieved. If the Second Chamber presented a list of three candidates, and the King was obliged to take one of these three as the new member, then the Chamber was assured that the chosen one would be a proper controller, also in connection with the rights of Parliament, and on the other hand the honor of the Majesty of the Crown was saved, in that the appointment of the new member did not proceed from the Chamber, but from the Crown. This honoring of the dignity of the Crown was further strengthened by the fact that the King appointed the Chairman entirely freely and that the appointment of the lower assistants also remained with the Crown. The King could, without fear that this arrangement would threaten his dignity, acquiesce in this idea, because the control that he himself exercised over his own officials could never be sharp enough, and because, in connection with this, precisely the appointment of the members from the nomination of the Second Chamber provided him with a guarantee that it would not be a control in name only. It did indeed require some formal effort to incorporate the precise formulation of this double control into the Fundamental Law and the statutes, but in substance there was no dispute between the parties on this point. All parties stood for the same control, and the combination once introduced, to have the Second Chamber propose three candidates and the King choose from such a trio, generally satisfied. Of course, it had to be determined at that time that a member of the Audit Office could not hold any other function that would involve him publicly with the expenditure of state funds. The chosen one had to free himself from such ties that bound him financially, and refrain from acting in various functions, but in return he received a lifetime appointment, and could only be dismissed from his office by a judicial decision, even in case of mental weakness or for whatever reason. The membership of this College became all the more desirable when it was further stipulated that the Audit Office would draw up the official report of the state finances for the States General, and even received the right to submit proposals for the improvement of discovered errors, not only in the figures, but also in the regulations, to the Government. Although this has not often occurred, the right to do so has nevertheless been granted to the Audit Office, and this has enhanced its position from the outset. It is not merely a chamber of accounting, but also a chamber of financial consultation and advice. §3. Control over Province and Municipality. The extension, however, that has been given to the control exercised by it, in that it not only oversees the state finances, but also those of the Provinces and Municipalities, after auditing the administration conducted, was not without objection. As for the Provinces, there is no objection to this. The provincial administration can hardly be judged otherwise than by the state, as standing above the Provincial Estates. If necessary, one could have given the Provincial Estates themselves the right to call into being their own Provincial Audit Office, but this would have been unnecessary, cumbersome and not sufficiently justified in a strict sense. Hence, there is hardly any criticism to be made against the control that the Audit Office also exercises over the provincial administration. It is different, however, with the control over the municipal administrations. That this control was also entrusted to the Audit Office was not as it should be, and seems to be due to the often disastrous influence that the spirit of the French administration has exerted on us. In France they have the Cour des Comptes, a college to which the high position of a high judicial college has been assigned, and this college now exercises its criticism not only over the state finances, but also over those of the municipalities and departments. This has led to a centralization of control that is not in keeping with the self-government of the municipalities and provinces. With us, the Audit Office has been given a similar task, without the justification of a high judicial position, and this has led to a centralization of control that is not in keeping with the self-government of the municipalities and provinces.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[195]

CONTROL OVER PROVINCE AND MUNICIPALITY. 183 brought into connection with the expenditure of state funds. The chosen one had to free himself from such ties that bound him financially, and refrain from acting in various functions, but in return he received a lifetime appointment, and could only be dismissed from his office by a judicial decision, even in case of mental weakness or for whatever reason. The membership of this College became all the more desirable when it was further stipulated that the Audit Office would draw up the official report of the state finances for the States General, and even received the right to submit proposals for the improvement of discovered errors, not only in the figures, but also in the regulations, to the Government. Although this has not often occurred, the right to do so has nevertheless been granted to the Audit Office, and this has enhanced its position from the outset. It is not merely a chamber of accounting, but also a chamber of financial consultation and advice.

§3. Control over Province and Municipality. The extension, however, that has been given to the control exercised by it, in that it not only oversees the state finances, but also those of the Provinces and Municipalities, after auditing the administration conducted, was not without objection. As for the Provinces, there is no objection to this. The provincial administration can hardly be judged otherwise than by the state, as standing above the Provincial Estates. If necessary, one could have given the Provincial Estates themselves the right to call into being their own Provincial Audit Office, but this would have been unnecessary, cumbersome and not sufficiently justified in a strict sense. Hence, there is hardly any criticism to be made against the control that the Audit Office also exercises over the provincial administration. It is different, however, with the control over the municipal administrations. That this control was also entrusted to the Audit Office was not as it should be, and seems to be due to the often disastrous influence that the spirit of the French administration has exerted on us. In France they have the Cour des Comptes, a college to which the high position of a high judicial college has been assigned, and this college now exercises its criticism not only over the state finances, but also over those of the municipalities and departments. This has led to a centralization of control that is not in keeping with the self-government of the municipalities and provinces. With us, the Audit Office has been given a similar task, without the justification of a high judicial position, and this has led to a centralization of control that is not in keeping with the self-government of the municipalities and provinces.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[196]

FOREIGN AFFAIRS. Not only over the finances of the State and its Departments, but also over those of the Municipalities. In France this was understandable, since the spirit of centralization in that country had almost completely destroyed the autonomy of the Municipalities and the Provinces. But precisely for that reason it was a violation of the autonomous rights that here in this country always belonged to the Provinces and the local Municipalities. The control over the municipal finances should have rested with the Provincial States. This needs to be emphasized more, because it later became clear how almost impossible and always so defective such control by the Court of Audit over the municipal administrations had to be. It is not well possible for a body like the Court of Audit to examine in detail thousands of administrations with the required accuracy. The Law recognizes this and therefore grants the Court of Audit the right to limit itself each year to the control of only a few Municipalities. But this double fault clings to this, that the control is not constant or comprehensive enough, and that moreover in a Municipality that has just been audited, carelessness is likely to creep in. For it will not come up for review again for some time. Chapter VII. FOREIGN AFFAIRS.

§1. Decline in the 19th century. Even after what we have said best about the Departments, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs still requires separate discussion. In the middle of the previous century this Department had become so insignificant and so deeply sunk, that Mr. Elout van Soeterwoude in the States-General once called the Minister of this Department: “To know something, one must have learned it!” We had better not mention the name of this Minister. He took office in the second week of January 1861, and had to hand over his portfolio already on March 14 of the same year. In 1878 we then wrote about this sad Department in our Program (see 1907 edition, p. 312 ff.):

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[197]

DECLINE IN THE 19TH CENTURY. 185

“From a Christian-historical standpoint, a sound, capable, energetic diplomacy, far from being a luxury, is rather the absolute indispensable instrument to safeguard its independence and to establish the organic connection with other peoples and governments, and to make each people cooperate in its part in the common task that rests on Christian Europe: namely, the sanctification of the law, the combating of Paganism and Mohammedanism; the honoring of the human in human society; and the upholding of the honor of God! But the establishment and maintenance of such a diplomacy requires incredible effort; and this not less because of the disruption of the European balance, whereby almost every calculation about the outcome of things, even in the nearest future, must fail. For more than before, it is now a requirement for such a diplomacy that it be composed of a corps of civil servants who, in the abundance of knowledge, maturity of development, quickness of perception, wealth of invention, and pliability of form, offer the data for the fulfillment of their almost unmanageable task. A task that is no less than to trace back to the first formative movements every movement that might turn against the interests or the security of the State; to extend the influence of one’s own country as far as possible with prudent policy; to create as favorable an impression as possible about us with cabinets and peoples; and further to intervene at every given moment where inaction would be the loss or ruin of our interests for the future. These demands are not being met now. Hardly any single foreign cabinet do we have at this moment a diplomat of sufficient influence; and if there is influence, it is more of a negative kind.”

[198]

FOREIGN AFFAIRS: SOCIAL AND POLITICAL SIGNIFICANCE; HONORING THE PERSON, BUT WITHOUT BENEFIT FOR OUR COUNTRY. What is lacking is, in the personnel, a certain capability; in the Department, unity of thought and willpower; and in the States-General, appreciation. It is now repeatedly evident how even the Belgian diplomats outshine us; how we become the victims of the cunning of others in almost every negotiation; how one regards us more as supernumeraries, but no longer counts us as a living people; yes, it has even become evident from irrefutable decisions that there was already talk of the annexation of our country, without the Court knowing anything about it. This fate even pursues us in our Colonies; the English settlers are too clever for us; and we were recently tricked in a very cunning way by Spain, which cheerfully accepted the Sovereignty of the Sulu Islands, and then sent us a rather comical report afterwards. And when one moreover considers the incredible damage that the right and wrong negotiations with England have caused us, both on the Gold Coast of Africa and on the north coast of Sumatra, while we are already lagging behind on Decima;—then there will surely already be enough on the table to support our serious complaint. A complaint which, however, for the sake of our calling in the European community of states, we cannot even decide to make here, but which also needs to be extended to a very serious protest against the cowardly silence and the dishonoring inaction in which one seeks salvation in every emerging dispute and keeps aloof from every alliance for the future.

[199]

FOUR DEMANDS - OUR POSITION IN 1905. 187

In the Transvaal question, yes, even earlier when Denmark was half and Nassau completely destroyed, our Government should have spoken; when the question of Mohammedanism was recently settled, the second colonial power should have presented itself to the rulers; and, what is more, now that it has become evident from both the facts and the diplomatic secret archives in a convincing way that the existence of the States of the second rank is indeed threatened, our Dutch diplomacy should long ago have taken the initiative for a policy of alliance that would again make it clear that we still are. “With what prostration and hand-folding,” as the Prophet says, one does not get there.

§2. Four Demands.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...

Courage counts, especially for smaller peoples, above life!

But in order to be able to reach such a more active diplomacy, and through it to a more powerful diplomatic action, this would also be the fourth demand to be made without delay. Namely: 1. that henceforth for the Ministry of Foreign Affairs one should never again take a titular, but always a real Minister; 2. that a college of very knowledgeable, experienced men should be added to Foreign Affairs, to maintain the unity of the traditions and the continuation of the threads, when the Cabinets change; 3. that our missions should be so well paid that even an unprosperous man of talent could accept them; and 4. that to every important mission the necessary staff should be attached to allow the envoy to remain an envoy. Think of what Venice once did! Think of our own diplomats, when they were not yet all noblemen. And what figures were among them then! Think also of Switzerland, which sometimes outshines more powerful kingdoms through its excellent consulates!

§3. Our Position in 1905. Even when I had to lay down the portfolio of the Interior in 1905, and undertook my journey “Around the Old World Sea,” it struck me again and again in the East how weakly we were represented in Diplomacy. At that time, for all the parts of the world together, we did not have more than thirteen regular envoys with the title of “Extraordinary Envoy and Plenipotentiary Minister.” In Persia only a Minister Resident. And the same in Romania. And not otherwise in Greece. In other States it was even more meager. In the three great powers of Europe, Russia, Germany, and France, we had only one envoy each.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[200]

FOREIGN AFFAIRS. The South American Republics of Argentina, Uruguay, and Paraguay had no more than one Consul-General, with the personal title of chargé d’affaires. In Brazil, a similar Consul-General, even without a further title. In Chile, it was no different. In Colombia, our Consuls had at least, as in Argentina, not the rank of chargé d’affaires, but still the personal honorary title thereof. In Ecuador, we did it with a pure Consulate. In Egypt, especially from the perspective of Islam, we did it with a Consul who was called a political agent. In Greece, at the time, Mr. Van Lennep still had no other than the personal title of chargé d’affaires. In Morocco, where even Belgium had an ambassador, we made do with a Consulate. In Panama and Paraguay, our Consulate again bore the personal title of chargé d’affaires. In Serbia, there was nothing but a Consul. In Siam, a Consul with the personal quality of chargé d’affaires. In short, as sparingly as possible, and yet this is still our entire arrangement. Where no Ambassador was needed, we did it with a Consul, and to give our embarrassed Consul still some position, he then received a mere title of higher rank. Now and then, this does involve a small promotion for the individual, but the result is that we get away with it as stingily as possible, and that is precisely why our diplomatic position in many capitals, for whom we represent ourselves, is often so painful.

§4. Guilty Frugality. It constantly emerges how stingy we are with Foreign Affairs, as is evident, for example, from comparing our budget with that of Belgium. Our entire budget for Foreign Affairs, as submitted for 1916, amounted to no more than ƒ1,508,436, while in Belgium for 1914 an amount of 4,990,686 francs was granted. So in Belgium, nearly a million guilders more. Our country had not kept pace with the times in this respect. When we re-emerged as an independent power in 1813/15, there was also great financial scarcity among us. Napoleon had also greatly impoverished our country. That is why, at the time, they did the Foreign Affairs as sparingly as possible. Something that stood out especially for our country, since our ancient

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.
[201]

CULPABLE EXTRAVAGANCE.189 Even the Republic often sent out Ambassadors. There could then be no more striking testimony of poverty given by the then Government, than by now doing away with such a highly ordinary representation. It would have been better in the past, if the Belgian treachery and Belgium’s defection had not once again financially placed us in a desperate situation, and had not once again damaged our prestige abroad. And while Thorbecke, with his followers, has led us abroad in a better direction since 1848, the bourgeoisie who triumphed under Thorbecke had neither eye nor heart for diplomacy, and not the slightest increase, however small, could be proposed in the Chamber, or in the period after ‘48 the proposal would repeatedly encounter fierce opposition in the States-General. There is also nothing in the salaries of the diplomats that strikes many eyes. In Japan, for example, the Minister-Resident, in accordance with the 1916 budget, receives only ƒ6,000, but he also receives an “allowance for residence” of ƒ17,000, and then another ƒ2,700 for an interpreter. So in total for this one mission ƒ25,700. In Turkey, a salary of ƒ22,000 must be paid as salary for the Minister-Resident, with his assistants, and then an additional ƒ12,500 per year for housing, etc. So a total of ƒ34,500. If one now considers that even a Prime Minister here at home only receives ƒ12,000, of which almost ƒ1,000 goes for a pension, one understands that whoever does not have a clear idea of the standard of living in Eastern lands and has no conception of what Diplomacy consumes for its highly elevated standard of living, must see in the amounts, which exceed triple, extravagance and unnecessary luxury. Even in Brussels, only ƒ13,800 was paid as salary for the Legation with its personnel, but in addition to this, there were also these salaries for residence allowances, no less than ƒ15,000. So a total of ƒ28,800, even though Belgium is counted among the small states and the standard of living in Brussels is far from high. Yet this amount also seems far too high. After all, until recent years, our then Minister in Brussels lived in such a small bourgeois house on a Boulevard, that one did not understand how the Minister of Foreign Affairs allowed such a low descent of our Minister in social life. Of course, this economical

[202]

FOREIGN AFFAIRS. Lending money for our representation not only adversely affects the position of our representatives, but also the position of our country itself in the eyes of foreigners. In more than one environment, it was clearly evident that our envoys were not taken seriously at all. Private wealth often made up for what was lacking, but then one had to be very rich to keep up with the higher social circles, e.g. in London. Our envoy in London receives a salary of 8,000 guilders and is also reimbursed 26,000 guilders for accommodation. Together, 34,000 guilders. But even this seemingly quite high amount cannot enable an envoy to maintain his position in an aristocratic milieu, where only the “upper ten thousands” are considered ordinarily wealthy. A ton a year is the least an envoy who wants to fully participate can dispose of in London.

§5. Demand of our past. Now a parsimony that goes against all this is to the detriment of the country that makes itself guilty of it. In some capitals, where our Government could send a man of the highest personal talent, one had to make do with less personal fortune, so that our country would not fall behind in the international arena and not suffer under it. For as a rule, it cannot be otherwise, or a representative who remains socially too low, lacks the influence that could have come to us for good. This can still be said of the smaller States that lag behind and can be content with a lower position, such as Portugal and Denmark; but such a barely noticeable one cannot be tolerated in a country like the Netherlands. Let us never forget, however, that our glorious, so mighty past must always urge us, however frugal we may be ourselves, to uphold our honor and name of old vis-à-vis the foreigner. Secondly, it must not be overlooked that our position between Germany, England and France envelops us with the all-encompassing politics of these three mighty States, so that we can never withdraw ourselves from the great international movement except in name and appearance, and are actually always involved in the mighty international action. This applies all the more in the third place, because our Colonies throw an disproportionately large weight into the scale for the smallness of our country.

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[203]

DEMAND OF OUR PAST. 191 For half a century one could still conceal this, when in East Asia there was little more than a silent grave and we could make a fairly powerful display with a couple of not too large warships in the Japanese port of Shimonoseki. We were still a small potentate in Decima. But now all this has entered into an entirely new phase. All East Asia is teeming with new vital energy. In this action, Japan took the leading role de jure. The effort to maintain our centuries-old position in the Archipelago will therefore in itself be extremely burdensome, but if in addition we now appear inferior in Siam with our diplomat and in Tokyo make do with a Minister Resident, while Japan maintains an envoy in The Hague, then we are weakening our own position even in a region where the decision on the future of the Netherlands will fall. To this is added in the fourth place that we are even behind Belgium in our aristocratic circles. Belgium has four times as many nobility, and the nobility in Belgium is often not low. The latest revelations that came to us from Berlin confirmed the suspicion that, generally speaking, the Belgian representatives occupied a fairly high position. The two Belgian envoys who stood out here as particularly knowledgeable and sharp-sighted were Baron Greindl in Berlin and Baron Guillaume in Paris. I had the honor to get to know both of them personally, and of both it must be testified that they were certainly not low. Now, where our envoys come from such a much smaller circle, it could not be otherwise but that they had to often lag behind. We too know honorable exceptions, but it is precisely the low salaries that are often the reason why one can hardly find an envoy for London or Paris outside the circle of the very wealthy. A decent man from the ordinary bourgeois class, who lacks capital, cannot go to London or Tokyo, to Berlin or Paris, or he will stand too low in social position in these luxurious cities to give our country what it must aim for through diplomatic channels.

[204]

192 FOREIGN AFFAIRS.

§6. No Longer Influential Envoy. It must never be lost sight of that the lamentable division of states into great powers and small countries already makes the position of a Dutch Envoy so depressed in the diplomatic circle of the great world powers. It is always the Envoys of the very great Powers who take the lead in affairs. They are the Ambassadors who move and peer on the great international chessboard, and ordinary Envoys of small States are perhaps invited to balls and dinners, but the actual handling of affairs goes on without them. And when it now happens that in an upcoming action their country is very closely involved, then the Envoy of this country is indeed summoned, but as a rule not otherwise than to ascertain how the old saying “vobis pro vobis” still holds good here. Often, in fact, it has already been decided about them before they are summoned, and they have to experience anew the painful experience that a smaller State is no longer regarded as a full-fledged entity by the Great Powers. It is precisely this that should move the Government of a Small State to not further depress the already so depressed position of its Envoys, whether by giving them a lower title or by not enabling them at least to “do something” socially, as it is called. Whoever loves the future of his country must therefore constantly protest against all stinginess and even against the too great economy, when it comes to maintaining our diplomatic position abroad at least somewhat at the desired level. Talented, powerful figures among our Diplomats should not be forced to live in cities with less splendor, while the more important diplomatic posts must remain with those who can supplement from their own fortune what the Government cannot provide them. As Calvinists, with an eye on our glorious past, we have always chosen the economical party in this respect. In the diplomatic council of Europe, we may not have set the tone, but we must still participate. If in such an influential circle a tone prevails that makes life dear, yes, overstrained, and with this grand style of living, the Netherlands too must go along socially, if it wants to participate in the political action in that circle. Certainly, then, private fortune can be drawn upon; but what is not allowed is that we have to make do with a well-to-do, but still second-class Envoy on a post of importance, when a man of the first order could have been sent, but who now has to remain inactive because the living allowance prevents him from maintaining the social standard. It should at least be so that, where it is a question of an important post between two diplomats, the one rich but second-class as a person, and the other an easy prince, but too little endowed to dare to undertake it, an adequate extra allowance could guarantee us the representation by the first-class diplomat.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.
[205]

NON-DIPLOMATS. - ROYAL VISIT. 193

§7. Non-diplomats. Not entirely reprehensible is the method hitherto followed here and still elsewhere, to send a delegate outside the Diplomatic Service, but otherwise very suitable, when a particularly serious question is at stake. Just think of Cats, when he appeared with a special mission from our Government in the English House of Commons. Nowadays it is often one of the Ministers who is entrusted with such a mission by the Council of Ministers, or it also happens that the Sovereign of the country himself provokes an encounter with the Head of State with whom one wishes to come to a special arrangement. Abroad, the good rule is always followed that on such a royal visit to the Court of another country, the Minister of Foreign Affairs, or even the Prime Minister, accompanies the Sovereign. That this is not customary with us can only be explained by the fact that the Netherlands has become so insignificant on the international stage that even a royal visit was deemed to be little more than a demonstration of friendly relations. §8.
