Royal Visit. In this, the custom is, as far as the Small States are concerned, that the Minister of Foreign Affairs, or even the Prime Minister, accompanies the Sovereign. But with us, this has not been the case up to now. Our Sovereigns have always traveled abroad without being accompanied by the Minister of Foreign Affairs or the Prime Minister. This has undoubtedly contributed to the fact that the international position of the Netherlands has become so insignificant. For a small country, a royal visit is always a diplomatic event of the first order, and the presence of the Minister of Foreign Affairs or the Prime Minister is then indispensable, both to give the visit the necessary luster and to use the opportunity for political negotiations. The fact that our Sovereigns have always traveled abroad without this accompaniment has undoubtedly contributed to the fact that the international position of the Netherlands has become so insignificant.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[206]

194 FOREIGN AFFAIRS. It is also often the case that the Sovereign, at least when he pays an official visit, is expected to refrain from personally involving himself in political affairs. And this can indeed be the case. The hereditary monarchy has undoubtedly provided us with the very serious advantage of an uninterrupted transition of the Crown upon death or abdication, but this advantage is always offset by the disadvantage that the Bearer of the Crown sometimes lacks the age and perhaps even the competence to immerse himself in the great political questions. This has also led elsewhere to the established rule that the Sovereign, when traveling for an official visit, is accompanied by one of his Ministers. As far as our country is concerned, however, our political international position had sunk so far below the usual level that such an accompaniment was hardly ever considered anymore. This cannot be applauded. In the Constitutional Monarchy, it is unacceptable for the Sovereign to undertake any act of government without it being covered by one of the Ministers. It is of no avail that a Sovereign, when visiting another Court, engages in all kinds of discussions about the mutual relations between the two States. One of two things must be true: Either the Minister of Foreign Affairs of such a Sovereign would have to be such a lifeless puppet that he simply says yes and amen to whatever his Sovereign sees fit to discuss and arrange; or else, upon the Sovereign’s return from his official visit, the question would arise whether the Minister could go along with what his Sovereign had somehow agreed upon. And if it turned out that he could not, then the position of the Sovereign vis-à-vis the Sovereign of the visited Court would be compromised. One could object that the Sovereign setting out on a journey could have discussed beforehand with his Minister what might come up between him and the Head of the other State; but anyone who is even slightly aware of the complications in which all international questions inevitably become entangled, will immediately admit that such a prior discussion of the issues that may arise is in no way sufficient to remove the objections that may subsequently arise and to arrive at firm conclusions. There is no other explanation to be found for the custom prevailing at our Court to leave the Minister at home, than that

[207]

THE PEACE CONFERENCE. 195 Such a princely visit has now, for nearly three-quarters of a century, been granted no other character than that of a royal international formality. Something which, in the meantime, even according to the isolating conception, sometimes seemed strange, if the foreign Prince did not appear unaccompanied at the return visit.

§9. The Peace Conference. But however this may have occurred in the previous century, due to our international insignificance, at least since July 29, 1899, a change has come over the Peace Conference in a way that is more favorable than burdensome for us. It certainly did not answer the expectations first aroused. In its first appearance, this Conference undoubtedly brought honor. Whether the choice that fixed the holding of this Conference on The Hague originated from the Tsar of Russia himself is not easy to determine. It is well known, and became increasingly clear later, that the statesman from whom the oracle on the field of international law went out for Russia, Mr. De Martens, and also the then Minister of Foreign Affairs, Count Muravieff, had a decided preference for the Netherlands. It was in the nature of the matter that the Tsar could not summon the Conference in Petersburg, nor could there be any talk of its assembling in the capital or residence of any of the other Great Powers. The choice had to fall on one of the smaller European states; and when it struck here for the first time, that where Brussels generally seemed to possess the greatest attraction for international gatherings, the Tsar nevertheless gave preference to the Netherlands. It had to be a small state, and one that lay in the middle of Europe and was easily accessible, and in this respect the choice could not be other than between Belgium, the Netherlands, and Switzerland. That the Tsar gave preference to a monarchically governed country could not be surprising, and when it came down to a choice between Brussels and The Hague, it was just as historically understandable that the Netherlands was ultimately preferred. When it finally came down to a choice between Belgium and the Netherlands, it could not be overlooked that Russia had never entered into closer contact with Belgium, while, on the other hand, the historical traditions concerning Tsar Peter at Zaandam, the monument at Bergen, and also the marriage of William II with the Grand Duchess Anna Paulowna, had always kept the bond between the two states alive.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[208]

196 FOREIGN AFFAIRS. For our politically sympathetic public, on the other hand, the choice of the Tsar remained a surprise. We were once and for all—also with regard to the Luxembourg question—so accustomed to being treated by Europe as a negligible quantity in the corner, that no one was really prepared for such a high honor to befall us. There was also a general feeling that the circumstance that here the Crown was borne by a Woman had promoted the Tsar’s choice, and with a view to this as well, there was something in the Tsar’s decision that attracted us.

§10. Disappointment. Now the Conference has brought forth what was born, certainly did not answer the first expectations so highly strung. Rather, it turned out to be a disappointment from all sides. The cruelest disappointment struck the first point in the Circular of January 11, 1898, placed in the foreground by the Russian government itself. For in this first point, the Tsar proposed that all States should set limits to the steady expansion of armies and fleets, by mutually agreeing on a maximum and regulating the expansion of this maximum. Naturally, this was a point that would dominate the whole outcome of the Conference. If, out of mutual rivalry, as happened after 1871, one proceeded to arm oneself ever more strongly, and in the matter of national armaments to engage in an endless competition, then it was to be foreseen that, whatever else the Conference might propose or attempt in the interest of the world’s peace, it would ultimately always end up again, and even on an ever more threatening war. Yet the Military Commission in the Conference could not come to any other decision than to reject this first and most important proposal of the Russian government. For while a resolution was still adopted that was intended to be a bandage for the bleeding, this could of course not remedy the evil.

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.
[209]

DISAPPOINTMENT - PERSEVERING NONETHELESS The Conference, so it was stated in this resolution, expressed the wish that the Powers would further consider the possibility of limiting military forces. Indeed, a second resolution was even more forceful: “The Conference is of the opinion that the reduction of the military burdens that now weigh upon the world is highly desirable, both in the interest of the material and moral progress of nations.” But all this was mere rhetoric, and whoever—even roughly—compares the state of military burdens in 1899 with that of 1914, when the current war broke out, faces a historically so completely reversed result, that one can hardly come to any other conclusion than that what Russia placed in the foreground has been pushed so far into the background that the very opposite has now befallen us. What began so sadly did not fare any better thereafter. One knows how, at England’s insistence, even the delegates of the fully independent Free State in South Africa were excluded from the Conference; how Minas Tscheras and his fellow delegates from Paris were not even allowed to submit a petition; how only weak resolutions were passed; and how then, at almost every point, so many reservations were made that in the end one only landed at a very shaky outcome. Undoubtedly, the position of our Cabinet at the time was extremely difficult, and one cannot deny our then Minister of Foreign Affairs the honor that he did what he could to have the Conference received with all due respect; but still, it is difficult to completely exculpate a certain weakness in the leadership. What was lacking was the then so indispensable energy and decisiveness. But here too, one must be fair. What made our position at the Conference stand so wretchedly was above all the painful insignificance to which our so-called international position had been degraded since 1830. From the Anti-Revolutionary side, there was also no cheering in the Second Chamber over the outcome.

§11. Persevering nonetheless. Yet this does not release us from the very serious obligation to, even after this disappointment...

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[210]

Although the outcome was in many ways so painful, we must continue to support the Peace Conference and the Court of Arbitration. Our Residence has now, thanks to the first Conference, become the central hub of the international peace effort; and not least Carnegie’s royal gift for the Peace Palace in The Hague gave preference to our Residence over competing cities. It could not have been more unfortunate that just after the opening of this Palace, the bitterest war that European history has known broke out, but our country was, thank God, still able to stay out of it, and our honor remained unscathed as the central position for all peace efforts. The Court of Arbitration also remains significant for our country. It was, however, very annoying that our Government only provided the Court of Arbitration with an extremely ordinary house on the Prinsengracht. This could not but give the impression that the Court was being treated with disdain. Applicants from abroad must have been more than once irritated by this, and no one who returned home after a session at the Court could have failed to get the impression that better accommodation would surely have been provided elsewhere. It also seems that this neglect of what should have been our calling has weakened the appeal of the Court for emerging disputes. If one asks oneself what international disputes have been resolved since 1900, and in which of these cases the decision of the Court of Arbitration has been sought, one inevitably gets the impression that all matters of importance have passed us by, and that one has only resorted to our Court for very incidental and insignificant disputes. Especially when the Russian fleet, on its voyage from the Baltic to Japan, recklessly fired on the English fishing fleet encountered off the English coast without proper investigation, it was fortunately agreed not to draw the sword over this, and to appeal to arbitration, but one passed by the Court in The Hague and called for an arbitral tribunal in Paris. This was naturally a slap in the face for our Court of Arbitration, which cast doubt on whether we would ever see the bar formed in our Residence for anything other than trivial cases.

[211]

The lack of cooperation. To consider this failure a political mistake would be to give in to this tendency. Making the most of it is the self-evident rule for every undertaking, which we and our government must now follow if we wish to act wisely. As far as it depends on us, we must exploit the significance of the Court of Arbitration and the fact that the World Congresses have sought their seat here, in our national interest. Although it is not enough in degree, we have nonetheless risen in significance in international politics since 1900, compared to the period of 1830–1899. Therefore, not relinquishing what has been gained must now be the lesson of international wisdom for our country.

§12. The lack of cooperation. Whether it will be feasible, with this goal in mind, to transform the Court of Arbitration into a highly international Court of Justice, may be doubted. The Pacifists are pressing for this, and even if the Court of Arbitration were to continue to exist alongside such a world Court of Justice for cases that did not fall under the jurisdiction of the Court of Justice, it would still certainly put an end to not a few disputes and conflicts. However, the good fruit of such a Court of Justice could only be expected if it proved possible to subject all States to its jurisdiction. But precisely this may be doubted. If such a Court of Justice were to act, a fixed rule would of course have to be established for the manner in which the rulings of this Court would be introduced, implemented, and executed. On a fairly broad scale, an international code of criminal procedure would even have to be promulgated; and in addition, it would have to be determined in what way eventual amendments to this code could be made. Now if a superior power were conceivable that could impose such a code on all States, establish the jurisdiction of the Court of Justice, and ensure the execution of its judgments, the realization of this whole plan would certainly be conceivable in every respect. But precisely such an earthly power that moves above the collective States and disposes of them, does not exist. The establishment and maintenance of such a Court of Justice is therefore not otherwise conceivable...

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[212]

FOREIGN AFFAIRS. But this is not contractual, i.e., by voluntary agreement; and from this arise at once the almost insurmountable difficulties that stand in the way of the establishment and the coming into operation of such a Court of Justice. One cannot compel a State to enter contractually into the execution of such a plan. Even if one now has the good fortune, as at the Peace Conference, to encounter a public opinion that is favorably disposed towards the whole plan, yet inevitably all sorts of subordinate difficulties will arise in the making of the regulations, about which there can hardly ever be complete agreement. Even if there is only one Power of importance that, since it does not see its desire fulfilled, hesitates and makes all kinds of reservations and raises difficulties, this is so likely to lead to a second and a third Power first half and then completely withdrawing, that naturally the whole plan then collapses. The willing countries can then indeed create a Court of Justice for their own mutual benefit, but this is then intended solely for disputes that may arise between these acceding States; and for disputes that might then arise without intent with the non-acceding States, such a Court of Justice would be of no service, and one would still have to call in the aid of the Court of Arbitration, or, where even this left one in the lurch, seek a decision on the battlefield. Even if it were still possible to contractually allow such a Court of Justice to act in all cases, it would likely acquire such high significance that it would gradually acquire moral force. But is this probable? Will any country bind itself once and for all? Yes, would this even be advisable, where the composition and regulation of such a Court remained subject to all kinds of modifications and could acquire a completely different significance with the passage of time? Will the accession to the establishment of such a Court therefore not always run the risk of being abandoned again by State after State, even after a brief trial? It is indeed not doubtful whether, if a mighty Power, after the lapse of years, had come to the insight that recognition of the decision of the Court would undermine the vital interests of the country, the fear would stand that even before the expiry of the stipulated term, notice of withdrawal could follow, which would then just as likely lead to war instead of averting it.

[213]

THE PRECARIOUS NATURE OF VERDICTS

§13. The Precarious Nature of Verdicts. Now, all these difficulties could not be simply stamped out from the root, but would directly flow from the contradiction that would remain between the character of such a Court and the manner of its establishment. Jurisdiction in a higher sense is obligatory, both for him who gives it and for him who must submit to it. The scoundrel, the villain, does not bind himself by contract to submit, should he commit a murder, to the Criminal Court. Such a contractual criminal procedure would be simply unthinkable. Even what in earlier centuries presented itself as a dispute, whether one would or would not submit to the judge who presented himself, in no way had its roots in a contractual basis. Now, if it concerns international disputes, over which the Court of Justice would have to pronounce judgment, excluding in themselves insignificant, merely formal questions, one might still assume that submission to the verdict would readily follow. But this is by no means the case. Continually serious disputes arise between State and State, and ultimately such international lawsuits may also arise in which one country accuses the other of committed violence and perpetrated injustice, so that a criminal trial would actually come to the fore. Now, such a lawsuit can find no solution except in Sovereignty. There would thus have to appear a sovereign power standing above the two States involved in the dispute. This high Sovereignty would, like every valid Sovereignty, have to be derived from the Sovereignty of God, and both the criminal jurisdiction and the execution of the criminal sentence would have to be derived from that Sovereignty. Thus, it always comes down to the question whether on God’s part a Supreme Authority has been established on this earth, to which all the States of the world have to submit themselves. In the medieval struggle over the Hierarchy of the Bishop of Rome, the solution to this painful question was often sought, and even where the Pope was set aside, it was still the holy “Roman Emperor” who was considered called to fulfill this high office. But whatever may be said about this, the outcome has sufficiently shown that neither hierarchical nor imperial power was to be found as an authority established over all States.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[214]

202 FOREIGN AFFAIRS

What seemed one, thus fell apart of itself, and it all came down to an agreement again, and it proved impossible to find the principle of authority from which the right to summon, sentence, and execute could arise. The prospects for such an international Court of Justice are therefore certainly not favorable. A firm starting point for it is no longer to be found. It would all have to be built up contractually, and how will the contract here ever be anything other than an extremely weak and unstable foundation?

§14. Academy of International Law

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”

The late State Councilor Asser, with whom I had the opportunity to discuss more than one point related to this, was therefore always concerned with making the legal insight more uniform in the different countries. He succeeded in achieving very commendable results in the field of private law, but was also constantly concerned to promote greater uniformity in public law as well. Hence the grand plan that gradually emerged to establish an Academy for International Law in our Residence, which as much as possible would make the legal insight in the different countries more uniform. It becomes increasingly clear how in the different countries a often completely different, and even contradictory, answer is usually given when it comes to questions of international law. This gave rise to the idea that it might be possible to establish an Academy for International Law, and this in The Hague, an Academy where International Law and what is connected with it could be taught, independent of the particular insights that had become more and more established at the leading State Universities, as a result of the history of the respective States and in connection with the pretensions that prevailed there, so that one could not really speak of a Universal International Law. It would therefore, so it also seemed to Asser, be so desirable to bring about a higher education in International Law that would not feel constrained by the folds of history and the aims of the competing Great Powers. It would have to be an objective academic education that would naturally come under the

[215]

ACADEMY OF INTERNATIONAL LAW. 203 European statesmen who had completed their studies would be born with a greater unity of insight. Nor would the costs of such an Academy be daunting. Twenty-five professors would suffice. Reckoned at a salary of £1000 each, they would together require £25,000 per year. Adding the costs of annual rent for a building, a library, and support staff, the budget would scarcely exceed half a million Dutch guilders, a trifling amount for all the States combined. An attempt has indeed been made to bring such an institution into being, but unfortunately, instead of appealing to the States for the costs, they knocked on Carnegie’s door, and I had to discuss the matter very preliminarily with him at the dinner with the then American ambassador. But it soon became clear that we had completely missed the mark here. Yet Carnegie was also now willing to provide financial assistance, but due to his intervention, the matter slipped entirely out of our hands in The Hague and passed into the control of the Committee that Carnegie had formed in Washington to develop his ideas. Mr. Brown Scott, who came as a delegate of this Committee to The Hague, exerted himself in every way to realize the grand idea. But from the outset, a difference of opinion came to light that ultimately caused the entire undertaking to fail. For the said Committee in Washington, which only had a small amount at its disposal and was moreover pursuing other goals, was not willing to make more than a very small amount available for the Academy in The Hague. They really did not want an Academy in the sense intended by Mr. Asser. It would rather become a Committee that would invite a certain number of international law scholars each year to come and give a few lectures in our Residence, preferably six by each. Lectures that could then be directed towards a universal result. Assuming that six gentlemen would appear each year, there could be about forty lectures, only in the winter months; and anyone who wished could attend them.

[216]

204 FOREIGN AFFAIRS.

§15. Perverse plan. I then felt compelled to resist this plan very decisively, as it would never achieve the intended goal, and I was supported in this by the Netherlands Central Council for Internationalism, of which I had the honor to be the chairman. This Committee had been established with the general aim of promoting a more stable arrangement of international relations. It was a long-standing Committee, driven mainly by Mr. Bunge in Amsterdam and Mr. van Hogenhuyze in The Hague, but had sought reinforcement by changing its name and including Mr. T.M.C. Asser as honorary chairman, and further Mr. Jul. C. Bunge, Mr. W. van Hoogenhuyze, Mr. J.A. Limburg, Mr. J.A. Loeff, with me as chairman, forming the board, and Jhr. Mr. H.W. van Asch van Wijck, Mr. H. Goeman Borgesius, Dr. C. Lely, Prof. Mr. Dr. W.H. Nolens, Prof. Mr. J. Oppenheim and Baron E.C. Sweerts de Landas Wyborgh as members. In this Council too, the question of the Academy of International Law came up, but unfortunately it turned out that Mr. Asser had also allowed himself to be persuaded by the Committee in Washington to replace the idea of the Winter Lectures with the idea of a genuine Academy. Considering that we could not acquiesce in this, an attempt was made on our part to persuade Carnegie to our plan for a real Academy. This, however, has not succeeded. This caused the actual idea of an Academy to fall through, and the plan for the Winter Lectures also remained stillborn. My withdrawal from the Committee was a consequence of this, and as I believe I may conclude, the Committee itself also shortly thereafter met an untimely death. For the international position of our Government, I cannot but see damage in this outcome of the matter. Lectures instead of courses by permanently appointed professors would have had the exact opposite effect of what was intended. For the aim should have been, with a view to what is taught in the various countries of the major Powers, to provoke here an education in international law that remained strictly impartial.

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[217]

MISGUIDED PLAN.205 This high goal would have been achieved if, in essence, the professors of international law from the smaller states had been appointed to the Hague Academy; if they, as permanent appointees, could have given a regular and comprehensive three-year course; if this Academy could have examined and granted diplomas; and if students from all countries had enrolled in its registry. But instead of this rational plan, resorting to a certain number of winter lectures by non-appointed, but specially invited scholars, would certainly never have achieved the intended goal—indeed, the very opposite. For such lectures would have—how could it be otherwise?—summoned the most renowned professors precisely from the great powers: England, Germany, France, Russia, and Austria. And how could it have been otherwise, or that these scholars, lured here from the various great powers, would not have zealously used this rare opportunity to preach the ideas most desired by their own country? Not impartial in the academic realm, but highly partisan in the political arena, would have been the fruit of these lectures. It remains and will forever remain inexplicable to me how Carnegie and his associates could have preferred such occasional lectures over a fixed and regular curriculum. It cannot be concealed that the attendance at these lectures would most likely have been painfully disappointing. Undoubtedly, the majority of the audience would have flowed in from our own country. A generally renowned man would have left men of lesser fame cold. And from abroad, a few visiting statesmen would certainly have shown up to attend the entire series, but we would not dare estimate their number above 50. For the opinions of these renowned professors were already to be found in their published works, and since their lectures would naturally also have been published, it would have been far simpler, less costly, and more convenient to order the lecture series from their bookseller each year than to seek shelter in the cold winter months in the princely The Hague to attend those lectures. Whether anything will still come of such an Academy remains to be seen. Its absence is in itself a detriment to our international position. Also

[218]

Mr. Asser saw this so clearly that, although he felt bound by his American friends, he too advocated the idea of appointing at least a couple of permanent professors. However, this would have been less than a half-measure, which could not have been of any use. For precisely the doctorate degree would have had to give this Academy the greatest appeal, and this idea could naturally not be realized by the appointment of only two professors. And this all the less, since it was inherent in the intention to teach not only abstract international law, but also the great economic issues that are increasingly influencing the practice of international law. I await with interest what the future may still bring us by way of surprises in this respect. The presence of such an Academy in our Royal Residence would undoubtedly have strengthened our international position. That it remained so far away, undoubtedly cut off no small amount of international influence for us.

§16. Congress Arrangements. A second disappointment came with the failure of the grandiose plan to gather the most important international congresses from the endless series of world congresses in our Residence, or at least in our country. In Dr. Eykman’s “L’Internationalisme scientifique,” published by Van Stockum & Zonen in The Hague in 1911, the second Appendix offers a list of Congresses, with what falls under it, of no less than 614 numbers, showing that the number of Congresses had already exceeded 500. Now, of course, there can be no question of attracting this endless mass of Congresses all to the Netherlands. Brussels, Paris, London, Buda-Pest, Vienna, Rome, indeed what capital city not, understood the art of binding the largest Congresses firmly to themselves. Other Congresses followed the rule from the outset to change the place of assembly each time; while some even preferred smaller cities. Those Congresses that needed a permanent office and had to set certain special requirements for their meetings were most firmly bound.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.
[219]

CONGRESS ORGANIZATION. It requires no further argument, when viewed in this light, that certainly no more than one-tenth, that is, about fifty, of these Congresses would be considered to find a home with us. Just those large Congresses now require very large, purpose-built facilities, and it already follows from this that there is no question of even half of the annual world congresses being brought together in the same city. From the outset, therefore, a division must take place here, a clear distinction must be made between Congresses of more general significance and those other, much smaller Congresses that deal with specific subjects. This distinction is most clearly felt in the case of medical Congresses. There are medical Congresses held that relate to medicine in general, but alongside these there are also many special Congresses where only specific diseases or therapeutic methods are addressed. Naturally, the influence of interested parties on the first-mentioned general Congresses of physicians is much greater than on a detail congress, for example, on dentistry, otology, or ophthalmology. This would naturally lead to the obvious choice of summoning the general medical Congress here, and having the special Congresses on Dentology, Otology, and Ophthalmology convene elsewhere. It is immediately understood, however, that this is not about anything other than a small two dozen Congresses of a general nature, which could hold their conferences either every year or at least every two years. Thirty-one weeks could be set aside for this. One could start in April and let the series run through the end of October. If this plan could have succeeded, it would certainly have greatly strengthened our international position in connection with the Peace Palace. Even in itself, it would have been worth so much that the hundreds of scholars who participate in such general Congresses would have paid a visit to our country and could have made personal acquaintance with our people. Far the majority of them would otherwise never come to visit our country, and know about us only from history or hearsay; and here too the maxim applies: “Unknown is unloved.” We will leave aside here the advantage that the regular holding of the great Congresses would have for our hotel industry, our retail trade, and our means of transport;

[220]

208 FOREIGN AFFAIRS. Here a higher interest is at stake. The goal aimed at here is, in a certain respect, to make our country “a city set on a hill” for all nations. What Delphi was for the ancient Greek world, the central point from which the highest guidance went forth, the Netherlands could at least have become for Europe. And in connection with the Court of Arbitration, there could arise, not only in Europe, but also in America and to some extent even in Asia, a quiet opinion that, whatever country might undergo, the Netherlands, as the sacred little jewel among the small States, would attain such high significance for all peoples as to awaken everyone’s love for our independence. Our past speaks so strongly here. By itself, Paris offers the visitors of such Congresses lavish opportunities to enjoy life, but instead of an advantage, this is much more a disadvantage. Where an important Congress is held, the distraction must not be too strong, or the meetings of the Congress will soon be disrupted. Here, on the other hand, there is almost nothing that distracts. Neither in Amsterdam nor in The Hague does so much call for sightseeing and attendance that the Congress could not be assured of a good turnout day after day. More than one great, well-attended Congress held here has shown how the meetings were attended to the very end. Hardly ever did one return from a Congress held here disappointed. This was not the case elsewhere, where, upon returning home, the complaint would involuntarily come to one’s lips: I have enjoyed much, but, as you can well imagine, I have brought little home from the Congress itself. This argument of distraction speaks so strongly against the great cities that more than one office has already summoned its Congress to a rural area. This has been tried very repeatedly, especially from the Christian side, and all testimony spoke afterwards of successes. And this now naturally connects with what the memory of our past evokes as the mood of the spirits. Whoever enters Paris sees with longing eyes the wonders of the world city and is carried away by himself into a mood of luxurious indulgence, while the historical memory speaks of a Bartholomew’s night and of September murders, and evokes a sharp contrast between what luxury offers and what evil passions swell up in that world city. Here at home, on the other hand, history and museum call to the foreign visitor, hardly evoking any other memory,

[221]

INDISPENSABLE BUILDING. 209 of a brilliant heroism in the past, an almost unparalleled industry, and a power deployment in our small circle that still amazes the reader of history, while the name of Orange, which is still inseparable from the name of the Netherlands for the foreigner, impresses of itself. This higher tone is now far from indifferent to a serious Congress. A Congress, gathered from all over Europe to discuss important political, medical, economic, literary, technical or philosophical interests, must not fall out of its role, and it is inseparable from that role that a more serious tone prevails in all its spirit.

§17. Indispensable Building. But if one is to attract these higher Congresses here, then of course the Netherlands must also understand the art of preparing for the reception of such Congresses, and this is precisely what it has completely lacked so far. One did almost nothing. One took an attitude, not as if it were an honor for us that they came here, but as if it were an honor for the visitors that they were admitted here. And this is not least due to the fact that the Congress affair went on outside the Department of Foreign Affairs. Naturally, the very distinct and diverse nature of these Congresses can lead to one Congress being more in rapport with Justice, another more with Agriculture, a third more with the Interior, and then of course care must be taken that the reception is preferably from the Department that is closest to the character of such a Congress. We would therefore be the last to want to lay down as a rule that the care for this whole series of Congresses should be entrusted to the Department of Foreign Affairs. On the contrary, it should be clearly stated that only the Department of Foreign Affairs can contribute an element to these Congresses, which has therefore been very wrongly neglected. Of all the Departments, it is only the Department of Foreign Affairs that has matured in the art of receiving men of name and significance from abroad. This applies especially to our country. In general, our people do not understand much of the higher titles and rank distinctions that often make an impression abroad. My own experience has confirmed me in this.

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the
[222]

210 FOREIGN AFFAIRS. When, after having served as a Minister, I undertook a fairly extensive journey through the lands to sail the Old World, I gained the experience that the governments abroad are at pains to receive foreign guests who have held a high position, even if only temporarily, in their own country, with almost exaggerated honors. So it was on the part of the Courts and Foreign Offices, and even in France the President of the Republic received me in the most flattering manner, just as I enjoyed the same treatment from the Minister of Foreign Affairs. Was this for my person? Certainly not in the least. It spoke only of the established custom in those countries to receive foreigners of a certain position in their own country not only with distinction, but even with a certain dignity. The foreign visitors to such Congresses are also always accustomed to this, and, this being so, it also seems old and proper that, at a Congress held here, the Department of Foreign Affairs should keep completely aloof and not even make an effort to contribute to a more honorable reception. And with us it was usually the case that not only did the Department of Foreign Affairs hold back afterwards, but not even a hint came from the Department of Foreign Affairs to make our Departments realize how one should receive such men of name as gathered at these Congresses. In Brussels, on the other hand, it was quite different. There one made oneself as little as possible of such a reception, and this is precisely one of the obvious reasons why one often refrained from a second Congress in our country. Comparison with what was done elsewhere for such a Congress cooled the love for our Residence.

§18. Rejected Proposal. With this in mind, the international Committee of which I was the chairman has also applied to the Cabinet in 1912 for at least a better locality that would be suitable for such Congresses to be held.

Analytical Summary: A Happy Turn.
[223]

REJECTED PROPOSAL. It could be arranged that the required care be taken. Our country does not possess a single locality that can provide what is needed for such a Congress. One has always had to make do, and the lesser suitability of the available localities was often no small cause that much at the Congress did not come to its right. The Kurhaus at Scheveningen could still serve best, but of course there could be no question of holding a dozen or more Congresses here year after year, always making the Kurhaus available for this purpose. Now this does not mean that elsewhere a locality where such Congresses are wont to assemble would always meet the highest demands placed on it. Not at all. That is precisely why a building ad hoc, i.e., a building specifically and exclusively designed for a large Congress, would become an attraction point in our Residence by itself; and almost every Bureau would soon be rushing to secure such a Congress building ad hoc for its own Congress in good time. Thus, almost every Congress would be turned away; but if one had one’s own Congress building ad hoc, one would already be attracting by the building itself. The terrain between the Peace Palace and Scheveningen offered in itself all that one could wish for the placement of such a building. It is true that the investigation previously carried out by Messrs. Bunge et al. had given rise to the idea of establishing an entirely new urban district around Waalsdorp with a view to such a Congress building, but this plan had long since been abandoned as gigantic. On the other hand, a solid, purpose-built Congress building could find a shady and quiet spot in the old avenues of Jacob Cats. And it was with this goal in mind that an application was made to the Government, asking whether it would be pleased to proceed with the establishment of such an institution. Naturally, the costs would be high; but if it was a building that excelled and thereby attracted, so that one could count on at least 10 to 12 Congresses a year, the rent could very well cover the costs, even though it seemed to me that such a calculation would have a somewhat petty character, and that, if the Congresses were to be able to increase our international significance, a more generous offer of use would be appropriate. So the Committee had also hoped that the Government, seeing the national interest involved in this undertaking, would at least...

[224]

FOREIGN AFFAIRS. If the government had responded by giving its principled consent, it would even have not withheld a word of sympathy from the Committee. For this reason, the request had been addressed not to a single Minister, not even to the Prime Minister alone, but to the Council of Ministers, since it concerned a highly delicate matter that could have significance for the future of our entire people. Yet the submission of the request was followed by nothing but bitter disappointment. Not only was this request rejected for the time being, but it was virtually rejected definitively, and rejected in such a sober word that the appreciation of the request was hardly expressed. Yet it seems to us that financial frugality was not the only motive here. Rather, the reading and re-reading of the government’s response left the impression that they were afraid of the plan. For the realization of this plan would necessarily provoke all kinds of interference, and it seemed as if the Departments were above all shrinking back from this expansion of activities and interference.

§19. Leadership in Foreign Affairs. This is now to be understood. A European Congress, or rather an even more global Congress, will last at least 3 to 4 days, and there is also a certain amount of preparatory time involved, so that a full week per Congress is certainly not too much to reckon with. If now a series of such Congresses follows one another, it is of course absolutely impossible for the Ministers involved to appear at all these Congresses, to hold receptions there, and to attend the opening or closing meals. Nor can the Secretaries-General lend themselves to this. The departmental affairs would suffer too much from it. And to entrust the official representation to a Referendary would be exceptionally very good, if this Referendary were a man of years and of high social position, but as a rule this would not be practicable. That is precisely why the question should have been asked long ago in what way, with the increase in the number of Congresses, the official representation could be made feasible without unduly burdening any Department.

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.
[225]

EXPERTS AT OUR LEGATIONS. 213 This could only be found by appointing a man of general linguistic knowledge, universal education, fluent in languages, and of aristocratic manners, specifically for this purpose. As the matter stood, then the Minister concerned could act on one occasion, another time one of the Secretaries-General, and in exceptional cases one of the secretaries; but if this did not appeal to others, then this general Congress Commissioner could in the name of the Government offer all due honor and respect to the Congress. This Congress Commissioner would then also have to be able to dispose of a small office to consult with the gentlemen of the Congress office on the details. This Commissioner with his office would then naturally be an expert in the reception. From this a fixed, well-considered regulation could develop. And even if then in the same year a dozen or more Congresses followed one another, yet everything could proceed in perfect order. This Commissioner would then also have to be the Administrator of the Congress building, who would always take care of the necessary service, the necessary repairs, the required furnishings, the cleaning, and if necessary the heating. All this would undoubtedly not cause small expenses; but suppose it amounted to half a ton a year, what would such an amount then still signify, if it served to enhance the impression made in our country on the Congress members who had come here from abroad, and to strengthen the reverence and awe for the Netherlands as the central host. That is precisely why, in our opinion, the Department of Foreign Affairs should be assured of sufficient influence on this new institution, so much so that we would prefer to add the Congress Commissioner to Foreign Affairs rather than to the Interior. Insofar as a medical Congress would also have to speak with the Interior, the Referendary of the medical department would then be designated to assist the Congress Commissioner.

§20. Experts at our Legations. The addition of such a Commissioner of Foreign Affairs would be all the less surprising if one considers how one has proceeded in the past with the appointment of experts at our legations.

Analytical Summary: The three types not equally characteristic. But this does not mean that, in the course of time, each of these three types has in turn dominated the national condition: the Roman Catholics in the Middle Ages, the Calvinists in the 16th century, and the French Revolutionaries after 1789. It does not at all follow from this that they are equal in national significance. Undoubtedly, the Seven Provinces, once living a common life with Flanders and other southern regions, have enjoyed a high and flourishing position and have gradually taken an increasingly important position in Northern Europe; but it must not be forgotten here that the national energy was at the time much more strongly developed in what is now called Belgium than in the northern provinces. Neither Amsterdam nor Rotterdam could in those days be placed on a par with Ghent and Bruges. The wealth of medieval life had more stamped its imprint on the national character in the South than in the North. The reference to the rich medieval type therefore has much less significance for the present Kingdom of the Netherlands than for the western part of Belgium. And in the second place, it must be added that at that time the unity of our national life was much less consolidated. Under the Burgundians, the Dutch provinces did not occupy an independent position, but were united with what lay outside our later borders; and under Charles V and Philip, we even came, though only in a personal way, into a Union with entirely different states from Central and Southern Europe—something which could not but be detrimental to the confirmation of our own national character. Whoever investigates the historical significance of our lower lands may well find the period we lived through in the Middle Ages highly important, but nevertheless hesitates for a moment to acknowledge without reservation that our glorious period dates from April 1, 1572. Not in the Middle Ages, but in the period of the Reformation, lies the high point of our national history. It cannot be emphasized enough how much scrupulous fairness is required in such a comparison, and we shall beware of the assertion that from the Calvinist side the correct toll of reverence has always been paid to the medieval period in our history, to which they undoubtedly had a right. But it stands to reason that from the Roman Catholic side, too, we have not always been done justice. It cannot be valued highly enough that especially in the second half of the 19th century a more impartial judgment gained influence. Just with a view to this more unanimous judgment, it may now at least be established that not only the foreign historians, one may well say without any exception of importance, honor the period after 1572 as the highest and most glorious, but that also within the country the best interpreters of each of the three types increasingly come to the unanimous conclusion that the world significance of our small country came out most richly in the heyday of Calvinism. This in no way denies that our national life in those days of our glory also showed dark sides. The resistance to the monarchical state form, and even against the House of Orange, has almost become a source of our unhappy discord until 1789, and it is impossible to say how much higher our country would have soared if immediately after the separation from Philip II the crown had been offered to Prince William. It had to come to this in 1813; but who calculates what national suffering would have been spared us if we had taken this important step two centuries earlier. But even if one plays open cards and takes into account all the suffering, oppression and restriction of rights that the temporarily dominant group has inflicted on the other two groups, it is historically established that we were never before 1572 what our national life was in the period between those two dates, and never again after 1789. To top it off, the fact that we offered a refuge to the politically and religiously persecuted from all countries is proof that the actual life here was a life in freedom, and that the publications and ordinances that claimed to infringe on this freedom had more the effect of satisfying formal demands than of inflicting suffering on those who deviated.
[226]

FOREIGN AFFAIRS. More than one country has already gone elsewhere to add an expert on trade matters to its Legations or Embassies. We too have already taken that path in London. Elsewhere, however, such a permanent institution is common. It was very clear that first and foremost the Consul, and especially the salaried Consul, had to perform this service, but the Consuls, even the general Consuls, are now local notables, and what increasingly proved necessary was having at one’s disposal a man of general knowledge who could take on what the Ambassador himself would not be able to bring to a good end. Our shipping and trade bring us into contact with foreign countries. Our Department of Foreign Affairs must therefore be able to intervene primarily there for the benefit of the country, where our interests come into contact with, and thus also into conflict with, those of foreign countries. It has been asked whether such a Trade Agent would not be a saving, if one were to transfer the training of our Ambassadors more to the economic field as well, but it has been rightly answered that the diplomatic intercourse smells of the salon, not of the office, and that above all when it comes to trade and shipping, the seasoned office man always proves to be the matador. It cannot be denied that shipping and trade create contact with foreign countries, and from this undeniable fact it follows as a consequence that Foreign Affairs also has a special vocation in this area. Thus, the Departments of the Interior, Finance, Agriculture, etc., would indeed take over this task as far as shipping and trade are concerned, but one knows how often it went wrong. The natural course of things is, of course, that what brings us into contact with foreign countries should always run through Foreign Affairs, and that the other Departments should only assist in so far as the subjects belong more particularly to those other Departments. So for judicial matters with Justice, for tariffs with Finance, for medical matters with the Interior. It repeatedly proved, however, that in the absence of this proper connection, our negotiations turned out disastrously, and how often another country, with its accredited representatives, got the better of us. This is partly at least the entirely different question of whether it is desirable for the Minister of Foreign Affairs to have an Ambassador appear, and then it is obvious that in

[227]

EXPERTS IN SPECIAL MISSIONS

It will be desirable to avoid falling into this trap. This also applies in part to the marriages of our Envoys. For an Envoy who takes up his post abroad at a relatively young age, it is so easy to find an attractive match in the foreign country. Such a marriage has repeatedly occurred, both with our Envoys and with those of other countries—the memory suffices of the much-celebrated Belgian Envoy Baron Guillaume and his captivating wife, who hailed from Bucharest. Yet precisely this made the Envoy thus married not the most suitable to act as the head of the Department of Foreign Affairs. The influence of the wife must not be underestimated, especially in diplomatic circles. If the interests of her own homeland remain paramount for such a high-ranking woman, it is by no means unthinkable that, especially in times of war, with conflicting interests between the land of her spouse and her own country, the Dutch interest could suffer harm. This sometimes emerges so strongly even in royal marriages; but if a second similar objection is now added to the Envoy, one certainly feels how such a diplomat married abroad can raise concerns for the Department of Foreign Affairs. Yet this first concern is not the main objection. The most serious objection to the performance of an Envoy as Minister of Foreign Affairs lies much more in this, that our Envoys, the longer they are in diplomatic service, are all too easily estranged from our Dutch household. We know how, even in London, we have had Envoys for many years who were in every respect an estimable diplomat, but who almost never set foot in our country and even preferred to spend their vacation days elsewhere. Such persons may have been deemed suitable in the period just behind us, when the Minister of Foreign Affairs was the Minister pro forma, but they are becoming increasingly untenable, now that a considerable part of what concerns our own household must also run through Foreign Affairs. This does not, of course, dispute that we have also enjoyed excellent Ministers of Foreign Affairs who have been entirely absorbed in the diplomatic service for many years, but a preference for such a choice is still not recommended. Whoever reviews the list of our Ministers of Foreign Affairs will see

[228]

216 FOREIGN AFFAIRS. It is also immediately apparent that Ambassadors remained the exception, and this must be held fast.

§21. A Permanent Expert at Foreign Affairs. It even seems not undesirable to add a second observation here. In our country, too, it cannot be otherwise, or a Cabinet change takes place almost every four years. A Cabinet that sits for two electoral periods is almost unheard of. Only recently, the Cabinet of de Meester itself lasted even shorter. Often now it is asked whether, with such a Cabinet change, it would not be desirable for at least the Minister of Foreign Affairs to carry over into the new Cabinet. As long as Foreign Affairs was a small, completely isolated department, this would certainly have been thinkable. However, the more it is seen that Foreign Affairs is also connected time and again with our domestic affairs, this possibility falls away. For this reason, one will also have to reckon with a constant new staffing of this Department in the future. But precisely this raises the question whether the continuity in the regulation of our relations with foreign governments is not thereby too much weakened. The secretary-general usually remains, and there is at least a certain continuity in his continued presence, but certainly not enough. Elsewhere, e.g. in Belgium, the refuge has been taken to appoint a General Advisor at this Department to counteract the objection arising from this. For this one generally chose an experienced and knowledgeable former Ambassador. This made the Minister less dependent on his subordinate staff, and always provided the advice of an experienced man of affairs, who to a certain extent guaranteed that the line along which all kinds of negotiations with Foreign Governments moved, did not become too double-edged and too crooked. Above all, in this general advisor the newly appointed Minister often had the very necessary backing. It was a matter of indifference whether such a bearer of continuity bore the title of general advisor or chief of the Cabinet. The main thing was and remained that Foreign Affairs, even with constant Cabinet changes, could follow a fixed line.

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over
[229]

FOREIGN AFFAIRS AND MISSIONS. The Department of Foreign Affairs should also be in a certain connection with the Christian Mission abroad, at least in so far as one of two things takes place: either that a Mission goes out from our country outside our Colonies, or that a Mission from a foreign country establishes itself in our Colonies. If it is a Mission from here to our own Colonies, the connection with the Department of Colonies suffices. On the other hand, if one has to do, as with our Mission of the late Dr. Otte to Amoy in China, or also with the German Mission on Sumatra, then difficulties may arise which also touch on Foreign Affairs. One knows how England has always kept an eye on Missions, and after the outbreak of the great world war, almost immediately set out everywhere to destroy the German mission posts. It did not even shrink from resorting to acts of violence in doing so. Many missionaries, with their wives and children, were summarily arrested and immediately expelled from the country. This was connected with the fact that England has always valued the missions of English origin as a means of propaganda for the expansion of its political and international influence. English missions have almost always been the forerunners of the establishment of English colonies, especially in Africa. It can even be said that it was a fixed method for England to gradually gain a certain dominion in a region where one could otherwise not have obtained any influence. I have never approved of this method. It was a subordination of the Mission to an egoistic thirst for land possession. It is therefore far from us to follow the example of the Government in this abuse of the Mission. But even if we completely exclude this misuse of the Mission, yet he errs who thinks that the Minister of Foreign Affairs can have nothing to do with a mission of ours to China, or with a German mission that went from Barmen to Sumatra. One knows enough how, especially in China, all kinds of conflicts often arose between the Missionary and the Consul or the Legation.

[230]

218 FOREIGN AFFAIRS. How even the great domestic wars in China have their origin, or at least their occasion, in the missionary work, has been shown by the events in Peking. In Foreign Affairs, one must therefore be fully informed of all such missionary work originating from here and operating on foreign soil. Our ambassadors and consuls must know the importance the Mission holds in the foreign land, and Foreign Affairs must oversee the Colonies to ensure that no cause for offense is given to foreign missions on their territory. Nor should the Department of Foreign Affairs merely be “occasionally” informed when something goes wrong in this regard—it must actively stay fully informed of this missionary work, and ensure that, for example, our ambassador in Peking and the consuls in the south know who of our side is active in missions and in what relationship these missionaries stand to the local government. Surprises in this respect must be entirely excluded. It is becoming more and more apparent that the Department of Foreign Affairs must not be a “private club” of a few diplomats, but that on every part of the national domain where Dutch interests or Dutch actions come into contact with foreign countries, the watchful eye of this Department must not fall asleep.

§23. More Active Role of the Consulates. This watchful eye of Foreign Affairs can no less be missed for the benefit of Dutchmen who either temporarily reside outside our borders or permanently settled abroad without renouncing Dutch citizenship. Naturally, this care for these compatriots falls to the Consulates. With them, registration takes place. At the Consulate, lists are kept. In this way, Foreign Affairs is already sufficiently provided with help. Yet the period of crisis that the war of 1914 brought us has sufficiently shown in how many respects the consular care can fall short. Here, of course, it is only a matter of the permanently settled persons of Dutch descent elsewhere. Quite different, on the other hand, is the position of the transient travelers from our country,

Analytical Summary: The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,
[231]

MORE ACTION FROM THE CONSULATES. This applies to those who are lured abroad by Congresses or in other ways. Of course, we can only account for those who register with our Consulate or with the Legations. However, if lists are made public, for example, of guests at a large spa, or of registered members for a Congress or an athletic, literary, or economic competition, then it will certainly be necessary for our Consuls, or if they are not on site, the assistants at the Legations, to take note of this, in order to provide the necessary protection where needed. On the English side, such contact is constantly evident, and it is undoubtable that the English owe much of the ease with which they move about abroad, even when they often fall short in language skills, precisely to the consular care. Especially for Congresses held abroad, this involvement must not be neglected. Congresses of significance are almost exclusively held in very large cities, mostly even in the capitals, and the names of the visitors do not remain secret.
