Just as we pointed out the desirability that, on the occasion of Congresses held in The Hague, the foreign Ambassadors should not neglect, through the intermediary of Foreign Affairs, the necessary courtesies towards their compatriots, so too, in what we are now discussing, the necessity cannot be concealed that our Legations or Consulates should not neglect, much less completely ignore, the Congressists coming from the Netherlands. Other governments often attach great value to the fact that their subjects who sojourn abroad constantly bring home friendly remembrances of the Consulate and Legation. So let it be henceforth with us. Painful exceptions aside, one thus all too often received the impression that the Ministry of Foreign Affairs was a secluded, quiet household that did not concern itself too much with Dutchmen abroad. The way in which Dutchmen abroad are treated by the officially acting persons depends to such a high degree on the seriousness with which our diplomatic service takes the fate of its compatriots. To the courtesy that we believe our Government in The Hague should observe towards foreign Congress visitors, can

[232]

FOREIGN AFFAIRS. The treatment that Dutch Congress visitors receive abroad should only be answered as required.

§24. Intercourse between Courts. A few words must also be added here about the reception of foreign Sovereigns or members of royal families. In this matter, a clear distinction must be made between an official visit and other visits that are purely private in character. If one has to do with a passing princely person who only comes into contact with the Court and not with the Sovereign as the Bearer of the Crown, the Government and, accordingly, the Minister of Foreign Affairs can remain completely outside, and even a brief audience on their part would not lend the slightest official character to what remained private. If, on the other hand, the Sovereign as the Ruler of his Realm comes to the Bearer of the Crown in the Netherlands, it is a matter of renewing or strengthening the friendly relations between the two Realms. Then the visit does not concern the Court alone, but the Court and the Government, and the Government must ensure that the reception of such a Sovereign is marked by the highest respect and the utmost courtesy. This, however, is entirely impossible with us as things now stand, for the sole decisive reason that there is no princely palace in the Residence worthy of the name. Recourse has indeed been taken on occasion to the palace on the Dam in the capital, but even this division of the reception makes a most painful impression, and it has also repeatedly proved necessary to accommodate part of the entourage of the visiting Sovereign in the large hotels. Undoubtedly the ministerial building of Foreign Affairs could provide assistance here, but hitherto only very marginally, and only now would the possibility be opened up, as Foreign Affairs is now finally moving from the auxiliary building on the Buitenhof to a better building on the Plein. But however one looks at it, it always makes an international and painful and harmful impression on the visiting Sovereign, on his entourage,

Analytical Summary: Our People.
[233]

THE RECEPTION OF FOREIGN RULERS.

221 Regarding the ambassadors, and even the correspondents who came from abroad on behalf of the King, one sees that the Netherlands is not properly equipped to receive such a distinguished visit. Romania is a State, compared to us, of very recent origin, and yet in Bucharest and Sinaia, and especially in the magnificent building of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, everything is already arranged in such a splendid manner that even the Emperor of Russia could find a fully proper reception there. With us, on the other hand, this is not the case, and even at the last visit of the German Emperor, we on our part came out in such a shabby manner that anyone who was in the know was deeply pained. It must certainly be acknowledged that there is no royal palace to be found in our Residence. What in the North passes for such, is nothing more than an old Stadtholder’s residence; and what the Queen Mother inhabits, is an ordinary gentleman’s house, which even lags behind more than one mansion in the capital. The Palace in Amsterdam is so called, but is no palace, while it must also be firmly established that foreign Princes cannot and may not be received here other than in the Residence of the Bearer of the Crown. The need to take better care of the construction of a princely palace at Sorgvliet is therefore not at all an attempt to please our Queen, but a demand that is urgently required for the sake of our international position. However small the extent of our country may be, we must not lose sight of the fact that our past, so significant for all of Europe, yes, for the whole world, the entire calling of the House of Orange, the great colonial importance that still makes us, after England, a colonial power, and, not to forget this either, our central importance as the land of the Peace Conferences, of the Court of Arbitration and of the Peace Palace,—do not allow that we should even be regarded as the least among the small States. Whoever may have been a witness in Bucharest, in Lisbon, in Madrid, in Brussels, and wherever else, of the splendid way in which the reception of foreign Sovereigns is prepared in those cities, feels what an undesirable impression it must make on foreign Princes and their envoys, when it goes with their visit here as it has mostly gone so far. It is even reported, and this is entirely understandable, that planned visits have been postponed with a view to our lack of proper accommodation.

[234]

222 FOREIGN AFFAIRS. The unpreparedness did not disappear, and sometimes even discouraged him. This must now come to an end. That we dared to do so earlier, when we were a Commonwealth with a Stadtholder, was not surprising. Even in the United States as a republic, they do not aim at such a brilliant reception of Princes. Nor in Switzerland. But now that the Netherlands is a Kingdom, and the more it begins to take a central position through its Colonies and the Peace Conferences, it must no longer continue the miserable deficiency that it is powerless to offer foreign Princes a hospitable reception with honor.

§25. Residences for Ministers. There are two other questions related to this, of which the palace question is the most closely related—the residence of the Crown Ministers. In Brussels, as is known, a series of monumental palaces have been built around the Palace building, which are, among others, stately inhabited by the Minister of Foreign Affairs, the Prime Minister, etc. In more than one of these monumental palaces I have officially attended festivities, which made an impression by the splendor of the halls. This also happens elsewhere. At least the most prominent Ministers mostly live in dwellings that speak, and impress by their brilliant furnishings. But now, how is it with us? Have we not experienced in our Residences that repeatedly a Minister, to whom the care of 40 million souls in our East was entrusted, had to make do in an extremely small bourgeois dwelling in a remote corner of the city, so that even the offering of a dinner to the guests at Colonial conferences almost failed. Could one not, for example, in the Figaro, when visiting the correspondents of Dutch Ministers, read the expression of amazement that one had to seek the men of Authority in the Netherlands Residence in less than a fairly bourgeois dwelling? Do you not now feel that this gives the opposite of a desired impression? For the resident of such a small bourgeois dwelling, who comes here as an Envoy or for the Press, does not naturally have the respect that such a Statesman by virtue of his high position is entitled to. For us it may not hang on the dwelling, the Minister himself may perform even outstanding work in a small, sober study, but towards the foreigner this does not apply, and what lowers the respect of the foreigner inevitably damages the international position of the Netherlands. For this reason too, it cannot be emphasized enough that at least for the Minister of Foreign Affairs, the Minister of the Colonies and the Prime Minister, such a treatment should be available that the occupation of a good presentable dwelling for these Ministers becomes possible. What also happened, that a Prime Minister in The Hague had no more than an upper floor with two, three rooms at his disposal, should not have been able to occur.

Analytical Summary: Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit
[235]

RESIDENCES FOR MINISTERS. - PRIME MINISTER. 223

§26. Prime Minister. The second question, which was already discussed elsewhere, but also speaks here again, is the question of the Presidency of the Council of Ministers. The Minister of Foreign Affairs must not be free to decide on his own occasions and on his own authority on the future of the Netherlands in any international transaction. Questions that may have international consequences must be judged and dealt with by the entire Cabinet. These conditions of security for the benefit of the country can never be met, unless the Ministers, united in council, decide under the leadership of a President, who knows that he, as head of the Cabinet, bears the full responsibility for the decision taken. This now directly touches the Cabinet issue. In former times, the gentlemen Ministers were always masters in their own Department, and often gave the appearance, as if they were coming forward for the honor of the Royal authority. The Council of Ministers was then no fixed college with a fixed Chairman, but merely a meeting of the gentlemen Ministers as required by the occasion, and for that meeting each of the gentlemen Ministers took turns as President. The intention (this was aimed at) was to give the impression as if it was the bearer of the Crown himself who made the decision in each case, and who, in order to arrive at his decision, had to be an advisor seven or eight times.

Analytical Summary: Guided by Orange. To this has been added: “guided by Orange”; an addition that must not be omitted. For it cannot be denied that in the characterized Calvinism, as it also penetrated our country from Geneva and France at that time, there was a danger not to be underestimated, the aftereffects of which are still discernible. In the last chapter of the First Part of this work, I have extensively explained how from the outset there was a decisive element in Calvinism that could only develop and mature later, and that, politically speaking, only came to full effect in the United States; but it was also then added that in Geneva (one need only think of the burning of Servetus) an intolerant dogmatism still prevailed, which, though indispensable for the church, could only result in schism and oppression in its political consequences.
[236]

Foreign Affairs. Even the late Mr. Franssen van de Putte still spoke up for this now entirely outdated position in the First Chamber in 1901. The hereditary monarchy can never guarantee that a scion from the royal house will always sit on the throne who has sufficiently investigated and comprehended the great interests of the land; and above all, there can never be talk of the administration being entirely wrested from it and that unity in the Government would be lacking. There must not merely be Ministers, but there must be a Cabinet, and this Cabinet must be homogeneous; and for this, the forging of the Cabinet into a fixed college and the giving of the leadership in this College to a fixed Chairman is an inexorable demand. Only thus is the country also safe internationally. That the Minister of Foreign Affairs himself should be the president is neither necessary nor desirable for that reason. Under the Roell Cabinet, this was so, and thanks to the great prudence of this administrator, everything then went according to plan; but it is much better if the president himself stands outside of Foreign Affairs, and so at least two men watch over and guard our international relations.

§27. Foreign Affairs and Flag Display. Much that still presents itself for discussion in this connection cannot be further elaborated here. Only two interests have been briefly focused on in this regard. The idea has been repeatedly expressed that we, leaving aside the care for the Colonies, should actually have to refrain from having our own grand naval fleet, in order to limit ourselves to coast defenses, mines, submarines, and airplanes. There is now some truth in this, in so far that the Netherlands cannot think of maintaining both fleets of Dreadnoughts in the Colonies and on our seas, which could defend our country in the event of a significant attack, and this the less so since, if a new world war were to break out, and we ourselves were involved as a belligerent power, our communication with our Archipelago would soon, perhaps even telegraphically, be severed. From this, however, it by no means follows that we can therefore also spare ourselves the trouble of maintaining ships for flag display at sea. It was already regrettable that we could not make a better appearance when an old model ship was offered in New York; and also at the coronation of England’s latest King, we did not cut a desirable figure. As an old sea power and colonizing power, our flag should also be able to be displayed with honor on such a solemn occasion. And secondly, it must not be concealed that the Ministry of Foreign Affairs must always be able to maintain contact with the Departments of War and Navy, in order to prevent anyone who wishes to do harm from surprising us. It becomes an urgent demand, given the increasingly complicated situation in which Europe is getting entangled, that the Government in The Hague also remains informed of what is threatening internationally in secret. Knowledge of people is becoming more and more a requirement for our Envoys, and Foreign Affairs must obtain broader discretion over who can prevent a harmful conflict for us.

Analytical Summary: The wrong done to us. Our party has set up a price in 1878, in its own starting point, to point to this happy influence of the Prince of Orange. Let no confusion creep in here. In matters of ecclesiastical life, Calvin’s insight has also been his own. His dispute with Dathenus does not touch the very life of the church, but the influence of the Church on the life of the people in the political domain. As a theologian, especially Johannes a Lasco deserves appreciation on this point, but on a broader scale it has above all been the influence of the Prince that has safely guided us. Meanwhile, one should not lose sight of the fact that this pointing with preference to the Prince is foreign to the intention, to praise anything that has later emerged from the House of Orange on this delicate terrain of the Princes. Prince Maurice aligned himself much more closely with the Calvinists. The then-emerging conflict with Arminianism necessitated this. But his life history would stand in a more glorious light for us if Oldenbarnevelt had died a natural death. Under Prince Frederick
[237]

CHAPTER VIII
THE ADMINISTRATION OF THE PROVINCES

PROVINCIAL STATES AND THE FIRST CHAMBER. A comprehensive discussion of the position now assigned to the Provincial States would not be worth the trouble in the practical part of this work. The Provincial States no longer live in the political heart of our people. For the Provincial Water Management interests in Polder and Dike administrations, there is still zeal on the part of those concerned; and although the involvement of those concerned has also gradually diminished in this area, the old desire to tackle things themselves still partly lives on. For the rest, however, the actions of the Provincial States are so little impactful on life that even most of the eligible voters for these colleges are hardly aware of the provincial Hatred.

[238]

The administration of the provinces hardly attracts any attention. However, the composition of the Provincial States is of high significance for the political character of the First Chamber, and without exaggeration it can be said that the electoral zeal which manifests itself in the periodic elections for the Provincial States, even if not exclusively, then at least primarily, is aroused by the struggle over the composition of the First Chamber. A very few issues of a purely provincial nature may also, to a certain extent, pique the interest, and it also happens that in the case of two candidates for the same seat, the personal influence of a large landowner or manufacturer may play a role; - but for the body of voters as a whole, the party choice regarding the First Chamber now stands so prominently in the foreground at every provincial election that is released, that at least in the wavering provinces, the political struggle of the parties now dominates the outcome of each ballot box. That this was not the case earlier, and only became so towards the end of the previous century, was due to the fact that in the preceding period the liberal government had filled almost all mayoral positions with men of its own color; so in South Holland, for example, 170 out of the even 180 municipalities. And since, moreover, the preponderance of the liberal members in the First Chamber was so oppressive that not a single one of our representatives, for example, sat there, there was often a lack of any interest in the choice for the Provincial States. And so the relationship remained as it was. Since, however, our party organization had succeeded in finally breaching this political wall, and people began to believe that the transformation of the First Chamber too would ultimately not belong to the impossibilities, there has been an awakened zeal in province after province, which has steadily increased, and has finally succeeded in completely transforming the composition of the First Chamber. A turning point in affairs, which did not, like a mirage, rise up and disappear again in the mists, but which has continued to dominate a considerable part of our political situation. With this, however, the provincial character of the choice for the Provincial States had almost entirely given way to national politics.

[239]

NO HEART FOR PROVINCIAL HOUSEKEEPING.227

§2. No Heart for Provincial Housekeeping. Even among not a few members of the States, interest and zeal in their own provincial housekeeping had gradually diminished. They left the housekeeping to the Provincial Executive Council. After all, this would be well cared for under the supervision and cooperation of the Royal Commissioner. The reports of the meetings of the Provincial States also show how little warm participation their minutes testify to. Increasingly, the handling of provincial affairs was left in a narrower sense to the Commissioners and the Provincial Executive Council. And even if the Perser reported on it, it did not captivate the public. Even the narrower principled organs of the Press could not stimulate interest in it. This might occasionally be the case in matters of agriculture or public works, but the provincial public as such often did not get involved in it. In this result, which has emerged since 1815 as the fruit of a century-long duration, lies partly even a judgment on the regulation that has been given to our provincial affairs. Van Hogendorp had certainly intended it differently, and Professor Buys saw precisely, when he drew the conclusion from the unchanged preservation of what was written in 1815 in Art. 1 of the fourth chapter of the Constitution, Part 1 and 2, that one had not wanted to kill the provincial life, but rather to awaken it. But the outcome, beyond dispute, has not answered that nobler intention. The fear is now that the Provinces would renew their ancient claims to sovereignty. This had to be prevented in every way, yes, the possibility of it had to be cut off. Hence the appointment of the Commissioner, even without nomination, by the King, the concentration of almost all provincial action and authority in the Provincial Executive Council under his leadership, and what did not less tighten the screw, a constant, ever-working magic of the central authority, partly through an ever-expanding legislative power over provincial regulations, partly through the ever-increasing power of the Minister to submit to the Crown the annulment of what the Provincial States had decided. No less than fourteen articles of our Constitution had the tendency to consolidate certain rights and powers to the Provincial States, and this remained so even in 1887. But what Thorbecke also did to breathe new life into the States from the side, their significance never got the so much desired exponent. And since the main weight of the provincial ballot box in the choice of the First Chamber was felt, the shadow that fell on what the States did below it became increasingly gloomy.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[240]

228 THE ADMINISTRATION OF THE PROVINCES.

§3. Centralization and Decentralization. It is the systems of Centralization and Decentralization that seek the right balance here. Leaving aside Monaco and the other three Lilliput states, Europe has only two Commonwealths, France and Switzerland; but now see the difference in both. In France, they have simply made all the provinces disappear from the national inheritance and thus fragmented the freed inheritance into more than 80 Departments. At the head of each of these 87 Departments, a Prefect has been set up from Paris almost as the sole ruler, and to this Prefect they have added a Departmental “Conseil” that has no other task than to say yes and amen to what the Prefect desires, or at least what the Prefect commands. This decentralization of power has in fact gone so far that the uniformity, as a curse of the modern direction, extends over the entire interior of the French State, and at least after 1870 a certain tendency began to emerge to bring some relaxation into the decentralizing embrace. In this strict centralization, the life-killing principle of the French Revolution expressed itself. Originally, the Kings of France had been almost unlimited rulers as feudal lords. Against their preponderance, the one-man had gradually risen up, so that in the end the King became a roi fainéant. This prompted the Princes to break the mighty estates, so that Louis XIV could finally declare: L’état c’est moi. Against this monarchical preponderance, the tiers état then rose up in revolt, and this brought us the French Revolution. The individualized nation now melted into a centralistic unity, and this unity was then further strengthened by the Napoleonic regime. In Switzerland, on the other hand, the cantons have maintained their ancient sovereignty to this day, and the Confederation has only gradually taken over certain tasks from them. The Confederation has no Prefects, no Departmental Councils, no centralized legislation, no centralized administration. Each canton has its own constitution, its own government, its own legislation, its own administration. The Confederation only takes care of what the cantons have voluntarily entrusted to it. This decentralization has given the Swiss Confederation a vitality and a power of resistance that the centralized French State has never known.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[241]

CENTRALIZATION AND DECENTRALIZATION. - SWITZERLAND. The unity was the people, the People. There was now no God, nor Master anymore. The people themselves had become God and Master. Naturally, there could no longer be any talk of an autonomy of the provinces. France became a map, a mosaic of fragments, and all the peculiarities and distinct character of the various historical population agglomerations fell away.

§4. Switzerland. But let us now look at the course of events in Switzerland. Switzerland has never been anything other than a Republic. And yet the preamble to the Constitution of May 20, 1874, even after the amendments of 1908, still reads: “In the name of Almighty God!” Article 1 emphasizes that Switzerland is a Federation, and indeed a Federation of “Völkerschafte,” which politically organized themselves into 22 cantons, and in such a way that each of these 22 cantons acted as sovereign in its own right. They are also called Eidgenossen, because they have bound themselves under oath before God. All the States together exercise their power through a large Federal Assembly (Art. 7) and this Federal Assembly is divided into two sections. One is called “Le Conseil National,” the other “Le Conseil des États.” And now Article 72 stipulates that the National Council is elected by the entire Swiss people, one member for every 20,000 inhabitants, and that the members of this Council are directly elected by the Swiss citizens of 20 years and older. This Council sits for three years and then retires in its entirety. The “Conseil des États,” on the other hand, forms a Senate. In this second Chamber, each canton elects two members. Besides these two representative Bodies, there is now a Council of Administration, the “Conseil fédéral.” This consists of seven members. They are elected as such by the two parliamentary Chambers in joint session; but only for three years. And finally, at the head of the entire Federation stands a President, who is elected for only one year by the federal assembly. There are no Ministers. The President and the members of the “Conseil fédéral” manage the various activities, we would say the ministerial Departments, among themselves. Its members have a seat and the right to speak in the parliamentary assembly,

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.
[242]

230 THE ADMINISTRATION OF THE PROVINCES. but no voting rights. Above and in addition to these Councils, there is now also a Supreme Court, we would say a High Council, and the members of this Council are elected by the “Assemblée fédérale” and it regulates its powers by law. In the Swiss Republic, therefore, the exact opposite of what brought the end of the struggle in France. The system of Decentralization was even carried so far here that a border office, in very few cases, as we indicated in Part I, could even enter into negotiations with a Foreign Government. Two extremes, therefore, and only the path between these two leads to the right solution.

§5. The Solution in America. What was found as the final solution in America borders very closely on the Swiss system. What the Cantons are in Switzerland, the States are here, whose number is still constantly increasing. Here too, each State is sovereign, although of course this sovereignty can only be nominal. For here too, the Congress stands above each State by itself. This Congress also has its two parts: “The House of Representatives” and the “Senate” (see Art. 1 in the first chapter) approximately what we would call a Second and a First Chamber. The House of Representatives is elected here by the combined population of all the States, and the Senate stands next to it, consisting of two members, each to be appointed by the legislative Body in each State. The members of the legislative Body sit for two years, those of the Senate for six years. Completely outside of this Parliament or Congress, composed of these two parts, stands the President as the executive power. He holds his office for four years. He is elected by the electors who each State appoints for this purpose, in a number equal to the total number of seats it occupies in the Representative Body and in the Senate. Effectively, therefore, by all the voters together, since they elect the Committee members. This President chooses his Ministers at his own discretion, as assistants. But not more than that. For these Ministers do not represent the authority in Congress. They do not come there. And next to these two parts of the Government: the Congress and the President, there then stands

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[243]

THE SOLUTION IN AMERICA - PENNSYLVANIA

231 Furthermore, the third branch of government, which acts as an independent authority, even in the interpretation of legislation. And finally, it is the Congress that appoints the members of this Court, establishes the lower courts by law, and further regulates their jurisdiction.

§6. Pennsylvania

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.

However, this is not enough. The organization of the individual states of the Union must also be examined more closely, at least with regard to their governing power. As an example, I have chosen Pennsylvania, which promulgated its latest Constitution on December 16, 1873. Pennsylvania had already had three Constitutions. The first came into being on September 28, 1776. The second was proclaimed on September 2, 1790. This later Constitution underwent more or less important changes in 1838, 1850, 1857 and 1864. And it was this Constitution, revised four times, that finally gave way in 1873 to the Constitution to which we now refer. I prefer to point to Pennsylvania among the states of the Union, because this state was originally populated not only by Swedes, but mostly by Dutchmen, even though it was Penn who first gave it a firm form and further development. As I remarked elsewhere earlier, the Dutch element was so strongly represented here from the outset that in the Philadelphia directory there are now no less than five columns of names with “Van” - names that had not been placed, as with us, on the letter of the surname, but that were all included under the prefix “Van”; something that is connected with the almost noble meaning that one attaches to the prefix “Van.” All the more did this large colony of descendants from our country impress me in Philadelphia, because not Philadelphia, but Harrisburg is the capital of Pennsylvania. The Constitution of 1873 that is now in force was also drawn up in Harrisburg, not in Philadelphia. Now let me immediately point out that in this very young Constitution, the preamble of the text is also tied to the confession of the King of Kings. For it says: “We, the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, give thanks to Almighty God... ”

[244]

232THE ADMINISTRATION OF THE PROVINCES.Bearing witness to the blessings of civil and religious liberty which they enjoy, and humbly invoking His aid, they establish and ordain this following Constitution.”In 1776 such a preamble would not have drawn attention, but it speaks strongly to the honor and recognition of divine origin even for the civil state, that they still spoke thus in Harrisburg in 1873. I point this out all the more, because in Art. 4 an even stronger provision in this regard is found: “No one who believes in the existence of God, and in a future life where good and evil will be rewarded, can be declared unfit for appointment to any official office on account of his particular religious feelings.” From this it appears that here the political edifice is very decidedly erected on the foundation of Common Grace; and that there is no question of a flirtation with the godlessness of the French Revolution. In this western State, which stands on the footing of our former provincial institutions, one now finds, just as in the great whole of the United States, a legislative power consisting of two parts, of a Senate and of a House of Representatives of the people. The latter sit for only two years, the former for four. These two bodies work together in an equal manner and in an equal proportion, as this takes place with the two parts of which Congress consists. Art. 7 of the third Chapter does indicate, however, what this Parliament has to refrain from, because the regulation thereof is reserved for Congress, but it does so by way of exception. It is not determined that the State Parliament may do this or that. No, it may do everything, except only what is excepted. Thus, what the provincial institution exercises is not a delegated power, but an original power, for which the Constitution prescribes exceptions. In the fourth Chapter, then, the executive power is regulated, and this consists of a Governor, a Lieutenant Governor, a Secretary of State, an Attorney General, an Auditor General, a State Treasurer, a Secretary of Foreign Affairs, and a Chief of Public Instruction. So a President, a Vice-President, and so on, five Ministers. The members of this executive authority are elected not by the Parliament, but by the Voters. The Governor serves for four years. And then, in the third place, comes the judicial power, which in Pennsylvania is also entirely independent in its many

[245]

PENNSYLVANIA.233There is a supreme court, composed of seven members, and these members are appointed by the joint voters of the State. They sit for 21 years. Under this high court, there are various lower judicial bodies of a very different nature, of which the so-called quarter sessions, which function for three months, are best known. Furthermore, the general assembly, with the cooperation of the Senate, can always establish new judicial bodies. Article 15 stipulates, and this is also noteworthy, that all members of these various judicial bodies must be jurists, but without this applying to the members of the High Court. Most members of these judicial bodies sit for ten years. Now, if one keeps in mind that Pennsylvania, although it is called a State, can still only bear a provincial character within the Union, and if one compares the significance and powers that have been left to Provincial Autonomy here, with the meager powers over which our Provincial States still have to dispose, then all further argument is entirely superfluous to make it sharply clear how miserably the independent character of the Provinces comes to its right in our provincial regulation. This is something on which I lay all the more emphasis, because our Provinces were in fact, before French centralization penetrated here, so far advanced in their sovereignty that the unity of the administration in the then municipal government could not come to its right. And when the comparison between Pennsylvania and South Holland, for example, now shows so overwhelmingly how almost nothing our provincial autonomy still means, compared to the autonomy of Pennsylvania, it clearly emerges from what argument, how little ground there is for the glory that one has ascribed to Van Hogendorp, that he at least in appearance has saved something of our ancient provincial independence. He may have intended it excellently, but neither he nor Thorbecke after him have succeeded, even from afar, in making our provincial being develop on its own roots. Certainly, our provinces are no longer “Departments” of France, and our Commissioners are not what the Prefects are in France, but even less do our present provinces resemble States like Pennsylvania, and even less is our Commissioner the equivalent of the “Governor” in this American part of the United States.

[246]

234THE ADMINISTRATION OF THE PROVINCES.

§7. Failed imitation of our past.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how

Now, one should keep in mind here that the present regulation of the United States represents the decentralization of power in the Congress. What was first tried in 1876 was an imitation of what had existed among us under the Republic; but as early as 1787, this had been put to an end, in fact illegally, and only from then on has the now still too centralized institution come into being. I have already explained this in my First Part. Here, however, it must be emphasized in particular that provincial autonomy in the United States has come to its full right precisely because it remained connected with the centralized organization of the common Government. What had existed among us under the Commonwealth had made provincial autonomy excessive at the expense of central administration. This erroneous system, which began with the Union of Utrecht, had been judged by history. It had been initially imitated in America, but it did not work; and what the Netherlands owes to the Prince of Orange never comes out better than when one historically examines what has nevertheless been achieved here, in spite of our untenable state organization. But conversely, it also now teaches us from America what a rich provincial autonomy can still very well exist, even if one cuts off what would doom the central power to impotence or helplessness. If we had here what, as I indicated, Pennsylvania possesses, there would be no more complaint about the restriction of provincial life among us.

§8. Central and South America. Argentina.

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.

To make this clear, it must be said that the system of the Union has mainly been followed for the States of Central and South America, and that it is still persisted in. Only a few examples are given here. First, Argentina. The fact that Article 2 of the First Chapter of the Argentine Constitution still stamps the Roman Catholic religion as the state religion, shows how much the old Spanish centralism still prevails there. The same applies to the fact that the President of the Republic is still appointed by the central government, and that the provincial governors are still appointed by the President. In short, the provincial autonomy in Argentina is still so limited that it can hardly be compared to what Pennsylvania enjoys.

[247]

A FAILED IMITATION OF OUR PAST. 235 In South America, just as in North America, the religious foundation in the state life persists undiminished. That here such one-sided honor was given to the Roman religion was a remnant of Spanish rule. Alongside this provision in Art. 2, however, Article 14 names among the unabridged rights of citizens also the freedom of religious worship. Far more than nominally, the maintenance of the Roman National Church is not the case. The Spanish tradition and the American idea of freedom have both here come to their right; and it is the latter that actually governs the situation. Our colonists, who, after 1901, settled in Argentina together with colonists from the Cape, and who, under the leadership of Dr. Van Lonkhuyzen, who now stands in Grand Rapids, organized themselves ecclesiastically, encountered no difficulties whatsoever. Argentina wants to attract foreign colonists, and therefore opens the floodgates as wide as possible. The main point for the subject that now occupies us is Article 5 of the Constitution of September 25, 1860. For there we read: > Each province shall give itself a Constitution on the basis of the representative system of government, provided that it is in accordance with the principles, declarations and guarantees of the national constitution. Each province shall introduce, within its own territory, an organization for the judiciary, for the municipal administrations, and for primary education. And it is under these conditions that the national government guarantees to each province the enjoyment of its rights. Part II of this Constitution further provides that here too there shall first of all be a legislative power, composed of two Chambers. For one Chamber, the choice shall be made by the people. The other Chamber shall be the Senate, which shall be filled by the choice of the provinces and the capital. All voters from the entire country will appear there, it is expressly stated in Art. 37, as the voters of a province. In the Senate, each province will appoint four members, and they will sit for nine years, while in the other Chamber the election takes place for four years. Art. 74 entrusts the executive power to the President, who bears the title of “President of the Argentine Nation.” He sits for six years. In his appearance, he must, according to Art. 80, take this oath: “I, N.N., swear before God our Lord and on His Holy Gospels, that I will discharge the office of President with loyalty and patriotism... ”

[248]

236THE ADMINISTRATION OF THE PROVINCES.

I will observe love. And that I will faithfully follow the Constitution of the Argentine nation. If I do not do this, then may God and the nation call me to account.” Here in Argentina, Ministers, and indeed according to Article 87, who act differently than in the United States, appear in the Chambers and may participate in the discussion, but have no voting rights. And according to Art. 94, the Judicial organization then comes in as the third power, which also exists here, consisting of a Supreme Court, with lower Judicial colleges underneath, who are appointed and must conduct themselves in accordance with the law of the land, which will be established by the two Chambers, with the cooperation of the President. And now that the three general powers have thus been regulated by both Chambers, as a result of their inherent authority, the regulation for the provincial or state administration follows in Art. 109 ff.; whereby it all comes down to this basic provision, that the Provincial Legislatures are not seen as assigned, with only a few exceptions, but are regarded as possessing all the powers and all the competences that are not granted in the first chapters of the Constitution to the legislative, executive or judicial power. The Provincial Legislatures are therefore not here an auxiliary institution of the central authority, but the autonomous bodies in which the people regulate their own affairs, and which, now joined together nationally, do not merge, but only see themselves set the limits and restrictions that the Constitution specifically indicates.

§9. Brazil and Chile.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.

Next to Argentina, we point to Brazil and Chile, together with their so-called A.B.C. States, because Argentina begins with A, Brazil with B, and Chile with C. The Constitution of Brazil is of very recent date, namely of February 24, 1891. The State already received the name of “the States of Brazil” in the Constitution of 1889. They were twenty in number, and now form a confederated Republic. The bond that binds these 20 States together is forever closed and cannot be dissolved. At least this is stipulated in Article 1 in the first Title of the Constitution. Each of the old Provinces received the name of State by this Constitution. The national Republican Government

[249]

BRAZIL AND CHILE. 237 The government, according to Art. 6, can only intervene in the administration of the provinces or regional States: 1° to repel military force from without or within; 2° to defend the federal-republican character of the country; 3° to restore order in any of the States; and 4° to ensure the observance of national laws. In Brazil, too, the National Congress, according to Art. 16, consists of two Houses, that of the Deputies and that of the Senators. This Congress exercises the legislative power under the sanction of the President. The legislature lasts three years for the Chamber of Deputies and nine years for the members of the Senate. The executive power rests with the President, and he is elected by the people (see Art. 41). He serves for four years. The President appoints the members of the Supreme Court, and likewise the Envoys, but with the approval of the Senate. The Ministers are his assistants. Each State issues its own Constitution and possesses legislative power, provided the principles laid down in the Constitution of the Union are strictly observed. The almost identical institutions of Chile need not be set forth in detail. What has been said so far applies almost entirely to the organization of the United States, and this holds good for almost all the Republics of South and Central America as well. However, to supplement this, it is now necessary to indicate in detail what rights and powers have been taken away from the regional States, which now belong to the functions of the Federal State. By way of example, we choose here what the Constitution of Argentina stipulates in this regard in Article 67, in 28 sub-articles. These 28 sub-articles come down to this in short summary, that the National Congress has reserved for itself: 1° the regulation of import and export duties; 2° the levying of taxes for all regions, insofar as the funds must be available for the defense of the country, the common security, and the general interests of the State; 3° the contracting of State loans; 4° the disposal of the national territory; 5° a national bank investigation; 6° the regulation and supervision of the internal and external loans of the State; 7° the establishment of the national annual budget; 8° the lending to the Regions that need assistance; 9° the regulation of navigation on the rivers; 10° the coinage; 11° the enactment of a civil code, a commercial code, a penal code, etc.

[250]

THE ADMINISTRATION OF THE PROVINCES.

12° to regulate shipping rights with foreign countries and between the Provinces; 13° the postal service; 14° the regulation of borders; 15° care for the Indians, and for their conversion to the Roman Church; 16° promotion of the development and prosperity of the entire nation; 17° the establishment of lower courts; 18° to judge the motives for the resignation of the President; 19° to conclude treaties with other Powers; 20° the admission of religious orders; 21° the right to declare war; 22° to regulate the proceeds of captured ships; 23° to maintain armies and navies; 24° to regulate the militia, and to exercise authority over them; 25° to admit foreign troops if necessary; 26° to declare a state of siege; 27° to administer all places and ports declared to be national property; and 28° to enact laws and ordinances for the implementation of all national executive power. Of course, sub-article 16 of all these provisions is the most elastic. This can be applied in a narrow, but also in a very broad manner. For the rest, however, it is clear from Art. 67 with its 28 provisions that the provincial autonomy in Argentina is anything but an empty phrase. In fact, only that which has a common character is taken away from the Provinces and reserved for the National Congress, the President and the Supreme Court.

[251]

IN 1815 PROVINCIAL AUTONOMY WAS NEGLECTED. To point this out would now serve no purpose, since all our seven united Provinces before the Union of Utrecht were never anything other than parts of a larger whole. This, however, was certainly not the case in 1581; and if one now compares the so insignificant autonomy that has been left to our Provinces after 1815 with what has been left to the Provincial States in Switzerland and both in South and North America, then the irresistible judgment imposes itself that the autonomy of the individual Provinces has been swallowed up by the then formed national government. In Switzerland too, in later years, more and more thought has been given to encroaching on the autonomy of the Cantons, but a reaction soon set in against this, and during the upheavals that the Great War of 1914 also brought to Switzerland as a border country, even in the French Cantons a not insignificant movement has arisen to strengthen the autonomy of the Cantons again. Now it should be immediately noted that both in Switzerland and with us the smallness of the Cantons and the Provinces worked in favor of this solution of their autonomy in the state unity or in the Empire. In America, on the other hand (not counting the small states on the Atlantic Ocean), one had to deal with states that were almost all much larger than our entire country. Only Holland could once boast of its extent, but this province has also been divided into two parts. And if one now objects that in Switzerland and America republics prevailed, and with us after 1813 a monarchy, we too gladly emphasize this distinction, but cannot concede that this had to lead to such a fatal destruction of autonomy. What has worked so disastrously here is twofold. Firstly, the example of the French Revolution, which simply cut up the French provinces and sought salvation in the Departments; but then also no less the condition that has prevailed in England since William the Conqueror. In England, you see, the autonomy of the ancient provinces has been almost completely abolished, the central government has become the sole legislator and power holder, and what in England alone gave the necessary counterweight, was the self-government that had been so powerfully carried out here as well. Something of this was also intended here; but the fact is that it was the French and English influences that have largely dominated the disposition of our leading statesmen since 1815; and this explains why from the very harsh autonomy of the days of the Commonwealth, after 1815 almost nothing remained, even if it was intended otherwise by Van Hogendorp. And although it may be said that Thorbecke in 1848 succeeded in at least somewhat loosening the oppressive bond of unity, Thorbecke too did not restore autonomy to our Provinces in any significant sense.

[252]

THE ADMINISTRATION OF THE PROVINCES.

§11. Autonomy and Self-Government. Yet again, the distinction that Buys pointed out comes to the fore: not autonomy, but self-government. That is indeed the actual state of affairs. Especially when it comes to the provinces, the law comes from above, and the province is accountable to the one who gave the law; and even insofar as they retained some own initiative in matters of roads, canals, water management affairs, and now presumably also in the matter of electricity connections, the central action from above dominates the entire provincial life. On the other hand, the multifaceted activity entrusted to the Provincial Administration in the execution of the laws, especially also in relation to the Municipalities, the polders, and the peat bogs, is under pressure; but even in this multifaceted domain, one is increasingly moving in the direction of also intervening from higher up by law in these matters. Thus, the free initiative is steadily diminishing in the provincial domain. Much, very much, remains the meddling, but it is increasingly taking on the character of subordinate labor performed at the behest of the Central power. The complaint that has just now, as we indicated, again arisen in Switzerland against this ever-increasing centralization power would therefore also be fully justified here. Ever less remains of the ancient autonomy of the Municipalities. Just as the Mayors and Municipal Administrations are also increasingly overburdened with work flowing from the law, so too the Provincial States are increasingly reduced to mere execution, and for a not insignificant part under the leadership of an outsider appointed as Chairman, the so-called King’s Commissioner.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.

§12. Our Commissioners. Also a brief word about this highly placed official. In Switzerland and in the American Republics, the Canton or State elects its own highest dignitary. With us, on the other hand, he is appointed by the Crown, entirely from the outside. There is no nomination, there is no consultation. From the Government Gazette, the Province learns of the appointment of the King’s Commissioner, who then takes up his position.

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[253]

AUTONOMY AND SELF-GOVERNANCE - OUR COMMISSIONERS The provincial commissioner, whoever he may be, lacks all initiative, and even the cooperation that could come from the Province is absent. Indeed, the very title of “Commissioner” has something about it that repels rather than attracts. The old title of “Stadtholder” at least had this in its favor, that it drew a connection to the Sovereign, however tenuous. The title “Governor” chosen in 1813 at least aimed at rule and administration. But “Commissioner” is a mere agent, and conveys no other impression than that of a higher power exercising control over you. We cannot but consider this title as not particularly well-chosen. And this is all the more true, since differences of opinion arise between the Commissioner and the Provincial States, the former having the right to suspend execution and seek support from the Ministry of the Interior. Even his vote in the meetings of the Provincial States is questionable. The Provincial States and the Provincial Executive are bodies emerging from the electorate. In such a body, where the majority, as in the Provincial Executive, already consists of a few persons, to give the Commissioner who has come from outside not merely the right of advice, but the full right to vote, means that it is the Commissioner who so often casts the deciding vote. If there are five or seven Provincial Executive members, this easily splits into two and three, or four and three, and depending on how the Commissioner votes, the outcome is determined. To this is added that as the network of legal provisions enveloping our entire system of government becomes more extensive, multifaceted and intricate, fewer members of those States are capable of feeling at home in this web, and more than one member of the Provincial Executive sometimes loses their way in this web. Hence the influence of the Commissioner, who is himself thoroughly versed in all this, steadily increases. Thus three factors work together. Finally, the entire States, which meet only very rarely, and receive everything prepared for them. Their decision is preceded by what the Provincial Executive does to prepare the decisions, and in this preparation the Commissioner not only participates, but also advises and votes. And through all these factors, the self-evident influence of the Commissioner, emanating from his legal knowledge, his personal position, and the support he has behind him at the Ministry of the Interior, works in and under it all.

[254]

THE ADMINISTRATION OF THE PROVINCES. In these large Provinces, this overwhelming influence certainly finds a counterweight in the more prominent persons who are elected to the Provincial States; in contrast, there are smaller Provinces where, in proportion, the power and influence of the Commissioner still carries more weight. This is nothing other than the usual outflow of all administrative arrangements that rob autonomy and extend self-government downwards. The more complex the system of legal provisions becomes that comes from above, the more one is compelled to place the execution under the guidance of knowledgeable leadership, since it increasingly goes beyond the sphere of the ordinary members of the States.

§13. The Good Side of Centralization. If one now asks whether this more centralizing administration of a Province does not also have its good side, we do not hesitate to answer with a resounding yes. Quite apart from our past and our sense of freedom, our Provinces, with a single exception, are not so large that they could not be governed almost entirely from above; and not only that, but it is beyond doubt that even for our Water Management, the great freedom left to the separate Provinces had its questionable side. It is indeed to the more centralizing tendency that we owe the absence of the great floods that so often troubled us in the past. Here too, what applies to all household management holds true: the greater and stricter the organization of the administration, the less danger of irregularity. In a large orphanage with 200 to 300 inmates, everything marches almost clockwork-like in the evening and morning, while in a small family with a small number of children, the orderly course and flow of things often leaves much to be desired. The more a household takes on a mechanical form due to its size, the firmer its regulation. But if you now ask where life thrives better and more luxuriantly, in the large orphanage with its draconian order, or in the organic family with its undeniable irregularities, no one would take the mechanical institution.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[255]

THE DIVERSITY IS LOST. 243 And so it is here. The decentralization of our administration, even in its application to the Provinces, has undoubtedly promoted regularity and prevented many plagues of too rigid order. On paper, everything now runs beautifully—just consult the bundle of reports that the press releases each year. But it does not at all follow from this that life is healthier and more luxuriant.

§14. The Diversity Is Lost.

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”

This would be less the case if the life in the different Provinces had not lost its own distinct character almost entirely. If one could say that wherever one travels in our country, one finds Dutchmen without further ado, as if they were identical in their bearing everywhere, it would still be manageable. But that is not the case. We are not a people whose diverse development has only just begun, but we are rather already old and well-seasoned in the political domain. A rich past lies behind us, and in this rich past the inhabitants of the different Provinces have developed somewhat distinctly according to their own character. The Zeelander has nothing of the Groninger, the Limburger almost nothing of the Frisian. Even the descent differed. The region of the country where one settled in ancient times gave diversity. Occupation and way of life further accentuated this difference. It is precisely in this multiplicity and diversity of our people that not a little of our strength lies. And this historical power is now diminishing and being lost through this centralization, just because it is spread out before us. We are impoverished nationally because of it. And what is even more sorrowful, we have already strayed so far from the old historical track through this centralizing drive that it must be seriously doubted whether it will still be possible, even if only to a limited extent, to approach decentralization again. The attitude recently adopted by the Province of North Brabant in the matter of the provision of electricity was in so far a happy sign of life, as it showed that something of the old spirit could still awaken after all. It was there a matter of subordinate importance, but something of the old spirit did speak in it. In general, however, it must be acknowledged that the spontaneous urge to leave nothing untouched of the autonomy of the Provinces manifests itself only very rarely. If a detail issue is on the agenda,

[256]

THE ADMINISTRATION OF THE PROVINCES.
