When considering a road that has been laid out or a canal that is to be dug, one naturally takes an interest in that part of the Province through which that road or canal will run, but usually in a way that leaves the rest of the Province cold. When I sat for Ommen in the Second Chamber, I became acquainted with such a case-position, from up close even. But if you ask in general whether there is interest in the Province for what the Provincial States accomplish, it must be acknowledged that this interest only very rarely manifests itself, and even when it does, it bears an extremely languid character. Hardly any notice is taken of the proceedings of the States, and nothing that one could say warms up all of Friesland or all of Utrecht occurs anymore. Everything looks to the Residence, reads about the States-General, takes note of the Government Gazettes, but the provincial bustle no longer grabs anyone, and often leaves them cold. This is to be regretted. In common life, everyone recognizes that the real power does not lie in the ordinary-taken type, but in the special modifications of the common type. Take a photograph of an entire family, and if it so happens that between the facial expressions and bearing of the persons, not even the sharpest eye can detect any difference, you readily know that this family will not amount to much. It is a type-family, but without the type of truly distinctive persons. And this is the difference that also applies here. Behind us lies a past in which each Province developed in its own way, unfolded its own life, displayed a distinct type, was and had something of its own, and this peculiarity of the entire life course of the people in that Province came to light. This even came out in the clothing and in the folk games. The Frisian cap betrayed a rich and refined spirit of chaste taste and dignified seriousness in the entire Frisian people. In the State of Flanders, a completely different type expressed itself. But taking the country as a whole, a richly developed peculiarity still manifested itself in so many varied and rich types.

§15. The Demise of Historical Beauty. But now one sees, on the contrary, how all too much of this historical beauty is in the process of dissolving into the central generality, which,

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[257]

THE LOSS OF HISTORICAL BEAUTY. 245 Our people, in their peculiar way, draw their model from Paris. Our critique of this centralizing and homogenizing tendency is therefore no mere side issue. Our Calvinists of old have understood the high national and moral power that lies in cultivating independence, in passionately loving freedom, and in clinging to their own ancient forms. If it had been granted to us to continue on that line, our people would have displayed a very different power than is now the case. The spirit of Calvin had enriched and strengthened us, the spirit of Danton, Voltaire and Robespierre has but too much stripped us of our Calvinistic peculiarity, and caused us to merge into the uniformity of European Liberalism. And against this, not even a protest any longer goes forth. Rather, it is praised. On the Scheveningen road, in your walk, you meet two types from the village; on the one hand, the old Scheveningen figures, dressed and coiffed in the old Scheveningen style. But on the other hand, you constantly encounter young daughters who, though belonging to the village, have purchased a dress of Parisian cut in The Hague, and whose bearing therewith does not at all fit. In Friesland you encounter the same. Whoever there lays aside the cap and consorts with Paris, considers himself a lady, and looks down half-contemptuously on the wearers of the old-fashioned model. Fortunately, it may be said that among us, Calvinists and Anti-revolutionaries, the love for the peculiar type is still much stronger than among other fellow-countrymen. If this could continue, our country would gain by it. Peculiar form displays a peculiar nature and a peculiar life. It should not be just about tone upon tone, but about the varied tones of the gamut. Not the many-leaved, coarse leaf, but the endlessly varied leaf and flower types make your court rich. This also applies in the political sphere. Suppose, for example, that education were so arranged that the municipalities would provide for primary education, the provinces for secondary and grammar school education, and the state for higher education, how then a peculiar life would already sprout forth in education, which is now somewhat lacking. See in the telegrams that report to us the course of the war, how the mutual rivalry between the Hessian, Baden, Saxon and other troops spurs on the fighting spirit, and ask yourself whether we too would not stand much stronger if, with us too, the provincial type had come more into its own in our national army.

[258]

246 THE MUNICIPALITIES. But as said, how far we have strayed from the earlier rich diversity, the less the chance that we will ever again be freed from the curse of this centralizing uniformity. It is especially the ever-increasing meddling of the State that also with us makes more and more people long for the all-encompassing care of the State. Chapter IX. THE MUNICIPALITIES

§1. Aroused interest. Now, in passing from the Provinces to the municipal system, one immediately encounters the striking contrast between the general indifference towards Provincial interests and their warm interest in municipal affairs. This is undoubtedly largely due to the often very heavy burden of municipal taxes, which especially in the largest cities keep climbing again and again, and now already approach or exceed 5 to 6% of net income. For the provincial administration too, it must be known, but the surcharges on the Personal Tax differ so markedly for both. Groningen remains outside the discussion, as it is known to have exceptionally high provincial expenditures for its Water Management; but take, for example, South Holland, and then, apart from the war situation, the surcharge rate in 1912 stood at 110 for the municipalities in The Hague and only at 11 for the provinces. So a tenfold difference. Yet it is not only the higher taxes that prick here. Much stronger is the stimulus that emanates from the ever-increasing number of officials and workers who are often in the service of the municipalities.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[259]

The salaried employees often control the situation. 247 In most municipal services, and with those where the peculiar phenomenon does not present itself that the salaried servant of the municipality, even if indirectly, cooperates in the regulation of his own conditions of service.

§2. The salaried employees often control the situation. In almost every large city, the phenomenon constantly occurs that, in particular, the teachers of the Public Schools and the municipal workers almost completely control the election of the members of the Municipal Council. To this is added, in the third place, that an ever-stronger desire is arising among the citizenry that the Municipal Council should in a mild way and in all kinds of ways provide for what they wish, that for the benefit of the citizens and for their enjoyment, the early fathers in the Municipal Council will make available. The theater performance then takes the lead. Furthermore, one demands urban bathing facilities. Increasingly rich municipal hospitals. Parks and promenades of beautiful design. Everywhere the laying of tramways. Excellent water supply. And above all, generous relief for the poor, provided it is never called relief. It could not be surprising, therefore, that where the action emanating from the Province is mostly very limited, and on the other hand that of the Municipal Council is constantly expanding, and sometimes becomes all-encompassing, even those who previously did not at all care about the Provincial interests, often stand more alert at their post, whenever an important decision had to be made in the municipal interest.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[260]

248 THE MUNICIPALITIES. If the independent development of this civic freedom has been far from favorable, yet a constant unfolding of life and energy is to be observed in the municipal field, which shows how municipal life, in spite of an unfavorable legislation, knows how to assert itself, sometimes exerting a rather rapid influence even on the Provincial States, and often brings it about that even in the highest government circles, at least with the largest cities, one reckons.

§3. The cities in antiquity and now. The Province or the State, where it lost its Sovereignty, can never be anything other than an intermediate link, between the national government and the local inhabitants scattered throughout the land. The municipality, on the other hand, has been from the outset a sovereign state in miniature, and this also explains why in ancient times the city of cities was often the bearer of central authority. So it was in Egypt with Memphis and Thebes, in Assyria with Nineveh, in Babylon with Babel, in Persia with Susa; and it was no different in Greece with Sparta, Athens and Corinth, and on the Italian peninsula with Rome. But neither Italy nor Greece should be mentioned. Athens in Greece was the representation of political power, and so the emerging political power often bore no other than an urban character. Even when the Roman Empire eventually ruled the entire known world, the Senate of Rome, which held the decision in its hands, was nothing other than a municipal body. Under Rome, even when it wielded its scepter over three continents, nothing else was ever understood than the city on the Tiber, and the Emperor of the Roman Empire was in so far a municipal office holder. Alongside and under the urbs Roma stood the Municipia, which bore more the character of what we call “Municipalities.” But however one looks at it, the central cities as we see them especially in Greece and in the Roman Empire, actually exercised state power, and yet in their essence were nothing other than what we call cities or municipalities. Now one immediately distinguishes between the name of cities and the common name of municipalities. With us, our Municipal Act has strongly accentuated this distinction.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[261]

THE INFLUENCE OF THE FRENCH REVOLUTION. 249 The official legal language completely disregarded any distinction. The fatal tendency to melt our entire human life into a deadly uniformity, formally, in the most absolute sense, wiped out any distinction between a capital, a city, a town, a village, and a hamlet. What had traditionally vied for an independent position would henceforth be called a municipality, uniformly, never anything else. Hence the law scarcely recognizes any distinction between a city like Amsterdam with more than half a million inhabitants and one of the many small villages, such as are found especially in Zeeland; villages sometimes not even counting half a thousand inhabitants. The number of municipal council members or aldermen makes a difference in the higher or lower population figure, but otherwise Amsterdam and Urk stand on a footing of complete equality. The political life, too, is dissolved into similar atoms, and there is no eye for the rich variation in life. All imitation of French revolutionary narrowness, which one constantly encounters in practice.

§4. The Influence of the French Revolution. Of this, there is no trace in lands that escaped the French revolutionary fever. In the Rhine provinces and in Belgium, the imitation of the French model has continued, but in the more eastern part of Germany, small villages and towns have a completely different organization for their household than Berlin or Breslau. With us too, from the outset, and from various quarters, protest has been made against Thorbecke’s unification of all cities and villages in his Municipal Act; and although in higher circles this criticism was initially insensitive, it can be said without fear of contradiction that the error committed is now generally recognized, so that, should it come to a revision of this organic law, a division into two or three, if not four classes or types of our municipalities, can be expected. The household of the small villages and towns can and must be greatly simplified, while the household of the larger cities must receive a much more elaborate regulation. As it is now, the legal regulation does not correspond to the demands of life. A maximum unity bond has now been applied to life, which hinders the free expression of life, and what we must demand in its place is a regulation that blossoms forth from the very root of life itself. It is not in an ordinary village, but only in a city of significance, that the richly developed civic life comes into its own. This was also the intention, and the realization, in the rise of the classical cities of old. All such leading cities were what we would now call states. They stood almost completely equal in significance and legal position. They had risen independently. They had known how to safeguard their independence through the weapon of their own army and sometimes even their own fleet. There was no question of subordination to others; rather, of the subordination of others to them. Yet it cannot be said that our present-day cities stand on a par with these sovereign cities from the days of antiquity. Rather, the condition prevailed here from of old that the common people engaged in agriculture under the rule of a territorial lord, who had subjected the entire surrounding area to his castle. In the days of territorial lordship, there were then also no cities of significance that arose here.

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.
[262]

250 THE MUNICIPALITIES.

There was now applied to life a maximum unity bond, which hinders the free expression of life, and what we must demand in its place is a regulation that blossoms forth from the very root of life itself. It is not in an ordinary village, but only in a city of significance, that the richly developed civic life comes into its own. This was also the intention, and the realization, in the rise of the classical cities of old. All such leading cities were what we would now call states. They stood almost completely equal in significance and legal position. They had risen independently. They had known how to safeguard their independence through the weapon of their own army and sometimes even their own fleet. There was no question of subordination to others; rather, of the subordination of others to them. Yet it cannot be said that our present-day cities stand on a par with these sovereign cities from the days of antiquity. Rather, the condition prevailed here from of old that the common people engaged in agriculture under the rule of a territorial lord, who had subjected the entire surrounding area to his castle. In the days of territorial lordship, there were then also no cities of significance that arose here.

§5. The Rise of Our Urban Life. Urban life was still unknown. This only came to the fore when, gradually, separated from the territorial greatness of the nobility and the population devoted to agriculture, business, manufacturing, shipping, trade, science, and art also began to emerge. Those who then devoted themselves to these various spheres of life stood partly too high to sink into the agricultural population, and were partly also too ordinary to find housing in the moats and castles. The business, industry, shipping, and trade also demanded a different mode of settlement and organization of life. This now gave rise, as it were, to the birth of cities, of greater or smaller caliber. The magic means of the mighty circles who then set their sights on settling in such cities was...

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[263]

THE RISE OF OUR URBAN LIFE. 251 school in their money. The financial position of the agricultural class was not high; the lords of the nobility who sat in their castles were often deeply in debt; and the only ones of whom it could be said that they disposed of ample means were the great merchants and bankers in the cities. These dear money-lenders then also lent to them, and even gave the noble landowners a good deal, but not for nothing. Rather, they stipulated rights and privileges for the money given or lent. And so it was not long before such cities could surround themselves with walls, hire their own guards, defend themselves against all violence, and further organize an ever freer and more independent life within their walls, in which they developed a power that far surpassed the power of the feudal lords and nobles in their castles. In the classical cities of antiquity, this had presented itself differently. Whoever devoted himself to any kind of traffic or factory did not even count as a full citizen in Athens. His position was far from that of a free burgher. Hence, in the old classical cities, a freer class of burghers of a higher caliber developed, who took a much broader flight in the political, philosophical and artistic domains, while shipping, trade and industry soon withdrew to the colonial cities that had been newly established. However, this did not happen with us. Yet even then, persons of scientific and artistic inclination soon gave preference to settling in the newly emerging cities, but they did not set the tone there. They profited from the art lovers who came to the fore among the merchants, and built and adorned houses like palaces, in which the financially mighty and the court could hold their splendor; but the city itself remained the city of the wealthy patricians, in which the merchants and bankers, whether from here or originating from Italy, displayed the splendor of their way of life. The consequence of this was that the magistrates and councils of these cities acted ever more independently. The chamberlain no longer needed his castle to bolster them, nor the feudal lord. And so the result could be no other than that these significant cities gradually attracted more and more political power to themselves. Their artillery stood on the walls within which they had enclosed themselves, their troops could march out, and as far as they were situated on main streams or by the sea, they knew how to strengthen their power no less through the establishment of a fleet on a larger scale.

[264]

252 THE MUNICIPALITIES.

§6. Amsterdam in former times and now. This alone explains why Amsterdam in particular has known times when its council acted as if it had to decide the fate of the whole country. Now it goes without saying that this unnatural relationship had to give way to a more normal relationship, and the end result has been that our cities, including Amsterdam, have been placed in the corporate bond of the entire fatherland, so that the council of Amsterdam now of course has to say only over Amsterdam. After 1815, Amsterdam too bowed to the Crown, and the world city of yore was no longer solely concerned with its own well-being. There was therefore nothing particularly painful in the fact that even the proudest cities of yore were now also subjected to the national law; the only question was whether that national law would guarantee such an existence to urban life that they could flourish in freedom. And on this, it is difficult to answer in the affirmative. The great cities had after all been from of old, and so also at their rise in our country, peculiar phenomena of life, which could thrive only as long as no disturbing hindrance stood in the way of their sprouting from the root of their life. Decentralization had to remain the watchword here, and precisely in this it has failed. §7. Our opposition came later. At first, even in Antirevolutionary circles, there was little eye for this shortcoming. There were so many holier interests on which attention initially focused. Moreover, the traditions in which one lived had borne the stamp of the old saying for too long and too strongly: “As the lords direct, so shall we praise.” Already after the end of the first third of the seventeenth century, the commercial aristocracy had managed to master power, and even in the great cities remained almost nothing to say for our small bourgeoisie, while in the countryside the aristocracy of the Dutch lords still exerted a tighter pressure on the small peasant class. From this

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.
[265]

OUR OPPOSITION CAME LATER. - OUR MUNICIPAL LEAGUE.

It is therefore explained that the Anti-Revolutionary party had already been in existence for many years before we on our side began to take a closer look at the interests of the municipalities. It is true that in “Our Program” there was already a complaint about the limitation of municipal rights, and a plea for the emancipation of the office of mayor, but in the party program of ‘79 there was not a single article on municipal administration, in not one of the political election programs was this current interest pointed out, and even in Groen’s days it had not at least been made the subject of broad discussion. Cabinet after Cabinet had caused liberal or conservative party members to act as mayors. These liberal mayors had endeavored to have a like-minded secretary appointed. Pressure from above had also been strongly exerted on the choice of municipal council members. And so it is explained that it was not until the 20th century that we on our side also began to focus more attention on municipal administration. In large numbers, our members had now been elected to the municipal councils everywhere. In the largest cities, one could already welcome some Aldermen from our ranks. The number of Mayors from our circle still remained very low, but it was nevertheless increasing. And so it could not be otherwise, or the question arose, what attitude would flow from our principles towards those who were thus elected, and therefore also for the voters from our ranks. This then also gave rise to the fact that in our local electoral associations the municipal problems gradually came to the fore and that an attempt was made twice to set up a first outline of a municipal program in vague terms and in a small number of articles.

§8. Our Municipal League. But even this did not stop there. In October 1908, they even proceeded to the establishment of a League of Anti-Revolutionary Municipal Councillors in the Netherlands, a league whose actions I might lead in Utrecht with a speech that, under the title: “Independent Municipal System,” saw the light of day that same year at the publisher Bootsma in The Hague. This gave impetus. And under the active leadership of Dr. Van Staveren in Rotterdam, the plan then quickly arose

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.
[266]

254 THE MUNICIPALITIES.

to create a separate organ for the benefit of our municipal councillors and for the dissemination of our municipal convictions. The first issue of this organ saw the light of day in 1910, and under the editorial leadership of Mr. B. de Gaay Fortman, this monthly is now already in its fifth year. With Mr. de Gaay Fortman, the members of the editorial board are Messrs. Mr. Dr. H.W. Hovy, Mayor of Loosduinen, Dr. W.B. van Staveren in Rotterdam, Mr. J.P. Chardon in Delft, and Mr. D.G. Wentink in Utrecht; the first for Business, the second for the Health section, the third for Educational Affairs, and the fourth for Urban Development and Housing. The magazine bears the title: The Municipal Council, and is generally no less than fourteen quarto pages in size. Both the actions of this League and the publication of this periodical may be regarded as favorable signs of life. It appears from this that we on our side are not directing our political efforts, as was once the case, exclusively to the national interests. The municipal regulations and administration are also beginning to occupy our men. And although we are still far from where we need to be, and although it must be acknowledged that even the principle questions that come up here are still not sharply enough formulated, compared to the days of our first entry, they have nevertheless taken a giant step forward in this field as well. It is only to be hoped that the serious distinction between the interests of the large cities and those of most rural municipalities will gradually lead to a separate consideration of the interests of both.

§9. Large and Small Municipalities. Naturally, it is above all the major issues that come up in the large cities, which attract the most attention in the press, captivate the most strongly, and thus automatically come to the fore in a one-sided way. Yet it is highly necessary that the investigation should also be directed more to the ordinary rural municipalities. Our League cannot live on the large cities alone, and it is certainly not the least in the municipalities that, because of their agricultural character and their small population, demand their own leadership, that the Anti-Revolutionary party must seek to strengthen its influence.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[267]

LARGE AND SMALL MUNICIPALITIES - THE ENTERPRISES

The majority of municipal members are adherents. It cannot be emphasized enough that our League should also direct its attention distinctly to this twofold type of municipal administration. If Thorbecke’s Municipal Act was the great fault, in that it treated our municipalities, large and small, rural and urban, almost entirely alike; against this fault, resistance must continually go forth, and it may very well be expected that this distinction will also express itself in the composition of the editorial staff of our organ. Undoubtedly, there are interests that come to the fore just as much in the small village as in the largest city. Such as, for example, the observance of the Lord’s Day, the regulation of drinking establishments, the fair, houses of ill repute, the relationship to the Diaconate, and much more; questions where all differences of opinion among us fall away, and where it will therefore be more a matter of harmonizing the proposed regulations in an unforced way with the local conditions.

§10. The Enterprises

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.

But we are not yet done with this. The social questions have also become no less serious in significance. The position of those who are in the service of the Municipality may not be left to the harsh views of an unsocial mayor. Also the Enterprises in the large cities, which have more than one Municipal Council under their control, demand investigation, both as to their nature and with a view to their independent regulation. What the Municipal Council may do, and what it may not take under its control, is a problem that awaits very serious investigation, if one does not want to drift unnoticed into Socialism. And then there is the almost all-encompassing financial question, which confronts us with the problem of whether the Municipal Council may take at its discretion from the treasury of those who have much, to benefit those who have little. We will now say nothing about the School System. This is heading towards its definitive regulation. May, therefore, the periodic report of the Municipal Council on these main problems also shed ever clearer light.

[268]

256 THE MUNICIPALITIES

How radical-blurred, ultra-radical A. Damaschke in his Aufgaben der Gemeindepolitik, especially in the 4th edition that saw the light in 1901, also went, it still has its use to summarize his ideas in a comprehensive whole; for this precisely leads back to the starting point in your political principle.

§11. The Municipality Autonomous

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.

Especially in municipal administration, the detailed questions press to the fore from all sides, just as in the private household; but precisely these detailed questions can inadvertently lead us down entirely wrong paths if we do not follow a firm guiding thread in their assessment; and yet, in stretching out those guiding threads, you can repeatedly come into contradiction with yourself, and on the one hand tear down what you build up on the other, if those distinct guiding threads are not unwound from the same skein. The Municipality as a phenomenon in political life is of the same nature as the State itself. Just as the State, the Municipality is also a legal person. It has its own domain on which its life arose and continues, and on this domain, which forms its territory, it exercises authority over a population, just as the State does in its domain. Its power of authority also extends, though in modified bounds, over all persons and all things that are on its domain, just as the State’s does. The Municipality also has an order in conformity with which it acts and operates; it has, just like the State, high officials, low officials, civil servants and employees. Hence, the Municipality must also have the necessary autonomy to issue rules and ordinances for public life on its domain. Separate from State and Province, the Municipality therefore also has its own Administration, just as there is an independent administration in every household through marriage and especially the birth of children. And it was not too high-flown that the Municipality formerly also had its own jurisdiction, as is still the case in the first instance in many places. Politics cannot take the lead here. Politics is for the maintenance of actual order, not for the administration of justice in complex relationships.

[269]

THE MUNICIPALITY AUTONOMOUS - CITY-STATE.

Because the Municipality, just like the State, is its own subject, the Municipality can also act as a buyer or seller, and can offer services to have itself paid for those services, and thus act in a private-law manner. But it does not stop there. The Municipality, in order to be able to live, just like the State, must have the necessary means at its disposal; which means that it must be able to have property that yields interest, and that it must be able to earn through business, but also that it may levy taxes on its citizens and on anyone who in whatever way comes into contact with it from the outside, whether as a passer-through or as an importer from outside. Paris has rather high income taxes. Formerly, these were levied almost everywhere. The guards at the gates watched over this. In order to maintain its freedom unimpaired, it is even strictly necessary that the Municipalities be self-sufficient in their own housekeeping.

§12. City and State

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.

This identity in the fundamental traits of state life and of urban life goes so far that not the city has imitated the State, but much rather in the great, independently emerging cities the popular life has developed, and for this popular life a regulation has formed itself, which has rather served as an example to the State. The correct concept of authority, the concept of subject and yet fellow-citizen, the awareness of the duty to watch over the general welfare and at the same time over the general order, has not first penetrated into the great agglomerations, but rather first and foremost into the classic cities of Antiquity and into the great commercial cities and the free Imperial cities, and from there has been transferred to the state regulation. Not the State went first, so that the urban community life followed, but in the great cities the order and the regulation first emerged from the very root of the community, which then shortly afterwards was followed on a broader terrain by the national government. Hence also the fact that in the course of history, time and again, the struggle for supremacy between the free great cities and the national government manifested itself. So it also began with us, with the almost absolute authority of the magnates under the lordships, who had nothing but an agricultural population under them and made these subservient to their own greatness at their own discretion. Against this one-sided domination, the resistance then went out, in particular from the great, flourishing cities. When these cities had then enriched the country and brought it into esteem, this in itself made the national government less powerful. It then tried to break the cities again, and they avenged themselves in new revolutions; especially at the end of the 18th century even through violent upheaval of what existed. And only out of this upheaval did the more ordered condition then gradually restore itself, which the 19th century brought us, though with the dreadful sacrifice of our so significant past, with the obliteration of what had thus formed our own peculiar character, and with the application to the whole country, both town and village, of a uniform order, which was not even of Dutch origin, but at least in part borrowed from the French revolutionary concepts of unity, closer to the Belgian Constitution.

[270]

Now it would of course be too strongly stated if one claimed that hereby all Municipal autonomy had been completely lost with us. This was indeed the case in France; not with us. The Municipalities soon regained a certain right to their own organization. Van Hogendorp felt for this, and actually thought the autonomy sufficiently secured. But if one consults what our Municipal Law has given us, as Thorbecke imposed it on us, then there certainly remained for us a considerable piece of autonomy, over which one in France is jealous, but one feels nevertheless how even with us this autonomy is hemmed in by so many bonds of provincial and national government, and is so interwoven with such an all-sided central power, that it still presents itself as too poor and too modest.

§13. The Position of the Mayor.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.

The limitation of this autonomy has not only been brought about by the power of the Provincial and National government standing above the Municipality, but very certainly also by the authority conferred on the Mayor.

[271]

THE DEPOSITION OF THE MAYOR.

The mayor’s authority is a delegated power. Take, for example, the oft-cited Article 188, which entrusts the police oversight of theaters, inns, taverns, and all buildings and assemblies open to the public, public entertainments, and public houses of ill repute—not to the Mayor and Aldermen, but solely to the Mayor. It is even added: “He, that is, the Mayor, watches over performances that conflict with public order or morality.” Now, considering that the Mayor is appointed by the Crown, outside of any advice or cooperation from the Council, and is thus an official imposed from above on the Municipality, it follows that in this extremely important matter the autonomy of the Municipality is in no way respected. This is done, according to the title of §2 of the third chapter, “for the maintenance of public order,” but experience has shown in more than one respect that this concerns matters over which the Municipality has very definite opinions, and yet the Mayor retains the unlimited power and authority to impose his personal will, often in a way that does not promote, but rather offends, public morality. The Police Commissioner, in whose hands such great power is placed, also comes from outside and is appointed by the Crown, just like the Mayor himself. Even his salary is regulated by the higher Government, after having heard the Provincial Commissioner and the Municipal Council. Even the agents and servants of the police are appointed and dismissed by the Mayor, and it is also the Mayor who, in consultation with the Provincial Commissioner, gives them the necessary official instructions (Art. 191). The rural constables are not even appointed and dismissed by the Mayor, but only in consultation with him, by the Provincial Commissioner. Thus, one sees that in every way where it comes to the exercise of power, not only the Council, but also the college of Mayor and Aldermen, is set aside, and that the regulation and execution of an official emanates from someone who received his appointment from elsewhere, and, moreover, from a higher power, and from outside, to whom his authorities were assigned.

[272]

260 THE MUNICIPALITIES.

§14. Self-government. It seems that the need to limit the power of the so-called autonomous Municipalities has arisen primarily because an overly one-sided right of interference was granted to the Council. For according to our Municipal Law, the Municipal Council is not only called upon to promote the general interests and to draw up and establish the necessary ordinances, but is also called upon to cooperate in the execution of what has been ordered from a higher authority or regulated and ordained by the Council itself. Here two questions are hidden, which require separate discussion. For first of all, the question arises whether it would be advisable to make a sharp separation between the Council and the College of Mayor and Aldermen, as between the legislative and the executive power; and secondly, whether the execution of the laws and ordinances from a higher authority can be entrusted to the local authorities in a manner and to an extent as is currently the case—the question of self-government. Now, as to the first of these two points, it must be established that the Council retains the regulatory authority. As far as autonomy has remained with the local circles of the people, it must be exercised by the Council. All regulations and ordinances that are subject to local establishment must emanate from the Council. For the Council is the body that represents the citizenry, and thus the college that, so to speak, has the legislative task to perform. Even though the execution of this task may almost never be free from the control of the Mayor, the Commissioner of the Queen, the Provincial States, and the National Government, yet it is and remains in its essence legislative in nature. If we now come, as is so urgently necessary, to the division of the Municipalities, according to their population and otherwise, into distinct classes, then in the one class less of this legislative authority will be used; but reduced to whatever small dimensions, the Council must be able to establish certain rules and ordinances for the household of the Municipality, to which the inhabitants of the Municipality must submit. Article 144

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”
[273]

SELF-GOVERNMENT - THE PROPOSALS OF 1912. 261 of the Constitution, as it now reads, goes further, however. For this article says: “The regulation and administration of the household of the Municipality is left to the Council.” Not only the legislative, but also the executive authority. Against this, serious objections have gradually been raised. Not that there are no Municipalities of smaller size and population whose Councils would very well be suited to the fulfillment of this double task, but this is not the question here. Now that our Municipal Law still treats all Municipalities alike, and Article 144, first paragraph, likewise declares equally applicable to the smallest and the largest Municipality, the question cannot be avoided whether it is appropriate, also in the Municipal Councils of our larger cities, not only to regulate, but also to entrust the administrative task to the entire Council. To this question, the Commission for the revision of the Constitution, whose report came out in 1912, gave a decisive negative answer. Hence its proposal to delete the words “and the administration” from Article 144.

§15. The proposals of 1912. One should not give this proposal a perverse interpretation, and understand what was proposed not in the sense that henceforth in no case the administration would be allowed to be exercised by the Council. This was by no means the intention of the Commission’s proposals. On the contrary, the Commission proceeded from the assumption that, even after this amendment of Article 144, there would still be many Municipalities whose Council would take care of the regulation of the administration. What was aimed at was merely to allow the Municipal Law to be so arranged, in a division of the Municipalities into classes, that there would be Municipalities whose Council would not at all concern itself with the administration, and that next to these there would be other Municipalities whose Council would also be entrusted, in whole or in part, with the administration, in addition to the regulation. In the Commission’s Report, on page 22, it is stated as clearly as possible: “The Constitution must break with the demand that, in addition to the power of regulation, that of administration in its fullness, under all circumstances, and in every Municipality, must also be granted.”

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[274]

262 THE MUNICIPALITIES. The administration of the municipalities, without distinction, must rest with the Council. It can safely incorporate the administrative organization in the points that it submits to the law regarding the municipal system. It must confine itself to demanding from the Council full and undiminished power to regulate and ordain. With regard to the administrative organization, the legislator then gets a free hand and can make his own insight the basis. He can organize the administration of some municipalities differently than for others; he can leave it to a large extent with the Council or take it away from them and shift it to Colleges, originating from or outside the Council, elected by the Council or in another way. In short, he can, provided he leaves the regulatory power to the Council, accept any conceivable arrangement of the municipal administration and thereby meet all the objections to the system embodied in the Municipal Act. I agree with this amendment. Through this change, we would come closer to what Steyn devised for Germany, and which proved so profitable for our eastern neighbors. It is the uniformity that also spoiled the matter here. What is usable for a municipality of 3,000 souls may by no means be so for a municipality of 300,000. Multiformity must also on this point adapt the organization to the multiformity of life. Only when the words “and the administration” fall away from Art. 144 of the Constitution, will the municipal legislator regain the freedom to take into account the strong distinction between the large and the small, the agricultural and the commercial municipality. In municipalities of a lower class, the administration can largely be entrusted to the mayor and aldermen, and in another class the task of the administration can be divided through the establishment of various commissions. Elsewhere, it will again be able to remain as it is now, since no hindrance was experienced. In short, the Municipal Act must so order that the organization of the organization in each type of municipality corresponds to its condition. It should only be noted here that the assignment of the administrative task to the college of mayor and aldermen would only be recommendable if the mayor was no longer like an agent of the national government.

[275]

A RICHER LIFE ALSO HERE. 263

§16. A richer life also here.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.

If one compares the proceedings of the municipal council in a city like Amsterdam or Rotterdam, as they are deposited in our city archives from the middle of the previous century, with the proceedings of that same municipal council in this century, the difference in the number of pages already shows, but even more the difference in the agenda and in the course of the debates, that what could once seem usable, and was even acceptable to Thorbecke, now runs aground. Entirely independent of the intentions and efforts of the framers of the Constitution or the Legislator, our official state and municipal life in Parliament and Municipal Council has also developed in such an extraordinary way towards all-sided unfolding, that one cannot be surprised if one speaks of a quadrupling of content. The Proceedings of the States General of 1850 and now are hardly comparable anymore, and the same applies to anyone who compares the municipal council reports of fifty years ago with those of today. And although it must now be acknowledged that much has been drawn into the public domain that would undoubtedly have remained better in private hands, yet no one would wish to return to the sober reaction that for seventy years made the pulse of national life beat so alarmingly faintly. Our people of today are so entirely different from the people of then. The entire standard of living has taken on such a changed character, that it was absolutely impossible to perpetuate the insensitivity that prevailed in olden times. As late as 1845, everything was left to its own devices; now one tends to get involved in everything. The press, still insignificant in 1840, has now awakened and warmed the public. One hears of everything, one gets involved in everything, one is eager to exert influence on everything for one’s own share. The expansion of the right to vote has further accelerated the wider and broader unfolding of public life. It must even be acknowledged that the mighty inventions that came to surprise the amazed public after 1850 necessitated a common public appearance, for which each person previously sought and found his own way in his own circle.

[276]

The municipalities. The master of the light - now gas and electricity have demanded the lighting of the whole city - pushed everything in the public direction. It was the same with the water supply and the means of transport. The municipal councils could not leave all this to private individuals in the long run. And just as with the physical given, so too it increasingly came to lie with social affairs. One could no longer, each for himself, stand up for his interests. Binding in clubs, in corps, in unions became the general slogan. As this all pressed forward of itself and unnoticed, the first college to which it all came down had to be the municipal council. Hence the organization of our municipal councils no longer proved to be in harmony with the actuality of life. The State Commission under the chairmanship of Heemskerk has recognized and acknowledged this, and it would indeed be a gain for our entire public life if the “administration” were to disappear from Article 144.

§17. The cooperation of the municipal authorities. We now come to the second point: the cooperation required in Art. 126 of the local municipalities “for the execution of laws, of general administrative measures, of the King’s orders relating thereto, and of provincial regulations and ordinances.” As a rule, only the mayor and aldermen are responsible for this cooperation. Only insofar as this is expressly stated in so many words does the council also intervene here; and if it happened that the council remained behind in this, then what was entrusted to the council also comes at the expense of the college of mayor and aldermen. If the cooperation was also not provided by the mayor and aldermen in a sufficient manner, then the task does not fall on the mayor alone, but on the head of the Provincial Administration (see Article 127). This whole regulation now lies in the English line. In England, too, the rule is just as valid that the local and district authorities lend themselves to the execution of what is commanded from above. This institution now intends that the national government, and partly the Provincial Administration, can order, command and order in all kinds of ways, but for the execution thereof not to appoint their own officials, servants or agents as a rule, but the local municipalities to be employed. In these municipalities, then, the task that went out is carried out by the council, or in the council by the mayor and aldermen. If localities are to be made ready for the execution of that task, permanent or temporary officials to be employed, offices and commissions to be assembled, expenditures to be made for costs, then the initiative for all this as a rule comes from B. and W., and it is the municipality that bears the costs. How high these costs can run can be seen in the accounts of the large cities, and how overburdened and almost cumbersome the care for the fulfillment of all the regulations laid down and also the execution of all the task assignments is, can be seen at the Town Hall in such large municipalities. For it is not a care and concern that is added as a part to the already so great municipal task, but it is a concern in itself so comprehensive that neither the mayor nor the aldermen can keep a close eye on it, and that it is rather a thriving bureaucracy that is settling in the halls of the Town Hall with increasing power. For a long time now, one could also hear the complaint that the mayor of Amsterdam was almost collapsing under it. And yet, instead of diminishing, the task hidden in this is still increasing. The Residence and the Province order and command, but the fulfillment, the elaboration and execution of what is commanded is as a rule placed on B. and W. This now does not present a predominant objection financially, since the municipality received the authority to levy a very large number of percentages on national taxes, but, leaving the financial aside, the objections that cling to this way of acting are of a higher serious nature. §18. Execution of Higher Authority. That the administrators of the municipalities burden themselves with the fulfillment and execution of what the municipality itself has ordained is entirely natural. For then the rules apply that emanate from it as a municipality, and that move in a field that every member of the municipal council can oversee. Against this, there has never been any objection either. But the case is different when the municipalities have to cooperate in the execution of what is commanded from above. Here the objection arises that the municipal council is not the appropriate body for this, and that the municipal council is not the appropriate body for this, and that the municipal council is not body for this, and that the municipal council is not the appropriate body for this, and that the municipal council is not the appropriate body

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the
[278]

But it is quite different when it comes to regulations, laws, measures, and orders that have been issued by a completely different authority and that impose themselves in connection with laws and circumstances that often lie entirely outside the domain of local action. Even the knowledge of such ordinances and commands requires a special study that can never be demanded of the great majority of municipal council members, and that remains a terra incognita even for most aldermen. And when one adds that not a month goes by without the bundle of this complex having swelled again, and that in what was thus gilded, smaller or larger modifications have been made, then remaining up to date with all that these central and provincial laws, regulations, and commands contain is not to be expected of anyone who does not devote themselves to it intentionally. Especially now that a spirit has permeated everything, determining everything, regulating everything with the utmost precision in legal form, the complex in question has taken on such an awe-inspiring scope that only those who devote themselves intentionally to its study can be considered up to date. Leaving the great municipalities aside, one cannot at all count on the ordinary municipal council members, and only exceptionally on the aldermen, and in fact only on the mayor and the secretary. Handbooks help to some extent. The provincial registry comes to the rescue. And so one gets by. But in the end, it is still the case that a huge part of the work of village administrations in this service is done for the benefit of the country and the province. Now that even the largest city and the smallest village are subject to the same almost completely uniform regulation, it is self-evident that this must lead to stifling disproportions. The mass of work in the great cities has become so great that the mayor can no longer handle it, and must entrust it in good faith to his bureaus, and in the village communities this entire composite task goes over the heads of most of the council members, and even of the aldermen, to such an extent that almost everything has to be left to the mayor and the secretary. In their own village affairs, such council members and aldermen are very much at home. They can assess such matters and cast their votes with knowledge of the facts and with mature judgment, but the many...

[279]

MUNICIPAL AUTONOMY. The broader-reaching work that the execution and implementation of the Central and Provincial mandates requires goes over their heads. The consequence of this is that the Mayor and the secretary, for whose ultimate responsibility all this must come, as a rule cannot be men from the Municipality, but must be persons of a higher quality who must be placed in the wheel-work of the Municipality from elsewhere.

§19. Municipal Autonomy. This has nothing to do with autonomy. The restriction of Municipal autonomy has been brought about, firstly, by the fact that the State and Province have drawn to themselves regulations that by their nature belonged to the Municipalities, and secondly, by the fact that a more central regulation has been established, so far-reaching that often every right to self-regulation seems to have been lost. Even the Mayor received in Art. 70 the authority not to execute a decision of the Council or a decision of the Mayor and Aldermen, if it seemed to him not only that it was in conflict with the law (this could still be understood), but also if he judged that it was in conflict with the general interest; a term so vague that in this a very decisive increase of power of a not unquestionable nature lay hidden. Nor has this authority been used all too rarely. But quite apart from this Municipal autonomy and this Municipal self-government, the issue here is a service that the local Municipality has to perform for the benefit of the National government or the Provincial authorities.
