The National government and Provincial authorities are thus not obliged to always appoint personnel and incur costs themselves for the enforcement of their orders and mandates. They rather consider that the Municipal authority is designated for this as a matter of course, and simply impose this burden on this authority. It is precisely this that is the reason why the Mayor in our country has been made into an agent of the National administration. He is not chosen by the Council, nor as a rule from among the Council. This was originally the intention. Hence the still so strangely sounding provision in the last paragraph of Art. 143 of our Constitution: “The Chairman is chosen by the King, also outside the members of the Council.” In this mode of expression it was still evident that, properly considered, the Mayor should not be brought into the Municipality from the outside, yes, that it should even be the rule that he was first chosen as a member of the Council by the citizenry. For a long time, however, there has been no longer any talk of adhering to this rule. Where it concerns a new appointment, the appointment of a city or village resident is now even the rare exception. Amsterdam, Rotterdam, Leiden, Utrecht and whatever city one may want, are also now under the dominion of a Mayor brought in from elsewhere; and for the small village communities this is so much the rule that one would have difficulty in bringing even 10% of Mayors to the list who were taken from their village, let alone from the Council of their village. Whoever is also Minister of the Interior, one can only take a man from the place by rare exception. This does not work if the place is small in population, because then one can only find a person by high exception in such a village who would be grown up to the extensive, highly demanding task to which our present-day Mayor is called. And this would go more by itself in a larger city, but precisely in those larger cities the mayoral task expands so incredibly, and requires such special qualities, that one always prefers someone who has already passed the test in a somewhat smaller, but still quite large Municipality.

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.
[280]

§20. How to provide counsel here? One must frankly admit that it would be an impossible thing, as things stand at this moment, to return the appointment of the Mayor to the Council. The Municipalities must now be treated like the Provinces, and the Crown must with its own authority appoint the Mayors. It only seems strange that while the Commissioner of the King in the Province, in accordance with this right of appointment, also receives his annual income, and his pension, yes, even his residence with appurtenances from the State, the Mayor, who is just as independent of the Council, appointed by the Crown, does not receive his annual income, his pension, or his residence from the State.

Analytical Summary: The three types not equally characteristic. But this does not mean that, in the course of time, each of these three types has in turn dominated the national condition: the Roman Catholics in the Middle Ages, the Calvinists in the 16th century, and the French Revolutionaries after 1789. It does not at all follow from this that they are equal in national significance. Undoubtedly, the Seven Provinces, once living a common life with Flanders and other southern regions, have enjoyed a high and flourishing position and have gradually taken an increasingly important position in Northern Europe; but it must not be forgotten here that the national energy was at the time much more strongly developed in what is now called Belgium than in the northern provinces. Neither Amsterdam nor Rotterdam could in those days be placed on a par with Ghent and Bruges. The wealth of medieval life had more stamped its imprint on the national character in the South than in the North. The reference to the rich medieval type therefore has much less significance for the present Kingdom of the Netherlands than for the western part of Belgium. And in the second place, it must be added that at that time the unity of our national life was much less consolidated. Under the Burgundians, the Dutch provinces did not occupy an independent position, but were united with what lay outside our later borders; and under Charles V and Philip, we even came, though only in a personal way, into a Union with entirely different states from Central and Southern Europe—something which could not but be detrimental to the confirmation of our own national character. Whoever investigates the historical significance of our lower lands may well find the period we lived through in the Middle Ages highly important, but nevertheless hesitates for a moment to acknowledge without reservation that our glorious period dates from April 1, 1572. Not in the Middle Ages, but in the period of the Reformation, lies the high point of our national history. It cannot be emphasized enough how much scrupulous fairness is required in such a comparison, and we shall beware of the assertion that from the Calvinist side the correct toll of reverence has always been paid to the medieval period in our history, to which they undoubtedly had a right. But it stands to reason that from the Roman Catholic side, too, we have not always been done justice. It cannot be valued highly enough that especially in the second half of the 19th century a more impartial judgment gained influence. Just with a view to this more unanimous judgment, it may now at least be established that not only the foreign historians, one may well say without any exception of importance, honor the period after 1572 as the highest and most glorious, but that also within the country the best interpreters of each of the three types increasingly come to the unanimous conclusion that the world significance of our small country came out most richly in the heyday of Calvinism. This in no way denies that our national life in those days of our glory also showed dark sides. The resistance to the monarchical state form, and even against the House of Orange, has almost become a source of our unhappy discord until 1789, and it is impossible to say how much higher our country would have soared if immediately after the separation from Philip II the crown had been offered to Prince William. It had to come to this in 1813; but who calculates what national suffering would have been spared us if we had taken this important step two centuries earlier. But even if one plays open cards and takes into account all the suffering, oppression and restriction of rights that the temporarily dominant group has inflicted on the other two groups, it is historically established that we were never before 1572 what our national life was in the period between those two dates, and never again after 1789. To top it off, the fact that we offered a refuge to the politically and religiously persecuted from all countries is proof that the actual life here was a life in freedom, and that the publications and ordinances that claimed to infringe on this freedom had more the effect of satisfying formal demands than of inflicting suffering on those who deviated.
[281]

HOW TO PROVIDE REMEDY HERE. 269 The Municipality must receive what comes from the Government - One should note here a threefold distinction in law. Formerly, the Mayor was elected by the locality itself, entirely outside the government of the land and province, and even in Tyrol I still found this right in force, that the local council itself appointed its own Mayor. The second right is that while the appointment of the Mayor emanates from the Crown, the Council now has the right to make a nomination. And the third right is that the Crown is completely free and sovereign, without any other advice than from its own Ministers and Commissioners in the Province, to appoint as Mayor whomever it pleases the bearer of the Crown. This has been consistently and seriously objected to, especially from the Calvinist side; something that is explained by the ecclesiastical usages of the Reformed. In a Reformed village, there are two persons who set the tone in the Municipality: the Mayor in matters of civil law, the Minister of the Word in ecclesiastical affairs. Now, before the Reformation, there was also with respect to the ecclesiastical head the fixed usage that the pastor or shepherd of the Congregation received his sanction from the Bishop; but precisely this was changed by the Reformation, and among the Calvinists this right of appointment remained with the Consistory. It is true that especially in Friesland and Gelderland, many high lords often refused to give up the right of the Lords of the Manor, so that in fact not a few appointments of Ministers of the Word emanated from these lords; but this was against the Calvinist principle and was mostly disapproved. In general, however, it can be said that the Ministers of the Word did come from outside, just as the Mayors do now, but in such a way that these Ministers were not installed by an external power, but were chosen by the Consistory. One would probably have acquiesced in this, even if in the case of the appointment of the Mayor, a man from outside had been the regular case, provided that in the choice the Council had been reckoned with. If only in some way the outcome had been reached that the father of the city had taken his high position in agreement with the municipal authorities, there would not have been such a serious objection to appointment by the Crown. But now, on the contrary, when one has fallen into the very objectionable extreme of letting the Mayor be installed in the Municipality entirely from the outside, without the knowledge of the Council, by a higher power, an entirely unnatural state of affairs has been brought into being.

[282]

270 THE MUNICIPALITIES. But this cannot remain so. It is already troublesome enough that this method was used in the appointment of a head for the Provincial Administration, but here an appeal could still be made to the former Stadtholders, which at least partially held up. However, in the case of the mayoral office, a principle has been introduced by Art. 143 of the Constitution that runs too much counter to the natural tendency of our free popular sentiment.

§21. Recapitulation. In short, the distinguishing contrast of political convictions regarding municipal life comes down to the following. On the one hand, one proceeds from the State as a whole, standing under the supreme national government. That national government now cuts the entire country into a certain number of Provinces, and again those Provinces into a certain number of Municipalities. It places those Provinces and those Municipalities under a chief official appointed by it, who is called the Commissioner over the Province and bears the title of Mayor in the Municipality. It now gives these chief officials, by law, measures of administration and necessary instructions, and to these heads of Provinces and Municipalities the voters may then add a Provincial States and a Municipal Council, who then in turn appoint from their midst, on the one hand, Provincial Deputies and on the other hand, Aldermen as advisors. These provincial and municipal administrations now receive directives for conduct from the Central National Government. Partially, autonomous authority has been granted here, but one that is bound and limited in all kinds of ways, by law, by general administrative measure, by order, and no less by a high authority that partly emanates from the Commissioners and Mayors, partly from the Provincial powers and the Central Government, and in a very extensive way hampers the freedom of movement.

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over
[283]

RECAPITULATION. - THE CALVINISTIC LINE. 271 Opposed to this view now stands the entirely different one, which, connecting to our past, found its advocate in Freiherr Von Stein; a view that also connects in principle to what became the rule in America. According to these two views, there is not first a whole country that is then divided into provincial parts, and those Provinces in turn cut into Municipalities, but one must proceed from the Provinces and the Municipalities as independently arisen formations, by whose combination the whole has arisen or been rounded off. By analogy, a later and single Province, such as Drenthe, may have been added here, and in the same way a new urban appearance may have come to the existing ones, but the starting point remains that the Provinces and Municipalities were there first, in general, and that the whole was not divided by the shears, but united by the bond of history. According to the first outlined representation, Provinces and Municipalities are merely auxiliary formations that serve as organs of the national whole, and, under the rule of national law, are in the service of the High Government, while, according to the historical and only correct representation for our country, the combination has led to the formation of the whole from their inherently independent partial formations.

§22. The Calvinistic Line.

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the

The latter conviction lies on the Calvinistic line. This does not dispute that a higher authority must watch over the combined whole. Conflicts of interest may arise between Provinces that they themselves cannot peacefully settle. The same can happen between Municipalities. In those Municipalities, too, a minority may be delivered without defense to a majority that violates its rights. Here the high Government watches through law and supervision. A river or a coastal water may threaten danger that threatens more than one Province or more than one Municipality. If the warding off of this is not found in friendly arrangement, then the high Government must provide. An epidemic disease may break out among people or animals, or also threaten from abroad. Naturally, then, the Realm has to take care of averting the evil. Never have the advocates of real autonomy and of real self-government conceded anything about the calling and authority of the State government to take measures that were contrary to it, and in what...

[284]

THE MUNICIPALITIES. The common warding off of danger required foresight. The struggle was about the starting point. In mechanical construction, one makes parts and puts them together at will; in organic life, the parts emerge from the life center and bring with them their own law of life. In conformity with this requirement of the life constitution, the Municipality, the Province, and the Realm itself must also provide for the performance of their own task. All that can remain with the Municipality without danger must be assigned to it, and under a control that does not intervene further than is indispensable for the preservation of the whole. What does not belong to its sphere, but concerns the common household of an entire Province, must remain with the provincial administration. And so also must the Realm itself provide for the fulfillment of its own task, and for this, if necessary, appoint its own officials, its own district authorities, its own commissions, and its own councils. This is already the case with the army and navy, with the notariat, with university education, with school inspection, with customs, with the greater part of the tax system, with the Health Council, and so much more. For all this, the Realm has constantly called into being its own services, its own official circles, its own bureaus, without this having posed any significant difficulty.

§23. Ready Outlet. There is therefore no reason to think that the burden of Realm work, which now weighs on the Mayor, the Aldermen, the Secretary, and partly even on the Council, could not very well be shifted from the Municipal organization to be organized independently. Where in foreign countries the Mayors are appointed by the Council itself, one also often finds such district organizations or district authorities, appointed and paid by the Realm, to execute and perform what the Realm’s general service requires. That much of this was previously shifted to the Municipal administrations is explained by the fact that this involvement was not much of a matter at the time, and could therefore find local provision without the local administration losing its independent character or being denaturalized into a Realm institution. If this unnatural task is now taken away from the Municipal administrations, and Realm organizations are appointed for this task, then, with a good division of the Municipalities into classes and types, the right of appointment of the citizen father could very well be left to the Council itself, even in the smallest village. And if one were to object that the appointment as such should come from the Crown, one could do with this what has been done with regard to the appointment of the Chairman of the Second Chamber, and bind the appointment to a nomination of three by the Council. The provision that this nomination should preferably focus on inhabitants of the place or on Council members could, in the event of a false appearance lying in this, be safeguarded in the sense that, if placing on the nomination candidates from elsewhere, reasons would be given for the data that had led to a deviation from the rule. Only this must be kept in mind, that the variis modis benefit also applies here. What applies to a village municipality of 2 to 3,000 souls need by no means be valid as a rule for a city whose population exceeds one hundred thousand. Uniformity was also here the curse that did too much harm. It is now in fact, with only very rare exceptions, all appointed from the outside, completely strange to the village, mayors who have become, and this has more than broken the freedom and the own development in the countryside. Autonomous self-government makes the people strong; the employment of the Municipalities by the Realm, in order to flatten every own fold of life in uniformity, slows down the pulse of life.

Analytical Summary: The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,
[285]

§24. Federative Association. Exactly the same principle governs, as a starting point, the answer to the question that arises when there is talk of the combination or merger of two or more Municipalities. The combination can have the entire household of the Municipality, or only a part of it, in view. Especially partial combination of action can be recommended, sometimes even necessary; provided that it then comes about federatively. Whoever binds himself federatively,

Analytical Summary: Our People.
[286]

The municipalities rightly maintain their own authority. Many municipalities are too small to establish their own gas plant or their own electricity works. If a certain number of small municipalities located close to each other can do this, why would they not proceed to federated cooperation? The same can apply to the effort to establish a tramway. In some regions, the police could even take on such a character, if one could appoint a group of men. Even in the school system, it may be necessary to combine forces. In a region where a Christian school cannot subsist on the number of students from a single village, we are also accustomed to combining. Why then would the same path of cooperation not be taken for a Public School that could thrive well in several neighboring villages? Against the danger of fire, it is often better to watch over a group of three or four adjacent villages banding together, than when each small village tries to provide for its own needs. In short, there are many interests that can be much better served by federated cooperation in a certain number of villages, than when each village provides for its own needs. The appointment of one mayor as the father of the citizens in two adjacent villages lies outside this, but can nevertheless be highly recommended in the interest of two or even three small municipalities. The remuneration of the mayor in a very small municipality may be too low to attract a man of knowledge and talent. If now two or if necessary three small municipalities unite, the remuneration of these two or three municipalities can attract someone who would otherwise not have applied; while as a rule it can be said that the task of helping to govern two or even three of these small villages does not exceed the available strength of a capable man. Even a closer combination of two or three of these smaller, adjacent municipalities is not an overriding objection, provided their nature is of the same kind. It would be harmful, for example, for such a combination to work between a village by the sea and an agricultural village behind it, as is the case with Katwijk and Noordwijk. The interests of a village by the sea and an agricultural village located behind it often diverge to such an extent that it is even inadvisable to place the same person as mayor over both.

[287]

NO ANNEXATION. 275 On the other hand, federal cooperation and combinations can be recommended from many points of view.

§25. No Annexation. It is quite the opposite with the merging of smaller municipalities into a larger city municipality, as has been applied, for example, in Rotterdam. It is also not a healthy sign that new plans for such mergers are constantly coming up. One knows the history of Delft, and one also knows what is still threatening in Amsterdam. In this respect, our country makes an unfavorable exception to what is usually the case elsewhere. One knows how in London and also in Brussels the case occurred that the city in population gradually expanded so that all distance between the city and many of its surrounding villages disappeared, and the street and row of houses of the village soon joined so completely to the street and row of houses of the city that the stranger walking through could not perceive the least separation, division or even distinction. Yet one has not gone so far there as to simply merge these surrounding villages into the large city. On the contrary, one has often been concerned to allow each village to maintain its own administration and household, and thus to maintain a multiplicity of formations and a variety of living conditions that upheld not only the historical character of the past, but also the social diversity between the center and the periphery. One then has a city and suburbs, a ville and faubourgs, what we would call: a city with its suburbs, in which a peculiar kind of life then maintains itself. But this too is found too troublesome in our leading circles. There one loves simplicity, and therefore enforces a simplicity that results in nothing but uniformity. That violence is even committed here does not matter. In those suburbs, life used to be cheaper and the rent lower. But when now the guillotine comes that simply cuts off the independent municipal existence of such a village, then that rent goes up, the city life with its high prices penetrates into the suburb, and there is again a circle with a life of its own and of a peculiar character, melted into the all-centralizing unity. From the Anti-Revolutionary side, one has therefore always pointed to London and Brussels to resist this revolutionary method of merging, as long as possible. The municipality has a pull of equality with the family. Not a few strive to deprive the family too of its autonomy and self-government, or at least to restrict both; which then ultimately results in what a wise man like Plato already aimed for of old, to melt the whole family into the State. But although it is perfectly true that the autonomy and self-government of the family also shows a very considerable dark side, and not infrequently calls for correction, yet one feels how much more vigorous a people-state is that still shows an unbroken family life, even if it is not free from defects, than when the family life dissolves into state educational institutions. And just the same applies here. The autonomy and self-government of the municipality can also show very serious defects, but yet a vigorous municipal life, even if it has its flaws, promises for the future of country and people a far higher bloom than the transformation of the historical municipalities into a new-fashioned state creature that threatens to become nothing but a state agency.

Analytical Summary: Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit
[288]

276 CIVIL AND ECCLESIASTICAL LIFE. Certainly the municipality must also conform to the “general interest,” but precisely this “general interest” suffers irreparable damage if it weakens the constituted municipality. Chapter X CIVIL AND ECCLESIASTICAL LIFE.

§1. Formerly material without antithesis. Now let us turn to the more particular questions for which the Anti-Revolutionary program has to provide an answer.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[289]

MATERIALITY WITHOUT ANTITHESIS. The organization of municipal life places us, first and foremost, in attention to what it has made sacred. Behind us lies a historical past, in which for no small part of our land, the ecclesiastical and civic life of the Municipality were almost interwoven, so much so that the church buildings were often under the care of the civil administration. As long as no more than one religion flourished in the Municipality, no one felt anything for sharply separating the material interests of Church and Municipality. Hence the not infrequently quite free-spoken authority that the “great lords” in the countryside and the city councils in the cities also exercised in ecclesiastical matters, even in the choice of the pastor. The spiritual interest was almost everywhere left to the authorities in the church, but the care for the external was often taken over by the lord of the manor or the city council. However, the Reformation brought about a principled change in this mutual relationship. For it actually broke the unity of the Church community. It is true that not infrequently in a not too large village, the joint inhabitants of that village still belonged to the old church, or went jointly to the Reformed or Lutheran or Anabaptist associations, but this did not take away the fact that, in the general popular conception, the unity of the Church was split. One then saved oneself by declaring one’s own church to be the only true one and condemning everything outside of it as heretical, but this did not prevent a very significant modification from taking place in the world of thought. However strictly one rejected the Greek and Roman Church as deviant phenomena in the ecclesiastical field, one could not maintain in Reformed Netherlands that in lands that had remained entirely Greek Orthodox or also Roman Catholic, no trace of the Christian essence would be discovered. The idea that a split had come into the Christian Church forced itself irresistibly upon one. And although one then still twisted and turned in all kinds of ways, on the one hand to make one’s own church pass for the only true one, and yet not to deny the presence of Christ’s Church in lands of divergent Confessions, it did not flow smoothly. It took a long time before this new awareness had penetrated sufficiently generally among us; but when it had finally come so far, it also naturally led to the separation lines between the various Christian denominations being drawn more sharply.

[290]

CIVIL AND ECCLESIASTICAL LIFE. The distinction between the ecclesiastical and civil organization is becoming sharper both in the city and in the village.

§2. The separation has been carried through. Now this principle of separation may be considered to have been sufficiently carried through to regulate the mutual relationship. This relationship now ought to be such that in every Congregation the ecclesiastical leaders and members, in conformity with Romans XIII:1–5, show all respect and reverence to the civil administration, and lighten its task by admonishing on the catechism and in preaching, and also through house visits and ecclesiastical discipline, to turn away all revolutionary tendencies, to exhort to obedience to the civil administration, and to oppose all immorality and unrighteous transgressions. But here, on the other hand, it is required that the civil administration show respect to the church in its servants, in its poor relief, and in its entire appearance. The civil administration ought to do this on a footing of equality, and can itself make no distinction between churches. The civil administration must watch over the maintenance of order during divine service, and suppress during the services all noisy behavior that would disturb the quiet of the service. In a village it is desirable that the mayor instruct the constable to greet the pastors of the Church when passing on the public street. In short, there should be a hand-in-hand going on such a footing that both the ecclesiastical and the civil administration are completely independent the one over against the other, but that nevertheless all mutual interference is excluded, and, on the contrary, a courteous and helpful relationship is regulated. The inhabitants must receive no other impression than that the powers of Church and civil Commune, each for the exercise of its own duty, indeed go their own way, but yet are more intent on mutual support than on a combative relationship. The former fixed custom that mayors and aldermen attended the religious services worked very certainly in this direction; but as there are now not infrequently mayors who have broken with all religion, this may have no influence on the mutual official relationship. The separation of State and Church, just by this alone, must become ever more absolute, but just at the final separation of the two domains the official contact can be all the more correct. Of itself, then, the official preference for the one Church above the other will fall away. Of a so-called “People’s Church,” which would still bear more or less an official character, there may no longer be the slightest mention in the administration of the Commune. It is the respect for freedom of conscience that must hold back the civil administration from granting any preference in the civil Commune. Even the synodical character that comes to expression on the part of Israelites must be treated by the civil administration with equal courtesy. Concerning the prohibition to consecrate a church marriage, if the proof of a concluded marriage has not been submitted, I have already dealt with this elsewhere. Here it need only be pointed out that it is desirable that the civil administration make available to the ecclesiastical administration, if requested, a copy of the population registers, if necessary against reimbursement of costs. Especially in our large cities, and with the heavy migration that is customary there, it is otherwise almost impossible for the church administration to exercise supervision; and if anyone, then it is certainly the local authority that has an interest in this ecclesiastical supervision.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[291]

THE OBSERVANCE OF SUNDAY. 279 Chapter XI THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN THE CIVIL ADMINISTRATION

§1. The Observance of Sunday. What comes up for consideration first under this heading is the observance of the weekly day of rest, or, if one will, the Sunday question. The Sunday...

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[292]

280 THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL ELEMENT IN CIVIL GOVERNMENT.

its observance in its specific form—that is, in the keeping of a day of rest at the beginning of the week—is an ecclesiastical-Christian institution, aimed at the remembrance of the Resurrection of Christ from the dead. But behind this specifically Christian form, which sets apart the first of the seven days, lies the concept of the weekly rest day as such, the choice of the day remaining free in this regard. For Israel, it was instituted as a day at the close of the week. Islam chose Friday for it. And while in ancient Egypt, and later also during the days of the French Revolution, people departed from the septenary cycle and preferred the observance of the tenth day, the general idea that lay at the foundation of this entire institution was and remained, nonetheless, that a rhythmic cadence pulses throughout all our human life; that working constantly without rest is contrary to the disposition of our nature; and that, just as the rest of the night proved necessary after the day, so too a fixed break in an overly large group of days was given by the very nature of our human constitution. In my “Treatise on the Sabbath,” and in a Paper for the Congress in San Francisco, anno 1915, this has been further elucidated by me. (See Sunday the World’s rest day, published for the New-York Sabbath-Committee, 31 Biblehouse, p. 47—63). I shall therefore not elaborate further upon it here, and suffice it to point out that, according to Genesis 2:2, even before there was any mention of Israel, the seventh day was designated as the normal day of rest for all that is called man, for our entire human race. The institution of one of the seven days as a rest day is thus not a specifically Christian, but a generally human institution, which falls not under Special Grace, but under Common Grace. From this now flows the consequence that the civil government, even in local cities or villages, must set apart one of the seven days as a rest day—not one of ten, but one of the seven, because seven here is the sacred number, the conjunction of 3+4, that is, of the rhythm in God and of human life, which is always indicated by the number 4, corresponding to the four winds. Even if one could conceive of a village where there was no longer a single Church or a single Synagogue, and where no one adhered to any religious confession whatsoever, still, one of the seven days would have to be established as a rest day. And since, due to our past, and likewise due to the still quite strong Christian population, the choice for this is very decidedly the

[293]

THE GOVERNMENT CANNOT DO NOTHING.281 Since Sunday has been designated, it is obvious that the civil administration in the Netherlands must also particularly honor Sunday and bring that Sunday to honor in the public life in every way as a day of rest. The unfortunate custom that, for example, in France and Belgium gained currency to hold political elections on Sunday, also happily remained behind with us, and will certainly never creep in with us. Yet the negative is not sufficient here, and it may be demanded of the civil administration that it itself honor the day of rest and make its enjoyment possible for others, at least as far as this depends on the Government.

§2. The Government cannot do nothing. Of course, there can be no question that the Municipal Government on Sunday would abstain from any manifestation of life on its part. The police must watch, the fire brigade must be ready to go out, the health service in the hospitals must continue, the gas factory must give light in the evenings and at night, the water supply may not withhold its thirst-quenching moisture from the population; and there are many such services which, though on a reduced scale, yet must perform their duty where necessary. This is so in the public sphere, just as it is in family life. Suspend as much work as possible on Sunday, but yet there remains the one requirement that dark light can be kindled; that heating against frost be provided; that as the hour of the midday meal approaches, food be ready; that the bed be spread for the night; that the clothing be put on and taken off; that the child in the cradle also receive the necessary care; and so much more. Yet Sabbatarianism, first among the Pharisees, later in certain Christian circles, especially under English-Scottish legalism, has bargained away all kinds of things here, and even to neglect the spreading of the night bed, but no good Calvinist allows himself to be misled by such legal mannerisms. The Apostle Paul has warned us against it in every way. Christ Himself has brought this Jewish Sabbatarianism back within its proper limits, and Voetius, our great teacher in this field, has most seriously warned our Dutch Calvinists against it.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[294]

282 THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN CIVIL ADMINISTRATION. Of course, no one denies that there are services that the Government cannot let rest on Sunday. To withdraw on Sunday, as if it were not and did not exist, the Government cannot and may not. The difference between the more and less serious part of the population actually only comes to the fore where it is a matter of what can remain closed or at least can be restricted. Not all shops can remain completely closed on Sunday. Just think of the pharmacy. Yet it is desirable that the closing of shops on Sunday be regulated, at least for all sales of what can just as well be purchased on another day; especially also for the tobacco shops. The same applies to payments. A decent shopkeeper lowers the curtains of his display cases on Sunday. Only then does the city and village acquire that quiet, peaceful appearance that elevates the mind.

§3. Public entertainments. The judgment is no different about public entertainments, though with a certain distinction. If the local Government itself has organized public entertainments, for example by opening a City Theater, then there can be no question that these should play on Sunday. Allowing such games by private enterprises has a somewhat different character in itself and is not to be prohibited in municipalities with a largely worldly population, as otherwise they might fall into even more outrageous excesses. Yet always so, that the Mayor and Aldermen keep a sharp eye on the character of the game, and that a very sensitive blow be given to the doors through which immoral entertainments, for which the stimulation of sensual passions is then the lure, enter. In fact, it currently fails in all control. No game or performance can be so common that permission is not granted for it; and what is most saddening, the Press sometimes even lends a hand to this. Not that it sees all excesses through the fingers. Repeatedly even the liberal Press has sharply criticized the scandalousness of many a theatrical performance, but the effect of this is alas all too often that this very criticism makes the next appearance of the public even greater. Only the local Government can...

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[295]

TO DERIVE REGULATION FROM Common Grace. 283 The government could have stopped this here, but they did not. And if they dare to issue a single prohibition, you will read in the morning paper what infringement of rights is seen in this. Although we cannot impose our own puritanical austerity on others through the government, it is clear from everything that our current municipal laws have failed to set the proper limits here, and that the government has practically disregarded even what our law presented as a means to curb the evil; so that in the end our entire Sunday law has literally become a mockery and a laughing stock.

§4. To Derive Regulation from Common Grace. As it now lies, it cannot remain so. A new, better, more decisive regulation must come. And this all the more so as the idea is gradually gaining ground to regulate these important matters not separately, but in the Labor Legislation, as has already been tried twice in bills to that effect. The aim was to rule over Sunday labor by establishing such legal provisions; where this was not possible, to ensure rest on weekdays; and, as far as necessary, to connect a higher regulation of wages to unavoidable Sunday labor. Thus it increasingly took on the appearance as if the goal of the whole arrangement was not the reverence for the Divine Being, but merely the assurance of rest for the laborer. With this, of course, it cannot remain, and therefore a completely new, separate regulation of the whole question must be set in motion. For in the Sabbath commandment, too, the “doing no work” stands in the foreground, and thus the resting from labor on the Lord’s Day, but by no means solely with a view to exemption from labor, but much more so that through the refraining from labor the mind of man would of itself be directed to the rendering of reverence to God’s holy Name. Yet on our part we must be cautious and ask of the law no more than it can give under the present circumstances. Three different situations are to be distinguished here. There are many villages where, for example, the entire population is still of the old Reformed type; there are other municipalities where almost no trace of Calvinism remains, and everything is either modern or Roman Catholic; and there are in the third place city communities where a very mixed complex of inhabitants presents itself. Now we on our part may not impose our Calvinistic ideas about the Sabbath on that part of the population that lives according to entirely different principles; and furthermore, it must not be allowed that the Scottish legal ideas, which have penetrated here in complete contradiction to our old conviction, should be set as the rule for us. In a country of such diverse convictions, we can only demand what flows from Common Grace. What one demands more remains a dead letter, merely in the letter of the law. Hence the idea deserves recommendation to provide opportunity on our Sundays for the proclamation of sacred poetry and sacred music in halls specially arranged for this purpose. Especially where the Reformed churches themselves treat psalm singing all too soberly, opportunity must also be given in another way for sacred poetry and music to make themselves heard. Especially for those who are still too strange in the holy domain to sanctify the Sabbath for themselves, such can contribute in every way to the sanctification of the Lord’s Day. §5. Prayer in the City Council. A third matter that must be discussed here is Prayer in the City Council. Already in the First Part it has been pointed out that elsewhere even the meetings of the Provincial States and of Parliament are still regularly opened with Prayer, while with us at this higher level no more thought is given to Prayer itself. Fortunately, the City Councils still make quite an exception to this. In the City Councils, Prayer has by no means been finally abolished. In these lower political assemblies, Prayer has still generally prevailed, not everywhere, but then still in the majority; and what deserves special appreciation, not only in the rural municipalities of a completely orthodox type, but even still in the capital. Now it is of course a glaring inconsistency that one would invoke God’s help and assistance in the lower political assemblies, but not at all in the higher ones. For if Prayer has a right to a place in the City Council, it has an even greater right in the Provincial and National Assemblies. And if one considers the great importance that the Calvinistic principle of the sovereignty of God over the state has for the whole of our political life, then it is simply incomprehensible that one would not want to give expression to this principle in the highest political assemblies through the solemn invocation of God’s name. It is therefore to be hoped that this inconsistency will soon be remedied, and that the opening of the meetings of the Provincial States and of Parliament with Prayer will again become a fixed custom.

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.
[296]

284 THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN CIVIL GOVERNMENT. villages, for example, where the entire population is still of the old Reformed type; there are other municipalities where almost no trace of Calvinism remains, and everything is either modern or Roman Catholic; and there are in the third place city communities where a very mixed complex of inhabitants presents itself. Now we on our part may not impose our Calvinistic ideas about the Sabbath on that part of the population that lives according to entirely different principles; and furthermore, it must not be allowed that the Scottish legal ideas, which have penetrated here in complete contradiction to our old conviction, should be set as the rule for us. In a country of such diverse convictions, we can only demand what flows from Common Grace. What one demands more remains a dead letter, merely in the letter of the law. Hence the idea deserves recommendation to provide opportunity on our Sundays for the proclamation of sacred poetry and sacred music in halls specially arranged for this purpose. Especially where the Reformed churches themselves treat psalm singing all too soberly, opportunity must also be given in another way for sacred poetry and music to make themselves heard. Especially for those who are still too strange in the holy domain to sanctify the Sabbath for themselves, such can contribute in every way to the sanctification of the Lord’s Day.

[297]

PRAYER IN THE MUNICIPAL COUNCIL However, this does not at all imply that prayer should therefore be allowed to fall away in the Municipal Councils. On the contrary, we should be grateful that prayer has still largely maintained its place in these Councils, and we should strive much more to restore it to honor even higher up. But then the standpoint we take in these matters must also be chosen with discretion. We should not ask how we can impose by force what goes against the convictions of many, or at least does not answer to them; but quite differently, how the Municipal Council’s prayer can be arranged to ensure the widest possible application; and this is only achieved by taking one’s stand on the common inheritance of Common Grace. For then nothing else comes into play than faith in God’s providence, in His providential arrangement, and in the hiding or the coming of His divine blessing. A prayer that remains within this providential circle can still be joined in by almost everyone. The Church membership already makes no dispute about this, since faith in God’s providential order is the common good of all Churches. Even the Jew does not lag behind in this. And even among those who held aloof and did not join any Church, one finds not infrequently persons who have already completely renounced all faith in God’s omnipotence and providence. There are undoubtedly declared atheists who no longer know any prayer in their personal life, but these are still so few in number that one need only reckon with such exceptions. Objection could only arise to the extent that the Mayor as Chairman of the Municipal Council would be a declared atheist. Then, of course, it could not be assigned to him personally to say the prayer. But this would by no means imply that in such a Council prayer should be omitted altogether. The one who reads the prayer is not the main thing here. The secretary could even be entrusted with this, or the reading of the prayer could be assigned to one of the Council members, for example the oldest member. We therefore speak emphatically of reading. For we will never go so far as to remove prayer from the Municipal Council.

[298]

THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN CIVIL ADMINISTRATION.

To introduce a so-called “free prayer” would be at odds with all good order. The Municipal Council itself must pre-determine the formative words of the prayer. This approved form of the prayer must be affixed to a neatly finished plaque, and printed or written on it in such clear and large letters that the reading can proceed as a matter of course. We also understand well that such a Municipal Council prayer must be brief. The Lord’s Prayer remains the example here as well. How brief is this prayer of the Lord, and what richness is contained in it! Only so much modality must be allowed, that for example, on the commemoration of the Queen’s birthday, a prayer for that is inserted. And so it could also be done at the inauguration of a new mayor, or at the outbreak of an epidemic in the Municipality. But even this should not be left to the free invention of the reader. Between two brackets, such clauses can be very well incorporated into the text itself, and they are then only used on the days when they apply.

§6. A Christian Character?

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.

Remains, with regard to this subject, the more particular question to be answered, to what extent the Municipal Council prayer also lends itself to imparting a more specifically Christian character. And then it immediately stands out that this could never pass as a general rule. If the members of the Council, as far as religion is concerned, form a mixed company, with Reformed, Roman Catholics, Mennonites, Jews and Theosophists sitting in it, then the prayer may not be one in which only some members find the more particular expression of their faith conviction. Prayer is a sacred matter, and if one allows prayer in an assembly where the persons do not come together according to their own will, but by virtue of their official vocation, then one may not proceed in the prayer in a way that does not sanctify them, but rather hinders and repels them. It is so, especially in the cities, that it has gradually become the custom that those who could no longer pray along, came a little later, or hid themselves for a moment in the corridor; and we admit, where this applies to a very single member of the Council, it would already be a serious objection.

[299]

A CHRISTIAN CHARACTER? 287

It could be so. But a rule can never be derived from this. Hence our adherence to the convention that the Municipal Council prayer must be a prayer within the circle of Common Grace, and may not then, except by exception, assume a more special character. Suppose, for example, that in Amsterdam the Jewish quarter had been isolated and made into a separate circle within the Municipality, just as this has very well been done on Java, where a civil Municipality was formed that consisted almost exclusively of sons of Islam. In that case, it would not have raised the slightest objection if, in the Council prayer for this exclusively Jewish or Javanese Municipal Council, an Israelite tint had been given to the prayer. But if it became apparent that such a Jewish or Javanese Municipal Council did not in fact contain within itself any non-Jews or non-Mohammedans, then this would not be permissible. And so the matter also stands with us. If there is anywhere a Municipal Council in the countryside that consists entirely of resolute confessors of our three Forms of Unity, then there is no reason whatsoever why the Council prayer of such a Municipality should not also plead on the cross of Christ. Only one must not forget that even in the “Our Father” the name of the Mediator is not mentioned. But this does not take away the fact that in a Municipal Council consisting exclusively of the believing members of one and the same Christian Church, there would be no overriding objection to giving the Municipal Council prayer a Christian character. Only with the choice of other kinds of Municipal Council members, who could enter after the lapse of time, would one have to return to the standpoint of Common Grace. Thus, for the Municipal Council prayer, the plea on the Mediator remains always accessory; something that need not disturb us, since the Christian Church also in its church building by no means sends up prayers only to obtain the fulfillment of its spiritual needs, but truly also prays for King and Fatherland. The question would not even be superfluous whether the ecclesiastical prayer in many Municipalities has not long since become too one-sidedly spiritual, and whether, on the example of our Formulary Prayers, which always commend “all the needs of Christendom” also with regard to civil life to God, more regularly in the Church the needs of ordinary, non-ecclesiastical life should not also be brought before God.

[300]

THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN CIVIL ADMINISTRATION.

§7. The Demon of Strong Drink. No less important for the flourishing of municipal life is the stance that the City Council takes in the moral realm, particularly with regard to drunkenness and prostitution. It is well known that—not least from France—highly questionable views on this matter have also increasingly penetrated our borders, and that as a result, drunkenness has systematically spread, and the vice of houses of ill repute has continued to proliferate. This twofold evil has assumed such alarming proportions that not only from the Christian, but also from the modern side, a resistance against these shameful abuses has begun to organize itself. As for drunkenness, the pressure on this came to us primarily from America and partly from England; and as for prostitution, it must be gratefully acknowledged that it was not least from Switzerland that a purer impulse came to stimulate us, in which the honorable Dr. H. Pierson had the honor of having taken an initiative that was crowned with rare speed and decisive success. Even in the middle of the previous century, the “let-it-be” system was still too much encouraged from the liberal side. Whether someone wanted to poison himself with alcoholic beverages was his own business, and the use of strong drink was not unprofitable for the treasury. It is precisely the same struggle against the jenever here at home that was waged against opium in Java. Something in which, alas, the evil came that under the influence of the wealthy gentlemen, the use of cognac and other spirituous liquors had also found too great an entry both at home and in the clubs, and the abuse of too much wine, especially in the north of our country, had even crept into church councils. It can therefore not be praised enough that the struggle against this national evil has been taken up with vigorous earnestness in the second half of the nineteenth century, and that, thank God, the use of alcoholic beverages per person and per liter has also declined to such an extent that the old people of today often no longer recognize the social condition. Whether the teetotalers pointed the most correct path here, I have always doubted; but although this has always kept me from personally joining any absolute Prohibition associations, I am nevertheless pleased to see that the struggle against this evil has been taken up with such determination.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[301]

THE DEMON OF STRONG DRINK - IMMORALITY

It is recognized that here too the evil can assume such proportions that ultimately complete abstinence will prove to be the strongest, if not the only, means to curb its abuse. The old licenses still stand in our way, and it remains a delicate question how, without too much interference in private rights, we shall be delivered from this evil; but we are progressing, and the hope may be cherished that the result achieved in Norway will eventually also be obtained in this country.

§8. Immorality

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.

More difficult, because more secret in nature, is the struggle against brutal immorality; for here one has to contend with immoral notions which, instead of gradually disappearing, are rather gaining ground. Immorality is naturally connected with the question of marriage, and one knows how many families in almost all circles are, where the bond of marriage is becoming ever looser. This begins with the idea that all married people must be left free, so that, if it seems good to them both, they may again dissolve the marriage they have entered into, and each go their own way. Facilitating divorce also lies entirely in the line of Liberalism, and behind the Liberals appear even freer groups, who would prefer to abolish all marriage, in order to introduce the so-called “free life.” Those who want to steer society in that direction, and who constantly appeal to Plato’s ideas on the relationship between parents and children, can of course not judge otherwise, or the still very strict requirements of the marriage bond stand in the way of the desired development of their culture. Then, too, the doctors come at the wrong time and tell them that the single life of persons of 20 and more years is in itself contrary to the demands of health. As far as young girls are concerned, one does not yet dare to assert this so positively, but many doctors consider it a foregone conclusion that sexual intercourse of such an age is indispensable for most of them. This, so they plead, makes the existence of houses where the young man can find what he seeks, absolutely necessary. The existence of such houses of immorality should therefore not be opposed, but rather encouraged. And this, they then assured us, implied the obligation for the Government to see to the danger that such houses bring with them for the infection of a syphilitic nature. Naturally, this worked immorality terribly into the hand. For at least half a century we have then also lived through, in which the supervision of Prostitution, which had crept in from France, promoted the evil, especially among the married of the higher social position. The police were often on very good terms with the houses of immorality, and even among the more respectable bourgeoisie one gave in to the principle that the Government lacked the right to close such houses, nay, that it even stood under the moral obligation to regulate Prostitution, and to safeguard those who made use of it against the danger of infection. We cannot be grateful enough that this diabolical conception has now been radically broken with. From the Heldring Institutions in Zetten the triumph of the opposite conviction has gone forth on this point. Almost everywhere the inspection has now been abolished, and the exploitation of immorality in houses specially opened for that purpose is strictly forbidden. If there is one reformer of public morals who may rejoice in a rapid and widespread success, it is certainly Dr. H. Pierson. He did not stand alone. Abroad, too, similar efforts emerged with vigor. But as far as our country is concerned, no one can be given the palm of honor more than Dr. H. Pierson. He was the pioneer in this whole action, the inspiring leader.

§9. The Fairs

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.

Partly under this heading of Municipal Care also belongs the question of whether or not to hold the Fairs. Two things must be distinguished in the so-called Fair: first the public festivities, and second the coming into contact with the products of less common industry. As for the first, it may be taken as an established fact that in the multitude a certain need periodically arises to be free for once and to give themselves over to certain festive moods;

[302]

290 THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN CIVIL GOVERNMENT.

and it would be a mistake to want to suppress this completely. The question is only how to satisfy this need in a healthy way. The second point, however, is more difficult. The Fair, as a place of contact with the products of less common industry, has undoubtedly lost much of its former importance, now that the means of communication have become so much easier. And yet, even in our time, it still fulfills a certain function, especially for the more remote parts of the country. The question is therefore whether the disadvantages outweigh the advantages, and whether the municipality would not do better to abolish the Fair altogether. Here, too, the principle must be that the municipality should not interfere more than is strictly necessary in the life of the citizens. If the Fair can be maintained in a healthy way, without serious disadvantages, then it should be maintained; but if the disadvantages clearly outweigh the advantages, then it should be abolished. And here, too, the municipality must be guided by the religious and moral interests of the citizens.

[303]

FAIRS - THE ABUSE.

Our life is now circulating in certain periodic cycles. The division of weeks and years is still felt everywhere, and even among Israel the change of months was also brought under a certain festive appearance. The celebration of birthdays is also closely connected with this innate need of ours. And although Calvin in his personal life outlook was still so strict and rigid, he too worked to have public stage performances even in Geneva. The opportunity to have a free day for common relaxation has also established itself in all kinds of ways. This matter has been connected with the change of seasons, with the harvest in the field, with the great cattle markets and horse markets, with an annual influx of merchants offering fine goods for sale, and much more. Later, such festivities have been tied to national memorial days and ecclesiastical remembrances. But however it went, to whatever people one belonged, public life everywhere required at least two or three days for relaxation. Even in the Bank Holidays in London this came out. The ordinary bonds of life encompass our whole consciousness so strongly that the urge to occasionally loosen this grip is not to be escaped. Even in the second ecclesiastical feast days of Christmas, Easter and Pentecost, the same need spoke. Only on such days could one visit one’s family living elsewhere; could one open up many visited gatherings for all classes of society. And whatever one does, the need for such free days will continue to assert itself. This need speaks in our human nature.

§10. The Abuse.

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.

The sad thing is now, however, that the liberation from the daily task, in which one rejoices on such feast days, gives occasion in so many ways to the abuse of strong drinks, alas, to even worse sensual sins.
