Not everyone participates in this. Only those who give themselves over to it, make themselves by their noisiness so easily masters of the public arena and spoil the festive character. To counteract this is then also our duty. The Church can cooperate in this, the School can cooperate in this, the Press can do its part, and noble display in public life can also make a very important contribution. But if we do not want to place ourselves outside of life, then on our part we must not aim at the complete suppression of such popular festivities. This does not succeed anyway, and the peculiarity of our human coexistence has a right to speak here. If one could now set aside two, three other days for this, among which especially the birthday of the Bearer of the Crown, this would deserve applause; but to grind down the public festivities out of life, you may not. It is quite different with the second motive that caused our Fairs, namely the display of the less common products of industry. This made sense in earlier times, when communication between countries was still so difficult and the display of all such goods was still so unusual. There was then a group of such industrialists who at certain times set up their wooden tents in this or that place, and to this group a group of jugglers, performers and amusement exhibitors often joined. This could not be otherwise at the time, and not without a certain greediness the pleasure-seeking public joined this industrial movement to make what was called “fun” in town and village for a few days. Now the necessity for this has lapsed. The shop window now offers everything, much more even than the fair display used to offer. And this is not only so in the smaller villages, but from those villages one now constantly goes to the city and finds there everything the eye desires. The reason for existence of the Fair seems to have lapsed thereby. But precisely for that reason it is all the more a requirement that the well-ordered public festivity come more into honor among us. This is desirable in a national sense for the whole country, and locally in every Municipality. There are always memories to which one can connect. On such memorial days the Church can also have its say. Music and other enjoyments of art can assert themselves. Even the comical need not be excluded here. We are not prude old maids, and even among the Calvinistic people public festivity has always been honored as a rejuvenation of the pulse of life.

[304]

Page 305—-

PUBLIC POOR RELIEF

§11. Public Poor Relief. The issue of public poor relief is much more complex. In Christian lands, the most desirable state of affairs is that the civil municipality can entrust the care of the poor entirely to those powers who do what they do for the poor out of the impulse of love. First and foremost, the Church comes into consideration here, with its Diaconate so rich in potential. And alongside this Diaconate, private charity should also step forward, under the same impulse, whether it takes on a personal character or comes to the aid of the poor through associations and societies. In earlier centuries, when Church and State were one, this flowed naturally. One did not feel like two, but like one, even under that duality. The care of the sick would only very rarely fall to the civil administration. An epidemic or a state of war might call forth an extraordinary need in life that demanded care, but in ordinary life it was the Church, it was the monastery, it was the private association that provided for all the needs of the poor. This could happen earlier because the civil municipality declined what wanted to establish itself without proper means of subsistence. However, this state of affairs could only continue as long as every inhabitant was a living member of the Church, and the Church was one. But since then, the state of affairs in this respect has been thoroughly changed. Especially the rather too large Reformed Church excludes a very large part of the population that only belongs to it in name, but neither in faith nor in ecclesiastical service, lives with it and gives to it. Hence, it is often simply impossible for this Church to provide for the full need of its poor, as regards the care of the sick and the distribution of alms. While on the other hand the care of the poor, given the more developed standard of living among the needy, has become so much greater, a state of affairs soon arose in which there could simply be no question of a complete care of the poor by the Church, and private charity, since it was organized on a much too small scale, could not provide adequate help either. This naturally led to the question of who was to be held responsible for the need of the unattended, and for this there was no other answer than to point to the civil municipal administration.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[306]

294 RELIGION AND MORALITY IN CIVIL GOVERNMENT

§12. Municipality, Ecclesiastical and Civil. The civil municipality is after all an institution of community life for a group of citizens who are settled in a particular place, often by virtue of the historical past of their family. The displacement, especially the migration to the big cities, certainly does a lot of damage here; but if one takes the matter as a whole throughout the country, the real poor are as a rule bound to the place where they now find themselves by their family. From this bond a certain responsibility has arisen, which necessitates the provision of assistance to those who would succumb. That this does not extend further than providing that assistance which can keep life going, goes without saying. But precisely the fact that the requirement is limited to this is also the reason why the assistance cannot be lacking. A municipal administration that, with knowledge of the facts, would let a poor family in one of the streets or alleys die of sickness without help, or let them starve in dire need, would be guilty before God and man. Even in the middle of the previous century, this need had not yet become so pressing. Then almost everyone still lived ecclesiastically, even if the faith had already become very weak. Therefore, one still considered the situation such that the Church could be held responsible for all its members, and thus remained responsible for a care that encompassed almost the entire population. Hence Groen van Prinsterer still believed in the possibility of excluding public poor relief. But since then the situation has changed to such an obvious extent that the population is actually divided into two distinct parts. On the one hand the group of believers, for whom the Church is truly the Caretaker of the poor in the name of Jesus, and on the other hand a group that is just as broad, of the unchurched and unbelievers, who, if the civil administration does not provide assistance, would simply let the needy perish in sickness or starve to death. This has naturally led to the fact that the responsibility for the care of the poor has been transferred from the Church to the civil municipality.

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[307]

MUNICIPAL, ECCLESIASTICAL, AND CIVIL - FURTHER REGULATION.

295 The path of philanthropy has opened up all kinds of hospitals to make good medical care possible for the helpless sick, and in the second place, poor relief workers have been appointed by the government to provide for the needs of the destitute. From this, this abuse has arisen, that poor relief now runs a double course for the same needy. Less in the villages, but especially in the large cities, this becomes public. Often, indeed, the needy also receive support from the so-called People’s Church, but a support that is either a mockery, then a removal of their need, and besides, the public poor relief then steps in with the pretension of offering adequate help. This is a very undesirable situation. Our aim must therefore be to lead to this, that what still lives ecclesiastically, also receives full provision either from the Church itself or from its members as a whole, but that in return the civil municipality takes over the remaining ones, if private charity has not preceded it.

§13. Further Regulation. Without further regulation, however, this would lead to palpable injustice. After all, the Christian believers who cared for the needy brothers and sisters in their Church in an adequate manner, would then have to pay double, firstly in the collection for their Church, but then also secondly in the taxes imposed by the civil authorities. As it is now partly the case, and this naturally must not remain so. Nor does it have to. If the civil authorities merely decide to levy a separate tax for poor relief, this threatening injustice can be met in almost complete way. In England, the regulation has been in force since 1601, which was brought into being by Queen Elizabeth, and in this regulation it was determined, among other things, that the Parishes would raise a separate tax for poor relief. The same could be done with us. The funds for civil poor relief would then not be taken from the general treasury, but would be raised as a separate poor tax, approximately in the same way as Minister Treub in 1915 wanted to introduce a series of separate taxes ad hoc for the needs of the elderly, for the Navy, for the Army, etc.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[308]

296 THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN CIVIL ADMINISTRATION.

Now, if this matter was regulated in this way, it would then have to be determined that Churches or Associations that took upon themselves to provide for all the needs of their poor, would receive a complete exemption from this poor tax for their effective members; on the condition, of course, that the poor who belonged to these Churches or Associations would then also receive no help or care whatsoever from the government.

§14. Order and Confusion. If one considers this too much demanded, and would only want to let the exception apply to the fixed allowance, then it would of course be found by calculating a percentage instead of the whole. Interconnected in this way, then the poor relief for those who persevered in the faith would retain a sacred character, and on the other hand, for those who received help from the public poor relief, it would become a police matter. No one would then have to complain, no one would be wronged, and the government would assist both factors. One could then wonder whether the Churches and private Associations would not be able to suffice with a smaller provision for their care, while their members still enjoyed full tax deduction, so that in the end the Church members would profit, and their poor would suffer, but here too, complete and effective measures could be taken by law. Each member of such a Church, or belonging to such an Association, would then have to be given full freedom to leave the poor relief of the Church or Association and go over to the public poor relief, with the necessary consequence, however, that the thereby diminished number of members of that Church or that Association would also proportionately receive less exemption from the poor tax. There is now a boundless confusion in this area, which makes the public poor relief of the government constantly expand; and this should not leave us indifferent, since the striving of the liberal side remains constantly directed towards ultimately making the poor relief entirely a matter of the government.

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”
[309]

DISORDER AND CONFUSION.297 The tendency to draw the Church under the authority of the government is no longer as calm as it was a quarter of a century ago, but keeps arising again from the antithetical principle. Yet the nature of our work does not allow us to go deeper into this matter here. Nor is anyone in a better position to make a comprehensive study of this problem. With this in mind, we briefly refer in a footnote to some important studies that can be of assistance here a). The main thing, however, is that a way be found to keep our Diaconate and our own Calvinistic poor relief on the right track or to put them back on it. In this, we are not free, because we have to reckon with the government’s care. In this paragraph, I believe I have indicated the remedy against the threatening danger. It would therefore be a cause of joy to me if I could put an end to the confusion that now reigns. The short list given below makes no claim to completeness, a) Cf. Dr. A. Emminghaus, Das Armenwesen und die Armengesetzgebung in Europäischen Staaten, Breslau 1870; Dr. P.F. Aschrott, Das Englische Armenwesen, Leipzig 1886; Charles Booth, Pauperism a picture of Old Age, London 1892; H. Smissaart, L’Assistance publique dans les Pays-Bas, The Hague 1900, also published in our language in a more extensive version by Tjeenk Willink, Haarlem; Lic. Ed. Simons, Die älteste Gemeinde-Armenpflege, 1894; Rud. Geller, Die Armengesetzgebung in ihrer gegenwärtigen Gestaltung, 1894; Léonce Larnac, L’Art de donner, 1874; G. Uhlhorn, Christliche Liebestatigkeit, 1884, three volumes; L.M. Moreau Christophe, Du problème de la Misère, Paris 1851, three volumes; G.L. van der Helm, Wetgeving op het Armenwezen, 1890; Mr. J. Everts, De verhouding van Kerk en Staat, mede ten aanzien van de Armenverzorging, Utrecht 1908; Aschrott, Armenpflege in den Vereinigten Staaten von Nord-Amerika, Jena 1889; the article Armenpflege in the Handwörterbuch des Staatswissenschaften, second ed. Jena 1898, first volume; Mr. Dr. Charles Raeymakers, Staat en Armenzorg, 1907, and Mr. Rh. F. Falkenburg, Armenzorg in Nederland, 1895; P. Biesterveld c.s., Het Diaconaat, 1907; A. de la Chevallerie, Armengesetzgebung und Armenpflege, 1854; A. Roell, die Reform der Armenpflege, 1880; Dr. J.J. Esser, De pauperum cura apud Romanos, 1902; Mr. D.P.D. Fabius, Armenzorg, 1912; Dr. J.C. de Moor, De ontwikkeling van het Diaconaat, 1913; Pyttersen, Hintz en H. Muller, Praeadviezen inzake de Armenwet, 1894; Aug. Seiffert, Die Armenpflege der Zukunft, second ed. 1889; Dr. P.A.E. Sillevis Smitt, Diaconie en Overheid, 1902; Dr. H. Visscher, Twee gevaren voor onze Kerkelijke Armenzorg, 1902; and J.L. Zegers, Het Diaconaat, 1892. I thought I might insert this brief list of what I had in my own library. Often even the simple, not the least among the Deacons, aims at a certain study in this field, but is completely unaware of the literature. The list provided here can at least help them on their way.

[310]

RELIGIOUS AND MORAL ELEMENTS IN CIVIL ADMINISTRATION. Idleness. I have only mentioned what my own library possesses. For the other chapters in this second part, I refrain from listing them. Only here do I consider a small exception permissible, and not superfluous.

§15. Expenditures and Revenues. That economy should also be the guiding principle for municipal housekeeping requires no argument. Yet one must not apply this economy with a spirit lacking in discernment. A municipal council that, in order to save on the costs of building a town hall, makes do with a room in an inn, falls short in the honor of its administration. Nor can municipalities be treated uniformly in this respect. For a large city, entirely different rules of housekeeping apply than for a remote rural village. Even among villages, further distinctions must be made between those that receive abundant summer visits from city folk who take up residence there for a few weeks, or those that permanently attract many urban permanent residents, and those villages where a stranger is seen only rarely. Villages with much short or long-term visitation, and especially villages with many resident citizens of urban origin, see their revenues increase remarkably, but they are then also obliged to orient themselves accordingly. Municipal life, especially in the Netherlands, offers endless variation. This has an influence on revenues, but must also have an influence on municipal expenditures. Especially with a view to the strongly differentiating village life, the classification of municipalities into categories is indispensable. According to the nature and type of municipality, what the law imposes on the municipality, and from whom it demands revenue as a requirement, must also differ. Yet it should be firmly established that even in the countryside, the concern of the municipal council must extend somewhat further. Promoting family life is truly Dutch, but this must not lead to neglect of the sense of community and community life. It should therefore be welcomed that gradually better than in earlier times, provision is made for enlightenment, road construction, water supply, and healthcare. Also in the proper regulation

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[311]

EXPENDITURES AND REVENUES - INDIRECT AND DIRECT TAXES. 299 of the police, as far as it is local, one will have to apply oneself, since otherwise one runs the risk that the police, including the rural guard, will pass over to the State; a measure that would again detract from the autonomy of the municipalities. And to mention this as well: Also in the wage standard for those employed by the municipality, a different rule than always bargaining for the cheapest, will henceforth have to apply. But more on this in a following section. Only this may be pointed out regarding expenditures, that now that primary education is a state matter, the municipal council should no longer add extra benefits on its part to these schools. For this would go against the equal rights of the inhabitants. And as for secondary and higher education, the aim should increasingly be to connect secondary education with the province, and higher education at universities with the state alone. Amsterdam has wanted it otherwise and tried it with a municipal university; but it has not lasted long before Amsterdam itself saw the impropriety of it. That finally the salary of the mayor, as long as he remains an official appointed from above, should also be paid from the state treasury, has already been indicated above, and need only be touched upon here.

§16. Indirect and Direct Taxes. As for the means to cover the costs of expenditures, the foremost consideration is that the distinction between direct and indirect revenues must be sharply drawn here. Fortunately, the municipality is to be praised that is rich in land and real estate, and can thus reimburse a considerable part of its expenditures from its own private assets. In our country, however, this municipal domain, if one may call it that, is far from extensive, and even where it occurs, is not of great significance. Almost all municipal revenues must therefore be found by having the inhabitants, or those from outside who want to deal with the municipality, pay either generally as a tax, or specifically for received aid and services rendered. Among these indirect receipts, there was formerly everywhere, and still exists abroad,

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.
[312]

THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN CIVIL ADMINISTRATION. Even now, the import duty is in the foreground. This import duty bears the name of “Octroi” in France and Germany, while with us this name of “Octroi” has by law been given to the exceptional right to sell a discovery made by an invention. The word “Octroi” is derived from the French Chancery style, was derived from the Latin word “auctorizare,” and means that one has obtained permission from the Authority for what one wishes to do. From this original meaning, two things have now been derived; first, the right to introduce certain goods into a Municipality, and secondly, to have the sole right to sell certain goods. The former now leads to a payment to the Municipal receiver of what is called “Octroi” in French, and “Accise” in German. Wrongly, by the law of July 7, 1805, these rights on imports have been abolished with us. All import into whatever Municipality is now entirely free with us. Only between The Hague and Scheveningen is there still the dwelling of the former Municipal toll-collector, which reminds one of the former state of affairs. Abroad, on the other hand, this Octroi right still gives the Municipality a fixed income. Whoever has visited Paris often knows from experience how, on arrival, his suitcase or trunk was examined by the city customs at the stations. And not only in Paris, but also in the other cities of importance, of which France may be proud, such Octroi rights are still levied, and they even form the main part of the municipal income. In England, these incomes mostly fell away, and only in London did the levy remain on the import of certain rights on coal, wine and wheat. In Italy, these Octroi rights are still collected in various ways. In Bavaria and Alsace-Lorraine, these Accises are still in full force. Also elsewhere in Germany they are still in order. Everyone who has passed through the magnificent bridges over the Elbe in Dresden knows this about Saxony’s capital. Counted together, there are still 1392 Municipalities in Germany that levy such tolls and import duties. On foodstuffs, by our law of 1902, the municipal rights have lapsed by the year 1910. In general, these rights do not recommend themselves, at least not for the smaller Municipalities nor for the middle class. Only for the largest cities may the question arise whether the re-introduction of them would not stem the influx of the rural population to the cities.

[313]

DIRECT AND INDIRECT TAXES - CITIES AND VILLAGES

301 at least moderately would. This flooding, however, of the large cities with workers from the villages is neither recommendable for those cities nor for the villages, nor for the departing workers. These workers are accustomed to living in a different environment, and the sad experience has but too well taught how little these workers and their families can withstand the weaker morality in the large cities. Imposing burdens on the means of subsistence could have a stifling effect on the villages, while what is happening elsewhere shows that it need not hinder the flourishing of the cities. Just look at Paris.

§17. Cities and Villages. This, however, concerns only a right that is levied on the enjoyment and convenience of living in a large, well-equipped and fully-provisioned great city. But the indirect levy does not stop there. Taxes are also levied indirectly, as one enjoys the facilities that have been established and maintained by the Municipal Council. Just think of the water supply, the gas service with factory, lamps and personnel, the electricity service, the pumps, the tramways, the cemeteries, the medical service, and so much more. For much of this, one now pays directly, as payment for the goods or services provided. It is even no exception if, for one thing or another, something above the costs and prices is charged, so that the Municipal Council also finds its own income in it. The street toll also falls under this. Whoever in a remote village has to wade through streets and alleys in bad weather, immediately feels what a privilege it is to live in a large city and have a well-maintained street leading to one’s dwelling, and to see how one is constantly busy cleaning and improving that street, or even making it smooth with asphalt. So there is nothing unreasonable in having those who enjoy all these services and amenities pay extra for them. Even the opponents of the impôt unique combat no other idea than that the Municipality should provide gas, electricity, water, tram transport, and what not, free of charge.

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the
[314]

302 THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN CIVIL GOVERNMENT.

to nothing other than useless waste, all kinds of brutality, yes, to boundless confusion. But once having set out on this line to do ever more for the convenience, safety and enjoyment of the inhabitants, one has also gone further in this.

§18. Betterment tax. So, for example, with what is called in England the betterment tax. It is very reasonable to levy a right on the territory that belongs in ownership to the various inhabitants within the Municipality, and on what is built on that territory. The State naturally goes first, as the State covers the entire country. The Municipality, however, has a second right on this territory, because it does for this territory within its own borders what the State cannot do. This can then lead either to a percentage on the property tax, etc., or also to the levying of an independent right. In this, the phenomenon repeatedly occurs that the owner of a piece of territory or of a house sees the value of his property grow of itself and without his contributing anything to it. A house or garden purchased by him for a sum of 20,000 guilders some years ago can, through the high significance that the Municipality has acquired, increase in value to 30,000 guilders. This is what is called in England the betterment. Now who has brought about this increase in value? The owner? In part perhaps through renovations and improvements; but the main gain came to him from the fact that the Municipality grew in significance, and this in turn found its cause in what the Municipal Council did for the Municipality. The author of this has known Brussels before the great Boulevards were laid out that run from the Gare du Nord to the Gare du Midi. It is this great work that the Municipality itself has raised to a considerable extent. Though less strong, yet also in Amsterdam and Rotterdam, in The Hague and in Utrecht, all kinds of things have been laid out by the Municipality, through which these cities have gained in every respect. This now brought about that the territory and the buildings in these cities have risen so remarkably in value. Hence this certainly not unreasonable demand that whoever personally benefits from this should also contribute to the costs.

Analytical Summary: A Happy Turn.
[315]

FIXED MUNICIPAL SERVICES - INCOME TAX.

303 profited, in an extra remission to the Municipality for which he pays his gratitude. That the levy can be indirect in this regard, stems from the fact that the benefit is regulated and privately enjoyed.

§19. Fixed Municipal Services. On the other hand, this is different with fire brigades, police, construction of bridges and crossings, public health, and so much more. This also provides the residents with a very highly valued sense of safety and security, of free movement and help in need, but the profit here is either less specialized or less durable. A fire that breaks out at my place and is extinguished, benefits not only me, but also my neighbors. For the flames could just as easily have spread from my house to their dwelling. Here, it is all about general interests, which at least cannot be specialized, and which are therefore to be counted among the common expenses. Now, if it has not been possible to obtain enough money from the indirect revenues for the entire municipal administration, then one must take recourse to direct taxation, and here commonly begins the painful part of the misery. If we could go along with what we suggested, to at least partially remove the burden of the State government from the Municipal Councils, and transfer it to separate district administrations, they could certainly reduce or eliminate a good part of this indirect taxation. Yet not entirely. The State then compensates the Municipalities for this by granting them surcharges on certain State taxes, which is all very reasonable; but this way of compensation has, what is less praiseworthy, also led to seeking a part of the municipal expenditures in this system of surcharges on State taxes; something that so easily undermines the frugality in household management. §20. Income Tax. Even so, one often does not get there, and then one must well

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.
[316]

304 THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN CIVIL ADMINISTRATION.

proceed to what is the most delicate: namely, to levy a direct tax on the income of the citizens, and this is of course the most sensitive point. This feeling is strongest in small Municipalities, where the population lives frugally, as they lack abundance, but where nevertheless often only a few manufacturers or rentiers of large capital reside. Now, since in the Council of such Municipalities the small bourgeoisie often has the majority, there arises from this the tendency to regulate the income tax in such a way that it is actually those two, three men of fortune who cover the main sum of the costs, and that the rest of the population lives at their expense. The ready means here is the difference in the percentage. With low amounts, the levy is kept low, but it is gradually increased as the income goes up. Only one fear brings moderation in this. The men of fortune can leave the village with their factories and their cars, and move elsewhere, and then the impoverished Municipality is left with the bitter fruit of its excesses. On the other hand, if the means of escape from the methodical plundering do not exist, then one soon sees abuse being made of it, and it is only the Provincial States that can curb the excess. So there is a way out, but a way out that on the other hand again presents the inconvenience that the control from above is tightened and thereby the independence of municipal life is again harmed. Moderation in municipal household management is therefore recommended. The tendency to completely withdraw into domestic family life can be harmful, if it breaks the community feeling for urban coexistence or does not allow it to develop. But one should not go too far here either. A life as we now more often observe it in cafés and restaurants without end in our gentlemen’s streets, does not raise the level of our cities, but lowers it. Leaving the newspaper aside, reading suffers from it, and no less the intimate intercourse among friends. In the café, every conversation is overheard. Once this kind of café life has become a habit, it celebrates its high days especially on Sundays. So the Church suffers, and with the Church the higher spiritual life. The city then gains in pleasant appearance, but the citizenry declines in high expression of life. Domestic life must not become number two, but must remain number one. Something we also need to be mindful of under these circumstances.

[317]

INCOME TAX - IN THE SERVICE OF THE MUNICIPALITY

305 to emphasize the factory, because it can withstand the constant increase in income tax. Indirect taxes must remain the main source of income for the Municipality; direct taxes must not have any other character than a supplementary one here; and only then will it be good, if not first the control of the Provincial Executive, but self-control by the Municipal Administration limits the amount to be levied directly, and not in case of need.

§21. In the Service of the Municipality.

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over

A Municipal Administration cannot fulfill its task, if it cannot count on the help of the serving personnel, whether officials, civil servants or laborers. The latter group is to be distinguished into two categories. There are laborers in the service of the Municipality who derive a public character from their appointment, but there are also others who stand entirely on a par with workers in the service of a private person or factory. The distinction between these two categories is immediately felt, as soon as one compares a coachman in the Fire Brigade with a porter who carries stones for the paving. The main question here is whether the entire corps of personnel in the service of the Municipality can be granted the right to strike. To that question the answer must be that there is no right to strike for the officially employed, and that it is only permissible to allow it for the laborers in the second category. Agents of the police are not entitled to come together one fine day and agree that they will leave their service the next morning in order to stay at home, in order to thus force satisfaction for a demand jointly addressed to the Chief Commissioner. Police service can suddenly become indispensable, and the public interest involved here cannot and may not be sacrificed to the private interest of the Agent. The same applies to the Fire Brigade. If a fire breaks out, the watch entrusted with extinguishing it must rush out with the extinguishing equipment entrusted to it at once. It is no different with the Health Service. If there were helpless injured persons lying on the street, no delay would be allowed, but immediate help must intervene. This mentioned makes it again that the telegraph service for the warning of the Police and Fire Brigade may not be doomed to idleness for a moment. Whoever is in private service may possess the right to put his private interest on the scales against the private interest of his master, but here there can and may be no question of this, as soon as his private interest comes to stand against the public interest of the entire Municipality. The same applies to the medical assistance of the pharmacy. Up to a certain height it cannot even be conceded that the right to strike in private service is unconditionally to be recognized. In working in the mines, in serving on large steam engines and so much more, a sudden strike could bring about the greatest disasters. On board in the open sea the same exception applies. But apart from these all too private relationships, it stands as a palisade above water that whoever is taken on for public service has to submit to the unavoidable conditions that flow from the nature of the public service.

[318]

306 THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN CIVIL ADMINISTRATION.

§22. Extra Wages for Officially Appointed Employees.

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the

In order to prevent inconvenience, this lack of the right to strike must be clearly expressed and accepted before the employment. The Municipality may not begin to leave this matter blue-blue, in order to dispute the right to it only after an unlawful strike has broken out. This issue must also be settled in the service regulations, and a signed declaration must be obtained from the incoming personnel that they accept the service under the tacit obligation that they will not participate in such a strike. This, however, causes these persons taken into official service by the official power to be in a less favorable condition than those who serve with private individuals. This now brings with it the demand that the conditions of their service must also have an exceptional character. Already in itself it is desirable that a Municipal Administration should go ahead in giving the citizens a well-cared-for position, both as regards their wages and their working hours, as well as their pension and other provisions, in order to make the service in the Municipality as attractive as possible. But this demand becomes even more pressing, when the personnel in question are deprived of the right to strike. The Municipality must then make up for this disadvantage by offering them better conditions. It is not enough that their wages are equal to those of private workers; they must receive a bonus on top of that. And this bonus must not be a mere gift, but a right that flows from the nature of their service. The Municipality must also provide for their old age and their disability, and must ensure that they are not worse off than those who serve in private employment. In this way, the Municipality will be able to attract the best personnel and keep them in its service.

[319]

EXTRA COMPENSATION FOR OFFICIALLY APPOINTED PERSONNEL. 307 As for their pensions, this should not be exaggerated. The municipal treasury is filled from all kinds of money, but not to play the role of a generous paymaster. The example set by the municipal administration must be virtuous and proper, but must never exceed the normal measure. It stands quite differently, however, when one considers the rights of which the worker, by entering private service, has relinquished. If in private circles the worker can, if necessary, improve his position through strikes, the man in public service cannot do so. His rights must therefore be guarded in other ways. On this basis, it is now fair that the wages paid by the municipal administration should not exceed the private wages; that there be a ready opportunity to file complaints; and furthermore, that the municipal administration strictly ensure that such complaints, if proven justified, are acted upon. I also come to the conclusion on my part that the wages of officially appointed personnel must contain an extra surplus; but it must be denied that this would serve to set an example. The example cannot go beyond the normal. But this extra is justified, insofar as it takes on the meaning of compensation for a very important right of which the worker has relinquished. The right to association is not thereby eliminated. There is no conceivable reason why those in the official service should not also be able to speak about their interests or subject certain troublesome conditions to criticism. The formation of an Association is also very well permissible for a certain kind or class of such persons or workers, but only in such a way that everything is done openly; that the regulation of such an Association has obtained the approval of the municipal administration; and that a representative of the municipal administration is always present at the meetings of this Association. A conspiracy, of whatever nature, is in no way to be tolerated in the public service. This also applies to the service of the State; and in the great strike of 1903, it was also found to be a gap in the criminal code that no measures had been taken against the abuse of a strike in an important public means of transport. Just as in this same manner, whether by the State Law or the Municipal Ordinance, not only dismissal, but also criminal penalties must threaten

[320]

RELIGIOUS AND MORAL ELEMENTS IN CIVIL ADMINISTRATION. Anyone who disrupts the public course of life by striking. The public service must therefore stand higher for officials and workers, and yield better conditions, but violent strikes must not be allowed in the public service. Whoever nevertheless undertakes this, shall not only be dismissed, but also punished.

§23. Justices of the Peace. The withholding from the Municipality of all legal competence does injustice to its character of self-reliance. More strongly than ever before, it is felt in all the land that a distinction must be made between what is a transgression against the common law and what are civil disputes and disturbances of order. Just as in the family the father as the head is not called in for all small disputes, but the mother settles all kinds of small disputes, or sometimes even the governess is empowered to do so, so it was also customary to distinguish between what was a legal violation, a dispute, or a disturbance of order. Especially in England, the justices of the peace have been acting for the latter purpose since 1327. In France, the juges de paix were introduced in 1790. Such peace judges are also found in Switzerland and Italy. The so-called arbitrators or Friedensrichter, or also Vermittlungsämter, can be found in various forms in almost all German States, even though they do not have the same character everywhere. Sometimes they are appointed by the higher authorities, at other times they are elected by the Municipality. It was sometimes stipulated that they should not be jurists, at other times jurists were preferred. In one country they were salaried, in England it was an honorary office. But in whatever form they appear, they expressed that there are disputes and disputes that do not touch the principles of absolute law, but are of a subordinate nature, and therefore should best be settled among friends, and if that is not possible, then among the neighbors. The Apostle Paul deals with the same problem in 1 Cor. VI: 1–7. The Apostle does not mean here that a transgression against the common law or a crime should be dealt with under the hand, but rather that one should not knock on the door of the judge for mutual quarrels, and really, in a deeper view, should not have such quarrels at all, but settle such disputes with each other in an amicable manner.

Analytical Summary: The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,
[321]

JUSTICES OF THE PEACE - PUBLIC ENTERPRISES. 309 That is why Paul himself declares in verse 4 that “the least among the brethren” should be allowed to settle such quarrels. Also in our country, the settlement of disputes outside the courts is maintained, by stipulating an arbitration tribunal in contracts or otherwise, or also by agreeing on the choice of arbitrators in a newly arising dispute. On the other hand, the municipal authorities have now withheld all mediation and jurisdiction of this kind from the Municipality, and sought a way out in the cantonal judge, whose function not infrequently closely resembles that of a justice of the peace. But the cantonal judge lacks any municipal character. He stands on the lowest rung of the imperial judiciary, but is nevertheless fully incorporated into the imperial judiciary. This too was intended to include the independent significance of the Municipality in a new division of the imperial constitution, and thus to expand the central power at the expense of municipal autonomy. From an anti-revolutionary point of view, resistance must also be noted here. The justice of the peace, provided he is empowered to make definitive decisions to a certain degree, could still remove much bitterness from civic life. A dispute that one would refuse to bring before the cantonal judge would hardly be brought unwillingly before the justice of the peace.

§24. Public Enterprises. More and more, it has become clear that there is a difference in principle between an administrative act and the exercise of a business, and on the other hand, that it may nevertheless be desirable to transfer the exercise of certain businesses from the circle of private individuals to the official administrative domain. A municipal administration that operates a tramway is doing something that is not an outflow of administrative competence, for a private association can also obtain a concession for the same purpose. The municipal administration usually takes over the operation of such a business only when private initiative fails to act or falls short in its prompt service. There are even businesses that one would prefer not to see in private hands. In Amsterdam, there was for a time an English gas factory next to the other, but as a rule the municipal administration took over the gas supply.

Analytical Summary: Our People.
[322]

THE RELIGIOUS AND MORAL IN CIVIL ADMINISTRATION.

It is still preferable that gas pipelines, water supply, etc. are under complete administrative control; and then it certainly deserves preference that such an enterprise be under the Municipal Administration. It can even be imagined that such an enterprise could result in a strengthening of the treasury; something that must not be abused, but which cannot be considered entirely unlawful either. Especially in the state of war that set in with August 1914, the Government found itself compelled to take over such enterprises that in peacetime had always remained in private hands. Of course, one cannot venture too far down this path. Otherwise, it would easily lead to the socialist demise of the socially entrenched life. Yet so many have gradually become convinced that at least in larger cities the Municipal Administration can no longer abstain from all enterprises. Just as the State took over the postal and telegraph traffic, so the Municipality often takes care of gas, water, telephone and hospital care. These kinds of enterprises must therefore also be reckoned with. Initially, the exercise of these enterprises was arranged under the ordinary service. Even the Postal Service has only recently been separated from the State budget as a separate factor, in order to shine only with its profit balance. So now it should also go with the Municipalities. The Municipalities can no longer abstain from all enterprises. They can very well withdraw from them, the better, but this can no longer be completely. The Municipality is better able than private individuals to provide for the citizens in the exercise of such enterprises. It is more expensive than a private association, but for the more smooth and regular running of the service, the citizens generally have something higher to pay for.

The taking over of such kinds of enterprises as monopolies by the Municipalities need not be opposed on our part either. It can even be encouraged, provided that for each enterprise it is clearly shown what and why private initiative fell short here. And it goes without saying as a rule that, when the Municipal Administration takes over the exercise of such enterprises, it is not the Municipal Council members or the Aldermen who should burden themselves with this. For this, most of them would not be competent in the ordinary course of things. Men of the profession must be sought for this business expression of municipal life, and it is the Commissions composed of such men who, under responsibility to the Council, must undertake the enterprise.

[323]

FINAL REMARK. - THREE KINDS OF ISSUES.

In proportion, the municipal household has been discussed in the foregoing perhaps a little too broadly, yet I could not leave this behind. In 1878, when the first explanation of the Anti-Revolutionary Program saw the light, there was still no occasion in practical politics to go into this subject more closely. At that time, hardly any thought was given to an appearance in the Municipal Councils, and the few who found themselves appointed confined themselves almost exclusively to a brief testimony on the matter of Sunday observance and school affairs. In any case, there was then no question of our side putting our own ideas on this terrain to the test. Hence, there was also no special article devoted to municipal interests in Our Program. Even our electoral associations in their Statutes generally cared little about it. Now, however, that entirely new conditions have set in, it would have been an unpardonable omission if these weighty interests had only been touched upon in passing even now. I hope the digression I allowed myself in this Chapter will be forgiven me. Chapter XII. THE STATES-GENERAL.

§1. Three Kinds of Issues. In Our Program I had, at least electorally, included the Municipal Councils and the Provincial States under the same heading as the States-General, and under the title of Councils and States. Now, however, that the previous

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[324]

The issues that the States-General, the Provinces, and the Municipalities have addressed separately now remain for the States-General alone to consider. Three questions demand our Parliament’s attention in this discussion. The first is in what form our States-General ought to appear: in a single Chamber, or in two Chambers. The second is what powers and authorities should be granted to the States-General. And the third or last is how the members of the States-General should be elected. In discussing the third point, I will then give preference to explaining the electoral system for the Municipalities, not only for the States-General, but also for the Provincial Estates and Municipal Councils. Separate treatment of the latter two would only lead to unnecessary repetition. Yet there is a profound distinction between the Provincial Estates and Municipal Councils on the one hand, and the States-General on the other, and this must not be overlooked. For the Municipal Councils and Provincial Estates are governing bodies, while the States-General have only a representative character—but as far as the electoral process and the suffrage are concerned, all three of these bodies raise sufficiently kindred problems that combining the electoral question for all three is not excluded. And if this proves feasible, it will spare the superfluous breadth of exposition.

§2. Principles and Practice. Insofar as the revolutionary, and by extension the liberal, principle desires to see the popular will reign unchecked, there is room in the state structure for only a single Chamber. This was indeed grasped with decisiveness at the outset both by the French statesmen themselves and by the lands that, like ours, sought to model themselves after their pattern. It is wrongly in connection with this that the German Reichstag instituted since 1871 has also been cited. For although it is true that in Germany no Senate has been placed alongside the Reichstag, one must not lose sight of the fact that the so-called Federal Council occupies a position that is almost senatorial. But even for the Estates that still live under the spell of the system of the French Revolution, one is...

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[325]

PRINCIPLES AND PRACTICE.313 Yet almost everywhere the move has been made to establish a Senate-like chamber alongside the actual House of Representatives. Even in France, after the fall of Napoleon III in 1870, something of the old Senate was revived. With us too, shortly after the establishment of the Monarchy, a First Chamber was already instituted as a Senate in 1815. This arrangement was maintained in 1848. And however much the advocates of the popular will have since progressed in their radical tendencies among us—with the exception of the Social Democratic faction—the abolition of the First Chamber has still never been accepted as an electoral slogan. But although this must be recognized as a tribute to our political moderation, the principled significance of this division of our Parliament into two Chambers can scarcely be acknowledged from the outset, and insofar as there may have been some initial concealment, it was nevertheless entirely lost after 1848. In 1813, under the influence of French theories, it had been thought that one Chamber of the States-General would suffice, but Article 78 of the Constitution of 1815 immediately stipulated: “The States-General shall consist of two Chambers.” To ward off too strong a popular influence, Article 80 (only formally amended in 1840) provided that the members of the First Chamber should be appointed by the King, and for life, and this first provision was further supplemented by the requirement that the King should appoint them from “those who have rendered services to the State, or who by their birth or wealth belong to the most distinguished in the land.” This now seems to us ultra-reactionary. But one must not forget that in Italy, according to Article 33 of the Constitution, the King still appoints the members of the Senate, and for life, and likewise from among the most eminent notables, including those who are assessed for 3,000 Lire in direct taxes. What was determined here in 1815 was even more moderate, in that no more than sixty-six members could be appointed, whereas in Italy the King is free to expand the number as needed—something that in itself implies that he has the power to break any resistance from the Senate to his will by adding new, loyal members. With us, 1840 does not count for this point; and in 1848 we did not get the text that the States-General consists of Chambers, but that it is divided into a First and a Second Chamber; the First taking precedence. See Article

[326]

314 THE STATES-GENERAL.76. And what is no less remarkable, the prominent persons now fell away, the Provincial States would appoint, and were eligible exclusively “Highly assessed in the direct state taxes.” Not until 1887 did they return to the old formula and placed alongside the highly assessed once again the practical chosen ones, who “had held one or more high and important public offices.” Yet the tax payment still took precedence. In 1815 it was: the distinction of persons and the amount of tax, in 1848 it was only the amount of tax; and in 1887 the chosen ones were now added again, however, with the proviso of taxpayers and the exclusion of all noble preference. One perceives from this struggle with the word, on how unstable a path one moved. Of a principled distinction between the two Chambers there could be no question; there might well and must be a strongly pronounced difference in speech, but this difference could only be gradual. In the Second Chamber the elected and by the ordinary voters, who stood closer to the people, and then in the First Chamber persons of a higher social class, who would temper or curb the excesses of the Second. Whether one now let these members be elected by the King or by the Provincial States, made no essential difference in the matter. They had to be chosen in any case from the wealthy and prominent in the land, or from men of high position; and even in the choice by the Provincial States, the election of the second would of itself give a tempering outcome. But further they did not come. A distinct character could not be imparted to the two Chambers of the States-General, except insofar as the distinguished and more affluent could put a brake on the wagon of the State.

[327]

ATTEMPT AT PURIFICATION. 315 Especially in the period when I myself began my political career, this Conservatism annoyed me not a little. Liberalism had in fact already gained supremacy in the States-General from 1848 onwards. It helped little, therefore, whether the reaction against this from the Anti-revolutionary, and in general from the Christian side, came to the fore in the Second Chamber, and gained more significance to the left of the Binnenhof; in the First Chamber we could at that time still not break through at all; and one saw it in 1889 in Keuchenius’ fall, how mercilessly they played their overpowering against us. It was with a view to this undesirable situation that I then in 1878 urged in Our Program that indeed the two Chambers should remain, but that then a principally distinct character should be granted to each of these two. In all the life of the people two motives manifested themselves. There was on the one hand in the life of the people an organic development from its roots, and there was on the other hand the significance of the personality in this organic whole. Hence then my proposal to retain both Chambers of the States-General, but to make the First Chamber a representation of the corporate in the life of the people, and to make the Second Chamber the organ of the political choice of the then current voters. The Chamber arising from the organic life of the people would then bear a social, and the other a political character. To make the distinction even clearer, I even spoke of the First Chamber as the corporate, and of the Second Chamber, in contrast to this, as the political States, the character of the brake fell away for the First Chamber, and the persistent conflict between the two Chambers of the States-General would naturally come to an end. In this line of thought one went back to the mighty struggle that had especially since 1789 broken out in the political life of the peoples. Opposed to each other stood of course on all, and thus also on the political field, the unifying force that sought to assemble the unity of peoples and States from the multiplicity of individuals, and on the other hand the force that made the individuals go out from themselves so as not to let their personal and individual significance be absorbed in the unity found. In the 18th century the Princes had terribly exaggerated the unifying unity at the expense of the personal independence of the individuals. This was avenged in the French Revolution, and from 1789 to 1813 the English rule, on the contrary, strongly emphasized the personal independence of the individual.

[328]

The States-General. Following the lead of Edmund Burke, they increasingly resisted the revolutionary activities emanating from Paris, yet as Stuart Mill, Spencer, and others showed, a half-century later, the individualism was all too convincing, and how it appealed so sympathetically to the English character. And so, in fact, this individualism, which politically, to use Da Costa’s words, results in the theory of “a heap of souls on a piece of land,” has come to almost sole dominance among Europe’s statesmen. Even in the proposals for constitutional revision that saw the light in 1915, this one-sided principle came out all too strongly.

§4. Corporate Life.

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.

In contrast to this, I pointed out in 1878 the significance of the freely forming corporate life that had flourished gloriously in the days of the Reformation and was already powerfully prepared in the Guilds and Chambers of Rhetoric of the Middle Ages. But at the end of the 18th century, this corporate life called for renewal of form. Neither the Guilds nor the Chambers of Rhetoric could any longer serve to enliven life in their old forms. They disappeared, partly through destruction. But then there was no immediate new action ready to inspire the organic life of the people corporately, though now in a more contemporary form. In legislative decisions, they then went to work as if there were no more corporate life, and as if it had all atomistically scattered. Hence the tyranny of uniformity, which, blind to all variation in life, could, if it wished, subject almost all the people of Europe as well as America to one norm, to one law of life, and to one set of constitutional life regulations. Against this tyranny, I have been in revolt from the very beginning. “The curse of uniformity” was one of my earliest lectures. Yet such a protest would not have helped me in the least if there had not gone out from life itself an irresistible protest against this uniformity and against this dissolving of the whole into its atoms.

[329]

Protest of Labor. This protest, however, did indeed go forth, and in an ever-stronger form, especially in the realm of labor. It was not long before, precisely in the circle of the workers, they began to feel all too painfully what this pure dissolving of the whole into its atoms meant for them. Alone and in his individuality, the worker stood before his patron as a defenseless lamb. Hence the urge to band together as workers, and precisely in this way to stand stronger against the patron, as soon as he did not shrink from burdening the worker’s existence too much for his own profit. The worker began to feel that the boundless individualism meant his social death, and that only social cohesion and connection could save him. Hence the simultaneous, almost everywhere emerging drive to push for mutual association of the workers. The patrons, for their part, soon banded together against this. And once this tendency to seek corporate connection had come to the fore in trade and industry, it seemed as if this corporate sense had seized the spirit of all.

§6. Longing for All-Sided Connection.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.

At least I know of no time or period in which, in a way like the present, club associations, trade associations, league associations, societies, and what not, have come to dominate our whole life. A complete register of all the corporations, social clubs, financial trusts, or whatever one wants to call them, as they have formed in our small country, in all cities and villages, among men and women, among young and old, in all areas, and are still constantly expanding, would form a volume almost without a closing page. Well proof that the corporate life avenges itself on pseudo-individualism. This corporate life now had two forms, the official and the private. The churches, the universities, etc. stood on the one hand as fixed institutions of a corporate character, and alongside them those endless private

[330]

The associations, societies, unions, leagues, and chambers whose origin lay in the free initiative of the citizenry. Especially in the realm of commerce, industry, and labor, this corporate action is gaining such an overwhelmingly significant role that organization in this area seems ever more necessary. The labor law has already made a considerable advance in this direction.
