There can be no doubt that this organic element in national life will eventually come to have a voice in the People’s Representative Assembly, but this will take time. Premature interference with this emerging element—as seen in the failed Chambers of Labor—does more harm than good. It is clear that the organic fermentation requires time to work through, and only when, especially in the labor domain, the pattern and the worker have each asserted their own rights, and federated in a common action, will the time have come to have this corporate life represented in the States-General; and then in the First Chamber, which will only gradually come to differ from the Second Chamber and to free itself from the financial clause that has still been pressed through.

§7. A Better Choice for the First Chamber. Pending this, the only thing that should be retained from the present structure of the First Chamber is that it arises from a graduated electoral process. The first step lies in the election of the Provincial States, and the second in the choice of the members of the First Chamber by the States, who, by majority vote, as the electoral product of a ballot box struggle, are wont to choose in a particular direction. Furthermore, the choice could be left to these States; at most, a term of office might be retained. The term of office of the members of the First Chamber, which now spans nine years, could then be reduced to six without difficulty. No other conditions need to apply for eligibility. But the question would then need to be considered whether the dissolution of the Provincial States might not be made possible. Dissolution fits neither the Municipal Councils nor the Provincial States, since both are administrative bodies and not representative bodies in the administration.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[331]

THE BETTER CHOICE FOR THE FIRST CHAMBER This representative character belongs solely to the States-General. The question is, however, whether this distinction between the two qualifications has not been drawn too far. Municipal councils and Provincial Estates undoubtedly have an administrative character in the first place; but in the fact that there must also be a more direct administrative rounding-off in the college of B. and W. for the Municipal Council, so that interpellations in the Council and even resignations as a result of an unfavorable vote are by no means unthinkable, the Council or the Provincial Estates as a whole cannot be denied all representative character. It could therefore be argued that if the dissolvability of the Provincial Estates were to be granted, not where their administrative action, but then where their electoral action was concerned, a dissolution of the First Chamber would have a quite different effect than it can have now. As it stands, a direction in politics that has succeeded in becoming master of the terrain in that Chamber has the actual power, for not much less than a small quarter of a century, to control the political course of events, even if a completely different direction has long since come to the fore in the Second Chamber. This cannot be a healthy state of affairs. It embitters the party struggle and hinders the healthy development in the political field. Dropping all requirements for eligibility in this Chamber, shortening the term of office from 9 to 6 years, and making it possible, albeit under limiting conditions, to dissolve the Provincial Estates when they act as an electoral and not as an administrative college, may therefore be regarded as the three wishes that deserve a recommendation regarding the First Chamber. It would then remain the fruit of a graduated choice. It could then continue to act as a moderating influence on the too impetuous character that the decisions of the Second Chamber can sometimes assume. The attempt to seek too much healing for our political life in leaps and bounds could be averted by it. A certain reserve would emanate from it. But its actions would no longer have the detrimental effect of holding back or at least paralyzing the regular shift of political influences for at least a dozen years. With such a modified regulation, one would in the country fall less into discontent with the existence of the First Chamber. It would now sometimes lose its too stern character. And in the meantime, the Provincial Estates could...

[332]

320 THE STATES-GENERAL. By granting a more official regulation to the corporate life, the acceleration will be hastened, whereby the First Chamber, as the representative of the corporate popular life, could acquire its own, independent character. Already in the two proposals for constitutional revision that saw the light in 1912 and 1915, at least one of the three modifications we advocated has been incorporated. No doubt, the other two prospective modifications we proposed will also gradually come to the fore, and, once the time is “ripe,” the corporate Senate will come of its own accord.

§8. The Government for the People. In tyranny, all government that sees in the people over whom it rules nothing but an instrument for its own greatness degenerates. The people do not find the reason for their existence in the power of Emperor or King, of Consul or Ephore; on the contrary, the reason for the existence of any government lies in the well-being of the people over whom, by the ordinance of God, it wields the scepter. This last point must be added. For the people cannot say: The government is there for my sake, so that it is my servant; nor can the Prince claim: The people are there for my sake, in order to enhance the glory of my Majesty. Both People and Prince stand in the service of the Almighty God, have to serve His honor, and exist for His sake. But this being once established, it is not the Prince who is the main thing and the People the secondary, but entirely the reverse—the People is what comes to the fore, and the Prince the power set over this People by God, called to serve the well-being of this People in the prosperity of that People.

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.
[333]

THE GOVERNMENT FOR THE PEOPLE. - THE PRESSURE OF UNIVERSAL ARISTOCRATS. 321 XIV: “L’etat c’est moi”; but even if it seemed that there was a free Commonwealth here at the time, yet in our country too the people had been pushed back in a harsh way, and an oligarchy of Regents ruled here, which reckoned just as little with the rights of the People. It may even be doubted whether the great Revolution in Paris would ever have broken out, if above all in the second half of the 18th century the disregard of those who ruled for the rights of the People had not so violently broken through, that in the end it seemed as if the People had no other calling than to please the Princes and Regents in their arrogance and excess. This could not continue; already in itself it created an untenable situation; and what made that situation even more unbearable, it kept getting worse and took on ever more intolerable proportions. However decisively we as the Anti-revolutionary Party may stand against the French Revolution, and not only abhor its horrors, but just as decisively its principles turned against God, it must nevertheless be openly acknowledged that the Princes and Regents of the time themselves were the guilty ones who provoked the revolutionary resistance. A complete reversal in the state of affairs had become unavoidable, and however painful the operation was, it was nevertheless the French Revolution that we have to thank for the deliverance from the tyranny that had gradually crept in under Crown and Regent’s staff in all lands. This must be stated unequivocally, so that the condition before 1789 may never return among us, and our antithetical stance against the principles of the Revolution may never aim at anything other than to lead both People and Prince in the track of God’s Majesty.

§9. The pressure of universal aristocrats. Do not forget, however, that the brand that has also been pressed on the French Revolution on our side has all too often seduced [people] to bring forth a false Conservatism, which indeed kept the power of the Prince in check, but on its part was intent on extending the authority of notable families, merchants, and leaders of science over the People as much as possible, even more than in olden times. Even Thorbecke’s Electoral Law confirmed a...

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[334]

The States-General: The Conservative Oligarchy of the Respectable Bourgeoisie. The figure of ƒ160 as the minimum tax threshold for Amsterdam leads the way. And year after year, this has been tolerated under the rhetoric of liberalism. In the years preceding 1848, Conservatism had indeed acted in a much more brutal manner; always with the tendency to make the old Regents, with the Prince in their midst, lords and masters in the land. It was always the census that divided the People into two. Wise were only those who met the census; unwise to even attempt to speak, those who fell short; and in any case, the “common folk” were entirely excluded. Hence Groen’s remark about “a guilder under the lowest bid”; something which he later retracted, but which still remains so significant for us, as a protest against the conservative clique. It was not the Crown from which this conservative, People-disregarding action emanated. Rather, it may be said that where it was possible, our Princes did not spare Conservatism. The action that gripped the People was much more the natural expression of life of the well-to-do, self-complacent bourgeoisie, who on the one hand increasingly reined in the Princely authority, but at the same time, on the other hand, kept the “common folk” in the land under their overwhelming power. The art was then simply to sharply distinguish the People into two kinds. There was on the one hand the entire People of the Netherlands, with its millions of adherents, but there was also on the other hand the pays légal, i.e., the part of the People that gained the right of co-determination; and this second, extremely small part of the People, which scarcely exceeded 100,000, was then honored as the only true, the genuine, the only one worth counting. What lay behind it was the quantité négligeable. This naturally entailed that the Prince, to recall Mr. Van Houten’s term, became a mere “ornament.” It was then claimed that one was pleading for the emancipation of the People, but in fact, by “People” was understood nothing other than the liberalized circle, which in cities and villages, as a result of the action of the Public School, seized power, refusing to let it go. Then emphasis was particularly placed on the fact that this higher-educated and census-rich group held the legislative power in its hands. In this legislative power, the mark of Sovereignty was then honored, and to it all that lived outside this narrow circle had to submit well.

[335]

DEMOCRATIC PRESSURE

§10. Democratic Pressure. Yet the Census-conservatism—which one could not call an aristocracy—could not sustain itself. From the outset, there was a gradual difference within this census circle. On the one hand, there was an increasing tendency to strengthen one’s own privileges and to increasingly restrain the power of the Crown, as well as the power that arose from the People; but this ultra-Conservatism itself awakened the free spirits within its own circle. The times spoke in favor of this. The elders often favored the oligarchy, while the younger generation was increasingly driven by a freer spirit. This initial gradual difference began to take on a more principled significance. And so the democratic group then came to stand opposed to the Liberalist faction. At first, these were just two shades within the Liberal party, but soon they became separate dwellings. This division was further promoted by the rise of Socialism. The Liberal group was alarmed by this new political phenomenon, but the more democratically-inclined circle saw in Socialism a ready-to-hand auxiliary force. Finally, it then also came to a clash. In that clash, the Democratic group proved to have a higher vitality, and in 1887 it even pushed through in the Constitution, because the Anti-Revolutionary Party, decisively driven by its Calvinistic past, aligned itself against Conservatism and against the aristocratic oligarchy, albeit in a modified sense, and yet still presented itself as Christian-democratic. Since then, all political currents have increasingly flowed into this ever-deeper dug democratic stream bed. The proposals for constitutional revision just submitted (I am writing this on November 19, 1915) show what giant strides we have taken since 1887. Women’s suffrage is just an ornament, but the atomistic, almost universal suffrage that they now want to introduce finds its attractiveness for the masses only in the fact that there is a chance that it will increasingly lay hold of the financial possessions of the more affluent, so that the less affluent will increasingly receive from the hand of the Government. So in the end, the political dispute again comes down to a money question. What in the period that coincides with this century

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.
[336]

The notion that the spirit guides, sifts, and accumulates, is not a philosophical thought, is not a holy motive, is not a new political system, but rather degenerates more and more into a financial question. The question, namely, whether it will continue to be based on what it thus was: a People with private enterprise, and as a fruit of this enterprise, with private property, and thus at least with the prosperity of some individuals and families, and then alongside the Government with its own cash funds and administration, or whether it will have to be a Government that ultimately has complete free disposal over all private income or property, in order to distribute the thus available amount from above among the population? It is not really socialist yet, but from the People will then nevertheless emerge as beneficiaries some persons as ministers and members of parliament, who, with the Crown Bearer as “ornament” in their midst, will give the required assistance to those whom they consider to have too little, and will take the necessary funds by force, through taxation or levy, from those who have it. In short strokes, this course of development had to be sketched here, in order to make it well understood that in politics it still always comes down to the great question of who will have the disposal of the power derived from money. This has now begun with a struggle between Crown and Parliament. At present, however, we have already progressed so far that the States-General are constantly urging the Government to make large expenditures. Just count up the amendments at a Budget debate that aim at expanding the budget. Formerly it was too much the Government that asked for permission to spend more money, and the Parliament that stood for economy, in order to spare the private purses of the inhabitants. Now, on the contrary, it is the minister who holds back an expenditure that is not highly necessary, and often tries to vote down the greedy amendments. The mutual relations have thus undergone a fundamental change in this respect. Coming to the point, one finds in the present as well as in the past that, much more than is good, the strings of the stock exchange are the ones that govern politics, from of old in the so-called Budget Law, now in the provision by the State of the private needs of the citizens. That the constantly increasing needs of the Army and Navy also play a dominant role in this, is felt everywhere in the land, but this point of state policy also now

[337]

BUDGET REFUSAL IN ENGLAND.325 The form has been completely changed. Formerly, the main question was whether the man who held the reins of government, whether from his own purse or by fulfilling his prayer, would be able to obtain the means to have mercenary troops, if necessary recruited from abroad; while now the People itself, in its international competition, feels the power it must bring together from its own population and must arm, in order to remain independent as a nation and to be able to enrich itself economically. The very character of the former prayer marks the difference here. At that time the Prince, in order to obtain an army at his own disposal, had to come with a request, even in the form of a prayer, to the mighty ones of his People; and now it is the People itself that serves in the war, sacrifices the treasures for that war, and economically hopes to regain what it has consumed in the war. Always, therefore, the money that sets the tone and gives in the political coexistence of Prince and People.

§11. Budget Refusal in England. This financial dispute arose after 1848 often in the dispute of scholars over the right of the States-General to refuse the funds requested by the Government for the conduct of the administration. Especially from the radical side, but also from the ordinary liberal side, the view was held that the States-General possessed the undisputed right to refuse the granting of these funds, and moreover to compel the Cabinet either to resign or to make an about-face. Without going into particulars, one usually appeals to England for the pleading of this right. Yet upon closer examination it soon appears that this right in England had a much more moderate character than one wanted to give it here. It will therefore be good to briefly explain what applied in England. In England, a sharp distinction is made from the outset between ordinary and extraordinary expenditures, and in a completely different sense than with us. For in England the Crown has full disposal over the so-called ordinary revenue, that is, the ordinary income, and only for the extra-ordinary revenue is it held to request it each year, and remains dependent on Parliament. For the ordinary means for the ordinary course of affairs, it is not even accountable in the strict sense that applies to us. The Queen spends those funds from year to year, because they have once and for all, with the cooperation of Parliament, been established for salaries and the like. Yet it should not be overlooked that the ordinary means have steadily declined in amount, and the extraordinary have increasingly expanded in amount. In principle, however, the old rule remained that only the “aides et bénévolences,” as the extraordinary means were wont to be expressed in French, required an annual grant.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[338]

326 THE STATES-GENERAL. The starting point here is the just-mentioned division, that Parliament makes decisions regarding financial management that have a legal character and grants funds for an indefinite time, and that alongside these there are entirely different decisions that more take the form of a resolution and only apply for one year. This brings about that in England, as one well distinguishes, there is a double budget, both for expenditures and for income. In the first list, if one may so call it, the income and expenditures are then filled in that Parliament has once and for all approved, while what stands on the other list relates exclusively to the new fiscal year that is about to begin. The ratio between these two also differs greatly in terms of expenditures and income. As for the income, the Crown possesses a legally granted right to levy three-fourths of the total, and this must only be granted for one-fourth for the one fiscal year. The ordinary expenditures, on the other hand, which can proceed of themselves, mainly relate to the national debt, the civil list of the King and the Royal House, the judicial officers, and those fixed annual salaries that have once and for all been established by an Act of Parliament.

§12. The Budget. The name “budget” is then also not applied to the ordinary, once and for all by law established expenditures, but only applies to the new additional expenditures. The ordinary course here is that the Cabinet submits the new expenditures to Parliament for approval, and Parliament then decides whether to grant them or not. This right of Parliament to refuse the granting of funds is then called the “power of the purse,” and is considered one of the most important constitutional rights of the English Parliament.

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[339]

THE BUDGET. The government first puts forward the expenditures for the navy and army. Parliament then begins by appointing a special Commission to examine these proposals. This Commission bears the name of the Committee of Supply. In this Commission, it is not the Ministers, but the Referendaries from their respective bureaus, who appear on behalf of the Crown to explain the requests and defend the objections that have been raised. From this Commission, the proposals for the Defense expenditures then come to the full Parliament, and the means are then requested for those expenditures under the title soon to be mentioned of “aides et bénévolences.” The resolution to that end is then called the Act towards making good the supply granted to Her Majesty. This proposal also first goes to a Commission appointed for that purpose, which bears the name of the Committee of Ways and Means. After that, the Parliamentary resolution follows, which shows how many of these means will be found from the “Consolidated Fund,” and in what other way the remaining deficit will be provided for. By “Consolidated Fund” is here understood all the revenues and taxes that were previously legally imposed. Formerly, all these separate taxes were considered as separate funds. But since then, they have all been made to flow into one “fund.” And once it has been provided in this way for the regulation of all expenditures and all the necessary means for them, then a third so-called Appropriations Bill is still added to the two bills thus created, which watches over the fact that the granted monies are not spent for other expenditures than those approved; always with a small free play for the entirely unforeseen. Now that the House of Commons has approved these three acts for the supply, for the means, and for the appropriation, they all three go to the House of Lords to be sealed in that Senate as well. The consequence of this mode of treatment is that in England the decision usually does not fall on the budget of a single Department, but covers the entire Budget submitted, but always with the understanding that the voting of the Budget does not bring the entire machinery of the State to a standstill, but only blocks the newly submitted one.

[340]

328 THE STATES-GENERAL.

§13. Safeguard Measure. This English system is therefore well-connected. Yet, precautions have been taken to further complete the breach. History has long shown how the Monarch could be intent on breaking even the Constitution; and one knows how, for a moment, Da Costa himself pleaded the idea that the King possessed the full right to do so. The correct observation was taken as the starting point—that the Constitution was originally issued by a unilateral royal decree, and that this inherently implied the right to revoke what one had oneself introduced. Only, it was forgotten here that the enactment of the Law entailed that the Council of State had been heard and common deliberation with the States-General had taken place. But even though this already overthrows Da Costa’s assertion, it cannot be denied that the establishment of a Constitution is in fact a thoroughly bilateral act, and that it would be formally more correct if, as in Israel, the foundation of the state life had been laid by a confederate decision on the one hand of the People, and on the other hand of the Head of the Monarchy. And although it is true that in England there has never been a transition to the drafting and promulgation of a Constitution, one should still pay attention in this context to the so-called Mutiny Act. Learned from history what a Prince could be capable of, this Mutiny Act determined that the army, which stood under the King’s command, was only appointed for one year, and that Parliament had to reaffirm the King’s authority over this mercenary army every year anew. If, therefore, the King did not timely request from Parliament the confirmation of his authority over this army, and obtain it from Parliament, this army would be dissolved by itself on the day when the preceding authorization expired. And there does exist in England, alongside this appointed mercenary army, an extensive militia, but this may not be deployed for the conduct of a foreign war without a separate act of consent. It is precisely this regulation regarding the English military power, which in 1915, when the introduction of conscription came up due to the war, made the decision on this point extraordinarily difficult.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[341]

SAFETY MEASURE - THE STATE OF AFFAIRS AT HOME.

329 that it has not come to a general rejection of the Budget, as was the case in this paragraph, in England except in the rarest of exceptions. It is precisely the pressing way in which everything related to the Budget was regulated in England that usually made this unnecessary. In a house that is well locked and bolted, talk of defense against burglary only rarely arises. Let it be noted here, finally, that there is nothing mysterious about the word Budget. Bougette was an old French word for a leather bag, which was brought to England by the Norman princes for money bag, and it is this French word that was later pronounced as Budget in England.

§14. The state of affairs at home. Although we have not followed the English track as far as the budget is concerned, yet it can be said: “that Parliament can do everything except making a woman a man,” as an example, and so here too the tendency was noticeable from the outset to sneak towards what has later been called Parliamentarism. It was initially thought that the right of dissolution could be used to guard against this, but the attempts in this direction by the late Mr. A. Heemskerk will not easily lead to repetition. They have, especially in the case that Keuchenius raised, miserably failed. The only complete success was the dissolution of the First Chamber in 1905, which was then obtained by the then Minister Kuyper from the Crown, but this dissolution had a completely independent character. It was by no means intended to make the Crown equal to the Parliament, but merely to bring a First Chamber, whose composition in no way matched the state of affairs in the Provincial States, into conformity with those States. It was therefore clear in advance what change the dissolution then provoked in the composition of the First Chamber; as indeed turned out. This drastic measure will not easily be applied again to the Second Chamber. Yet, quite apart from the issue of dissolution, the rejection of the Budget here will not easily fall into complete disuse. There has never been any question of a complete Budget refusal here. In the law on the means, hardly any discussion took place. Whoever intended to provoke a ministerial crisis, almost without exception resorted to the obvious means of throwing himself on one single budget, and indeed on that budget, the acceptance of which was indispensable for the continued existence of the Cabinet. Sometimes the attack was even directed at only one article of such a budget, but then at one of which one was almost certain that the Minister would not let it go and that the Cabinet would not abandon the attacked Minister. Two consequences could then follow. The Cabinet could remain in office, and seek another Minister for the dismissed, or the Cabinet could itself offer its resignation and retire with its defeated colleague. Which of these two was chosen depended mostly on the question at issue, and on the stronger or weaker position of the Cabinet. In 1906 the De Meester Cabinet remained in office, even though its Minister of War fell; but when in December 1907 his deputy also got into trouble with the Chamber, after Heemskerk’s energetic action, the Cabinet saw no salvation in once again coming out on the scene, and made way for the Heemskerk Cabinet. The blow had been foreseen months in advance; and when the Cabinet refused to continue to lead the way, its resignation request ultimately surprised no one. §15. Groen’s precautionary measure. The attempt made by Groen van Prinsterer to introduce the conviction that a financial proposal might be rejected by the States-General for reasons outside politics, however excellent the intention, could of course not find lasting application, and even insofar as it was applied, it did not achieve its purpose. This would probably have turned out differently if here too there had been a vote on the entire budget. However, this could only take place here in the case of the Law on the Means, and since the Law on the Means did not lend itself to this here, one could not get anything other than separate votes on the various budgets of the separate Departments, and then beforehand, at least in the Second Chamber, on the separate items of those budgets.

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”
[342]

Page 343—-

Groen’s precautionary measure.331 Separate articles of such a budget. Now, in fact, such serious objections could be raised against a particular article of the proposal that one would reject that article, not as a deliberately sought means to provide the minister with a disconcerting check, but out of concern for the state. In the meantime, the matter at hand was usually still connected with the political insights or political intentions of the Minister or the Cabinet, so that the political considerations still spoke from the vote, even if a considerable part of the Chamber turned against the proposed measure rather than against the general policy or against the person of the Minister. Even if we had had a division like ours, which would have allowed rejecting a ministerial budget proposal without thereby disrupting the administration, rejecting a budget to criticize the policy would have been acceptable. Now, however, since all our expenditures are included in one budget, so that the ordinary salaries and other expenditures already ordained by law are also included, there was hardly any room for hesitation, and one had to either approve or refuse, and in that refusal also strike the Minister as a person. There is disorder here among us. The First Chamber usually does not come into action until the end of January of the following year, so that the approval of the two Chambers of the States-General is often not obtained until mid-February on the entire series of budgets; but still, custom brings with it that the government continues quietly with its usual expenditures in those first 4 to 6 weeks of the year, without yet having received the seal of the States-General. An irregularity that inevitably leads to unconstitutional acts. The ministers themselves receive their one-twelfth of their salaries on February 1 and take it without hesitation, even if they will only go through their budget a day or more later. In such a state of affairs, it is of no use whether your admonition is heard, not to reject any budget whatsoever for reasons outside of it. It is perfectly correct that the States-General, if they wish the dismissal of a Minister or a Cabinet, need not keep this desire a secret, and have various means at their disposal to make this desire known to the King, whether by motion or by a special address.

[344]

332 THE STATES-GENERAL and the Crown; but voting always has this in its favor, that the measure itself is much simpler, and moreover, there is an entirely different motive at play. In a motion or address, reasons must be given for one’s intention. This now goes exceedingly well if there is only one party in power.

§16. Mixed Action.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.

However, if the action is against the Cabinet, from the cooperation of two or three parties, not infrequently also of parties that are fundamentally hostile to each other, it is in no way possible to clearly state the motives, just as in a motion or address to the Crown cannot be otherwise. This also explains why one often avoids both the motion and the address, and takes refuge in voting on the budget item or on an entire departmental budget, since the motives need not be unanimous here and the Minister is nevertheless put in a tight spot. Nevertheless, the Anti-Revolutionary Party has always endeavored to heed Groen’s advice, but it has not always been possible for it to persevere in this. Direct exceptions, however rare, have occurred, as far as our own initiative is concerned. But apart from this, one was constantly faced with the case that one had to vote on a budget proposal that had come from the bosom of other parties, and that certainly with a political purpose. Even if the Anti-Revolutionary Members of Parliament then cast their votes against certain articles solely for the sake of the matter itself, this could not prevent the Minister from seeing his article voted down and thus being unseated. In a Chamber with an Anti-Revolutionary majority, the non-voting of a budget article for reasons outside of it would have been a more established rule, but as a small party in the midst of the pressure of the larger parties, we were often not masters of our own position. Where it was once established that the Government, in order to be entitled to its expenditures, needed the prior consent of the States-General, it followed as a matter of course that the more revolutionary-minded groups of this would make use of their power to push through their views in the administration as much as possible. And if this intention existed, it was in the nature of things that whoever wanted to exert coercion on the Cabinet, or also wanted to make way for a combination of their own kindred spirits, allowed themselves, in their choice, to resort to any parliamentary means of attack on the government, in each given case choosing that means which promised to be the most effective.

[345]

MIXED ACTION - PARLIAMENTARISM. 333 In fact, it has also turned out that the Second Chamber, without much scruple, according to its composition, has repeatedly enforced its own will at the expense of the Government by withholding funds.

§17. Parliamentarism. Now it is perfectly correct that this way of acting has led to what is called Parliamentarism at our expense, which detracts from the honor of the Royal authority. For one is increasingly moving in a direction as if the real government rested with the States-General and as if the Crown only lent the required form to the execution of what the States-General wanted. Articles 72 and 109 of our Constitution are to blame for this; and the evil has been aggravated by the fact that we do not know a vote on the Budget as a whole, and in particular not, after having made a prior separation in this Budget between what must be available by itself to let the State’s household run, and what provokes new opposition. Yet this must never lead to the conclusion that, in revising our Constitution, the power to decide freely on the monies that will be placed at the disposal of the Government should be taken away from the People in their representation. On every tax bill, the States-General must be able to show whether or not this new burden will be imposed on the people; and for all the money that, by virtue of the decision taken at the introduction of such a tax, is placed at the disposal of the Government, the Government remains accountable.

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the
[346]

THE STATES-GENERAL. The administration is firmly established. When this was previously in a less firm connection, the most deplorable abuse of power has arisen from it. The dangerous fluctuations in European state life, of which the 17th and 18th centuries bear witness, have been largely due to this defect in the financial administration.

It was only the taxpayer’s right to be taxed that put an end to this nameless confusion.

§18. The Anti-Revolutionary Standpoint. It is therefore also strictly and firmly maintained from the Anti-Revolutionary side that the Government has no right to dispose of any other money than what has been entrusted to it by the States-General. Even over the proceeds of the taxes, the right of disposal must be granted to the Government by the States-General each time anew. A tax law indeed gives the Administration the right, and even imposes on the Government the duty, to collect the taxes due from the subjects, but this in no way implies that it may dispose of the monies thus collected at its own discretion. For this, it requires precise proposals in the budgets, and it is only through the approval of these budgets that the Administration acquires this claimed right of disposal.

Analytical Summary: A Happy Turn.

To insist sharply on this all-sided budgetary right of the States-General is in no way a questionable Parliamentarism, but rather the a.b.c. for all healthy public finance. Neither Groen van Prinsterer nor any other Anti-Revolutionary statesman has ever compromised on this undisputed right of the States-General. This was done by the old Conservatism; on our side, it has never been done. Of course, a people can still be at such a low level of development that it is still in its infancy, and then naturally it stands under paternal or guardianship supervision. At that low level of development, there can be no question of popular autonomy. The Government must then proceed according to its own judgment, and even dispose of the good money of the ward according to its best knowledge.

There is then still no question of a constitutional relationship between Sovereign and People.

[347]

THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY STANDPOINT. - OUR CULTURAL MATURITY.

§19. Our Cultural Maturity.

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.

But in this the old Conservatism erred, in that it also wanted to apply this guardian and ward relationship to a People like that of the Netherlands, which had surpassed the whole of Europe in cultural maturity three centuries earlier. The old Conservatism has underestimated our People; and insofar as the Regents had cut off the political development of the People to our detriment, and thereby became the cause that the wild song of Paris in 1789 also here tore the long-suppressed People with wanton longing from the bonds that embraced it, the blame lay with them that at the turn of the 18th to the 19th century things went so insanely here. The game played by the Regents took its revenge in the wild behavior of the People, which had outgrown its guardianship. Instead of now leading this disorderly drive into better channels, the government has constantly tried since 1848 to perpetuate the essentially unfree state of the People. Hence the turmoil that then arose. Even Thorbecke did not bring us to where we should have been. The suffrage he introduced was a mockery of our cultural development. It was precisely this that first caused the Anti-Revolutionaries, and soon after them the so-called Radicals, and finally the Socialists, to tighten the financial bond again, in order to secure the rights of the people in this way, as by a means that was self-evident. The mistake was only that at that time, in our Constitution of 1848, the budgetary problem was not regulated according to its requirements. No distinction was then made between what could not be lacking for a moment for the political breathing of the People, and what required a separate, conscious and deliberate decision each year. Everything is mixed together, what must go on without interruption for the state economy, and what should be placed as a means for further development. In England this was well provided for. Hence the courageous action of Parliament is always greeted with applause from the People, and on the part of the Crown is always honored. With us, on the other hand, it has always been attempted by small pushes and shoves to draw the power to a relatively not so large circle of statesmen, who, though in a very modified form, have always wanted to maintain the old Conservatism.

[348]

336 THE STATES-GENERAL. They proceeded in the manner of the old Regents, as now aiming against the Crown with the People, what had been pursued in former times at the expense of all the lower classes. It remained the rule of the Gentlemen over the national life. As our Constitution now reads, and the custom of more than half a century has determined the course of political life among us, no remonstrances are of any avail anymore, and Parliamentarism is no longer to be avoided in a mitigated form.

§20. Ministerial Resistance. The constitutional power which the States-General possess is so all-encompassing that a Minister or Cabinet struck by a parliamentary vote always acts with the obligation to abstain from any resistance thereto. Dissolution has proved not to be to the taste of the Dutch electorate. Rather, it has repeatedly provoked the voters, as if forcing them to reconsider their once-made choice. To our Second Chamber, in the literal sense, no single power has been withheld. It disposes, in the utmost case, of all conceivable means of defense. And with the history from the past of what has occurred in other countries, and also what has been experienced here at home upon dissolution, it can be safely professed that the final decision in the outbreak of fierce political struggles among us still rests with the States-General. Only mutual assurances of goodwill can moderate and temper the friction and collision that may arise therefrom. Detachment from the right to make the necessary expenditures for the normal state service from the danger of Budget refusal could give some greater unity. It may well come to that. But for the rest, the difference in the composition of the First and Second Chambers alone sets the limit to the omnipotence of Parliament. It will therefore always be a matter of carefully ensuring that in the revision of the Constitution, as long as we do not get an organic choice of the First Chamber, the position of the First Chamber at least does not undergo such a modification that it would ultimately be nothing more than a shortened duplicate of the Second. It is not otherwise. In the end, the final decision in a constitutionally governed country must always remain with the representation of the People. The People

Analytical Summary: The three types not equally characteristic. But this does not mean that, in the course of time, each of these three types has in turn dominated the national condition: the Roman Catholics in the Middle Ages, the Calvinists in the 16th century, and the French Revolutionaries after 1789. It does not at all follow from this that they are equal in national significance. Undoubtedly, the Seven Provinces, once living a common life with Flanders and other southern regions, have enjoyed a high and flourishing position and have gradually taken an increasingly important position in Northern Europe; but it must not be forgotten here that the national energy was at the time much more strongly developed in what is now called Belgium than in the northern provinces. Neither Amsterdam nor Rotterdam could in those days be placed on a par with Ghent and Bruges. The wealth of medieval life had more stamped its imprint on the national character in the South than in the North. The reference to the rich medieval type therefore has much less significance for the present Kingdom of the Netherlands than for the western part of Belgium. And in the second place, it must be added that at that time the unity of our national life was much less consolidated. Under the Burgundians, the Dutch provinces did not occupy an independent position, but were united with what lay outside our later borders; and under Charles V and Philip, we even came, though only in a personal way, into a Union with entirely different states from Central and Southern Europe—something which could not but be detrimental to the confirmation of our own national character. Whoever investigates the historical significance of our lower lands may well find the period we lived through in the Middle Ages highly important, but nevertheless hesitates for a moment to acknowledge without reservation that our glorious period dates from April 1, 1572. Not in the Middle Ages, but in the period of the Reformation, lies the high point of our national history. It cannot be emphasized enough how much scrupulous fairness is required in such a comparison, and we shall beware of the assertion that from the Calvinist side the correct toll of reverence has always been paid to the medieval period in our history, to which they undoubtedly had a right. But it stands to reason that from the Roman Catholic side, too, we have not always been done justice. It cannot be valued highly enough that especially in the second half of the 19th century a more impartial judgment gained influence. Just with a view to this more unanimous judgment, it may now at least be established that not only the foreign historians, one may well say without any exception of importance, honor the period after 1572 as the highest and most glorious, but that also within the country the best interpreters of each of the three types increasingly come to the unanimous conclusion that the world significance of our small country came out most richly in the heyday of Calvinism. This in no way denies that our national life in those days of our glory also showed dark sides. The resistance to the monarchical state form, and even against the House of Orange, has almost become a source of our unhappy discord until 1789, and it is impossible to say how much higher our country would have soared if immediately after the separation from Philip II the crown had been offered to Prince William. It had to come to this in 1813; but who calculates what national suffering would have been spared us if we had taken this important step two centuries earlier. But even if one plays open cards and takes into account all the suffering, oppression and restriction of rights that the temporarily dominant group has inflicted on the other two groups, it is historically established that we were never before 1572 what our national life was in the period between those two dates, and never again after 1789. To top it off, the fact that we offered a refuge to the politically and religiously persecuted from all countries is proof that the actual life here was a life in freedom, and that the publications and ordinances that claimed to infringe on this freedom had more the effect of satisfying formal demands than of inflicting suffering on those who deviated.
[349]

MINISTERIAL RESISTANCE - REFERENDUM. 337

remains, according to the Anti-revolutionary principle, the main thing, and the Government is given by God to this independent people to lead it on its historical path of honor and national happiness.

§21. Referendum. The federal Constitution of the Swiss Republic, as promulgated on May 29, 1874, contains in Article 89 the provision “that federal laws are subject to the approval and disapproval of the People, if the request for this is made by 30,000 legal voters, or by eight of the 22 cantons. The same applies to federal decrees of a general nature that do not have the character of urgent decisions.” In accordance with this Art. 89, a second federal law was promulgated a month later, on June 17, 1874, which in 16 articles regulated this power of the Swiss people. According to this second law, see Art. 2, the Federal Council now decides whether a law or decree has a general character and is to be considered an urgent measure. If a canton or a group of citizens wishes to hold a referendum on a particular decision or an adopted law, official notification thereof must be given to the Federal Council within 90 days. If such a referendum is to be held, it will be held on the same day throughout Switzerland, which day is then determined by the Federal Council. Art. 10 stipulates that every Swiss who has reached the age of 20 years has the right to vote here. Art. 13 prescribes that the minutes of the cantonal votes must be submitted to the Federal Council within 10 days. If the majority of the votes then declares itself against the law or decree, these are void; if only a minority votes against it, the law and decree immediately receive validity through this negative outcome and are promulgated by the Federal Council (see Art. 15). It is especially the smallness of the individual cantons that has led to the introduction of this referendum. The canton of Uri has only 22,113 inhabitants; the two Unterwaldens not more than 13,788 and 17,161. Zug not more than 28,156, Schwyz 58,428, Glarus 33,116, the two Appenzells 59,973 and 14,659, Schaffhausen 46,097, Basel-land not more than 76,488. These are small states.

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over
[350]

338 THE STATES-GENERAL. Already 10 of the 22 cantons, which do not count more souls than one of our average cities; and, as a matter of course, the voter in such small cantons does not feel the necessity to have himself represented. Why could he not just as well vote on laws or resolutions himself? In some cantons, they have not even confined themselves to the proper Referendum, but, as especially in Zurich, Zug, Solothurn, Basel, Schaffhausen, Aargau, Thurgau, Vaud and Neuchâtel, introduced the right of initiative; which means that a numerically determined number of voters can provisionally vote on a draft law, which is then sent to the Federal Council for further consideration. Such a Referendum is in part on a par with Proportional Representation, insofar as the smaller district division is eliminated here, in order to have the voters cooperate directly, over the entire country, or over a very large part of it, in the composition of Parliament. Even closer to the Referendum comes the so-called Plebiscite, which in the old Tribunician Comitia of the Romans was issued by the Plebs. Napoleon I, who always showed a tendency to imitate the form of the Roman World Empire, also revived this Plebiscitum. The last Napoleon also based the validity of his imperial crown on such a Plebiscite. Victor Emanuel made use of the same legal form, in the annexation of each new part of Italy, and finally of Rome itself. And even in the Peace of Prague, the chance was given to North Schleswig to express itself by popular vote, whether it wanted to join the decision that had been made. Stoerk, in his Option und Plebiscit, (Leipzig 1879) has brought together everything that relates to such a Plebiscite. In such a Referendum or Plebiscite, the desire always comes to the fore to have the voters not by delegation, but themselves and directly, govern the political condition of the country.

§22. Free from the Voters? Undoubtedly, there is more to be said in favor of such a Referendum than one would say at first hearing. As it now stands, the voters hand over the affairs of the country, and it is the elected members of the Chamber who become masters of the field; and this all the more, now that Article 86 of the Constitution stipulates that the members of the States-General have to vote without instruction or consultation with those who appoint them. This article, of course, reverses what was previously the rule here, when the elected were merely instruments who had to execute the instructions given to them by the States or Councils. The Referendum now, on the contrary, aims precisely to enable the voters themselves, if necessary, to correct what has been wrongly done by their representatives, and in any case to control and correct the opinion of their representatives if necessary. In this lies something that has a claim to appreciation from the Anti-Revolutionary side. In contrast to the position, which has been constantly emphasized, partly by some Liberals, partly from the Conservative side, that the members of the Chamber are completely free from the voters, our side, already from the days of Groen, has always advocated the view that a member of the Chamber does not stand outside all connection with his voters. We immediately came up with a general program and a special program for each constituency, and considered it a duty to investigate to what extent the candidates who offered themselves agreed with these programs. We did not demand any promise from the candidates, we did not bind them by any agreement, but we did constantly insist that the voters should not vote for an N.N., but should only support those candidates who clearly showed their political sentiment, especially on the pending bills or the subjects to be regulated more closely. It even happened to us that a member of the Chamber, of whom we had previously learned that he agreed in political conviction with his voters, but who, once elected, went over to an opposite view, would do well to take his resignation, in order to make a new election possible. From this position, it follows that there are certainly cases conceivable in which there would be good reason for the direct consultation of the voters, over the heads of the elected members. §23. In the Unforeseen. Such a case could especially occur if, in a completely unforeseen way, an important decision had to be made on a matter that had not been the subject of the election campaign, and on which the elected members had not yet had the opportunity to form a considered opinion. In such a case, it would seem justified to consult the voters directly, in order to ascertain their will, before the elected members definitively decide. This would apply, for example, if a war were to break out unexpectedly, or if a new and weighty international treaty were to be concluded, or if a fundamental change in the economic system were to be proposed. In such cases, the elected members would not yet have had the opportunity to sound out the opinion of their voters, and it would then be appropriate to consult the voters directly, before the final decision is made. The Referendum would then serve to ascertain the true will of the people, before the representatives of the people definitively decide.

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the
[351]

FREE FROM THE VOTERS? - IN THE UNFORESEEN. 339 As it now stands, the voters hand over the affairs of the country, and it is the elected members of the Chamber who become masters of the field; and this all the more, now that Article 86 of the Constitution stipulates that the members of the States-General have to vote without instruction or consultation with those who appoint them. This article, of course, reverses what was previously the rule here, when the elected were merely instruments who had to execute the instructions given to them by the States or Councils. The Referendum now, on the contrary, aims precisely to enable the voters themselves, if necessary, to correct what has been wrongly done by their representatives, and in any case to control and correct the opinion of their representatives if necessary. In this lies something that has a claim to appreciation from the Anti-Revolutionary side. In contrast to the position, which has been constantly emphasized, partly by some Liberals, partly from the Conservative side, that the members of the Chamber are completely free from the voters, our side, already from the days of Groen, has always advocated the view that a member of the Chamber does not stand outside all connection with his voters. We immediately came up with a general program and a special program for each constituency, and considered it a duty to investigate to what extent the candidates who offered themselves agreed with these programs. We did not demand any promise from the candidates, we did not bind them by any agreement, but we did constantly insist that the voters should not vote for an N.N., but should only support those candidates who clearly showed their political sentiment, especially on the pending bills or the subjects to be regulated more closely. It even happened to us that a member of the Chamber, of whom we had previously learned that he agreed in political conviction with his voters, but who, once elected, went over to an opposite view, would do well to take his resignation, in order to make a new election possible. From this position, it follows that there are certainly cases conceivable in which there would be good reason for the direct consultation of the voters, over the heads of the elected members.

[352]

THE STATES-GENERAL. The subject that has come up was one on which the voter could not have learned the conviction of the Member of Parliament at the ballot box. Take, for example, the bills introduced by the Cabinet-Kort v.d. Linde in October 1915, which aimed to provide for the financial distress that had also resulted here from the European war. For here too it was a subject, and, in connection with this subject, legislative proposals, about which neither candidate nor voter had even remotely thought at the 1913 election. At the same time, this was a legislative matter of such an entirely exceptional nature that the opinions about it often clashed within the bosom of the parties. It could therefore rightly be said that these were decisions of the highest importance, without the voter in 1913 knowing what conviction the candidate he was then voting for was attached to, or whether he had even been able to express his feelings in any way. A new Chamber election could in such a case be the ready means to fill this gap. If, however, one considers that dissolution would be too drastic a measure to consult the will of the voters, then the Referendum could be of service here, so that the voters could directly express their feelings.

§24. What is against it? For the rest, however, the Referendum does not seem recommendable. If all representation of the People is lacking, and it votes directly itself, then the decision lies in the hands of thousands and again thousands who lack sufficient knowledge of the matter in question, and do not even have the means to inform themselves about it. By far the majority of voters almost entirely lose their available time to their ordinary life’s task. Often the light must come to them from political meetings, and one knows how often an excited speech there sets the tone, while all guarantee for a thorough discussion is lacking. On the other hand, if the People choose their representatives, then the elected members are free to fully inform themselves down to the smallest details through the documents, in the Committees, through Commission meetings, and through public debate. They can always fully acquaint themselves, and the ministerial offices provide folio after folio of all conceivable data that is desired. In this way, the Members of Parliament, also thanks to their regular club meetings, can form a properly grounded conviction, and at least all superficial and thoughtless action can be excluded. Whoever loves solidity over superficiality will therefore certainly give a far greater preference to a popular sovereignty that works through representatives than to a for or against vote of the mass of voters, who often go by a mere slogan and cannot be fully informed of the matter in question. This is not to say that the voters could not directly express their feelings on some simple questions, as for example on women’s suffrage; but as a matter of principle, we would only dare to recommend the Referendum where a decision of very far-reaching import had to be taken on a subject about which at the ballot box hardly anyone had spoken, yes, to which well considered no one had even given a thought.

Analytical Summary: Our People.
[353]

WHAT IS AGAINST IT? - REJECTION OF THE BUDGET. 341 It is true, such a Referendum limits the competence of the States-General to some extent, but this limitation can logically flow from the circumstances under which it came into being at the last election.

§25. Rejection of the Budget. Both Article 8 and Article 9 of the Anti-Revolutionary Program give us the guiding principle here. Rejection of a Budget for reasons outside that Budget, our party in its program disapproves of, as an overstepping of the power that belongs to it, but not without adding: “unless in very extraordinary circumstances,” which of course may only be understood to mean that there are cases conceivable where a malicious Cabinet would seek to undermine the fundamental rights of our People or our Royal House, and where the warding off of such an attempt could hit no other target than to deprive such a Cabinet on the spot of the disposal over the monies of the State. That for the rest, in order to establish better regularity, a distinction applies with us other than between ordinary and extraordinary, or better said, between normal and exceptional.

Analytical Summary: Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit
[354]

As already stated, the introduction of a new tax in addition to the already legally ordained taxes would be contrary to Article 8 of Our Program. And in connection with this, Article 8 of Our Program also professes that the Anti-Revolutionary Party demands the confirmation of the legitimate popular influence, which, by virtue of the moral bond between electors and elected, is exercised over the State authority by the States-General, according to the requirements of our history. Just as Article 9 guards against the revolutionary misconception that it lies within the power of the Representative Body to overthrow the Government at any moment it pleases, and thereby to upset the sitting Cabinet, so too does our program in Article 8 guard against the tendency of Conservatism to make the People, with their rights, a plaything of the Government. It is not the Sovereign who first creates the People; first there is the People, and the Sovereign is placed over this People so that the People may develop and flourish under the blessing of the authority of the Government. Whatever one may complain about Parliamentarism, the Anti-Revolutionary Party will never shirk this its duty to stand up for the free unfolding of the forces that lie hidden in the life of the people. As the House of Orange has helped the People in this, our Princely House will always enjoy our love, but an appendage of whichever Princely House it may be, the People in the Netherlands, as our Party intends it, will never be.

§26. Our Program Regarding Suffrage. The Anti-Revolutionary Party spoke in more detail about the Suffrage in 1878, as evidenced by Article 11 of its Program.
