It did so in the following manner: > In order that the States-General may be rooted in the nation, not merely represent the People in name, and in their composition no longer constitute a violation of the rights of the minorities, the Anti-Revolutionary Party demands the introduction of a different Electoral System, and, as a preparation for this, the lowering of the Census. In the then-current explanation of this article, the case was made for organic Suffrage, and this in such a way that in the First Chamber the various interests in all areas, whether economic, scientific, literary, aesthetic, or social, would find their appropriate representation.

Analytical Summary: Guided by Orange. To this has been added: “guided by Orange”; an addition that must not be omitted. For it cannot be denied that in the characterized Calvinism, as it also penetrated our country from Geneva and France at that time, there was a danger not to be underestimated, the aftereffects of which are still discernible. In the last chapter of the First Part of this work, I have extensively explained how from the outset there was a decisive element in Calvinism that could only develop and mature later, and that, politically speaking, only came to full effect in the United States; but it was also then added that in Geneva (one need only think of the burning of Servetus) an intolerant dogmatism still prevailed, which, though indispensable for the church, could only result in schism and oppression in its political consequences.
[355]

OUR PROGRAM REGARDING SUFFRAGE.343 The representation would find its expression, so that a Chamber of Interests might arise, and that in the Second Chamber the political directions would come to the fore, emerging organically from the full life of the people, according to its division into parties. The system of Proportional Representation, already so warmly recommended by Her, was therein extolled as the most desirable. From this, the idea gradually developed that the Household-Head or Family-Head suffrage for the Second Chamber deserved preference. The later-arising home rule in England also seemed, even though this system rested only on home ownership or house rent, to readily lend itself to the organic idea. It also gained adherents among the Roman Catholic Party. And finally, it found expression in the draft Constitutional Revision of 1912 to a large extent. The difficulty lay merely in this, that everywhere else the atomistic Suffrage had gained entry, and, once accepted, had been carried through with ever stricter consequences. The cause of this was the atomistic State-idea. One no longer thought of a People that was organically connected in a historical sense in the households, families and lineages, and now ordered under an Authority, but understood by State a collection of individuals who had come to live together on the same piece of ground not otherwise than by virtue of their free will, and who now, as an atoms-group, appointed an Authority which was then indeed called Authority, but was in fact the servant of the group of individuals who had appointed it. Always the Contrat Social! This atomistic conception of the State-concept could not but lead to the Universal Suffrage of men, and, with the ever greater disappearance of the distinction in legal status between man and woman, to the equally Universal Suffrage for women. The path to this did not run in a straight line for the People. In all kinds of ways, a halt has often been made. But more than one halt was yet not a stopping. Once the organic conception of the State had been abandoned, the logical consequences pressed ever further towards atomism. Although the State-idea in an atomistic sense encounters principled opposition among us, this pressure was by no means to be suppressed; and now that the less privileged increasingly cherish the hope that the Universal Suffrage in an atomistic sense will be able to bring them, at the expense of the more privileged, an improvement in their financial position, the action in that direction does not come to rest.

[356]

THE STATES-GENERAL. This is an even more precarious case for us, because we too wish to give the right to vote as broad a foundation as possible; only in a different way.

§27. The People in the Mir. The most natural way for several people to live together in the same place is for the interested parties to come together and discuss their common interests. What is most remarkable about this is not the least the way in which this has succeeded with the Russians in their so-called Mir. In my Around the Old World Sea, I, p. 119–124, I gave a more detailed picture of this Mir; here I can only briefly refer to it. This Mir seems to have arisen at the beginning of the 15th century, when the family bond no longer sufficiently held the inhabitants together. The more the patriarchal bond, which was initially even organized in a matriarchal way, relaxed, so that it ultimately no longer served its purpose, the more the widely dispersed population of Russia felt the need to call the joint inhabitants of a village or of a region with three, four small villages together from time to time for a meeting, to deliberate on the common neighborhood interests. For this purpose, a certain number of families who lived close together were then coupled together, and this coupled group of peasant farms bore the name of Mir. If necessary, this Mir was then divided into smaller groups of peasant farms, and these were called Osmarks, while at the head of the Mir stood a head who bore the name of Starista. This coupling in the Mir now aims at the common ownership of land and forest, and at everything that belongs to the civil community. The so-called Volost is an even broader integration that supports the Mir. Now it is already highly interesting in itself that the woman in the Mir appears as an equal alongside the man in many questions, and on the other hand that one, as far as possible, refrains from outvoting a minority. One prefers to keep a matter pending for three, four years, and to bring it up again and again, than that an outvoted minority would feel aggrieved.

Analytical Summary: The wrong done to us. Our party has set up a price in 1878, in its own starting point, to point to this happy influence of the Prince of Orange. Let no confusion creep in here. In matters of ecclesiastical life, Calvin’s insight has also been his own. His dispute with Dathenus does not touch the very life of the church, but the influence of the Church on the life of the people in the political domain. As a theologian, especially Johannes a Lasco deserves appreciation on this point, but on a broader scale it has above all been the influence of the Prince that has safely guided us. Meanwhile, one should not lose sight of the fact that this pointing with preference to the Prince is foreign to the intention, to praise anything that has later emerged from the House of Orange on this delicate terrain of the Princes. Prince Maurice aligned himself much more closely with the Calvinists. The then-emerging conflict with Arminianism necessitated this. But his life history would stand in a more glorious light for us if Oldenbarnevelt had died a natural death. Under Prince Frederick
[357]

THE PEOPLE IN THE MIR. - THE BERBERS. All those who belong to such a Mir do not do their business through delegates whom they elect for this purpose, but themselves, and for this they come freely and frequently to meetings. Everyone takes part in these meetings. These gatherings last for hours, and everything concerning the Mir is discussed extensively and with warmth. One sees the Russian peasant that he really lives in his Mir. So there is no question of the right to vote. One does not elect, but comes to the Mir meeting itself, and it is in this Mir meeting that the People themselves, in a very democratic sense, settle their affairs. This was the case at the time, and it can still be the case to some extent, because the population is so thinly scattered. At a distance of hours from his village, the Russian peasant finds nothing but meadows, arable land and mountainous regions. It is precisely this that makes it unnecessary to have any further or higher administration. One lives and governs oneself.

§28. The Berbers. Of more or less similar nature is the life among the Berbers or Kabyles in Algeria and Morocco. Here it is not about peasants, but rather about groups of industrialists and merchants, but it is quite peculiar that these Berbers live together everywhere in small towns and have surrounded these their towns with walls, in order to shut themselves up in them. Between the one town and the other, a fierce war rages again and every time, and every inhabitant of the Berber fortress is obliged to take part in these civil wars; something that occurs more often, since the blood feud in the families has not yet been eradicated here. In this respect, the situation is like that on Corsica. If one enters such a town through the thick gate, then the attention is immediately drawn to the open square, often provided with benches, where the meetings of the People are held. And here too, just as in the Mir, the entire population of the Berber fortress gathers on these squares, deliberates and makes its decisions. The resemblance to the Mir cannot be denied. Only in this respect is the remarkable difference between the two, that the Mir arose in the 15th century, while the Berbers already held their popular assemblies in the days of the Roman Empire. The family bond

Analytical Summary: Enduring Bond. No Calvinist, in any country, has ever thought of whitewashing any injustice for the benefit of a princely ruler. But apart from what criticism may later have become necessary, we still always honor in the appearance of Prince William the Silent a gift of God to our people in their then so distressed condition; we thank God for what the time has bestowed on our fatherland in the generation of the Prince; we now still refresh ourselves in the national glory that has emanated from this House of Princes over our people as a nation; and we now still take sides, not on ecclesiastical, but on political terrain, for what the Silent One, the first among all princes in Europe, as the consequence of the Reformation, brought us in the bestowal of full spiritual freedom. Precisely for this reason, protest is constantly lodged by us against any attempt to discredit Orange as an ornament, as Mr. S. van Houten has dared to do. Not, we hasten to add here, as if this Statesman wanted to detract from the glory of our House of Orange. What he spoke of as an “ornament” was only the formulation in a single word of his constitutional theory. But here lies the difference, that such a declining expression of “ornament” was more dear to him, while it strikes us as offensive to our love for Orange. Especially after the shameful treatment that the House of Orange has suffered through its expulsion and forced flight to England in 1797, and after the more than shameful threat with which in 1848 in the liberal press one even dared to speak of the scaffold to unsettle Orange itself, it betrayed a stiff lack of tenderness and self-control in a man who ultimately became an advisor to the Crown, to hear him speak of “ornament.” Not daring to criticize a Prince openly is a cringing cowardice that Orange demands less than it requires, and which is unthinkable especially in the Calvinist. But this does not detract from the firm demand that we remain thankful for honoring what our God has bestowed on us in Orange through his great Princes. A glory in the past, the happy echo of which was to be heard in our revered Queen, as the last scion of the ancient House of Orange.
[358]

346 THE STATES-GENERAL. It seems to have originated much earlier. Hence, the assemblies have a very ancient origin.

§29. Our Constitution of 1798. In the days of the French Revolution, nothing similar arose. It was also brought to our country in 1798. However, in the Constitution that was introduced here that year, the so-called primary assemblies immediately appeared. See Title I, Article 6. First, the country was divided into Departments; those Departments into Circles; and those Circles into Municipalities; but besides this threefold distinction in Article 5, Article 6 stipulated: “Besides this division into Departments, Circles and Municipalities, the entire Republic is further divided into primary assemblies and District meetings, suitable for the activities of the People. Voter registers were drawn up of those who received the right to participate in these primary assemblies. Article 18 stipulated that these primary assemblies were composed of neighboring Houses, Estates or Neighborhoods, together constituting 500 voting citizens; and from these primary assemblies, forty in number, the District assembly was then formed. On these two types of assemblies, discussion and voting took place, and, though at some distance, these assemblies also showed a certain resemblance to the Mirs and the Berber gatherings. It was not a voting in a dead ballot box, but a coming together of the People in specially convened assemblies. To this it must still be added, that a similar phenomenon occurred, and had to occur, when the Pilgrim Fathers and other exiles from England landed in America and united there. Although under Jefferson the ideas of the French Revolutionaries also crept in here, yet neither Jefferson, nor after him Hamilton and Washington, retained the leadership, and these two stood sharply opposed to Rousseau’s Contrat Social. But although this was so, it could not be otherwise, or the settling of a still very small number of exiles in a mostly wild region, would have led them to hold assemblies of all who settled in the same neighborhood, and in these assemblies not through representatives, but themselves to take care of their common public interests. Decidedly democratic was thus also here the starting point, but without there being initially even dreamed of a Contrat Social. Before one deliberated, one prayed. Even once in a while one knelt down together.

Analytical Summary: According to the needs of our time.
[359]

347 REPRESENTATION WAS DEMANDED.

§30. Representation was demanded.

Analytical Summary: The Church and the State in Mutual Relation. One immediately feels this in the relationship between Church and State. In the 16th century, the struggle that the Reformation undertook against Rome’s overpowering authority naturally brought the ecclesiastical question to the fore in all political domains. The ecclesiastical question dominated the entire political sphere, both domestically and with a view to foreign relations, and here the foreign relations mostly stood...

This self-assembling of the People gradually became unusable for two reasons. The first was the expansion of the population, soon followed by a greater concentration of a larger population in the cities, and the second cause arose in the more general interests that came to the fore. As long as one had to deal here exclusively with rural hamlets and villages, under a predominantly male population of only a few hundred persons, it was still very well possible to assemble together, deliberate together and decide together. But when, on the contrary, the population in a place began to produce some thousands of persons entitled to speak, this was no longer possible, and then the obvious custom naturally came into use, that the too many let themselves be represented by a smaller number; and so one then spontaneously obtained representative bodies in Council or State. And this was all the more necessary because of what was stated in the second place, namely, that one no longer had to reckon only with the village housing, but also with the common interests of a group of villages or hamlets in the same district or province, and furthermore had a share in the care of the interests of an entire People. This goal could not be achieved in large popular assemblies of 50 to 60,000 persons, and therefore one had to resort to elections to appoint a much smaller number of representatives, who would take care of all common interests. It is true that we read in the history of Israel that either a prophet like Samuel or shortly afterwards a Prince summoned the whole People together, but this may not now be understood as if all persons from all tribes flocked together. Such gatherings were popular assemblies, just as the Germans later also knew them, so that one no longer came together with all and sundry, but that the persons entitled to vote in a certain district or province elected from among themselves a smaller number of representatives.

[360]

THE STATES-GENERAL. Persons of significance who had to bear responsibility, gathered on a square or in a forest to unite around their King as their supreme commander. Even Moses had, by God’s command, appointed seventy elders to assist him in the regulation of public affairs, even in matters of legal practice.

§31. Historical Development. This transition now from the original popular assemblies to the representative system did not everywhere come about by choice. That the patriarchate, or even the matriarchate, still had an effect in Israel, and on a very broad scale elsewhere, has already been recalled above; but our history no longer shows any traces of this, as other representative motives were at work among us. A powerful man who, at the end of the Middle Ages, drew an entire region to himself or conquered it, and gave the peasants a piece of his land, was automatically considered the lord of the village, the man who stood up for the village. Alongside this, the clergy steadily gained more representative influence. The cities later came to the Prince as independent creditors. And the industrialists and merchants, who, though still on a very small scale, were spread all over the country, had already long since demanded what one would have thought, certainly Huisman’s suffrage. So the estates of Nobility, Clergy and Burghers gradually and as a matter of course came into being, and it was on these three that the common cause drove century after century, until the first blooming cities brought a breach. Yet the transition to a more personal suffrage, bound only to external characteristics, was not carried through for the whole country until later. In the Constitution of 1815, Article 80 reserved the right to the King to appoint the members of the First Chamber for life, and the members of the Second Chamber were appointed by the Provincial Estates. And if one now asks how the Provincial Estates were then elected, Article 129 gives the answer, that they were elected by the following three estates: 1° the Nobles or Knighthoods, 2° the cities, and 3° the rural estate. The distribution of these seats among these three estates was left to the King by Article 130, and the method of appointing the representatives of these three estates was also entrusted to the Prince in the main.

Analytical Summary: The People’s Representation. It is no different with the People’s Representation. Under the republican form of government, our Commonwealth has not known representation as we understand it today. There were estates, but no People’s Representation; the cities and manorial landowners held the power; the deputies were in no way representatives of the nation before the Government, but rather the installers and maintainers of the Government itself. There was certainly popular choice in the election of the power-holders, but the right to participate in that choice was extremely limited. As shown in the last chapter of the First Part, Calvin did not praise such a system, and ultimately he himself went over to advising in a democratic sense. Whoever now tries to apply the completely antiquated representation ideas of the 16th century to the present state of affairs would thereby show themselves to be anything but faithful to the pure principle, but rather to blatantly disown it. To now still advocate a State form that does not fully do justice to the representative principle, would brand one as backward and out of one’s time. The question of a proper People’s Representation has now itself become the main question for the future development of the State life. And the Calvinist direction, which has always proceeded from the people, and even found its strength in the “common folk,” would lose its inner driving force and let itself be written off, if it did not take the lead rather than follow on this point of our constitutional development in the spirit of freedom and love of the people. To be a Christian Democrat remains the title of honor for every Calvinist. §32. Navy and Army. As a third example, let the National Defense be pointed out. In the days of the Reformation, there was hardly any mention of a national army, as we know it today. The Condottieri had indeed lost ground, but the army with which one went to war still consisted mostly of volunteers offering themselves, and even of mercenary troops from foreign countries, just as we still used to employ, and partly still do, not a few Swiss, Germans and Belgians for our East Indian army. Only of the army of Gustavus Vasa and of the army of Cromwell can it be said that they were driven and urged by a higher and holier interest. For the rest, however, the armies were mostly composed of rough men, and the holy enthusiasm for the cause of the country was sometimes all too little to be noticed. That the same regiment, after the lapse of ten years, drew the sword against a higher national interest, which it had first helped to defend at the price of its blood, occurred more than once. All the more the custom came into vogue with us to assemble the power for the defense of the country’s interests from hired manpower, inasmuch as the navy with us, more than the army, stood in importance, and the manning of the navy, both here and still in England, was constantly recruited by free choice. The civic guard had a more national character, and knew no wages as a motive; but the militia, as it is now in force, first appeared under Napoleon’s domination, and has since also been maintained in our Kingdom. Meanwhile, our people have been far from eager for the new military idea. To become a soldier was not considered an honor, but a plague, and the system of substitutes soon came up to free oneself from this plague by money. It has therefore lasted until the very end of the 19th century before we really got a national army, and the idea of universal military service, which now imposes itself of its own accord, is still not realized with us. Now, because in the glorious days of our fathers the condition prevailed in navy and army that excluded a national army, shall one identify the system then in force with Calvinism, and therefore, as ultra-conservative, set it against the military force that is now so absolutely indispensable? Certainly not. The principle of Calvinism and this is, to highly esteem the significance of the national life; and the future of the national life is now inexorably, at least for small nations, in danger, if not the whole people finally girds the sword as one man for the country’s independence.
[361]

HISTORICAL DEVELOPMENT. - MONEY GAVE THE DECISIVE VOTE. 349 Only for the cities did Article 134 already provide a tax minimum for the choice of the so-called Electoral Colleges. For the rural choice, finally, Article 134 divided each province into districts, without the Constitution giving any further regulation for this. Enough to make one feel how little the People came into their own even in 1815, now just a century ago. It was rather as if everything had been arranged to obtain Colleges, under the semblance of popular representation, on whose composition the People in its more general meaning could not exert any influence worth the name. A States-General in two Chambers, the one entirely appointed by the King, and the other elected by the Provincial Estates, who themselves (except only the cities and even those only partially) came into office outside the People. The one fitted the other as anti-democratically as possible, and therefore as anti-Calvinistically as possible.

§32. Money Gave the Decisive Vote. So it remained until Thorbecke in 1848 cleared away everything that still had a medieval aftertaste, and made money the sole fulcrum of the representative system. Article 76 of the Constitution promulgated that year tied the suffrage for the Second Chamber, and likewise Article 123 the suffrage for the Provincial Estates, “to the payment of a sum in direct taxes, corresponding to the local situation,” ranging from ƒ20 to ƒ160. The Electoral Law provided for the proper implementation of these provisions, and did so in such an indicative anti-democratic direction that the census of ƒ24 in many Frisian municipalities was calculated to favor only a few Liberals. A proposal from the Anti-Revolutionary side, made to reduce this amount, also met with the most vehement opposition from the Liberal side in the Second Chamber, even as late as 1875. Especially in Sneek this gave the most outrageous injustice, just as after 1887, in the new edition of what is now Article 80, in a way that allowed no contradiction, it appeared from the figures on the Liberal and Anti-Revolutionary candidates.

Analytical Summary: Money Gave the Decisive Vote. So it remained until Thorbecke in 1848 cleared away everything that still had a medieval aftertaste, and made money the sole fulcrum of the representative system. Article 76 of the Constitution promulgated that year tied the suffrage for the Second Chamber, and likewise Article 123 the suffrage for the Provincial Estates, “to the payment of a sum in direct taxes, corresponding to the local situation,” ranging from ƒ20 to ƒ160. The Electoral Law provided for the proper implementation of these provisions, and did so in such an indicative anti-democratic direction that the census of ƒ24 in many Frisian municipalities was calculated to favor only a few Liberals. A proposal from the Anti-Revolutionary side, made to reduce this amount, also met with the most vehement opposition from the Liberal side in the Second Chamber, even as late as 1875. Especially in Sneek this gave the most outrageous injustice, just as after 1887, in the new edition of what is now Article 80, in a way that allowed no contradiction, it appeared from the figures on the Liberal and Anti-Revolutionary candidates.
[362]

THE STATES-GENERAL. The convergence on the Census of the entire suffrage question could not surprise the times. With Louis Philippe in France, an official period was ushered in, in which a break was made with all other distinctions, and the money power alone gradually distinguished between citizens and citizens. These thoughts also began to prevail among the Dutch statesmen, even though it is possible that Thorbecke himself had this Mammon service less in mind at the ballot box. Yet it cannot be denied that another intention can preside over the Census. In itself, the thought of paying more in proportion to what one enjoys more from the Government is not an impossible one. A Government, even a Municipal Council, acts to serve the interests of the citizenry. Of what the Government does on this account, one citizen draws much more profit than another. What then would be unfair in letting the less enjoying pay less, but also the more enjoying pay more? Now that what the Government brought about in favor of the subjects required legislative decisions, it seemed desirable to give the preponderance in this decision to those who enabled the Government to do this through their payment of higher taxes. Tax then became the payment sum of what the Government did for their benefit. A less endowed person, who did no business, merely occupied a couple of rooms in a back alley, and had nothing else to lose, hardly enjoyed any profit from all that the Government brought about through its grand measures; but it was quite different for him who needed canals and landing stages for his ships, constant guarding of his wealth, and the opening of roads for his trade and industry. For many, then, no other thought underlay this financial suffrage than that the more the Government did for the benefit of the citizen, that citizen should also pay more of his means to the Government, but that he who contributed the most to the Government should also have the authority to see to it that the Government spent this money properly.

[363]

ANOTHER INTENTION. - OUR PROTEST AGAINST THE CENSUS.

§34. Our protest against the Census. Yet this privilege of money has not held its ground, and already in 1878, that is, thirty years after the introduction of this privilege, Article II of the Anti-Revolutionary Program contained a protest against it, which read as follows: “In order that the States-General may be rooted in the nation, not merely represent the people in name, and in their composition no longer constitute an infringement of the rights of the minorities; the Anti-Revolutionary Party demands the introduction of another electoral system; and as a preparation for this, a lowering of the Census.” Further, one could not yet go at that time. At least not in its program. Article 139 of the Constitution had determined in 1848 that the Census for the Municipal Councils should only be half the Census for the States-General and Provincial. As a result, we were in possession of fixed figures to judge to what result even a halving of the Census would lead. And when it then appeared from these figures how unfairly the Anti-Revolutionaries had been left behind in the matter of the States-General, it was obvious that our first effort was to promote a reduction of the Census figure. From the Liberal side, one constantly tried to keep the high figure above 20 guilders of Article 76. Our endeavor was therefore directed to lowering it as much as possible to the 20 guilders. This has also helped, albeit very partially, and was not without influence on the realization of the revision of Article 76 in 1887. For in that year the Census fell, and we received as a condition of the suffrage in Article 80 “the marks of fitness and social well-being to be determined by the Electoral Law,” on which Mr. van Houten later tried his electoral system. These so-called “marks” were undoubtedly, compared to the Census as the only standard, an improvement. The formula: “You, Government, do this for my benefit, now I pay you so and so much a year to your collector,” was now replaced by the foregrounding of the person. One now only wanted to know which citizens were fit and which were not fit to vote, and then there was also “social well-being,” but this aimed more at his free

Analytical Summary: Keeping Pace with Our Time.
[364]

The States-General sought independence as individuals, not to be made the playthings of others financially, and also to sort out those who possessed the lowest political ideas, or to determine who could be considered to have sufficient social development to possess the most votes. Yet, and this already foretold its failure, the amount paid in taxes still spoke in a very significant way, if not directly, and therein lay the self-evident reason that discontent in the country persisted, even among us. When it came to the implementation of Art. 80, the Anti-Revolutionary Party had to hold more to the line of Van Poortvliet, and resisted the implementation that Van Houten drove through.

§35. The entire People as a People must speak. The search continued for a representation, but always for one that would truly represent the People as a whole and not just an upper stratum. Naturally, the men of the Left increasingly and more unanimously grasped at the so-called Universal Suffrage. Yet it always proved that by means of such an electoral system, a representation of the entire People, not even remotely, was attainable. The data for Germany may serve as an example here. According to the Statistical Yearbook of 1911, the latest in which this subject was treated, page 302 gave the total population of Germany as 60,641,300 souls, and yet in that same year the number of voters was no more than 13,354,900. So something more than a fifth of the population. Nearly four-fifths, or in any case more than three-quarters of the population, remained unrepresented. For it was established that each voter acted on his own behalf, representing no one else but himself. With an individualistic electoral system, one would never, however broadly conceived, come to anything other than a very fractional representation of the Dutch People. According to the latest population figures of 1910 (see Annual Figures p. 7), our population in 1909 showed a figure of

Analytical Summary: Dogmatic and Political.
[365]

NUMERICAL RATIO IN THE ORGANIC SYSTEM. 35,858,175 souls. Of these, 49.9% were of the male sex; that is, 2,899,125. Even if the right to vote were made available at the age of 20, still 1,300,740 persons would have to be deducted from the total as too young. Only 1,598,385 male persons would thus, in the most favorable calculation, come into consideration with us for the right to vote. If one now subtracts the non-valeurs, at least in excluding the destitute, one will not retain much more than 1,300,000 persons available for the right to vote, i.e., still something less than a quarter of the population. Naturally, if one also admitted women to the ballot box, this figure would be more than doubled; but even if one went over to this, one could still never arrive at a representation of the entire People; at most, then two-fifths would be represented. The Universal Suffrage for both sexes also always stumbles on the age limit, and, since it is individual in nature, can never let the non-voting half be represented by the half that votes. For all individual suffrage lacks the element of representation of the one by the other. It is mainly and personally and remains in its effect limited to him who desires it.

§36. Numerical ratio in the organic system.

Analytical Summary: The Shift in England. In the nearly forty years that have elapsed since the first publication of Our Program, yet another complete transformation has taken place, which must equally not be passed over in silence, namely, in regard to our connection with the Anti-revolutionary groups or circles in other countries. Since 1878, a wholly different relationship has also emerged outside our own circle, as far as the foreign countries are concerned, particularly in Great Britain. Gladstone’s death on May 19, 1898, and Chamberlain’s attack in 1900 on the South African Boer Republics are the two historical monuments that have been decisive here. Those who were Calvinists in the democratic sense had often turned their political sympathies towards England, and I too have allowed myself to do so. I did not forget how England had often done us highly unjust competition in the maritime and colonial sphere in the 17th century, nor how England had taken not only Ceylon but even the Cape Colony from us at the beginning of the 19th century. Yet our sympathies still seemed to have to lean towards England. In 1890, Gladstone had published his well-known work: The Impregnable Rock of the Holy Scripture, in which he had advocated more clearly than ever before his steadfast agreement with the Statesmanship that rests on God’s Word. He felt a deep aversion to the Radicalism that was then already creeping into England. He moved unconditionally in the line of the Old Whigs. And without hesitation it must be stated that all that was of Calvinistic origin, both in England and especially in Scotland, supported him. It is true that it already raised some doubts that a man like John Morley, who was Gladstone’s political confidant, moved entirely outside that rock of the holy Scripture, but here too Groen van Prinsterer found support in Allard Pierson, and Calvin was also rehabilitated here in our country by Allard Pierson. Forster’s school policy could serve as an example to us here, and the encounters I had with Forster and other English statesmen, through the mediation of my academic contemporary Lord Reay from Leiden, always warmed my sympathies anew. I felt again and again that, as an English citizen, I would be a Gladstonian, and how, conversely, Gladstone, whatever his ultimate fate may have been on our terrain, was undoubtedly a Groenian.

The ratio stands quite differently, however, if one takes into account the People as an organic whole. It is one that consists of two sexes and perpetuates itself by birth. Now the primordial formation of the People is given in the Family. As Da Costa expressed it, “a heap of souls on a piece of ground” is neither Nation nor People. The fabric for the unity of the people first takes shape in the Family. For in a Family one does not deal with loose and self-standing individuals, but with persons who stand in organic connection with one another. Between these mutually bound persons, fixed relationships exist, which the Higher Ordinance has thus regulated. There is the father and the mother, there are the children, and the latter are brothers and sisters to one another. This family bond brings with it duties with regard to the Family members. Hence the joining together under one Head, who as such is called upon in all civil disputes

[366]

354 THE STATES-GENERAL. To come to the aid of the members, to protect them in their capacity as Heads of Households, and to represent them as needed. The representation of the one by the other, which on an individualistic standpoint can never be anything but nominal and arbitrary, here naturally arises from the organic connection. Give the Head of the Household, in his capacity as such, the right to vote for the States-General, and in him the whole of his Household is represented. According to the annual figures on page 4, in 1909 there were no more than 125,475 persons living separately on their own, and the entire remaining population was divided into 1,181,063 households, i.e., nearly precisely five persons per household. For precisely five, the population, after deducting the separately living persons, should have amounted to 5,779,840, and in 1909 it was only 5,732,700. If, therefore, a general right to vote were introduced by summoning the Heads of Households, then already in this way five and three-quarter million inhabitants would be represented, and not as a mere sum of individuals, but as living in national-organic connection, i.e., as a People. Only a very small part of the People would then fall outside. If, in addition to the Heads of Households, the right to vote were also granted, in connection with social life, to everyone who had gained an independent position in Society and acquired a self-sustaining existence, then in this way a representation of the genuine People would be obtained, which would in fact shield and stand up for its rights and freedoms almost as well as the entire People.

In the Note to Her Majesty the Queen, dated May 13, 1912, which I appended to the Report on the Constitutional Revision, I gave the same argument; only a bit more elaborately. As then, so now my conclusion must remain, that the General Right to Vote leaves nearly three-quarters of our People unrepresented, and that for this reason in the Household Suffrage, which, with the exception of one-twentieth, represents the entire People, a much richer democratic idea speaks, and the national character of the States-General comes to its incomparably better right.

§37. Restore the good money. However fierce the struggle had been, especially from the Socialist

Analytical Summary: The Antirevolutionaries in Germany. In Germany, there was no compensation to be found for this. Stöcker’s appearance did have my warm sympathy, but as I have told Stöcker himself more than once, he failed to take a political stance in a general sense. In the social sphere, he acted energetically and decisively, but his social policy hung too much in the air and lacked a general political foundation. That is why joining his efforts was not possible for us. The Junker parties, the Agrarians, who made up the core of the Conservatives, had increasingly disowned Stahl’s political testament. Von Bismarck’s political conduct moved almost entirely in the sphere of strengthening Germany’s global significance, and although he openly professed, “We Germans fear God and no one else,” the religious vein running through his politics was still strongly tinged. In the May Laws, he even dared to attack the freedom of the Church. With all the admiration for the mighty results he achieved, he could still not elicit any political affiliation from us Antirevolutionaries. The “Reformists,” who were reviving in Germany, did try to approach me once or twice to join their ranks, but their school tactics, their avoidance of all political involvement, and their sober Calvinism seemed to me to promise too little for an attempt at affiliation. In Barmen and Elberfeld, there certainly would have been favorable prospects; but even there, the conditions under which the action took place were too specifically German, and too little principled and comprehensive in a general sense, for me to engage in a closer understanding without suffering harm myself. The Protestant element in general, and the Reformed in particular, lacked entirely what made the Roman Catholic Center in Germany so strong, in that it did not have the courage to act as an independent party. It was therefore understandable that some more decisive men like Kleist-Retzow and Von Gerlach sought to be incorporated into the Center. But this too failed on its own. And so, alas, it must be admitted that the rich prospect that opened up for the powerful formation of an Antirevolutionary party in Germany after Stahl’s appearance has not been realized. Personally, there are thousands and thousands in Germany who are kindred spirits to us and envy our independent party position; but it has not come to an independent formation, and there is hardly any prospect of it happening in the future. Switzerland offers no hope for the future either. In the French cantons, what is outlined in France is repeated in a bioscopic way. The Italian cantons are too small in population to take an independent position. And in the German cantons, it is the Roman Catholic ones where the most emphasis is placed on the honor of one’s own principles, while in the Protestant ones, German theology often marries French political ideas, and thus here too a revival of Calvinism is excluded. One saw it at Calvin’s centenary. Glorification of a name in which part of Switzerland spoke of ancient glories, but of spiritual kinship with the traditions of the man of Geneva, not a trace in all that was manifested and celebrated. Religion and politics here too almost hermetically sealed off from each other. Not even the faintest indication of political action in Calvin’s footsteps. §38. In Roman Catholic lands. In predominantly Roman Catholic lands, there could naturally be no revival of the Antirevolutionary movement. The Roman Catholic Church, with its hierarchical structure and its centralized authority, does not allow the formation of independent political parties within its fold. The Ultramontane movement, which has spread from Rome, has indeed brought about a certain political mobilization, but this has been entirely in the service of the Roman Catholic Church and its interests. The Antirevolutionary principle, which seeks to maintain the independence of the various spheres of life, is diametrically opposed to this. The Roman Catholic Church, with its claim to sovereignty over all of life, cannot tolerate any independent political movement that would limit its power. And so, in Roman Catholic lands, the Antirevolutionary movement has found no foothold. The political activity of Roman Catholics has been entirely absorbed by the struggle for the interests of their Church. The Antirevolutionary principle, which seeks to maintain the independence of the various spheres of life, has found no echo there.
[367]

AGAINST THE GOOD MONEY. 355 On the one hand, as long as the traces of capitalism were bound to the terrain of the Franchise, it did not last long before all who were involved could discern how, now more and more, precisely from the Socialist side, pressure was being exerted to sharply define financial objectives. In the struggle against capitalism, it had gone against an overly empowered right that the men of possession derived from their capital; now the reverse was true—a zeal for Universal Suffrage, in order to thereby gain power, and to divert no small part of the capital of the possessors towards those who were needy or at least less endowed in their lot in life. Increasingly, the striving and intention emerged to raise salaries, stipends, and wages, beyond and in addition to pensions, until the balance between those who had to pay more in taxes and those who had to expect more financial income from the increased tax burden, wavered in pure equilibrium. As long as only the Socialists pursued this goal, there was no danger in it. But gradually, their visions of higher salaries, higher wages, and higher positions served to move not an insignificant number of voters who felt more antipathy than sympathy for the system of Marx, to cast their vote for the Socialist Candidate. This naturally resulted in a loss of votes for the Liberal-Democrats, and thus it explains how they too, gradually, out of a desire for self-preservation, allied themselves with the struggle for higher taxation, in order to be able to meet the demands for higher salaries, wages, and pensions, in the name of their own party. This higher taxation to be able to distribute generously, naturally took on a social character in a general sense; something that led to this very stark contrast, that at the polling booth each voter appeared as an atomic individual, but shortly thereafter in the State’s Housekeeping the entire people were considered as if one firm, which actually had the disposal over the collective capital that the individual members of the community had managed to acquire. That Government would now be governed in its actions and omissions by the parliamentary members coming from the more affluent; but under the inspiration and pressure of those parliamentary members, the expenditures for the benefit of their socially lower-standing fellow-believers would be prioritized, and the government would know how to obtain the necessary funds by levying them from the great treasure-keepers.

[368]

THE STATES-GENERAL. This was a bitter disappointment in the execution of the grand plan, for it became increasingly clear how even the less well-off had to pay dearly, but what remained was the victorious dominance of the idea that the State, according to the demands of the interests of all the less well-off, could freely dispose of the capital or income present in the country in the form of taxes or levies.

§38. In three domains.

Analytical Summary: In Roomsche landen. In overwegend Roomsche landen kon natuurlijk van een weer op-bloeien

This social fermentation even worked in three domains: in the Municipal Councils, in the Provincial States, and in the States-General. What had thus brought a certain limit to the grasping and at least a certain moderation in the levying, was the control of the Provincial Executive and partly of the First Chamber, but it became increasingly clear that there was a tendency to weaken this control in every possible way, and then, though in reverse order, the former regular manner of the well-to-do ran out on the new regular manner of the less well-to-do. If one accepts the system that the State must provide for all needs in every emergency, and may take the necessary money for this entirely at its own discretion wherever it is to be found, then for the second time the money question becomes the decisive factor and governs the whole of politics. As long as society is divided into two parts, of which the one disposes of everything, while the other receives no more than a—x, this financial struggle cannot come to rest until both parts, as far as the financial position is concerned, have been brought into equilibrium. Of course, no one outside the Socialist circle intends this to continue in a straight line. But the question remains: Will this come to an end? And will the pressure among the less well-off, who are the majority, not ultimately prove irresistible? A State that proceeds to direct distribution, not as an exception but as a fixed rule, like the proposed State pension of Mr. Treub, has lost its power over the rudder of the ship; and drifts with the winds, with those winds. And yet this is still only the beginning of the matter. It is only in Socialism that this system finds its final form. For then the household becomes like one mighty national association that swallows up all the individual self-sufficiency that was once guaranteed.

[369]

IN THREE DOMAINS. - WOMEN’S SUFFRAGE. 357

In the insurance system, the contribution covers the costs, in the State pension the State is the distributor. And it is precisely this distribution that, once ripened as a principle, will continue to penetrate further and grasp more broadly. The distribution will become ever younger, ever milder, ever freer, and there will be no more talk of control.

§39. Women’s suffrage. On our part, there can be no talk of excluding women from all involvement for the benefit of what is called “the community.” From 1 Tim. V:9 ff. it appears that this was condemned from the outset in Christ’s Church. Also in Jesus’ immediate environment, it is women who take on this serious task of securing an economic existence, without their own home, for the Savior, and at the open grave their role has also been an important one. The Apostle of Tarsus in particular repeatedly points to various women who held a position of significance in the church life. And in no case can it be said that Scripture shuts up the woman, as it were, entirely within her own family. Miriam, the sister of Aaron, and Deborah, from the time of the Judges, were women of far-reaching influence. And even in our own day, there would be a deficiency in social life if no women offered themselves for education, for hospitals, for the Red Cross, and so much more. One must even go further. Also in public administration, where particularly the interests of women and children are at stake, it has always proved very desirable to seek advice and guidance from the female side, and it would certainly not be a loss, but a gain for public life, if what was intended with the office of the Deaconesses in the early Christian Church were also followed up officially. Already in the Child Welfare laws this proved to be impossible among us, and in all that has an educational, guiding and caring character in matters which also have the woman and the child as their task, it has proved more and more desirable to have female assistance at one’s disposal as well. What we so sorely need is a return to the old Diaconate, if you will, in Church and State alike.

Analytical Summary: Russia.
[370]

THE STATES-GENERAL. In a study that appeared in 1914 at my publisher Kok in Kampen, under the title: The Honorable Position of Woman, I have sufficiently elucidated this theme, so that I will be allowed to refer to it now. Gifts and talents have been bestowed upon the woman, which fade and impoverish if she withdraws from her own peculiar terrain, on the domain of the man, the man tries to imitate. On her own terrain, far the greater part of the man, she will always appear the lesser on the terrain of the man.

§40. State of Strength and Weakness. There are two phenomena in life: the one of full power, the other in weakness. This latter can have three causes: first, that the person is still a child and has not yet grown up to adulthood; the second, that the person is sick; and the third, that he lost his father before he came of age. In each of these three cases, the woman appears as the natural caretaker: as a mother with her child, as a nurse with the sick, and as a substitute father with the orphan; and in each of these three case-positions, the regiment is with her. On the other hand, when dealing with the adult person, with the normally healthy person, and with a household that still possesses its father, the regiment does not rest with the woman, but with the man, and this by virtue of the fact that the man, as a rule, is the stronger in physical strength, in mental power, in willpower and in readiness to act. According to this self-evident rule, it then also becomes clear in what and to what extent the leadership belongs to the woman, and where not. From this it follows that there can be no question of active or passive suffrage for the States-General for the woman; nor for the Provincial States; and as for municipal administration, she may well be called upon to perform various functions, but not in the government itself. This also remains the case in the ecclesiastical domain. The government of the Church is with the Elders, teaching or ruling, and the woman can only appear as a deaconess in the Diaconate, since the Diaconate is not part of the church government, but only of the ecclesiastical care. This does not mean, however, that the Anti-Revolutionary Party will always succeed in permanently opposing the introduction of active and passive suffrage.

Analytical Summary: State of Strength and Weakness. There are two phenomena in life: the one of full power, the other in weakness. This latter can have three causes: first, that the person is still a child and has not yet grown up to adulthood; the second, that the person is sick; and the third, that he lost his father before he came of age. In each of these three cases, the woman appears as the natural caretaker: as a mother with her child, as a nurse with the sick, and as a substitute father with the orphan; and in each of these three case-positions, the regiment is with her. On the other hand, when dealing with the adult person, with the normally healthy person, and with a household that still possesses its father, the regiment does not rest with the woman, but with the man, and this by virtue of the fact that the man, as a rule, is the stronger in physical strength, in mental power, in willpower and in readiness to act. According to this self-evident rule, it then also becomes clear in what and to what extent the leadership belongs to the woman, and where not. From this it follows that there can be no question of active or passive suffrage for the States-General for the woman; nor for the Provincial States; and as for municipal administration, she may well be called upon to perform various functions, but not in the government itself. This also remains the case in the ecclesiastical domain. The government of the Church is with the Elders, teaching or ruling, and the woman can only appear as a deaconess in the Diaconate, since the Diaconate is not part of the church government, but only of the ecclesiastical care. This does not mean, however, that the Anti-Revolutionary Party will always succeed in permanently opposing the introduction of active and passive suffrage.
[371]

STATE OF STRENGTH AND WEAKNESS. - THE WOMAN IN OFFICE. Even then, it will be able to exert influence on the way this will happen. And then it should be clear that on our part there should be no petty quibbling, but that on that occasion we should insist on the broadest possible conception. If the atomist succeeds in establishing the purely individualistic system as undisputed rule, then it is precisely in this atomistic system that the woman should not pass over the electoral terrain as an exception, but as a rule. If the woman has to appear on an equal footing with the man, then let it be so: all women alongside almost all men. Not what is now intended: alongside all men some women; the married as well as the unmarried. And this not as if our conviction did not in principle exclude all female voters, both active and passive, but because, if this were to have no other effect than to make the unbelieving or unbelieving woman vote, and to prefer the woman who has turned to Liberalism or has already gone over, then this would be an undesirable effect. The position that the widow occupies in this is of course entirely separate. If her husband passed away, she automatically takes on the role of head of the household, and in this capacity, too, she must fulfill her duty at the ballot box, just like the male heads of households, either by appearing in person or by obtaining the right to transfer the powers granted to her to a man.

§41. The Woman in Office. Of a completely different nature is the question whether the woman is inclined to take up all kinds of positions; as a doctor, as a lawyer, as a clerk, as the head of a business, and so on. The answer to this is that in a regular state, i.e., if marriage for young men and women is the rule, the family life makes it desirable to relieve the woman as much as possible from other service in private and public life. That the woman in the lower classes of the people so often has to hand over her family cares to others in order to help provide for the necessities of life is a deeply regrettable situation. That the woman in her family can also produce marketable goods with the needle or the loom is presented to us in the image that

Analytical Summary: Radicalism in France. With France, our relationship could not be other than strained. Not because the Monarchy gave way to the Republic. We ourselves have been a Commonwealth, and our country has been able to fulfill the most glorious role in Europe’s history precisely in that Commonwealth form of government. The form of government decides nothing here in the least. It is precisely with the United States, who also chose the republican form of government, that we feel the closest political kinship. A Calvinist can never forget that in Geneva there was no talk of a Monarchy, and that Calvin himself lived in a Commonwealth, so to speak. What makes reconciliation with the French form of government unthinkable for us is solely this, that France still moves along the straight line for which Rousseau first indicated the two fixed points, and that line is precisely the line of Unbelief and Revolution. It is perfectly true that first Napoleon, and after him both the returned Bourbons and what came after them, have in many ways deviated from that line. But the fact remains that the basic direction of France’s political development has been and still is the direction of Unbelief and Revolution.
[372]

The States-General—King Lemuel in Proverbs 31:10–31 portrayed the “virtuous housewife” in an attractive manner. The condemnation of all domestic industry, as is now the order of the day, certainly has its dubious side. Not only does household production kill the woman, but the deplorable abuse that the powerful shopkeeper makes of it cries out for vengeance. It remains highly necessary, therefore, that stricter control here ward off the abuse, and that all direct relationship between the one who orders and the one who delivers be under supervision. And as for the unmarried woman, it goes without saying that for the young daughter who can no longer lean on parental care and does not possess enough of her own capital, the things to which she may aspire are self-evident. So she will strive to become a governess and teacher, a nurse and midwife, a maid and seamstress. But this cannot remain the case, at least not if the extravagance and immoral practices of men continually increase the number of young women who cannot marry. Even in earlier years, when marriage was the rule for all, there was already a certain need for the woman who was about to marry, but for the moment still stood on her own, to provide for her own necessities. But now the number of young women who either do not marry at all or do not marry at all is, at the very least, quadrupled. Not so much in the lower classes of society, but very certainly in the higher classes. The man then shrinks from the expensive household that would force him to content himself with a less fine cigar or a less fine suit of clothes, and immoral mistresses or public women offer themselves to lead such respectable men into sin, which they now miss due to the lack of marriage. The deficiency in moral conception comes from both sides. The young woman is too eager for luxury and adornment, and nourishes too little the strict conception of the relationship between the two sexes that in England is called flirtation. The young man, on his part, misses the luxurious freedom that the quiet family life cannot offer him. And the upbringing of both has deprived them of that stricter conception of social life that naturally directed itself to marriage. The consequence of this is that the throng of unmarried young women continually expands. The male sex is already less and less

[373]

THE WOMAN’S CALLING - PUNISHMENT FOR NEGLECTING TO VOTE The number of representatives is still less than the female sex. But this difference is further increased by the less healthy life of the man, and in years as we now struggle through, by the tens of thousands of those enlisted in the Navy and Army who are killed and wounded. Now it would be highly unjust, in such a state of affairs, to want to deny women the right, by seeking all kinds of offices and occupations, to strengthen their social position. The contradiction here is, however, that the competition they thereby inflict on the young men whose places they take, further diminishes marriages. Here speaks a consequence of the social decadence that punishes itself in every way. Only a return to a more religious, more ethical, more earnest and more simple life could bring salvation, and the possibility at least exists that the social disruption that awaits us as a result of the world war can work this better end. In the first Explanation of Our Program in 1878, I have added a brief exposition under the title: Anti-revolutionary also in Your Household. You will find this study printed after Our Program, in the 5th edition on pages 410–476. Let me be permitted to refer to this also for the Women’s Question. Finally, the otherwise superfluous remark suffices here, how it is so ordained from on High, that even among women writers there are those who are of significance; that poetesses have arisen among them who are beloved by the people; and that in the musical and other artistic fields, the woman still constantly produces something excellent that bears a distinctive stamp and could not have been given to us by any man. These, however, are exceptions, by which the general rule for the life of women must never be judged. By wanting to masculinize the common woman, one robs our human existence of a wealth of life of its own kind, which the feminization of the man can never make up for. The effemination and enervation of the manly is a second bankruptcy that the denaturalization of the woman drags in its wake.

§42. Punishment for Neglecting to Vote. In the Government’s proposals for a constitutional revision, which were recently...

Analytical Summary: Disappointment. The disappointment brought upon us by France was thus complete. As followers of Calvin, nothing could have been more congenial and dear to us than to incorporate, in tempering the one-sided Germanic element in our national character, the French variation as well. Already under the Burgundians, the first germination of this had manifested itself, and even in our language the admixture of a French element is unmistakable. They are not all Gallicisms that one wants to banish from our language as such. Words like: pleasure, umbrella, province, fruit and the like have, by introducing variations in meaning, enriched our language. The affinity between the Swiss-German and our Dutch also points to the fact that, in contrast to High German, Low Dutch prefers to maintain itself through this French admixture. Through Calvinism, had it been able to gain a foothold in France, this intrusion of a Romance variation into our people would likely have truly enriched us. Yet it was not to be. The Bartholomew’s Night has given us the Huguenots, which of itself brought a breach with France.
[374]

The States-General, as the last member of Article 80, proposed: “The obligation to make use of the right to vote can be imposed by law.” For the time being (i.e., in the then-current draft that has since been amended), compulsory voting was not yet introduced, but it was intended to make the introduction of it possible by ordinary law. Here too, an important subject came up in the political struggle. Dr. Hermann Rehm, professor at Strasbourg, stated in his latest work: Germany’s Political Parties (1912) on page 130, the dispute that arose here so accurately, when he distinguished between two kinds of political aims: On the one hand, the desire to stand up for the rights of the people, and on the other hand, to strengthen the power of the government, and with a view to this, he stated firmly that whoever wishes to plead for the rights of the people must oppose compulsory voting, but that, conversely, whoever wants above all to strengthen the power of the government can find a welcome means to achieve his goal. History also teaches that compulsory voting had already been introduced at an early stage in some of the American colonies of the British Empire, in order to hold them more firmly in hand. This was particularly the case with Delaware, Virginia and Maryland. However, as soon as these colonies had not yet come to independence and become free states, they abolished this power instrument that no longer fitted their new existence, and left the voters free. And so in America one still stands so firmly on this point, that when in 1889 in Kansas City, a city in Missouri, the absence from the ballot box was to be punished with a fine of two dollars, the Supreme Court did not uphold this decision as contrary to the Constitution. Free America has always resisted coercive measures, and this despite the fact that in some cities the number of voters who turned up for municipal elections had sometimes even fallen to 30 percent. Ostrogorski, in his La Démocratie et l’Organisation politique, 1903 II 340, also notes that this unwavering adherence to the law of freedom speaks all the more strongly in America, since here, more than in Europe, the well-to-do in particular deliberately keep themselves away from the ballot box. He points to a voter who boasted that he could testify: “I am proud to say, I have not voted for twenty years, and I have made a vow never to vote again!”

[375]

THE REPORT OF 1910 - COMPULSORY VOTING. 363

Also in the report of the Revision Commission of 1910, compulsory voting came up to the extent that the introduction of compulsory voting would be a measure that could only come up for consideration after the acceptance of Proportional Representation. Compulsory voting was only judged favorably to the extent that its introduction could put an end to “some less praiseworthy voting practices.” In particular, Mr. G.W. van der Feltz urged in his Note appended to the Report the combination of these two thoughts, but apparently based on a view that does not fit our constitutional law. This becomes clear from his speaking not of popular representation, but of representation of the State (see p. 12). He apparently regards the elections in the State, Province and Municipality as all three leading not to representation of the People in Parliament, in the States and in the Municipal Council, but as representation, literally as it stands, of the State, of the Province, of the Municipality, a system that our constitutional law does not know. In the Provincial States and in the Municipal Councils, administrative bodies appear, while in the States-General the representatives of the People appear precisely to control the administration and to speak and decide in defense of the rights and freedoms of the People.

§44. Compulsory Voting. Compulsory voting has thus found much less favor abroad. Belgium and Switzerland were mainly the two small states that first brought this new electoral device to broader discussion. It is since then that one has become more accurately aware of the significance that this electoral artifice can have. Especially in America, where the right to vote is so highly valued, compulsory voting has always been rejected with great determination.

Analytical Summary: Compulsory Voting. Compulsory voting has thus found much less favor abroad. Belgium and Switzerland were mainly the two small states that first brought this new electoral device to broader discussion. It is since then that one has become more accurately aware of the significance that this electoral artifice can have. Especially in America, where the right to vote is so highly valued, compulsory voting has always been rejected with great determination.
[376]

THE STATES-GENERAL.

imprisonment, and so it was here in our land as well, with its supporters and opponents. What the fundamental dispute was in this case is self-evident. Whoever descended from the State-idea to the People was in favor of it, and whoever, conversely, ascended from the People-idea to the State, set themselves against it. The modern State-idea tends to see the People’s representation as nothing more than a self-offering aid to smoothly run the State machinery, if I may so express it. If the People are still underdeveloped, they can also remain politically immature, and the State authority can do everything that needs to be regulated by itself. On the other hand, if a certain political development has come to fruition, it can be more profitable for the State to invoke the cooperation and support of a People’s representation. Now, if a Parliament enters this line of thought, it can only serve as an aid to the State authority, and this State authority itself has to determine in what way, to what extent and in what form it wishes to use this support. The Parliament is then not an institution to defend the rights and freedoms of the People against the State authority if necessary, but an aid that the State authority itself desires and calls into being, to support it. From this it follows naturally that if the State authority declares by law that it needs this support, the voters are then also obliged to provide this support, and that whoever as a voter stays at home, withholds from the State what it needs, does not respond to the purpose for which he was made a voter, and thus falls short in fulfilling an obligation placed upon him. And hence the action of the State against the negligent and duty-forgetful voter, so that it is the State that then punishes this neglect of duty, and tries to prevent this by the threat of punishment.

§45. The modern State doctrine and this. Whoever is devoted to this modern State doctrine, does not yet need to introduce compulsory voting, but is nevertheless fully justified according to its starting point. Just as with military service, the State can then also impose compulsory voting on citizens for the electoral campaign, and punish them for neglect of duty. But directly opposite to this stands the regulation in the electoral field, which arises from the respect for the rights and freedoms of the People, as being the inherent competence of this People. Then the State authority has to negotiate with the representatives of the free People, and there can be no talk of a duty covered by punishment to go to the ballot box. Well, of course, in a moral sense, insofar as someone who stays at home neglects to cooperate in maintaining the proper course of state policy, but not legally. Whoever has the right of intervention in any matter whatsoever, leaves this right unused as long and as often as he himself thinks that it will also go well without his intervention. Formally, it is then up to him to decide whether at a given moment he will consider any intervention on his part superfluous and avoid wasting time.

Analytical Summary: The modern State doctrine and this. Whoever is devoted to this modern State doctrine, does not yet need to introduce compulsory voting, but is nevertheless fully justified according to its starting point. Just as with military service, the State can then also impose compulsory voting on citizens for the electoral campaign, and punish them for neglect of duty. But directly opposite to this stands the regulation in the electoral field, which arises from the respect for the rights and freedoms of the People, as being the inherent competence of this People. Then the State authority has to negotiate with the representatives of the free People, and there can be no talk of a duty covered by punishment to go to the ballot box. Well, of course, in a moral sense, insofar as someone who stays at home neglects to cooperate in maintaining the proper course of state policy, but not legally. Whoever has the right of intervention in any matter whatsoever, leaves this right unused as long and as often as he himself thinks that it will also go well without his intervention. Formally, it is then up to him to decide whether at a given moment he will consider any intervention on his part superfluous and avoid wasting time.
[377]

THE MODERN STATE DOCTRINE AND THIS. - THE CALVINISTIC STANDPOINT.365From a right of this nature, insofar as it is perceived not as a favor granted to him, but as inherent in his free position, a duty threatened with punishment can never, not even with the semblance of logical consequence, be derived.

§46. The Calvinistic standpoint. The Calvinist is therefore already offended by the very idea of compulsory voting. For it proceeds from the for him unacceptable thought that the right to vote would be a gift granted to him by the State, which it could withhold from him and which it could bestow upon him, depending on whether the State authority deemed it advisable. Of course, the distinction in degrees according to the degree of development also applies here. A People who are still children cannot yet exercise their innate rights themselves, and thus stand either under parental care or under guardianship. But as the child grows up to be a man, the man exercises his rights himself, not because father or guardian now offer them to him as a gift, but because they now belong to him. Formally, this of course happens by way of legislation. He cannot take his right to participate in the electoral process himself, as one entitled to it. But this only concerns the form of entering into his rights that belong to him. The form does not bind the principle. Even if the percentage of voters who show up were to drop far below half, there would still be no question of their having forfeited their right.

Analytical Summary: The Calvinistic standpoint. The Calvinist is therefore already offended by the very idea of compulsory voting. For it proceeds from the for him unacceptable thought that the right to vote would be a gift granted to him by the State, which it could withhold from him and which it could bestow upon him, depending on whether the State authority deemed it advisable. Of course, the distinction in degrees according to the degree of development also applies here. A People who are still children cannot yet exercise their innate rights themselves, and thus stand either under parental care or under guardianship. But as the child grows up to be a man, the man exercises his rights himself, not because father or guardian now offer them to him as a gift, but because they now belong to him. Formally, this of course happens by way of legislation. He cannot take his right to participate in the electoral process himself, as one entitled to it. But this only concerns the form of entering into his rights that belong to him. The form does not bind the principle. Even if the percentage of voters who show up were to drop far below half, there would still be no question of their having forfeited their right.
[378]

It is not permissible to deprive a municipality or district of the right to vote for a certain period, if not at the price of being placed on the list. But to compel complete turnout by threat of punishment is in irreconcilable conflict with the very principle of representative government of the people. This is felt all the more when one compares the voters who always turn out with the many who stay at home and especially the never-voting citizens, in terms of their moral value for the politics of the country. Those who come out of themselves, or under the encouragement of their organization, participate in politics; the stay-at-homes, on the other hand, who are almost never present, have no heart for it and know nothing about it. How could it ever be according to reason and right that the decision over the politics of your country should be placed in the hands of these citizens who are so politically indifferent, merely by means of compulsion under threat of punishment? And yet this would be the outcome, especially for us. For the parties of Right and Left often stand so nearly equal in the electoral figures, that compulsory voting and a new number of indifferent voters at the ballot box would easily surpass the figure of the difference between Right and Left. The final decision would thus in fact be placed in the hands of those who had forfeited all right to have a say in politics. It could happen that the groups of the Right would attract a greater number of these politically indifferent ones than the groups of the Left, and this alone explains why compulsory voting has sometimes even found supporters among the advocates of the Right; but to go by this would become a hunt for success, in which the principle might well be betrayed. We could therefore not recommend cooperation in the introduction of punishment for failure to vote.

§47. The Electoral System for the Provincial States. Once the so-called Universal Suffrage has gained ground, no more difference can be conceived between the right to vote for the States-General and the States of the Province. The only distinction that strikes us here is that the division of the electoral districts is not the same for both elections. This is in the first place

Analytical Summary: The Electoral System for the Provincial States. Once the so-called Universal Suffrage has gained ground, no more difference can be conceived between the right to vote for the States-General and the States of the Province. The only distinction that strikes us here is that the division of the electoral districts is not the same for both elections. This is in the first place
[379]

VOTING WISELY FOR THE PROVINCIAL STATES. 367 This is due to the fact that the districting for the Second Chamber has been shifted across the boundaries of the Provinces, and secondly, that for the Provincial States the rural population takes more of a leading role than in the largest cities. Not that their interests would be systematically taken into account. For that, too much power has been taken away from the Provincial States out of a kind of jealousy. In between, the regulation of roads and canals was left mainly to the States, and since this subject is of such high importance for the rural population, it is quite understandable that the districting that still largely applies to the Provincial States has a modified character.
