And this all the more so, since in the elections for the Provincial States there was hardly ever any mention of a distinct program by the various parties. Especially in the last half-century, a distinct provincial program has fallen away with almost all parties. In one province, for example in Groningen, this was often different, but in general it cannot be denied that since the seventies of the previous century, the elections for the Provincial States have been almost entirely dominated by the choice of members for the First Chamber entrusted to the Provincial States. This has led to the fact that in the choice for the Provincial States one almost solely asked oneself how one could best combine and on what candidate the association should focus in order to bring a member into the First Chamber, from whom it might be expected that he, in general, would profess one principle. Of course, it would be most desirable if the organic integration of the various provincial expressions of life also governed the electoral law for the Provincial States. If agriculture, industry, shipping, and so on could generally bring their representation into the States, it would give the States an entirely new life and do much better justice to the care of the social condition than is now the case. As long as this remains excluded, and the First Chamber is chosen by our States, we will remain in the same quagmire in which we have been struggling for half a century. It has been suggested whether the members of the States of all the Provinces together, as in one electoral college, could not provide the members of the First Chamber, but this at least seems

[380]

THE STATES-GENERAL. For now, not to proceed. Adding up our provincial colleges, we count 590 members. Now they wanted these almost 600 members to cooperate in the election of members for the First Chamber; the consequence of which could easily be that a majority of 291 electors formed themselves from all the Provinces, causing the First Chamber to consist entirely of one identical political direction, so that the minority would be completely excluded. Others then tried to introduce more proportion in the joint election. It can be maintained with certainty, that, if the atomistic system also remains valid for the Provincial States, election per province is the only tolerable one. There will then still be indirect elections, but the difference between North Holland and Gelderland, for example, or also between Drenthe and Utrecht, will remain strong enough to offer a chance to both main directions, taken over the whole country. Only Proportional Representation could also provide help here.

§48. MUNICIPAL COUNCILS. As for the Municipalities, not all chance has yet been cut off to lay more emphasis on their autonomy. We have already indicated how absolutely necessary it was not to lump together the Municipal Councils in our large cities and villages. It may well prove desirable to divide the Municipalities, as regards their administration and the choice of the citizens who will acquire the right of co-decision, for example into four categories. But then, as far as the large Municipalities of the first or highest class are concerned, it must be closely examined whether there will also be self-control of the citizenry, or whether the control against abuse and mismanagement will have to come exclusively from the higher Authorities. If one follows the system that has also been introduced here from the time of the French Revolution, one comes to an autonomy that is little more than mere appearance, with, on the other hand, a central control from above, which in fact leads the administration of the Municipalities to the Central Government of Home Affairs, via the Provincial Delegates. If one now rejects this un-Dutch system and returns to an autonomous municipal life, then naturally the control of the Central Government must only occupy a modest place, and self-government must be so arranged that one can leave it to itself without fear of confusion or injustice. But then, and this is what matters here, in the administrative regulation of the Municipalities of the first class, the indispensable control must also be able to arise from the Municipalities themselves. The Municipalities are then not pieces or parts of the country, but independent political appearances, which must possess both elements within themselves, the element of Administration and the element of control over this Administration, and both, the Administration as well as the Administrative control, must arise from the Municipalities themselves and not be imposed from above. This, however, brings us to the question of whether the action of a single college in the Municipality can suffice. In the national government, one distinguishes between the Executive power and the Popular control, and in the Popular control one commonly distinguishes, moreover, between a Second and a First Chamber, a Parliament and a Senate. The distinction between Administration and Popular Representation, and in that Popular Representation between a Lower and an Upper House, a Congress and a Senate, or, as with us, between a First and a Second Chamber, is intended to provide a safeguard against reckless, ill-considered actions.

Analytical Summary: MUNICIPAL COUNCILS. As for the Municipalities, not all chance has yet been cut off to lay more emphasis on their autonomy. We have already indicated how absolutely necessary it was not to lump together the Municipal Councils in our large cities and villages. It may well prove desirable to divide the Municipalities, as regards their administration and the choice of the citizens who will acquire the right of co-decision, for example into four categories. But then, as far as the large Municipalities of the first or highest class are concerned, it must be closely examined whether there will also be self-control of the citizenry, or whether the control against abuse and mismanagement will have to come exclusively from the higher Authorities. If one follows the system that has also been introduced here from the time of the French Revolution, one comes to an autonomy that is little more than mere appearance, with, on the other hand, a central control from above, which in fact leads the administration of the Municipalities to the Central Government of Home Affairs, via the Provincial Delegates. If one now rejects this un-Dutch system and returns to an autonomous municipal life, then naturally the control of the Central Government must only occupy a modest place, and self-government must be so arranged that one can leave it to itself without fear of confusion or injustice. But then, and this is what matters here, in the administrative regulation of the Municipalities of the first class, the indispensable control must also be able to arise from the Municipalities themselves. The Municipalities are then not pieces or parts of the country, but independent political appearances, which must possess both elements within themselves, the element of Administration and the element of control over this Administration, and both, the Administration as well as the Administrative control, must arise from the Municipalities themselves and not be imposed from above. This, however, brings us to the question of whether the action of a single college in the Municipality can suffice. In the national government, one distinguishes between the Executive power and the Popular control, and in the Popular control one commonly distinguishes, moreover, between a Second and a First Chamber, a Parliament and a Senate. The distinction between Administration and Popular Representation, and in that Popular Representation between a Lower and an Upper House, a Congress and a Senate, or, as with us, between a First and a Second Chamber, is intended to provide a safeguard against reckless, ill-considered actions.
[381]

§49. LACK OF SELF-CONTROL. The same need naturally also makes itself felt in the field of the Municipal Council, but there the internal control is completely lacking, as a result of which it is that from above, again and again, warnings must be given and action taken against the evil, and all such control naturally almost completely nullifies the autonomy of the Municipality. Here, therefore, a choice must be made. One must know what one wants. Municipalities as parts of the State, without any noteworthy autonomy, and completely subject to the discipline of the Central Government, or free Municipalities with far-reaching autonomy, but then also with the necessary and indispensable control from their own bosom. In Rome, they had therefore a distinction between Senate and Tribunate. Also in the Italian cities of the 12th and 13th centuries, they created such a

Analytical Summary: LACK OF SELF-CONTROL. The same need naturally also makes itself felt in the field of the Municipal Council, but there the internal control is completely lacking, as a result of which it is that from above, again and again, warnings must be given and action taken against the evil, and all such control naturally almost completely nullifies the autonomy of the Municipality. Here, therefore, a choice must be made. One must know what one wants. Municipalities as parts of the State, without any noteworthy autonomy, and completely subject to the discipline of the Central Government, or free Municipalities with far-reaching autonomy, but then also with the necessary and indispensable control from their own bosom. In Rome, they had therefore a distinction between Senate and Tribunate. Also in the Italian cities of the 12th and 13th centuries, they created such a
[382]

The States-General split the Colleges and thereby gained control over its own bosom, precisely in order to strengthen the autonomy. Here too, it cannot lead to a sufficient restoration of autonomy, unless a distinction is also made in the Municipality between two Colleges - the one the Municipal administrative powers, and the other that steps forward to exercise criticism and control; both arising from the citizenry.

§50. The financial question. We have already pointed out earlier how the financial affairs within the bosom of the Municipality had to lead to such a distinction. If everything remains mixed and intertwined, as it is now, then the choice of the citizenry constantly leads to the composition of a College in which the less well-off form the majority, and such a majority is then naturally inclined to constantly assign more and more to the Municipality for the provision of everything that benefits the inhabitants. The demands placed on a Municipality are constantly growing - of traffic and lighting, of water supply and care of the sick, of public entertainment and poor relief. An ever-larger part of what was otherwise private life is then transferred to the Municipality, and it collects the necessary money for this by way of taxation. By preference, this taxation is then levied pro rata to what is assumed to be the pure income of the citizens. The percentage to be collected for this is preferably levied in a gradually increasing manner. And if there are now among the Municipalities a great many needy and small people, and on the other hand very few rich manufacturers or prosperous landowners, then it comes down to this, that from the coffers of these fortunate ones, more and more money is drawn to fulfill the wishes of the less well-off. This then logically leads to the less well-off constantly appropriating more and more from the coffers of the more well-off, and the more well-off having to pay out more and more for the benefit of the Municipalities. If there are now landowners, then these gentlemen can escape by selling their land and by moving, and this in itself provides a control that the Municipal Council often shrinks from. This, on the other hand, does not apply so freely to a manufacturer. His business ties him often to remain.

Analytical Summary: The financial question. We have already pointed out earlier how the financial affairs within the bosom of the Municipality had to lead to such a distinction. If everything remains mixed and intertwined, as it is now, then the choice of the citizenry constantly leads to the composition of a College in which the less well-off form the majority, and such a majority is then naturally inclined to constantly assign more and more to the Municipality for the provision of everything that benefits the inhabitants. The demands placed on a Municipality are constantly growing - of traffic and lighting, of water supply and care of the sick, of public entertainment and poor relief. An ever-larger part of what was otherwise private life is then transferred to the Municipality, and it collects the necessary money for this by way of taxation. By preference, this taxation is then levied pro rata to what is assumed to be the pure income of the citizens. The percentage to be collected for this is preferably levied in a gradually increasing manner. And if there are now among the Municipalities a great many needy and small people, and on the other hand very few rich manufacturers or prosperous landowners, then it comes down to this, that from the coffers of these fortunate ones, more and more money is drawn to fulfill the wishes of the less well-off. This then logically leads to the less well-off constantly appropriating more and more from the coffers of the more well-off, and the more well-off having to pay out more and more for the benefit of the Municipalities. If there are now landowners, then these gentlemen can escape by selling their land and by moving, and this in itself provides a control that the Municipal Council often shrinks from. This, on the other hand, does not apply so freely to a manufacturer. His business ties him often to remain.
[383]

THE FINANCIAL QUESTION. 37 We now have the control of the Central Administration to prevent excesses in this regard, but just this control carries the evil in itself, that it undermines the autonomy of the Municipality. Hence the desirability, as in the Senate a preservative was created to curb the excesses of Parliament, so also, in the large cities at least, to bring about a split between the members of the administration elected by the citizens and the representation of the inhabitants in that Administration, if necessary in two branches: on the one hand in a College where the tax-payers appear, as elected by the tax-payers, and the other emerging from the entire circle of the inhabitants. Thus alone would, in strict consequence, the so desirable and educating autonomy of the Municipalities be restored to organic power. In the College chosen by all heads of households and free men, then the election of the Mayor could take place, and in the second College, chosen by the taxpayers, then the sitting chairmen or delegates of all kinds of associations in the industrial, social or educational field could sit. The autonomy would then be complete, and the control from above would then only apply to the violation of rights and an active co-living of the citizens could thus develop. Yet such a state of affairs will not be conquered without a hard struggle. The French spirit of centralization, so greatly promoted by our Municipal Act, still works so powerfully. For the lower classes of Municipalities a simpler arrangement would be preferable; but we do not need to express ourselves on this for the time being. Only when the autonomy of the first-class Municipalities has been won, will one also be able to proceed to the liberation of the smaller Municipalities. To what extent Proportional Representation should also be enforced here, at least in the first-mentioned College, is a question that should not be raised until after the experience gained with this proportional system in the elections of the States-General. Hasty application of a completely new system to Colleges of such entirely different nature could later prove to create untenable conditions in practice. Proportional Representation recommends itself primarily there where party life ‘calls for direct choice, so that the indirect choice does not deprive the minority of its influence twice over. This motive is now completely missing in many, especially smaller, Municipalities, notably in North Brabant, Limburg,

[384]

372 THE STATES-GENERAL. North Holland and Drenthe. It therefore remains a very serious question whether such Proportional Representation would immediately suit the demands of life in so many almost homogeneous Municipalities.

§51. Supplement. Separately, the case that is now (i.e. in October 1916) presenting itself should be discussed here, now that we can hardly hope for any more allied cooperation with the entire Right in preparing the struggle for organic Suffrage. This could indeed place us in the most difficult position, that there is no longer any thought of accepting the organic system, and that we are therefore either, as in former days, only concerned with warding off evil, or ultimately had to limit ourselves to tempering. This case position now requires its own explanation, and it is for this reason that we have to add a supplement on the modus quo as a matter of principle. We must be brief here, but we must not fall short in clarity.

Analytical Summary: Supplement. Separately, the case that is now (i.e. in October 1916) presenting itself should be discussed here, now that we can hardly hope for any more allied cooperation with the entire Right in preparing the struggle for organic Suffrage. This could indeed place us in the most difficult position, that there is no longer any thought of accepting the organic system, and that we are therefore either, as in former days, only concerned with warding off evil, or ultimately had to limit ourselves to tempering. This case position now requires its own explanation, and it is for this reason that we have to add a supplement on the modus quo as a matter of principle. We must be brief here, but we must not fall short in clarity.

§52. The course of the question. In the middle of the previous century, Groen van Prinsterer, and so later also our Program, as we have already remarked, could still suffice with a bargaining on the outrageous way in which the Liberal side had applied the property qualification at the time. An own system had not yet been placed by Groen against the liberal system. And although in 1878, at least in our Program, the demand for a different system was clearly expressed, the lines for this better system were still only outlined. The protest was then mainly against the deadly Census for us. And although Groen van Prinsterer later let his protest be heard against any too serious lowering of the Census, yet this resistance also moved within the sphere

Analytical Summary: The course of the question. In the middle of the previous century, Groen van Prinsterer, and so later also our Program, as we have already remarked, could still suffice with a bargaining on the outrageous way in which the Liberal side had applied the property qualification at the time. An own system had not yet been placed by Groen against the liberal system. And although in 1878, at least in our Program, the demand for a different system was clearly expressed, the lines for this better system were still only outlined. The protest was then mainly against the deadly Census for us. And although Groen van Prinsterer later let his protest be heard against any too serious lowering of the Census, yet this resistance also moved within the sphere
[385]

THE COURSE OF THE QUESTION - THE SYSTEM ON TRIAL.

373 of the then prevailing system. Without fear of contradiction, it can also be said that all this previously lamented and occurred belongs to a now completely elapsed period, with which none of us can reckon anymore. What made the Electoral Question so dangerous in our circle in recent years was the event with the draft of Tak van Poortvliet. As everyone still remembers, Tak’s proposal did not at all include universal suffrage. Even in his draft, the right to vote remained subject to very serious restrictions. But against this stood the fact that in Tak’s proposal, albeit indirectly, the Family was referred to, and that this gave it an undeniable attractiveness for those who, like our party in particular, had always pressed for the Family head and the right to vote as the embodiment of a healthier electoral system. Tak’s proposal even favored us in another respect. Not only did it at least partially allow the Family quality to have a say, but it also led to an extension of the right to vote to the lower classes, and could thus lead us to the more Christian-democratic paths in which the promise of the future had always been sought on our side. What then struck so hard was only that the gentlemen De Geer van Jutfaas et al. came out in principle against such a serious extension of the right to vote, already then almost actually separating themselves from us, and, in alliance with the Conservatives under the leadership of Mr. Van Houten, helped to introduce the right to vote that is now in force, which, however, in its effect, increasingly proved to deviate from what the Conservatives, and with them the gentlemen De Geer van Jutfaas et al., had intended in 1894.

§53. The System on Trial. This whole course of events had in the meantime the extremely important consequence that the principled difference between the Revolutionary and Anti-Revolutionary System in the matter of representation came into the arena much more strongly than before. The form in which the system advocated by us took on here was the Household Suffrage, and the way was gradually found that not long afterwards the Family Head Suffrage was also accepted by the Roman Catholic State Party, yes, that even a part of the Christian-Historicals began to show sympathy for it. In 1913 it could then be said that in the matter of the Electoral Arena, system was increasingly set against system. The struggle was then no longer mainly about a greater or smaller number of voters, but about the quality from which the right to vote would arise, and about the way in which it would work. At least the draft contained in the Report of Heemskerk’s Commission clearly set forth the contrast between the two systems. So one felt, back and forth, that a more principled struggle was at hand. Since the defeat at the ballot box in 1913, however, this initial gain has, extremely regrettably, been lost again. Cort van der Linden, who now took the matter of the new regulation of the right to vote in hand, turned out to have taken a stand with both feet on the revolutionary inheritance. Leaving aside additions on Proportional Representation, Compulsory Voting, Women’s Suffrage, etc., what Cort van der Linden proposes is nothing but a continuation of the individualistic drive that has dominated the Electoral Arena on our side ever since Thorbecke. Also what Cort van der Linden now offers us is pure atomism, and even the weakest attempt to do justice again to the organic connection of the people’s parts in the matter of the right to vote remained entirely absent. §54. Two Systems.

Analytical Summary: The System on Trial. This whole course of events had in the meantime the extremely important consequence that the principled difference between the Revolutionary and Anti-Revolutionary System in the matter of representation came into the arena much more strongly than before. The form in which the system advocated by us took on here was the Household Suffrage, and the way was gradually found that not long afterwards the Family Head Suffrage was also accepted by the Roman Catholic State Party, yes, that even a part of the Christian-Historicals began to show sympathy for it. In 1913 it could then be said that in the matter of the Electoral Arena, system was increasingly set against system. The struggle was then no longer mainly about a greater or smaller number of voters, but about the quality from which the right to vote would arise, and about the way in which it would work. At least the draft contained in the Report of Heemskerk’s Commission clearly set forth the contrast between the two systems. So one felt, back and forth, that a more principled struggle was at hand. Since the defeat at the ballot box in 1913, however, this initial gain has, extremely regrettably, been lost again. Cort van der Linden, who now took the matter of the new regulation of the right to vote in hand, turned out to have taken a stand with both feet on the revolutionary inheritance. Leaving aside additions on Proportional Representation, Compulsory Voting, Women’s Suffrage, etc., what Cort van der Linden proposes is nothing but a continuation of the individualistic drive that has dominated the Electoral Arena on our side ever since Thorbecke. Also what Cort van der Linden now offers us is pure atomism, and even the weakest attempt to do justice again to the organic connection of the people’s parts in the matter of the right to vote remained entirely absent. §54. Two Systems.

The question of more or less voters is not what decides the system. Thorbecke’s law shrinks the number of voters to a minimum, while Cort van der Linden wants to expand it as much as possible. The old Heemskerk took a middle position here, and what is now, under Van Houten’s leadership, decisive for the ballot, is just as little, if not different, than a middle position between Thorbecke and Cort van der Linden. For with Thorbecke, with the old Heemskerk and with Cort van der Linden, it is and remains the one and the same starting point, fundamentally an entirely identical system, and in this system advocated by these three statesmen, it still invariably manifests itself as a direct outgrowth of the basic idea of the French Revolution. They are the individual citizens who contractually unite to make rights and order flourish in the State. It is and remains the principled system of the Contrat social. For those who are devoted to this system,

[386]

374 THE STATES-GENERAL.

it is and remains the only possible system. But for those who, like us, have broken with the French Revolution and its individualistic starting point, this system is and remains unacceptable.

[387]

TWO-PARTY SYSTEM - THE OLD PROBLEM REMAINED.

There is no people, there are no races, and there are no families. The proponents of this system recognize no organic bond that has arisen from the past. They know nothing but the sum total of individual citizens. And it is these individual persons who come together, who deliberate together, and who appoint representatives together, and in this way the life of the State must seek its progress. This system was taken over from France into the constitution of the Batavian People, just as it was proclaimed from the porticos of our city halls on May 1, 1798. And although the form in which one clothed one’s ideas has been continually modified since that time, although that form itself has ultimately been squeezed into the corset of Conservatism and thus become a mockery of the past, yet from 1798 up to 1889, not a trace of any modification whatsoever has been made to the principle.

§55. The old problem remained.

Analytical Summary: The old problem remained.

So we now stand again before exactly the same problem that we faced in 1887. There was an atomistic suffrage, and all that has changed in that suffrage is that it will now undergo a certain expansion once again. Our position on this matter in 1887 was that we had indeed tried to establish an organic suffrage, but that when this did not succeed, we thereafter only asked ourselves whether the less was not to be preferred over the more. As always, we then lent our support in a democratic direction. And so now too we would not break with our past if we first tried to work towards an organic suffrage, but then also, if this did not succeed, and it appeared that we could not, as far as the outcome depended on our vote, always operated further in a democratic direction. Whether the suffrage is general or limited, can never decide the matter for us. For us, the principled antithesis remains only an organic or an individualistic suffrage. The individualistic, and thus atomistic, suffrage, we reject as being in irreconcilable conflict

[388]

376 THE STATES-GENERAL. Departing from our principles, and for the organic Electoral Law, as sealed by the Almighty’s governance in the history of the Peoples, we continue to advocate with the utmost earnestness. But it has also become clear again that our powers and influence do not suffice to push through the organic Electoral Law—indeed, that even our allies are almost entirely abandoning the struggle for this organic Electoral Law. We therefore still cling to our starting point, but then, just as in 1875, 1878, and 1887, when the opposing party prevailed, we can persist in championing the democratic principle. In doing so, we do not depart by a single letter from the revolutionary principle that confronts us. Rather, we continue to stand squarely against the system resting upon it, just as we did in 1850, 1875, and 1896. But if we cannot establish our principle, it is certain that it will still end up in an individual Electoral Law, and we are thus ultimately faced with the question of whether this revolutionary-individual, atomistic Electoral Law will be aristocratic, so that the lower classes are pushed back, or democratic, in a healthy sense, so that all parts and members of the demos attain equal rights. As good Calvinists, we then stand diametrically opposed to aristocratic Conservatism, and we plead the cause of the People in all its ranks. It remains as we have always professed. The system of a Personal Electoral Law is no good, but Thorbecke’s law suffered from the same fundamental flaw, and the law of the old Heemskerk falls under the same judgment. All these laws rest on the very same false principle, and yet, with all these laws, we as good Anti-revolutionaries have not sought salvation in mere obstructionism by voting against them, but have participated in the voting in order to strengthen the healthy Democratic element. Therefore, our goal-oriented aim must now be one of two things. Either we must find allies who, together with us, want to take up the plea for the organic Electoral Law, and see the possibility of fielding a Representation in new Chamber elections that will stand in the breach with us for the organic Electoral Law; or, failing that, we must now press for the rejection of the submitted bills, and immediately thereafter bring an organic proposal to the fore. If, on the other hand, it turns out that this is not possible; if it is settled that the indispensable allies for such an operation are lacking

[389]

DRIVE TOWARDS DEFINITIVE REGULATION. 377 not to be found; and in the end it will come down to this, that we will once again, just as three times in the past, only have to decide whether the new Electoral Law will be more Aristocratic-conservative or Democratic in a healthy sense, - then there is no hesitation for us, that we cannot cooperate with the Conservative elements in any case. If it ultimately comes down to a principled struggle, and thus to a choice between the individual or the organic Electoral Law, then we do not yield an inch. But if the parliamentary constellation does not seem to move towards this, and in the end we are also asked to vote on whether our Electoral Law, in any case remaining atomistic, will be limited or broadly conceived, then we will remain what we were from the outset: Christian-democratic even at the Ballot Box.

§56. Drive towards definitive regulation. Yet another attitude would of course be possible. If we cannot find any allied help to ultimately plead our case for the organic principle, and it is nevertheless deemed that the giving of our vote to a draft bill now offered cannot be, then one could still ask whether it would not be a demand of our position to simply cast our vote against the draft bill that will soon be put to the vote in the Second and First Chambers, after having lodged a serious protest. This is after all such an important question that it will be desirable that the opinion of our like-minded people on this be clearly and clearly expressed both in the Central Committee and, if possible, also in the Deputies’ Meeting. Not that we would then have to vote against it in the Second Chamber and for it in the First Chamber. On such a fundamental issue as the Electoral Law, this would not be a stance for a party that honors its past. Now there is of course much that speaks in favor of it, but not less what speaks against adopting this third attitude. In its favor is that we remain on a purer line; do not take responsibility for what does not accord with our conviction; and can proceed more forcefully with recommending what we deem most desirable. But yet again there is so much practical against it, that neither Groen van Prinsterer nor our former predecessors dared to do it, and that one therefore ultimately always chose the second way. Never has the Electoral dispute been put to the vote so far in the States-General, or the members of the Anti-Revolutionary Party have almost unanimously voted with it, and thus tried to achieve a relative gain through their cooperation, which would otherwise have escaped them. Principally, after all, all the draft bills on the Electoral Law over which our men have voted in the past have been just as decisively opposed to our principle as what will now be put to the vote for us, and yet the Anti-Revolutionary Party has never before definitively determined to vote against it. It only did this once, and that was when Van Houten’s Electoral Law was put to the vote. What this means, therefore, is not that it then voted against it because there was too much, but because there was too little offered. To Tak’s draft, which offered more, one could not withhold one’s vote.

Analytical Summary: Drive towards definitive regulation. Yet another attitude would of course be possible. If we cannot find any allied help to ultimately plead our case for the organic principle, and it is nevertheless deemed that the giving of our vote to a draft bill now offered cannot be, then one could still ask whether it would not be a demand of our position to simply cast our vote against the draft bill that will soon be put to the vote in the Second and First Chambers, after having lodged a serious protest. This is after all such an important question that it will be desirable that the opinion of our like-minded people on this be clearly and clearly expressed both in the Central Committee and, if possible, also in the Deputies’ Meeting. Not that we would then have to vote against it in the Second Chamber and for it in the First Chamber. On such a fundamental issue as the Electoral Law, this would not be a stance for a party that honors its past. Now there is of course much that speaks in favor of it, but not less what speaks against adopting this third attitude. In its favor is that we remain on a purer line; do not take responsibility for what does not accord with our conviction; and can proceed more forcefully with recommending what we deem most desirable. But yet again there is so much practical against it, that neither Groen van Prinsterer nor our former predecessors dared to do it, and that one therefore ultimately always chose the second way. Never has the Electoral dispute been put to the vote so far in the States-General, or the members of the Anti-Revolutionary Party have almost unanimously voted with it, and thus tried to achieve a relative gain through their cooperation, which would otherwise have escaped them. Principally, after all, all the draft bills on the Electoral Law over which our men have voted in the past have been just as decisively opposed to our principle as what will now be put to the vote for us, and yet the Anti-Revolutionary Party has never before definitively determined to vote against it. It only did this once, and that was when Van Houten’s Electoral Law was put to the vote. What this means, therefore, is not that it then voted against it because there was too much, but because there was too little offered. To Tak’s draft, which offered more, one could not withhold one’s vote.
[390]

378 THE STATES-GENERAL. What, then, has moved us in these years to adopt this somewhat inconsistent attitude? Namely two things. In the first place, that one did not yet see any chance of getting a principled decision in our spirit, so that for the time being it had to remain with the atomistic Electoral Law, and it was therefore all about bringing as favorable provisions as possible for the “little people” into the law with each new regulation. And secondly, that everything that was bargained for the plutocratic character of our Electoral Law promised our release from oppressive bonds. The past would therefore also now urge us to go along with it, if the struggle for the principle offered no prospect of success, and where one has unfortunately already heard too much from the Roman Catholic side about its divergent intentions, there may well be little prospect that we would now be able to count on stronger allied help for the principled struggle than in the past. One sometimes even gets the impression that part of the Roman Catholic press has completely ceased to reckon with the great problem of organic representation. Something that is disappointing, but over which one need not be too surprised. Do not forget that in Germany and Belgium, from where we have still received so much support in the past, the situation has now become much more unfavorable for us.

[391]

The strongest inspiration for Roman Catholic politics in this country has been that the right to vote has been almost entirely established in an atomistic sense for a long time. One cannot completely disregard the great current of general ideas here. The question of the right to vote has come up in almost every country, and has been decided in an atomistic sense, both in America and in our own part of the world. England held out the longest, but England too is gradually giving in, and it will not be long before the personal right to vote, as a right granted to every citizen, also finds its way into the British Empire. Now, of course, we have been very eager to swim against this current in the Netherlands, but both among the Anti-Revolutionaries and among the Roman Catholics here, the question of the right to vote has come up much too late. The great educational problem also constantly pushed this mighty question aside. And unfortunately, it did not stop there. For when the question of the right to vote has been seriously considered among us since 1875, it has by no means succeeded in placing this electoral problem on the agenda simultaneously and in the same way for all the righteous parties. Yes, what was even more sorrowful, among the Anti-Revolutionaries themselves a certain division soon broke out over this question, and the conservative-aristocratic motive continued to work too strongly in this question. So it ended up in a waste of strength and time, and people imagined that, if the need arose, we would still have plenty of time to realize unity of mind and view, both in our own party and among our allies. But it is precisely here that we are now disappointed. The driving force that constantly pressed the opposing party too strongly for a final solution of the electoral question, has overtaken us. And so a case situation arises in which the Anti-Revolutionary Party, with a view to the future, will have to decide whether, after having never taken a stand before, it now feels strong enough to go against the strong current in isolation.

[392]

380 Justice.

CHAPTER XIII
JUSTICE.

§1. The Domain of Jurisprudence.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.

In the Introduction to this work, on pp. 1–87, the Law in general has been discussed. Here only that law comes up that governs the sphere of Jurisprudence. Now the Government that acts as a judge is by no means the universal or the only and sufficient upholder of the Law. The Law in a universal sense embraces our entire human life, and even partly the life of angels; and this not only for the life they now and then experience here on earth, but for the expression of the self-conscious creaturely life in all spheres. As often as in the self-conscious creature a thought arises, or even a word or an action goes forth, the question immediately arises whether this thought, this word, this deed does or does not correspond to a rule, ordinance or disposition given for it by the Creator. What is in conformity with the will of the Creator is right; what comes into conflict with that will is wrong. But precisely this universal character of the law is the reason why one errs who says that the Sovereign of any country or the judge appointed in that country has to watch over all Law, or would also be the absolute upholder of the Law. The finding, administration and enforcement of the law by the Sovereign or judge on earth can be of a limited nature only; now since the law proceeds from the will of the Creator of all things, God alone is the upholder of the Law in its full extent, and the earthly finder, administrator and enforcer of the Law is only the power appointed by God to assert the Law that is rooted in His will, under certain conditions and in certain relationships. Outside the earthly judge, a threefold law enforcement takes place of itself, which announces itself in the conscience, makes itself felt in suffering and distress, and will one day take vengeance in the final judgment. For the sake of completeness, it must be added that the earthly judge is not the only one who has to do with the law, but that the law also has its own sphere of validity in the life of the family, the church, and other social spheres.

[393]

ADMINISTRATION OF JUSTICE - PUBLIC AND PRIVATE. The absolute Right already found its highest reconciliation on the Cross of Golgotha. But leaving the Cross aside, we then see that the circle of the administration of justice, which the Sovereign exercises on earth, compared to the entire legal sphere, is so small and limited, that the imagination, which not infrequently expresses itself in the conception of the juridical power, as if it were the judge on earth who, by virtue of his own competence or in the name of the Sovereign, appears in every land and among every people to administer justice, judges itself. This can and may only be done by the Creator and Disposer over all self-conscious creaturely life, and to the judge on earth, who is responsible for the administration of justice, the maintenance of the Law is entrusted only in a very limited circle.

§2. Public and Private. Now if this works well, then no one else interferes in it, and it is the father, or in his absence the mother, or also by appointment the older brother or sister, or even by order the teacher or caretaker, who maintains the Law within this small circle. Likewise, it is in private associations or societies, where the members themselves can establish the rules for how things will go, in order to prevent any differences of opinion from being resolved by arbitration or otherwise. On the other hand, for the general society, the Sovereign prescribes, as far as necessary, by law, the regulation of the Law that shall prevail under his sovereignty; with him rests the competence, yes, even stronger, it is his duty to maintain the Law, ordained in that law, against violations and infringements. The reason why his power must be, to punish those guilty of this necessity, and if possible to restore the violated or infringed Law.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[394]

§3. Defective Character. Yet this finding, the administration and maintenance of the Law, always exhibits a defective character. The original direct connection between God’s Law and our consciousness of it has been severed by the Fall into sin in our human self-consciousness. Hence the constant uncertainty and fluctuation in what shall count as Law. Even in our defective state of Law, there would scarcely be any talk, if the consciousness of our human race had not, as a psychological phenomenon, imprinted and rooted this Law in such a way that it is comparable to the life in the plant, which, though it can lose the purity of its growth and development through atmospheric influences, defective soil and insufficient care, yes, through all kinds of harmful influences, yet, under all this, through the inherent nature of the being of the plant, its character remains intact, and strives to restore itself. The Law is a psychological phenomenon in our human race, which gradually unfolds of itself, seeks to restore itself when violated, and gradually gains in influence and power. The Holy Scripture, and in general the Special Revelation, gives only a few fixed indications for this growth in the Law, and it is from the psychological nature of our race that it must each time again let its bud come up and unfold. The all-controlling question now remains only whether the order in this psychological phenomenon comes from God and is established by Him, or whether man has set and can modify this Law according to his own good pleasure and arbitrariness. §4. From Man? This latter is now the most prevalent thought, the consequence of which is that all Law is increasingly lost in a subjective wave and lapses into “shuffling the coins.” This is openly expressed.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[395]

THE HUMAN BEING? - THE FALL OF RIGHTS IN PARADISE. 383 One knows how the call of “property is theft” still finds admirers, how the difference of insight in the question of Right in the same country continues to be disturbing, and how it ultimately depends on the appointment of a professor at one of the universities which concepts of law will prevail for a time in a part of the country. There could then also be no talk of a higher legal order, if the Law itself as a psychic phenomenon in human life did not possess the inner power to continually bend back against all arbitrariness and to gain access. Yet this would not avail us, if the development and unfolding of this inherent psychic order could not begin at least with one fixed point of attachment outside our subjective consciousness; and this it can now only find in what has been revealed to us from God’s side. Just as the plant withers and hides away in the darkness of your cellar, and only then revives and comes into bloom when it is allowed to be irradiated by the light of the sun, so also the right withers and becomes nothing more than an up-and-down fluctuation of consciousness, if it renounces this point of attachment to God’s holy Revelation. This point of attachment need not offer much. All broader elaboration comes of itself from the nature of the psychic phenomenon that we call Right, but just as the plant cannot thrive without a root, and just as that root cannot miss a soil, so also must the Right have its root in God, and the place where the root of the Right is hidden must be assignable in the life-soil. Just as decisively as it must be recognized that it is absolutely unthinkable that we should find the full unfolding of the Right in Holy Scripture, and must be recognized without reservation that the psychic phenomenon of the Right, like the physical life in our veins and blood, possesses an inherent power of development, so also must it be firmly held that the starting point for the administration of justice must also be given to us in God’s Special Revelation.

§5. The Fall of Rights in Paradise. Not as if this had been necessary in itself. In a world without a fall, without sin, of course the pulsation of the breath of

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[396]

384 JUSTICE. the Right would have gone on in perfect purity of its own accord. But just as in illness all kinds of symptoms become noticeable in our body, of which we had not the faintest suspicion, so also it stands in the psychic realm. Without a fall there would be no injustice and thus no reaction against injustice. Now, on the other hand, it is precisely in the reaction against injustice that the sense of right is most strongly awakened. The most worrying thing is now, however, that in our pride and self-conceit we constantly want to determine for ourselves what is right and what is wrong. This constantly puts the Right on shaky ground. This then leads to a collapse of the Right, out of which the question of doubt naturally arises, whether there would really still be any firmness in the Right, and thus also in the administration of justice. And this doubt cannot be removed unless, drifting on the ocean of doubt, you finally find at least one fixed point of attachment, or want one dolphin to which your little ship can be chained. Therefore, never ask the Scriptures for what they cannot give you, and much rather open your eyes to the inner nature from which the plant of the Right always shoots forth its buds anew. But take care that, with all subjective need for clear self-awareness also in the matter of the Right, you never and never let slip the one fixed point of attachment to the administration of justice of your God in His Word. Then you feel the ground sinking under your feet, and you run the risk of soon beginning to weep over the absurdity that sees the robber and murderer in the victim, and in the “brave Henry,” as it is then called, the perpetrator of the injustice that drove the misled to murder and robbery.

§6. Capital Punishment.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.

If Justice is not a power in life that has arisen from subjective human consideration, but an order instituted by God, in which a psychic phenomenon that has been imprinted in our human nature at its creation finds expression, then this Justice works of itself without the fall into sin, but after the fall into sin, in which it was disturbed, it must, by means of an intentional, purely healing-oriented surgical operation, restore it as much as possible to its original state in the meantime.

[397]

CAPITAL PUNISHMENT. The necessity that the psychic order established by God in the right be maintained as much as possible among all peoples through legislation, adjudication, and punishment. This raises the question of capital punishment, which requires deliberate discussion. In 1878, when Our Program saw the light, the view of capital punishment was still almost universal among all peoples in all lands. However, since the last quarter of the previous century, more and more countries have moved towards its abolition. It had already been abolished in Portugal in 1867, in Michigan in 1847, in Rhode Island in 1852, in Wisconsin in 1853, in Venezuela in 1866, and in Romania in 1864. In Switzerland too, it was removed from the Penal Code in 1874, but was reintroduced in 1879, except for political crimes. In Bremen, Oldenburg, Anhalt, and Saxony, capital punishment had also been abolished before 1870. However, the total population of these ten American and European States and Cities was still far too small for one to speak of a general abolition. Since the seventies of the previous century, the movement aimed at abolition has nevertheless gained the upper hand among the circle of legal scholars, and not only among those who do not reckon with a Supreme Judge as the Disposer of all human life, but to a certain extent even in the circles of believing jurists; and this while, conversely, Lombroso, who could see in the murderous instinct nothing but a hereditary phenomenon, peculiar to certain families, precisely urged that the scaffold be used for the extermination of the most dangerous elements in these families. Even a marriage ban in such families was once urged; something that would of course never achieve its purpose, since a marriage ban does not exclude procreation. Leaving these exceptions aside, it can nevertheless be established that the movement against capital punishment has been gaining ground in the scientific-liberal domain; so much so that capital punishment now finds hardly any defenders in legal circles anymore. It is increasingly felt that it does not belong to anyone, and thus not even to the judge, to deprive someone of life; that the severing of life cuts off moral education and improvement; and that the cruelty of public execution rather promotes coarseness of morals than a higher reverence for human life. Emphasis is then placed above all on the fact that it sometimes turned out afterwards how the executed was completely innocent of what was imputed to him. And even more, how bad upbringing, bad company, immoral reading, or lack of the necessary means of subsistence had not merely tempted the unfortunate to his crime, but almost forced him into it, so that our anger should turn much more against the capitalist society that created such deplorable conditions than against the murderer who had become its victim. On the other hand, it was also pointed out from the Methodist side that capital punishment cuts off the possibility of later conversion in the case of an unconverted person.

[398]

§7. The application diminishes. Even where this juridical-political movement has not yet led to the complete abolition of capital punishment, the phenomenon nevertheless manifests itself on an increasingly wider scale that its application is omitted in far the majority of cases. In Prussia, over a period of four years, in which 231 death sentences were pronounced, only 10 of the condemned were executed, which amounts to 8%. In Austria, this percentage remained even below four. In Belgium, hardly a single execution of those condemned to death occurred. And even in England, where one still proceeded most sharply, 154 of the 299 condemned were spared execution in the course of ten years. Something that speaks even more strongly, since even the hanging of women had occurred nine times in that period in England. Against this stood the fact that, as also with us, every Government maintained capital punishment for its Colonies, and that in most of the countries that went over to abolition, an attack on the life of the Sovereign remained most threatened with death. In fact, the diminishing significance of capital punishment went so far that, even though it remained in the law, it was often set aside by the judge, excluded by the jury, or pardoned by the Sovereign where it had been pronounced.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[399]

HUMANISM AND SCRIPTURE - GENESIS IX:5,6.387

§8. Humanism and Scripture. The motive for this ever-expanding movement lay in Humanism, as the influence of Christianity was increasingly supplanted by a certain cultural power in the estimation of most. One could no longer reckon with God’s right. In fact, the very concept of rights also fell away, as the conviction gained ground that one should be much more concerned with the moral improvement of the guilty. Something to which was still often added that the execution did not help, since the so-called deterrence theory apparently did not hold up. For the statistical data showed that the number of murders committed, after the abolition of the death penalty in a certain country, had not increased to such a significant extent as had been predicted. This calculation went completely awry, however, and in addition to this, the abolition was not dared to be carried out not only in the Colonies, but often also in the army and on the navy. Against this, it was constantly referred to from the Christian-juridical side as the decisive that what took place after the Flood. Up to the Flood, there had been a lack of well-ordered legal institutions. Cain indeed carried the fear with him that he would be killed because of his murder of Abel, but the death sentence did not follow. And the savage wildness had increased to such an extent at that time at the expense of human life, that Lamech dared to call out to his two wives, Ada and Zilla: “I have killed a man for wounding me, and a young man for bruising me.” Now, after the Flood, when this certain promise went out that such devastation would not be repeated, the Common Grace immediately entered with the Noahitic Covenant, i.e. a divinely established order of life, which aimed to make the restoration of social life in our fallen race possible. For this purpose, ordinances were then given by God Himself to Noah, and through him to all his descendants. §9. Genesis IX:5,6.

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.

Among these ordinances now governing the entire world life from now on, this holy order also came: “I will require your lifeblood from the hand of every beast; from the hand of man, from the hand of every man’s brother, I will require the life of man. Whoever sheds man’s blood, by man his blood shall be shed.” An ordinance that finally received its seal through the addition: “for God made man in His own image.” Luther, and with him not a few scholars of a later time, such as Professor Keil in his Commentary, read here only a firm ordinance that the murderer must be put to death by the authorities, and nothing else. Calvin, on the other hand, recognizes just as clearly in his Commentary on vv. 9, 10, that the death penalty is certainly intended here, which the judge has to carry out, but he still goes further and reads in these words also that God often so arranges it that the murderer soon finds his own murderer. Others are of the opinion that here above all the blood revenge is meant. But in whatever diversity of interpretation one may have fallen, up to our time all the experts were still always agreed that the execution of the murderer by the authorities was also very definitely prescribed in this divine word as the main thing, and this as an ordinance of God and under His Providence. This cannot and may not be understood otherwise, if one pays attention to two things. First, that this utterance of the Lord occurs in the series of ordinances that were given to Noah after the Flood as a rule for the Covenant. For in that it could not fit a mere prediction of what would accidentally happen, but only the establishment of a rule. And secondly, every other interpretation is excluded by what is added, namely, that it must be so “because God made man in His own image.” What sense would it have to understand it thus: “Murder will likely be followed by private blood revenge,” and to motivate this by saying that this blood revenge would arise from the fact that God made man in His image? Everyone feels that this would be nonsensical. On the other hand, the connection runs entirely naturally if one understands it thus: It must be so, for God has made man in His image. This is what gives man his high worth and significance in God’s eyes. It is for this reason of this high worth that the commission of murder on a man is an assault on what

[400]

388 JUSTICE.

God has established as inviolable.

[401]

CONVERSION AND MORAL IMPROVEMENT. 389 Since God has sanctified Himself in His image, it is for this reason that the government, which I have instituted as your God, must avenge the murder of a human being by the punishment of death. Even if a different interpretation has been recommended from the Methodist side, such tampering with the commandment was undoubtedly a distortion of the meaning of the text. Whoever, armed with the necessary exegetical knowledge, interprets this ordinance of God, cannot differ from Luther and Calvin, but must confess with both that the ordinance of the Death Penalty undoubtedly speaks here. The former professor Domela Nieuwenhuis, who had previously also upheld the erroneous exegesis, recently told me that he too had now come to a clearer insight. Something we would rather mention, because his predecessor, Professor Gratama, was the man who, more than anyone else, has pleaded the indefensible view.

§10. Conversion and Moral Improvement. On the basis of Genesis IX: 5, 6, the obligation of the Magistrate to avenge the murder committed on a human being by the death penalty is also firmly established. To say that this is not allowed, because in this way one would cut off someone’s chance of conversion, would, if so understood, exclude the use of an unconverted man as a soldier. Even as a police officer, the unconverted could not be appointed, since every year some police officers lose their lives in the line of duty. In the fire brigades of large cities, it is no different year after year. Even worse is it in the case of shipwrecks at sea. The argument that one removes the chance of moral improvement of the one sentenced to death also falls away for exactly the same reason. And even if we readily admit that the education and improvement of the criminal is highly desirable, yet this can never detract from the demands of justice. Prolonged imprisonment is generally no more conducive to moral improvement. Even the cellular prison, which was devised with this in mind, proved to be just as much a means of corrupting the individual. But even if the judge can often promote moral improvement through the choice of the punishment to be imposed, this is still only a secondary matter for the judge. The primary task of the judge is and remains to uphold the law against the guilty, and this not primarily as a human right to be upheld, but for the maintenance of the law of God. The murderer has offended God and assaulted an ordinance of God when he struck down his victim; the starting point and remains therefore for all death penalty that God had made the man who was murdered in His image.

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.
[402]

390 JUSTICE. That the administration of justice must proceed with the utmost caution in leading to the death penalty, needs no argument. Nor does it raise any objection that the death penalty will not be applied in every case, nor that pardon should be excluded. All this, however, relates to the manner of proceeding that one follows; not to the matter itself. On premeditated murder, on a murder where no mitigating circumstances come into consideration, the death penalty must be ordained in the law of every country.

§11. Looser from the Law. In not so small a circle, the question has arisen at the end of the previous century whether the criminal law did not deprive the judge of too much of his freedom. The law, as it had been from time immemorial, sought to analyze all crimes in detail and then to establish the necessary punishment for each separate and distinct case. This led to difficulties, all the more so as the legal way of thinking, which is increasingly penetrating especially among the younger jurists, cannot boast of the legal insight that had presided over the drafting and promulgation of the criminal law and other threatening laws. To modify all these laws at once was not possible; but it was then thought that a way could be found to ensure that the freer view would penetrate into what was now being established by the legislator, and henceforth be left more to the judge to fix. Thus, over time, a new theory came to stand opposite the previously prevailing one, and the main thrust of this theory was to make the administration of justice more independent of the provisions of the Law. What had thus far been determined in the legislation down to the details regarding criminal threats, it was thought that the judge should now be left more free to determine.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[403]

DETACHMENT FROM THE LAW - THE SUBJECTIVITY OF THE JUDGE

In the future, the law would be established in a less definite, less elaborated, and less detailed form. The law could then confine itself to a general statement of what would be punishable; the judge could then work this out further in its application according to their own insight. And so a system of jurisprudence would arise that, being more detached from the law, would be left more to the judge for further elaboration and application. Two contrasting ways of proceeding began to stand ever more antithetically against one another. In the end, not only was it asked whether a particular subject matter lent itself better to being worked out in more detail by the judge himself, but meanwhile, in other areas, the old more precise determination by the law remained in force. No, it became a new system, to bind the law ever less and to leave ever more to the judge. And one sees in the penal provisions of later laws that for their maintenance a much greater freedom of distinction and application has been entrusted to the judge than before. With this, one is moving along an undesirable line. Certainly, some correction of the previously valid system can be admitted, yes, even deemed desirable - what is now being aimed at in ever-widening circles goes much too far, and tends to give the judge an authoritative position, taking a not insignificant part of what belongs under legislation, and transferring it to the bench.

[404]

§12. The Subjectivity of the Judge

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be.
