The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.

Thereby the subjectivity of the judge in the administration of justice gains a not inconsiderable preponderance. Not only in the international juridical world have there been schools in every century, yes, in every half-century, leading to very divergent conclusions, but even in our small country one finds, as a rule, a striking difference of insight regarding jurisprudence, as far as the punishment of evil is concerned. Each faculty then tries to imprint its ideas on the students who follow its lessons. The student who regularly attends these lectures, and moreover enriches his study by the acquisition of the products of other juridical schools, and by acquainting himself with other theories, ultimately chooses, with more or less depth of insight, for this or that theory; and armed with this, he then immediately aspires to a judicial seat. A seat on which he has hardly taken his place or his passions and preferences prick him to now officially apply his subjectively calibrated ideas. Considering now how far these juridical theories, both in general views and in detailed application, diverge, so that not a few ultimately almost completely abandon any idea of a punitive justice, in order to aim solely at the protection of society and the improvement of the offender, one sees at once to what pure arbitrariness it can lead, if one leaves it to the judge to decide what would otherwise have been firmly established by the law. This handing over to the judge of what the law had otherwise established takes on an even more serious character if, as is constantly the case, there is no longer any question of the judge considering himself bound by the law of God. Not a few judges, however, make no secret of the fact that Viviani’s scornful assertion about the extinguished lights of heaven also finds full echo in them. Thus the unbounded subjectivity presses ever more boldly to the fore, and the citizen in the process is ultimately entirely at the mercy of the incidental insight of the judge who will pronounce judgment over him.

§13. Tempering the Danger

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.

This unbridled arbitrariness is certainly tempered, if not by a single judge, then by a court judgment. And it must not be forgotten that appeal or cassation can curb the great subjectivity of the first judge, and undo what he had rashly ventured. But this last consideration does not take away the fact that, now that even the oath on taking office is no longer imposed on the judge, a very questionable subjectivity is constantly gaining ground, to such an extent that, the more the judge in appeal or cassation begins to be subjected to this subjective theory, even the safeguard that lay in the revision of the judgment is

[405]

TEMPERING THE DANGER - CONDITIONAL CONDEMNATION The notion that a jurist, once appointed to office, would ipso facto acquire a superior quality that would protect him against excessive subjectivism and reckless conjecture, is nothing but an illusion. The same firm rule applies to the judicial power: that the persons of the upper class are rarely voracious (rare birds in the big flock), that the middle class of a not too high order is quite numerous, but that there is always a heavily occupied lower class whose subjective authority is quite ordinary. So it is, and not otherwise, with all official professions; and so it is here as well. It must be recognized, however, that an excess of rules and distinctions in the Law can also give rise to difficulties. One then faces a casuistry that forces the judge to discover, in the particular case before him, the analogy with the case normalized in the Law. Not infrequently, the legislator may also be tempted to over-specialize, thereby obscuring the law rather than clarifying it. And the complaint is also repeatedly heard that the legislator established his regulation in an environment that underwent considerable changes due to circumstances or phenomena that subsequently arose. Against all exaggeration, therefore, vigilance will always be required in the specialization within the Law. Revision of the law, insofar as it has become obsolete, must not be neglected. In the analogies to be applied, a certain tradition must prevail in legal practice. But however one may turn or twist it, the Law must always provide the necessary rule. With the judge, the proper application of that rule is to be sought; with the legislator, the establishment thereof.

§14. Conditional Condemnation

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”

No less weighty a point, which was also not yet on the agenda in 1878, is conditional condemnation. This type of condemnation consists in the judge passing sentence on the convicted person, but the punishment imposed on him being postponed. During the thus freed interval, the convicted person then stands under legal supervision. This supervision aims to ascertain whether the repetition of the evil, as in his first case, given his character, is to be feared or not.

[406]

JUSTICE. If the oversight placed him in an unfavorable light, but then failed to materialize, and the outcome of that oversight shows that his transgression was indeed an exception, then one wishes to see that the sentence be considered withdrawn and that the person be set free. As one immediately sees, a highly appreciable motive is at work here. One knows what a difference it makes for someone’s future whether they were still allowed to go free or were imprisoned. Whoever ends up in the latter case has often irreparably damaged their reputation in a wide circle, and often has great difficulty in regaining a profession or position. This then frequently leads to very serious embarrassment. And it is precisely this embarrassment that so often drives the convicted to seek a way out in new crimes. It is very certain that, in contrast, the knowledge of how one can squander one’s entire future through a single dishonest act deters not a few from the first offense; and it cannot be denied that excessive compassion for the one can weaken the resistance to evil in others; which is why it is so desirable that the conditional conviction not be too much trumpeted abroad. But even if we weigh all these considerations well, it still seems to us that there are many arguments in favor of not always and immediately cutting off all the favorable connections with the society in the midst of which the convicted lives. Of course, there must always be a limit here. Such sparing of the convicted can only be spoken of in the case of crimes of lesser quality and limited scope. The oversight that one must exercise over him must be no winking eye, but strict control. Even certain restrictions on freedom need not be excluded. But if it is to be found that the convicted is not made unhappy for all time, and at least does not deprive him of the chance to get on better paths, then this pleads not a little, even from the point of view of justice. Justice assigns him a certain measure of punishment, it gives him half a year’s imprisonment. According to the scales, this must be the equivalent of what he committed. But if it now results from undergoing this punishment that he loses his position and can then only with the greatest difficulty find a way through life, then this is, especially if he has a family, an aggravation of his punishment that at least triples the weight of this punishment.

[407]

OBJECTIONS TO THE EXECUTION

§15. Objections to the Execution. On this ground, the idea of conditional sentencing has also evoked sympathy in our circles. The writer of this also repeatedly expressed himself in this direction in De Standaard, and we know what the deeply lamented Mr. Gwin and after him the former professor Domela Nieuwenhuis had to say about it. But although we accept the idea itself, in general, the objection remains insurmountable that the way in which one believes to have found the solution is irreconcilable with every legal concept. A sentence gives a final conclusion of the judge about the guilty person. Circumstances that may or must influence this sentence, the judge must therefore investigate and establish before he will pronounce judgment. Therefore, he may not pronounce judgment before he is completely ready with this serious investigation. But when he is ready with this, and has once pronounced his judgment on the basis of the results of this investigation, then the judgment has become a fact and must either be executed or be annulled by pardon. The question of whether one cannot then convert the conditional sentence into a freely regulated and repeatedly recurring pardon must also be answered in the negative. Pardon is granted if the law could not sufficiently reckon with the case at hand, and therefore had to lead to a subjective conviction by the judge, and if now a way out had to be sought to bring the provision of the Law into harmony with the particular character of the case. But this was not the case here. It was assumed, after all, that the provision of the Law was fully applicable, but that considerations entirely outside the Law led to the release of the bond that the judgment had placed on the guilty.

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[408]

396 JUSTICE

§16. Before Pronouncing Judgment. This does not, however, exclude that perhaps another form is to be found that would still do justice to what one aims at; provided one only recognizes that the judge is of the opinion that he is not yet in a position to pronounce a just, righteous, and fitting sentence on the guilty. The mistake is now that one nevertheless wants the judge to pronounce the sentence, in order to bring to clearer light after the pronouncement of the sentence what was lacking in his investigation. And this is the upside-down world. One cannot first half investigate and then pronounce sentence, in order to complete the investigation with the missing half afterwards. Before the sentence is pronounced, the investigation must be fully completed. Hence our proposal to indeed introduce the case, but with the reversed order of sentence and investigation. If the judge still does not know the accused well enough and is not yet able to make up his mind about him, if he asks for more time for this, and insists that the accused himself be observed for some time in his social life; then give the judge the authority to postpone the sentence until such time as this further investigation and more personal control has put him in a position to judge the accused with accuracy. If then, after such a period of investigation and control, the guilt that has been factually proven can, in connection with the character of the person, be atoned for by a less impressive punishment, then impose on the guilty a fine, and do so in a judgment that does not pronounce in advance, but only takes effect when the investigation is fully completed. This investigation, under good control, should then take on a serious form. It should not be a bandage over the wound, but a real investigation into the relationships as they are. In this way, the law maintains its normal and proper course, insofar as the sentence comes only after the completion of the full investigation. It is not a sham judgment, but a real judgment. Only, it now becomes a judgment that takes into account the true position of the guilty, and does not inflict on him a punishment three times as severe as the judgment itself would otherwise have been.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.
[409]

THE OATH QUESTION - DIFFERENCE IN DEGREE

§17. The Oath Question. Regarding the oath question, the Calvinist recognizes no other jurisdiction than that which is conducted in the presence and before the face of God. In itself, no person, whoever they may be, has the right or authority to take the life of another, to temporarily or permanently deprive them of their freedom, or to deny them a portion of their possessions. Yet it is this threefold exercise of power that has also been granted to the Government in our land, and with this it places a power at its disposal, which it then also actually exercises. One may have set aside the death penalty, as shown above, yet the Government still has full authority over the lives of its subjects in war, and in our East Indies the death penalty is still carried out. Imprisonment has likewise remained in place. Behold our prisons, as the sun goes down, but the gas light shines through the peepholes. And that the Government compels and forces us, under threat of punishment, to still bring a portion of our income to its offices in the form of taxes or fines, requires no further explanation. In fact, our Government is still exercising a power that does not inherently belong to any person as a person. God alone has authority over our life, our personal freedom, and our possessions. Only by appearing as God’s servant does the Government have the right and authority to subject us as subjects to these three legal institutions in the name of God. Whoever appears in court, whether as accused or as witness, stands therefore, just as the judge himself, before the face of God, speaks in His holy presence, and falls short of the holiness and omniscience of God, if they speak or act in the legal proceedings apart from God. § Difference in Degree. Every playing with the truth renders a person guilty, but there is a difference in degree in this guilt. Amidst the multiplicity of life, seriousness can sink within us and the sense of responsibility can fade. This is precisely why now the judge and the accuser, the accused and the witness, are intentionally summoned before the face of God, in order thereby to heighten the sense of responsibility, to fully awaken the seriousness, and to make him to whom it applies understand that from God’s side a double guilt will be imputed to him, if he dares, thus placed before the face of God, nevertheless to play with the truth. The realization that must prevail in court can therefore be no other than that of one who speaks, as it were, directly opposite God, sees in God’s eyes, and feels that the eye of God penetrates into the hidden recesses of his soul. The person who appears in court, on the other hand, is by no means always in such a frame of mind. The unfamiliarity with appearing in court, the strangeness of the entire scene in the courtroom, and also the embarrassment that many cannot shake off, often make them unfree, so that more than one is no longer master of himself at such a moment. Since this proves to be the case time and again, one has resorted to eliciting a separate act that can clearly and distinctly awaken the consciousness of standing before the all-knowing God, and this act is now the taking of the Oath. If the Oath, the person himself confesses that he knows he stands before God’s holy presence; that he acknowledges God’s omniscience; and therefore confesses that God Himself will hear what he is about to say as a witness, and will now punish the speaker for untruth; from which it follows that in God the right is upheld to apply that punishment also to him, as a witness, if the testimony he is about to give does not in every particular correspond to what he must say.

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the
[410]

§19. Solemnity Neglected. With a view to this high significance of the Oath, it is certainly to be condemned that the taking of the Oath has been increasingly simplified. If the Oath is to be what it should be according to the intention of the Lord, namely a placing and standing of the witness before the face of God, then our Oath formula is too short and too indefinite in content, and it is no less to be condemned that not a single word is spoken that reminds the witness of the holiness of God, of His omniscience, and of the fearful punishment that awaits him if he dares to speak untruth.

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.
[411]

NEGLECT OF SOLEMNITY - ANABAPTISTS AND MENNONITES... even the introduced custom of displaying the Cross, or taking the Oath on the Bible, deserves recommendation. At such a moment, such a symbol works a higher mood, just as is required here. It is also a very serious question whether a judge who is an atheist can demand the Oath in the Name of the Lord. With this in view, it would undoubtedly be advisable for a Minister of Religion to administer the Oath. It would then be the same as calling in a physician when a medical opinion is needed. If, however, one looks at the high costs that this would entail, it would certainly be worth considering to lend the proceeding itself a somewhat more solemn character. As it often happens with us now, what can bring solemnity is almost entirely lacking. There are indeed some judges who try to put something elevating into it, but it also happens that the taking of the Oath is almost mechanical, and almost becomes a mockery. There is reason enough not to multiply the taking of the Oath. If possible, it should even be made more rare. If this can be done in a process where fifty or even more witnesses appear, then the question would even deserve consideration whether one could not introduce a sworn written declaration instead of an Oath.

[412]

400 JUSTICE... this is also why a change must be made here, and everyone who, as a Mennonite, refuses the Oath, shall only be exempted from the Oath if he testifies, either orally or in writing, that he does not dare to take the Oath on the basis of the pronouncement of Christ in the passage of the Sermon on the Mount referred to. Any playing with the Oath, as is all too often the case with the exception of the Mennonites, deserves unconditional disapproval and must be stopped.

§21. Objections to the Oath

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over

This must be emphasized all the more, because in itself the objection to the Oath is very well explained. Whoever demands the Oath from you, shows that he starts from the assumption that one cannot always rely on your yes or no with sufficient certainty; and it is understandable that this is an obstacle. It is as if the one who demands the Oath from you suspects that an untrue expression of thought is not foreign to you. This is precisely the critical point around which the whole question of the Oath revolves. Man, after the Fall, no longer provides a firm guarantee of absolute sincerity. On the contrary, lying and slander is still a daily practice in all kinds of circles. Just listen to the conversations sometimes even in the most civilized circles, how one is constantly on the lookout to accuse someone and to call his good name into question. In the political domain, the public organ of the Press is all too often the sinful interpreter of this. Evil-speaking, tearing at anyone’s good name, smearing and slandering one’s neighbor, is still all too much at home even among women of high standing. It is generally not easy to find a remedy against this, and one also lacks the power to do so. But it is quite different when testimony must be given in the courtroom. Then it is about someone’s guilt or innocence, and a true or false testimony can depend on someone’s entire life position; as far as the death penalty is still applied, even on his life. Above all, the shameful fact that proof has been provided from behind, how the sharp judge has once brought an innocent person to death on false testimony, inspires here to the highest seriousness.

[413]

THE VALUE ATTACHED TO THE OATH.—If we had to deal only with truthful persons, so that, as a rule, we could be fully certain of hearing the truth testified to, the introduction of the Oath would never have been thought of. But now, since the exact opposite is the case, and in ordinary life the strict telling of the truth by one about another is often the exception, recourse must be had to the taking of the Oath, and it becomes our duty as sinners to willingly undergo the self-abasement which always lies in the taking of an Oath.

§22. The Value Attached.—Even from the unbelieving side, great value is still attached to the taking of the Oath. When, under the Ministry of Cort van der Linden, the Oath question came up for the first time in the States-General, the Government took pains to declare clearly that it continued to set great store by the advantage which the taking of the Oath yields. Nor can it be denied that the taking of the Oath still checks many a lie upon the lips of witnesses. They even hesitate not to testify that the atheist here is still quite an exceptional case, and that even he who in ordinary life poses as living without God, in the court of justice still has a keen sense that he has to do with the All-Knowing God. This is still continually observed as the shadow of death hovers over one, whether in shipwreck or on the battlefield, and also in the ordinary dying; and it is this same phenomenon which also continually manifests itself in the Oath question. Certainly, there are some scoundrels who stand on guard, and who for a guilder will let themselves be bought to give false testimony in a case of which they know absolutely nothing; but even this evil could very well be overcome, if one would attack it with a more holy, but also more inexorable, earnestness. But whatever abuse one may have to wrestle with, the fact remains that the testimony given in the court of justice can decide a person’s guilt or innocence, and this now demands, if one takes into account the evil-speaking and slandering, that the testimony in the court of justice be strengthened and purified by the Oath.

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the
[414]

402 JUSTICE.The consideration, too, that as a rule the “respectable” people tell the truth, and that only the men and women of the lower classes are not to be trusted in their testimony, so that the former could very well be excused from the Oath, but with the latter the Oath must be maintained, is pure aristocratic arrogance. To a certain extent it can be acknowledged that in the lower classes shameless lying more easily creeps in, but it is equally indisputable that up to the highest classes of society it is continually not only the gentlemen, but also the ladies, who are caught in untrue representations, as soon as their own or family interests are closely involved.

§23. Higher and Lower Classes.—The attempt which is uppermost with many to bring it about that the Oath remain for the common man, but that with the “declaration” the man of higher social position can be satisfied, proceeds from entirely incorrect premises. Just consult, in connection with the great world war which broke out in 1914, how the greatest statesmen have publicly accused each other in Parliament and in printed documents of false testimony, and the reading of this will of itself cure you of the cherished notion that untruthful testimony occurs only with the common man. In itself, there is nothing to be deducted from the fact that the judge who in God’s Name will have to pronounce upon a person’s guilt or innocence, must possess the means to raise the testimony upon which his judgment must rest to the highest possible degree of truthfulness. And this means now is and remains, through all the ages, the placing of the witness before the face and in the presence of God, which can take place only through the Oath. The question now remains only as to how the judge will proceed if a summoned witness refuses to take the Oath. Three ways are open here. This refusal can, in the first place, be forbidden and punishable by law. Or it can, in the second place, be accepted, and on that ground the one summoned be discharged from giving testimony. Or, finally, there can, in the third place,

Analytical Summary: The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,
[415]

HIGHER AND LOWER CLASSES - CIVIL DISABILITIES A way is being sought to ensure the credibility of testimony without requiring an oath. A brief word on each of the first two ideas, before elaborating on the third. Regarding all objections to taking the oath, insofar as they arise from atheism, no mercy has been shown from of old towards those who refuse, since it was believed that atheism had no grounds for existence.

§24. The Civil Disabilities The so-called civil disabilities, which were associated with a particular confession, were originally applied strictly; and although, as Macaulay so beautifully argued, this system was not tenable, and has therefore been largely abandoned in all lands, it does not at all follow that this abandonment also needs to continue where one has to deal with either half-mocking or high-minded atheism. The former system of making full citizenship dependent on the profession of a particular Church is directly contrary to the well-understood Calvinist principle, and cannot for a moment be advocated on our part; rather, it must be opposed without compromise and in an absolute sense. But it is quite a different matter to openly declare that one does not believe in God. If the entire state community hangs on God’s sovereign disposition, then whoever denies the existence of God excludes himself from that community as far as its full scope is concerned. And if one asks whether it would not then still be harsh to deny those who do not believe in God the full enjoyment of citizenship, our answer can only be that the so-called atheism, if taken in its absolute sense, is far from possessing the proportions that are often attributed to it. We have already pointed out how, in shipwreck, in sudden mortal danger, and even on the quiet deathbed, such atheism constantly belies itself. Therefore, making the refusal of an oath punishable would by no means have the serious character that is often claimed for it; yet we would most emphatically protest against it. Even the appearance of doing violence to conscience must, wherever it is even slightly possible, be avoided by us.

Analytical Summary: Our People.
[416]

JUSTICE. This cannot always be avoided. That it cannot always be done was shown recently when a new movement arose and, on pacifist grounds, refused to be drafted into active military service, which was done by a small group, not without some ostentation. Here too a critical “case of conscience” presented itself, and the judges did not hesitate to sentence the objector. One should never say, therefore, that no more cases can arise in which it is absolutely impossible for the government to avoid personal scruples. However, this rule cannot apply here, because the forced taking of an oath by a mocker or a brutal atheist would not enhance, but rather diminish, the value of his testimony. The purpose in summoning a witness in a court of law is that he may shed a true light on the circumstances of the case at issue, and this purpose would not be furthered, but rather entirely missed, by coercing the conscience. Against every witness being compelled to take an oath, the most serious objections would immediately arise in the mind of any earnest man. Much rather, therefore, should the deprivation of full civil rights be constantly applied to the atheist. There are more reasons that hinder and even prevent the taking of testimony even about facts to which one has been an eyewitness. Just think of mental weakness, of incomplete development, of idiocy, and the like. Much could also be argued for completely dispensing with any appeal to the testimony of a genuine atheist. This cannot be conceded by one who knows no other judge than an earthly one, and concludes that what the earthly judge does not punish goes free; but it does proceed from our standpoint. For we confess that the punishment of the earthly judge is but imperfect, and that the true retribution comes only from God’s side, whether already here on earth in conscience and in life’s suffering, or hereafter in the Judgment. Those who recognize no other judgment than that of the earthly judge are therefore extremely perplexed by the undeniable fact that a multitude of iniquities fall outside the concern of the judge; that in more than one case his judgment has had to be regretted in hindsight, as resting on incorrect grounds; and above all, that more and more crimes cannot be judged by the judge because the criminal succeeds in remaining unknown or making himself untraceable.

[417]

THREATENING ABUSE. The claim that the right to refuse to testify on oath should not be denied, solely out of fear that the oath would be violated, cannot be upheld.

§25. Threatening Abuse. However, here arises a completely different objection—that, if refusal to take the oath were to lead to dismissal from giving testimony, this new institution would be constantly abused to evade giving testimony. For one never loses sight of the fact that giving testimony, and this in public, arouses hatred in the one who is condemned by it, and that therefore in far the majority of cases the summoned witness would much rather keep himself aloof from it. If the summoned person were to be discharged on his peremptory declaration that he has overriding objections to taking the oath, then the refusal to give testimony, especially in serious criminal cases, would undoubtedly increase by leaps and bounds. It is on this ground, and on this ground alone, that we would not dare to recommend the dismissal from giving testimony, solely on this ground. And we do this the less, because it is becoming increasingly clear how the so highly exalted objections to the oath turn out to be more fabricated and artificial than genuinely felt. For one fact speaks strongly here. Whoever is affiliated with the Anabaptist Community is free from the oath. Now since joining this Community is entirely free, and does not require the acceptance of a particular confession, one would think that the objections in other Churches would simply transfer to this Community. Then they would, as the case may be, be free from all difficulty. Yet this does not happen. At least we know of hardly any transitions with this purpose. Modernists, who still found it too confining in the Reformed Church, do sometimes go over to the Remonstrants, but almost never to the Mennonites. The little significance attached to this whole issue is moreover not less confirmed by the fact that, after the well-known judgment of the Supreme Court, by which this issue was once again brought to the fore, hardly any procedural difficulties have been heard of. They are and remain very few, quite isolated cases. It is therefore not the practice, but exclusively the theory of the oath, that heats up the minds on this point. The oath points to God, and to reckon with the all-knowing God officially in the courtroom and thus before the Government, is precisely what one does not want.

Analytical Summary: Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit
[418]

406 JUSTICE. Thus only the third way remains open, consisting in seeking a means to obtain from him who says he cannot take the oath, such a certainty that the judge who pronounces judgment in the name of God, can nevertheless take peace with it.

§26. Way Out. On the one hand, one has wanted to seek such sufficient certainty in imposing a more severe punishment, if the “declaration” should turn out to be wholly or partially untrue. Perjury is severely punished; can the attempt at false testimony not now be cut off by an even more severe threat? This could certainly be the case, and would undoubtedly hit the mark in more than one trial; but the entering upon that path is blocked by the mere thought that perjury would then be punished less severely than an incorrect declaration, and this seemed to lead to an underestimation of the sacred. It is on this ground that we too would not dare to recommend taking that path. The administration of justice must take place in the Name of God, and the entire process must therefore take place as if taking place in His holy presence. It may not proceed as a mere human transaction with human. The sacred character of all administration of justice must also, as regards the witnesses, be maintained. That is precisely why it is so completely inexplicable that, as is well known, more than one Christian Statesman, both from the Roman Catholic and, alas, even from the Christian-Historical and, regrettably, also from the Anti-Revolutionary side, has almost without hesitation gone over to the proposal that came from the other side, to equate oath and declaration in legal value; and thus, without more ado, to allow the refusal of the oath and the restriction to a declaration (or promise).

Analytical Summary: Guided by Orange. To this has been added: “guided by Orange”; an addition that must not be omitted. For it cannot be denied that in the characterized Calvinism, as it also penetrated our country from Geneva and France at that time, there was a danger not to be underestimated, the aftereffects of which are still discernible. In the last chapter of the First Part of this work, I have extensively explained how from the outset there was a decisive element in Calvinism that could only develop and mature later, and that, politically speaking, only came to full effect in the United States; but it was also then added that in Geneva (one need only think of the burning of Servetus) an intolerant dogmatism still prevailed, which, though indispensable for the church, could only result in schism and oppression in its political consequences.
[419]

OATH-HELPERS - MINISTER ORT

§27. Oath-Helpers. This incident, which also applied to some Anti-Revolutionary Statesmen, has caused bewilderment—because already deep in the previous century a means had been provided to extricate oneself from the resulting inconvenience, and yet one nevertheless so quickly went along with the liberal advice, and even came forward for it with a certain warmth, without even testing the means we had provided with any seriousness. One simply glossed over it. That had to give offense, and still does. That at least some turning has now come in this attitude is only to be thanked to what Mr. Ort, as Minister of Justice, intimated in the First Chamber about this proposal. §28. Minister Ort. He indeed took up this proposal so fully seriously that he read the main point from “Our Program” and, after that reading, expressed his opinion that the matter could very well be regulated in this way, but that it seemed to him only too cumbersome to be practically serviceable. In the Proceedings of the First Chamber, of June 17, 1915, page 392, we are reported this literally: “What was now,” so the Minister spoke, “in the face of the state of emergency in which the Government found itself placed, its duty? To let the matter remain as it was, in the hope that the Oath question would only be of a passing nature, as the gentleman Bosch van Oud-Amelisweerd indicated? But for that, the situation of today, as I shall soon explain, was indeed too serious.” “To re-examine the situation, as Mr. Kuyper indicated, and to consider some solutions that until now had found no defenders, as is outlined in ‘Our Program’? One reads in that book what procedure would be necessary for the exemption from the Oath compulsion for atheists.” “I read here: ‘that from atheists, i.e. from those who do not unconditionally declare belief in the living God, no Oath shall be taken, nor even a promise as indicated under §3, but that one shall proceed with these exceptional people in this manner.’”

Analytical Summary: The wrong done to us. Our party has set up a price in 1878, in its own starting point, to point to this happy influence of the Prince of Orange. Let no confusion creep in here. In matters of ecclesiastical life, Calvin’s insight has also been his own. His dispute with Dathenus does not touch the very life of the church, but the influence of the Church on the life of the people in the political domain. As a theologian, especially Johannes a Lasco deserves appreciation on this point, but on a broader scale it has above all been the influence of the Prince that has safely guided us. Meanwhile, one should not lose sight of the fact that this pointing with preference to the Prince is foreign to the intention, to praise anything that has later emerged from the House of Orange on this delicate terrain of the Princes. Prince Maurice aligned himself much more closely with the Calvinists. The then-emerging conflict with Arminianism necessitated this. But his life history would stand in a more glorious light for us if Oldenbarnevelt had died a natural death. Under Prince Frederick
[420]

408 JUSTICE

“First, such a person shall have to prove that he is not a member of any Church community. ‘For an atheist who yet remains a member of a Church of God, thereby already shows himself to be a dishonest man.’ Secondly, he shall have to declare in writing that his refusal to take the Oath has no other cause than that he does not believe in the living God. ‘Thirdly, he shall have to submit a properly signed certificate by at least three persons that he is known to them as an honest and truth-loving man. On the understanding that every atheist shall be deemed incompetent to issue this certificate, and the right of challenge shall remain with the Authorities.’ ‘Fourthly, he shall have to submit a written declaration that the promise he is about to make, if he comes to belief in God, shall stand for him equal to an Oath.’ ‘And fifthly, he shall have to declare himself willing, to the satisfaction of the Authorities, that, if they so demand, he shall have this declared under Oath by a respectable person (towards whom the right of challenge shall likewise remain with the Authorities, and of whom above all it has been proven that he has known the person concerned sufficiently closely and long enough) that in his case no suspicion against the truthfulness of this atheist in the matter at hand exists.’” And what was now His Excellency’s judgment on this proposal? Behold it here: “Undoubtedly,” he continued, “this regulation is very ingeniously conceived, and it cannot be denied that in this way truly testing safeguards are provided. But it also follows from this that as soon as one gets a different test than the psychological judgment of the judge, this regulation, through its cumbersomeness, will practically not be possible. Can one really believe that a bill, cast in such a mold, would have any chance of a favorable reception in the States-General?”

[421]

THE INSTITUTION OF THE OATH-HELPERS

§29. The Institution of the Oath-Helpers. But while we must commend those more liberal legal scholars who handled the scales of justice with impartiality, it requires no further argument that their objection did not decide the matter. For if what our proposal contained could have done both—maintain the sanctity of the administration of justice and yet make the testimony of the oath-refusing witness usable—then surely the question should have been asked whether a way could not be found to overcome the cumbersomeness that was hindering it; and this all the more since on our part we frankly acknowledged that in 1878, having only just passed from the ministry into politics, we did not yet possess the data to delineate the proper course of action with strict precision, and were now ourselves entirely of the opinion that a limitation of the proceedings would be quite conceivable and permissible. But what did our proposal essentially come down to? We appealed to the Oath-Helpers, who had come into vogue in the Middle Ages. The peculiarity of this legal institution of the “Oath-Helpers” consisted in this, that it was of Germanic origin, and therefore naturally fell into the background as the Roman law began to dominate our legal institutions more and more. This institution from Germanic law had also been incorporated into Canon Law and had gained sympathy there. Both Dr. Gemeiner and Dr. Hildebrand gave the necessary explanation about this institution from the Roman Catholic side in 1841 and 1849. The work of Dr. Gemeiner, which saw the light of day in 1849, bore the title: “Oath-Help and Oath-Helpers”; that of Dr. Hildebrand, “The Purgatio Canonica et Vulgaris.” Officially, these “Oath-Helpers” bore the name of Conjuratores or also Consacramentales, and their action consisted in this, that both in civil proceedings and in criminal cases, they supported by their own oath-taking him who had to take the oath.

Analytical Summary: According to the needs of our time.
[422]

They then testified under oath that they knew the witness or the party personally and sufficiently well, and that his testimony contained no deception. The consideration that applied here was that the judge did not know the witness or the party well enough to judge whether one could rely on his statement, and that they, the Oath-Helpers, on the other hand, knew him very well, and now stood surety for the honesty of his testimony. They were usually seven in number. Since the 13th century, however, this institution of the Oath-Helpers has fallen into disuse, and the more the Roman law, strengthened by Canon Law, gained dominion, the less the Oath-Helpers appeared. Of their own accord, they did not pronounce on the case itself. Their testimony served only to vouch under oath for the credibility of the man. Especially in the purgatio canonica, they gained decisive importance from the 11th century onward. Sometimes their number here rose to twelve, and even now this institution is still valid for Canon Law in matters of impotence, and is then called the juramentum septimae manus, since then seven blood relatives must appear as Oath-Helpers.

§30. Similar Service. Now let us consider that this institution of Oath-Helpers did not serve to strengthen the oath-taking, but to support the reliability of it. Not for a moment has it been asserted on our side that it could now render the same service as in former times. It would not come down to the same service, but to a service similar in kind. Then, and now, the question arose when hearing the declarations made in the courtroom whether what the person in question was saying, and attesting, was entirely in accordance with the truth; and the uncertainty that one wished to temper then, and that we also wish to temper now, by having Oath-Helpers appear. For the present, this institution would therefore be completely equivalent to what the Minister of Justice had proposed in his first draft in June 1915. In this proposal, the possibility was likewise assumed that in the case of refusal to take the oath, an attempt might be made to evade the obligation to give the full truth in his statement. Against this, Minister Ort then took refuge in his draft in the legal provision that the judge should be empowered and obliged to assess independently to what extent there were reasonable grounds to acquiesce in the witness’s refusal to take the oath. Against this, a rather strong opposition arose from both the Right and the Left. They gave their consent without reservation that, as long as the oath institution remained in force, it was not permissible to simply release someone who had scruples about taking the oath, and acknowledged that it remained desirable...

Analytical Summary: The Church and the State in Mutual Relation. One immediately feels this in the relationship between Church and State. In the 16th century, the struggle that the Reformation undertook against Rome’s overpowering authority naturally brought the ecclesiastical question to the fore in all political domains. The ecclesiastical question dominated the entire political sphere, both domestically and with a view to foreign relations, and here the foreign relations mostly stood...
[423]

SIMILAR SERVICE. - MY PROPOSAL OF 1878. 411 to find a means to determine whether the refusal to take the oath arose from atheism or from a pious intention. But even if this purpose was approved, and even if it was felt that a certain guarantee had to be sought here, there was an objection to the judge, entirely on his own authority, having to decide this important point in every case. In the vast majority of cases, he did not know the person concerned at all, and this person was completely strange to him. He would therefore have to rely solely on the impression he received of the person in court or on what he was informed about him by others. And this seemed to come down to pure arbitrariness. If, however, for these entirely conclusive reasons, this proposal is not further pursued, so that the Minister himself completely deleted it in the revision, then everyone feels that it is even less a matter of rejecting any further guarantee outright here, and, as Minister Ort proposed in his second draft, leaving it solely to the summoned witness whether he would take the oath or refuse it. For in that way, all certainty would be lost, and everyone would be given the opportunity to withdraw from the oath without any recourse.

§31. My Proposal of 1878. Precisely in connection with this, our proposal of 1878, i.e., the recourse to a modified institute of oath helpers, recommends itself. The first criticized bill recognized the need for a certain guarantee of the truthfulness of the assertion that the person summoned could not take the oath for lack of belief, and it was precisely this guarantee that was promised on our part. The first bill, which entrusted this investigation to the judge, stood entirely on a par with our proposal in terms of the intended purpose. In both, in the bill as well as in our proposal, it was recognized that further proof of credibility was indispensable, and the difference between the two consisted only in this, that the government bill left the search for the guarantee to the judge, before whom the summoned person was often a complete stranger, while in our proposal it was entrusted to at least three persons who were shown to know the person well enough to be able to form an opinion about his statement. Naturally, the latter held up much better, and provided what the judge’s opinion could not give by itself. Thus, our proposal recommended itself from all sides. For in this way the dignity of the oath remained intact, the administration of justice was also held entirely before the face of God, and the person who believed he had to refuse the oath out of conscience was helped out of this difficulty by the appearance of friends or acquaintances who had every opportunity to discuss the whole matter with him beforehand and to convince themselves personally of the sincerity and truthfulness of his assertion. If now these friends appeared in his place and testified under oath that they were assured of the sincerity of the man’s testimony, and this under the risk of undergoing punishment themselves if it later turned out that there had been deception, then indeed the seriousness in the proceedings would not have been relaxed for a moment, and the highest certainty would have been obtained that one can count on among men. §32. Against the Complexity Alone. With this, the judgment of the liberal Minister Ort entirely agrees, that our proposal could be rational and effective, and thus also deserving of recommendation, if only it did not condemn itself through too great complexity. To this, we first of all object that this argument is very weak. If one considers to what complex actions recourse is often taken when it comes to medical-psychological uncertainties, or when other technical investigations are also necessary to determine the true character of a fact, then it does not sound very reverential when one is only concerned about some complexity where the guarantee of the holy is at stake. Moreover, it is not proposed that such assistance of oath helpers should be called in as a matter of course. Often in the legal case it has not occurred more than once or twice a year, as is evident from the Appendix attached to the Second

Analytical Summary: The People’s Representation. It is no different with the People’s Representation. Under the republican form of government, our Commonwealth has not known representation as we understand it today. There were estates, but no People’s Representation; the cities and manorial landowners held the power; the deputies were in no way representatives of the nation before the Government, but rather the installers and maintainers of the Government itself. There was certainly popular choice in the election of the power-holders, but the right to participate in that choice was extremely limited. As shown in the last chapter of the First Part, Calvin did not praise such a system, and ultimately he himself went over to advising in a democratic sense. Whoever now tries to apply the completely antiquated representation ideas of the 16th century to the present state of affairs would thereby show themselves to be anything but faithful to the pure principle, but rather to blatantly disown it. To now still advocate a State form that does not fully do justice to the representative principle, would brand one as backward and out of one’s time. The question of a proper People’s Representation has now itself become the main question for the future development of the State life. And the Calvinist direction, which has always proceeded from the people, and even found its strength in the “common folk,” would lose its inner driving force and let itself be written off, if it did not take the lead rather than follow on this point of our constitutional development in the spirit of freedom and love of the people. To be a Christian Democrat remains the title of honor for every Calvinist. §32. Navy and Army. As a third example, let the National Defense be pointed out. In the days of the Reformation, there was hardly any mention of a national army, as we know it today. The Condottieri had indeed lost ground, but the army with which one went to war still consisted mostly of volunteers offering themselves, and even of mercenary troops from foreign countries, just as we still used to employ, and partly still do, not a few Swiss, Germans and Belgians for our East Indian army. Only of the army of Gustavus Vasa and of the army of Cromwell can it be said that they were driven and urged by a higher and holier interest. For the rest, however, the armies were mostly composed of rough men, and the holy enthusiasm for the cause of the country was sometimes all too little to be noticed. That the same regiment, after the lapse of ten years, drew the sword against a higher national interest, which it had first helped to defend at the price of its blood, occurred more than once. All the more the custom came into vogue with us to assemble the power for the defense of the country’s interests from hired manpower, inasmuch as the navy with us, more than the army, stood in importance, and the manning of the navy, both here and still in England, was constantly recruited by free choice. The civic guard had a more national character, and knew no wages as a motive; but the militia, as it is now in force, first appeared under Napoleon’s domination, and has since also been maintained in our Kingdom. Meanwhile, our people have been far from eager for the new military idea. To become a soldier was not considered an honor, but a plague, and the system of substitutes soon came up to free oneself from this plague by money. It has therefore lasted until the very end of the 19th century before we really got a national army, and the idea of universal military service, which now imposes itself of its own accord, is still not realized with us. Now, because in the glorious days of our fathers the condition prevailed in navy and army that excluded a national army, shall one identify the system then in force with Calvinism, and therefore, as ultra-conservative, set it against the military force that is now so absolutely indispensable? Certainly not. The principle of Calvinism and this is, to highly esteem the significance of the national life; and the future of the national life is now inexorably, at least for small nations, in danger, if not the whole people finally girds the sword as one man for the country’s independence.
[424]

412 JUSTICE. for whom the summoned person was often a complete stranger, while in our proposal it was entrusted to at least three persons who were shown to know the person well enough to be able to form an opinion about his statement. Naturally, the latter held up much better, and provided what the judge’s opinion could not give by itself. Thus, our proposal recommended itself from all sides. For in this way the dignity of the oath remained intact, the administration of justice was also held entirely before the face of God, and the person who believed he had to refuse the oath out of conscience was helped out of this difficulty by the appearance of friends or acquaintances who had every opportunity to discuss the whole matter with him beforehand and to convince themselves personally of the sincerity and truthfulness of his assertion. If now these friends appeared in his place and testified under oath that they were assured of the sincerity of the man’s testimony, and this under the risk of undergoing punishment themselves if it later turned out that there had been deception, then indeed the seriousness in the proceedings would not have been relaxed for a moment, and the highest certainty would have been obtained that one can count on among men.

§32. Against the Complexity Alone. With this, the judgment of the liberal Minister Ort entirely agrees, that our proposal could be rational and effective, and thus also deserving of recommendation, if only it did not condemn itself through too great complexity. To this, we first of all object that this argument is very weak. If one considers to what complex actions recourse is often taken when it comes to medical-psychological uncertainties, or when other technical investigations are also necessary to determine the true character of a fact, then it does not sound very reverential when one is only concerned about some complexity where the guarantee of the holy is at stake. Moreover, it is not proposed that such assistance of oath helpers should be called in as a matter of course. Often in the legal case it has not occurred more than once or twice a year, as is evident from the Appendix attached to the Second

Analytical Summary: Money Gave the Decisive Vote. So it remained until Thorbecke in 1848 cleared away everything that still had a medieval aftertaste, and made money the sole fulcrum of the representative system. Article 76 of the Constitution promulgated that year tied the suffrage for the Second Chamber, and likewise Article 123 the suffrage for the Provincial Estates, “to the payment of a sum in direct taxes, corresponding to the local situation,” ranging from ƒ20 to ƒ160. The Electoral Law provided for the proper implementation of these provisions, and did so in such an indicative anti-democratic direction that the census of ƒ24 in many Frisian municipalities was calculated to favor only a few Liberals. A proposal from the Anti-Revolutionary side, made to reduce this amount, also met with the most vehement opposition from the Liberal side in the Second Chamber, even as late as 1875. Especially in Sneek this gave the most outrageous injustice, just as after 1887, in the new edition of what is now Article 80, in a way that allowed no contradiction, it appeared from the figures on the Liberal and Anti-Revolutionary candidates.
[425]

NOT OVERLY COMPLICATED - NOT A CRISIS LAW.

The proposal communicated to the Chamber by the Government when submitting its initial draft. The occurrence of such a case is and remains a very rare exception. But even apart from this; and we have also declared ourselves clearly on this, one does not at all need to follow precisely the manner of action that we suggested. It can proceed much more simply. One can suffice with having the witness who objected to taking the Oath do this through a written declaration, in which the refuser attests in a prescribed form on what grounds his refusal is based. Secondly, a statement could then be requested from him of at least three persons who were willing to declare under Oath that in their opinion he was a completely reliable person. Those three could be replaced by others under challenge or by others, provided that it was established that they too knew the person sufficiently and gave the impression of a certain reliability. And with this the whole matter was settled. In nothing, as you see, complicated; rather brief in form.

§33. Not a crisis law. We also do not hesitate for a moment to maintain our proposal in principle, regardless of the form, and we still see those Anti-revolutionary Jurists who, without further argument, went along with the Liberal system, also somewhat facing the proof that either my proposal cannot achieve its purpose, or that it would be impractical. It is precisely with a view to this that we have repeatedly insisted that the Oath question should not be dealt with in passing and by surprise, but that the decision on this institution should be postponed until such time as a definitive draft emerges that aims to raise the debates on this weighty issue to that high level that the sacred character of the matter itself demands. Postponement of consideration and decision is therefore all the more permissible, because the Government side continues to insist that it is alien to any intention to banish the Oath from the courtroom, and he who is convicted on a declaration without Oath, continues to raise the objection that full justice has not been done to him. It can therefore not be emphasized enough that in the Oath question one must once and for all cease to see only the difficulty for the summoned witness, and to understand all the duty to also revive the question of how to watch over the condemnation of an innocent person on false testimony. Briefly, to add this, this issue can be reduced to the proposition that those who do not believe in God cannot take an Oath, but that there must be a right of assessment to judge the reality of the refusal of the Oath on that ground. From which it follows that the right of assessment of the judge cannot apply here, since he does not know the person concerned closely, and for this reason, as an organ for this right of assessment, persons must appear who know him personally closely, and are thus able to pronounce on the truth and sincerity of his assertion. This organ is, in our opinion, to be found in the Oath helpers.*) *) On April 26 and 27, 1916, the Oath issue was decided in the First Chamber by the adoption of the amended draft submitted by Minister Ort. The adoption took place with 33 against 10 votes. The Roman Catholics voted almost all in favor, with the exception only of Messrs. Reekers, Haffmans, Van der Biesem and Michiels van Kessenich. Of the Anti-revolutionaries, only Messrs. Lucassen, ‘t Hooft and Franssen voted against. Dr. Kuyper was in Budapest, and was unaware that the decision was about to be made, the more so as the postal correspondence with the Hungarian capital took almost six days. He too would have voted against, just as all Anti-revolutionary members did in the Second Chamber on February 22. It is true that in the previous draft he had pointed out that the position could present itself in such a way that he too would vote in favor, but only if by doing so a concession could be obtained. This, however, was not at all the case here. What was now under consideration was the most unfavorable draft that could be imagined, and even the Minister’s explanation colored it even more unfavorably. It can therefore only be confessed that the adoption of this draft in the First Chamber was one of our most grievous disappointments that could be imagined.

Analytical Summary: The Social Question. With a brief mention of the social question, let us refer to these examples and conclude. In the 16th century, there was no social question. The Peasants’ Revolt in Germany seemed to point in that direction, but it arose from a completely different motive. What called the social question into a very peculiar form of life in the 19th century was twofold. Firstly, the fact that the increased technology made it possible for factories to arise, in which hundreds of workers were set to work simultaneously; something which naturally caused the close and cordial relationship between employer and worker to be entirely lost. And secondly, the more mature development of the working class, thanks to the high flight that popular education also took in connection with vocational education. This higher position of the worker demanded more abundant satisfaction of the needs of social community life, and the heaping up of hundreds of workers outside of all higher and more intimate connection with the employer, brought into existence conditions that our fathers had never known. Not that our fathers had no care for the workers. The Guild system certainly promoted an excellent understanding to no small degree. The needs of the working class spoke less loudly; but the needs, as far as they spoke, were then as a rule well and not too narrowly satisfied. What only characterized the then existing condition in a less desirable way, was the excessive dependence in which the worker found himself on his employer. Slavery had come to an end, but the conviction had not yet gained entrance that the worker, as a free man, stood not under, but alongside his employer, in civil rights and in social life. But if one now asks whether this then existing condition could be logically deduced from the principle we profess, in the sense that the introduction of an entirely changed condition would be a denial of that principle, then it must be answered with absolute decisiveness, that a denial of principle can only come into question with him who, where such a totally different condition has entered, refuses to reveal the power of his principle also in the solution of the questions now presenting themselves.
[426]

Page 427—-

STATE ORGANIZATION AND STATE ENTERPRISE. 415

CHAPTER XIV
THE FINANCES.

§1. State Organization and State Enterprise. Regarding the Finances, we can therefore suffice with a shorter discussion in this chapter, because we still defend as correct the main lines regarding this so important question, which were drawn in “Our Program.” Brevity is also possible because the financial question, as far as these public bodies are concerned, has already been discussed more extensively in Chapters VIII and IX. It may even be gratefully acknowledged that since 1878 something has also been recognized in our official State life, which at the time could only be brought forward as a protest. We are here particularly referring to the distinction between the financial management that flows from the organization of the State, and that entirely different management that has no other character than that of a State enterprise. These two categories of management differ not only in form, but in principle, and in such a way that the second category, that of the Enterprise, could if necessary be entirely eliminated, and either be left to the Municipalities or to private individuals; while conversely the entire other financial management, which is required by the character of the State as an organism, can only be entrusted to the rulers of the Realm. This has now been officially recognized, at least in the case of the Postal, Telegraph and Telephone services, the State Printing Office, etc. In our State budget, one has now gone so far that what is purely an enterprise is kept separate and accounted for separately. Yet we cannot be satisfied even with the external acknowledgment that this is about enterprises here. Our protest against the direction that refuses to recognize this as the outflow of the organic character of our State system must also be maintained in the recognition of what is not an enterprise.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[428]

416 THE FINANCES.

It cannot be denied, however, that the view that everything done on the financial terrain by the Government is to be regarded as a transaction between the Authorities and the individual citizens, or independent persons, found defense not only in an earlier period and well into the 19th century, but is even now still advocated. This came out very strongly in the problem of tolls on the highways. This toll system was based on the view that the traveler, whoever he may be, owes the Realm compensation for the construction and maintenance of the State roads. There are still a few local tolls left where even the pedestrian paid a toll or bridge fee, but from the side of the Realm, at least in our country, such a toll on pedestrians has long since been abolished.

§2. The theory of Montesquieu. The fundamental error in this view was that the Government was regarded as a power that guaranteed the possession and enjoyment of his own property and income to the individual citizen, and therefore demanded rightful payment for this service. Especially Montesquieu pleaded extensively for this system, which was then dubbed the “assurance theory.” The citizens were then in the position of persons who did not know how they would obtain the same protection for their possessions or income. Both were constantly in danger. And now the Government offered itself, or rather, now that many citizens together had established a Government, which could provide the otherwise completely lacking security. Now if the Government proved this important service to everyone who had to secure certain property or certain income, it followed naturally that one had to compensate the Government for the expenses it had incurred for this, and moreover to ensure the Government itself such a salary that its existence would not be endangered by lack of means. The whole representation, however, rests on the false assumption of the Contrat Social, and may therefore be regarded as no longer finding defenders who can claim a hearing.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program.
