In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[429]

THE THEORY OF MONTESQUIEU - TWO KINDS OF GOVERNMENTAL FUNCTIONS. This theory, and with it the fundamental idea of the insurance system, though in a modified form, was revived. The difference consisted merely in this, that in this second system the citizens were taken as pure individuals in an even more stark manner. The one has more, the other less profit from the care of the Government. To one who possesses no capital, the Government need not by itself guarantee the safe and unimpaired possession. Imagine two citizens side by side, the one who has nothing but a weekly wage of say 10 guilders, and the other who annually receives the interest on a ton of gold—the State cannot cover that wage laborer, because there is almost nothing to cover with him. The possessor of the ton of gold, on the other hand, needs all his ample coverage, in order not to be disturbed in his quiet possession. From this, they then tried to deduce that therefore nothing was to be claimed from the wage laborer, while on the contrary the man of the ton was under obligation to the State or the Government to reimburse the services rendered to him and the costs, and to offer an honorary reward. This theory of compensation, however, could not maintain the favor it had enjoyed for a short time in public opinion, because it actually rested on the false idea that the State was merely a casual agglomeration of independent persons, and thus completely misunderstood the organic character of the life of the people. Also practically, this theory proved to offer a wrong starting point. It led to the result of making only a certain part of the population liable to pay. And not only this, but it soon became more and more impossible to calculate according to a fixed standard what each individual citizen had actually received from the State, and thus to determine the rightful share of each citizen in the common burdens.

§3. Two Kinds of Governmental Functions. Only an excessively penetrating confusion of concepts kept this theory of compensation in force for a fairly long period, and it is this that should be pointed out here. The Government performs, for the benefit of the subjects, two kinds of activities, and from these two kinds the first flows quite certainly and of itself, but the other only at will and as an incidental result of the task of the Government, in connection with the character of the State. Regulating the import at the borders is something that cannot be left to each border village; this import, and likewise the export, touches the relationship in which our State finds itself towards the neighboring State that lies on our borders. There is therefore no question of the citizens being able to regulate this interest for themselves. Here the one central government authority must act as the power over the whole people. On the one hand, therefore, a series of obligations rests on the Government, which it must fulfill for the benefit and on behalf of the entire population, and which cannot be reduced to the task of the individual citizens. As has already been remarked, this also applies to what concerns the Army and the Navy, to what concerns the care for the payment of interest and redemption of the national debt, to what concerns the colonial possessions in other climes, to what concerns the Department of Foreign Affairs, and so much more. §4. Of a Completely Different Kind. Yet in all this it is always a task that is to be performed for the benefit of the whole people as a national unity, even in connection with its past thought. Of a completely different kind, on the other hand, is the task that is entrusted to the Government more for convenience, even though the citizenry itself could have acted for it. The sending of letters and documents was still largely done through private assistance even in the Reformation period. If one heard that someone was going to Russia, and one had family or business connections in Russia, one would then request such a departing person to be so kind as to take along such a letter or such a document. The mutual traffic, as far as letters and documents were concerned, was at that time so extremely meager and insignificant that it hardly counted. This has now all been completely changed by the rise of the postal service; a state function to which the telegraph and telephone service has since been added. Naturally, one could also have brought about these various services for mutual traffic through associations of private individuals, and in more than one sense this has also happened. Especially one sees this in the Railways. In Belgium and in Germany

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[430]

418 FINANCES. the private companies were the first to build the railways, and only later did the State take over the management. The same applies to the telegraph and telephone. The State did not create these means of communication, but only later took them over and organized them. This second kind of governmental activity, therefore, is not rooted in the essential nature of the State, but is rather a matter of convenience, which the State has taken over from the citizens, either because the citizens themselves did not feel inclined to organize it, or because the State could perform this task more efficiently and more economically. This second kind of governmental activity, therefore, is not an essential part of the task of the State, but is rather an incidental addition, which the State has taken over from the citizens, either because the citizens themselves did not feel inclined to organize it, or because the State could perform this task more efficiently and more economically.

[431]

OF A DIFFERENT KIND. - AGAINST FIXED PAYMENT.

419 It was the government that provided for the railways; whereas here, on the other hand, various railway companies have emerged that have a private character. Yet it has also become increasingly clear to us, 1° that these private companies must be placed under strict law, and that their traffic cannot remain without closer regulation by the state; as was most clearly evident in the great railway strike of 1903. And 2° that these private companies need the assurance from the government that, if the covering income does not meet the costs, the state will step in or take over the entire traffic. From this one already sees that the activities of the government that have a business character, yet appear in such a different character, that one can say of the one business that the state really no longer needs to concern itself with it, while the other business can hardly be taken care of sufficiently in any other way than by the government. All kinds of freight and passenger transport services are still private, without this being a hindrance; the post, on the other hand, is hardly conceivable as anything other than public.

§5. Against fixed payment. Yet all these state services that have the character of a business have this in common, that whoever makes use of them must pay a fixed price each and every time. We pay separately for each piece that goes by post, in the form of a postage stamp. Likewise, we pay directly for each telegram. Also where the railway system has passed into the hands of the government, each traveler pays for his ticket each time, or does so by subscription. The peculiar character of all these business services is therefore that whoever wants to make use of them may do so, but whoever does make use of them must pay the due amount as often as they do so. It is indeed the individual citizen for whom the government opens the opportunity to make use of its good services, and whoever makes use of them pays what it costs the government, and this even to the full for their share, and often even a little more, so that such a business commonly also yields an extra income for the government. This is so with the post, with the telegraph, with the telephone; it is the same in Belgium and in Prussia even with the railway system. The same applies to gas plants, water supply, and so much more, although here it is usually only the municipal authorities that concern themselves with it, and not the state. There is a distinction here, but the character of the business remains. But even if one sets aside everything that has the character of a business, there still remains for the government an ever-expanding task that comes to it from the essence of the state, and it is in this fundamental task that the financial involvement of the state, apart from any business idea, presents itself as a matter of course. Naturally, with the business activities of the government, all kinds of arbitrariness have a place. As is known, there are countries where the government itself has made a monopoly of more than one business, as for example of the sale of tobacco and matches, and this makes a distinction in so far that the government in the monopoly does not take on the business activity in order to accommodate the citizenry, but solely with a view to its own gain. This certainly cannot be recommended, as it hinders the free life activity of the subject; but even so, such a monopoly remains a supporting business, and can never be derived from the essence of the state or from the national existence of the people.

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[432]

420 FINANCE.

§6. The government better than the private individual.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.

Does it now follow from this that, in order to keep the governmental character of the state pure, all business of this kind must again be handed over to private initiative? In part, yes, as is the case in particular with the public school, which has also been introduced in part as a business. It was especially with it that it became evident to what a mistaken outcome the desire for all kinds of interference by the state can lead. For the rest, on the other hand, there are many services of which the business can be regulated and taken care of by no one better than the government. History also shows that, with the ongoing development of the people, all kinds of businesses of this kind, which for centuries had a completely private character, had to be taken over by the government precisely because of the richer development of the people.

[433]

AGAINST FIXED PAYMENT - THE GOVERNMENT BETTER THAN THE PRIVATE SECTOR.

The government can take over what the private sector has appropriated. There is no fixed rule to be set here. Each intervention must be judged on its own merits. Not only is the school system increasingly reverting to the private sector, but in our colonial administration, private initiative has also come increasingly to the fore. Naturally, whenever the state’s finances are involved, the question must always be considered whether the state’s finances are improved or worsened by it. But even if a state enterprise must be maintained for this reason, what is a state enterprise must always be sharply separated from the financial issues that the state organism presents. What the government does in business, it takes over from the care that was otherwise imposed on its subjects, and solely on the ground that private initiative is not able to do it just as well, just as quickly and just as cheaply as the government. There is only a preference at play here. Just as any citizen can switch from one merchant to another if the latter can serve him better, quicker and cheaper, so too do the subjects naturally switch from private initiative to the government enterprise, if they see that the latter yields them more profit. In nothing, however, does this touch the proper state task, nor the primordial vocation of the government. That task and that vocation arise from the people’s being as such. This people’s being is not the result of the private good judgment of the subjects who come together in assemblies, but has its roots in a past that spans centuries, arises from a common nature that perpetuates itself from generation to generation, and which for that very reason manifests a character that differs from the national character of other nations, continually unfolds in a completely peculiar way, and works even in the newly arising generation, even though it still lies in the cradle, because the people does not make its nature itself, but has it gently bestowed upon it by its ancestors, entirely outside of its own free will. Imagine that in a people all persons above the age of ten suddenly died on one day - even then, within the next twenty years, a people would grow up with the same national sense, because it was of the same origin. The organism of the entire people governs this, and determines the character of the masses. This became immediately apparent again in 1914 and thereafter in the great world war. It was not the individual persons who then set the tone, but people stood against people, and all political difference

[434]

422 FINANCES.week, in order to do justice to the organic unity of the People.

§7. Concentration on the Realm.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how

The principle of the French Revolution also led financially to deprive the constituent parts of the State of their relative independence, until finally nothing remained but the all-providing State. So it also went with us under the influence of this revolutionary principle. The finances of the Municipalities and the Provinces thereby came to stand in an increasingly unfavorable relationship to the finances of the Realm. For all the Provinces together, the total of all revenues in 1911 was no more than 11¼ million, while the expenditures amounted to only 10¾ million. With loans excluded, the budget of all Municipalities together in that same year was 161¼ million, and against these two amounts of 173 million stood the Realm’s budget in 1914 with a sum of 208½ million, already rising in 1913, i.e. two years later, to 239½. Set against this now Switzerland, where the Cantons retained such a degree of independence, and one finds that the Realm’s revenues in 1911 amounted to no more than 98 million francs, i.e. about 47½ million guilders, while the population stood at 3¾ million. In the rest, the Cantons provided. In the United States of North America, a total ordinary receipt of 938½ million dollars for the entire Commonwealth was calculated in 1911, while the separate States together had 620 million dollars. Had one then also in 1813/15 left more of their former independence to the formerly sovereign Provinces, so would also with us the Realm’s finances stand much lower, and in contrast the Provinces show a quite different budget. But precisely this partly autonomous character of Provinces and Municipalities the principle of the French Revolution did not tolerate. All autonomy is now also in France broken and almost nullified; and in 1813/15 one has, out of fear that the Provinces with us might rise up against the unity of the Realm, placed them all, also financially, under the guardianship of the Realm.

[435]

CONCENTRATION ON THE REALM. - DIRECT AND INDIRECT TAXATION. 423 A total revenue for all Provinces of only 11¼ million, in 1911, of which then, to consider the matter substantially, still 2½ million are borrowed funds and almost 5 million for Realm surcharges, so that in fact from their own resources and levies there remains barely four million for all the Provinces together, is, with a view to Provincial autonomy, a mockery.

§8. Direct and Indirect Taxation.

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.

As for the contrast between direct and indirect taxation, the leading position of the French Revolution has here lost its play. The absolute demand of the newly arisen system actually came down to this, that only one single tax would be levied, and that in this tax all citizens would contribute in proportion to their annual income. It seemed so natural. The community certainly needed a certain amount for state administration and state defense. In the community one found the citizens as members. Whoever had any income at all per year, therefore had to contribute in proportion. Preferably, everyone should pay this contribution voluntarily for their share. And so all the financial needs of the State would be provided for of their own accord. But soon it became apparent that this was a utopia, not at all capable of being realized, and so the indirect levies soon went through again as of old. With the exception of the German Realm’s finances, it can then also be said that now still in only a few German States, in Austria, in England, in France, in Russia and in Italy, not to speak of the smaller States, the indirect taxes deliver the lion’s share in the finances of these States. The indirect taxes have their pros and cons. In their favor speaks that the discharge of them goes as it were of itself and unnoticed, that one has it not largely in one’s power whether one will pay in it or not. This is especially evident with the consumption taxes. What was imported from abroad, fell under it as a matter of course. This was the case with wine, all kinds of distilled liquors, beer, tobacco, coffee, tea and much more. With us one left tobacco largely free, but as a rule it can be said that all such luxury articles, whether imported from abroad or even manufactured in the country itself, were affected either by the Tariff or by the excises.

[436]

FINANCES. As we have already seen, this trend even went so far in France that they sought to make such objects into state monopolies, just as we later did with opium in our colonies. The octroy has been abolished here at home, with a few minor exceptions, but in France this means of revenue was still maintained on a large scale. Even our excise duties alone yielded a total of nearly 67 million guilders in 1913, that is, more than a quarter of the entire budget, while all direct taxes together brought in only 52¾ million. If we now add to this the import and export duties, etc., amounting to 17 million, and other indirect burdens that yielded 32¼ million, this already amounts to nearly half of our entire budget. Yet it was soon realized that our budget was steadily climbing too high to be covered mainly by what came in indirectly; something that had to be acknowledged more clearly when the high indirect taxes began to weigh too heavily on consumption, and thereby also harmed industry, both on the side of the manufacturers and on that of the wage earners and shopkeepers. Hence an income tax could no longer be avoided, and this in such a way that only the lowest income classes remained free from it, but for the rest all the different classes of the citizenry had to be affected pro rata according to their income. The limit indicated here is that the tax must not touch the income itself in its sources, since an annual decline in the national income through taxation, without a corresponding increase from other quarters, would of itself diminish the national wealth from year to year, and thereby undermine the vital strength of the People. The difficulty remains, however, that estimating the actual income of each citizen continues to be a struggle; a reason why we would prefer to see the levy of such a tax pass from the State to the Municipalities. In the Municipalities, one’s neighbors’ incomes are estimated most accurately.

§9. Personal and real estate taxes. A piece of land of some extent must pay land tax every year, quite apart from the person to whom it belongs. The

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[437]

PERSONAL AND REAL ESTATE TAXES. The value of such a piece of land lies in the land itself, and it is the government that, through the proper administration of the country’s affairs, causes the intrinsic value of the land to rise or fall to no small degree. If the government took care that the access roads could naturally perform their service to increase the value of the piece of land, if the waterways were well maintained, and if measures were taken against water damage as far as possible—then, through all this, the monetary value of the land would increase, and it would naturally follow that a certain sum had to be paid annually to the government from the yield of what grew on the land as compensation. Already in Israel, as is well known, not less than a tenth of the yield was set aside. But the very fact that the land enjoys this care of the government, entirely apart from the person of the owner, makes that in the purchase and sale, this real estate tax is taken into account impersonally. The buyer then pays so much less as the tax capital value represents. The tax is actually included in the lower purchase price, and when calculated precisely, the buyer is not really paying any tax, since the interest on the lesser amount paid covers the tax. Of course, changes occur here, as when the value of the land is increased by the construction of a railway or a canal. The real estate tax must also constantly take this into account and must rise as the pure value of the piece of land goes up. This is fair, since the owner, without doing anything, has seen the value of his property increase. In the case of a decrease in value, the opposite effect should be applied. It is the same with built property, especially in the cities. A municipal administration can make such changes in the layout of the city that a house, which thus yielded a rental price of 1,000 guilders, climbs to a rental price of 1,500 guilders. An inflexible system of real estate tax, and of real taxes in general, must therefore be so arranged that it takes these declines and increases into account. If it is solely the government’s interference that brings your house from 1,000 to 1,500 guilders per year, then it is only fair that the tax that is paid for such a property should also be increased. Consumption and luxury articles are also generally counted among the objects of real taxes, since everyone has it in their power to refrain from such objects or to use them.

[438]

FINANCES. The personal taxes, on the other hand, are of a completely different nature. These include poll tax, income tax, wealth tax, and business tax, etc. Here, one is dealing with income that represents the person in their personal capacity, and which, if they leave the country or die, changes in character. A person’s personal possessions depend on what they have inherited or acquired through their own enterprise. They are lord and master over what they own or possess. They alone have authority over it. Now, if the government provides them with all the necessary information to safeguard their possessions or property, it is a well-ordered state of affairs in the country, which, thanks to the government’s care, makes social action with this capital or this income possible, so that if you think the government’s care away, it would shrink like snow in the sun, then it is only right and fair that the government receives compensation in the form of taxes for this care that secures their possessions, and indeed a tax that increases proportionately upwards. And this last not out of consideration that the wealthy possessor can pay more, but because for large sources of income, the government’s dominion possesses a higher value, not only extensively in terms of the amount of the property or income, but also intensively, insofar as for the safeguarding of the larger capital or income, much more serious measures have to be taken. For the security of an income of 10,000 guilders, much more is required from the government than for the guarantee of an income of 10 guilders per week. The great difficulty remains, however, to estimate possessions and income with precision. Although hoarding in silence has a very serious downside, just as this so often turns out with the miser, yet it weighs on the household and can have a detrimental effect on upbringing, if an 11-year-old boy already knows precisely what his father earns each year. There lies in this temporal mystery a power that can very certainly work for good. In general, it may also be added here that the national government is also obliged, in its financial policy, to proceed cautiously and modestly. Care must be taken that the tax terms are so regulated that they fit the practice of life. The tone in which the government speaks about taxes should not be too harsh or too threatening. The costs of collection must

[439]

PERSONAL AND MATERIAL TAXES - THE TARIFF. 427 As much as possible, this should remain limited. And even if the imposition of taxes is according to the absolute proportionality of the position, an ideal that will never be reached, yet it must be felt by the citizenry that the Government intends a precise and strict proportionality, and has an open ear for any complaint that provides proof of a shortcoming in what the requirement of justice demands here. Precisely for this reason, the establishment of the budget must also always stand under parliamentary authority. No people may have this right to decide on this taken away from them. The entire constitutional system is never otherwise than by the maintenance of this right to be guaranteed.

§10. The Tariff. The most suitable method to also achieve a more effective tariff of import duties here can remain outside of discussion. For this is a question of tactics, which, as the circumstances change, will have to be solved in very different ways. Our party has been pushing the Tariff increase more to the forefront since 1878, and eventually went so far as to include it in the Election Program. At first, this did not succeed. Notwithstanding the fact that our party’s Election Program also seriously addressed the Tariff increase, we still managed to achieve a significant victory in 1901. Also in 1905, the Left’s struggle against the Tariff increase was only tangential. This remained so in 1909, when we once again knocked the Liberal Party out of the saddle with the Tariff in our Election Program. It was only in the 1913 election that the Left has taken the Tariff as their main weapon against us, and, supported by foreign suppliers whose imports were threatened, set in motion a campaign with heaps of money that resulted in our defeat. This is something we will have to reckon with in the future. There is indeed a danger that a similar campaign against the Tariff increase, as was set in motion in 1913 and succeeded, will be immediately repeated, so that we will once again bring it up in our Election Program. Hence it seems advisable, in view of the experience gained in 1913, to change the method; from now on, not to bring about the Tariff increase all at once, but to do so in parts; and

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.
[440]

428 THE FINANCES. to begin with this immediately, so that we regain mastery of the terrain. The Liberal Cabinets have also, on their part, caused the yield of the Tariff to increase. In 1885, now thirty years ago, the Tariff on imports and exports still only brought in 5 million, and since then it has climbed to an amount of 177* million. Now our population has grown from 4 1/4 to 5 1/4 million in those years, yet it is clear how considerable an increase in revenue, even proportionally, was contained in this climb from 5 to 17 million. However, there is no need to go into this in more detail here. The issue here is election tactics, and this can only be decided at the ballot box. Even the claim that the appearance of Mr. Kolkman’s bill had caused the vote to turn against us in 1913 does not hold water. In 1909 they had the virtually identical draft of the former Minister Harte van Tecklenburg before them, and yet this did not prevent our brilliant electoral success in 1909. In 1913, however, the foreign suppliers had become more alert. Then the money poured in. It was this money that ruined us. And now that these suppliers have passed the test, there is no doubt that they will attack us in the same way time and again.

§11. Mixed with us. Now as for the Tariff itself, there is by no means a smooth free trade among us. On some articles even a very high import duty is levied, such as 50% on beer, 25% on sugar goods, 10% on cigars, and an equal amount on carpets, but these are exceptions to the rule. Overall, what is now levied, if not nothing or a very insignificant amount, and apart from that, the Netherlands still enjoys the honor of being counted among the Free Trade countries. A fixed rule of conduct is not to be established here, nor is it to be derived from a principle professed by us. There may very well come days when the urge will be overwhelming to abolish even what we now still levy. It must only be confessed that the levying of import duties has been the practice followed by almost all countries from ancient times until now. In justice, it can be demanded,

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[441]

MIXED AMONG US.429 That whoever resorts to our market to bring his wares here, compensation is given for his profit, for indeed his sale here in our land also takes place under the protection of our entire state system, and it cannot be unreasonable that he should also contribute to the costs of the government administration. This dominant idea has now also been put into practice everywhere. Even in England; and what England has given the name as the land of free trade is not to be explained by the fact that England did not also benefit from levying duties on imports from abroad, but only by the fact that it did this in a quite peculiar way, partly by levying tariffs only on what was not produced in England itself, and partly by shifting it to very high excise duties. To avoid the war years, it may be noted here that England in 1913 levied £36,207,793 on customs and £41,159,574 on excise duties. Together, therefore, 76 million pounds sterling, on a population of just over 46 million. Higher, therefore, almost anywhere else, while, as is known, in 1915, for the sake of the war, even the customs duties were considerably expanded. If one now consults the statistical data with regard to other countries, one finds that all the larger States are fully engaged in tariff grazing, and that the smaller States, with which we can better compare ourselves, do the same. Belgium’s budget still accounted for 6774 million francs in customs duties in 1913, on a population of 7 1/2 million. Switzerland already drew in 1911 almost 81 million francs, on a population of 3 3/4 million. Sweden drew from its tariff in 1914, from its customs and excise duties together, 111 million crowns; the crown calculated at 1 fr. 39 c; on a population of about 6 million. And so it is more or less with all the smaller States, not a single one excepted. Whether in America or in Europe, in Asia or in Africa, high tariffs are levied everywhere, and in fact the Netherlands is the only smaller country where any kind of serious opposition to an increase is still being contested. The advocates of the free trade system among us are therefore under the presumptive obligation to show on what grounds the Netherlands would be called upon to deviate from the rule that the whole world has always followed, and still follows, in principle. They cannot do this, and therein lies their weakness.

[442]

430 ON FINANCES.

§12. The World Empire. It would certainly be conceivable in itself that all tariffs were abolished and free trade for all goods was allowed throughout the world, in every land, by the entire human race. Then it would certainly be equal for all lands, and the consequences of it would be felt everywhere. Countries rich in mining, agriculture, and manufacturing would then overflow with trade and become ever richer, while conversely countries with little underneath and little above the ground would also lose more and more of their manufacturing and impoverish. The idea of the world empire would then also be carried out to the utmost in the financial sphere, and the materially favorable or unfavorable conditions would determine the fate of the various peoples and nations. Against this material starting point, the nations have constantly risen up out of a desire for self-preservation; partly to increase their revenues, partly to better secure their own production and manufacturing, they have placed tariffs on the borders and levied import duties. England also did this in the early period of the 19th century, and only when it had gradually, thanks to its overseas possessions and its mighty fleet, secured for itself a dominant world position, did it grant free entry for what it itself produced, at least in its tariff, if not in its excise duties. It owed a temporarily unparalleled success to this. Yet here too a turning point came. The Conservatives turned around and aimed at restoring the earlier tariff, while even the Liberals went along with this during the war years. When England turned around for the first time, the economists here too have also changed their opinion, and have also managed to push through the reduction of the Tariff Act. This has promoted the emigration of the factory population to Germany in a very striking way. Whereas the number of Dutchmen in Germany did not exceed 30,000 before, their figure stood at 144,175 persons in 1910. This placed the Netherlands second on the list for immigration. Austria had accommodated 6 1/4 million of its nationals in Germany, and we immediately followed with almost 145,000. In proportion to the population, the Netherlands therefore had the most of its sons go out to Germany to find work. It thus became the opposite of what it had been before. In the days of the hand-loom weavers, it was the Germans who came here to seek work, and now, on the contrary, it was the embarrassed Dutchmen who went out in search of wage-earning labor to the economically much better-off Germany. Something that deserves all the more emphasis, because it is exactly the same with Belgium. According to the figures for 1910, over 70,000 Dutchmen went from the Netherlands to Belgium, while the number of Belgians coming here was no more than 18,338. Belgium had about 7 1/2 million inhabitants, and yet more than three times as many Dutchmen went from here to Belgium as Belgians came to us.

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[443]

§13. Any absolute system unthinkable for the time being. It may therefore be stated without reservation that, under the given circumstances, the desire on our part to enforce the free trade system cannot be justified in any way. Even if one admits, as we have always done, that such a system, if universally accepted, can be conceived and accepted—as it now stands, the wish of anyone who aims at it cannot in any way be granted. The state revenues do not flow so abundantly that we can miss the tens and more millions that we sacrifice through our current system. Especially when it was a matter of the Invalids Insurance and Free Trade, it was self-evident that we could not give up such a concrete and strongly speaking interest for the sake of an exception in this. But apart from this, the too free import is the cause—that a very large quantity of what we need in all kinds of ways, and which could very well be supplied here, now comes to us from abroad; the consequence of which is that the foreigner attracts the financial profit, gets various kinds of manufacturing here, and that many workers who would otherwise find plenty of work must now cross the border to manufacture for German manufacturers in Germany what they could normally have prepared in their own country for our own manufacturers.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[444]

432 PUBLIC FINANCES. The foreign competition here did not cut prices in a way that excluded all competition. Now it is true that industry here has adapted to the financial-economic regulation of Liberalism, and consequently, especially in Overijssel, a native industry has flourished, which does excellent business, especially in the East. However, this is nothing strange. This always happens. England showed in its tariff reversal how Ireland became the dupe of that reversal, and how not only British India, but also Ireland literally impoverished under England’s economic prosperity. Although our industry has adapted with rare energy to the newly entered situation, yet this was and remained nothing more than a bandage on the bleeding, as they say. It was, and still is, an impossible situation that we are clothed from Germany and provided for in all kinds of necessities of life, and that our men take care of China and Japan. Charity well commanded begins with oneself, and so it is also with industry and trade. The Netherlands should not let other countries dictate the law here, but first of all know how to provide for its own vital needs. As it now stands, almost everything in this area has been neglected with us. Hardly anything was done for the industrial education of our workers. The trade schools themselves could not yield sufficient profit, since the teaching staff was too low. And when at least in 1904 an attempt was made to establish in Haarlem what Germany had had for a long time in many cities, this was even opposed by the friends of Free Trade. Now in Rotterdam at least a first step has been taken with the Commercial School. But for the rest, it is still the pseudo-love for Free Trade that has kept us backward in every field.

[445]

SHAME AND PROPRIETY. 433

CHAPTER XV
THE PUBLIC PROPRIETY.

§1. Shame and propriety.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.

At the beginning of the first and second period of man’s life on this earth, shame comes to the fore. First with Adam and Eve, the second time with Noah. Of course, both Adam and Eve were created naked, and lived without clothing in Paradise. As long as the sense of absolute innocence suppressed the sinful in them, no feeling of shame could arise in them. But as soon as the loosening from God did not become dominant in their soul life, the feeling of shame arose in them. In Genesis III:6 we are told that they ate of the forbidden tree, and immediately thereafter in vs. 7 we read that they made themselves aprons of fig leaves to cover their nakedness. And that this outburst of shame was pleasing to the Lord is evident from Genesis III:21, where we are told that God made garments of animal skins for Adam and his wife and clothed them. Here, then, there is a twofold thing: on the one hand, the arising of the feeling of shame, and on the other hand, the ordinance of God to cover the shameful parts of the body. In connection with this, it is most remarkable that the second period of human life, which dates from Noah after the Flood, also immediately draws attention to something that had to do with shame. For we read in Genesis IX:20: “Noah began to be a man of the soil, and he planted a vineyard; he drank of the wine and became drunk, and he uncovered himself within his tent.” Not, as is often depicted, in the open field, but in his tent. Ham first pointed this out to his brothers. And then Shem and Japheth “covered the nakedness of their father,” and turned their faces backward so that they did not see their father’s nakedness. Awakened, Noah noticed this, and now the period of Common Grace begins.

[446]

434 PUBLIC DECENCY. Here the prophetic voice sounds forth, that the curse falls upon Canaan, the blessing descends upon Shem, and that Japheth shares in Shem’s blessing. The two mighty sinful factors, which come from the lust of intoxication, are thus introduced as all-controlling in significance—the first at the Fall in Paradise, and the second after the Flood. Whether wine or strong drink was drunk even before the Flood, we are now left to conjecture. The fact is that in the period before the Flood, with all the horrors that arose, no word was mentioned of anything that could intoxicate. This therefore presupposes the possibility that they may have drunk grape juice, but that the preparation of wine only came later. But however this may be, it is clear from the observation that at both the beginnings of human life, both in Paradise and after the Flood, shame gives the main signal and shamelessness provokes the wrath of God. Now if the Government is the handmaid of God, it follows that it is called to curb shamelessness in public life, and this now sets the duty upon the Government to watch over propriety in public life.

§2. Private and Public. It is noteworthy here that in Paradise the clothing of Adam and Eve with animal skins already took place when they were still alone. Shame thus extended to the fellowship of man and woman. Even here the animal must be avoided. Now if shame goes so far that it sets a feeling of shame even between man and woman, and thus in the whole family, and even in the most intimate intercourse, it is self-evident that especially in public life, whatever approaches nakedness provokes the sense of shame and calls for covering, and since in the life on the way, on the street, at windows and open doors, this awareness of shame has an absolute dominion, it lies in the nature of the relationships that the Government, as the guardian of public life, must prohibit, punish and suppress all that is shameless. With the Government this is called: watching “over public decency.” Immediately here the profound difference between two spheres of life comes to the fore. There is a private and there is a public human life. The first is lived within chambers and in a closed house, the other on the public domain, which is open to everyone. In connection with this distinction, it must always be kept in mind that the Government has to abstain from the first, but on the other hand has to subject everything to its will on the second. The English expression: “my home is my castle,” speaks sharply, but not at all exaggeratedly, even though there is often talk of shame and propriety. Within the home, the head of the household is responsible, outside the home the Government. Something to which it must also be noted that everything that gives an insight into domestic life from the outside, and thus also every room or house to which everyone who wishes has free access, falls under the public domain.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[447]

PRIVATE AND PUBLIC - IMMORALITY. If one professes with the Apostle in Rom. XIII:1 ff. that the powers that be are ordained by God, and that the Government is the handmaid of God, then it follows that the watching over public decency must also be carried out for God’s sake, and in obedience to His ordinances.

§3. Immorality. We speak intentionally of Immorality, not only of prostitution, and still less of the shameful nature of the sin to conceal it, as is often done in the French word. What the Government has to prevent from the public domain is any immoral display or action. As for the display, this includes, first of all, the improper clothing, which was previously self-evident among us, but in recent times has taken an increasingly shameless form in our cities, both through the partial exposure of women’s breasts on the street, and through the shortening and splitting of skirts, and to some extent also through the tight tension of women’s attire around the body, with no other purpose than to display the luxuriant forms of the female body as strongly as possible to the eye. It is especially the power of fashion that causes this evil to proliferate so rapidly, and further the dominance of Parisian fashion, which, if it does not actually create the evil, at least promotes it in a very detrimental way. The sense of shame diminishes as a result. The damage done by displays in the field of art will be discussed later.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[448]

The public decency. The most difficult part remains. In this section, the art remains outside the discussion. All the more reason to emphasize here the indecent acts that have become all too common in street prostitution; and this in two forms, partly as direct street prostitution and partly in the establishments that emanated from the brothel. It cannot be denied that, thanks to the energetic actions of Mr. Pierson, this evil has also gradually diminished. The so brutally enticing, addressing and half-coercing of gentlemen and young men by public women on the public streets has decreased. Yet it is far from gone, and nothing of this nature may be tolerated by the police. The brothels helped little in this, by placing a prostitute at the door as a local, or by providing a glimpse through a transparent curtain into an illuminated room where the prostitutes displayed themselves. Especially the so-called visitation gave a kind of recommendation, that the public house itself brought with it a certain assurance of lesser danger. With the abolition of the visitation, which fortunately continues to progress further among us, this evil has also been tempered, although one should not imagine that prostitution has given up the display of its establishments.

[449]

The Police. - The high character of marriage. The impure understanding between the police and prostitution remains an evil that must be guarded against here above all. The brothel keepers and even the private prostitutes once had an interest in remaining on familiar terms with the police. The police officer would then pretend not to see what was going on, and the brothel keeper did not tighten the purse strings and granted the lustful agent free access to his subjects. As long as the visitation continued and brought in evil money, this wicked practice could not be curbed. Now, on the other hand, this evil must be rooted out with all one’s might. Every commissioner and police agent must know that the slightest improper understanding with this will be punished with loss of office. The personal and strictly domestic in immorality does not fall under the jurisdiction of the government, but insofar as it acts against immorality, its conduct must always be marked by a holy, honorable bearing. An approach that not only leads to not tolerating the evil, but even more to combating it, and that also turns against the immorality itself through discipline. Sinful acts must not only be opposed, but also punished, punished above all by restriction or deprivation of civil rights; a means of chastisement that has been applied all too little among us. The high character of marriage. But apart from this, an equally serious vocation comes to the government in its legal regulation of marriage. This has now been done by the manner in which every married person had the opportunity to compel the judge to dissolve his marriage by means of completely fabricated data. It would be highly desirable if the Law could declare every divorce, whether from table and bed or in the fullest sense, inadmissible. The arrangement borrowed from the Roman Catholic Church from the supposed sacrament of marriage must, as far as the purpose is concerned, be given all honor. The transfer in Ephesians 5:25 ff. of the marriage bond to the bond that binds the Church to Christ pleads for this; and Christ Himself has clearly stated that what Moses allowed, that a man should leave his wife, was only “because of the hardness of their hearts” (see Matthew 19:7,8,9). From an ideal point of view, there can be no doubt that among the regenerate and converted, and these alone have their firm standing in Christ’s Church, the giving of a bill of divorce, and thus all divorce, is condemned. Yet the People’s Church, in which one has increasingly strayed, even among us, has severed the hold on this high ideal. Now there are also constantly cases in the families of members of the church where...

[450]

438 PUBLIC DECENCY. There are conditions of the popular church that, not only in the case of divorce, but also for other reasons, make a life together until death almost impossible, both for the persons themselves and for their children. But precisely where the government allows divorce, there also rests on that government the weighty obligation to completely cut off any possibility of arbitrariness in this matter, and it is precisely this possibility that is now sanctioned by law. The sin of the parents to avenge on the illegitimate children does not appeal to us; but just as much must it be guarded against that the begetting of children outside of marriage gradually takes on a normal character. The marriage of those who first lived together outside of marriage, on the other hand, ought to be facilitated, and it remains the duty of the legislator to also watch over the more licentious for the sake of the child. With a view to all immorality, it is therefore so desirable that in the appointment of mayors, the appointment of aldermen, the election of council members, and above all in the appointment of the police force, and of course also in that of the judiciary, it is strictly avoided to place government power in the hands of those who are known as patrons of immorality.

§6. Birth Prevention. Among the issues that touch on decency, and over which the government ought to exercise oversight, the Neo-Malthusianism and abortion must also be counted without a doubt. The government cannot simply push these two problems aside, because the population question is of great importance for the country and the nation, now and with a view to the future. There was less attention to this as long as the population growth in our country continued from year to year; but now that a reversal threatens here, more attention is also being paid to the state of the population. Especially the example of France in relation to Germany showed how the entire future of a country can be dominated by the decline of the population. Yet it was especially since 1798 that this question also came more to the fore in the scientific field. For then Robert Malthus published his famous Essay on the Principles of Population, in which he argued that a population, provided no checks, as he called them, stood in its way, has the tendency to expand almost endlessly. The population, he thought he could demonstrate, climbs steadily towards the geometric progression of 1, 2, 4, 8, etc., while the progression in the means of subsistence is only arithmetic and does not rise any further than according to the rule of 2, 3, 4. The consequence of this had to be, therefore, that there is always a lowest class of the population that can no longer find proper nourishment, and consequently perishes in misery, in distress and in hunger. Here too, Darwin’s later struggle for existence would apply, and help had to be sought against this.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.
[451]

BIRTH PREVENTION - ALSO AMONG US. 439 All efforts now made to provide for the constantly arising need, such as emigration, colonization, the fight against unemployment and the public poor relief, failed to alleviate this grievous evil. To conclude from this that one must therefore resort to the suppression of births did not, of course, hold up. As long as, especially in Russia, in South America, Australia and wherever else there was still vast, fertile but uninhabited land available, there could be no talk of the need to curb population growth. This, however, did not prevent the so-called Neo-Malthusianism from emerging in England in 1877, through the founding of the so-called Malthusian League and the publication of the monthly magazine The Malthusian. This league, you see, sacrificed the principle of morality to the so-called Populationists, by suggesting all kinds of means that could be used effectively to prevent pregnancy, without the slightest restriction in the exercise of sexual intercourse.

§7. Also among us. Unfortunately, this system also found acceptance among us. Especially in the poorer neighborhoods, fewer children would then be born. The reduction in the number of workers would elicit higher wages. And so, from two sides at once, the social evil of having too many children would be remedied. That such a practice now thrives in private life, and thus in secret, is a matter of great concern.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[452]

The Government cannot prevent this striving, but it is obligated to oppose every public expression of this tendency. And this all the more, because the propagation of this system along public ways also attacks the honor of life, by virtually issuing a free pass to anyone who wishes to indulge in sexual sin outside of marriage, as has now been observed in more than one country, especially among the higher classes, as a harmful consequence of the public advocacy of Neo-Malthusianism.

§8. Abortion. The theory of Abortion stands no better, which consists in this, that one allows the fertilization to proceed unhindered, but then works to expel the approaching fruit, either by means of administered substances, or by perforating the already accessible fetus. In this second system, the assistance of dishonorable midwives and physicians was then invoked, who, mostly for large sums of money, lent themselves to the application of the means of abortion. The temptation at play here consisted in the fact that the physician could once prevent a pregnancy that could become dangerous to the life of the mother, without being able to promise a normal birth of the fetus. In such a case, the system of Abortion had already been applied from ancient times; and this not only in the decay of morals in Greece and Rome, but also out of purely medical consideration. This very fact now seemed to many a physician to be able to provide all kinds of occasion to extend the system of Abortion ever further. Especially in New York, one already counts on the expansion of this system to a tenth of pregnancies. Many, in fact, give preference to Abortion over Neo-Malthusianism, because the first system rarely fails, and the second, if applied early and immediately, only gives an unpleasant sensation for a short time. Whoever confesses the origin of all human life to be in God, of course condemns both of these evil practices, both that of Neo-Malthusianism and that of Abortion, as absolutely criminal and cursed by God. One may therefore trust that the application of neither the first nor the second artificial means will occur in our circles.

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.
[453]

FALSE POPULATION THEORY, AND THE DOGS. - SUPERVISION OF GAMBLING.441

Furthermore, the Criminal Law has everywhere issued its threat against Abortion, not least in Germany and Austria, and also with us. The question now is whether Neo-Malthusianism should not also find its combating in the Criminal Law; whether all public recommendation of it should not be opposed; and whether the sale of its means should not be stopped. In the case of Abortion, there is only one decisive remedy to be applied with hope of success, and this consists in the fact that a physician or midwife who is caught lending themselves to it, should for good and all have their diploma taken away.

§9. False population theory, and the dogs. At the same time, the false population theory must also be combated in every way. What goes for weeds and for our animals, does not by any means go for man. Now, since we have spoken of animals in this connection, it may be noted here that in Israel the dog was not admitted, for well-known reasons.
