That now with us the dog is used in the domestic circle for hunting, and, on a leash, also for walking, is up to that point, but the Government should also curb the freer roaming of the unleashed dog on our streets. One must have seen in the East, and especially in Constantinople, to what nuisance the freer roaming of the dogs can lead, in order to understand that our Government here too should set limits and boundaries to what is decidedly at odds with the decent character of our public ways. §10. Supervision of gambling.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.

Gambling in itself is not to be condemned. The enjoyment of play belongs to the life of the child. Also, that part of the population that still leads a more natural life, still seeks entertainment in all kinds of innocent play in itself. All kinds of play of such a character

[454]

THE PUBLIC DECENCY.

This is why the Government not only permits this, but even actively encourages and promotes it at public festivities. Even indoor games, such as ninepins, goose board, checkers, and chess, and whatever other games of that nature there may be, can in idle moments divert from much that is wrong, and even exercise the mind itself. That dancing has a bad reputation in our Calvinistic circles and is therefore banned, is not due to the movements themselves, which as young girls dancing together would be entirely innocent, but finds its cause in the abuse made of dancing in the association of the two sexes. The real game, however, that poses a danger to society, is gambling for money, especially as this game for money has gradually developed into lotteries and casinos. The fatal effect of the casino was eventually so obvious that public casinos have now been abolished almost everywhere, and that Monaco has been left not only with the reputation of almost monopolizing coarse gambling for money, but can also point to the many graves on its small territory where the suicides of gamblers have been laid to rest. State lotteries were then devised partly to get money into the treasury, and partly to give a more innocent character to gambling for money; but better economists have eventually unanimously condemned even these State lotteries as well; and for the past forty years, the fight against State lotteries has taken on a rather general character. Here too, Minister Hartogh van Tecklenburg submitted a bill in 1903 to let them gradually die out within a certain number of years. However, this bill did not come up for political discussion; and Minister Kolkman after 1909 rather strengthened the State lotteries than suppressed them, even if he could not make them harmless. The unseemly gambling also came in from England with the harness racing here. But here too, a stop was put to it, and participation in English races from here was prohibited. One may therefore say that we are on the right track in curbing the evil of gambling for money. Maintain this restraint on the evil. Gambling for money to a certain extent and in a certain way, as is seen in the races, the casinos and in many lotteries, degenerates of itself into a poisonous, soul-destroying gambling frenzy, which must be opposed by the Government in the public domain because it violates decency.

[455]

ART.443

§11. Art. The most difficult problem for the government’s care lies ever more in the ever-increasing claim that Art makes upon public life. Art cannot be of a domestic nature. All Art wishes to be seen and heard, and therefore naturally presses towards publicity. This gives rise to difficulties in three different areas in particular. First, in the Theater. The Theater too is a part of public life. Now and then one gives a little domestic piece at a wedding or anniversary celebration, but here the evil does not threaten, for then it is outside its proper sphere. The Theater, on the other hand, stands open to all who wish to pay their ticket. In the Theater as well, then, the government must watch that the play remains elevated and does not degenerate into shamelessness. The sad fact, however, that the more common the piece, the more it attracts, and that high-class performances are scarcely attended, creates here the danger of a very serious violation of morality, and against this the government must be on guard. That it actually does not do this, redounds to its discredit and is an evil that calls for improvement. In the second place, the art museums come into consideration here. Increasingly, one turns, in order not only to display the nude beauty of woman out of a high artistic sense, but also verily to stimulate sensual lust through the representation of immoral scenes and positions. Already now this sometimes goes so far that everyone who thinks feels ashamed. In Naples they have at least gathered the shameful scenes of Pompeii in a separate room, to which access is only granted by special permission. Here too the government must proceed with caution. All that does not legitimize itself as true high art, and merely stimulates sensuality, must remain prohibited. The same applies in part also to literature. There is a French literature of almost bestial nature, which is also devoured by many among us, so that it assaults public decency. Hence, in the third place, the government must also pay attention to the display in our bookstores, to advertisements and posters. What is displayed in the store is of public nature, and all representations of shameful nudities and of immoral nature must be prohibited there. Yet in this area one now thinks that the shameful should not only be permitted, but even recommended. This is connected with the prevailing intellectual direction, which is ever more bent on pushing back religion, if not actually displacing it, and allowing art to take the place of honor that has belonged to religion from of old. The mentality of many is now of such a nature that they feel an inspiration of fanaticism arise in them when art comes into play, while they feel unfree when religion is spoken of. And in this evil the often leading public opinion, alas, also sways the government.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[456]

444 PUBLIC DECENCY

§12. Intoxication. The intoxication resulting from the abuse of wine, strong drink or opium, touches only in part the honor of public life, insofar as it can namely become the cause of scandal on the public way and in public gathering halls or through public sale comes directly into contact with public life. On the other hand, what occurs within the home must not be reckoned to this; insofar, at least, as the protection of the police against drunken violence is not invoked by the household members. Within doors the government must leave the combating of the craving for intoxication to the Church, to the family, to literature or to private associations. This is connected with the fact that the system of total abstainers, insofar as they aim at a legal prohibition of the sale or use of whatever can intoxicate, is not acceptable. That private associations rightly aim at the complete abstinence from whatever can intoxicate, with a view to the harmful abuse, is justifiable and even praiseworthy; in itself, however, it is incorrect that out of conscience all use of wine or alcoholic beverages should be omitted. The wine too comes from God’s creation, and aims, when taken in moderation due to weakness or overstrain, at strengthening and elevating the vital spirits. The question stands quite differently, however, whether the government may not also intervene in private life to prevent abuse, by legally regulating the places of sale and consumption.

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[457]

INTOXICATION - ALL EXCESS MUST BE PUNISHED. To limit it, just as is done among us through the system of licensing. The so-called levying of money from the drunkard cannot move in the wrong direction, so that it becomes a source of state enrichment from this vice. On the other hand, if the law aims to limit the use, then, according to the experience gained with us and elsewhere, such legal regulation works excellently.

§13. All excess must be punished. Yet the government must not stop here. It must not only temper the police excesses of those who intoxicated themselves, but also punish them severely to prevent repetition, and the deprivation of certain civil rights could also be of good service here, as for example in guardianship and otherwise. Also where the government acts as an employer and hires personnel for wages, all use of alcoholic beverages must be excluded from that service. Whether the financial restraint of the evil is imposed on the manufacturer, the seller, or the buyer, is as a rule indifferent. Whichever choice is made, the effect remains the same, for both the manufacturer and the seller try to pass on the burdens imposed on them to the drinker. To such an intervention against the abuse of intoxication, the government is not only entitled by virtue of its vocation to watch over good order and public decency, it is also itself obliged, partly in order to reduce criminality, since statistics repeatedly show what a large percentage of legal violations and crimes flow from the abuse of intoxication, but also partly out of concern for public health, for hardly anything affects the state of health as destructively as this abuse. Even the opening of institutions to wean the drunkards from their sinful habits may lie on the path of the government, even if only as part of its care for the insane. Yet the government cannot succeed in combating intoxication if private initiative does not support it. There is therefore every reason to be thankful that this initiative is constantly growing stronger, as a fruit of which the percentage of alcohol consumption per inhabitant has also visibly diminished among us. Intoxication we took as the last section in this chapter. Yet this subject forms of itself the transition to Chapter XIV, dealing with public health, or if one will, the hygienic vocation of the government.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[458]

CARE FOR PUBLIC HEALTH. 446

CHAPTER XVI
CARE FOR PUBLIC HEALTH.

§1. Cleanliness. The honor belongs to Calvinism that it constantly urged for purity and cleanliness. In our country, in Scotland and in the United States of North America, Calvinism exerted the mightiest influence, and in connection with this it is precisely in these three countries that cleanliness has always flourished most in domestic life; and it may even be said that among these three, the Netherlands still enjoys the highest reputation. To a certain extent it must even be acknowledged that the Dutch housewife was not always free from all exaggeration in the matter of cleanliness; something that must be emphasized all the more, since it is undeniable that in our households in the 18th century the care for cleanliness in clothing and table setting, in corridors, stairs and rooms, even up to the attics and lofts, often weighed heavier than the care for the cleanliness of the body. At least the bath came to us only later. But apart from this, it may nevertheless be praised that even domestic cleanliness constantly contributed greatly to maintaining public health at a high level. In this direction, moreover, one has constantly progressed further, especially in the second half of the previous century; the consequence of which was that much illness was prevented or mitigated; that finally the mortality rate among us declined remarkably; much more sharply even than one had previously considered possible. This is

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[459]

CLEANLINESS - THE DUTY OF THE GOVERNMENT.

447 Of course, this cannot be explained solely by the cleanliness that prevails among us, insofar as this trait of cleanliness has not adorned us less in the preceding period with a much higher percentage of mortality. It is true that the use of public baths in our cities has increased, and this too has contributed to the good. But the great progress in life expectancy must be sought elsewhere for its explanation. Yet, even if this is so, it may not be overlooked that the national trait of cleanliness in household management has contributed to the effort to prolong our life expectancy. Our physicians found with our housewives a support on which they could count much less elsewhere. The only question that remained was whether the Government itself, in its own buildings and in its own household management, upheld our old national virtue of cleanliness sufficiently. On this, one could not boast too strongly, neither in our barracks, nor in our prisons, nor in our administrative buildings; and in that sense, it may also be considered beneficial to public health that higher demands on cleanliness are gradually being made in this public domain as well. What the Government is now doing to remove the filth from our streets, to keep our sewers flowing, and to provide wholesome water, must also be regarded as an increased expression of our love for cleanliness and as beneficial to the hygienic well-being of the population.

§2. The Duty of the Government.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...

That the Government has a serious obligation with regard to public health finds its immediate explanation in the contagious character that attaches to many diseases. Everyone is exposed to this contagion who comes into contact with an infected person in any way. Even a letter has more than once been the carrier of infectious matter, as in the case of scarlet fever. It is the living together in the same village or city, and the intercourse resulting from this, from which the danger so often arises. Now it is almost impossible to protect oneself against this; first, because the danger arises so often

[460]

CARE FOR PUBLIC HEALTH.

This duty arises, on the one hand, because the danger often comes from society itself, before it can be recognized, and on the other hand, because you lack the power to isolate the evil. From this it directly follows that when the danger arises from society, only the Government, which is entrusted with the formal regulation of this society, can step in to protect you. Private initiative cannot save itself here. The Government must protect what stands in an organic life-relationship against the danger of this relationship. This compels it to impose on physicians the obligation to report every manifestation of contagious disease; to give such public notice of this that everyone can be on their guard against the danger; and, if necessary, to isolate the sick person in a barracks or hospital, if their home is too small to separate them from their housemates. Especially in the outbreak of epidemics, the authority of the Government must sometimes extend very far. Even the “my home is my castle” principle cannot always be so strictly respected, since strict respect for your personal independence could become the cause that either your housemates or your neighbors are placed in mortal danger. A second reason for this duty of the Government arises from the fact that medical and pharmaceutical science is not always self-sufficient. In the cities, there is less fear of this, as there is more to be earned here. In more remote areas, on the other hand, and in small villages, this is not the case, and therefore such urgent supervision and provision is necessary here, so that the necessary help does not fail. Something else to which others must also pay attention is that neither physicians nor pharmacists abuse their function or business. We have already pointed this out in the case of abortion, but the sale of poisons cannot be left unregulated either. In fact, we find ourselves in such a state that if the Government were to withdraw its hand, not only would often be lacking what is indispensable, but the available could also give rise to far-reaching abuse. Here too, of course, it is extremely difficult to draw the exact line between what the Government has to do and what it has to leave. Especially in America, the physician lives in a somewhat freer position; with us the bond is usually drawn very tight. But although we cannot completely avoid a certain exaggeration here, in principle we must also confess that the Government has to watch over the entire field of the health of the People, which is organically connected and forms a unity even in a physical sense.

[461]

CONSUMPTION’S CANCER.449

§3. Consumption’s Cancer. One of the most dangerous diseases with regard to public health is consumption, which is still constantly claiming victims in our country in whole groups. It is so dangerous because it propagates itself in families, not certainly through heredity, but because the disease of consumption, unwittingly and unintentionally, brings about the danger of contagion for our household members as a matter of course. Mere breathing can already infect. When the cough starts to work more strongly, the atmosphere in the household takes in the poisonous elements. And even if one is very careful with the excretions, they disperse in the air and bring the danger where no one suspects it. Now, in well-to-do households, one can isolate a room for such a patient and keep him as much as possible in isolation, but in small households this is already very difficult, and with the poor there is no question of it. Hence the constant efforts that are being made to place consumptive patients in an institution, and thus to put the family in safety from the danger of contagion. Yet mutual aid can only provide very partial and insufficient help here. The annual costs that the stay in such an institution requires are far too high even for the fairly well-to-do bourgeoisie. More well-to-do families must then step in. Philanthropy must provide help. But even so, the masses are not helped. The disability insurance in Germany has succeeded in reducing its expenditures by taking under its care those who were in danger or posed a danger; but even here the Government will not be able to refrain. Its vocation demands more of it. Partly through care for better housing, as we shall see later; but also through supervision and isolation. Fortunately, it may also be said that, thanks to this increased care, the curse that consumption so powerfully attacked our people is gradually losing in intensity. When we see the mortality figures from pulmonary tuberculosis stand at 7,250 in 1909, and already four years later at 6,557, then there is a gain to be noted that is gratifying and gives hope that continued care will be able to push back this national scourge even much further. A decline of almost 700 cases in four years, and that despite an increase in population in those years, is something for which we must thank God.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.

Alongside consumption stands cancer, as still the strongest endemic evil threatening us. Fortunately, it is reported that the power to exorcise cancer too would have been discovered; but the overall results remain gloomy. If we also make a comparison here of the mortality rates of 1909 and 1913, there is no trace of exorcism of the evil, and even a demonstrable worsening. In 1909 the mortality rate for cancer stood at 6,012, and in 1913 it had already climbed to 6,748 - an increase of 736 in four years’ time. Here too, therefore, the vocation of the Government remains to exorcise this serious national disease, if possible, with God’s help.

§4. Vaccination. The cowpox disease has both an epidemic and an endemic character. In countries and regions where vaccination is not applied as a rule, this evil plague still occurs, though on a smaller scale, while the cowpox disease gains a much greater expansion and can become epidemic. The main issue for us in this disease is the question of direct or indirect compulsion. The character of this compulsion is direct when vaccination is made compulsory by law within a certain period after birth; indirect, on the other hand, as with us, when school attendance is prohibited for the unvaccinated. We have no objection to vaccination itself. The appeal to Job XII:6, where it says, “Those who provoke God have assurances,” is in no way applicable here. What Job cries out here means something quite different. It is Job’s bitter complaint that God has so ordained it that the pious suffer and the godless enjoy the joys of life or seem to possess assurances against all adversity. Whether by vaccination the latent germ of the disease can be awakened and driven out is a purely medical question, and the outcome seems to show that vaccination applied generally reduces the prevention of the disease. But the quite different question remains whether vaccination, while warding off the smallpox, may not have other detrimental effects, and this too the medical science must determine. Likewise, the neglect of repetition cannot be defended against.

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.
[462]

450 CARE FOR PUBLIC HEALTH.

Alongside consumption stands cancer, as still the strongest endemic evil threatening us. Fortunately, it is reported that the power to exorcise cancer too would have been discovered; but the overall results remain gloomy. If we also make a comparison here of the mortality rates of 1909 and 1913, there is no trace of exorcism of the evil, and even a demonstrable worsening. In 1909 the mortality rate for cancer stood at 6,012, and in 1913 it had already climbed to 6,748 - an increase of 736 in four years’ time. Here too, therefore, the vocation of the Government remains to exorcise this serious national disease, if possible, with God’s help.

[463]

VACCINATION - EPIDEMIC DISEASES. 451 The vaccination itself pleads its case. From a medical standpoint, we have never involved ourselves in this dispute. Our resistance was directed solely against the compulsion, insofar as it encroached upon the freedom of the private citizen in his own body. That each of us must at times bring an offering to the community, goes without saying. We cannot become a source of contagion for our environment. But this imposes upon us the obligation to most carefully prevent and ward off whatever, in connection with this affliction, could inflict harm upon others. And here the question may arise whether we ought not also, on our own initiative, to resort to vaccination for ourselves and our children. But this leaves entirely open the serious question whether the government may exert compulsion upon our person and our own body, and on this question we have always maintained, and still hold, that a decisive negative answer must be given. The government, too, should work here through conviction. And then it appears to us that vaccination will continually gain more ground. Personally, I have never concealed that I found all freedom to proceed with it for myself and my children. But as regards the School Question, I have also, as Minister, defended the position that vaccination cannot be made compulsory for the school, if the parents and guardians of all school-going children declare that they do not wish to make vaccination a requirement for the particular school to which they send their offspring.

§5. Epidemic Diseases. When the country is visited by a serious epidemic disease, the calling of the government takes on an even more comprehensive character. Here one has to reckon with epidemics of a milder and more deadly character. Scarlet fever can locally and temporarily manifest itself much more severely than is commonly the case. In 1911 the deaths from scarlet fever amounted to only 113. Right after that, in 1912, this figure rose to over 200. The same is the case with influenza. From this 298 persons died in 1912, while in 1911 the figure was 767 - more than double. Measles are usually spoken of less, because it seems as if everyone in turn must go through this disease; yet it must not be overlooked that to this affliction no less than 616 children of the male and 580 of the female sex, so together almost 1200 children, fell victim in 1913.

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[464]

452 CARE FOR PUBLIC HEALTH.

§6. The Law of Moses. Of the dreaded epidemic diseases, cholera alone is still reckoned among us as being of a more serious character, the bitter devastation of which I have twice experienced. If we now read in Leviticus XIII et seq. what was commanded in the Mosaic legislation, under divine authority, for the combating of leprosy and other afflictions, then it becomes most evident that all assertions, as if the conscious and energetic combating of such epidemic diseases were a striving against God, and therefore unlawful, are much rather out of the Evil One than in harmony with Scripture. There is a demonic power which, as a consequence of sin, in all manner of ways threatens human life and well-being, and therefore it rests upon us as a stern duty to avert, as far as lies in our power, all danger to life, both in ourselves and in those entrusted to our care. This duty rests in the first place upon the father of the family, but by implication also upon the patriarch for his whole clan, and upon the government for the entire people. When such an epidemic disease breaks out with any force, the population must be warned immediately, the physicians must be obligated to report all cases forthwith, strict disinfection and removal of feces must be provided for, and where distressing poverty reigns, better nourishment must be supplied, the sick must be isolated as much as possible, and when death follows, it must be seen to that the body is interred as speedily as possible. Especially the poorer, poorly housed population must then be able to count on all-sided assistance, and the government must be vested with the authority, where the carelessness of one citizen could harm another, to intervene for the protection of the endangered. The example of what was permitted, nay commanded, to the government in Israel in such a case, speaks here, for him who says he submits to God’s Word, so strongly that he cannot hesitate. In the face of the outbreak of a serious epidemic disease, the government has not only the right but the duty to take all measures necessary for the protection of the public health, even if these measures should temporarily restrict the freedom of the individual.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.
[465]

THE LAW OF MOSES - SYPHILIS. 453 The emphasis on the breaking of the plague only underscores this all the more, and especially in recent years, the pestilence on Java has shown how, without the powerful assistance of the government, there is no salvation against these most dreaded of all epidemics.

§7. Syphilis. In its most painful character, the poisonous venereal disease known as Syphilis stands out among all diseases. Whether this sinful and highly dangerous illness also prevailed among the ancients remains uncertain. In the past, this was firmly denied; but since then, voices have arisen that claim to have grounds for the opposite view. But however that may be, it is certain that it was only in the 15th/16th century that this evil gained a more general significance. This gave rise to the opinion that syphilis came to us from America. It struck especially hard in France. Hence, it long bore the name of “morbus gallicus” or the French disease. Especially in the army of Charles VIII of France, it caused such devastating destruction that it was punished with defeat. The sad end of this venereal disease is that, although in the beginning it was often curable, out of shame it is kept secret, thus creeping along in silence, gradually becoming more serious in danger, and then, either outside or in marriage, is also transmitted to the woman, and all too often also to the born children. Since not only persons of lower rank, but also men and women of higher order, even from the highest classes of society, were found to be infected with the primary, secondary or tertiary forms of this disease through a sinful life or by sad inheritance, this had the consequence that the government did not hesitate to try to keep the occurrence of this disease as secret as possible. The doctors who treated these disease cases were under strict prohibition from letting anything at all about it become known to the public. As long as the visitation system held, the government even tried to ward off the danger that prostitution posed in this regard through strict measures. And more and more it seemed as if one saw in syphilis a disease that, as a consequence of a more licentious life, could even make a claim on the protection of the government. Especially among the public that soon has to make the acquaintance of the church, this disease spread in a most alarming way. The situation also often proved to be sad in the Navy. It was not much better for the Army. For here and for the East Indies, the statistics attached to this work provide a clear insight.*) §8. Lifting the Secrecy. The General Association against Prostitution, whose report can be found in the Journal for Poor Care and Child Protection 1915, No. 722, shows how seriously the danger threatening from this disease is still seen; and it is therefore not surprising that a Committee has been established in The Hague, calling itself: Committee for the Promotion of Medical Examination before Marriage and having its secretariat in the Residence on Plein 17a; and however indelicate it may sound and however hard it would be for many an innocent case if he or she were to go there to be suspected, yet there is much that such an examination would recommend, and the more so as one can be syphilitic without ever having given oneself over to sexual sin. Also the deep-reaching studies of Cesare Lombroso lead to the lifting of the secrecy and to the relentless publicity. Even in connection with this, the question has already arisen whether the Eunuch should not be applied to these deeply corrupted natures, in order to prevent the continuation of the evil race. As can be seen from the Appendices given here, the figure for Tuberculosis among the prison staff in 1907 was only 62, while the number of venereal patients was not far from 200; in 1910 it even went over 200. And if one takes not only the prisoners, but also the population of the State Institutions and the Reformatories, it appeared that the percentage had risen from 3.90 in 1907 to 7.83 in 1912. Truly alarming figures. Fortunately, an improvement is already noticeable in the attitude previously taken by the government in this regard. But even with what *) See Appendices A, B, C and D at the end of this Second Part.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[466]

454 CARE FOR PUBLIC HEALTH. the government has done, the disease has spread in a most alarming way, especially among the public that soon has to make the acquaintance of the church. Also in the Navy, the situation often proved to be sad. The situation was not much better for the Army. For here and for the East Indies, the statistics attached to this work provide a clear insight.*)

[467]

LIFTING THE VEIL.455 Now this must be, and it cannot remain so. The advantage of the mystery may no longer, in the strictest sense, be granted to this guilty disease. This secrecy nourishes the evil, and this can have no other consequence than that sin increases and the rising generation sinks into psychic and physical decadence. Yet it also remains firmly established for us that only a holier motive can create the circle from which this evil is far removed. Only where all fornication and all extramarital sexual intercourse are condemned, branded, and excluded in the name of the Lord, does syphilis also strike an impenetrable wall. And without boasting, it may be said of the Calvinists that slipping into this demonic terrain was always and remained a rare exception among them.

§9. Improvement of Housing Conditions. What was discussed in the previous section is directly connected to what awaits the government’s care for the improvement of housing. Fortunately, we are on a better path here, and not the least has the Law that came into being in 1901 for this purpose opened up better paths. Even if we admit that this Law too greatly encroached on the right of ownership and handed over as worthless what still represents real value, it is nevertheless also recognized on our part that it is this Law that has put us on the better track. For it clearly opens the eyes to the very serious danger that the miserable housing conditions pose, both for morality and for health, to the less privileged population. Where father, mother, and children, as is often the case, have to spend the night together in a small room, it cannot be otherwise but that the feeling of shame suffers, and all health is impaired. Of strict and punctual secrecy, for the growing youth, for all that touches the sexual life, there can then be no question, and hardly any thought can be given to the necessary refreshment of the air. The undeniable fact that precisely among that part of the population that has to live in such hovels, the mortality rate, especially among children, climbs to the highest proportions, has also often taken on disquieting dimensions. Even for proper cleansing of the body, there is almost no opportunity. In all corners and crevices of the tiny dwelling, unhealthy substances and even gases develop; and by no means so rarely is the condition that the Minister of the Word also encounters in such dwellings in his house visits, in a literal sense the very opposite of what it should be according to the requirements of Leviticus XIII et seq. Here one now stands before the almost insoluble population problem. If these conditions are improved, and life in those lowest circles is so much improved that even marriage becomes possible at a much younger age, then marriage increases by leaps and bounds, the population increases, labor is by no means always to be found for all, and one is automatically faced with the question of how and in what way to find housing and food for this ever-growing throng, so that at least the most basic needs of a healthy human life are met for both adults and children. We do not conceal in the least that a solution to the housing problem in itself does not offer the slightest guarantee that the poisonous sting will be drawn from the population problem. Yet it also remains a matter of the highest importance that not only in our large cities, but also thoroughly in the countryside, a steady improvement in housing conditions be strived for. Landowners who have workers living on their land are indebted to their workers to let them live as befits a child of man and as one who must live a healthy life. We therefore also place this whole question very highly, that even in Antirevolutionary circles the great importance of the housing question is beginning to arouse more and more interest. Only recently, the architect D.E. Wentink, who is also an inspector of Public Health, gave in his work entitled: The Building Regulations and the Housing Question, another sample of the study that is also beginning to be devoted to this question among us. §10. Cremation. Although cremation is only tangentially connected to Public Health, yet a complete silence on this issue would not be right. For cremation is not only a matter of hygiene, but also touches on the deepest feelings of the people, on their customs and conventions, and on the propriety that must be maintained in the treatment of the dead. It is therefore a matter that cannot be ignored, and that must be considered from various points of view.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[468]

456 CARE FOR PUBLIC HEALTH.

gingvanhetlichaamontbrekendanschierallegegevens.Vanhetnemenvaneenbadiszoogoedalsnimmersprake Inallehoekenengatenvandenietigewoningontwikkelenzichverkeerdestoffenenzelfsgassen;enlangnietzoozeldenisdetoestand,dienookde Be-dienaardes Woordsbijzijnhuisbezoekinzulkewoningenaantreft,inletterlijkenzinvlakhettegendeelvanwathetnaareischvan Levi-ticus XIIIv.v.zijnmoest.Hiernustaatmenvoorhetschieronop-losbaarbevolkingsprobleem.Verbetertmendietoestanden,enwordt’tlevenookindielaagstekringenzooveelverbeterd,datzelfshethuwelijkreedsopveeljongerleeftijdmogelijkwordt,dan,neemthethuwenhandoverhandtoe,debevolkingzetzichuit,arbeidislangnietaltoosvoordieallentevinden,enzoostaatmenvanzelfvoordevraag,hoeenopwatwijsvoordiesteedsaanwassendescharewoningenvoedselinzulkeenvormtevinden,datalthansaandeallereerstebehoeftenvaneengezondmenschelijklevenbijdevolwassenenenbijdekinderenvoldaanwordt.Weverhelendanookin’tminstniet,dateenoplossingvanhetwoningprobleemopzichzelfnoggeendenminstenwaarborgbiedt,datuithetbevolkingsprobleemdegiftigeangelzalwordenuitgetrokken.Ookzooechterblijftheteenaan-gelegenheidvanhethoogstegewicht,datnietalleeninonzegrootesteden,maarwelterdegeookophetplattelandopgestadigeverbeteringvandewoningtoestandenwordeaangestuurd.Grondeigenaarsdiearbeidersophunlandhebbenwonen,zijn’taanhunarbeidersschuldig,datzehenwonenlatengelijkeenkinddesmenschenlevenmoetenalleengezondlevenkan.Westellenhetvoorgeheelditvraagstukdanookophoogenprijs,datookin Antirevolutionairekringenhethoogebelangvandewoningquaestiesteedsmeerbelangstellingbeginttewekken.Nogpasschonkdearchitect D.E.Wentink,dietevensinspecteurvande Volksgezondheidis,inzijnwerkgetiteld:Debouw-verordeningenhetwoningvraagstuk,eenproevetemeervandestudie,dieookonderonsaanditvraagstukbegintgewijdteworden.

§10. Lijkverbranding.Alishetdatde Lijkverbrandingnietdanzijdelingsmetde Volks-gezondheidsaamhangt,tochiseengeheelzwijgenoverditvraagstuk

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.
[469]

CREMATION.457 This cannot be tolerated here. From July 1, 1874 to June 12, 1875, a series of ten articles on this subject were published in the then newly established Standard, which are also printed in the large edition of “Our Program,” with the Appendices. This made a renewed entry into this important question unnecessary. It has, one may say, been fully elucidated by us at the time, and no new arguments of significance have since been brought forward with regard to this question. Yet it does not suit us to remain completely silent about it, and all the less so because Minister Cort van der Linden has not hesitated to illegally introduce cremation among us. The Burial Act lacked, as an advocate of cremation had discovered, the express prohibition. Instead of immediately filling this gap, Minister Cort van der Linden has allowed this gap to be used to push through what was not expressly enough prohibited, outside the law. Even more than one Minister from the then Cabinet has been able to find approval in the cremation of a liberal colonial statesman, honoring this unlawful destruction with his presence. Much love for cremation has thus not yet come to light. It remained a rare exception. But the sad fact is that this un-Christian practice has nevertheless been introduced among us by devious means, and that it has neither been desired nor dared to elicit a decision on this matter by legal means. Even the legal objections to the thus disappearance of a body have been brushed aside. Yet these legal objections will continue to weigh heavily on the whole issue, and it was precisely to counter these objections that Cremation could not have been established other than by a deliberate Act. Our grievance against cremation is naturally not that it would, according to our conception, hinder the resurrection of the deceased. Even outside of cremation, there are countless corpses that find no resting place in the quiet grave. Just think of the many shipwrecked who perish in the waves, or of the unfortunate who, especially in British India, are still torn to pieces by wild animals year after year, numbering more than 20,000. Even in great fires, corpses still come up; and on the battlefield, bodies can suddenly accumulate in such a way that, especially in hot

[470]

CARE FOR PUBLIC HEALTH. The burning of the dead is almost the only way out on summer days. For anyone who believes in Almighty God, there is no question that even those who perished in such a fateful manner will rise again on the last day. But everything a believer objects to in cremation is quite different. First, that cremation is a pagan invention, which must not be revived in the Christian world. Secondly, even in entirely pagan lands, there has been an appreciation for the preservation and proper care of the dead, which has put all cremation to shame in Egypt. Thirdly, the soil of the earth is of such a quality that it is prepared to receive the dead and brings about their gradual decomposition in a normal way. And what speaks to us most seriously is the undeniable consequence that, if cremation had been the fixed, ever-continuing, and thus also the only valid treatment of the body under Israel, the body of Christ, if one may so call it, would also have fallen victim to cremation, and the entire resurrection of Immanuel would have been erased from sacred history—something that would have turned the whole Gospel upside down. Therefore, if the opportunity for cremation is to be opened, for the sake of freedom, let it be done in a completely secluded place, under strict legal safeguards, and under the application of regulations that eliminate all display of cremation.

§11. Infant Mortality. Infant mortality, since efforts were made to reduce its extent, has increasingly drawn the attention of hygiene. The results that can be pointed to are also eloquent. Even if one only consults the figures for the ten consecutive years from 1904 to 1913, one still finds a decrease that is astonishing for the short period the data covers. For our entire country, the dreadful figure in 1904 still stood at 13.66 out of 100 live births, and this was exclusively for the first year of life; and if one now consults the data for 1913, then ten years later this figure of 13.66 has already shrunk to 9.14, an almost unbelievable decrease in these few years.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[471]

INFANT MORTALITY. 459 Moreover, the number of live-born children who die in their first year of life is much greater among the illegitimate than among the children born in wedlock. Across the entire Realm, the mortality rate of legitimate children in the first year of life was not higher than 13.49 before 1904, while the figure for the illegitimate stood at 22.25. And in 1913, even though the first figure had shrunk to 9.00, the corresponding figure for the illegitimate was still as high as 15.73. Truly a proof of how the deprivation of good motherly care, which so often fails the illegitimate, threatens the lives of the newborn. No less remarkable in this context is the difference between province and province. Comparing Limburg with North Holland, this distinction even jumps out in an almost inexplicable way. In 1904, the figure for Limburg for all children together stood at 17.16, and for the illegitimate at 37.28, only to decline in 1913 to 13.94 and 35.47. And if one now takes the figures for North Holland, one finds a total figure of 12.58 in 1904, which had already fallen to 6.88 by 1913, where for 1904 there was still 19.26 to be given for the illegitimate; a figure that ten years later had also fallen to 11.27. In Friesland, the figures in 1913 were even lower, namely 5.87 for the total, 10.15 for the illegitimate, and 5.80 per hundred for the legitimate, always taken in their first year of life. In other countries, especially in Sweden and Norway, even more favorable figures are found. To explain these figures on infant mortality, it appears again and again that this mortality is promoted by inherited diseases, especially syphilis; that among the poorer classes of the population the mortality is much higher than among the more affluent class; that the withholding of the mother’s breast increases the figure; and that the aversion that creeps into some circles against motherhood contributes to increasing the chance of an early death. Yet it would be mistaken to think that the decline in this sad mortality figure is due solely to better hygienic care. For it is beyond doubt that two other factors have had an even greater impact on this decline: first, the sin of Neo-Malthusianism, which has so significantly reduced the birth rate,

[472]

460 ON THE SCHOOLS. It has caused the birth rate to decline, precisely outside of wedlock, and to an even greater degree the sin of abortion, which has found increasingly widespread application. This would not be the case if the number of births remained the same. Now, however, even among us the number of births declines with each decade, and compared to the figures that still stand at 40 and more in Russia and the Balkans, we can scarcely boast of more than a good 20, so with child mortality, of course, this fact must also be taken into account. Chapter XVII. ON THE SCHOOLS.

§1. The Changed Position.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.

In 1878, the chapter “On the Schools” was written in “Our Program” under the full impression of Kappeynes’ assault on free education. Nor could it be otherwise, for the advice we gave at the time was by no means little influenced by what Kappeynes v. d. Copello dared to attempt. The peculiarity here was that Kappeynes did not at all take a venomous position against the Anti-Revolutionary Party. When one now comes into contact with Statesmen of the Left, this is almost never otherwise than on a very tense footing. Of familiar intercourse there is no longer any question. It is already much if one can take the gentleman-like courtesy back and forth to heart. At that time, on the other hand, it was quite different. The writer of this was even on terms of friendship with Kappeynes. One visited each other regularly. In full confidence, one could also discuss state affairs. When later the Doleantie broke out, Kappeynes even offered himself as a completely open and willing advisor. He was then frequently consulted on our side. He even issued a written opinion in favor of the Reformed Churches at that time. And in Amsterdam, a grand local dinner was even held in honor of Kappeynes, when the battle was fought and the fight was finally, if also legally to our disadvantage, decided. Of such a relationship there is now, between Anti-Revolutionaries and Leftist Statesmen, such as Mr. Drucker, Mr. De Meester, or Mr. Goeman Borgesius, no longer any question. This had to be emphasized here to give a correct insight into the mutual position in the School Dispute, as it presented itself in 1878.

§2. The Changed Tactic.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...

This turn of events has then ultimately forced us on our part to abandon the position we had previously taken on the school costs vis-à-vis the State, and to move on to the almost opposite position, where the demand would be that from the government side, just as much as in all the costs of education that are not covered by the school fees, both of public and of free education, would be provided for. The almost incredible increase in the costs of education, which gradually proved to be unavoidable, made the maintenance of our previously adopted position literally impossible for us. The figures spoke volumes here, as the Englishman says. Calculating from the year 1860 to 1912, i.e. over a period of more than half a century, one finds these figures almost completely divergent, with the additional consideration that the population stood at 3,309,128 in the year 1860, and has since increased to 6,328,448.

[473]

THE CHANGED POSITION. - THE CHANGED TACTIC. 461 the Reformed Churches to issue at that time. And in Amsterdam, a grand local dinner was even held in honor of Kappeynes, when the battle was fought and the fight was finally, if also legally to our disadvantage, decided. Of such a relationship there is now, between Anti-Revolutionaries and Leftist Statesmen, such as Mr. Drucker, Mr. De Meester, or Mr. Goeman Borgesius, no longer any question. This had to be emphasized here to give a correct insight into the mutual position in the School Dispute, as it presented itself in 1878.

Since Groen van Prinsterer’s appearance in 1869 against Beets in Utrecht, every idea had become foreign to us to seek an accord between the Public School and the demands of the Christian religion in the upbringing of the youth; and even Groen’s relapse shortly before his death in 1876 was more a coup de désespoir of the “general” abandoned by his troops and so bitterly disappointed. However, the position as such should not be judged according to this last sad incident. We must always go back to 1869, and then certainly with the painful impression that Kappeynes’ assault on us must have made in connection with this.

§2. The Changed Tactic.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...

This turn of events has then ultimately forced us on our part to abandon the position we had previously taken on the school costs vis-à-vis the State, and to move on to the almost opposite position, where the demand would be that from the government side, just as much as in all the costs of education that are not covered by the school fees, both of public and of free education, would be provided for. The almost incredible increase in the costs of education, which gradually proved to be unavoidable, made the maintenance of our previously adopted position literally impossible for us. The figures spoke volumes here, as the Englishman says. Calculating from the year 1860 to 1912, i.e. over a period of more than half a century, one finds these figures almost completely divergent, with the additional consideration that the population has since increased to 6,328,448.

[474]

THE SCHOOLS. According to the census of 1909, the number of students had already risen to 5,858,175, indeed even reaching twice what it was in 1860. But even if one fully takes this doubling into account, there is still a lack of proportion with the stark difference in what was paid for Elementary Education by the government in 1860, and what is now being spent for it. In 1860, the total amount for this stood at only ƒ2,259,735, while it had already risen in 1912 to a total of ƒ35,302,087—thus to more than fifteen times as much. Now observe how this immense increase in expenditures for Elementary Education began almost immediately after the implementation of Kappeynes’s law. Already a year and a half later, in 1880, the total figure stood at around 8 million guilders. Ten years later, it had already risen to over 11¼ million. And this steady progress held so little in check that the figure, ten years later again, had already reached over 17½ million; thirteen years further, it stood at over 31½ million, and has not yet come to rest, so that the financial thermometer keeps rising still. It hardly needs reminding that the increase in population all along caused the number of schools to grow, which naturally led to an increase in expenditures.

[475]

CHANGED TACTICS—FINANCIAL CONSEQUENCES.
