In 1880, the numbers for Public and Private Schools were as follows: Public Schools, boys 227,023, girls 180,769, total 407,792; Private Schools, boys 59,346, girls 73,857, total 133,203. Thus, for both Public and Private Schools together, one still only had to provide for a total of 540,995 students. In 1913, however, i.e. a full quarter-century later, these figures had already risen, for the Public Schools, to boys 313,855, girls 248,270, total 562,125; and for the Private Schools, to boys 168,919, girls 212,162, total 381,081. For both types of schools together, this thus amounted to a total of 943,206—indicating an increase of over 400,000 students in thirty-three years. The ideal conception of providing for the needs of our Christian Schools could no longer be put into practice given this completely changed situation. The ideal was and remains that every parent pair would provide for the schooling of their offspring, and that where the meagerness of income cut this off, Christian charity would step in to make up the difference. But Christian philanthropy also has its limits, and these are increasingly exceeded by the school crisis. The number of students in the Private Schools amounted in 1875 to no more than 55,485 boys and 67,912 girls, i.e. 123,397 in total; and in 1913 this had already risen to 168,919 boys and 212,162 girls, making a total of 381,081. Now since the vast majority of these over 300,000 children belonged to families that could not think of defraying the costs of education entirely from their own means, without subsidy, such a high amount would have to be made up by Christian charity that it could by no means provide the required total. This inconvenience would have been less serious if the costs of elementary education had remained at the old level. But this was anything but the case. The salaries of principals and teachers kept going up higher; ever higher demands were made on the facilities; the auxiliary means kept becoming more expensive. The inconvenience thus worked from two sides at once. There was need to step in for so many more children, and what had to be paid per child kept increasing.

[476]

464 ON THE SCHOOLS.

It soon became more than triple. And to this was added yet a third inconvenience. The more the Government increased the financial expenditures for the Public Schools, the more we also had to contribute to the costs of our schools. For in the matter of taxes, we stood on an equal line with the supporters of the Public School. Now, as already shown in the previous section, the expenditures for the Public Schools had risen from barely one and a half million to 27 million, it followed that the supporters of the Private School had to contribute in proportion to this as well in the taxes. An exact amount cannot be given for this, but if one estimates it at 7 of the 27 million, the guess is certainly not too high. And now it was self-evident that as we had to add a seven million for the Public Schools to our taxes, the financial source of our own schools was increasingly tapped, and in the end dried up. Even now, although the Private Schools were granted a subsidy of 9 1/2 million for 1916/17, there still had to be collected throughout the whole country to support many needy schools; what then would have become of our schools, if with so much greater a number, with so much higher costs for each school, and for each taxpayer so much higher an amount on his tax bill, we ourselves had had to provide for everything that was lacking. Here we run up against a clear impossibility. Our group, composed for the far greater part of “common people,” would have succumbed to the ever-increasing demands. School after school would have gone bankrupt. And the despair that overcame Groen van Prinsterer shortly before his death would have extinguished the life courage of the Christian School throughout our circle. Therefore, our former ideal had to be abandoned, and from now on we had to strive for equal government provision for both types of schools.

[477]

THEN CONSISTENTLY. - TEMPORARY PROPOSAL. 465 But then this equality had to be carried through to the end. Now since 1869 the principle and starting point on our side had been the proposition that the Free School should be the rule, the Public School only a supplement, not even a thought could or might be entertained that the Public School, which was only to supplement, should be made the darling child of the state and the municipal administration by the finances of the government. As it should have been, both sides would have had to strive for self-provision in the school need. But since the supporters of the Public School were not even remotely thinking of this, and allowed themselves to be showered ever richer and more luxurious from the public coffers, the system of free contribution was thereby cut off. If the Public School, apart from a small school fee, drew all its means from the public treasury, then the government had to make available at least an equal amount per child for the Free School as well. This is where we went. For 1916/17, as already stated, a subsidy of almost 10 million has been requested; and even if the number of pupils remained the same, this would still have to rise to 15 million in order to maintain parity. The balance of the scale must be in equilibrium. Per child it must be equal for both schools. It must not even be allowed that the municipal administrations still tip the scales. If one wants to consolidate the administration of the municipal administrations in Elementary Education, then the law must also impose on the municipal administrations the obligation to make available per child what they spend from their own treasury for the Public School or its teachers, just as much per child for the Private School. It would only be possible to escape this system of equal financial provision by the government if one covered all the costs for the Public School exclusively through its supporters, and in the end imposed a separate tax for the Public School, to which those who chose the Free School would not have to contribute - but as far as the slightest inclination to seek a way out here is not apparent, there must be an equal amount per child from the government on both sides of the scale.

§5. Temporary proposal. As a first temporary provision, it might well be considered to have the municipal administration in large municipalities take over one or more schools as state schools.

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[478]

It would be possible to make the public schools available to parents of the same religious conviction; but then these parents would also have to be granted the complete free choice of teachers, textbooks, school curricula, etc. However, this system would recommend itself in far fewer cases than in very few instances. For in large municipalities, the distances from home to school are soon too great for the children to all be brought together in one school, and likewise, on the part of the teachers, there would be no small difficulties to fear between them and the parents. Nor can the way out of having enough like-minded religious people in the population give the public school itself a religious character be traveled. For first of all, there are in every village at least some parents of a divergent school system. One need only think of a single Jew or a single official who has moved in from elsewhere. Moreover, the arrival of a new family can upset everything at any moment. And what decides it all, the public teacher could never be bound by the government to certain religious views in his teaching. Where a religious character is to be given to the school, it must be a Free or Private School. By virtue of its principled character, the public school does not lend itself to this. The attempt to lead it in that direction almost exclusively emanates from a single village pastor who is still half-and-half orthodox and who would find it unpleasant to see a second spiritual shepherd appear at the head of a Christian school, one who may enjoy warmer sympathy from the congregation members than he himself. In earlier years, this artifice of a public school with religious instruction from a half-orthodox pastor has been repeatedly tried in all the provinces, but without exception it has failed everywhere. Where the population is neither asleep nor slumbering, but alive and knows what it owes to the baptized offspring, every attempt of this kind also fails. The antithesis between the public and the free school creates such an unbridgeable gap between the spirits that one must either return to the people’s school as one’s own school, or else, alongside it, the free school, and then, if necessary, for the whole people, must be respected. For the course of this dispute, I refer to H. de Wilde: “The Anti-Revolutionary Parties and Their Principles,” fourth edition, Bootsma 1915, The Hague.

[479]

TEMPORARY PROPOSAL - PUBLIC SCHOOL. 467 p. 209 ff. We will never come to a final solution to the School Dispute, unless Primary Education becomes a matter for the State, and the principle of the Free School according to Groen van Prinsterer’s slogan is established, and the Public School only comes in as a supplement.

§6. Public School. It cannot be denied, however, that the idea of the Public School, embracing the entire youth of the nation, still has great appeal. It is the national solidarity that finds expression in it. It was no different with the National Church, in which the entire People, forming one complex, presented itself. Both the concepts of National Church and Public School have thus carried each other for many years. It was indeed the unity of the National Church that made the unity of the Public School possible, and next to that Public School one really did not allow anything else than private Schools of a completely private character, especially also for the Jews. Even in the United States of America, one has always done, and still does, what is possible to maintain that unity. It is true that the spiritual element, the reading of the Holy Scriptures in prayer, has been reduced to an almost insignificant minimum, but in name it is still called the public school as the People’s School. Even this strong preference, however, has not been able to prevent the Roman Catholic population in particular from continuing to found their own schools, and some other groups from also giving preference to the Free School. In Germany one sees the same process in action. It is increasingly evident that the Public School and the National Church belong to an earlier period, but are now out of date. In that earlier period, rich in mighty historical events, nation after nation had opposed itself, especially in contrast to the all-dissolving Roman power, in order to maintain its own independence unimpaired. For this, a strong appeal to the national chords was required. And this could at that time naturally lead to a Public School, because at least in the field of primary education hardly anything else than what that confessional school could give was desired by the entire population. There was a unity of spirit, and that unity of spirit also demanded the one Public School for the youth.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.
[480]

468 ON SCHOOLS.

§7. Completely changed since then. Since, however, especially under the influence of the French Revolution, a struggle of the spirits has come to the fore, which has set group against group within the same nation. Especially in the Netherlands, the strong individualism that is inherent in our national character has sharpened these differences that have arisen, and has brought various directions to the fore, which also set very distinct demands for the world of children and therefore could not be accommodated in the one School that would embrace them all. From this has then, since the middle of the previous century, arisen a struggle in the field of schools between the Calvinistic disposition of the old period and the new world of ideas that, coming over from Paris, has transformed the minds of many. These transformed spirits differed among themselves, but in the face of the after-effects of the historical traditions, these distinct modern directions joined together. From this combination then emerged the sectarian school of the moderns, and with this the fate of the Public School was sealed. In the new period, the variation in the spirits had become too strong. The unity of the Primary School has now degenerated into what amounts to national self-deception. The higher the development of a People climbs, the more that higher development must result in a richer variation of spirits, and with this the one Public School has been judged unprofitable and unusable from now on. §8. The Free School for every direction.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how

One may undergo an anxious experience when one hears from the Social Democrats or also from the atheistic camp how they too see the choice for the Free School and against the Public School gaining ground. One had intended it as if only the Christian groups in the country would benefit from this liberation, but now it appears that the non-Christian groups also make use of this freedom, and that the Public School is being abandoned by them as well. This is a development that must be accepted, however unpleasant it may be for those who had hoped for the maintenance of the Public School. The principle of the Free School for every direction has now become a fact.

[481]

FUNDAMENTALLY CHANGED. - FREEDOM OF SCHOOL FOR EVERY DIRECTION.

If one were to apply this same system to the irreligious groups and grant freedom of school establishment, with equal subsidy, even to the atheistic leaders, then, it was thought, one would avoid an ever-looming war. For us, freedom in the school domain was a right, but this could never be granted to them. This objection arises from a misunderstanding. Once the system of the Free School is accepted as the rule, and the Public School as a supplement, then a freedom emerges here that must be without distinction for everyone; naturally, on their own full responsibility. Even in Roman Catholic schools, dogmatic representations are imprinted on the youth, some of which we very decisively reject as not in accordance with the Truth. Yet it has never been inferred from this that there should be no free Roman Catholic schools. One has to choose between two standpoints here. One can claim, as the advocates of the so-called Neutral School do, that the Government itself must determine what is truth, and that it may then teach this exclusively to the children of the School. Or one professes that the Government does not possess that power; that it is the Churches that have to act here, and that in connection with these Churches each citizen has to judge for himself which are the principles on which he must build his own life and the life of his children. The former is the system of coercion, which still prevailed when the Government professed a special creed, but has become intolerable now that the Government has renounced all confession. If one has once chosen for the freedom of conviction and deduces pedagogically the freedom of the upbringing of one’s children from this, then one must also do justice to this same right for every citizen, and cannot demand for oneself and one’s fellow believers what one would refuse to other citizens. We openly acknowledge that there is something in this release that offends us. But now look at what the Lord our God is doing. The Christ Himself points us to how the Disposer over the lot of us all lets His sun rise over the evil and the good, and rains over the righteous and the unrighteous. Since the Flood, God’s government has allowed both those who confess the Lord and those who oppose the Lord to live under the same natural conditions.

[482]

470 OF THE SCHOOLS. to continue their existence in freedom. Sometimes one even gets the impression that it is the godless who enjoy even more abundant favor. Just look at it in Psalm 73, just read it with Job. And herein lies the plumb line for us.

§9. School Years. The urge to continue the Elementary Education, which now generally does not go beyond the 12th year, up to the 14th year, and thus to extend it by a couple of years, does not diminish, but rather becomes stronger. Especially the example set by Prussia and Switzerland continues to have an effect, and it cannot even be denied that the great world war that broke out in 1914, and the undeniable success that Germany then achieved on all fronts, more strongly than ever recommended the extension of the school term. Such an extension offers a twofold advantage. The two-year longer stay at school naturally has the consequence that the boys are not put to heavier work until they have reached a sufficient physical development, and that this also reduces unemployment among the elderly. And on the other hand, it cannot be denied that two years longer at school brings the mental condition of the lower population to a higher level. Against this, however, stands the not inconsiderable objection that the wages for many a needy family fall away; that the school costs for the Rich and the Municipalities increase by a third, and that above all the transition from the five-year to the seven-year period will present unusual difficulties, both as regards the locality and the teaching staff. By no means all schools lend themselves to setting up an extension or adding local buildings; and an increase in the teaching staff by a third would also run into very serious objections; all the more so since an extension of the school term up to the 14th year would also require an expansion of the curriculum itself. The question that we have repeatedly raised on our part as to whether, if one proceeds to extend the school years, this system would be applied to the entire school youth, also comes into consideration here. If, after all, the German and Swiss system were modified to the extent that the lowest groups of students were exempted from the extension, so that it would only apply to half or two-thirds, this would already considerably alleviate these very serious difficulties. Even now, extensions and expansions have been found in the Evening Schools, and the Continuation Schools, and the Extended and More Extended Elementary Education; institutions that all work in the spirit of broadening and extending education. Yet the number of students who benefit from this is, in proportion to the almost one million students who come into consideration here, too small in number to make us acquiesce in the existing situation.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[483]

SCHOOL YEARS. - THE KINDERGARTENS. 471

§10. The Kindergartens. The name Kindergarten is not a happy choice in both parts of its composition. The whole idea of a School should have been eliminated here, and the talk of a School to preserve should not have been allowed. Before Fröbel’s rich idea of what he called the “Kindergarten” went out, one certainly knew what were nothing but children’s shelters, but it is precisely the honor of Fröbel’s invention that it has largely put an end to this “misery” of these “shelters.” Naturally, it would be far preferable if every child could receive the necessary care and guidance at home until the age of 6. But this cannot be. There are too many widowers with children, too many mothers who have to go out to work for wages, too many weak and sickly mothers, and the poor housing also often makes it impossible to keep the children at home. In the past, the need arising from this was met by giving the children who were in such a situation somewhere in the neighborhood to a teacher for safekeeping. They were then taken there and picked up at four o’clock, and in the meantime they were then, so to speak, left to their own devices, and were only kept safe, in the sense that this word has, only to prevent misfortunes. This was not only the case with us, but also in other cities, and it was Friedrich Fröbel from Schwarzburg-Rudolstadt who succeeded in at least curbing this evil. Born in 1782, he died in 1852, and with remarkable talent he devoted the greater part of his life to the establishment of the “Kindergarten.”

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.
[484]

SCHOOLS. His first kindergarten he founded in 1839 in Blankenburg, and since then their number has grown not only in Germany, but almost throughout the world, and especially in America, to such an extent that the kindergarten has already become an indispensable element in education in the fourth century. Lina Morgenstern, Angelita Hartmann, and also A. Köhler, Friedrich Seidel and others almost took over Fröbel’s work. Private seminaries were even established to provide the necessary training for the leading personnel in this field of education. At first, all of this remained entirely private. More than one government has even opposed the kindergarten. Since 1872, however, when the Austrian government lent a helping hand, support for the kindergarten has been provided in all directions, and it has also gradually become a helping power in public education among us. It is only to be regretted that Fröbel’s watchword: “No letters and no numbers on these schools!” no longer finds strict observance. Especially among us, too much preparation for the Elementary School has been seen; municipal authorities have interfered in it; and so the shadow side of our Public School system has also come to weigh on the kindergarten, against which the Christian kindergarten has held its ground with honor. It is a pity, however, that here too the money question has crept in. And then one often saw that the municipal authorities also wanted to bind the Christian kindergartens to regulations that came into conflict with their private and their Christian character; a dispute in which, unfortunately, the temptation of the subsidy broke the pride of the Christian Schools. It is certainly also our duty to promote this kindergarten system in the most vigorous manner, but then let it remain private and let it not degenerate, as far as education is concerned, into a mere preparatory class for the Elementary School. The half-playful character of these institutions must not then be given up, and above all, care must always be taken to ensure that the premises guarantee freshness and light. At the end of 1912, there were public kindergartens among us with a total of almost 33,000 children, and in addition private or independent institutions with about 141,000 little ones. The public ones are only progressing very slowly. In 1900 they already had 27,000 children, and in twelve years they have only increased by 5,000. The private institutions, on the other hand, have progressed from 117,533 to a group of 141,000 in the same period of 12 years, i.e. an increase of 24,000, and this in an equal period of 12 years.

[485]

THE DAY NURSERIES - THE TRAINING OF TEACHERS.

473 It will increasingly be the case that the government will not make a help school for the elementary school. If they degenerate in character, then they are already doomed. If ever there is a place, then on these children’s playgrounds the nature of the child must be cared for. It remains to be seen whether what the State Commission tries to promote for the day nurseries will prove to be a blessing.

§11. The training of teachers. The now definitively introduced and firmly established duality of character in our entire system of elementary education naturally does not allow for any longer a unity in the training, with the resulting equality in the examination. Already in so far as the unity of character in the training had long since been abandoned, that one could obtain admission to the examination either by visiting a particular training school, or by following Normal lessons, or finally by private training with a teacher. In these three cases, government involvement was now in the foreground, by what they themselves did to encourage others; but in so far as there was still freedom, that private training schools also counted, Normal schools worked against us from our side, and Christian teacher trainees were also trained. Although the public school system, and that alone, was favored by the government, and the financial benefit was constantly expanded in that direction, it must still be acknowledged that the preparation for education remained free. For the greater part, however, this freedom soon proved to be illusory due to the unity of the examination. In the first attempt, almost all public teachers were appointed to the examination commissions; and since the school supervision, both for the arrondissement and for the district, was initially entrusted exclusively to public teachers, it could not be otherwise but that the examination was taken under such conditions and in such a way that it derived its character entirely from the public state school. Since then, this state of affairs has undoubtedly not remained so exclusive. In the school board, there are now arrondissement and district school inspectors who have wholeheartedly sided with the Special Christian School; and in the examination commissions one has seen here and there at least a minority of Christian teachers appear. Thus the former rigidity gave way to more flexibility, and it is also recognized that there are at least some commissions that have stood up in order not to let the former harshness continue any longer. But however much we may rejoice in this, the intolerable one-sidedness has not yet been overcome by this.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[486]

474 THE SCHOOLS.

§12. The examinations. The examinations, as they are now still being taken, remain a torment. Whatever one may try, the demand that the duality of the school system must now always bring, is also not to be melted into unity in the examination, and Mr. Ds. H. Pierson of Zetten has so truthfully expressed it: The duality of school education imperatively and inexorably also demands duality of the examination. Not in the commissions, which often proved to be accommodating enough, but in the one examination as such lies the evil. For that one examination compelled the candidates to immerse themselves entirely in the ideas, the literature and the pedagogical methods of public education; and those who had to train the candidates could not refrain from doing the same; and herein lay the corruption. The years of training gave the candidates for public education everything they needed, and what could serve them for life. Our candidates, on the other hand, had to temporarily set aside what came from our camp, and live entirely in what was honored among the public school men, in order to understand the questions on the examination and to know what to answer. Whoever has ever gone through an important examination himself knows how strongly the apprehension of what the examiner is likely to ask, strains the mind. All examination is in general a guessing at what may be asked, and a preparing oneself for the answers to those questions. And this does not succeed, or the person to be examined, if he is to become such a Christian teacher, will, to his very serious detriment, live as much as he can in that modern...

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[487]

EXAMINATIONS - MARRIED FEMALE TEACHERS

475 world of thought. This cannot and must not remain so. Every group that occupies a distinct position within the Elementary Schools, and has certainly acquired a certain scope, say for example at least a hundred schools, must obtain the right: 1° to receive the necessary means from the government treasury on an equal footing for the training for the examination; 2° to appoint their own examiners; and 3° to have these examiners determine the requirements for the examination and the necessary literature for it. Only when we have reached this point will freedom be complete. What the coming constitutional revision now promises will also certainly be a step of some significance on the better path; but whoever might even for a moment imagine that this would end the School struggle and that we would have obtained our full rights, has still never understood the a, b, c of our School program.

§13. Married Female Teachers. As for the female teachers, the question of the marriage of female teachers also needs to be briefly discussed here. That the validity of the diploma continues even after marriage does not require further argument; the only question is whether they can remain in actual service in the Elementary Schools after marriage. Those who on every terrain want to put the woman on an equal footing with the man can of course not tolerate any exception for the female teachers either. It is true that the married female teacher can become pregnant, but this need not be the case; and if it occurs, one can accommodate it. Illness is also not infrequently the cause that a female teacher, and likewise a male teacher, cannot appear in the classroom for a few weeks, sometimes a few months, and then this is still tolerated. Why, one then also asks, can this not be entirely the same with a pregnant female teacher as it is to be tolerated with a bedridden teacher? Stated this way, the question seems almost silly, and the case for the pregnant female teacher appears to be won. However, this is offset by the fact that the mother should be in her family and not have to entrust her own children to outside care in order to go and teach other children; that for the married woman the School may often be a strong temptation to take refuge in the sinful practices* as

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[488]

476 ABOUT THE SCHOOLS. indicated above, and that the pregnant woman is not a display that should be given to the youth in the classroom; and that where illness is the exception, motherhood in a married woman is the rule, one may not conclude from a lawful exception to what would here be the rule. Even otherwise, in the end, pregnancy would have to be tolerated even in the unmarried. Just read how one increasingly pleads for free love on all sides. What one should only have to do in this case is to readmit the widow without children to the usual footing, and also after the critical age to restore to the female teacher, even if she is married, the freedom of contract. For the rest, however, marriage must exclude the female teacher from the School. The married female teacher does not belong there.

§14. Our Secondary Education. As a Minister, the writer of this had the privilege in 1903 to recommend to the Queen the appointment of a State Commission, which under the chairmanship of Professor Dr. J. Woltjer, of the Free University in Amsterdam, had to investigate the entire subject matter that belongs to or is connected with Secondary Education, and to submit the necessary proposals in a report. This prompts me not to venture too far in discussing Secondary Education at this point. For the Report of that Commission saw the light of day in a very voluminous octavo, and as a result of the consultation between the leading men in this field, from the various parties. I therefore think it better to mainly refer to this important Report. The more so, as in the field of education there is no terrain that carries so many pitfalls in itself as precisely what is commonly lumped together under the concept of Secondary Education. There is no question of a tripartite ladder of Elementary, Secondary and Higher Education, as if one, after having gone through Elementary and Secondary Education in succession, would only finally end up in Higher Education. Up to now, at least, the Gymnasium has been counted among Higher Education, and whoever aimed at higher studies, passed directly from Elementary to Higher Education, with all

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”
[489]

SECONDARY EDUCATION - SERIES OF STUDENTS. The complete exclusion of Secondary Education. Much earlier, the three-part distinction aimed at three different results of Education in society. Above all, Primary Education, with its adjuncts, only benefits a portion of the visitors to this School. Higher Education is intended only for those who wish to pursue their own scientific studies or open up high positions for themselves in State and Society. And between this very general group of Primary Education, and that extremely limited one that seeks Higher Education, there now lies a third group of a very mixed nature, which, though not overly numerous, yet spreads its tents much more widely than the scholars at Higher Education.

§15. Series of students. Here a general and a special series of students presents itself. A general development of a higher kind than the Primary School with its adjuncts up to the M.U.L.O. is aimed at for men and women who, without taking up a specific salaried position, still wish to take a higher standpoint of development in society. Entirely separate from this, however, there is also under this Secondary Education a whole series of Schools that prepare for special positions in State and Society. This latter part is then again divided into two subgroups, firstly of such an education that aims at a single position, e.g. the Commercial Schools, the School for Navigation, Military Schools, etc., but also on the other hand on a more general group, as for example in the case of Vocational Education, Agricultural Education, Art Education, Education in Women’s Handicrafts, and so on. It is now this extremely mixed character of Secondary Education as a whole that makes its regulation so very difficult. Already in itself, but also in connection with the opposition of principles. What has a purely technical character does not touch the principle, but the treatment, the handling and the equipment of the students exerts a real influence on those who are in contact with it for years. Hence the partly even successful attempt to also go over to a division into Christian and non-Christian Schools in this area, and to let this difference in starting point also come out in the Associations that form themselves for the sake of this instruction. To carry this out consistently, however, the principled distinction is not on this field. Especially not in the case of education for very special subjects, since the number of students applying for them is far too small. All such education must be given locally. Only large cities provide the students for these special subjects. In smaller places, therefore, a division is completely unthinkable, and even in Amsterdam it appears to be increasingly impossible as the specialty of the subject to be taught becomes more pronounced. Much of this education the Government and the Municipal Administration prefer to leave to private initiative, and only help to maintain it by subsidizing. There is nothing against this, provided that the allowance and subsidizing of such private Schools are bound to fixed regulations, and the arrangement that gives these regulations also watches over the freedom of conscience of the students, and against the exercise of a freethinkers’ propaganda, which must remain completely foreign to all such education.

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[490]

478 ON THE SCHOOLS. already successful attempts to also go over to a division into Christian and non-Christian Schools in this area, and to let this difference in starting point also come out in the Associations that form themselves for the sake of this instruction. To carry this out consistently, however, the principled distinction is not on this field. Especially not in the case of education for very special subjects, since the number of students applying for them is far too small. All such education must be given locally. Only large cities provide the students for these special subjects. In smaller places, therefore, a division is completely unthinkable, and even in Amsterdam it appears to be increasingly impossible as the specialty of the subject to be taught becomes more pronounced. Much of this education the Government and the Municipal Administration prefer to leave to private initiative, and only help to maintain it by subsidizing. There is nothing against this, provided that the allowance and subsidizing of such private Schools are bound to fixed regulations, and the arrangement that gives these regulations also watches over the freedom of conscience of the students, and against the exercise of a freethinkers’ propaganda, which must remain completely foreign to all such education.

§16. European Competition. As a publicist, and temporarily in my capacity as Minister, I have always insisted on further developing the special technical and practical education that falls under this. Whoever, like the author of this, had the opportunity to observe this technical and more special work abroad, was saddened every time by our backwardness. Although it must be admitted that in a few branches, such as small silverware, cigar manufacturing, cheese and butter making, and much more, we enjoy European fame, in the more recently emerged industries we have miserably lagged behind. One sees this in the many imports. English clothing is almost indispensable for a man of higher standing, and our ladies steal with their eyes what comes from Paris. For the construction of major works, workers from Germany, England or Belgium must always be called in to help.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.
[491]

EUROPEAN COMPETITION. 479 We are lagging behind in two respects: our men work as laborers for less pay than foreign laborers, and our goods are sold in our own shops and stores for less than the goods that come from abroad. Yet it cannot be said that no efforts have been made to improve this situation. There is hardly any trade for which we have not sought to provide training. In particular, our Trade Schools have been steadily expanded through the faithful care of the Inspector, whose zeal deserves all praise. In 1870 there were only 2, with 214 students and 32 teachers. In 1912 their number had already reached 83, with 7,894 students and 807 teachers. And yet, we are still far from where we need to be. Whoever visits England notices this immediately, and even in Belgium, Professor Pyfferoen had achieved much more effective results in a short time. The cause of this backwardness was to be found in the lack of suitable teachers. The salaries of these teachers were good, but whoever was truly skilled and energetic in their craft could earn much higher income as a master in that trade. As a result, it was often only those who could not make it as masters in their trade who applied for these teaching positions. In any case, they were teachers of the second class, and our Trade Schools were irrevocably entrusted to them. Feeling the worrying effects of this, I as Minister then submitted a bill to establish a well-equipped teacher training college in Haarlem, and the City Council of Haarlem had gladly agreed to provide assistance. However, the members of Parliament from the North gave preference to institutions of lesser quality, as long as they could be established in the North. One knows what has since been tried, especially in Dordrecht, and to some extent succeeded. Yet this way of proceeding is much too slow. Germany, England, and Belgium continue to outpace us; and the freedom of Tariff, in connection with this technical backwardness, is the cause and remains the cause that we are being outstripped in the competition with the development of these crafts in the surrounding States. The training of those who will instruct the craftsmen must be organized differently. Without this, we will not make progress.

[492]

CHRISTIAN SECONDARY SCHOOLS

§17. Christian Secondary Schools. The question of how to establish the distinctive character of the Christian school in the realm of Civic Schools, Higher Civic Schools, and later perhaps Gymnasiums, must be faced with increasing seriousness. For the past fifty years, the establishment of our own schools in this domain was scarcely even considered. Groen van Prinsterer barely mentioned it. At that time, attention was almost exclusively focused on the Elementary Schools. But this has since changed. It has been recognized that attendance at the Secondary and Gymnasium Schools, established by the State or the Municipality, could greatly damage the confessional character of our youth. Hence the earnest efforts undertaken since then to secure our own schools in this domain of General, Secondary, and Gymnasium Education as well. The initial progress of the Private Gymnasiums that gradually emerged was a clear indication of this. As late as 1903, there were only 9 such Gymnasiums with 739 students, while by 1912 there were already 15 Gymnasiums providing assistance and 1593 students benefiting from them. Moreover, there were an additional 19 related institutions with 1769 students. No one has been more meritorious in this regard than Professor Dr. J. Woltjer. This shows how the Free University has also borne fruit indirectly. The Christian Gymnasium founded by this professor is one of the most flourishing and sought-after in the country. Even liberal parents sometimes prefer to entrust their sons to this particular Gymnasium. As for Secondary Education in general for the purpose of general development, the complaint about our poor performance continues. The number of Higher Civic Schools, which stood at 21 in 1865 (18 with 5-year, 2 with 4-year, and 1 with 3-year curricula), had already grown to 88 by 1912. Of these 88 Schools, 27 were under the State, 48 under the Municipalities, and only 13 were of private origin, 6 with and 6 without State subsidy. Most of these Public Schools could also be attended by girls. In addition to this first series, there were 17 more Schools exclusively for girls. None of these Municipal Schools or private ones receive State subsidy. It is only the high school fees that cover the costs. Even for the salaries of the teaching staff of these 17 Schools, over three tons was already being spent in 1912 for 280 certified teachers.

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the
[493]

CHRISTIAN SECONDARY SCHOOLS - DISASTROUS CONSEQUENCE

Here, however, lies the great difficulty, that the proponents of Christian Secondary Schools must themselves, on an equal footing, contribute to the municipal and state taxes in favor of the Public Secondary Schools, and then still have to provide for their own needs. And even though the State now grants subsidies, as has already been achieved to a certain extent for Christian Gymnasium Education, the Municipal administrations that maintain their own Higher School do not even think of this.

§18. Disastrous Consequence. This now has the disastrous consequence, that we not only have to pay the monies to the benefit of the Public Secondary Schools, which we so sorely needed for the establishment and maintenance of our own School, but that the Public Secondary School also inflicts on us an absolutely untenable competition in the form of lower school fees. Parents who entrust their sons or daughters to our Secondary Schools, and even sought a way out, bear this burden: 1) that they must contribute to the costs of the competing Public School; 2) that they have to pay much higher school fees for their own School; and 3) that they still have to raise what is needed to cover the inevitable deficit of their own School. Added to this is the fact that parents from our circle who cannot bear this burden, and are therefore forced to send their children to the Public Secondary Schools, for whom they do not want to make this choice, further exacerbate the financial setback.

Analytical Summary: A Happy Turn.
[494]

As for the schools, if a school is to be found, or at least a place to stay for the day, and a considerable travel allowance must be afforded for the relocation, then everyone feels that the unequal financial demands placed on citizens of the same country, solely on account of their difference in conviction, cannot satisfy the sense of justice. At least, then, the municipal authorities must be obliged to pay a subsidy per pupil for our schools, equal to what is paid per child in the municipal school. If this applied to the rich and the municipality, then at least some relief would be afforded to the existing need. But even so, we are still far from satisfied, and the effort must not be lacking to establish at least two centers of our own secondary boarding schools in each province, where young people from the villages and small towns can find the desired education at the lowest possible price. Once full equality with the public school has been achieved for the elementary school, then our willingness to sacrifice can find another altar of an equally sacred purpose, and spread a blessing over our Christian group in the land.

§19. Confusion of Tongues in Higher Education. The expression “Confusion of Tongues” is borrowed from Gen. XI:7, and as such contains something of a rebuke. In itself, however, it is nothing more than that there will be a variation in the speech of the peoples. And so it has come to pass. The peoples differ from each other in nationality, and this difference in nationality expresses itself in the ubiquitous diversity of their speech. This, however, has not prevented the Babylonian tendency, whenever possible, to have at its disposal one common language for the whole world. This then gave rise to a double usage: the one of the common speech of the common people, and the other of a language set apart, used by an aristocratic group that felt itself elevated above the common people. Already in the ancient Roman Empire, the high aristocracy spoke not Latin, but often Greek, and when now, with the Migration of Peoples, the existing order in the Roman Empire fell apart,

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.
[495]

CONFUSION OF TONGUES IN HIGHER EDUCATION.

In higher circles, one sought a certain unity—to perpetuate it by not going along with the distinct national languages; but rather to let them serve the common people alone; and to use Latin themselves. To a certain extent, this achieved a language for the world community. The Church, under the leadership of the Bishop of Rome, first established itself firmly in this, and introduced the use of the Latin language for part of the services and for high assemblies; and as a consequence, the use of the Latin language was soon also introduced for the Respublica litterarum, that is, for what we would call “the learned world.” After a certain historical course, a certain division always arises between the great multitude and a certain elite, which has risen to a higher level through development. This elite then formed a nobility, an aristocracy, not in blood, but in learning and knowledge. This higher group is then constantly striving to use, among themselves, not the vernacular, but a chosen language. For the nobility, this was often French; in business circles, one now uses more English; and in the world of scholars, one now often prefers German. Only the churches affiliated with Rome continued to use Latin in the service. This usage also long prevailed in our academies. Even in the middle of the last century, this author himself participated in Latin disputations and also wrote his prize essay dissertations in Latin. However, since that time, this custom has become increasingly obsolete. Nowadays, one speaks almost everywhere, even in university life, in the mother tongue. Now this does not apply to countries whose language is generally understood, as is generally the case with French, German and English. For countries like Denmark, Romania, and also for the Netherlands, the reason is that no more students from abroad visit our schools; the reason why in Belgium, both in Liège and in Brussels and Leuven, one arose to not neglect French in the lectures. With the Flemish language, however, one severed the world community. With this tough thing, one must now seriously account, if one wants to understand the course of Higher Education. When the universities arose in Italy, Germany and France and elsewhere, Latin was still the common language of the learned world, as well as of the

[496]

THE SCOPE OF HIGHER EDUCATION. - ON THE UNIVERSITIES.

§20. The Scope of Higher Education. Originally, one spoke of scientia and artes, and then understood by scientia all the higher sciences that were connected with the metaphysical, and by artes all those other sciences that aimed at the technical and practical aspects of our human life. In England, they even now have the habit of using the word “sciences” for only one part, but this usage has gone in the opposite direction, so that they even excluded the metaphysical from the sciences, and under the sciences only understood those sciences that investigated the technical and practical. Yet at the oldest universities, the aristocratic element was so predominant that even in the schools of the Renaissance and in all that arose from Humanism, they continued to adhere to the use of the Latin language. Schools that prepared for the visit of these universities therefore received the name of Latin Schools. In the long run, however, this could not satisfy. One began to see that not only the knowledge of the classical world and of the metaphysical, but also the knowledge of the technical and practical life was indispensable. So they also introduced the teaching of modern languages, natural sciences and history in the Latin Schools. This eventually gave rise to the Gymnasia. But even this did not stop there. Alongside these already mixed Gymnasia, one gradually saw Normal Schools, as they were most pleased to call them, also arise for the demands of practical life. This naturally led to the question whether the medical and natural science faculties at the Academies still needed Latin. In two directions, one thus began to run alongside each other: on the one hand, the old classical education continued to set the tone, and this continued to demand the knowledge of Latin and Greek and classical literature; but alongside this, the knowledge of nature, of technology and of practical life came to the fore, and it was in these circles that one began to protest more and more against the rule of Latin.

Analytical Summary: The three types not equally characteristic. But this does not mean that, in the course of time, each of these three types has in turn dominated the national condition: the Roman Catholics in the Middle Ages, the Calvinists in the 16th century, and the French Revolutionaries after 1789. It does not at all follow from this that they are equal in national significance. Undoubtedly, the Seven Provinces, once living a common life with Flanders and other southern regions, have enjoyed a high and flourishing position and have gradually taken an increasingly important position in Northern Europe; but it must not be forgotten here that the national energy was at the time much more strongly developed in what is now called Belgium than in the northern provinces. Neither Amsterdam nor Rotterdam could in those days be placed on a par with Ghent and Bruges. The wealth of medieval life had more stamped its imprint on the national character in the South than in the North. The reference to the rich medieval type therefore has much less significance for the present Kingdom of the Netherlands than for the western part of Belgium. And in the second place, it must be added that at that time the unity of our national life was much less consolidated. Under the Burgundians, the Dutch provinces did not occupy an independent position, but were united with what lay outside our later borders; and under Charles V and Philip, we even came, though only in a personal way, into a Union with entirely different states from Central and Southern Europe—something which could not but be detrimental to the confirmation of our own national character. Whoever investigates the historical significance of our lower lands may well find the period we lived through in the Middle Ages highly important, but nevertheless hesitates for a moment to acknowledge without reservation that our glorious period dates from April 1, 1572. Not in the Middle Ages, but in the period of the Reformation, lies the high point of our national history. It cannot be emphasized enough how much scrupulous fairness is required in such a comparison, and we shall beware of the assertion that from the Calvinist side the correct toll of reverence has always been paid to the medieval period in our history, to which they undoubtedly had a right. But it stands to reason that from the Roman Catholic side, too, we have not always been done justice. It cannot be valued highly enough that especially in the second half of the 19th century a more impartial judgment gained influence. Just with a view to this more unanimous judgment, it may now at least be established that not only the foreign historians, one may well say without any exception of importance, honor the period after 1572 as the highest and most glorious, but that also within the country the best interpreters of each of the three types increasingly come to the unanimous conclusion that the world significance of our small country came out most richly in the heyday of Calvinism. This in no way denies that our national life in those days of our glory also showed dark sides. The resistance to the monarchical state form, and even against the House of Orange, has almost become a source of our unhappy discord until 1789, and it is impossible to say how much higher our country would have soared if immediately after the separation from Philip II the crown had been offered to Prince William. It had to come to this in 1813; but who calculates what national suffering would have been spared us if we had taken this important step two centuries earlier. But even if one plays open cards and takes into account all the suffering, oppression and restriction of rights that the temporarily dominant group has inflicted on the other two groups, it is historically established that we were never before 1572 what our national life was in the period between those two dates, and never again after 1789. To top it off, the fact that we offered a refuge to the politically and religiously persecuted from all countries is proof that the actual life here was a life in freedom, and that the publications and ordinances that claimed to infringe on this freedom had more the effect of satisfying formal demands than of inflicting suffering on those who deviated.
[497]

THE SCOPE OF HIGHER EDUCATION. - ON THE UNIVERSITIES. 485

This now seems to open up this way out, that for all higher studies a partial familiarity with Latin will remain required, but that for the rest the higher education studies will split into two groups: on the one hand the group of the technical-practical, which will gather in the Polytechnics, and on the other hand the deepening of the studies that make the university life flourish. At the Polytechnics, Latin would then be completely dropped, and at the Universities it would retain its old place of honor in the theological, legal and literary faculties, while for the medical and natural science studies it would only be maintained to a very limited extent for etymological interests. This will naturally lead to also returning the Gymnasia from Higher Education to Secondary Education. As soon as not only the Gymnasium, but also the Normal School prepares for the university studies, and even the Gymnasium, next to the classical, also takes up the modern form, all reason disappears to include the Gymnasium education exclusively in Higher Education, and the concept of Higher Education will no longer be able to include anything other than the education at the Polytechnics and at the Universities.

§21. On the Universities. That our Higher Education, if the Gymnasia are removed, does not consist solely of the Universities, but that alongside the Universities as a second kind of Higher Education the Polytechnics are to be mentioned separately, was included in my proposal in 1909 in the Higher Education Act; and it did not even stop there, that this name was inserted in Art. 4, but further on in Art. 30–69 a whole new section was included on the Technical University in Delft. And although the immediate effect of this distinction between Universities and Polytechnics has been opposed from the liberal side, a further application of this split has nevertheless quickly emerged from life itself. One need only think of the Commercial University in Rotterdam, which rests on exactly the same basis. This same system will later be applied just as much to agriculture, shipping and much more. But even if this is still pending, the principle is

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over
[498]

The polytechnic school, which Thorbecke had quite wrongly placed under secondary education, has now finally come to its rightful place as a Technical University, and with this, the formal distinction between the Universities and the Institutes of Higher Education has been settled. To such a degree, in fact, that a Doctorate is now also obtainable in the technical disciplines. The distinction between secondary and higher education now manifests itself clearly in both the Universities and the Institutes of Higher Education. In secondary education, only the already discovered is imparted to the students, and the means are provided to the best of one’s ability, but without any independent research or striking out in new directions. Yet this latter aspect is precisely what constitutes the distinctive character of higher education, both at the Institutes of Higher Education and at the Universities. Higher education, says Article 1 of the Law, encompasses two things: 1) the formation and preparation for independent scholarly study, and 2) the formation and preparation for assuming societal positions. In this, the engineer stands on par with the jurist and the theologian. Hence, the training of both requires scientific, i.e., higher education. It is certainly a deficiency that the Law knows only the formation of disciples for the service of the state and for science as the raison d’être of the Institutes of Higher Education and the Universities. Even if there were no disciples in a certain discipline, the professor in that discipline would still have to maintain his honorable position. Behind the provision of higher education lies scientific research, from which not only the student but also the State, Society, and the Church profit, and this too must be indicated as the purpose of the higher studies; and this can be done, provided one does not too anxiously confine oneself to the concept of higher education. That it would be entirely sensible in this connection to entrust the training of a part of our physicians to an Institute of Higher Education, lies in the nature of the matter. Not every physician needs to be a scientifically independent researcher. Many of them live solely for practice.

§22. Of the Universities. The Universities pose a difficulty that the Institutes of Higher Education do not have...

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the
[499]

THE UNIVERSITIES. It is not in a very small measure that they penetrate into the deepest principles of life, and thereby of themselves impose the requirement that professors who are called together to spread the necessary light over the same subject, should start from the same fundamental principle. An atheist and a Calvinist can in no way cooperate in the formation of the same group of students.
