Then the one breaks down what the other builds up, and the result is a painful confusion in the minds of the students. The argumentation of the one professor may differ from the proof given by his colleague; one may lay more emphasis than the other on a particular part of the science; personal difference can even enrich the content of the instruction, otherwise the talent would not come to its right. But there can be no question of simply reading out an identical textbook. Here too, uniformity is no ornament, but a deprivation of the instruction of its luster. Only, it must and shall remain the joint building of one and the same temple of science, which rests, and indeed in all its parts rests, on one and the same foundation. This, however, did not present any difficulty, at least in part, as long as the Vatican set the tone throughout Christendom. The Church then watched, and whoever forsook the life and worldview of the Church, ultimately had to deal with the Inquisition. First the Renaissance, and later Humanism, have then brought a certain laxity in this, but the history of the Reformation, and especially that of Calvinism, proves how sharply every deviation from the fundamental principle was watched.

[500]

488 THE SCHOOLS. Now that the Reformation has also broken through here, and a divergent principle of life has gained preference in State and Society, the censorship of the Vatican has indeed been set aside, but by no means in order to henceforth surrender the study to unprincipled arbitrariness. On the contrary, even at Leiden University the signing of the three Forms of Unity was required, and elsewhere I have already pointed out how even professors Scholten and Kuenen had signed their names under the formulas prescribed for that purpose. And although one has since the middle of the last century principally departed from this, and all Higher Education has then been declared neutral, this had no other consequence than that now at all State Universities the principle of free personal insight, which unbelieving science has always put to the fore, has also been accepted by the government as the standard for the appointment of professors. The only measure that could still be applied was that of skill and talent.

§23. Variation of talent. Candidates of one, three, five and even ten talents always come up for an appointment. This distinction always continues. This is the general creaturely variation, which applies not only in every human society, but even in the animal world. Just look at how enormously the price of the one and the other race horse, of the one and the other hunting dog can differ. This skill, this talent must therefore be valued the least. Whoever, where the choice is between a brilliant teacher and a dunce, appoints the dunce and leaves the man of genius at home, is guilty of a blunder that cannot be justified. Only, talent never replaces the principle. A father who, seeking a tutor for his son, chooses a morally depraved one because he is more skilled, would betray his child. To appoint as a theological professor a wonderfully talented man, but who is an atheist, would be a grievous sin. And so it is here: talent stands high, and skill demands full appreciation, provided it is always under the control of the principle professed by the School to which the appointee will belong. Whoever is not wholeheartedly devoted to that principle may not be appointed, no matter how wonderfully talented he may be; and even if he later fell away, he cannot remain. Control remains the inexorable demand of every governing principle. §24. Cooperation. We have already pointed to the requirement of “cooperation.” When a team of four horses goes out, all four must be choice stallions, and not four, however splendid, grays, of which one pulls to the right, the other to the left, and the other two in opposite directions. There must be unity of principle, unity of direction, unity of action. This is the only way to achieve the desired result. And this applies not only to the team of professors, but also to the entire organization of higher education. The university is not a collection of independent faculties, but an organic whole, in which each part is subordinate to the whole, and the whole is supported by each part. The theological faculty may not stand apart from the others, nor may the legal or medical faculty be independent of the theological. They must all work together, each in its own way contributing to the building of the one temple of science.

Analytical Summary: The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,
[501]

COOPERATION - SOLVING THE UNIVERSITY PROBLEM. 489 To veer to the left, and to loosen the straps of the harness. If one falls short here, one naturally lapses back into the neutral wake, and, by abandoning one’s principle, reckons again solely with technical or intellectual talents. The rule flowing from the principle must, of course, govern the entire setup of every principled University. Directors, curators, professors - they must all, without distinction, yield to the principle, and recognize no higher honor than to serve the professed principle. In the founding of such a principled, and therefore free, University, this principle must also be professed in the most absolute sense as the power governing all things, just as the Free University in Amsterdam stipulates in its statutes that “all education shall rest entirely and exclusively on Reformed principles.” This must naturally also continue in the examinations, and when the student comes to his final examination, especially for his doctorate, so that he will be granted the right by the University to now teach himself and appear as a teacher, then there can be no question of any deviation from the principle with such a scholar. Whoever raised objections to this would show that they do not understand the significance of the doctorate at a principled University. For the distinction between a neutral and a principled University lies precisely in this, that the former asks only for a certain degree of knowledge, while the latter, in everything that emanates from it, is always under the absolute sovereignty of the principle it has placed in the foreground. Curators too have a double task here, not only to check the solidity of the education given, but first of all to maintain the loyalty of the foundation to the principle. Attending the teaching is therefore, from time to time, a part of their task, which they may not neglect. That they too must stand firm in the principle in order to be able to be Curators, requires no further argument.

§25. Solving the University Problem. The demands defended in the previous section can, however, even with the strictest maintenance, still never bring the University problem to a final solution in a small country like ours. Perhaps

Analytical Summary: Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit
[502]

Could the Roman Catholic faction of our population, just as in Freiburg, establish a university fully serving their principles? And for theology as well as for law and the humanities, it would be entirely conceivable that their university could be adequately staffed with professors, equipped with resources, and provided with a library. There is already an association that is quietly working towards this end goal. The costs of these three faculties need not be so high that the Roman Catholic population, which is nearly a third of the entire country and commands broad groups of the wealthy and businessmen, could not raise the necessary funds and would not be willing to do so. But it stands differently with the medical and technical disciplines. For these fields, the instrumentation of the school requires such high expenditures for establishment and maintenance that they will always exceed the means of any faction, while also cutting off all serious competition due to inadequacy. But even if we assume that the Roman Catholic faction could succeed in this as well, for smaller groups that scarcely make up a sixth of the population, or even less, the possibility of attaining a fully equipped and instrumentalized university of their own would naturally be excluded. Nor would the Calvinists have the men or the money for this end. Groningen has the smallest of the four state universities, but even this university we could by no means equal, neither in personnel nor in instruments and resources. Perhaps, though on a much smaller scale, a presentable whole could still have been obtained, if all Calvinists in the land had cooperated towards this end, as was the case in the beginning. But when the ecclesiastical antithesis later divided and weakened the forces sufficiently, this ideal collapsed of its own accord, and now nothing else can be aimed at than the occupation of four or five faculties, each with three or four professors, and the acquisition on the smallest possible scale of the absolutely indispensable resources—while even this would still entail the requirement that the government be willing to subsidize this free Christian education in an energetic manner financially. This is the goal that must be pursued. Something has already been achieved; and much more can still be accomplished.

[503]

SOLVING THE PROBLEM

§26. Solving the Problem. Yet one should never imagine that in this way the University problem itself would be resolved. An institution like the Free University is and remains a corrective to the wrongly constituted public education. However one may turn it, it aims to rectify a fault and fill a void that disfigures the all-dominant, neutrally named but very anti-neutral government education. Less even than primary or secondary education can higher education be neutral. A professor penetrates deeply into the foundations of the scientific edifice with his research. To have one field taught in stark contrast to another within the same faculty sows nothing but woeful confusion in the minds of the students. The one is continually tearing down what the other sought to build up. And in the higher circles that, as the product of such conflicting education, will soon govern social and ecclesiastical life, the result can only be wavering and uncertainty in the minds. Hence the proposal already made in 1878 for solving this problem, which still seems to me the only effective one. This proposal amounted to this: that the government would call into being one or more central institutions for university education, but would absolutely refrain from appointing the personnel. The government would then have to confine itself to preparing all the material aids—buildings, libraries, and instruments—and place this material on a generous scale at the disposal of any university school that applied for it. That university school would then, each for itself, determine the principle, spirit, and direction of higher education, and would have to appoint and remunerate the directors, curators, professors, lecturers, etc. Certain legal regulations would have to establish the formal conditions that such a university institution would have to meet in order to be counted. It could be stipulated, for example, that at least three or four faculties would have to be established; that in each of these three or four faculties a certain minimum number of professors would have to be appointed, with a yearly income not below a certain level. Scholarships could be made available by the government, and on a basis of equality, and more in proportion to the number of professors in office. We refrain here from any further sketching. What we aim at is clear: complete provision by the government of all higher education with all material, external aids, without the slightest influence on the choice of principles and direction; and every institution of higher education that applied for this, provided it met certain formally established requirements, to be generously admitted to make use of these all-sided aids. Only in this way can equality of rights be obtained in the field of higher education. As long as this ideal, if one may so call it, has not been achieved, the government will always, with the aid of the money that comes from all the inhabitants, continue to pull the spiritual direction of higher education in one direction, and the part of the population that, out of conviction, moves toward the opposite direction will always be deprived of its rights and oppressed in an unlawful way in what is holiest to everyone.

Analytical Summary: Guided by Orange. To this has been added: “guided by Orange”; an addition that must not be omitted. For it cannot be denied that in the characterized Calvinism, as it also penetrated our country from Geneva and France at that time, there was a danger not to be underestimated, the aftereffects of which are still discernible. In the last chapter of the First Part of this work, I have extensively explained how from the outset there was a decisive element in Calvinism that could only develop and mature later, and that, politically speaking, only came to full effect in the United States; but it was also then added that in Geneva (one need only think of the burning of Servetus) an intolerant dogmatism still prevailed, which, though indispensable for the church, could only result in schism and oppression in its political consequences.
[504]

CHAPTER XVIII
THE SOCIAL QUESTION

§1. Then and Now. When in 1878, nearly half a century ago, the basic principle for the solution of the Social Question had to be indicated in Art. 19 of the Anti-Revolutionary Program, I drafted and saw approved this general declaration: “The Anti-Revolutionary party recognizes the necessity of also cooperating, by means of our legislation, to a greater extent than hitherto, so that the relationship between the different social classes may as much as possible correspond to the demands of God’s Word.” This formulation excluded...

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[505]

Now, as I turn to the speech I delivered in the Second Chamber on November 28, 1874, on the Social Question, which is printed in the publication titled: Some Chamber Advice from the Years 1874 and 1875, pp. 191 ff.—as for the guiding thought, I had already taken the very same position in that 1874 speech that Article 19 of the Anti-Revolutionary Program of 1878 expressed, and I may add that on November 22 and 24, 1910—so 32 years later—I once again pleaded for it in the Second Chamber with a speech that was printed in De Standaard of December 10 of that year. Yet, in the time between these two speeches, the way the Social Question presented itself here in our country had changed completely. In our Parliament in 1874, Mr. van Houten was still the sole one who had tried to draw the attention of his fellow members to this mighty problem. Imagine now what position this eminent Statesman, without having changed his principles, still occupies in his old age, and one immediately feels why what he then offered as the best was no longer sufficient for me. It is also remarkable how the first International, which in 1873 here in our country made a beginning with its appearance, turned to me, who was then still a minister, to provoke, as it wished, a public debate between one of its leaders and myself. I refused this, for from the outset I saw how radically we differed in principle; and yet, all that was within me pressed me even then, no less cordially than they, to insist on the protection of the worker by law. A thought for which the Liberalism of that time in its press had nothing but a mocking laugh, but which, thank God, has now at least in part been realized. The Netherlands had lagged behind in the Social Question. In England, the vigorous action of Maurice had already succeeded in bringing about partial relief of the distress. Among the German Roman Catholics, a broad-based action had already gone out from Von Ketteler, the Bishop of Mainz, which continued and still works on. Rudolf Meyer was at that time already expressing himself in a similar spirit on the political terrain. Here with us, on the other hand, they still slept the sleep of the secure, until Domela Nieuwenhuis too, sparing nothing, soon made the penitential trumpet sound; something that earned him Kuyper’s appreciation and in turn constantly earned me something of his sympathy.

[506]

494 THE SOCIAL QUESTION.

§2. A Feeling of Anxiety. The first perception that the raising of these horns among our citizenry and in our government circles provoked was certainly a feeling of anxiety; and soon enough, there was a drive to counteract this sudden rise of the international aspiration, precisely among the lower population. Who does not still remember how in Amsterdam they sang on the streets: “All the socialists in a herring barrel”? A cowardly way of combating, provoked by what I myself, as a young girl at the time, heard shouted from the other side on the street: “We’ve had enough of loving, we finally want to hate.” Soon enough, one heard nothing but the roar of a wild street riot and an uprising that was secretly being prepared, about to burst forth. Of system against system, one heard little at the time. Rather, it was the threat that went out from the dangerous corner. Stuart Mill and his school had intended it excellently with their all-unraveling individualism, but the outcome had bitterly belied their assertion, and as Rawson Birks expressed it so sharply: “The avowed object has been to achieve the greatest happiness of the greatest number; but the greatest discontent of the greatest numbers seems almost to be the result.” Fortunately, we saw a Christian counter-action arise at Hovy’s Brewery, on the Kadijk Heights, and in Wolber’s Friend of Truth, and soon after in Patrimonium under Poesia and Kater. It is mainly to this that we owe the fact that our Christian labor circles were surrounded and not corrupted by this revolutionary spirit. In the more revolutionary circles, on the other hand, the spirit of the Paris Commune was at work. In 1871, this Commune movement broke out in Paris even under the German siege. This action of the Communards seemed then to portend a barricade uprising in all Western states, and even in Russia. In the circles of the capitalists, one could not help but imagine that one would soon have to flee one’s country, perhaps even one’s life and property. Only in the army did the preservation of safety seem to be partly guaranteed, until even in this army tones began to sound that made one apprehensive of a repetition of what had kept France in constant unrest after 1789, and might now also break the civic peace in our country.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[507]

A FEELING OF ANXIETY. - THE CONVENTION. - THE HOLY SCRIPTURE. 495

§3. The Convention. Comparing this with the current state of affairs, one sees how since 1880 this order-disturbing and everything-upending drive has come to a standstill. It became clear that the military power could no longer be trifled with. One came to the insight that the wild street uproar offered no lasting gain. And at the head of the revolutionary action, men of study and clearer insight gradually emerged, who from the outset saw that not on the barricade, but in the Convention, through deep investigation and vigorous deliberation, the means was offered to arm the popular action, not with pistols and daggers, but with social knowledge and social willpower. Although there was also a certain riotous and restless mood here in our country in 1871 and the first following years, with the appearance of the International, this came to an end of its own accord. And when it soon became clear that influential statesmen from both the Liberal and the Christian side openly acknowledged the necessity of creating better conditions for labor, the opportunity was given to also bring about such decisive changes in the existing situation in our country that much distress could be averted and a happier future prepared. This political action was then joined by the international movement that had been called into being by Marx; and although the whole of this action still had much unthought-out in it, in which later criticism could bring better improvement, the trend was around 1880 nevertheless towards a more well-considered, therefore more serious and more promising endeavor, to renew quite a bit more than just the facade, without overturning the social structure. §4. The Holy Scripture.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.

When the labor movement first arose, the impression prevailed in Christian circles that the adherents of this movement were almost entirely under the influence of the Holy Scripture.

[508]

496 THE SOCIAL QUESTION. That Jesus himself had kept far removed from this, is evident. To move the employers to a milder and strictly just treatment of their personnel, to evangelize and work upon this personnel, and to offer help in case of sickness or otherwise, is considered to be a very definite demand of Christian philanthropy; but that here it was not merely a question of charity, that it was a mighty social issue and in part even a matter of rights, was not immediately perceived. Was not even slavery in Holy Scripture left untouched as a normal phenomenon of that time? Even the Apostle Paul did not attack the right of the owner over his slave. See his letter to Philemon. Slavery had originally arisen among the agricultural and warlike peoples as a matter of course. Not among the nomads, nor even among the coastal peoples, but among those nations who, like many tribes in Africa even today, are bent on subjugating neighboring tribes by force of arms. At first they used to kill the prisoners of war outright, but soon they saw that this was a waste of strength, kept them alive, and set them to work. This then gave the double advantage that it put an end to the slaughter, and that the owners, freed from manual labor, now aimed at a higher cultural level and thus promoted the development of our human life in the crafts. In the Israelite legislation, slavery was likewise still considered justified; only with this difference, that an Israelite slave became free again after 7 years, unless he himself chose to remain with his master. But although this mitigation of fate and stopping of much that was abominable in slavery had also taken place under Israel, yet slavery still had a legal existence even there, and the Apostle himself does not venture to abolish it. He softens it through brotherly love and takes away all its harshness, but the social institution as such he leaves as it is. The conclusion is therefore inescapable, that in the sinful development of our race, slavery was a phase of our human life that could not be avoided; that it had to come; and that it undoubtedly bore its fruit. Especially the short apostolic epistle of James is instructive here. He does not even touch slavery as such, but cuts sharply into the words of the employers who indeed hire their personnel for wages, but treat them in a wounding and inhuman manner. “Come now, you rich people, weep and wail for the miseries that are coming to you.”

[509]

THE HOLY SCRIPTURE - THE SURRENDER OF VARIATION.

497 “Behold, the wages of the laborers who mowed your fields, which you kept back by fraud, are crying out; and the cries of the reapers have reached the ears of the Lord of hosts” (James 5:4). Two things must thus be recognized. The first is that the relationship between those who serve and those who are served cannot be regulated by a single fixed method for all lands and for all times. The difference in conditions and circumstances, and no less the historical process, can at times make what was once useful become an abomination; and in the second place, every phase in social coexistence breeds its own evil, and this evil can only be countered by godliness and neighborly love. So it is also now. Even in the present state of the social problem, a juridical, an economic, a religious, and an ethical motive is speaking out.

[510]

498 THE SOCIAL QUESTION. There must be a push for change in many relationships, there must be a striving for a more just economic order, and tirelessly the spiritual-philanthropic power of godliness and moral sense must work upon society.

§5. The Surrender of Variation.

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.

The entire social question hangs on the relationship in which those who work and those who employ others will stand towards each other. Egoism constantly tends to either demand too much or to deliver too little. This recurring historical fact has from of old led to pondering on a system of human coexistence in which this entire antithesis would disappear, in order to thereby cut off all cause of sin and misery that continually arises from the relationship between these two categories. Socialism, which now seeks to conquer the minds, is the mightiest manifestation in history of this purposeful endeavor. On a smaller scale, however, we constantly encounter this same attempt from of old. Even in the first Christian community in Jerusalem, a tendency to realize the idea of the commune manifested itself. We read after all in Acts 2:45: “And they were selling their possessions and belongings and distributing the proceeds to all, as any had need.” No compulsion lay behind this. Peter spoke clearly to Ananias and Sapphira (Acts 5:4) that the sale of their property had been a voluntary act, and that even after the sale, the money obtained thereby had remained at their own free disposal. So in the tendency that then arose to promote communal ownership, there was in the least no Apostolically ordained social system, but merely a paradisiacal feeling. No more “I and you.” No conflicting interests to dominate life. It would be one holy household under God’s care. The striving for equality has continually resurfaced in history, but just as in Jerusalem, it has always ended in bitter disappointment. In the absence of sin in a paradisiacal state or heavenly bliss, bounded possession, or the struggle for possession, is an unthinkable thought. Hence the communist idea kept drawing attention to itself again and again. But only because everyone carried sin in their heart, the outcome was never anything other than an increase in misery. When the Apostles later had to travel around to collect alms for the impoverished Church of Jerusalem, this expressed a judgment on the misstep of the early Church. This very same outcome would also await the International, if it ever succeeded in realizing the socialist idea of a state founded on common ownership. The delusion that lay in the “Verelendungstheorie” can rest here, even though it has very much shaken the attractiveness of Marx’s idea, that the Socialists themselves had to admit the incorrectness of the theory. The “whole Marx” has therefore been abandoned since.

§6. Marx Clipped of His Wings.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.

In what is now still being driven, the original theory has been clipped of its wings. Yet so genuine is and remains this entire Socialism, a comprehensive attempt to make the antithesis between possession and non-possession, between wage-giver and wage-earner, between rich and poor, disappear once and for all, and to entrust the care of our human affairs to a central power that would administer it all, dispose of it all, and distribute it all. A human coexistence without struggle, without privilege, equal for all. Here too, therefore, a return to Paradise, or a reaching forward to what will regulate itself on the new earth. Yet it is not the

[511]

CLIPPED BY MARX’S WINGS - RETURN TO THE NATURAL STATE

As a rule, it can be said that the Socialist no longer believes in either a Paradise or a future perfection and heavenly bliss. He even admits that what he is pursuing was not possible in earlier times. However, he maintains that the period has now arrived in which this new form of life will dominate society, and therefore he zealously advocates for its realization.

§7. Return to the Natural State

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how

This idea of a return to a natural state was also enticing in the early days of the French Revolution. Just think of Gumalen and Lina, and reckon with the Aboriginal Society that was founded in London. Even in the clothing of that time, this return to the natural state sought to express itself. The Socialists, on the other hand, did not fall into any such fantasies. They posited a process of culture with a regular course, but also maintained that in this process the moment had now arrived, not only for the first time, but also with certainty, in which private property had to abdicate and community action had to take the place of personal action. In Henry George, we perceive a similar tendency, but only with regard to land ownership. In 1880, his Progress and Poverty appeared in New York, in which he definitively developed his system. The land, the soil, the habitable terrain, had to permanently dominate the situation as a source of livelihood and life activity. It was the unequal distribution of this terrain that was now corrupting the situation. For the land was now the possession of a few, and the majority were deprived of every plot of ground for the sole of their foot. Especially England and Scotland offered a spectacle here that aroused indignation. Hence the call that now came to the government to successively expropriate or confiscate all land if necessary, and to place it at the disposal of the entire population. Here too, a perfectly genuine element was at work, but the application that George gave to it failed. In a similar direction, albeit under a completely different motive, the great Trusts are now also working. It cannot even be denied that the municipal administrations of large cities are not only inclined to take various things, such as gas supply, water supply, cemeteries, tram traffic, and so much more, under their own management, but even feel compelled to do so. Especially during the terrible war that broke out in 1914, it has been seen how even in neutral countries the national government and the municipal administration were forced to a considerable extent to take the supply of necessary foodstuffs, their sale, and the determination of prices under their own control. This has always been the case with investments in fortified cities. And so it may, and cannot be denied, that there can be a definite necessity, both in the general situation and in particular relationships and events, to introduce certain community management for a certain time.

[512]

500 THE SOCIAL QUESTION

There is even more than one country that, in order to raise money, has thought of introducing a monopoly; so of tobacco and matches in France, and of opium in our East.

§8. All Appeal to Holy Scripture Excluded

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.

There is also no doubt that Socialism has found certain inducements for its utopias in the existing conditions, but it can never appeal to Holy Scripture. The scriptural history goes through various historical periods, and each of these periods sets its own demands. What remains constant under all periods is only these three things: first, that what applied in Paradise will not be applicable again in the Consummation, in the currently disrupted state by sin; second, that each historical period brings its own demands with it; and third, that amidst the change of the periods of culture, one demand remains fixed and never changes, and this expresses itself in the words: “You shall love your neighbor as yourself.” In the motto of the French Revolution, which inserted “equality” between “freedom and brotherhood,” this ideal of leveling is simply incapable of realization. There is nothing so unequal as the human world, and it only brings a curse if one wants to impress the evil mark of “uniformity” on our human life. Whoever wants to squeeze our human life into the corset of “uniformity” does away with the free breathing. Precisely in the variation lies the wealth of a free life that expresses itself, which may not be smothered in the unity of the model.

[513]

THE GUILDS HAVE OUTLIVED THEIR TIME. NEW DEMANDS.

§9. The Guilds Have Outlived Their Time. The rising labor movement that set in during the last quarter of the 19th century, and still continues with heavy waves, arose from a radical change in social conditions. For the Middle Ages, the Guilds, at least in the cities, had rendered excellent service; and they still do, though in a modified form, the “Innungen” (this is the German name for Guilds) among our Eastern neighbors. These Guilds for trades and industries were, compared to what now coalesces, active on a very small scale. The masters joined these Guilds, in connection with the Church, and with the permission of the Authorities, firmly together; and thus they even placed their own workers under stricter regulation of their own movement. One was given, after examination, a diploma for the trade in which one wanted to act as a master. And at the same time, one was concerned with ensuring good treatment for the workers, especially with regard to Sunday rest and night rest. Under the rule of these Guilds, there was also a very strong mutual trust between masters and workers. Only, the figures of the personnel remained, in connection with the then prevailing state of trade and industry, incredibly small. In somewhat larger textile enterprises, one often allowed the workers to set up the loom in their own homes, so that nothing separated the father from his family. In many respects, a firmly regulated and desired condition. There reigned peace through contentment. The flaw was merely that these Guilds, as is often the case with such institutions, did not keep pace with their time, and therefore ultimately became obsolete. §10. New Demands. So the newly born condition gradually began to make quite different demands, especially on four grounds. First, the steam engine brought about a complete change in the technical field. One still saw this recently when the steam crane was introduced for unloading ships, and with the help of the steam machine, three men now did what otherwise gave work to twelve. Indeed, steam, as it succeeded more and more, has brought about a complete revolution in working life by applying its power to all points of labor through all-encompassing machines. Secondly, this technical upheaval led to a very strong expansion of the workforce, and thus the familiar bond between master and worker fell away. The technical facilitation in the production of goods brought these goods to lower prices and thus within reach of many more buyers. So business, industry, trade and shipping continued to expand, and naturally the working population responded with more abundant breeding. Thirdly, these colossal factories now arose, which called for hundreds of workers, and through this gathering of so many workers under one master, all domestic relations between the two were destroyed, and the mass was led to think of joint defense of rights and interests against the master. And fourthly, this naturally led the workers, first in the same factory, then in the same trade in several factories, and finally all together, to a certain understanding and cooperation, which ultimately even extended beyond borders in the international defense of rights and interests. §11. The Masters Hesitate. At first, the masters did not go along with this. They often disputed their workers’ right to act in this way. As indicated above, the better-minded masters did not lack, who even cared paternally for their personnel and often helped them on a religious and philanthropic basis, but this too brought no change in the conception of the mutual legal position. This has then led to sharp, sometimes bitter conflicts, and not until much later, also under the leadership of the Roman Catholic clergy, has a different awareness gradually come to the fore. In Belgium, it even went so far that many Roman Catholic masters preferred to retire to a monastery for a few days a year, to consider before the face of God whether they had not fallen short in the treatment and handling of their workers. When I “Boaz.”..

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[514]

502 THE SOCIAL QUESTION.

the more it succeeded, has brought about a complete revolution in working life by applying its power to all points of labor through all-encompassing machines. Secondly, this technical upheaval led to a very strong expansion of the workforce, and thus the familiar bond between master and worker fell away. The technical facilitation in the production of goods brought these goods to lower prices and thus within reach of many more buyers. So business, industry, trade and shipping continued to expand, and naturally the working population responded with more abundant breeding. Thirdly, these colossal factories now arose, which called for hundreds of workers, and through this gathering of so many workers under one master, all domestic relations between the two were destroyed, and the mass was led to think of joint defense of rights and interests against the master. And fourthly, this naturally led the workers, first in the same factory, then in the same trade in several factories, and finally all together, to a certain understanding and cooperation, which ultimately even extended beyond borders in the international defense of rights and interests.

[515]

HESITATION OF EMPLOYERS - ORGANIZATION INDISPENSABLE

503 When I helped establish this, I had hoped to also connect a similar purification of conscience to these institutions, but this did not succeed for me. Perhaps this idea was not yet ripe for realization in the Calvinist circle. Yet we are now so far along that even among the employers, the eye is beginning to open more clearly to the new situation that has arisen. Already at our Social Congress, work was done in this direction. Writings such as those from the hand of Dr. Slotemaker de Bruine and Smeenk brought the light, and without exaggeration it may be said that the investigation has now progressed sufficiently to lead both sides to a clear insight into the completely changed situation. Now this change consists in this, that the relationship between employer and worker in the large enterprise has lost its former domestic character, and in its place has arisen a need for public regulation of mutual relations and mutual rights, resting in their own organization on both sides and seeking the solution of the dispute arising therefrom in the reconciliation between those two organizations. On the line of Stuart Mill, only one locomotive moved, namely that of employer individualism; but now alongside it, the locomotive of the worker has also obtained equal rights of passage, not yet in the eyes of all, but yet in the steadily more prevailing public opinion.

§12. Organization Indispensable

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.

In England, and to some extent also in America, it had not yet come to this ultimate antithesis, which now calls for solutions and reconciliation. On a fairly extensive scale, employers and workers had managed to understand each other here, and one had succeeded in making a workers’ organization of a more practical nature appear, which ultimately disposed of a gigantic capital. Yet this no longer helped. A conflict that had actually once arisen forces its way through to the outermost contours of its own accord, and one also becomes increasingly aware that the principled antithesis from the mainland of Europe is now also passing over to Great Britain, and there too the International has gained a firm foothold.

[516]

504 THE SOCIAL QUESTION

Although this may be regretted on the one hand, yet one should not be discouraged by it. A dispute such as the one that has now arisen demands a principled solution and regulation, and one forfeits one’s best chances if one loses courage to push through. Yet one should not flatter oneself with the delusion that the dispute that has arisen here could be settled without friction and shock. On the contrary, that organization will have to remain the lever, but almost insurmountable difficulties stand in the way of that organization on both sides and of the cooperation of both sides for the achievement of the goal, and neither the employer nor the worker can penetrate deeply enough into the difficulty that arises here. Immediately, the ever-increasing world traffic stands in the way of what could perhaps soon be achieved in one’s own country. This world traffic, which is increasingly absorbing everything particular in each country and people, cuts off the possibility of looking only at the national conditions in what is to be organized and reconciled. This fading of the national, in order to solve it in the international, will bring one country an advantage, the other a very serious disadvantage. A country with rich sources of production will gain by it; a country with a poor soil and small production will lose by it. This naturally reacts back on the business of all kinds, and so it cannot be otherwise, or in the one, richly blessed country, the position of the workers will almost be able to attain a luxury, while the other country will face meager provision, if not outright poverty. One can therefore not apply the national standard exclusively to the organization, but must also take into account what has already been organized among our neighbors. One must not shrink from the inconvenience that arises from this. Whoever wants the goal must also want the means, and without adapting to what the general conviction leads to, one does not get there.

§13. Objections

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.

But even apart from this, the slogan of organization is more quickly proclaimed than realized; even in one’s own circle, one is still far from ready with this. For immediately one runs into the almost insurmountable objection that very many among the interested parties do not want to hear about such an organization. They appeal to...

[517]

OBJECTIONS - SELF-ORGANIZATION

The desire for personal freedom. If there is a patron who wants to go his own way, there is only one question for him: whether he can also find workers who, like him, do not want to belong to an organization. If he succeeds, such a person does not bother with anything and stubbornly goes his own way, unconcerned about the problem that this calls for a solution. And just as many a worker does. So if he can only find a patron who is socially compatible with him, why would he then get involved with that “gang that seeks unrest”? Those who are in the know feel much for this song of freedom. In the organizations, one all too often encounters agitators instead of leaders and guides. Especially since the Socialists have gained so much influence in the organizations, they sometimes literally rage and rave against every patron or worker who does not come their way. One knows all too well how, at a proclaimed strike, they often cause damage to those who do not participate, so that even women and children are not left unharmed and personal violence against body and property has occurred. Of course, everything that is sacred to you rises up against this. On the other hand, it must also be recognized that it is often not feasible for many serious workers to participate in the meetings that are held for the benefit of the organization. One is often forced to listen to a language that one cannot or may not listen to. If one speaks out against this, one is met with mockery and scorn. And what is no less offensive, in such meetings decisions are often made that one’s conscience cannot but disapprove of, and in which one may not participate. This and much more is the reason why many patrons and many workers stubbornly refuse to participate in such an organization, and who can blame them for that? In England it was originally, and is still partly, quite different. Also in America, the organized circles generally have a more sober, more serious tone. On the other hand, in France, Belgium and partly also in Germany, Austria and Italy, the socialist agitation remained dominant. And precisely this has often caused the organization to miss its target.

§14. Self-Organization. The one-sided realization of the necessity of organization and the recognition, on the other hand, of the unusable nature of the organization that has arisen, has led elsewhere and with us to strive for a separate organization that would then have to be based on Christian principles. The Roman Catholic patrons and Roman Catholic workers went ahead elsewhere in this. The ecclesiastical bond that guided them and stood by their side made this possible for them; but in this self-organization they then also kept to themselves. They did want to admit a Protestant once in a while, as was done in Berlin with Von Kleist-Retzow, but they usually still encountered resistance. In Twente, as one remembers, cooperation between Protestant and Roman Catholic workers was already in full swing when the Roman Catholic clergy condemned and opposed it. One still maintains this cooperation to some extent, but it then became clear that regular cooperation in the economic and social field was excluded here. From the Roman Catholic side, too, there was a constant insistence on separate organization. On our side, too, this was done, and the Anti-Revolutionary Party has always tried to promote this. Patrimonium took the lead, and soon many other, more specialized associations, unions or groups followed.

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”
[518]

506 THE SOCIAL QUESTION

§15. Peculiar Difficulties. It cannot be denied, however, that here too peculiar difficulties arose. For among us it was not the local Church, nor the Synodical Assembly of the churches, that set the necessary things in order and indicated the boundaries, but it came down to individual membership. What then prevented another from not joining? Something that by itself undermined the generality of the organization. From an organization only the full power goes out, so that a potential unity force is born that embraces all those who want to join. On the other hand, if there are candidates who refuse to participate, a conflict arises between their interests and those of the organization; something that then, instead of leading to cooperation, so easily leads to bitterness and alienation. How self-evident the idea then also arises to set up, on our side, a separate organization under the Christian banner, alongside and partly in opposition to the socialist organization.

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[519]

PECULIAR DIFFICULTIES - THE CALLING OF THE GOVERNMENT

To place them, and to persuade your fellow-believers to join, is extremely difficult. Something that is added to this is that all organization costs money, which many are not willing to spend; as well as the fact that attending the meetings, sometimes far from home, steals time; a time when one would rather take a rest for one’s physical rest and for life in one’s family. So one encounters difficulties from all sides, and we are still far from any kind of fully organized organization. It certainly requires it, but far too slowly, and one must almost admire the perseverance with which the predecessors managed to hold out in this. Our thanks and our praise are due to them. We will always encourage this attempt to come to our own organization, and we would like nothing better than to see our Press plead for this endeavor in the broadest sense. Yet it is certain that in such a general organization as in a special Christian organization, as long as it must arise entirely voluntarily, it will never succeed in encompassing the totality of the workers. Hence the constant search for whether a regulation could not be devised that would include all under legal regulation. This brings us to the calling that comes to the Government in this regard.

§16. The Calling of the Government

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.

The reproach has not been spared us that we began in the sociological field with some hope in private initiative, but later deviated from this first position taken and surrendered to State Socialism. This representation is incorrect. For historically, government interference in the Guilds already lay from of old, which could not arise anywhere without the cooperation and recognition of the authority of State and Church. And as for our own first appearance in the sociological question, this took place in November 1874 in the Second Chamber, when we just gave the summary of our intention in the demand that, just as there was a Commercial Code, so also there should be worked towards obtaining a Labor Code. This was then mocked, and the then Minister of Justice did not hesitate to compare the idea of such a Labor Code to a little flower stem; that little flower stem to break with two fingers before the eyes of the Chamber; and further to add to this that it would not go otherwise with my efforts at legal regulation of labor. The outcome has not proven that Minister, but me, right. We already have Labor Chambers, a Safety Act, a Nuisance Act, and a Labor Act, the Accident Act, and much more; and the legal attempt to regulate insurance in the field of labor also falls under the same rubric. I myself introduced a very extensive Labor Act, in which the regulation of the apprenticeship system was also included; and it hardly needs to be remembered how Minister Talma was also active in the same direction. Undoubtedly I had initially expected more to be obtained from private initiative; but that the help of the Government was indispensable to create an orderly state of affairs in life that would provide an acceptable solution to the Sociological problem, has always been emphasized by me. I opposed the Cobden School from the outset with its senseless Individualism. Especially when it came to the organization of working life, it has always been and still is my conviction that free private initiative will never achieve the goal. There is not enough unity of insight among the workers as well as among the employers, and the attempts that have been made in the event of the lack of voluntary participation in the Sabotage and so much more have been used to compel the unwilling by harassment and violence, have aroused resentment and aversion, but no conviction. Even posting has not achieved its purpose. For never will all want to, and the position that one then takes towards the so-called “strikebreaker” does not gain, but rather alienates.

§17. Speech in the Second Chamber on November 22, 1910

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the

Especially in my speech in the Second Chamber on November 22, 1910, which is printed in the Supplement of De Standard No. 11877, I have tried to answer the question whether the organization of employers and workers, each for itself, and yet in mutual connection, should not be so regulated by the Government that all would be included without distinction, and thus the bitterness that increasingly separates the organized and the unorganized.

[521]

SPEECH IN THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES ON NOVEMBER 22, 1910. 509 I then discussed this problem, referring to the excellent work of Etienne Martin Saint-Léon, second edition titled: History of Trade Corporations (Paris, Felix Alcan, 1909) which was awarded the gold medal by the French Academy, and in the last chapter of which, under the title: The Present and the Future, a far-reaching proposal was made to list all employers, according to their respective trades, by government authority, and likewise to do the same with the workers in each trade, and then to have them elect heads from among those who, according to these lists, belonged to the same category, and to allow a central representation to emerge from the circles of these heads, to bring about cooperation between this central body of employers and the central body of workers, but in such a way that both met and deliberated entirely separately, and after this, to have the decisions required taken by their delegated representatives. I criticized this proposal from the School of De Mun on three main grounds. In the first place, it gave a complete organization. Secondly, it cut off all irritating quarrels with those who did not want to join the organization. And in the third place, it ultimately gave a decision to which both parties had contributed after complete independent deliberation.

[522]

510 THE SOCIAL QUESTION.

§18. The starting point. The starting point should lie in the apprenticeship system. The fault for which our Dutch workers in many trades are so far behind the English, German, Belgian and French is to be attributed to the lack of necessary training. This also applies in part to the employers; but whoever wants to act as an employer has the opportunity to acquire his knowledge abroad, just as many emerging industrialists owe their high level of education to visiting German schools. In Rotterdam, we finally got our own School of Commerce, and as already mentioned, my intention in amending the Higher Education Act was not only to elevate technical education, but also to bring many other universities into being. And when my attempt then met with too much opposition, we are now at least slowly making progress. Only with the Trade Schools does it still remain a struggle, and this for the reason that the Training School for the teachers who have to serve these Trade Schools has not materialized.

Analytical Summary: A Happy Turn.

§19. One whole. What I aimed at in this was one whole. Training of the workers through regulation of the apprenticeship system, elevation of the Trade Schools through deliberate training of the teachers for them, secondary and higher education for the various parts of working life, and culminating in the Technical University in Delft. The plan that lay in this presupposed, of course, that everyone would be certified for his trade and according to his profession. Just as a diploma is now required for the practice of medicine, the legal profession and so much more, so every manufacturer in his trade would have to possess a diploma, and have obtained this diploma after passing an examination. Such a diploma would also, with a good vocational organization, have to be the rich possession of every trade worker. Neither employers nor workers would be able to start a business just like that, but only if they were certified for their trade.

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.
[523]

THE STARTING POINT. - A UNIFIED WHOLE. - A WELL-ORDERED BODY. There would be no more letters patent; it would be a diploma that would grant the right to practice, whether as a master or as a worker, a particular profession or trade. All the strife between the organized and the unorganized would thereby come to an end. It would provide for each trade, for each city, and for the entire country, one all-encompassing organization for each vocation. In this way, local circles would naturally form themselves, which could appoint administrators and delegates. The delegates from both groups would bring the pending issues to a decision in the regulatory meetings, make this decision known to the Government, and the Government would then act accordingly.
