Of course, the Government would have to begin, as St. Léon indicates, by bringing into being such an organization by compiling complete lists of masters and workers for each trade, but through what I remarked about the apprenticeship system, the examinations, and the diplomas to be awarded, the compilation of these lists would present no difficulty. And even if there were still some loose groups of laborers without trade knowledge, the vast majority of men in business, whether in agriculture, industry, commerce, or shipping, would still come under a regulated organization and be able to assert their rights.

§20. A Well-Ordered Body. The whole must be a well-knit combination of the parts, and the government’s involvement would not aim to limit the freedom of the business, but on the contrary, to ensure its full development. Only the former hope of the individual would be transformed into a well-ordered body. Such an organization would also provide the so necessary opportunity for masters and workers to come together in their own direction. The Christian propaganda would not be disturbed by it, but rather promoted, inasmuch as under such an arrangement no one would be left out, but all men and all

Analytical Summary: The three types not equally characteristic. But this does not mean that, in the course of time, each of these three types has in turn dominated the national condition: the Roman Catholics in the Middle Ages, the Calvinists in the 16th century, and the French Revolutionaries after 1789. It does not at all follow from this that they are equal in national significance. Undoubtedly, the Seven Provinces, once living a common life with Flanders and other southern regions, have enjoyed a high and flourishing position and have gradually taken an increasingly important position in Northern Europe; but it must not be forgotten here that the national energy was at the time much more strongly developed in what is now called Belgium than in the northern provinces. Neither Amsterdam nor Rotterdam could in those days be placed on a par with Ghent and Bruges. The wealth of medieval life had more stamped its imprint on the national character in the South than in the North. The reference to the rich medieval type therefore has much less significance for the present Kingdom of the Netherlands than for the western part of Belgium. And in the second place, it must be added that at that time the unity of our national life was much less consolidated. Under the Burgundians, the Dutch provinces did not occupy an independent position, but were united with what lay outside our later borders; and under Charles V and Philip, we even came, though only in a personal way, into a Union with entirely different states from Central and Southern Europe—something which could not but be detrimental to the confirmation of our own national character. Whoever investigates the historical significance of our lower lands may well find the period we lived through in the Middle Ages highly important, but nevertheless hesitates for a moment to acknowledge without reservation that our glorious period dates from April 1, 1572. Not in the Middle Ages, but in the period of the Reformation, lies the high point of our national history. It cannot be emphasized enough how much scrupulous fairness is required in such a comparison, and we shall beware of the assertion that from the Calvinist side the correct toll of reverence has always been paid to the medieval period in our history, to which they undoubtedly had a right. But it stands to reason that from the Roman Catholic side, too, we have not always been done justice. It cannot be valued highly enough that especially in the second half of the 19th century a more impartial judgment gained influence. Just with a view to this more unanimous judgment, it may now at least be established that not only the foreign historians, one may well say without any exception of importance, honor the period after 1572 as the highest and most glorious, but that also within the country the best interpreters of each of the three types increasingly come to the unanimous conclusion that the world significance of our small country came out most richly in the heyday of Calvinism. This in no way denies that our national life in those days of our glory also showed dark sides. The resistance to the monarchical state form, and even against the House of Orange, has almost become a source of our unhappy discord until 1789, and it is impossible to say how much higher our country would have soared if immediately after the separation from Philip II the crown had been offered to Prince William. It had to come to this in 1813; but who calculates what national suffering would have been spared us if we had taken this important step two centuries earlier. But even if one plays open cards and takes into account all the suffering, oppression and restriction of rights that the temporarily dominant group has inflicted on the other two groups, it is historically established that we were never before 1572 what our national life was in the period between those two dates, and never again after 1789. To top it off, the fact that we offered a refuge to the politically and religiously persecuted from all countries is proof that the actual life here was a life in freedom, and that the publications and ordinances that claimed to infringe on this freedom had more the effect of satisfying formal demands than of inflicting suffering on those who deviated.
[524]

THE SOCIAL QUESTION. The law should naturally regulate the relationship of these distinct groups, but in such a way that each group would meet separately and discuss its own affairs, and that only the delegates would come into contact with one another. This need not pose any difficulty. As far as this has already been intended and sought, it is easy to avoid any unpleasantness between these delegates. And so the end result would be that no one would be left out, and that all interested parties would be included in the organization and find their place, that internal strife would be excluded, and that it would not just be a matter of discussion at the meetings, but that all action would be directed towards the goal, and that the Government, thus remaining fully informed of the social movement, would equally assist employers and workers in promoting their interests and respecting their rights. Until we have succeeded in elevating our social life to this higher sphere, it remains in the meantime the duty and calling to strive for this as much as possible in the private domain, and as well as we can, to promote the trade unions, and as far as we are concerned, the Christian trade unions. Not—this always takes precedence—as if our quiet trust and our rest in God should ever be transferred to the trade unions. Let this, as Jesus called it, be left to the Gentiles. For those of us who kneel with the Lord’s Prayer on our lips, the trust in the future does not lie in what the trade unions will bring us, but solely in the promise of our God. And therefore never be anxious about what you will eat or what you will drink, or with what you will clothe yourselves, for indeed your Father knows that you need all these things. But just as your prayer: “Give us this day our daily bread,” does not mean that you now, like Israel the manna in the wilderness, should passively wait for it, but rather that it should spur you on to more diligently acquire the indispensable money for the purchase of your bread, so it is here. The sick man on his sickbed begs his God for healing, but precisely in connection with this takes the medicine that his doctor has prescribed for him. And so it must be here as well. In nothing shall you as an employer or worker neglect your right or the interest of your family. As far as the organization can serve this purpose, you may also not neglect it. Only, it may never become the object of your trust.

[525]

UNEMPLOYMENT. 513 Otherwise, it is but a means in God’s hand, with which not you yourself, but He as your Father in heaven and the ages will bless you.

§21. Unemployment.

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over

The most dreadful evil in the realm of labor is unemployment; can the Government also be held responsible for this wicked evil? In part, undoubtedly. An overly driven system of free trade and free movement can deprive work from those who would otherwise have it in abundance. Free trade can result in countless goods being “made” across the borders, so that there is no more labor to be performed here in the country. One sees this most simply in the case of sawed timber. If the unsawed beams come to us, then the wages for sawing can be earned here. If, on the other hand, the sawed timber is supplied to us, then this wage for our workers is gone. The imports from Germany, France, and England are not offset by our exports to those same countries, at least not in terms of manual labor. Especially for a country that is poor in raw materials, it naturally lends itself to greatly increased imports. It is also beyond doubt that our provision of our needs for food, drink, clothing, household goods, technical aids, etc. comes to a much larger extent from abroad than from our own soil or our own factories. Whoever would go through shop after shop in the Kalverstraat in Amsterdam or the Hoogstraat in Rotterdam and examine which goods and wares are of domestic origin and which are of foreign provenance, would come to a painfully conclusive result for our own national labor. The free traffic works in the same direction. From abroad, many workers come into our country, and also office personnel who are better trained and more capable, and thus take positions that would otherwise be filled by our own countrymen. Above all, the ease with which our cultured public speaks foreign languages contributes to this. Indeed, without exaggeration, it can be said that, in this respect, the situation is now rather the reverse of what it used to be. Formerly, the fact that something was of Dutch manufacture increased its price in London and Cologne; now our fading nationality tends to make many young ladies, and also many gentlemen, prefer anything that comes from Paris or London, and to have an almost unfavorable opinion of anything that is of domestic manufacture.

[526]

514 THE SOCIAL QUESTION. Fortunately, there are other goods that are sought out by us abroad; for example, our cigars and our cocoa, but the fact remains that our free trade and our free movement deprive our own people of a great deal of work, so much so that unemployment forces many to go and seek work across the borders. Thus, before the war broke out, there were certainly 100,000 Dutch workers who, because they remained unemployed here, went to seek and find work in Germany.

§22. Overpopulation. An entirely different cause of the increasing unemployment lies in overpopulation. This is explained by the long peace, by the better hygienic care that lengthens the duration of life, by the decrease in mortality of newborns in their first year of life, by early marriages, by improved housing, and by increasing prosperity. Although the law that Malthus established may be subject to some deductions, it cannot be completely denied that the production of children naturally increases in proportion to the possibility of existence. Especially in a small, overpopulated country like ours, this must lead to the utmost poverty, even to a lack that undermines the honor of the country. Such a constantly growing overpopulation can now only be curbed in two ways: either by the immoral means that are used to hinder birth, or by emigration. Emigration, alas, we hardly know. Our colonies in Asia attract only the government personnel and the trade, even though a settlement of people from the Netherlands could very well thrive in mountainous regions. In Suriname, the growth of the European part of the population would even be highly desirable, but our West does not attract. Our only emigration remained, as a result, to Belgium with 75,000, to Germany with 120,000, and to the United States of North America with a fluctuating number. Comparing these figures with the increase in population, which grew from just over 2¼ million to just over 5¾ million from 1830 to 1909, and increased by almost half in the much shorter period from 1880 to 1909, and since the last

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the
[527]

OVERPOPULATION - HOW TO COMBAT UNEMPLOYMENT.

The census of 1909 already showed an increase from 5¾ to 6 million, which indicates that emigration, which is running at a very low rate, is by no means capable of remedying the evil of overpopulation. The unemployment thus created now generally weighs upon the weaker and less capable part of the population, may at times press upon a particular class of workers, e.g., in times of severe cold, and can, as was recently evident, even assume a very serious character in times of war. It is well known how for a time almost all building activities were brought to a standstill.

§23. How to combat unemployment. Against this unemployment, collective labor contracts can now be resorted to, so that the employing firm contracts not with the individual worker, but with an entire group of workers. Then, however, the group covers the individual. Against this unemployment, help can be sought in a kind of insurance. Help can be offered by Philanthropy. From the Government’s side, various measures can be expected to accelerate the provision of work. And if, despite all this, there still remains a temporary, strongly pronounced state of distress, the municipal authorities can temporarily step in. But with too strong an increase in overpopulation, all this in the end is of no avail, and there will always remain a layer of lower-standing, physically and psychologically less mature workers who cannot save themselves.

Analytical Summary: The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,
[528]

516 THE SOCIAL QUESTION.

Now, if we consider the still uninhabited part of the globe in Asia, America, Africa and Australia, and apply what the Lord God, according to Genesis 1:28, intended when He laid the burden upon us as the human race: “Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth”; how this burden was repeated to Noah in Gen. IX:1, when it was said again quite literally: “Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth”; and how, at the confusion of the Tower of Babel, according to Gen. XI:4 ff., it again came out as the will of the Lord that our race should not concentrate in a few places on the earth, but as much as possible spread itself over the whole earth; then the selfishness that makes us, the Dutch, want to stay put, so to speak, appears in a very unfavorable light, and it becomes clear that what the English and German nations did, by spreading themselves over the whole earth, answered much better to the will of the Lord. Hence also the fact that unemployment here at home threatens to become an ever more serious plague, which, leaving aside the temporary and exceptional cases, can never be fully remedied by the provision of municipal support as a general phenomenon in the life of the people. The law goes on to say that even among the workers there is a difference, that they are of one, three, and five talents, and that under the general unemployment it is almost exclusively the first who fall, whose resilience, willpower and perseverance have sunk the lowest.

§24. The wage. The abolition of slavery, which had become an abomination, and also the abolition of serfdom and similar forms of attachment of those who lived and worked on the land to the owner of the land, has raised the lower classes of the people psychologically in an incredible way, but physically has subjected them to an equally incredible risk. Psychologically, in their inner being, he who was once a slave has become a human being through this abolition. When I had the opportunity in the Chesapeake Bay near Baltimore to get to know those who had been born as slaves and had lived as such for a time, and had since become free, the newly emerged humanity in these happy ones struck me. This humanity did not yet fit into their new way of life, it made rather the impression of something foreign that had been implanted or ingrafted in them, but it was still so glorious. Only, with this liberation came a care that was not known before. Formerly the “master” cared for and provided for all needs, now the task rests on themselves. And this now, this daily existence from hand to mouth, this lack of any firm ground under their feet, this possibly being helpless on the street tomorrow, this is the human misery that must find its redress in the wage.

Analytical Summary: Our People.
[529]

WAGES - MITIGATION.

Wages, when paid out, give a certain stability to life and create a position, at least of a certain duration. As long as a salary or stipend is running, one at least knows what one is tied to. And if any concern remains, it is only the question of how to ensure the durability of one’s fixed position; and especially how to continue one’s existence when old age or illness puts an end to the enjoyment of this position. Wages, on the other hand, are a compensation ad hoc for a specific, executed service, sometimes per day, even for less than a day; often for a week. So one is always sitting on a stool, and at any moment the situation can arise where one stands helpless in the world with wife and children, and even has to leave one’s dwelling, to stand on the street.

§25. Mitigation.

Analytical Summary: Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit

Now the custom has certainly brought some mitigation here. A maid has her six weeks, others are hired on a term basis, and in factories and workshops, the wage relationship is no longer severed from the day; but the possibility still arises at any moment that one stands on the street. Many are without any property, their clothing and bed excepted. The savings bank first brought a glimpse of something better. But it always comes down to the wages again, always with the double question of whether it is sufficient to live on, and whether it is secure. In Jesus’ days, among the Jews, serfdom had already largely receded, and working for wages had come into vogue. Just look at the parable of the servants who were hired at different hours of the day, and to whom the hirer still paid the same wage. Jesus’ admonition against saying, “What shall we eat or what shall we drink” also points to the wage standard. With a slave, this could not be. He was assured of his food. But it was precisely the wage that brought the uncertainty of itself. This uncertainty expresses itself in the cry: What shall we eat, what shall we drink, or with what shall we clothe ourselves? And against this longing for a more secure life, Jesus now placed not a wage theory, but merely the trust of the believer in the care of his God. “Your Father who is in heaven knows that you need these things!” For the inner life of the soul, this still continues,

[530]

518 THE SOCIAL QUESTION.

and the worker for whom, through all kinds of wage theories, this trust in his God is lost, or even shaken. But apart from this, it is self-evident that we must also give an account of the wage theory. In everything to which we are called to act or to undergo treatment, clear awareness and clear insight are required, and therefore the wage question must also be clearly thought through from the Christian side.

§26. The Ideal to be Pursued.

Analytical Summary: Guided by Orange. To this has been added: “guided by Orange”; an addition that must not be omitted. For it cannot be denied that in the characterized Calvinism, as it also penetrated our country from Geneva and France at that time, there was a danger not to be underestimated, the aftereffects of which are still discernible. In the last chapter of the First Part of this work, I have extensively explained how from the outset there was a decisive element in Calvinism that could only develop and mature later, and that, politically speaking, only came to full effect in the United States; but it was also then added that in Geneva (one need only think of the burning of Servetus) an intolerant dogmatism still prevailed, which, though indispensable for the church, could only result in schism and oppression in its political consequences.

The ideal that imposes itself on us here is, of course, sharply outlined. That ideal demands a wage that is capable of providing for us and ours in all the strictly necessary necessities of life; to give this provision on a fixed footing; to include in this provision what we need, even if we can no longer work, whether through illness, accident or old age; in addition to work, to also attain the fulfillment of those entirely different needs that manifest themselves in the spiritual realm in reading, church attendance and recreation; and moreover, to have something left over for higher purposes, so that the laying of an offering on the altar or the giving of alms does not become an absolute impossibility. If we were in a situation that met this ideal, then a healthy and skilled workman, in the vagueness of his life, would have to be able to count on a wage from which all this was attainable. Here, however, gradations in life immediately present themselves. The workers themselves distinguish between skilled laborers and ordinary workers, and even among the ordinary workers one constantly encounters the distinction between trained craftsmen, who stand a little below the skilled laborers, but still occupy a higher and better position than the common laborers. Especially in America, these specifications have been driven through sharply, but in connection with this, no trouble has been spared to calculate as precisely as possible how much the worker in each of these three categories must receive per week, in order to see his performance fully converted into wages.

[531]

THE IDEAL TO BE PURSUED - COOPERATION INDISPENSABLE

It is necessary to calculate the proper wage and the corresponding standard of living required. As far as can be determined from these endless data, it can be said that for a married man the minimum wage should be around 12 guilders, for the second class around 16 or 17 guilders, and for skilled laborers it should fluctuate between 18 and 25 guilders. Country life naturally sets lower requirements than life in the big cities, especially when it comes to housing costs. Certain variations must therefore be allowed; but in order to provide for the necessities of life in a fair manner, the wage standard should be approximately as stated. However, such a standard does not remain stationary. If heavier taxes come, if competition weighs more heavily, if many prices of necessities of life rise, then 12 guilders will soon no longer do what 10 guilders did before. One must always be mindful of variations in the standard of living to be determined. Not too high, but to some extent one may also count on the earnings of the wives and children over 16, about which more later; but the main income must still always come from the head of the household, and therefore we must not desist from the demand that the wage, on a normal footing, must be sufficient to make provision for the ordinary human necessities of life, calculated in simple frugality. Such a wage standard cannot be forced. This always revenges itself, but the government can take the lead here, noble landowners and great industrialists can set a good example, and whoever sets out to elucidate the social problems must tirelessly and unflaggingly keep pointing to these minimum ideals, etc.

§27. Cooperation Indispensable. However, success is absolutely impossible if the workers do not cooperate from their side to achieve this goal. For this, accurate knowledge of the existing conditions is indispensable. Data must be provided even from the back alleys of the country. The man of social study must know how things stand in the country, and it is precisely this knowledge that is so largely lacking. Whoever investigates such issues usually knows much more precisely how things are in America than in our own country. In addition, we must always gain more accurate knowledge of the earnings of the employers and of capital. Naturally, the employer must make an enticing profit for his enterprise, for his daring, for his knowledge and versatile care, which will always encourage him again. Any attempt to put the employer on the same line as the worker is sheer absurdity. The existence and life task of both are completely different, and these two factors must therefore be judged by their own measure. But however decisively this must be stated, there must still be a certain just proportion between business profit and wages, and the various ways in which this has been tried to be realized have certainly not been disappointing. However, all prospect falls away if the necessary cooperation is lacking on the part of the workers. Nothing has worked so destructively in this respect as the spirit of bitterness and distrust, sometimes even to the point of rudeness and rebellion, in which the worker often sought his strength. The laziness and neglect of work when the eye of the employer is not watching. Deceiving him in all sorts of ways, and actually not wanting to cooperate, has held back the rise of the wage standard and will soon cause it to fall again, even if it has risen. The employer must realize that “his people” are doing well, are making a good impression outwardly and recommend his firm; but conversely, “his people” must rejoice in the flourishing of his business and boast of how alert, how ready, and how sought after on the market their boss is, because they know that their own well-being also depends on his prosperity.

Analytical Summary: The wrong done to us. Our party has set up a price in 1878, in its own starting point, to point to this happy influence of the Prince of Orange. Let no confusion creep in here. In matters of ecclesiastical life, Calvin’s insight has also been his own. His dispute with Dathenus does not touch the very life of the church, but the influence of the Church on the life of the people in the political domain. As a theologian, especially Johannes a Lasco deserves appreciation on this point, but on a broader scale it has above all been the influence of the Prince that has safely guided us. Meanwhile, one should not lose sight of the fact that this pointing with preference to the Prince is foreign to the intention, to praise anything that has later emerged from the House of Orange on this delicate terrain of the Princes. Prince Maurice aligned himself much more closely with the Calvinists. The then-emerging conflict with Arminianism necessitated this. But his life history would stand in a more glorious light for us if Oldenbarnevelt had died a natural death. Under Prince Frederick
[532]

520 THE SOCIAL QUESTION

§28. A Living Wage. Of course, it would be precious if a living wage could be assured to every worker’s family, as they call it in America, but do not forget that John A. Ryan and Richard T. Ely in their work published in New York in 1906, A Living Wage, on page 145 set the minimum income for a worker’s family of 5 persons at an annual total of over 600 dollars, i.e. 1500 guilders.

Analytical Summary: Enduring Bond. No Calvinist, in any country, has ever thought of whitewashing any injustice for the benefit of a princely ruler. But apart from what criticism may later have become necessary, we still always honor in the appearance of Prince William the Silent a gift of God to our people in their then so distressed condition; we thank God for what the time has bestowed on our fatherland in the generation of the Prince; we now still refresh ourselves in the national glory that has emanated from this House of Princes over our people as a nation; and we now still take sides, not on ecclesiastical, but on political terrain, for what the Silent One, the first among all princes in Europe, as the consequence of the Reformation, brought us in the bestowal of full spiritual freedom. Precisely for this reason, protest is constantly lodged by us against any attempt to discredit Orange as an ornament, as Mr. S. van Houten has dared to do. Not, we hasten to add here, as if this Statesman wanted to detract from the glory of our House of Orange. What he spoke of as an “ornament” was only the formulation in a single word of his constitutional theory. But here lies the difference, that such a declining expression of “ornament” was more dear to him, while it strikes us as offensive to our love for Orange. Especially after the shameful treatment that the House of Orange has suffered through its expulsion and forced flight to England in 1797, and after the more than shameful threat with which in 1848 in the liberal press one even dared to speak of the scaffold to unsettle Orange itself, it betrayed a stiff lack of tenderness and self-control in a man who ultimately became an advisor to the Crown, to hear him speak of “ornament.” Not daring to criticize a Prince openly is a cringing cowardice that Orange demands less than it requires, and which is unthinkable especially in the Calvinist. But this does not detract from the firm demand that we remain thankful for honoring what our God has bestowed on us in Orange through his great Princes. A glory in the past, the happy echo of which was to be heard in our revered Queen, as the last scion of the ancient House of Orange.
[533]

A LIVING WAGE - STRIKES AND LOCKOUTS

As we explained on page 140, raising the total by $87. Although we understand very well that one would consider oneself fortunate if such an income could be assured permanently, yet everyone feels that in this way one falls into absurdities. Here too, it is not difficult to see through it. Raising this living wage almost immediately results in higher prices, and then the profit is automatically reduced by itself. And if one now says: Then it must lead to socialist equalization, then one does not get a step further. With forced equality, the incentives of production become blunted, and one is even worse off after a short period of apparent happiness. What stands in the way of any good arrangement is the lack of mutual trust. In America, this is much more clearly seen on both sides. In some major businesses, they have already progressed so happily that it became an open book-keeping between employers and workers, that they played completely open cards with each other, and precisely thereby knew how to bring the whole matter, and in that matter the interests of the employer as well as of his workers, to the highest bloom. What is still so much lacking with us is a sufficiently clear insight into the state of the question; from which it follows of itself that one does not understand the art of making the interests of both parties interlock. Employer and worker belong together. They must support and defend each other’s interests in turn. Upright justice must be considered from both sides. And the fear of God must be the power that banishes distrust and ignoble treatment.

§29. Strikes and lockouts.

Analytical Summary: According to the needs of our time.

If this mutual open-hearted trust had governed the situation, the very idea of the indispensable strike that now surrounds us would never have arisen, or at least never have been put into practice. As it now stands, it was not to be avoided. Under the changed conditions of the labor process, abuses often arise on the part of the employers. The English School of Stuart Mill had bewildered the minds. It all came down to a struggle of individuals. Whoever held out the longest won the game. The fittest was the victor, and the less well-equipped succumbed to his fate and perished. This has then led to the disappearance of all mutual trust, and the manufacturers, as the stronger ones, have drawn the workers into the struggle for survival, and according to the rule that the survival of the fittest governs the entire economic situation, have exploited the domestic industry, the woman, the children and finally also the male factory workers in an unpardonable way. Many of them felt this themselves, but it seemed to them that there was no escape from this economic law. Everyone did so, and so did they too. A mighty reaction has then naturally arisen against this. Eminent men who stood outside it took up the cause of the exploited working class. Despite all powerlessness, this one thing always remained, that the employer or manufacturer needed the workers. If one had set out to threaten the mighty capitalist with the loss of his workforce by limiting his needs, by serious saving and by close cohesion, there would have been hope. This, then, was the strike, and already at the Social Congress in 1891 it was clearly stated on our side that the strike is not to be rejected as unlawful. Not that the strike could become the panacea for all evils. Rather, the workers have also increasingly come to see that the strike is by no means the always valid, generally applicable means of relief. It also requires a spirit of cooperation among the workers, which only rarely works through. If this does not succeed, then the one who does not readily join in gives the impression of a “strikebreaker.” In this strikebreaker there is then nothing but betrayal of the sacred cause, which has not infrequently led to a vehmgericht and to murder.

§30. Counter-action of the employers.

Analytical Summary: The Church and the State in Mutual Relation. One immediately feels this in the relationship between Church and State. In the 16th century, the struggle that the Reformation undertook against Rome’s overpowering authority naturally brought the ecclesiastical question to the fore in all political domains. The ecclesiastical question dominated the entire political sphere, both domestically and with a view to foreign relations, and here the foreign relations mostly stood...

And in addition to this, the clearer the insight into the situation on both sides becomes, the more the “lockout” on the part of the employers can thwart any strike almost completely. The strike requires a free, strong development of moral power. One already has not too much of it, and now one would have to set aside from the already meager wages for a long time in order to make the strike possible. In the strike weeks themselves, the family lives in great distress, and the strike leader, who has to keep the troops together, is often in a very difficult position. The lockout, on the other hand, does not require this moral effort, and the employer can simply close his factory and wait until the workers, driven by hunger, give in. This is why the lockout has become a much more effective weapon in the hands of the employers than the strike in the hands of the workers.

[534]

Page 535—-

COUNTER-MEASURES BY EMPLOYERS.523 One must dare to suffer even the half-deficiency of many fewer. Credit must be obtained from the baker or grocer, and this still weighs heavily weeks after the strike has ended. Even if the strike later leads to an accord, often those who led the strike are nevertheless excluded from this accord. Now all this still goes on, if it is a strike on a small territory and at most over all the workers in the same place. But this too was soon found to be impossible. For the great employers from all over the country, sometimes even across borders, soon realized that they possessed the power to call a counter-factory strike into being, as succeeded with the great textile strike. If now everything was strained to gather a capital that could enable a strike of say three months by 2 or 3,000 workers in one place, all this became child’s play as soon as all the manufacturers across the whole country put together some 50 to 60,000 workers. With diamond workers, and a few singular professional organizations, this danger may be less to be feared, but for all major industries it can immediately set in. The Trusts have in various ways brought the great manufacturers into closer cooperation in their fierce competition, and if this spirit of cooperation becomes master over the whole field, then in the end all labor strikes would be doomed to miss their goal. The outcome also shows that labor strikes have gradually become subject to much sharper criticism. The more far-sighted among the socialists themselves are in many ways backing off, naturally to reserve them for the great political strike; but of this there can be no talk among us. Political strike is, after all, to transfer an economic measure to a terrain where it was forbidden. For the economic strike, on the other hand, we continue to plead, though we add: Be more careful with it! Recklessness does not improve your condition, but rather threatens to greatly worsen it; and if there is anything that speaks for the strike, it is well that it has often actually improved the condition of the workers in many respects on the open field. Only one must not forget here either that improvement of the workers’ condition on a broad scale has all too often also driven up the price of goods, and thus nullifies the just-made profit.

[536]

524 THE SOCIAL QUESTION.

§31. Boycotts and Sabotage. Boycott was the name of a director in Ireland who had made himself hated in wide circles by his heartlessness towards his workers. His full name was Charles Livingsham Boycott. Lord Erne, a powerful landowner, had appointed him as director over his estates, and it was in this capacity that Mr. Boycott literally squeezed the working population. This aroused such aversion that the Land League in 1879 decided to pronounce the ban over him; which then meant that all the workers, and even all the servants, ran away from him, that no shopkeeper would sell him his goods anymore, and that even the railway companies no longer wanted to transport his cattle. For a full year Boycott then struggled on. But in the end he had to give up. And in 1881 he left the country a pauper. This same system has then been applied in England, and once in America and elsewhere, to similar tyranny, and not infrequently this measure also bore excellent fruit. Yet boycotting will never become a generally applicable means. For, in the first place, the times are past when the exploitation of the workers assumed such a blatant and outrageous character. Secondly, “the workers alone” cannot achieve their goal here; the shopkeepers must be drawn into it as well; which does not come about so easily. And in the third place, one must be faced with a truly outrageous case, otherwise public opinion will not support the Boycott.

Analytical Summary: The People’s Representation. It is no different with the People’s Representation. Under the republican form of government, our Commonwealth has not known representation as we understand it today. There were estates, but no People’s Representation; the cities and manorial landowners held the power; the deputies were in no way representatives of the nation before the Government, but rather the installers and maintainers of the Government itself. There was certainly popular choice in the election of the power-holders, but the right to participate in that choice was extremely limited. As shown in the last chapter of the First Part, Calvin did not praise such a system, and ultimately he himself went over to advising in a democratic sense. Whoever now tries to apply the completely antiquated representation ideas of the 16th century to the present state of affairs would thereby show themselves to be anything but faithful to the pure principle, but rather to blatantly disown it. To now still advocate a State form that does not fully do justice to the representative principle, would brand one as backward and out of one’s time. The question of a proper People’s Representation has now itself become the main question for the future development of the State life. And the Calvinist direction, which has always proceeded from the people, and even found its strength in the “common folk,” would lose its inner driving force and let itself be written off, if it did not take the lead rather than follow on this point of our constitutional development in the spirit of freedom and love of the people. To be a Christian Democrat remains the title of honor for every Calvinist. §32. Navy and Army. As a third example, let the National Defense be pointed out. In the days of the Reformation, there was hardly any mention of a national army, as we know it today. The Condottieri had indeed lost ground, but the army with which one went to war still consisted mostly of volunteers offering themselves, and even of mercenary troops from foreign countries, just as we still used to employ, and partly still do, not a few Swiss, Germans and Belgians for our East Indian army. Only of the army of Gustavus Vasa and of the army of Cromwell can it be said that they were driven and urged by a higher and holier interest. For the rest, however, the armies were mostly composed of rough men, and the holy enthusiasm for the cause of the country was sometimes all too little to be noticed. That the same regiment, after the lapse of ten years, drew the sword against a higher national interest, which it had first helped to defend at the price of its blood, occurred more than once. All the more the custom came into vogue with us to assemble the power for the defense of the country’s interests from hired manpower, inasmuch as the navy with us, more than the army, stood in importance, and the manning of the navy, both here and still in England, was constantly recruited by free choice. The civic guard had a more national character, and knew no wages as a motive; but the militia, as it is now in force, first appeared under Napoleon’s domination, and has since also been maintained in our Kingdom. Meanwhile, our people have been far from eager for the new military idea. To become a soldier was not considered an honor, but a plague, and the system of substitutes soon came up to free oneself from this plague by money. It has therefore lasted until the very end of the 19th century before we really got a national army, and the idea of universal military service, which now imposes itself of its own accord, is still not realized with us. Now, because in the glorious days of our fathers the condition prevailed in navy and army that excluded a national army, shall one identify the system then in force with Calvinism, and therefore, as ultra-conservative, set it against the military force that is now so absolutely indispensable? Certainly not. The principle of Calvinism and this is, to highly esteem the significance of the national life; and the future of the national life is now inexorably, at least for small nations, in danger, if not the whole people finally girds the sword as one man for the country’s independence.

§32. Sabotage. Akin to the Boycott is the so-called Sabotage; derived from sabot, i.e. clog. Here too the vindictiveness of the embittered mind speaks. Where the Boycott wanted to make the inhuman employer socially impossible, Sabotage goes one step further, and does not shrink from attacking his goods, his possessions, his house, his carriage, his furniture, and even, if necessary, his child, yes, even physically assaulting him. Especially employers who did not hesitate to seduce young girls in their service were the victims of such.

Analytical Summary: Money Gave the Decisive Vote. So it remained until Thorbecke in 1848 cleared away everything that still had a medieval aftertaste, and made money the sole fulcrum of the representative system. Article 76 of the Constitution promulgated that year tied the suffrage for the Second Chamber, and likewise Article 123 the suffrage for the Provincial Estates, “to the payment of a sum in direct taxes, corresponding to the local situation,” ranging from ƒ20 to ƒ160. The Electoral Law provided for the proper implementation of these provisions, and did so in such an indicative anti-democratic direction that the census of ƒ24 in many Frisian municipalities was calculated to favor only a few Liberals. A proposal from the Anti-Revolutionary side, made to reduce this amount, also met with the most vehement opposition from the Liberal side in the Second Chamber, even as late as 1875. Especially in Sneek this gave the most outrageous injustice, just as after 1887, in the new edition of what is now Article 80, in a way that allowed no contradiction, it appeared from the figures on the Liberal and Anti-Revolutionary candidates.
[537]

BOYCOTTING AND SABOTAGE - SABOTAGE - TRADE UNION ORGANIZATION

Sabotage is not infrequently the deserved object. One knows the story of such a scoundrel, whom the people in an uprising had castrated and then carried in triumph through the village. But even if sabotage does not always take on such a violent and shameful form, it remains the means by which the discontented masses make the life of an obstructive employer unbearable at his place, and force him either to do penance or to disappear. The intention is then to instill fear in other employers through this, and thereby achieve economic improvement. It need hardly be said that all such violent means are to be condemned from a Christian standpoint, although it must be added that employers without honor, who provoke such acts of violence, by their heartlessness certainly sink deeper into guilt than the people whom they have so mercilessly embittered.

§33. The Trade Union Organization

Analytical Summary: The Social Question. With a brief mention of the social question, let us refer to these examples and conclude. In the 16th century, there was no social question. The Peasants’ Revolt in Germany seemed to point in that direction, but it arose from a completely different motive. What called the social question into a very peculiar form of life in the 19th century was twofold. Firstly, the fact that the increased technology made it possible for factories to arise, in which hundreds of workers were set to work simultaneously; something which naturally caused the close and cordial relationship between employer and worker to be entirely lost. And secondly, the more mature development of the working class, thanks to the high flight that popular education also took in connection with vocational education. This higher position of the worker demanded more abundant satisfaction of the needs of social community life, and the heaping up of hundreds of workers outside of all higher and more intimate connection with the employer, brought into existence conditions that our fathers had never known. Not that our fathers had no care for the workers. The Guild system certainly promoted an excellent understanding to no small degree. The needs of the working class spoke less loudly; but the needs, as far as they spoke, were then as a rule well and not too narrowly satisfied. What only characterized the then existing condition in a less desirable way, was the excessive dependence in which the worker found himself on his employer. Slavery had come to an end, but the conviction had not yet gained entrance that the worker, as a free man, stood not under, but alongside his employer, in civil rights and in social life. But if one now asks whether this then existing condition could be logically deduced from the principle we profess, in the sense that the introduction of an entirely changed condition would be a denial of that principle, then it must be answered with absolute decisiveness, that a denial of principle can only come into question with him who, where such a totally different condition has entered, refuses to reveal the power of his principle also in the solution of the questions now presenting themselves.

The right way to lasting improvement of fate remains, after all, the trade union organization. That it can also decide to strike, we do not deny; but as soon as the strike emanates from the trade union organization, it stands under more reliable leadership. Precisely because it does not aim at general improvement, but occupies itself solely with a particular trade, the trade union organization has a number of men who enjoy great confidence among their trade colleagues, who are well informed about the state of affairs in the trade, and who possess sufficient knowledge of the state of the trade throughout the country, and even beyond our borders, to judge what can be dared and what should be discouraged. Moreover, the trade union organization has this great advantage, that it enhances the competence and thus the economic value of the personnel, and on that basis may make higher demands. It develops the workers, trains them, and thereby gives them a higher significance in social life. Of course, not all its members are skilled laborers, but the guiding thought in the trade union organization is then to pursue this as a goal. Naturally, the trade union organization is less political in nature. Not what comes up at the meetings or comes to the fore in the press occupies it. For the most part, it confines itself to what concerns the one trade for which it acts. The more this trade union organization understands its vocation, the more at home it will be in everything that pertains to the trade.

[538]

526 THE SOCIAL QUESTION

And to express it in monetary terms, whoever has been a member of his trade union organization for five years and has not neglected any effort to better understand his trade and become more at home in it, will in the years to come, as far as his wages are concerned, easily climb three to five guilders per week. Not that we would not highly appreciate workers’ associations of a more general nature, like Patrimonium. The trade unions divide, and that is why there is a lasting need for a more general organization that unites. And even if a good trade union organization must also possess a Central, under which the provincial and local organizations unite, this remains a special task after all. Hence the necessity to nevertheless make the solidarity and unity of the entire working class stand out again in a general bond. A trade union organization can also inspire, but it aims at a more domestic goal, and therefore, for the high inspiration and enthusiasm that must always set the tone, especially among the Christian workers, lies not in the trade union organization, but much rather in a bond like Patrimonium as the cherishing hearth. That is why both must be appreciated.

§34. The So-Called “Posting”

Analytical Summary: Keeping Pace with Our Time.

If all workers, without exception, had joined the organization of their trade, as they ought, there would be no talk of posting. Posting is intended solely to dissuade workers who, because they do not belong to the trade union organization, would still want to continue working after a strike has been called, to implore them not to serve the cause of the employer against the cause of the workers in this way, and to make them well understand that if they lend themselves to this, their action will amount to betrayal of the workers’ cause. Such betrayal is of course nothing other than the result of the lack of an all-encompassing organization. As long as...

[539]

The So-Called “Pickets.” Now that this lack exists, there will always be unorganized strikers who say: “I don’t care about your strike. I’m not part of it. I’ll keep working.” This then makes a fatal impression on the organized strikers. They feel that whoever keeps working is ruining their cause. He becomes their enemy, and while he should have been their support, a traitor to their cause. This cannot be otherwise. This is human nature. That one now wants to make one last attempt to prevent such betrayal through “pickets” is understandable. But the end does not justify the means here either. And also, such a means can, when properly applied, be praiseworthy, but when wrongly put into practice, can also be very certainly condemned. Hence many have already stigmatized such pickets as a pestilence. One knows how far they went here. Two men were placed at a man’s door, who, when he wanted to go to his work, threatened him and followed him, sometimes even warning him that if he went, his wife and children would not be safe. Horrors that cannot be condemned sharply enough. The better way has already been taken, where all such pickets at home were cut off as unworthy, and one only wanted to know of a picket at the factory gate. This is of course something quite different. And if one succeeds in sending out two or three of the highest-ranking workers from the management of the Trade Union to plead with the men who still want to go in, not to do so, then the picketing already takes on a much milder character, and we could go along with it. Yet it would also, in our view, be preferable if the Trade Union, through the picket, sent a written request to the address of those who were still willing to work, in which the entire strike was explained and defended. If necessary, this document could later be handed over again at the factory gate. But be careful and remain with this “picketing.” For time and again a hidden evil intent lurks behind a friendly appearance. One wants to frighten the unwilling and instill fear in him, and this is precisely an unholy act. Warn, convince, win over to your cause, and stay on the right track. But above all, promote the trade union, and even more, steer it in the direction we have outlined in the spirit of Martin St. Léon. Draw everyone from the government side into your organization, and the picketing will disappear by itself.

[540]

528 THE SOCIAL QUESTION.

§35. The Duration of Labor. Duration of labor and wages are two sides of the same matter, which are also directly connected with each other. If one considers, however strictly incorrect, the labor performed in service as the sale of strength, it follows from this that both the effort shown in the labor and the duration of that effort determine the value of what has been delivered. Suppose the effort in labor is set at one to three energies, as a, b, or c, depending on whether the worker dawdles, works through, or surpasses himself, then this, calculated according to the duration of time, will give a result of time multiplied by energy, and the wage will have to be regulated according to the proportion of the two moments, energy and time. Suppose the price of labor, according to the energy, is 25, 50 or 75 cents, then you only have to multiply these three moments by the duration of time of 1–12 hours to determine the price of the delivered product per day, and thus indicate the amount of the wage. But it also follows from this that the worker must relate the increase in his wage and even the normal calculation of his wage both to the energy he has shown and to the time spent. A first-class worker who worked with three energies could, in eight hours’ time, produce more labor than a slower worker in twelve. In pressing for a reduction in the duration of labor, the rule must therefore always be that the worker must also produce more. This now depends on a proper apprenticeship system, a good trade union, effective hygiene, good housing, sufficient food, etc., but also on moral seriousness, a sense of duty, and the overcoming of our innate laziness. But if one succeeds once in increasing the value of one hour’s labor by, say, 10%, it can be deduced from this either that the wage must rise by 10%, or also, and this is the point at issue here, that the duration of their labor be reduced by 10%. Whoever delivers 10% more in one hour of time can, as far as the duration is concerned, suffice with 10% less. This is of great importance for the working class. The worker must not be completely absorbed in his craft. He also has to take care of his person, enrich his mind, be mindful of higher interests. He must also be able to live for his wife and children. Also the

Analytical Summary: Dogmatic and Political.
[541]

WORKING HOURS - THE WORKER’S COOPERATION

529 Friendship makes its demands. The church life must not be neglected. Meetings must be able to be attended. And the night’s rest must be such that what was consumed is restored in the night hours. Long and sluggish, dawdling work consumes the worker aimlessly. Short and vigorous doing what needs to be done elevates and inspires.

§36. The worker’s cooperation. Pressing for a reduction in working hours is therefore very certain; only, the worker must make this possible through better training, regular practice, and better effort. Even then, however, it has been observed that the concentration of more labor in a shorter period of time does not yield equally favorable results in all businesses. There are even businesses where the worker has such a completely mechanical character that time savings are almost entirely excluded. This must almost always be the case, for the result shows that one always does something more or less than the other in the same business. However, these differences are so extremely small in an almost completely mechanical business that no pricing for the labor delivered can be determined according to the rules. The reduction of working hours is therefore a question that partly depends on the type of business, but for the rest depends on the worker himself. On his training as an apprentice, on his constant practice, on his demonstrated energy. And only partly does a law arise here from our human nature. That law comes into effect as soon as more labor is demanded of the worker than the normal healthy condition of his constitution allows. It is not too strongly said that a master who not once, but regularly, in determining the working hours, goes beyond these limits, commits a slow murder on his workers. It therefore only depends on whether the worker does the time he works without dawdling and with energy. If not, then the guilt of his suffering lies with him. But if this is the case, then in nature itself a limit is set which neither the employers nor the workers may exceed. Providing labor is also not the sole purpose of life for the worker.

Analytical Summary: The Shift in England. In the nearly forty years that have elapsed since the first publication of Our Program, yet another complete transformation has taken place, which must equally not be passed over in silence, namely, in regard to our connection with the Anti-revolutionary groups or circles in other countries. Since 1878, a wholly different relationship has also emerged outside our own circle, as far as the foreign countries are concerned, particularly in Great Britain. Gladstone’s death on May 19, 1898, and Chamberlain’s attack in 1900 on the South African Boer Republics are the two historical monuments that have been decisive here. Those who were Calvinists in the democratic sense had often turned their political sympathies towards England, and I too have allowed myself to do so. I did not forget how England had often done us highly unjust competition in the maritime and colonial sphere in the 17th century, nor how England had taken not only Ceylon but even the Cape Colony from us at the beginning of the 19th century. Yet our sympathies still seemed to have to lean towards England. In 1890, Gladstone had published his well-known work: The Impregnable Rock of the Holy Scripture, in which he had advocated more clearly than ever before his steadfast agreement with the Statesmanship that rests on God’s Word. He felt a deep aversion to the Radicalism that was then already creeping into England. He moved unconditionally in the line of the Old Whigs. And without hesitation it must be stated that all that was of Calvinistic origin, both in England and especially in Scotland, supported him. It is true that it already raised some doubts that a man like John Morley, who was Gladstone’s political confidant, moved entirely outside that rock of the holy Scripture, but here too Groen van Prinsterer found support in Allard Pierson, and Calvin was also rehabilitated here in our country by Allard Pierson. Forster’s school policy could serve as an example to us here, and the encounters I had with Forster and other English statesmen, through the mediation of my academic contemporary Lord Reay from Leiden, always warmed my sympathies anew. I felt again and again that, as an English citizen, I would be a Gladstonian, and how, conversely, Gladstone, whatever his ultimate fate may have been on our terrain, was undoubtedly a Groenian.
[542]

530 THE SOCIAL QUESTION.

§37. Exemption.

Analytical Summary: The Antirevolutionaries in Germany. In Germany, there was no compensation to be found for this. Stöcker’s appearance did have my warm sympathy, but as I have told Stöcker himself more than once, he failed to take a political stance in a general sense. In the social sphere, he acted energetically and decisively, but his social policy hung too much in the air and lacked a general political foundation. That is why joining his efforts was not possible for us. The Junker parties, the Agrarians, who made up the core of the Conservatives, had increasingly disowned Stahl’s political testament. Von Bismarck’s political conduct moved almost entirely in the sphere of strengthening Germany’s global significance, and although he openly professed, “We Germans fear God and no one else,” the religious vein running through his politics was still strongly tinged. In the May Laws, he even dared to attack the freedom of the Church. With all the admiration for the mighty results he achieved, he could still not elicit any political affiliation from us Antirevolutionaries. The “Reformists,” who were reviving in Germany, did try to approach me once or twice to join their ranks, but their school tactics, their avoidance of all political involvement, and their sober Calvinism seemed to me to promise too little for an attempt at affiliation. In Barmen and Elberfeld, there certainly would have been favorable prospects; but even there, the conditions under which the action took place were too specifically German, and too little principled and comprehensive in a general sense, for me to engage in a closer understanding without suffering harm myself. The Protestant element in general, and the Reformed in particular, lacked entirely what made the Roman Catholic Center in Germany so strong, in that it did not have the courage to act as an independent party. It was therefore understandable that some more decisive men like Kleist-Retzow and Von Gerlach sought to be incorporated into the Center. But this too failed on its own. And so, alas, it must be admitted that the rich prospect that opened up for the powerful formation of an Antirevolutionary party in Germany after Stahl’s appearance has not been realized. Personally, there are thousands and thousands in Germany who are kindred spirits to us and envy our independent party position; but it has not come to an independent formation, and there is hardly any prospect of it happening in the future. Switzerland offers no hope for the future either. In the French cantons, what is outlined in France is repeated in a bioscopic way. The Italian cantons are too small in population to take an independent position. And in the German cantons, it is the Roman Catholic ones where the most emphasis is placed on the honor of one’s own principles, while in the Protestant ones, German theology often marries French political ideas, and thus here too a revival of Calvinism is excluded. One saw it at Calvin’s centenary. Glorification of a name in which part of Switzerland spoke of ancient glories, but of spiritual kinship with the traditions of the man of Geneva, not a trace in all that was manifested and celebrated. Religion and politics here too almost hermetically sealed off from each other. Not even the faintest indication of political action in Calvin’s footsteps. §38. In Roman Catholic lands. In predominantly Roman Catholic lands, there could naturally be no revival of the Antirevolutionary movement. The Roman Catholic Church, with its hierarchical structure and its centralized authority, does not allow the formation of independent political parties within its fold. The Ultramontane movement, which has spread from Rome, has indeed brought about a certain political mobilization, but this has been entirely in the service of the Roman Catholic Church and its interests. The Antirevolutionary principle, which seeks to maintain the independence of the various spheres of life, is diametrically opposed to this. The Roman Catholic Church, with its claim to sovereignty over all of life, cannot tolerate any independent political movement that would limit its power. And so, in Roman Catholic lands, the Antirevolutionary movement has found no foothold. The political activity of Roman Catholics has been entirely absorbed by the struggle for the interests of their Church. The Antirevolutionary principle, which seeks to maintain the independence of the various spheres of life, has found no echo there.

Exemption from labor demands the Sunday, demands the other holidays, demands the Saturday afternoon, demands domestic events, and demands a small outing in the summer. We combine this, because it all flows from the fact that the worker is not a machine, but a human being. As a human being, he demands Sunday rest, as a human being he has a need to celebrate the holy days, as a human being he may be in marriage, and birth, marriage or death, which form an exception, may occur in him, and also as a human being the worker has a need to get out of it for a while, however short. The free Saturday afternoon is of later invention and is intended to provide some extra rest, which others shift to Sunday, especially when buying clothes, etc. from shops, in order not to disturb the silence of Sunday. But however viewed, whoever is human is not a machine, does not tick day and night like a clock, but knows exceptions, and these exceptions must also be reckoned with in the worker. This certainly poses more than one objection to a business that does not allow any exception; such as the business of the doctor, the pharmacist, the servants, etc. and it also proved not always possible in some large factories to bring the large machines to a standstill. The Minister of the Word, the organist, the sexton, etc. are also to be mentioned here.

§38. The Government should regulate this.

Analytical Summary: In Roomsche landen. In overwegend Roomsche landen kon natuurlijk van een weer op-bloeien

An agreement regulation must then be made about this, just as this has already been done for the pharmacy in places where there are more. And as for the Sunday regulations and the service of the official officials, and those who serve, the Government is the responsible person here. To open a polling station on a Sunday, as is done in France and Belgium, is an annoyance that we fortunately do not yet know; but for the rest, the service of the fire brigade, the water supply, the police, etc., is entirely under the authority of the Government, and a trade union to oppose the Government if necessary does not apply to those who serve the Government.

[543]

EXEMPTION - THE GOVERNMENT REGULATES HERE - NIGHT WORK.

One need not enter the service of the government; but whoever does so, must know beforehand that he then entrusts his fate into its hands and cannot or may not engage in any conflict with it. A certain organization of civil servants is therefore not excluded, but it can never proceed otherwise than under the leadership and strict control of the government itself. The idea of granting a few free days first came up in England with the bank holidays. Here too, the introduction of two or three days of this nature should no longer be hesitated. The birthday of our Queen must be one of them; a second should be taken from our national history; and a third with the onset of summer. The Socialists sought the May Day. In itself a fruitful idea. Only, May is too early, and furthermore, such a free day must never divide, but rather unite the People. One saw, therefore, how the design of the Social Democrats completely failed.

§39. Night Work. Nature points to the day for work, the night for rest. Even among the animals this natural rule applies, with only predatory animals and venomous insects as exceptions. Man too must take the lead here. His is the light, and the darkness must give him the restoration of strength in sleep. Exceptions naturally exist here as well. With a seriously ill person, one must keep watch. Watching is just as necessary as the fire brigade. And the police have to see to it that thieves and robbers do not abuse the night. Now there are also businesses where it has not yet succeeded in ensuring the night’s rest. Especially the Journalism and the Bakeries still suffer under the misery of it. Telegraph and Telephone could be left to stand still in an emergency, for these means of communication did not exist before, and life went on quietly. We have already spoken of other government services. The Bakery too will sooner or later come to its night’s rest. Here, after all, the question of luxury often applies. The ordinary worker is very satisfied with the bread that was baked the previous evening. Only, in the cities, luxury demands the freshly baked roll. Now this too could have been overcome, if one had respected the freedom of the business. Then it would have won a better custom of its own accord. But unfortunately, one did not confine oneself to this. One wanted to force the matter, and then one lost the game. This was foretold, and it came true. It could not be otherwise. Better planned, the attempt will, however, succeed sooner or later. Not at once, and with one stroke, but gradually. With Journalism, on the other hand, it is different. The night describes the history of the day, and cannot present that history to the public later than in the early morning; something that is all the more to be regretted, since the nocturnal overstrain so easily leads to the disruption of that historical account. In a country like ours, exceptions could still be made to some extent in ordinary times, but the Press throughout the world is now one, and in great States the letting pass of a speechless night is completely cut off for the Press. In nothing, however, may this exception for Journalism push aside the firm demand of nature that the night is for sleep.

Analytical Summary: Russia.
[544]

532 THE SOCIAL QUESTION.

Scholars who sit at their writing desks at night, and students who cram for their exams at night, refuse obedience to the definite demand of nature. Soon this will be avenged in sleeplessness, and then the often dangerous sleeping pills and the evil chances of this resistance to nature will complete the ruin.

§40. Anarchy. A deliberate discussion of the Anarchist tendencies in the field of labor is less necessary for our country. We have already pointed out how Nihilism, Syndicalism and so much more found entry in Russia, France and Italy, but could never develop into a power of significance among us. In 1903 this striving still gave a sign of life during the Railway strike, but has then also been suppressed forever, and, as one may hope, above all forever. True, the newspaper Het Volk trumpeted after the adoption of the then already all-Anarchism suppressing Law, that this Law must be repealed at once, and the sooner the better; something for which the Social Democrats would also immediately make themselves available; according to those high-sounding words. But in reality this was nothing but a last gasp of a dying cause. The Anarchist tendencies have been definitively defeated among us.

Analytical Summary: State of Strength and Weakness. There are two phenomena in life: the one of full power, the other in weakness. This latter can have three causes: first, that the person is still a child and has not yet grown up to adulthood; the second, that the person is sick; and the third, that he lost his father before he came of age. In each of these three cases, the woman appears as the natural caretaker: as a mother with her child, as a nurse with the sick, and as a substitute father with the orphan; and in each of these three case-positions, the regiment is with her. On the other hand, when dealing with the adult person, with the normally healthy person, and with a household that still possesses its father, the regiment does not rest with the woman, but with the man, and this by virtue of the fact that the man, as a rule, is the stronger in physical strength, in mental power, in willpower and in readiness to act. According to this self-evident rule, it then also becomes clear in what and to what extent the leadership belongs to the woman, and where not. From this it follows that there can be no question of active or passive suffrage for the States-General for the woman; nor for the Provincial States; and as for municipal administration, she may well be called upon to perform various functions, but not in the government itself. This also remains the case in the ecclesiastical domain. The government of the Church is with the Elders, teaching or ruling, and the woman can only appear as a deaconess in the Diaconate, since the Diaconate is not part of the church government, but only of the ecclesiastical care. This does not mean, however, that the Anti-Revolutionary Party will always succeed in permanently opposing the introduction of active and passive suffrage.
[545]

ANARCHY - CONCLUSION. The anarchist movement has quickly declined. One heard nothing further about it, and even when in 1913 the Socialists were momentarily sixteen members strong in the Second Chamber, not even a rumor went out of an attempt to now finally repeal that fatal Law. This is because it has been recognized here that the transient nature of Anarchy on this point, and now systematically aims at transforming the situation. The Anarchist striving, whether it took the form of Nihilism or Syndicalism, had no patience, took no time for it, to transform the situation, and was always intent on suddenly, all at once, turning the existing order of things upside down. One did not need to wait one’s time, nor even systematically prepare anything. One simply waited for a favorable moment, and then would unexpectedly and all at once unleash the great struggle; a struggle in which victory would of itself accrue to the Workers. The distinction between the Anarchist currents and the more moderate Social Democracy of the Revisionists was not that only the former were revolutionary. Both emanated from the glorification of the revolution idea. But herein they differed, that the entire Anarchist current, and so also the Syndicalists in France, did not take the time and the patience for arming themselves for the struggle, but simply wanted to strike without further deliberation. Hence also, that all such attempts could now and then lead to an uprising, but could not change the situation in any way. The much larger military force at the disposal of the Authorities, cut off every chance of success of the Anarchists once and for all.

§41. Conclusion. Summarizing what has thus been argued, it remains our conviction that as much as possible should be directed towards a government involvement with labor, which ultimately derives the greatest percentage of its energy not from the Government, but from the workers themselves. So in the Insurance system. However, a return to earlier conditions, when labor almost entirely organized itself domestically and regulated, as a result of the expansion of industry and technical gains, is cut off.
