The Government must then lead to this, by obtaining through a proper educational system with annotation, possession of a complete list of employers and employees, in order to unite these in vocational organization, each independently, but yet in contact with each other, so that all workers are included in it, as well as all employers, and that both may come to that connected organization which should serve the Government for advice, exert influence on legislation, and can lead to the removal of disputes. Entirely in the manner of Martin St. Léon. This complete organization will in the meantime be so arranged that any fundamental difference of conviction can lead to a separate grouping within the one great organization. The workers of Christian conviction will be able to act here entirely freely. As long as this ideal has not yet been achieved, in case of a serious dispute the Court of Arbitration will have to serve, and one will have to learn the art on both sides to fully submit to the pronouncement of such a court of arbitration. Especially we Calvinists should not let ourselves be led astray here. Although Professor Dr. Werner Sombart, in his masterwork “Der Bourgeois,” Munich and Leipzig, 1913, p. 323, has angrily railed against Calvinism, and not without some ground, but as appears from the history of Calvinism, the warmest zeal for the worker has always come forth from our circles. Herein Sombart was right, that Calvinism, as soon as it loosens its religious ideal, tends to turn over into capitalism. One saw it in our own history, how after the brilliant period of our faith-struggle, a grasping for gold and money followed, which ultimately caused all higher ideals to perish. Precisely for this reason we pressed so restlessly for an unchangeable maintenance of our spiritual-religious foundation. Neither the organization, nor the rise in wages; nor the insurance may become the ground of our hope for the future. That ground must for every child of God always and solely lie in his trust in God. Always according to Jesus’ definite statement: “All these things do the Gentiles seek after; but ye rest your soul’s needs solely on what your Father in heaven possesses.”

Analytical Summary: Radicalism in France. With France, our relationship could not be other than strained. Not because the Monarchy gave way to the Republic. We ourselves have been a Commonwealth, and our country has been able to fulfill the most glorious role in Europe’s history precisely in that Commonwealth form of government. The form of government decides nothing here in the least. It is precisely with the United States, who also chose the republican form of government, that we feel the closest political kinship. A Calvinist can never forget that in Geneva there was no talk of a Monarchy, and that Calvin himself lived in a Commonwealth, so to speak. What makes reconciliation with the French form of government unthinkable for us is solely this, that France still moves along the straight line for which Rousseau first indicated the two fixed points, and that line is precisely the line of Unbelief and Revolution. It is perfectly true that first Napoleon, and after him both the returned Bourbons and what came after them, have in many ways deviated from that line. But the fact remains that the basic direction of France’s political development has been and still is the direction of Unbelief and Revolution.
[546]

Page 547—-

DEFENSE AND OFFENSE

CHAPTER XIX
CHURCH AND STATE

In the First Part, the discussion of the Principles has already touched on this subject in its historical relations. There is therefore no need to fall into pure repetition. Hence, we will simply refer to the First Part. Chapter XX. NATIONAL DEFENSE

§1. Defense and Offense. The fundamental principle here is that in the Netherlands we have never thought of our Navy or Army in any other way than as instruments for a defensive war. Yet we do not push this so far as if our Sovereign could never draw the sword except to repel an enemy who attacked us with armed force. Even with the most peace-loving disposition, there are very well more than one case imaginable in which we would have to take the offensive, without yet having been attacked. We have the duty to stand up not only for the rights and the people of our land, but very decidedly also for the rights and the honor of our national existence. Without an attack coming upon us from across the borders, a threat may come to our Sovereign, a violation of our Sovereignty, an injury to our honor, and an insult to our Colonies, so grave that we could not refrain from a protest, and if necessary, have to act against the offender with armed force. One knows how there have been moments in the present war where our position already became precarious. But although a state of war on our borders may sometimes force us to endure much that would not be tolerable in normal times, yet it can in no case be asserted that it could ever become our duty to draw the sword ourselves, even if the sword were not yet drawn against us. This does not prevent us, however, from speaking exclusively of National Defense, provided that one never loses sight of the fact that a defense will commonly bear a defensive character, but can equally well, where it concerns the defense of our rights or our honor, assume an offensive character. What is excluded and remains excluded is solely that we may wage war or even threaten war, if the only motive for this were gain in territory or increase of our influence. If we were to take that path of war, our action would acquire an aggressive tendency, and anything that even remotely smacks of this is, in the most absolute sense, excluded for our country. §2. All Attempts at Conciliation Come First. We must even go a step further than this. Not only does everything that even remotely suggests an attack out of a desire for gain lie under a strict prohibition, but also where there is a question of violation of rights, prejudice to interests, or injury to our honor, we may never resort to attack unless all other means to resolve the dispute have first been tried in vain. This applies to every country, in an even stricter sense to the smaller States, and most particularly to the Netherlands as the country where the Peace Palace arose, the Court of Arbitration is seated, and the Peace Conferences have been held. What offends, or injures, or damages, may befall us without there having been any malicious intent involved. There may have been a diplomatic misunderstanding, there may have been unintentional errors in the community of nations, there may have been a clash of interests that no one foresaw—in all such cases we must first exhaust every peaceful means before we can even think of taking up arms.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[548]

536 NATIONAL DEFENSE

There may have been a clash of interests that no one foresaw—in all such cases we must first exhaust every peaceful means before we can even think of taking up arms. Only when all other means have been tried in vain, and it becomes clear that the offender refuses to make amends, and that our rights or our honor can only be maintained by force, may we—but only then—resort to the sword. And even then, the use of force must be strictly limited to what is necessary for our defense. Any excess, any desire for revenge, any intention to humiliate the opponent, is strictly forbidden to us. Our action must be purely defensive in character, no matter how offensive the initial provocation may have been. This is the principle that must govern our national defense.

[549]

ALL ATTEMPTS AT ACCOMMODATION COME FIRST - THE PERMANENCE HAS FAILED. 537 Diplomatic relations have been established, and there may be differences over the interpretation of previously concluded treaties. And in all such cases, it is obvious that, before resorting to arms, one must first try to reach a compromise through a thoughtful exchange of ideas; that if this fails, one must call in the intervention of others; and finally, if nothing else can be done, hand the dispute over to an arbitral tribunal for decision. The most desirable thing here would be that, at a moment of undisturbed peace, we should come to a friendly understanding to the effect that, whatever differences might later arise, it would always and without exception be submitted to arbitral decision.

§3. The permanence has failed. If such a permanent arbitration could have gained ground, a more pacifist future could have unfolded. This, however, was not to be. The conclusion of such a comprehensive Arbitration Treaty was attempted and succeeded on our part with Denmark in 1904, and the credit for this first and only treaty of this kind goes to my then colleagues Melville van Lynden and Loeff. However, a subsequent attempt to achieve the same result with Sweden and Norway failed. And when later, especially with a view to Belgium, it was hoped that we would be just as successful with our nearest neighbors as in Copenhagen, the attempt to do so led to such complete disappointment that even the continuation of the negotiations was not to be thought of. Since then, it has become increasingly clear that the other Empires could never be moved to anything more than a limited Arbitration Treaty, as indeed more than one of this kind has already been concluded; but the exception that was always maintained there, that questions of national honor and higher vital interests would be excluded, ultimately deprived all such half-measures of any decisive value. It could never be determined in advance what would be understood or included under these exceptions; and if ever, from whatever quarter, the evil disposition arose to set us right by force of arms, it would soon become apparent that our half-measure treaty was no more than waste paper. We have therefore never failed to exclude any offensive thought on our part as well. The shifting of the casus positions that could have forced us to take the offensive have not been eliminated altogether.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[550]

538 NATIONAL DEFENSE. What provoked America, that the decision on a dispute that had arisen should not be allowed until after the lapse of a year and an armed intervention, can be an excellent means of deterrence. In the evil years of 1914 and following, however, even this proved to be of little use. If ever, then it became all too clear in those wretched years how powerless even the firmest parchment is against the sharpness of the saber and the shot from the cannon.

§4. The former Militarism. Where we have always pressed for the armament of our country from the Anti-Revolutionary side, this has never implied sympathy for Militarism, and it may even be said without exaggeration that the active action of our party contributed to curbing a pseudo-militarism that had crept in here in the past, from which a kind of soldiery had even been born. The writer of this still remembers from his student days how even in the sixties of the last century half-drunk dragoons or hussars did not hesitate to go out on the full Sunday Breestraat in Leiden with drawn sabers to terrorize the citizenry and throw the whole city into turmoil. Drinking, raving, and cursing were then still part of the true soldiery. An ordinary citizen was an inferior being, and above all a volunteer who rode on horseback would have fallen short of his honor if he had not made the superiority of his position over the ordinary citizen known in every possible way. This was all the more true in a university town, because the students of that time held precisely the same class pride towards the “rabble,” alias citizens. Naturally, the citizenry of Leiden benefited from both the University and the Garrison, but without torn clothes they got off neither with the Students nor with the Dragoons.

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.
[551]

COMBATING RELIGIOUS LIFE.539

§5. Combating It. However, that desire to curb the military life has been combated from more than one side, so that in the end even the morale of the Army was improved by it. This cannot yet be said to the same extent of the Navy, but the Army has acquired a more humane character. Cursing is still prevalent, and, alas, even still too much by officers, but it is no longer the setting of an honor in a special God-blasphemous curse, as in former times. The religious propaganda, as far as prayer, Bible reading, and church attendance are concerned, is still constantly overshadowed, but it is nevertheless constantly breaking through. The sin with the woman still creeps on, but it is no longer the so brutally open prostitution of old. One must not misunderstand our meaning here. We know how miserably much there still is to purify and improve in our Army, and what sad aspects are constantly emerging from our Army. The mobilization has also shown this all too sadly and aggravated it. But even if we do not settle everything here, it must not prevent us from being thankful for what has been gained; and that this gain is very considerable, everyone knows who has previously been offended by the soldiery. We are now moving in a better direction. The Military Homes work splendidly. From all sides, the better-minded in the Army are supported. And although the complaint about much that is troublesome continues, it must still be acknowledged that we have made progress, and that what we have already achieved contains the promise of even further gain. The field preachers must now, as soon as peace returns, not be abolished. Rather, they must be doubled in number, and appointed by the Church for the own members among the soldiers, and also in peacetime be helpful to us in the struggle against what is poisoning our Army. On board our larger warships, the former almoner must no longer be absent. Why should everything receive guidance, but only the religious element not? §6. The Religious Life.

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.

But then we must also guard against the repetition of the serious mistake that has been made in this regard during the mobilization. The Department of War understands nothing of the religious life, and therefore appointed twelve field preachers for 240,000 men, i.e. one for every twenty thousand, and this was done in such an ill-considered manner that without choice or distinction they placed a modern Socialist with one 20,000, and a strict Calvinist with the other, and it even seems that they did not even think of how they would add like to like; something which, if they had recognized the Churches, would have shown itself naturally. Especially after the compulsory church attendance of the military has been abolished, they should have sought a completely different provision for the religious needs. But War had not thought of this at all. After having abolished church attendance, it did nothing more. Everything now went on without religion. And only when the mobilization spoke, did one feel that one could not do without it, and gave in to a certain provision, but in such a thoughtless and ineffective way that it became a risk in the country. It was only the former Minister Talma and after him Dr. de Visser who then tried to bring order into this chaos, but however high their merits are in this, the impossible remained impossible even for them. Religious and moral power had to be deployed with more vigor. What is done in this sense from the private side, must also be able to count on the authoritative and financial support of the Army administration; also for the distribution of good reading material. The small 3,000 extra military subscribers that De Standaard received during the war years shows sufficiently how much progress can be made here; and even if we leave the Soldiers’ Gazette out of this, in any case the Department of War should not have supported this paper one-sidedly, but also financially considered the paper published from the Roman Catholic side for this purpose, and thus provide “something for everyone.” And as far as morale is concerned, the commanders could very well forbid the troops to visit certain streets and inns. In The Hague, a sentry stood every evening at the entrance of a common neighborhood, and no soldier could pass through. Even hygienically, this works excellently. Our soldiers are mostly big children, and the child’s path requires supervision. But above all, it comes down to the exemplary example of the officers in the higher and lower ranks.

[552]

540 NATIONAL DEFENSE.

At Breda, or wherever officers are trained, there is a shortage, so that the high moral obligation of the officer corps remains unfulfilled.

[553]

RELIGIOUS LIFE - OUR NAVY

The officer who is to lead the soldier in the religious and moral life is not from the outset the one who will later bear the responsibility. Although it is perfectly true that we do not have a Gustavus Adolphus or Cromwell at the head of our divisions, the religious and moral responsibility of our officer class cannot therefore be disregarded. The man who commands troops, whether as an officer or as a non-commissioned officer, and who does not honor the men under his command above all else, falls below the tolerable level.

§7. Our Navy. More than once there has been talk of abolishing our separate Department of the Navy. The appointment of Mr. Colijn as Minister of both the Department of War and the Department of the Navy already laid the groundwork for this intention. The fall of the Cabinet in 1913 has for the time being thwarted this plan; but still, one will see, it will ultimately result in the dismantling of the Navy Department. As will be set forth in a following chapter, the fleet in the East Indies Archipelago can no longer remain a part of our Navy in the Netherlands. The fleet that will have to guard our Archipelago will for its maintenance and armament have to move entirely from the Navy to the Colonies; and in the end there will be no other interest left here at home for a Department of the Navy than covering our coasts and keeping a small number of cruisers ready for flag display.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[554]

542 NATIONAL DEFENSE

That this is extremely painful for our Navy, we understand. With a past behind it of which our Navy may boast, this is tantamount to the self-effacement of its existence. With a time behind us when Holland’s Fleet was the first fleet in the world, when it made Spain’s Navy succumb, and still held out for a long time even against England, an officer of our Navy cannot bear the thought that our Navy has now served its purpose. Once a fleet under De Ruyter and Tromp that every other naval power envied us, it wounds the self-esteem of our Navy that it has sunk into such insignificance in an irreparable way. At first one still got over this. Our action against piracy in Algiers, shortly after the restoration of our independence in 1813, the sending of a warship to Beirut, our participation in the Battle of Shimonoseki in Japan, and so much more, still gave somewhat the impression that our ancient glory could revive. Our wooden cruisers or line-of-battle ships of those days still counted for something, and the entire transformation of the armed forces at sea was yet to come. It already began with the first monitor at Baltimore; but this first beginning was nothing compared to what was soon to come; and he who now compares the English, German and American squadrons of Super-Dreadnoughts with what seemed a power on the waters forty years ago, immediately feels that every fleet that remained unable to go through this process of evolution had already been written off at sea. Spain was the first to experience this in the most painful way. When in the harbor of Manila in 1898 an entire Spanish squadron was sunk by a few cruisers of the Union, and shortly afterwards off Cuba the entire fleet of newly built Spanish cruisers had to yield to the American fleet, the fate of the fleet of every small State was decided.

§8. Magical progress. If one now adds to this what in the fifteen years of the twentieth century has been literally conjured up in the maritime field, and how ship after ship, nay, castle after castle, was launched that had not cost less than 25 to 30 million guilders to build, and demanded fortunes for crew, armament and maintenance, - then one immediately feels how with this the maritime situation has taken on proportions that not even the smallest State could maintain with the utmost effort. Neither the Swedish, nor the Norwegian, nor the Danish fleet count for anything anymore. Greece now has at its disposal one colossal warship, which it received as a gift from a Greek in America, and later bought two old battleships from the American Union; but for the rest it knocks in vain at the door of Portugal or Spain, of Belgium or Brazil, of Peru or Chile, everywhere with the same sad result. One can no longer hold one’s own at sea against the Great Powers; and

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.
[555]

MAGICAL PROGRESS - COASTAL DEFENSE

543 What Argentina and Chile still tried, these lands have also soon abandoned to stop building their fleets. One stood, both financially and in terms of personnel, simply before an absolute impossibility. For us, this has not yet come out so immediately, because with us it was not only about the coverage of our coasts, but above all about the protection of our colonies. All together, it gave on our list still a total of 102 warships, but these together represented not much more than 80,000 tons of content, and could bring into action little more than 283,000 horsepower. This fleet carried 455 pieces of artillery, and had a crew of nearly 7,000 heads, including the officers. Yet among all these warships there were only 14 cruisers, of which still six from the previous century, and only partially fully equipped in steel. The others not even that. Moreover, they were unequal in size and construction. Unequal in speed and armament. They hardly formed an squadron. Even if it still gave a certain fleet display, it was and is no longer a fleet, and there is no talk of a naval power on the waters in this way. If it were to come to a real war with this state of affairs, the tragedy of Manila and Cuba would experience a second performance in Den Helder and Tandjong Priok.

§9. Coastal Defense

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.

Leaving the Colonial demands aside for a moment, no other defense can be conducted on our coast in the Netherlands than partly 1° through the army, supported by batteries and forts; partly 2° by laying mines; and partly 3° by submarines and airships. This combination alone makes that almost the entire defense now already falls under the Department of War, except the mines and the submarines; for the airships do their work just as well on land as on the waters. It has already been pointed out long ago that the coastal defense can only very partially come from the Navy. Although a blockade is still conceivable, in which a not too large cruiser, as mentioned above, could harm the enemy by shooting out, yet this is too accidental in nature for the defense of the coast to be controlled by it.

[556]

544 NATIONAL DEFENSE

The former delusion, which was especially applied in Zeeland, as if there could only be talk of a concentrated Defense in the center of the country, is now increasingly abandoned, and under the present war it had no small effect that we did not leave the Scheldt to its fate, but remained mostly on our own terrain. The great fort that is now being built on Walcheren also shows that the former system of abandonment has been abandoned, and the defense is again conducted along the entire long line of the coast. This has also drawn attention to Den Helder. If Den Helder is not given up, then the defense there too will have to be directed according to the demands of the latest times. Naturally, the mine system has come more to the fore by this changed state of affairs. All study and care will therefore have to be directed to bringing the mine system on our coast to the highest conceivable perfection, and to defending it against the natural attempts of the enemy to render the laid mines harmless.

§10. The Small States in Distress

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.

But although we are now on the better path as regards the improved fort construction and as regards the mine system, no less seriousness will always have to be made with the submarines and with the airships, and this poses an almost insoluble problem especially for small states like the Netherlands. In the first place, it will be completely impossible to acquire both from abroad. If anything was clearly shown in the recent war, it was the inexorable necessity of making the construction of both these instruments of war entirely and fully possible in our own country. All orders for them from abroad must be broken. Just as it proved to be the most effective way, it was precisely the war itself that provided the exact data for the construction of these instruments of war from month to month, and it was not before, but during the war, that the vast majority of these instruments were ready and are still being expanded. We too must therefore be able to manufacture them ourselves, and the narrowness of our country imposes on us the obligation to have the construction take place within our own borders.

[557]

THE SMALL STATES IN DISTRESS - CRUISER SQUADRON The small states must have a territory as protected as possible. Otherwise, the enemy air fleet would primarily aim at the destruction of our shipyards for this purpose. It has become equally clear from the present war that a too small number of these instruments is of no use. Overwhelmed by superior force, they are already deprived of them in the first months of the war. And in the third place, the personnel who are to serve these weapons must be trained and ready for their operation in the most effective way. Disasters like those that have befallen the commander-in-chief of our army in the Indies are otherwise the fixed punishment for neglect. Finally, one must also be equally prepared to enable a submarine to sail at great distances from our coast. What the German submarines are now accomplishing in the Mediterranean shows to what distances the action of these boats can extend; but then provision must also be made for the supply of food and ammunition through means that must be organized in peacetime. Here, both with submarines and with airships, it is a means of defense by attack, on which the fate of our entire country could depend at a given moment. The question of how we can prepare for such a struggle cannot be considered seriously enough. No money should be spared here. Proportionately, these two instruments are not expensive; but whoever is too economical, only creates an advantage for the enemy.

§11. Cruiser Squadron. Alongside forts, mines, submarines and airships, we must also point to the necessity of always being able to dispose of a squadron of cruisers. We referred to this, entirely apart from the defense of our Kingdom in the Indies. A very considerable part of our defense of the Indies will have to be confirmed in the Indies itself. More about that in the following chapter, which deals exclusively with the Colonies. For the most part, the defense of the Indies will have to be sought on Java itself, and can therefore never find its strength here at home in a separate Department of the Navy. But even as a State...

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[558]

NATIONAL DEFENSE. If we consider what relates to the Defense of the Indies, it appears that the possession of a small squadron of cruisers is also indispensable in Europe. The display of the flag is here in the foreground, and also the defense of our possessions in Central and South America. Neither Curaçao nor Suriname may be left to themselves; and although at this moment there seems to be less to fear from the side of Venezuela, yet history has taught us that we were even for a moment in danger through the initiative of a traitor in Paramaribo. As long as we let our Dutch tricolor wave over Suriname and the islands to the north, our Government must be able to assert itself there, if necessary, by force of arms. This, too, is only possible through a small number of cruisers. One must not make too high demands here either. There is also a limit here which we cannot exceed; but in no case may the precarious position return, in which our authority in those regions is actually threatened, and we in The Hague are powerless to send help. Quite apart from this, the disposal of a small number of cruisers is strictly necessary to remove minor inconveniences. There may be a small dispute on a remote island, or in a small state of South America, or anywhere else. Commonly this then also settles itself, provided at such a moment a couple of cruisers appear that command respect for our flag; while conversely, if one cannot act in any way at such a moment, our good name suffers and our interests are abandoned. A final significance of such a squadron lies in what is now associated with the names of the Emden and the Möwe. These two names indicate that even small cruisers on the high seas can be of serious importance, if they know how to make maritime heroism shine; something that seemed to remind us of our Geuzen period this time. Although one usually only speaks of flag display with this squadron of cruisers, it appears that very serious interests can also be involved here. Only, if one grasps the significance of such a cruiser squadron in this way, it follows from this that it must not be a combination of half-worn-out bottoms. What we have seen steaming in and out under the name of a cruiser squadron in the Indies for tens of years has not had a serious character. Our armored ships

[559]

THE STRENGTH OF OUR ARMY. 547 They were ashamed of their own wooden keels, and even with the later, entirely steel-clad cruisers, the inequality in speed and armament, the much too slow pace, and the defective armament were such a lamentable cause of weakness that hardly any mention could be made of a squadron. What is meant here by a cruiser squadron is a four or five cruisers of about 5,000 tons—no larger is needed—but with a speed of at least 30 knots and armed with the best artillery.

§12. The Strength of Our Army. For the army that will be entrusted with our national defense, the immediately pressing question that dominates all considerations is in what proportion the number of this army must stand to the number of the population. An absolute number for the army cannot be specified. When I wrote the explanation of Our Program in 1879, our population still barely numbered four million souls; now in 1914, according to the population registers, it consists of 6,339,705 persons. An increase in the number thus in 37 years of more than one-third. Yet in determining the figures for the army, this gigantic increase was by no means proportionately taken into account. There seemed to be no necessity to let both figures increase equally. Now, however, the war that broke out in 1914 has so inextricably linked the connection between the two figures that for calculating the numerical strength of the army, no other basis can be used than the population figure. Only a small detail needs to be noted in this regard. The improved hygienic conditions have brought about that in the population figure there is now proportionately a greater number than before of persons over 60 years of age, who no longer come into consideration for military service. For this reason, a certain percentage would have to be deducted in a precise calculation. Likewise, a certain percentage would have to be deducted for the greater number of women, above that of men. And even if one wanted to proceed with the utmost precision, a certain number would additionally have to be left out of account of persons who, thanks to better medical care, now remain alive, where they would have died earlier.

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[560]

548 NATIONAL DEFENSE.

§13. One-tenth.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.

Yet it seems superfluous, with the high figures involved here, to go into all these minor differences, and one is quite safely and certainly justified in adopting the rule of thumb that one-tenth of the population must be incorporated into military service. We are in the Netherlands at this moment presumably not far from 6 1/2 million souls. As I already indicated, the precise figure in 1914 was 340,000 above the six million. In 1912 it was still only 115,000 above the six million. An increase of 225,000 in two years’ time. This will likely have continued so in 1915 and 1916 as well. Putting the number of souls at a good 6 1/2 million, we can proceed quite safely. Now, for the above-mentioned reasons, let this half million slip away, and take as the ratio figure only the 6 million, so that for the army one-tenth of this, and thus the figure of 600,000 men, would have to be assumed. Not that we consider this in itself so desirable, but it must be. A country as exposed as the small Netherlands, lying at the most dangerous point between England, France and Germany, has not to ask what we would consider desirable, but has to recognize that the number of its own army must be proportionate to the ratio figure of the armies abroad. Countries with such gigantic populations as Russia and Germany may still allow a small play in this, but where it is a question of a small state like the Netherlands, which is already in such an unfavorable disproportion to its neighbors, it is also without any further argument established that with us the real figure must fully correspond to the ratio figure. If one now asks oneself what the ratio was in the war of 1914 between the figure of the population and the figure of the army that the various warring states actually put into the field, then all reasoning can be omitted, and it stands established by the indisputable figures that one-tenth indicates the actual ratio; Russia alone excepted to some degree. One soldier for every ten inhabitants is what even holds true in the small Balkan states. France with its 39 million inhabitants has over 4 million men under arms. England with its 45 million souls

[561]

ELEVENTH - STILL UNCERTAIN.549 It now also counts on 4 1/2 million soldiers. Russia already stands at 12 million. And in the Balkans, from Romania to Greece, all armies had even climbed to more than one-tenth of the war. Of Germany with its 67 million inhabitants, experts claim that it now already has more than 6 million soldiers in the field. Naturally, it is extremely difficult to collect entirely accurate figures in this list.

§14. Still uncertain. The tremendous losses on the battlefield, which run into the millions in dead, wounded, and prisoners, have now been filled in everywhere to be able to dispose of completely new corps again. Also, entirely new formations have been called into being from all sides, which, composed of the reserves, found no place in the normal army organization. But even if it is not possible to give the number of armed men down to the smallest details, up to the last division, yet it is beyond doubt that one in ten of the inhabitants has now actually become the figure for the armed part of a people. We have also not made the slightest illusion to ourselves in our country. With the one-tenth of the population, that is below 600,000 men, we will not get away with the new army formation, just as it will be governed by the recent war, after the restoration of peace. It is not even yet established that it will be able to remain at these 600,000. One only needs to imagine the situation as it actually presents itself to us. One need only look at the army figures in the Old Testament, as I have already remarked earlier, to convince oneself that in the struggle between the old world empires of the East, these figures stood even higher; so high that almost the entire people, from the youngest to the oldest, went to war. The figures given for Israel prove this. Later, these figures have certainly decreased significantly; and when the Christian religion had called a completely different relationship in the Middle Ages, all these massive armies have finally been lost, so that in the end one sought salvation in the system of condottieri. Above all, the transition to mercenary armies has then caused the figures to decrease significantly. So much so that even in the Napoleonic wars the figures were still far below what they are now.

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”
[562]

550 NATIONAL DEFENSE. 15. Since 1870, everything has changed. However, all this has not prevented the alarming figures from rising regularly upwards again since 1870, so that it sometimes even seemed that they were making leaps. The general conscription, which became increasingly common, has of course aggravated this. Almost all previous exemptions have fallen away because of this. The better hygienic conditions have increased the number of those who previously fell out due to illness or physical defect, but now take up the rifle. And so the already formidable figures of the last quarter-century have almost gone up again from year to year. That is why one now already comes to one-tenth of the population. But do not forget, it has not been much below one-fifth before. One-fifth indicates the number of families, and that, taken on average, with one man from each household grasping the spear or girding the sword, had long been the common state of affairs in many regions. Even now, with one soldier per ten souls, the highest conceivable maximum has by no means been reached. After the restoration of peace, country after country will again ponder how to strengthen the land for the threatening war that is to come. But this is left to be decided later. For the time being, the Netherlands is doing its duty, as it arms itself according to the generally applicable rule, and that rule, as it appears practically from the war just fought, is that we have to set the figure of the Army at one-tenth of the inhabitants. For the female population, the largest half falls away. So it remains one in five men. Of those one in five, again the young under 20 and the old over 40 years fall away, and if one now excludes the physically unfit from what remains, it soon becomes apparent that one will not even be able to maintain the 40 years, and will have to move up to the 45 years. It will be hard. It will disrupt much domestic happiness. And it will require enormous sums.

[563]

PERMANENT VOLUNTEER CORPS - TWO POSSIBILITIES

§16. Permanent Volunteer Corps. As early as 1878, Our Program pressed for the urgent need, alongside our military army, for a small corps of mercenary troops that would stand entirely on its own and be composed of 4,000 to 6,000 men, enlisted for a fixed period of four, six, or ten years. The endless tinkering that occurred especially after 1900 with the so-called “permanent contingent” repeatedly confirmed the pressing necessity to have such a self-standing mercenary corps of volunteers. With the later introduced regulation, it was in itself a demand that the newly enlisted men, on an equal footing, complete their training during the most suitable months of the year. But the consequence of this was that there would be three or more months when our barracks would be empty, and only the officers with the cadre, cavalry, artillery, and other groups that had been training longer would remain in service. This, of course, cannot be tolerated. A certain number of infantry must steadily remain under arms, even in the winter months. The choice of this, however, can only be made at will; and so we also got that endless fuss over the permanent contingent, which still continues. If now the training period were reduced from 9 to 6 months, this inconvenience would become even greater. The government can never be entirely without troops. One of two things is therefore inevitable. Either a completely arbitrary selection must be made from among the ordinary troops, or, to avoid this, a small permanent corps of mercenary troops or volunteers must appear alongside the military army, and the government must have complete discretion over this corps. §17. Two Possibilities. Of these two possibilities, only the second one seems acceptable to us. The regulation of the training then remains entirely free, and there is not a moment when the government is left completely unprotected in the country. We estimated the strength of this corps at 6,000 men. For the permanent contingent, 4,000 men were counted, and then there would also be a small 1,000 gendarmes. It could thus stand at around 5,000, but with our population increasing so considerably, it seems advisable to let this figure of 5,000 rise a little. What stood at 5,000 in 1900 should now, without exaggeration, be set at 6,000. This corps could then consist of two regiments, each of three battalions. Predominantly infantry, and only a single squadron of mounted troops. If necessary, a few machine guns could be made available to this small corps. The task of this corps would be: first, guard duty at the palace and magazines; second, providing for the State Police; third, garrisoning the major cities; 4th, immediate intervention wherever violent resistance might occur in the country; and 5th, taking up positions on the borders as soon as signs of war danger appear. In fact, such a corps would have a policing character, but in the broad sense that the state would have at its disposal a powerful, always ready police force that could intervene both outwardly and inwardly as needed. If our advice, as it went out in 1878, had been followed, much of the endless fuss with the “permanents” would have been avoided. As it turned out, this fuss has even cost ministers their lives. §18. General Conscription. A military force of 600,000 men in wartime is not to be introduced in a country like ours, unless General Conscription becomes the starting point of the entire system. However limited the exemptions are introduced, they immediately take on such considerable numbers across the country that there can never be any question of a significant strengthening of the army. Now there is undoubtedly something harsh in this, so that as one lifts these exemptions, the one after the other. The exemption of the only sons especially has the purpose, with a view to wartime, of preventing the extinction of a generation. But this consideration must give way to the higher interest of the nation’s defense. The only sons must therefore also be called up, and the exemptions must be reduced to a minimum.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.
[564]

552 NATIONAL DEFENSE.

§17. Two Possibilities. Of these two possibilities, only the second one seems acceptable to us. The regulation of the training then remains entirely free, and there is not a moment when the government is left completely unprotected in the country. We estimated the strength of this corps at 6,000 men. For the permanent contingent, 4,000 men were counted, and then there would also be a small 1,000 gendarmes. It could thus stand at around 5,000, but with our population increasing so considerably, it seems advisable to let this figure of 5,000 rise a little. What stood at 5,000 in 1900 should now, without exaggeration, be set at 6,000. This corps could then consist of two regiments, each of three battalions. Predominantly infantry, and only a single squadron of mounted troops. If necessary, a few machine guns could be made available to this small corps. The task of this corps would be: first, guard duty at the palace and magazines; second, providing for the State Police; third, garrisoning the major cities; 4th, immediate intervention wherever violent resistance might occur in the country; and 5th, taking up positions on the borders as soon as signs of war danger appear. In fact, such a corps would have a policing character, but in the broad sense that the state would have at its disposal a powerful, always ready police force that could intervene both outwardly and inwardly as needed. If our advice, as it went out in 1878, had been followed, much of the endless fuss with the “permanents” would have been avoided. As it turned out, this fuss has even cost ministers their lives. §18. General Conscription. A military force of 600,000 men in wartime is not to be introduced in a country like ours, unless General Conscription becomes the starting point of the entire system. However limited the exemptions are introduced, they immediately take on such considerable numbers across the country that there can never be any question of a significant strengthening of the army. Now there is undoubtedly something harsh in this, so that as one lifts these exemptions, the one after the other. The exemption of the only sons especially has the purpose, with a view to wartime, of preventing the extinction of a generation. But this consideration must give way to the higher interest of the nation’s defense. The only sons must therefore also be called up, and the exemptions must be reduced to a minimum.

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the
[565]

UNIVERSAL MILITARY SERVICE - THE DIFFICULTIES ARE NOT INSURMOUNTABLE.

553 to spare the head of the household. And on the other hand, the concern increases for those who would otherwise remain unattended at home. From the harsh mobilization days of 1914/16, one knows what this caused in terms of inconvenience and suffering when people were called away from home and business. There will always be special cases where certain exemptions, however temporary, cannot be withheld.

§19. The difficulties are not insurmountable. Yet the outcome in other countries has shown that universal military service can very well be implemented, and that even if war breaks out, life can still take its course. One must make do in many areas. Not only can education continue regularly. Boys must step in for an adult. And women’s help must also be accepted, even in public service. But however much disruption and inconvenience this may cause, the outcome has shown in various ways that it works, and in the countries where universal military service is already in force, no one thinks of abolishing it again. Even England has now turned to it. It may be said that this is only temporary until the coming peace, and only for the unmarried; but already in England the prophecy is to be heard that the peace will bring no change in this and that the married will soon follow in the footsteps of the unmarried who are now leading the way. With us too, the struggle for nothing more than a repetition of what we experienced here regarding personal military service. The question then was whether we could do without it, so that we could bring the necessary manpower to the field, and whether in our modern wars it was not much more a matter of the moral strength that the appearance of men from the highest ranks had to give to our entire military action. Personal military service only implied that the higher-ranking could not shift his service to the lower-ranking. And although this brought the danger with it that in case of war a larger part of the higher-ranking men would lose their lives, yet the conviction always gained ground more and more that this sacrifice for the honor and safety of the country had to be made. And whatever one may have struggled against earlier,

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.
[566]

554. NATIONAL DEFENSE.

now almost everyone agrees with this system that was so fiercely opposed at first. Just as it has now gone in Germany, in France, and where not, with the universal military service. First fiercely opposed, this system has also already succeeded in winning over public opinion. Gradually, it is becoming more and more accepted everywhere. With us too, the door is already opening wide. But then it is also desirable that the introduction of this only good system should not be delayed too long. As it now stands, a large part of the population is being spoiled too much by a freedom that cannot be maintained. Here too we must move forward. And the sooner the better. That universal military service naturally brings with it state provision for the needy families, goes without saying. Yet this provision only takes on a serious character in times of war or mobilization. For the regular months of exercise, and for the refresher service, there is never any question of astronomical figures in the regulation of these costs.

§20. Extension of service years. In more than one State, they are now already so far that they let the military service begin with the 19th year and continue until the 50th year. A service that thus runs over thirty years. Especially on the Balkans, this came into use. Here too, as we have already noted above, there is little else than a constant return to ancient customs. As for Israel and Judah, we find in the Old Testament figures repeatedly given that show that without any limit, all who could still wield a sword or spear went out to the field. In the earlier maturity of the East, this sometimes began as early as the 16th year, and then, very unequally, continued sometimes beyond the 60th year. It was no different, and it is still the case with the nomads and with the Negro peoples. Then there is no other rule than that everyone marches who can or still can. How a government should proceed in this, depends not only on itself, nor only on the people over whom it rules. All that concerns the state of war is primarily governed by the actions of those who can soon become your enemy. Is it sufficient

Analytical Summary: The three types not equally characteristic. But this does not mean that, in the course of time, each of these three types has in turn dominated the national condition: the Roman Catholics in the Middle Ages, the Calvinists in the 16th century, and the French Revolutionaries after 1789. It does not at all follow from this that they are equal in national significance. Undoubtedly, the Seven Provinces, once living a common life with Flanders and other southern regions, have enjoyed a high and flourishing position and have gradually taken an increasingly important position in Northern Europe; but it must not be forgotten here that the national energy was at the time much more strongly developed in what is now called Belgium than in the northern provinces. Neither Amsterdam nor Rotterdam could in those days be placed on a par with Ghent and Bruges. The wealth of medieval life had more stamped its imprint on the national character in the South than in the North. The reference to the rich medieval type therefore has much less significance for the present Kingdom of the Netherlands than for the western part of Belgium. And in the second place, it must be added that at that time the unity of our national life was much less consolidated. Under the Burgundians, the Dutch provinces did not occupy an independent position, but were united with what lay outside our later borders; and under Charles V and Philip, we even came, though only in a personal way, into a Union with entirely different states from Central and Southern Europe—something which could not but be detrimental to the confirmation of our own national character. Whoever investigates the historical significance of our lower lands may well find the period we lived through in the Middle Ages highly important, but nevertheless hesitates for a moment to acknowledge without reservation that our glorious period dates from April 1, 1572. Not in the Middle Ages, but in the period of the Reformation, lies the high point of our national history. It cannot be emphasized enough how much scrupulous fairness is required in such a comparison, and we shall beware of the assertion that from the Calvinist side the correct toll of reverence has always been paid to the medieval period in our history, to which they undoubtedly had a right. But it stands to reason that from the Roman Catholic side, too, we have not always been done justice. It cannot be valued highly enough that especially in the second half of the 19th century a more impartial judgment gained influence. Just with a view to this more unanimous judgment, it may now at least be established that not only the foreign historians, one may well say without any exception of importance, honor the period after 1572 as the highest and most glorious, but that also within the country the best interpreters of each of the three types increasingly come to the unanimous conclusion that the world significance of our small country came out most richly in the heyday of Calvinism. This in no way denies that our national life in those days of our glory also showed dark sides. The resistance to the monarchical state form, and even against the House of Orange, has almost become a source of our unhappy discord until 1789, and it is impossible to say how much higher our country would have soared if immediately after the separation from Philip II the crown had been offered to Prince William. It had to come to this in 1813; but who calculates what national suffering would have been spared us if we had taken this important step two centuries earlier. But even if one plays open cards and takes into account all the suffering, oppression and restriction of rights that the temporarily dominant group has inflicted on the other two groups, it is historically established that we were never before 1572 what our national life was in the period between those two dates, and never again after 1789. To top it off, the fact that we offered a refuge to the politically and religiously persecuted from all countries is proof that the actual life here was a life in freedom, and that the publications and ordinances that claimed to infringe on this freedom had more the effect of satisfying formal demands than of inflicting suffering on those who deviated.
[567]

EXTENSION OF SERVICE YEARS - WARTIME. If in the countries from which an invasion could be expected, there is a service obligation of twelve years, then this example is followed. But if this figure rises in the surrounding countries, from twelve to twenty, and from twenty to thirty, then there is no longer any question of you being able to persist with your much lower figures. Even a Great Power has had to adapt itself to its potential enemy. And especially for small States, which have always lagged far behind in their military figures, this puts their future in very serious danger, if they fall even a little behind their neighbors. It can already be said that our current system, which is based on a service of only 20 years, can no longer be maintained. With us, one now begins with his 20th year, and there is a complete break with his 40th year, both from the Army and from the Landwehr and the Landsturm. This system is now no longer tenable, and we must inexorably shift from 40 to 45 years. As for physical condition, this generally raises no objections. A healthy, moral man of 45 is still a man in the prime of his life with us, and there is at least no single reason to keep the men of 41, 42, 43 and 44 at home. If we adhere to the law of nature, then it can be said that he who marries as a young man of 25 years - and earlier was only an exception - can first send a son of twenty years into the field to replace him on his 45th year. There is therefore nothing in extending the service to the 45th year that would seriously object. Of course, there will be more dropouts at that age due to accidents or illness, and there will also be a loss through mortality, but apart from that, in setting the final term at 45 years, there is nothing that does violence to the ordinary course of life.

§21. Wartime. There is all the less need to relax any higher demands, since the pressure of serious matters really only comes in wartime, whether the mobilization is only intended to maintain neutrality or whether war has already broken out. On closer inspection, however, the state of actual war can well be thought away. But if war does indeed break out, which drags our own country along, and threatens a danger that our fatherland would lose its independence, then all desire to take up arms spontaneously, and a man of still sufficient vital force wishes nothing more than to be able to act himself for his country and people. But then this distinction arises, that without general conscription and without a service figure up to the age of 45, whole groups of untrained men would have to take the field, who otherwise, as old veterans of former service, would be fully ready for war. When a real war breaks out in which we ourselves become involved, our solid men of 40–45 years would only regret that they now have to go out too, but they would completely miss what could have kept them back. Without repetition exercises, a man of more advanced years stands helpless in the ranks. And that is why it is so much better for him to remain enrolled, as long as there is talk of him going along. He who has once left the army stands outside any connection, no longer knows his superiors, and belongs nowhere. This gives an unstable feeling and deprives of guidance and progress. And just for that reason, remaining enrolled as long as the years continue in which he actually wants to go along himself, gives a much better position than the later loose rejoining. Such a voluntary service, however proud and epic it may be, always gives a less significant position. We do not need to go beyond the age of 45 for the time being, but up to this year the line of service can be drawn without difficulty.

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over
[568]

§22. Exercise Years. One is increasingly moving away from the long duration of the exercise years. It was the old wisdom of warfare, which had always insisted on this, in order to make the man into nothing but a link in the whole. Of free initiative, of independent action, of moving according to one’s own insight, of letting oneself be governed by the situation that the battle demanded at a given moment, there was in olden times no question. The more the man became a machine, the better one could also govern the whole mechanism. There had to be complete subordination, complete obedience, complete adaptation to the prescribed forms. The more the man became a mere cog in the wheel, the more he could be relied upon. But this view is now being abandoned more and more.
