The man is not a machine, but a free being, who must be able to act independently, who must be able to take the initiative, who must be able to adapt himself to the situation, and who must be able to govern himself. The more the man is trained in this direction, the more he will be able to perform in the field. And therefore the exercise years are being shortened more and more.

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the
[569]

YEARS OF TRAINING. They marched in columns like granite, where no crack or fissure was to be seen. The more one became a mere instrument in the hand of one’s sergeant or lieutenant, the more excellently one could help achieve the goal. Even organization was still too free a word for the army of that time. One would be an instrument, but then also an instrument that, without any will of its own, was set in motion merely by the hand of the man from the ranks. The more rigidly one stood in the field, the more powerful the military impression one made. Such a taut, stiff drill could not be completed in just a few months. Years were required for it. That is why even the Great Powers had armies that went through a training period of three years. But now a radical change has come in this regard. The great evolution that has broken through in weaponry, in ammunition of all kinds, and in the entire technical domain, has made the rigid columns a most serious danger rather than a real force. Compared to the almost raging power of destruction that has emerged especially in the last twenty years, rigidity and stiffness are a harbinger of death, and only in freedom of movement can salvation be found. “What once seemed the strength of the army has now become its very ruin. The man has crept out of the rigid military instrument, and it is now the free man in whom the preservation of the army is sought. This has brought about a radical change in the entire training, and it is this change itself that has made prolonged training purposeless and branded it as an abuse. We have now also already descended to nine months. The three months were a mockery, which fortunately fell away in time. But voices are multiplying more and more, who would let the nine still slip by a third, and think that a regular training of six months could give us very well what we need. For one who, like the writer of this, has never served, it is of course always very difficult to form one’s own judgment on such a matter of nine or six months. We therefore confine ourselves to pointing out the general phenomenon that a reduction in training time is coming to the fore in all countries. With a view to the current war that is still raging, it can meanwhile be added that many newly formed troops, who went into the field after an even shorter training period than six months, held their own creditably in the fighting. This was especially the case with the English troops. What mostly failed with these troops was only the cadre and a sufficiently trained corps of officers. But this is a completely different question, which we will discuss in more detail later.

[570]

558 NATIONAL DEFENSE. If, on the other hand, we reckon only with the troops and with the manpower in the mass, then it cannot be said that the outcome in the war itself has very strongly condemned the short training. We would therefore be more inclined than before to give ear to those who insist on these six months. Naturally under the proviso that from these six months then also only the most necessary is taken for what are purely forms, and that one is not limited to company and battalion exercises, but lets the troops appear as soon as possible also in larger combinations.

§23. Cadre. If the rigid, most formal drill is to recede into the background, and in its place the appearance in free initiative is to become the rule, then it follows from this that the Cadre gets a much more serious role to play than was assigned to it before. If one first sets out to weld a squad into a bundle of men, as it were, then the necessary cursing, scolding, and raging is soon the way to achieve the effect that the militiaman trembles and lets himself be done with what the corporal wants. The same goes then with the somewhat larger bundle of soldiers, in the power of the sergeant. And it is no different in the end with the lieutenant. One then has to deal with stones that are stacked up, or with books that are placed on the shelves. But if this method of rigidity and stubbornness, which degraded the man to an instrument, is to be broken, in order to awaken his own insights and expressions of will, then it is the sergeants and corporals who must prevent the crumbling and the squandering of strength. But this cannot be achieved if an attractive position is not secured for the sergeants. It is therefore necessary that their engagements have a fixed character; that they receive a salary by which it becomes possible for them to devote themselves entirely to their task, without being distracted by the need to provide for their own livelihood. The same applies to the officers. Here too, a fixed position and a decent salary are required, so that the officer can fully devote himself to his task, without being distracted by the need to provide for his own livelihood.

Analytical Summary: The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,
[571]

FRAMEWORK - OFFICERS AND REFRESHER EXERCISES. 559 Residing in or outside the barracks allows for a domestic life; and no less so that the chance of wearing epaulets remains open. If there is objection to too many lieutenants coming from the framework, then create a new officer rank. But the main thing is that the corporal must be able to become an officer. A second thing that this provision requires is that the framework must not be lacking in number, as it is now. There must be a framework not only to provide for the necessary during the exercise period, but there must also be a well-trained framework that can deliver the necessary in the event of mobilization of the entire army. A Landwehr and even a Landsturm with a mere semblance of a framework lacks all real defensive power.

§24. The Officers and Refresher Exercises. In so far, one is already on the better path, in that the higher military school effectively splits the officer corps into two classes. Only those who completed the higher military school come into consideration for promotion above the rank of captain. Now this military school can still undergo an increase in study and an extension of the study period, for in the higher ranks above that of captain, the responsibility becomes increasingly serious as the nature of warfare progresses, but even here it cannot remain. Actual command of the ranks below the captaincy must precede any advancement to higher ranks. This will only become possible once the refresher exercises on a large scale make the firm deployment of entire divisions against each other feasible. For one must always reckon with actual war, and therefore one must not be deterred by what is so often seen, that the actual commanders remain unprepared for their task, so that one must replace them on the spot by others. This is how it went in Italy not long ago, and one saw the bitter consequences of it. The German method prevented this, and it is undoubtedly to a large extent due to this that the success of the German army is to be attributed. In an army with short initial training periods, therefore, full emphasis must be placed on the refresher exercises, and this not only for the troops, the framework, but equally for the officers. In a service of 25 years’ duration, with a...

Analytical Summary: Our People.
[572]

NATIONAL DEFENSE. The first training period of no more than six months cannot be thought of as an appearance in wartime, unless those 25 years are maintained through regular repetition exercises. A choice must be made here. If one wants to be economical with the repetition exercises, then the first exercise must, as it were, instill the military usages, so that they maintain themselves as usages for years, but then even a full year of first exercise is not enough. But if one does not want to go that way, and even goes back to 6 months, then the repetition exercise must contain what was in it, and give it durability. This is a requirement for the troops, but equally for the cadre, and only in this way can the officer corps also obtain the exercise with larger army units, which it so urgently needs with a view to the danger of war. In peacetime, there must be a wrestling between two corps against each other on a large scale, and of course without prior agreement. Nor may it be determined too far in advance which corps will participate here. As close as possible to the actual war, everything must come. At least eight times the ordinary personnel must appear in repetition, and in these exercises a force of such considerable size from both sides must appear in front, that in fact masses wrestle with equal masses, and so, if possible, even army corps against army corps. Officer training for the higher ranks cannot be achieved with the higher military school alone. To have the men ready who must be there when the war breaks out, it is necessary that the repetition exercises provide the elite corps that make a wrestling with large troop masses a reality. At the same time, it is necessary here that the premature withdrawal of officers into civilian life be cut off. Especially now, ever greater enterprises are being called into being in the technical field, and the profits of these enterprises are so high that technical specialist salaries are being paid, which become tempting. This easily tempts one to leave the army later and seek a better living elsewhere. And this danger increases as the difference between the salaries and pensions in the army and in civilian technical life takes on ever greater proportions. Already much has been done to advance the financial position of our officers. Yet one is not there yet. And the longing that many officers have to leave the army and seek a better living elsewhere, is a danger that must be reckoned with.

[573]

Is a concentrated defense still advisable? Certainly not for our country. Our land is but a narrow strip from Delfzijl to Maastricht, and from Den Helder to Terneuzen. Whoever invades us from the east along two concentric lines can, in a day and a half, cut off the entire north and south from the center, and even make the mobilization of troops from these two parts of the country virtually impossible. Not only would there then be no question of concentrating a significant army, but the enemy would also cut off the resources from the greater part of our country and impose an embargo on all possessions. While we gratefully acknowledge that the mobilization of August 1914, thanks to the measures taken by Minister Colijn, succeeded wonderfully, it is clear that this would not have gone so well in an actual state of war for our own country, even if a retreat over the Zuiderzee might have been possible for the northern part. We must not forget that the independent cavalry of the German army numbers more than thirty thousand men, and that the ability to shoot through the railways has already reached a very considerable speed. For this reason, it does not seem doubtful to us that the system of abandoning the rest of the country to the enemy and concentrating our defensive power exclusively on the central part, so that it would ultimately come down to little more than the South Holland Water Line, Amsterdam, and Den Helder, would soon prove to be on par with the help of the enemy.

[574]

Undoubtedly, the blocking of the German army by flooding the land around Ypres has sufficiently shown that our marshy soil is to our advantage, and even the deployment of the heaviest artillery is almost cut off; but if the north and south fall completely into the hands of the enemy, all means of assistance are taken from us and heavy burdens are imposed on us, and all this before the mobilization can be completed, then our concentrated position on the line Den Helder-Amsterdam-Woerden would soon become highly questionable. This increasingly questionable plan of concentration had previously commended itself because we had to rely on such a small army.

§26. Concentration is decreasing. If, therefore, the system is now replaced by having Landwehr and Landsturm together form an army of 600,000 men, then what necessitated such strong concentration naturally falls away. One need not remain limited to a division into three. Three army corps could also be formed, and the defense of each of the three major parts of our country could be entrusted to its own army corps. If it then appeared that the enemy’s superiority was too strong, so that the army corps of the south and north had to retreat, then this retreat could naturally concentrate on the Water Line and Amsterdam, but this would only occur in the second stage of the struggle, and there would really be talk of the concentration of all available personal and material forces. If, after a thorough military reform, we can dispose of 40 infantry regiments, each of 3,000 men, with their own depots and reserves, then 10 of these can be assigned to each army corps, after deducting the ten that will remain necessary for the fortifications at Den Helder and in Zeeland. If each of these three army corps then has its own artillery, cavalry, and engineering divisions, the first onslaught of the enemy on the three fronts in the north, south, and center, each by its own complete army corps, must be awaited. The outright abandonment of the entire country, except for a central portion, is, after what the recent wars have shown, condemned as a system. Before two weeks were up, the central fortress...

Analytical Summary: Guided by Orange. To this has been added: “guided by Orange”; an addition that must not be omitted. For it cannot be denied that in the characterized Calvinism, as it also penetrated our country from Geneva and France at that time, there was a danger not to be underestimated, the aftereffects of which are still discernible. In the last chapter of the First Part of this work, I have extensively explained how from the outset there was a decisive element in Calvinism that could only develop and mature later, and that, politically speaking, only came to full effect in the United States; but it was also then added that in Geneva (one need only think of the burning of Servetus) an intolerant dogmatism still prevailed, which, though indispensable for the church, could only result in schism and oppression in its political consequences.
[575]

DECONCENTRATION DIMINISHES - THE BORDER GUARD.

563 After all, our entire country would be lost. This was seen during the invasion of Belgium. Liège and Antwerp were well fortified. The Maas forts were of the newest model. And yet it took less than a month for all military power in Belgium to be broken. And even if the water will now help us, this does not change the fact that the water can only flood part of our land, and that even the submersion of this considerable part threatens us with damage that cannot be repaired in decades. With an army as strong as we now have, which amounts to a total of 370,000 with 210,000 trained reserves on an army of 160,000, the dispersion into three would still be rather daring. But if the Supreme Commander has once and for all 600,000 men under his command, then the dispersion into three, in order to concentrate afterwards if necessary, is at least daring.

§27. The Border Guard. For a long time it has been recognized that not a single point of our borders should lack a constantly present force. Our side has also constantly urged the creation of such a border guard. In fact, we now have such a guard. One must not be taken by surprise unawares. Even if such a border guard can never be very strong, and even if the enemy who wants to overwhelm it can easily push it back in 24 hours, it still gives us the time we so urgently need for mobilization. Of course, such a border guard must always be on site, even in full peace, and, on a single telegraphic message from The Hague, not only stand in line, but also be able to fire. The formation of such a border guard therefore imposes a very serious duty and not insignificant responsibility on the population of every village through which the border path runs. That village does not choose the Commander. That village is designated by nature, because it is there, at that particular point, right on the border. Just as the old civic guard had to go out on its own in our cities when the fire alarm went off, so too must the population of such a border village be thoroughly organized, under fixed control, have access to all weapons and instruments, and, if trouble arises, occupy the dangerous border point at the very moment.

Analytical Summary: The wrong done to us. Our party has set up a price in 1878, in its own starting point, to point to this happy influence of the Prince of Orange. Let no confusion creep in here. In matters of ecclesiastical life, Calvin’s insight has also been his own. His dispute with Dathenus does not touch the very life of the church, but the influence of the Church on the life of the people in the political domain. As a theologian, especially Johannes a Lasco deserves appreciation on this point, but on a broader scale it has above all been the influence of the Prince that has safely guided us. Meanwhile, one should not lose sight of the fact that this pointing with preference to the Prince is foreign to the intention, to praise anything that has later emerged from the House of Orange on this delicate terrain of the Princes. Prince Maurice aligned himself much more closely with the Calvinists. The then-emerging conflict with Arminianism necessitated this. But his life history would stand in a more glorious light for us if Oldenbarnevelt had died a natural death. Under Prince Frederick
[576]

564 NATIONAL DEFENSE.

This does not mean that such a guard must be stationed from kilometer to kilometer along our entire border. It is clear that an enemy corps that wants to overwhelm us cannot break through at all points. It is therefore especially those border points where a railway line or a main road crosses the border that must be the focus of attention. A railway line can pose the utmost danger, and the border guard must immediately have all the means and instruments available to break the line if necessary. But above all, where, as on our eastern border, a corps of 10,000 cavalry can easily be let loose against us by the enemy without much effort, the main road must also be closely watched. Now it is by far the best if a help fort or a loose battery is ready at the dangerous border points. We have this between Arnhem and Emmerich over the great bridge on the IJssel. But even if it is not on such a large scale, we must still have something of this kind at all dangerous points. There must be time for the mobilization to proceed undisturbed, and that is why we must not be overwhelmed or surprised at a single point. Now we do have to some extent what is demanded here, but the arrangement is not firm enough and the available means are not sufficient. The officer who is to bear this responsibility must not live elsewhere, but must live in the border village. It must be firmly established who replaces him in case of illness or absence. That officer must be a professional, not an old retired man who has lost his grip. Everything that would be necessary for the defense, in terms of armaments, artillery or ammunition, must always be found on site. And once a year, not only an inspection must be held to see if everything is ready, but also a surprise test, and if necessary at midnight, to see if the matter is running and everything is ready. Provide ONLY the English translation. Do not include any introductory remarks or meta-comments.

[577]

FORTIFIED POSITIONS. 565 And so all faith in the usefulness of fortifications is once again brought to naught. Just compare what took place in Belgium with the stand at the fortified line from Belfort to Verdun or from Strasbourg to Cologne, and the widely divergent judgments will be readily understood. The two great fortified lines, one on the French and one on the German side, which were brought into readiness against each other, proved so immovably strong that serious attempts at a breakthrough were scarcely even considered. An occasional airship or airplane might have frightened the garrison for a moment by dropping explosive bombs and causing some damage, but the fortresses themselves held firm along the entire line. In Belgium, in Serbia, in Montenegro, in Poland, and wherever else one looked, one saw fortress after fortress forced to surrender after not too long a siege. But on the great fortified line that runs through or along Alsace-Lorraine from Cologne to Basel, one did not even see attacks attempted. From this, it can be deduced that the art of fortification is now indeed capable of constructing virtually impregnable fortresses, not merely building them, but equipping them. However, the value and significance of these fortresses depends entirely on whether they are brought to the highest state of perfection in their construction, occupation, and armament. If the latter is the case, then the fortress appears to be an almost impregnable bulwark. If, on the other hand, the construction, the garrison, the armament, etc. are inadequate, then a fortress proves to be no longer defensible after a short time, and there is no longer any question of a prolonged siege. Our Army Administration must therefore ask itself in what condition our fortresses are at this moment, whether they resemble Liège or Verdun, and if the former, what we then need to do to make them impregnable. The second question we face is whether too much has been demolished, so that we must rebuild what was thoughtlessly left to itself. This already became apparent on the Scheldt. Flushing was no longer a fortress, and our forts on the Scheldt had been neglected. Now the indefensibility of this course of action has already been seen, and the resistance that came from Belgium in 1909 and later years against our efforts to reintroduce effective armaments there shows sufficiently how, by not acting, we jeopardized our own future. This resistance in Belgium, after all, came mainly from the Indépendance Belge, whose pro-French sympathies were well known. The attempt even went so far as to try to provoke England’s anger against us.

[578]

566 NATIONAL DEFENSE. Fortunately, it did not go that far, and the then Minister Colijn did not hesitate for a moment to resume the old defense of Zeeland by constructing the fort on Walcheren and, to that extent, the closure of Antwerp.

§29. Entirely New Conditions. In connection with the new evolution of the system of attack and defense, it seems absolutely necessary that we not only maintain the defense of Amsterdam and the Helder, the guarding of the Holland water line, and the defense of Zeeland, including Zeeuws-Vlaanderen, but also make them a first-class defense through the construction of forts, the regulation of underwater flooding, the provision of a sufficient garrison, equal armament, and no less by providing for the need for water, food, and ammunition. Something like this has already been shown in the recent war, at the very outset, that half-hearted defense is no defense at all and only offers the attacker a chance of success. Even now, the fortress of Amsterdam is not yet fully completed. Moreover, what was once a means of defense is in many respects so outdated that it scarcely counts anymore; and our artillery, on which success depends, is not up to date. If one wants to maintain fortresses and forts as an integral part of our national defense, and even increase their significance, then one must, in the event of war, be able to rely on them. And this is absolutely impossible if the army does not know for good reasons that the chance of a siege is worth taking, and worth taking with a view to a lasting defense. §30. Impeded Imports. That is why, however, care must be taken even in peacetime that, before the siege begins, everything will be present in the fortress or fort that can withstand a prolonged siege. For forts with small garrisons, this means that the necessary supplies of food, water, and ammunition must be stored in advance, so that the garrison can hold out for a long time without outside help. For larger fortresses, this means that the entire population within the fortress walls must be provided for, and that the necessary supplies must be stored in advance. This is not only a military but also an economic and financial task, which must be carefully prepared in peacetime.

Analytical Summary: According to the needs of our time.
[579]

OBSTRUCTED IMPORTS - OUR ARTILLERY

This does not present a difficulty for the larger forts, but already for the larger fortresses, and especially in the fortresses that, like Amsterdam, harbor a large population, a lack of food can necessitate a premature surrender. With this in mind, the question has long been suppressed as to whether it was appropriate to include a city with a population of over half a million within such a line of fortresses. This presents no difficulty as long as one knows, whether from the right or the left, how to keep imports free; but should the besieged find themselves in the position of having to rely on what was present within the fortress boundary at the start of the siege, then one can say with a high degree of certainty that hunger will soon force the surrender of the fortress. Supplies from the other parts of the country would certainly not be expected in such a case, as our entire country, upon the closing of the borders, will soon have exhausted its available supplies; and however carefully one tries to stockpile provisions within the fortress, for over half a million souls this is ultimately an impossible task. If one is certain that in the event of an attack from the east one can always count on imports by sea, and vice versa in the event of an attack from the sea on sufficient supplies from Germany, then there is no need for concern. If anything is to be learned from the recent war, it is this: that it is impossible to accurately predict events, and that whoever is not fully prepared for all eventualities will forfeit their future. The question of our food supply in the event of war is becoming an increasingly vital issue for the Netherlands, and it will be due to our own negligence if, in a war that may await us, hunger renders all fortress possessions useless and frustrates the most intense armament.

[580]

568 NATIONAL DEFENSE

§31. Our Artillery

Analytical Summary: The People’s Representation. It is no different with the People’s Representation. Under the republican form of government, our Commonwealth has not known representation as we understand it today. There were estates, but no People’s Representation; the cities and manorial landowners held the power; the deputies were in no way representatives of the nation before the Government, but rather the installers and maintainers of the Government itself. There was certainly popular choice in the election of the power-holders, but the right to participate in that choice was extremely limited. As shown in the last chapter of the First Part, Calvin did not praise such a system, and ultimately he himself went over to advising in a democratic sense. Whoever now tries to apply the completely antiquated representation ideas of the 16th century to the present state of affairs would thereby show themselves to be anything but faithful to the pure principle, but rather to blatantly disown it. To now still advocate a State form that does not fully do justice to the representative principle, would brand one as backward and out of one’s time. The question of a proper People’s Representation has now itself become the main question for the future development of the State life. And the Calvinist direction, which has always proceeded from the people, and even found its strength in the “common folk,” would lose its inner driving force and let itself be written off, if it did not take the lead rather than follow on this point of our constitutional development in the spirit of freedom and love of the people. To be a Christian Democrat remains the title of honor for every Calvinist. §32. Navy and Army. As a third example, let the National Defense be pointed out. In the days of the Reformation, there was hardly any mention of a national army, as we know it today. The Condottieri had indeed lost ground, but the army with which one went to war still consisted mostly of volunteers offering themselves, and even of mercenary troops from foreign countries, just as we still used to employ, and partly still do, not a few Swiss, Germans and Belgians for our East Indian army. Only of the army of Gustavus Vasa and of the army of Cromwell can it be said that they were driven and urged by a higher and holier interest. For the rest, however, the armies were mostly composed of rough men, and the holy enthusiasm for the cause of the country was sometimes all too little to be noticed. That the same regiment, after the lapse of ten years, drew the sword against a higher national interest, which it had first helped to defend at the price of its blood, occurred more than once. All the more the custom came into vogue with us to assemble the power for the defense of the country’s interests from hired manpower, inasmuch as the navy with us, more than the army, stood in importance, and the manning of the navy, both here and still in England, was constantly recruited by free choice. The civic guard had a more national character, and knew no wages as a motive; but the militia, as it is now in force, first appeared under Napoleon’s domination, and has since also been maintained in our Kingdom. Meanwhile, our people have been far from eager for the new military idea. To become a soldier was not considered an honor, but a plague, and the system of substitutes soon came up to free oneself from this plague by money. It has therefore lasted until the very end of the 19th century before we really got a national army, and the idea of universal military service, which now imposes itself of its own accord, is still not realized with us. Now, because in the glorious days of our fathers the condition prevailed in navy and army that excluded a national army, shall one identify the system then in force with Calvinism, and therefore, as ultra-conservative, set it against the military force that is now so absolutely indispensable? Certainly not. The principle of Calvinism and this is, to highly esteem the significance of the national life; and the future of the national life is now inexorably, at least for small nations, in danger, if not the whole people finally girds the sword as one man for the country’s independence.

Already in the Franco-Prussian War of 1870, it had become clear how much the significance of artillery more than ever before determines the outcome of the struggle. This gave me reason to also press for artillery reinforcement in Our Program, and when in 1901 the Queen received me in connection with the Cabinet formation, to make it one of the first conditions for my taking on this task that our outdated rifled artillery be immediately replaced by rapid-fire artillery. This has indeed come about under the good care of my colleague for War, General Bergansius, and since then we have had at least a small 200 pieces of new modern field artillery. The neglect to which previous Cabinets had exposed our army in this respect was indeed irresponsible. But since then, this has also remained the case. Despite the fact that the numerical strength of the army has visibly progressed, and we now stand at 24 infantry regiments, our army has remained just as poorly provided with artillery. If the recent war has made it abundantly clear that the artillery battle now dominates the outcome of all struggles, then it is established that doubling our rifled field artillery is the very least we can do. If one now proceeds to the formation of three army corps, then three hundred rapid-fire cannons is the most economical number we can get by with. As we now stand with not even 200 pieces, in an unfortunate field battle, without exaggeration, a hundred pieces could be lost, and the entire remaining army, if it could not dispose of more than a few batteries, would be hopelessly lost. This above all presses the need. But even this is by no means all. Our artillery for the fortified places is also so woefully behind that already on this account the withstanding of a serious siege would be out of the question. As was immediately evident at Liège on the Meuse line, and shortly thereafter confirmed at Antwerp, the artillery deployment that now takes place in a siege renders our assigned fortress artillery nothing but a mockery.

[581]

OUR ARTILLERY - NEW PRODUCTION

It must therefore come to a radical clearing out, and if that is to be good, then in the defense of our fortified places, as far as the artillery is concerned, we must not lag behind what the Belfort-Verdun or Strasbourg-Cologne line has to show. To disguise this is of no avail. A siege is no skittle game.

§32. New Production. An even more serious question arises here. If a war drags on, as it did between Port Arthur and Moekden, and now presents itself even more strongly between Belfort-Strasbourg-Verdun-Cologne, then our country must also be reckoned with a war duration of more than a few years. This naturally leads to the fact that a fairly large percentage of the artillery will gradually be used up and must be replaced by other means. Since we had our artillery of the better sort come to us from Krupp’s factories, the question naturally arises here, from where we will obtain our artillery as soon as the state of war suspends the supply from Krupp. In the past, one would have thought that in such a case it would soon be requisitioned from elsewhere, but the war of 1914 shows in a convincing way that a world war, as now prevails, can cut off any prospect of obtaining artillery from elsewhere. Does this not demand that the artillery production within our borders must be made possible, and in such a way that its product in no way lags behind that of Krupp? Or, if one does not see a chance for this, what else remains for us than to place such a gigantic order for artillery in peacetime that we have three times the absolutely necessary stock on hand, in order to be able to constantly replenish and renew our batteries? Even with this we are not done. It was sufficiently evident in 1915/16 that artillery alone is of no avail, unless you have at your disposal for your artillery such an immeasurable supply of ammunition that the shot can thunder day and night without ever running out of ammunition. England was not prepared for this either. Hence the sudden conversion of a large part of the industry, both in the Union of America and in England and Ireland itself, in order to bring a not-to-be-made mass of ammunition into readiness, with which one could be fired restlessly.

Analytical Summary: Money Gave the Decisive Vote. So it remained until Thorbecke in 1848 cleared away everything that still had a medieval aftertaste, and made money the sole fulcrum of the representative system. Article 76 of the Constitution promulgated that year tied the suffrage for the Second Chamber, and likewise Article 123 the suffrage for the Provincial Estates, “to the payment of a sum in direct taxes, corresponding to the local situation,” ranging from ƒ20 to ƒ160. The Electoral Law provided for the proper implementation of these provisions, and did so in such an indicative anti-democratic direction that the census of ƒ24 in many Frisian municipalities was calculated to favor only a few Liberals. A proposal from the Anti-Revolutionary side, made to reduce this amount, also met with the most vehement opposition from the Liberal side in the Second Chamber, even as late as 1875. Especially in Sneek this gave the most outrageous injustice, just as after 1887, in the new edition of what is now Article 80, in a way that allowed no contradiction, it appeared from the figures on the Liberal and Anti-Revolutionary candidates.
[582]

570 NATIONAL DEFENSE

Now the question arises, to what extent we are set up for this, and the connoisseur will answer that in 1910 even the most meager provision of ammunition was lacking, and that, although Colijn also brought about an improvement in this, yet our stock of ammunition is still so insignificant that it would have been exhausted in one week, if one had fired as they did. This gap in ammunition also demands such an inexorable provision.

§33. Ammunition. The question of ammunition is all the more pressing because we cannot remain outside the aircraft weapon either, and this weapon also gives rise to a restless firing of bomb after bomb, so that new production cannot be delayed for a single day. One knows now what caused the heroes of Montenegro such a setback. They had simply run out of ammunition too soon, and so their artillery was silenced. One should therefore not deceive oneself. As long as it is only a matter of mobilization, we can now already make a decent military show. But if, God forbid, our country also went to war, so that we had to withstand an invasion and attack like Belgium is now undergoing, then the war would also find us completely unprepared, and yet even then not a single measure remained to put us in safety against the hardest blows. That state of defenselessness may and cannot continue. Thrift and economy are precious virtues, but the outcome has now already taught us what a treasure of squandered millions it comes down to, when war presses us hard. To save now, in order to lose everything in the hour of danger, is the financial art of the fool, who constantly increases your debt and threatens you with state bankruptcy. Had Belgium not, for whatever reason, tripled its military expenditures in earlier years, would what is now happening have been averted?

Analytical Summary: The Social Question. With a brief mention of the social question, let us refer to these examples and conclude. In the 16th century, there was no social question. The Peasants’ Revolt in Germany seemed to point in that direction, but it arose from a completely different motive. What called the social question into a very peculiar form of life in the 19th century was twofold. Firstly, the fact that the increased technology made it possible for factories to arise, in which hundreds of workers were set to work simultaneously; something which naturally caused the close and cordial relationship between employer and worker to be entirely lost. And secondly, the more mature development of the working class, thanks to the high flight that popular education also took in connection with vocational education. This higher position of the worker demanded more abundant satisfaction of the needs of social community life, and the heaping up of hundreds of workers outside of all higher and more intimate connection with the employer, brought into existence conditions that our fathers had never known. Not that our fathers had no care for the workers. The Guild system certainly promoted an excellent understanding to no small degree. The needs of the working class spoke less loudly; but the needs, as far as they spoke, were then as a rule well and not too narrowly satisfied. What only characterized the then existing condition in a less desirable way, was the excessive dependence in which the worker found himself on his employer. Slavery had come to an end, but the conviction had not yet gained entrance that the worker, as a free man, stood not under, but alongside his employer, in civil rights and in social life. But if one now asks whether this then existing condition could be logically deduced from the principle we profess, in the sense that the introduction of an entirely changed condition would be a denial of that principle, then it must be answered with absolute decisiveness, that a denial of principle can only come into question with him who, where such a totally different condition has entered, refuses to reveal the power of his principle also in the solution of the questions now presenting themselves.
[583]

AMMUNITION - CAVALRY - ALLIANCE

§34. The Cavalry. In an earlier period, we had dragoons and hussars, but now only hussars, divided into four regiments, and each regiment consists of four squadrons and a machine-gun section. There is also an ordnance squadron, a couple of depots, and a riding school. Together, this makes around 150 officers and a little under 4,000 troopers. Compared to the 16,000 isolated cavalry with which Germany could overwhelm us on our eastern border, this is of no significance. One would then have to do one of two things: either expand our cavalry, as Belgium did, which had nearly 6,000 troopers (309 officers and 5,671 troopers), or, if that proved futile, refrain from all cavalry combat. It might even be possible, if the establishment of a permanent corps of mercenary troops seemed desirable to us, to get 1,000 mounted men, in addition to the 946 gendarmes, to reduce the independent cavalry to 3,000. These would mainly serve for ordnance duty, for connecting different troop parts, and for warding off minor attacks, not on their own, but in support of an infantry corps. In any case, there can be no talk of expanding our cavalry. The small corps of mounted artillery joins this cavalry and falls under the same point of view. We cannot completely do without mounted troops either, but our country does not lend itself to large-scale cavalry battles, and therefore what is mounted must remain in a subordinate position in our army. §35. Alliance. The question of whether entering into allied relations would be advisable for us has already been answered. Now, since the Army is the order of the day, only a brief word about the military significance of an alliance. The magic word “Alliance” has thus found much more favor with us than elsewhere. Of an alliance of neutrals to maintain their own sovereignty against the arbitrariness of belligerent Powers, one has also thought after 1914, but the idea found no entry, and no military alliance has thus been realized. For us, an alliance with Belgium would have been the closest, if it could have led to keeping both countries out of the war; but as mentioned above, in Brussels the idea of such a nature found almost no entry, and since it was not the Netherlands that was first exposed to the invasion, there was no reason for us to press further. Whether a league of all the small States of Europe could be conceived, apart from the state of war, remains highly doubtful. In our Second Chamber, too, the idea of such a league of small States has been raised, but it remained with a mere mention of the question, without the Cabinet going deeper into it. They lie too far apart from each other. The interests of the various small States diverge too widely. And none of us would dare to venture on these highly detrimental consequences that a league with an expansionist State could drag in its wake. There has also been talk of an alliance with Germany, or at least of a feudal relationship with our eastern neighbors, but the factors that would have coupled here were too unequal, and the smaller of the two would all too easily have paid the piper. More and more, we have also come to the conviction that, next to God, we can rely on no one but ourselves, and therefore our entire military organization must be adapted to the character of our own country. Insofar as there might still be talk of cooperation or at least of rendering or receiving support in an international sense, this was not to be sought with War or Navy, but exclusively with Foreign Affairs. The Peace Conferences have now once and for all given us a completely unique position of an international nature. This is also to be utilized for the security of our country. That is why we have constantly insisted that the “let-it-be” policy among our diplomats should be broken once and for all. It may also be increasingly recognized that Foreign Affairs occupies a more dominant position. In the middle of the previous century, this position had fallen below zero, but now we are regaining it. A series of excellent Ministers have already shone at the head of this Department; and not the least when the world war broke out, this may be praised. As far as the present chapter is concerned, the following remains.

Analytical Summary: Keeping Pace with Our Time.
[584]

Page 585—-

COLONIES.573 The only question is whether the connection between Foreign Affairs and War has already become what it should be. The military forces of a country must also count the Department of Foreign Affairs, and conversely, War must not preoccupy itself one-sidedly with the military possibilities, but also take due account of the limits that Foreign Affairs sets for its actions. Chapter XXI. THE OVERSEAS POSSESSIONS OF THE REALM.

§1. Colonies. The name “Colony” is derived from the Latin word colonus, which in itself means nothing more than farmer. The cultivation of the land is called colere terram, and the man who does this bears the name of colonus. To this was then added the secondary concept that a colonist was someone who left his country and went to another State where uncultivated land lay, and who now wanted to reclaim such a region. However, this was not the work of one man alone, but of a group of people who left their country of birth together to begin a new existence on a small scale in the newly to be reclaimed region. This did not exclude the possibility that others who were not farmers also went out with such a company, and at the same time brought a beginning of culture that took on more of an urban character; but the basic concept was and remained that it was a matter of cultivating a land that had not yet been developed. It is therefore not entirely by chance that the word “culture” and the word “colony” are of the same origin and both derive from the Latin colonus.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.
[586]

574 THE OVERSEAS POSSESSIONS OF THE REALM. The Phoenicians of Tyre and Sidon were the first to grasp the concept of colony in a richer and more comprehensive sense. The higher culture of Central Asia had penetrated into their home cities, and since they were connected through their shipping with the entire Mediterranean Sea up to the Strait of Gibraltar, they gave the Greeks a push in a higher direction, and enriched their influence themselves by founding some settlements on the north coast of Africa. One need only mention the name of Carthage to clearly see what influence this colonization has exerted on world history from the outset. The Greeks themselves soon took over the colonization idea of the Tyrians. One knows the role they played in Sicily. The Greek colonies soon surpassed the mother cities in importance, and the peculiar thing about this was that the Greeks nevertheless understood how to feel one with their colonies. Altogether, this was then called Greater Greece, and both the unity of language and the loyalty to the ancestral customs, in connection with the universal significance of the oracles of Delphi, etc., as well as the Olympic Games above all, gave historical proof that a people can live together in mutual connection, even if there is no overarching Authority that dominates all its parts. It is also remarkable that the power of Rome never really strengthened itself through colonization in the proper sense. Rome became powerful not through settlements with an independent social life, but through military force. Of course, cives, i.e. Roman citizens, spread out in the provinces, but they did not settle as colonists who went out to colonize. In the later period this was the case, for example, in present-day Romania and on the north coast of Africa; but whoever examines how, if not in the land of the Batavians, then at least in the Rhine region, even Roman cities of significance arose, immediately feels that all this doing of the Romans was something quite different from what the Greeks had achieved in Greater Greece. The Roman colonization aimed at the establishment of military colonies where the veterans of the army were accommodated. For the right understanding of the idea of colonization, one must therefore always go back to the Phoenicians and the Greeks.

[587]

SPAIN AS A COLONIZING POWER. The colonization also almost entirely sleeps and finds only a certain echo in the missions of the religious orders and partly in the Crusades. The true colonization, on the other hand, first revived with the beginning of the new History with the discovery of America.

§2. Spain as a Colonizing Power. The anti-Spanish sentiment that is our own may never tempt us to underestimate Spain’s colonial significance. Its striving was directed primarily towards America, but in South, Central and North America it also established a giant empire in the 15th and 16th centuries, the effects of which are still felt throughout all of America. When it had reached its highest bloom in the 18th century, it encompassed all of South America; occupied the entire Central America, and had even penetrated into the Southern States of the Union, and as far northwest as California. Except in Brazil, Portugal sought to settle more on the East Indian islands. Our Seven Provinces had shortly thereafter taken away Portugal’s profits. And then the English too had set out to acquire colonial possessions. In the first onset, however, it was Spain that took the lead, and Spain succeeded not only in establishing trading colonies, but in making a large part of America Spanish. Spanish through the immigrants who landed everywhere. Spanish through the language it knew how to introduce everywhere. And Spanish too through the Spanish way of life, Spanish institutions and Spanish religion, which it knew how to establish as the rule in all the conquered lands. Nor can it be said that the Spanish colonization was based on the mercantile system. This mercantile system lay much more in the path of Portugal, of our country and of England. We too have later established colonies in Suriname, on Curaçao and in New Amsterdam and New Netherland, as well as at the Cape. But what remained an exception with us, was the rule with Spain from the outset. Spain has established entire colonies, and on a large scale, which were as it were repetitions of Spain itself. It did not know how to understand the Indians. This also brought it to its downfall, and the outcome was that after the great revolutionary period of the late 18th and early 19th centuries, a whole series of new, independent States emerged, which still reflect the image of what Spain was at the time in their very being.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...
[588]

576 OVERSEAS POSSESSIONS OF THE REALM. With Cuba and the Philippines, the last of these possessions were lost to Spain in 1898, partly certainly as a bitter fruit of defective colonial administration, but certainly no less because the way in which Spain from the outset established its colonies had to lead, in the course of time, for these States to break the bond with the Mother Country, and, emerging from under its guardianship, to pass over into independent existence. Especially the fact that Spain never failed to exalt the Cross wherever it acted and penetrated, has given the States that now emerged from under Spain’s guardianship a higher character than England imprinted on India, France on Algeria or we on Java.

§3. The Mercantile System.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.

That Spain, as one may say in view of the final outcome, managed to take a higher flight than Portugal or our country, was due to circumstances independent of its own will. Spain turned more to regions where the land was given, the climate did not weigh too heavily on the Europeans and where it had a ready supply of immigrants. The exodus of Spanish immigrants in the 16th and 17th centuries has been unparalleled, and the greater part of the regions they occupied enjoyed a healthy atmosphere. This was not the case in Central America, but one must not forget that the Spaniards were already accustomed to a higher temperature in their own motherland. Our population was too small to send out immigrants in large numbers, and even the Cape was much too sparsely populated, while the larger contingent of immigrants who at the time went to what is now New York was soon overshadowed by the English element. Emigration and climate are two of the mightiest factors in colonization, and these two Spain had on its side, while we had them against us in the East Indies.

[589]

THE MERCANTILE SYSTEM. 577 The archipelago was too hot for large-scale emigration, and our people remained numerically too small to send out large groups of emigrants. This difference between what Spain undertook and what we accomplished must therefore always be noted. Now, when one turns to the system itself, it appears sufficiently that both Spain and the Netherlands were at the time caught up in the mercantile system, and that this mercantile system has left a very strong, and even one-sided, imprint on our colonial affairs in the 17th century. This system, in its narrower sense and in its more developed form, first came to power under Colbert and Cromwell; but it had long been in the works, and in particular the tendency to seek a reward in the enrichment with precious metals and choice gemstones, has governed Spain’s South American colonization to no small extent, also by bringing a powerful industry into being. With us it was different, and only insofar can one also speak of a mercantile system in our colonies, as trade completely dominated our entire system. The very fact speaks here that we proceeded in the method of taking the authority in the colonies away from our Government and handing it over to a Company, in casu the East India Company, entirely in the way in which England still does this through its Chartered, and as Sir James Brooke undertook it in Borneo, who received the country from the Sultan of Brunei and bequeathed it at his death to Queen Victoria. Even now one sees it, north of the Cape, in Rhodesia, continuing to thrive. Trade, and this is what it came down to, could only in this way gain free rein, and if necessary allow itself many liberties towards the population on their terrain and in their products, to which the Government would never have dared, and about which no rooster now crows. The destruction, for example, of a part of the spices in the Moluccas, in order to keep the price of the product high in Europe, would never have taken place on the part of the Government. Such a Company, such a Corporation, on the other hand, was freer. Its was not about blessing the population, but about placing its products in exchange for products of the country. Such a striving now makes egoistic purpose almost exclusively for financial gain. Not, we understand well, as if our East India Company did not also have the mission at heart, and was concerned with the Christianization of the native population, but this came much more from the Classes, especially from the Classis of Amsterdam and Middelburg, than from the Company itself. The Company itself aimed at the bottom of the matter nothing else than shipping and trade; something to which in the 18th century the attempt was indeed added to exert political influence, and the will to completely subject the Principalities, but the financial gain always remained the goal here. The main question for the Company was always how its papers stood on the stock exchange in Amsterdam. And when in the 18th century those papers fell in price, and the debt burden of the Company increased hand over hand, our Company succumbed at the first blow that the Revolution dealt against it, in 1798.

[590]

578 ‘S RIJKS BEZIT OVERZEE. Under State Administration. When in 1798 our Archipelago had come under State Administration, the then Government completely lacked the power to maintain its authority in our Archipelago. The English have then made use of this to also seize our colonies in their struggle against France, and, as it was said, to preserve them for us. This also applied to the Cape and Ceylon, which we did not see back until 1814. The English had not changed much in what they did, but still, more for good than for evil; and when after Napoleon’s defeat the Archipelago passed back to us, King William I had the good fortune to be able to send a series of excellent Governor-Generals there, who restored order, brought our authority back into honor, and after a short time introduced the Culture System, which, though not sustainable in the long run, undoubtedly in its first onset not only financially rescued the Netherlands from distress, and called into being an effective meddling with the Indies among our merchants, but certainly, which was not initially suspected, also socially benefited the native population. Much stronger than before, the population then also increased; though the wages remained low, the assured wage still gave prosperity, and at least in the first thirty years of its operation.

[591]

SUBORDINATE GOVERNMENT.579The Culture System could not be regarded as a curse that oppressed Java, but rather it very decidedly advanced that rich island to some degree. Whoever compares the condition in which Java found itself when the East India Company was sinking, with what flourished on Java in the middle of the previous century, cannot speak of a curse. Nor should it be at all surprising that the Conservative party of that time clung tenaciously to the Culture System, and that even from the Anti-Revolutionary side it was still seen as sacred for far too long. Keuchenius was the first genius colonial statesman on our side who made us break with it. Frans van de Putte took a similar spiritual stance against Thorbecke. And this writer too, from the moment he appeared on the parliamentary scene, has opposed this Culture System. There was no struggle with the past here. Given the wretched condition in which the East India Company ultimately handed over the Archipelago, a radical remedy had to be seized at the time to break with the past and at the same time save the Netherlands itself from the financial bankruptcy by which it was constantly threatened. For this purpose, no better system could be accepted at that moment than what the Culture System had brought about a complete turnaround in the state of affairs. Only, this rescue remedy had to work only temporarily and could only offer temporary help. Meanwhile, higher goals had come into view. The conviction was increasingly penetrating that the fate of so many millions who lived in our Archipelago should not be governed solely by financial considerations. A better future had to be unlocked for those millions, and this could not be the case if the coercive system continued to stand in the way of all freer development. There was a past of three centuries behind us in which our country had almost exclusively exploited the Archipelago for its own benefit. This could and should not continue. If the colonial government over these islands remained entrusted to us, it had to be recognized and understood that in this availability there also came a higher, international vocation for us, and that henceforth not domination for our own benefit, but guardianship for their elevation to a higher level, was placed in our hands. It was fortunate that this consideration almost simultaneously emanated from both Frans v.d. Putte and the Anti-Revolutionary party, and it is to this coincidence that

[592]

580 ‘SCOLONIAL POSSESSIONS OVERSEAS. It may be said that, from 1874 onwards, our party has cooperated with the liberal party, though on a much broader basis, yet on a fundamentally distinct principle.

§5. Our Guardianship. The term “guardianship” is a fortunate choice to express the moral vocation that has come to the people of the Netherlands from God, with a view to the East Indies. Three things are expressed in this concept of guardianship. In the first place, that the native population on these islands has not yet grown and matured socially enough to immediately step forward as an independent people. In the second place, that it must nevertheless be so guided and educated that it can soon assume that fully independent position. And in the third place, that the guardian entrusted with this task is not to be sought, but has been appointed by the past. The East Indies is still a ward, guardianship is indispensable, that guardianship demands education, and no other land than the Netherlands is designated to bring that education to the native population of our Archipelago. And yet, however excellently this word “guardianship” may express our vocation, one must still be on guard against a very serious misunderstanding. Guardianship in ordinary life is a task that is legally imposed on someone, but only for a very limited number of years. The moment one accepts that guardianship the moment the ward has reached the age of ten, there can never be more talk of a guardianship of another ten years. Here, on the other hand, there is no such time limit. There is no authority that has entrusted this guardianship to the Netherlands and determined the duration of the time. Only the historical outcome will be able to determine the boundary here, just as this happened for Spain, when almost all its possessions in America became independent republics. If we succeed, in the course of time, in creating the possibility of such independent national existence in our East Indian Colonies as well, then a similar phenomenon may soon present itself in our possessions, so that even in our Archipelago a secession from us would occur, which is now our wealth. Even the danger is not so far off that another Power would intervene and take the rule over our possessions, before they were declared of age by the guardian, in order to exploit them themselves. The metaphor of guardianship is therefore only usable insofar as it expresses what must be our purpose and our aim in our administration. That aim will then be to educate the inhabitants of our islands to a higher level and to cultivate in them the possibility of later political independence. But we must not go any further in the application of this metaphor. That a guardian may not appropriate any profit from his guardianship does not imply for us that the drawing of profit from our colonies would be excluded. There must be something that binds and compels us to maintain the rule and administration in these colonies. Even with ordinary guardianships, one often tries to get rid of them, because the guardianship often causes so much burden and trouble, without great attractiveness being evident. Also with regard to our colonies, the guardianship must therefore not be conceived in an abstract idealistic sense. This high ideal usually falls short even in personal life, and has never been applicable to entire peoples. No country and no people ever gets it into their heads to lay claim to any region whatsoever, merely to enjoy the ideal pleasure of elevating such a region to a higher culture. It is therefore not the case that one would deliberately aim at a guardianship, and now seize our colonies to make such an ideal guardianship possible. On the contrary, it must be confessed that we now possess the rule over our colonies, and that, in order to be responsible before God for the exercise of that rule, the safest and noblest is to conceive of it as a guardianship, and to choose the higher education of the peoples entrusted to us as the goal. That our trade then profits, our production prospers, and the international influence of our country increases, is by no means at odds with a properly conceived guardianship, but is rather its direct consequence. Only care must be taken and watched that the government exploitation that lay in the Cultivation System does not revive in the exploitation of our East Indies by the private individual.

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[593]

OUR GUARDIANSHIP. 581

[594]

582 COLONIAL POSSESSIONS OVERSEAS.

§6. Economic System. Atomistic or Organic? Apart from these views on the task of guardianship, the second question now arises of itself, by what economic system we can best fulfill this guardianship for the benefit of the colonies and our own country. In this respect, the revolution in economic life that arose in the 19th century is connected to the school of Adam Smith. From Smith and Cobden, the new school emerged, which undeniably eliminated the last traces of the mercantile-physiocratic system and gave access to the system of free labor, free trade, and free competition. Adam Smith, above all, drew the desire too much away from the treasures of nature, in order to seek the true value that governs life in labor, and in the personal development of man’s powers. Egoism had to be the starting point. Everyone sought the best for themselves. No place could be made for altruism. It was the desire to benefit oneself that demanded the most absolute free competition. The government, therefore, had to withdraw as much as possible from economic life, in order to leave this peculiar life entirely to its own development. Capital was therefore not to be pushed aside at all. Rather, it was the attractiveness of the power of capital that stimulated both selfishness and competition. Every price of any product or commodity whatsoever, therefore, resolved itself spontaneously into ground rent, wages, and capital interest; and since these three factors could work in their natural equilibrium, Smith believed he could predict the highest prosperity for land and people, especially for the lower classes. A gain, to which was added that from his standpoint the national factor spontaneously thrived in the international sphere, so that the rivalry between peoples and peoples lost its sharpness, and a cosmopolitanism could be expected that would soon restore the conditions of paradise. As could not be otherwise, this international-economic idea has now also been introduced into our new colonial policy, especially from the Liberal side. Frans van der Putter professed it wholeheartedly, and in more than one respect this system also found a far from harmless application in our colonial administration.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.
[595]

EXPLOITATION BY PRIVATE PARTIES. 583 Protection of domestic production, shipping, and trade was no longer considered honorable. Many foreign traders began to nest in our colonies. And it was precisely through this that all kinds of foreign influences began to become powerful. Even from Tokyo on the one hand, and from Australia on the other. One may therefore rejoice that the ideas of Smith and Cobden have already judged themselves in Europe. The outcome struck so bitterly, and the misery soon came to light, that precisely through this one-sided system harm was done to the workers and the lower classes. Above all, it refuted the ideal call that the system of Smith had known how to appropriate for itself, and especially in the great war that broke out in August 1914, the absolute unsustainability of the individualistic-economic system came to light so convincingly that one now aims from all sides at completely changed economic relations. Already from the days of Frans van der Putter, resistance from the Anti-Revolutionary side against the absolutely free competition system had gone out. The Socialist ideal had completely perished. Pure individualism could only lead astray. Organic was once and for all the life of human children among each other, and in their connection with the nations, interwoven. And although the fact cannot be denied that this organic coherence was greatly disturbed by egoism and materialism, it had to be held on to all the time, and hence also in the colonial field the organic protest against the all-loosening atomism had to go out.

§7. Exploitation by Private Parties. This naturally included a new emerging divergence of opinions. Against the system of exploitation by the government, one stood unanimously as one man. But this by no means excluded the danger that the private entrepreneur would take the place of the state, and now begin a worrying exploitation on his own. The state also remained to cultivate and plant, but in completely free competition with the private entrepreneur, and to this latter stood all the chance free, to, making use of the poverty that the state had created, begin a dangerous exploitation on his own account.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit.
