This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[596]

The fruit of overpopulation was that labor forces, agricultural and forestry lands, and the treasures beneath the soil had to compete with the State. It is well known what treasures of millions and again millions have been amassed through the tobacco cultivation in Deli, through mining exploitations, and through factories in our East Indies, by private individuals and then carried off to the Netherlands or even to other countries. This could not continue indefinitely on a completely free footing. However rich the treasures that could be gathered from the soil and from beneath the soil, everything could not simply be emptied out, as had already happened with the tin in the East Indies, leaving behind a plundered land and an impoverished and powerless population. Hence, the path had to be taken to embrace State exploitation, albeit as a private enterprise, on a broad scale, and to set such limits and levy such rights in the private exploitation of the individual enterprises, that the use of the land would not degenerate into abuse, and the previous condition might ultimately prove to have left open the possibility for later, even higher prosperity. All the more reason, then, to post a sentry here, since the impoverishing private exploitation can be driven not only by Dutchmen, but also, as the situation now stands, foreign capital can penetrate among us, to the detriment of our colonies, and thereby also to the detriment of our own country, more than doubling itself. Fortunately, in the most recent period, we have also found the better way in this important respect. The exploitation of the treasures to be won from our colonies is increasing in many ways. Government and private enterprise are competing with each other here, and the use and exploitation is being brought under such firmer rules that the State’s treasury is prospering, waste of resources is being increasingly cut off, and there will be no more talk of any abuse of the population in the ever richer mining and cultivation. Only in one respect does a not insignificant danger still threaten. The Chinese labor force is of a higher caliber than that of the ordinary natives, at least for the time being. This easily tempts the private entrepreneur to summon a more dexterous instrument from China than is available to him here on Java or Sumatra in the native population. Here, however, strict measures must be taken

[597]

THE KINGDOM. 585 looked upon. Not as if every foreign worker were to be excluded. That does not happen in the Netherlands either. But it must be closely watched that, in order to make an enterprise more profitable, we do not later increase the national danger from the side of China.

§8. The Kingdom.

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.

The first article of our Constitution underwent a considerable modification in the revision of 1887, in so far as now also mention was made of “the colonies and possessions in other parts of the world.” Even in 1848 this had not yet occurred. Article one then only contained that “the Kingdom of the Netherlands consists of the present provinces.” It was only added: “in Europe.” This already then left room for the idea that the Kingdom also consisted of territory outside of Europe, only it was not yet expressly stated. This addition came only in 1887, and with this peculiarity, that now in Article 2 it was declared: “The Constitution is binding only for the Kingdom in Europe,” while in section 2 it was determined: “Wherever in the following articles the Kingdom is mentioned, only the Kingdom in Europe is meant.” Against this manner of expression there is no overriding objection, provided that the distinction between the Mother Country and its colonies and possessions is clearly kept in view. Also in personal life one can say that a person does not appear only in his person, but as a person also possesses the garment he wears and the household goods that serve him, and if he has possessions, also of that property or of securities and monies. One sees this with the highest placed. This does not take away, however, that between the person and his possessions or property there always remains a principial distinction. He can lose all his goods, yet he as a person remains, and conversely he can die, yet all his goods remain available. And so it is here as well. When one speaks of the Kingdom of the Netherlands as an international appearance, then naturally everything is included that this Kingdom possesses. In the colonies as well as on the palace in the Residence the Dutch tricolor flag waves, as an indication of the authority. Yet always in such a way, however, that something quite different and distinct is meant, if one has our country and people themselves in view, as the subject that possesses, or the colonies in the Far East that are an object of possession for our Kingdom. Imagine that all colonies were taken from us, yet the Kingdom would still remain; but imagine, on the other hand, that our country in Europe disappeared, then our possession in the Indies would not be “The Kingdom of the Netherlands.” Here in Europe the supreme authority is exercised by the grace of God by our Queen, in the colonies, on the other hand, the authority of the Kingdom of the Netherlands applies, and the authority of the Governor-General counts only as granted by the Government of the country. The country in Europe is and remains the actual Kingdom. This Kingdom has possessions in the colonies and conquered territories. The administration of these possessions is entrusted to the Government of the country, in connection with the States-General, and the authority of our Sovereign counts here only in the sense that she is the God-ordained and constitutional Head of the national government. The defect in our Constitution is therefore not included in Article 2. When one speaks of the Kingdom, one also counts our possessions. But it is a mistake that in Art. 1 mention is made of a Kingdom that includes the possessions. In this lurks a confusion of concepts that is not permissible. Properly speaking, the entire expression of Kingdom should have been avoided. Elsewhere one also speaks of the State, and one should have used this word also in Art. 2, so that all difficulty would have fallen away. No part of the Kingdom can be placed outside the Constitution, and therefore the provision in section 2 of Art. 2 already indicates in itself that the Kingdom as such is only the Kingdom in Europe. A Constitution is no Constitution, or it must go over country and people. To say, as is now done: There is a population of 45 million, the Constitution applies only to 6 1/2 of these 45, but not to the remaining 39 million, is a legislative absurdity. Not the Queen, but the Kingdom has colonies, and what one has, that one is not.

§9. The Administration.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.

The administration in our colonies is entirely separate from the administration over our own country and people. The Department of

[598]

586 THE KINGDOM’S OVERSEAS POSSESSIONS.Colonies and the Department of the Interior are two entirely distinct departments, each with its own minister. The Governor-General in the Dutch East Indies is the representative of the Kingdom, not of the Queen. The Queen’s authority in the colonies is only in the sense that she is the constitutional Head of the national government, which has entrusted the administration of the colonies to the Governor-General. The Kingdom has its own colonial policy, which is not identical to the domestic policy for the Netherlands. The colonial policy is determined by the Government in connection with the States-General, and the Queen’s authority in this is only in the sense that she is the God-ordained and constitutional Head of the national government. The Kingdom has its own colonial budget, which is not identical to the national budget. The colonial budget is determined by the Government in connection with the States-General, and the Queen’s authority in this is only in the sense that she is the constitutional Head of the national government. The Kingdom has its own colonial legislation, which is not identical to the national legislation. The colonial legislation is determined by the Government in connection with the States-General, and the Queen’s authority in this is only in the sense that she is the constitutional Head of the national government. The Kingdom has its own colonial administration, which is not identical to the national administration. The colonial administration is entrusted to the Governor-General, and the Queen’s authority in this is only in the sense that she is the constitutional Head of the national government. The Kingdom has its own colonial jurisdiction, which is not identical to the national jurisdiction. The colonial jurisdiction is exercised by the colonial courts, and the Queen’s authority in this is only in the sense that she is the constitutional Head of the national government. In short, the Kingdom has a colonial domain that is entirely distinct from the national domain, and the Queen’s authority in the colonies is only in the sense that she is the constitutional Head of the national government, which has entrusted the administration of the colonies to the Governor-General.

[599]

ADMINISTRATION.587 The Department of Public Works in The Hague has no say over public works in Java. The same is true of the Department of the Interior, of Agriculture, of Justice, and so on. In contrast, there is a completely separate Department in The Hague for the colonies and possessions—and whenever this Department must consult with the Department of Foreign Affairs or the Department of the Navy on matters pertaining to the colonies, it remains a distinct Department that bears responsibility for our entire administration in the colonies, across all domains. Alongside this colonial administration in The Hague, there is also a distinct administration established in Buitenzorg, Paramaribo, and Curaçao; and in our Archipelago, this administration is concentrated in the Governor-General, who represents the national government in every respect. This Governor-General, in order to exercise this administration, has a departmentalized bureaucracy under him that serves to advise and execute the various branches of this service. However, this naturally raises the question of whether this administration ought to be so concentrated, or whether it would be better to introduce a significant decentralization here. Thus, strong centralization and concentration in Buitenzorg has been the guiding motive. The Governor-General, with his Council of the Indies and his departmental chiefs, governs the entire Archipelago. This dates back to the days of the Cultivation System, when it could not have been otherwise. In contrast, the entirely different purpose that now animates our administration of the Indies requires us to consider whether the bureaucracy that is the necessary outgrowth of all this concentrated administration does not stand in the way of exercising a guardianship that is truly beneficial for the population. The growing population also pushes us in this direction. The masses are becoming too large. And this is true not only on Java, but elsewhere as well. Thanks to the actions of General van Heutz, and under him of Colijn, the Outer Possessions have begun to make themselves heard and to demand that they too be taken into account. Under the Cultivation System, and the multifaceted bureaucracy that emerged from it, the Outer Possessions were, it must be said, effectively neglected. In a period when The Hague did nothing but ask what the revenue from the Indies would be in the coming year

[600]

588 The Overseas Possessions would have flowed into our Dutch treasury, had the Overseas Possessions not been a superfluous burden. People had no heart for them. They did not care for them. They had no affection for them. But it was just as self-evident that the abandonment of the Culture System and the consideration of our Archipelago as an extremely important possession, both for our international influence and for our trade and shipping, as well as for the exercise of a higher guardianship over 30, soon 40 million souls, had to bring about a complete change in our ideas about governance. Especially under the governorship of Van Heutz, this awareness penetrated ever deeper. The evil that was seen ever more clearly in that so strictly centralized bureaucratic school. And so, almost of itself and gradually, even from Buitenzorg, a completely different view of our Archipelago outside of Java has emerged, and the idea is gaining more and more ground to divide our Archipelago into a certain number of provinces, each with a permeating independence. Yet the name of Province has not been included in this line of thought, and reference has been made to the appointment of several governors over their own “governments,” but this only makes a difference in name. The Romans divided their foreign territory in the same way into parts, but then also gave those parts the name of province. We could have done the same, and it would have been clearer to our ordinary public what was meant by this division. For the time being, we still have only one Governor with his own Government on Sumatra. If it is good, then one will soon have to proceed to not leave even Java undivided. According to the population, Java is divided into three major ethnological provinces. In the West live the Sundanese, in the East the Madurese, and in the middle of Java the original Javanese of pure blood. These three ethnic groups do not belong together, and should stand apart. Over each of the three parts, a separate Governor, under the Governor-General, could then be appointed, and a separate development of these three well-distinguishable parts of Java could be promoted. The other islands outside of Java would also gradually, like Sumatra, have to form their own Government under their own Governor.

[601]

589 FOREIGN GUESTS. The most natural for this would be, next to Sumatra, due to its colossal dimensions, Borneo, except for Sarawak on the north-west coast, where Sir James Brooke managed to obtain his chartered princely domain. In the third place, Celebes with the smaller islands belonging to Celebes would require its own Government. And finally, the Moluccas with the Lesser Sunda Islands and Timor should in the fourth place acquire their own position. So we would obtain seven provinces or autonomous Governments, and the all-suffocating and deadening breath of the monopolistic bureaucracy would no longer emanate from Buitenzorg. There would only remain New Guinea to dispose of, a massive island that in its dimensions is even the largest island in our world and has an area of 805,541 sq. km., but is so sparsely populated that it is usually estimated at not even a million. This gigantic island belongs to us for more than half, while the other half is divided between England and Germany. In fact, however, we have nothing of it, while with a view to Australia, it could very well pose a danger to us in the future. There would therefore be no objection whatsoever to letting this piece of New Guinea go and, if possible, exchanging it for the Portuguese half of Timor. Our entire Archipelago would then form a well-ordered whole, and the hindrance that the half-and-half situation on Timor still constantly causes in the south would have disappeared.

§10. Foreign Guests.

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.

In our Archipelago, along with the natives, live Chinese who have come from elsewhere, and on a much smaller scale also Arabs. The Japanese are mainly represented by an unfavorably known female personnel. Furthermore, more and more English and German merchants have settled, especially on Java; but among the foreigners in a narrower sense, it is still the foreign Orientals, and among them again the Chinese, who stand in the foreground. The influx of these Chinese to our Archipelago was already long ago explained by the double circumstance that China, due to its overpopulation, has a constant need for emigration, and on the other hand that the Javanese remained too one-sidedly an agricultural people and therefore did not in itself provide for the need for labor.

[602]

THE OVERSEAS POSSESSIONS. 590

The technical needs of social life could be provided for. Just as the English, the Chinese also penetrated everywhere. They have few needs. They are quick and skillful in small-scale enterprise. And above all, they are excellent financiers. Just as now among us the Jews, especially in Amsterdam, but also gradually further in our country, managed to take up a peculiar position through small-scale trade and the money business, so it also went in our Archipelago with the emigrants from China. The danger they began to pose was only due to their much greater number and their understanding of the art of exerting a certain social dominance. Although one need not be concerned about the Arabs, the Chinese question has become a serious social problem. We cannot ban or expel them; but anyone who remembers with what pretension Li Hung Chang once appeared in Buitenzorg, and reckons with the steadily increasing influx of Chinese, cannot conceal that it must be with open arms to receive every Chinese out of necessity, and that our Government must set limits and bounds to the arrogance with which, especially in recent times, a flood from Further India has moved towards us. Henceforth, a sharper distinction must be made between the descendants of the Chinese who have long lived among us, and the newcomers. Order must be established on their admission. The school system must be organized separately for them. Those who come to work temporarily must also disappear again after the end of their period. In short, we must remain master and lord on our own inheritance even over the Chinese. They may profit from our lower middle classes through their small-scale trade, but the regulation of admission and legal status must remain with us, not with them. There can no longer be any talk of encouraging their immigration. Their immigration must rather be tempered. Just as in America they first did everything to attract immigrants, but now they are hitting the brakes, so too our line of conduct towards the Chinese in the Indies must be.

[603]

THE INDO’S. 591

§11. The Indo’s. The Indo’s in our Archipelago are what the Mulattos are in America. They are sprung from mostly sinful sexual mixing of incoming Europeans with native women. This evil did not just arise now, but has tainted our colonial life from the outset. It went quite differently at the Cape. What is called the Boers was a Dutch tribe that never intermarried with the negroes. The English did this, at least in part, but the Boers from the Netherlands did not. In Java, on the other hand, the sinful habit crept in from the outset, that one went there unmarried and immediately began living with what was then called his “housekeeper,” who soon brought children into the world, conceived in immorality. This sinful custom crept in among officials and merchants, among soldiers and officers. In short, all who came from elsewhere and did not separate themselves as believers, participated in this sinful procreation of a mixed race, and hence there are two distinct groups among these Indo’s to be distinguished. On the one hand, there are Indo’s who had a father of high intelligence and ability, and on the other hand there are Indo’s begotten by lower-standing, clumsy natures. The former get ahead, the latter continue to struggle, and yet it is precisely from these latter that a certain reaction emanates, which tries to gain acceptance for the idea that they are the natural lords and masters in our Archipelago. They see in themselves a kind of nobility, native in origin, but elevated by Culture and enriched with a spiritual treasure that came to them in the blood from Europe. For a time it indeed seemed as if a formidable force would emanate from them. But soon it became clear how, just as elsewhere with such mixed races, so here too no life-giving initiative could come from them. One will do well to teach them how to provide for their own social needs, but should they ever attempt to assert a completely empty pretension, energetic suppression of the evil will be required in the interest of the mother country and of Java.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[604]

592 COLONIAL POSSESSIONS OVERSEAS.

§12. Industry. If there is ever to be any talk of a higher social development in the more populous parts of the Outer Possessions, at least in the larger cities, Java cannot remain at the agricultural level. Everywhere—in Asia as in Europe, in America as in Australia—agriculture was the starting point, but agriculture alone cannot suffice. If a people is to awaken and unfold the dormant powers within it, and thereby naturally attain a higher standpoint, it is absolutely necessary that the agricultural part of the population separates itself off into an industrial and commercial part. That industrial part will always remain much smaller, but on the other hand the emergence of a somewhat significant industry always has the consequence that there arises a need for industrial workers; that these workers receive higher wages than the farm hands; and that in this way a historical process occurs such as was the case with us. If this does not succeed, and such a transition from rural life to industrial life is lacking, then one of two things happens: either the population remains forever on a lower level and does not progress, or else foreigners from outside penetrate and take the industrial activities under their control. The people then remains on the lowest level of development, and strangers come to socially dominate it. Now it is not to be seen why the population of our Archipelago, especially on Java, should be doomed to such an unhappy lot. History testifies that from India, under Buddhist leadership, a not insignificant unfolding of higher powers once took place on Java. One still sees it, at least in the centers, not only in the Javanese man but even in the Javanese woman, how a higher civilization once penetrated. They are extremely polite and accommodating, more refined than coarse in their forms, and one knows what power has emanated from Java up to the 14th century. Whether Islam is also partly to blame that all higher civilization has since sunk, let that be as it may; but the historical fact is that the Javanese have once experienced a period in which they had raised themselves above the peasant class. Whoever compares our Dutch petty bourgeois who went to Java in the administration with the Javanese, cannot but confess to his shame that the Javanese surpasses our lower officials in cultivated forms. Even in the missionary era the same complaint was heard. There is therefore no conceivable reason that could prevent the population of Java from becoming ripe for a higher social standpoint again. Already now there are three distinct social strata on Java, with the two more aristocratic groups appearing alongside the lower ones, which our government also honors as higher classes. But it must not remain at that; the industrial development of Java must be prepared by the Government with great caution, with a gradually rising policy, and through constant assistance. An industrial bourgeoisie, if we may so express ourselves, must arise, which gradually takes a higher standpoint and in this way prepares, and ultimately promotes, a higher social development, not of foreign intruders, but of the descendants and relatives of those once so highly standing Javanese. To elicit this, and not to rest until it is achieved, will be one of the noblest obligations that our guardianship imposes on us.

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[605]

593 INDUSTRY. - EDUCATION.

§13. Education. One of the most self-evident means to this end is the establishment, both in Java and in the Outer Possessions, of a school system suitable for and adapted to the needs of the Indies. Lamentably, what a gigantic misunderstanding the revolutionary monomania of our liberal rulers has allowed to creep in here. Deeply rooted in the core of the French Revolution is the deceptive slogan of “liberty, equality and fraternity.” The curse of the then newly emerging life was uniformity. All people over the whole earth were to be one and the same. According to one method, one system, along one and the same line, all that was human, over the whole world, was to be formed and educated. One constitutional system was to serve for all government. And so also one educational system for all schools. Our School Act of 1857 also drove this for the Netherlands, and once the disappointing fruit of this State...

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.
[606]

Sadly, the wretched law that was foisted upon us, that the perfect method of school education had now been discovered, made it obvious that the Liberal agitators would now also try to drive through the very same method for the East Indies on the colonial front. And as nonsensical as it may now seem to us in hindsight, it is historically certain that for half a century the salvation of the East Indies has been seen from the Liberal side as lying in a precise and strict imitation in the East Indies of our liberal lower state school. Fortunately, people are now beginning to see the foolishness of all such efforts even outside our own circle, and it can now be safely said that the development of the native population in Java has been hindered not so much by anything, as just by what has been set in motion from the Liberal side to promote that development. In particular, the social democrat Mr. Van Kol has lashed out at this senseless drive of our liberal spokesmen; and above all, Minister De Waal Malefijt has practically shown us the better way. A Javanese, and in general an Easterner, must go through a completely different kind of lower school than can be of service here at home. If anywhere, then in Java the strong penetration of the intellectual element is completely misplaced. The Easterner develops not prosaically, but poetically. Not at all merely in his brain, but always much more through his imagination. In Java, all education must appeal to the imagination, and already in the schoolchild, put the power of imagination into practice. Not for a moment may education in the East Indies be separated from life. All abstraction is dead to the Eastern spirit. Neutral education is a contradictio in terminis in every land of the East. It is as if one were to speak of dry waters and the cold of the sun’s glow. Then it must also be grasped, the higher the better. The entire native school system must be directed towards a reformation that adapts it to the nature and character of the Eastern soul-existence and to Eastern practical life. And nothing is more inexplicable than the sad fact that there are still teachers from Christian homes who have offered to lend themselves to this Indian mimicry of the School Act of 1857. Shortly, when we come to the mission, we will return to the school question. The East Indies must come to a higher development, even a university education must grow up there; only, whether it is a question of lower, middle or higher education, all

[607]

THE CIVIL SERVANTS. The copy of what blossoms among us must be most strictly excluded from the Indies. Whoever wants to help the Indies must immerse themselves in the Indic blood as if baptized, in order to learn to feel, realize, and see for themselves what corresponds to their spiritual life. Hence, the idea must increasingly come to the fore that the teachers themselves may soon be of Indic origin and may be formed in the Indies. In particular, we must not make a mistake with the promotion of Higher Education in the Indies. We now already have, as a preparatory step for this purpose, the medical school that trains Javanese doctors, a law school, an agricultural school, and instruction in Language, Land, and Ethnology at Section B of the Gymnasium Willem III. These schools too must acquire an even less European and much more strongly Indic character. Only then can faculties gradually emerge from these preliminary schools. And so, in the end, a university institution will emerge that, being Indic in nature, can respond to the needs of the Indies.

§14. The Civil Servants. “For the administration of our colonies, the strength must be sought not in the multitude, but in the instruction, the quality, and the proper placement of our civil service personnel. As for the instruction, it must be firmly established that no official invested with authority shall have anything to do with any industrial enterprise or government cultivation; nor shall there be any of the ever-fatal blending that merges the ruler and the judge into one person; but there shall be solely administrative acts, to maintain our high authority and to uphold our moral prestige. No less do we emphasize the quality of our civil service personnel, which, much more than here at home, sets the highest demands. Nothing is more questionable than the delusion that the Indies can get by with less than we do. On the contrary, since in the Indies the control of public opinion is so much less, the task so much more comprehensive, and the temptation to abuse so much more ready, each man who represents us there must possess a probity of character and moral earnestness such as the ordinary class of people neither here nor anywhere else offers. It is also on policy and maturity of judgment, rather than on knowledge, that it comes down in such an exceptional situation; and a government that, to win these precious ingredients and, once won, to preserve them, does no more than our Department of Colonies has done, will certainly not acquit itself of its duty. And then the placement!

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”
[608]

596 THE KINGDOM’S OVERSEAS POSSESSIONS. It would be an illusion to imagine that the Netherlands can continually provide this good personnel in sufficient quantity to occupy the entire administrative framework of the Indies. Against the system of repeatedly bringing a branch of administration under European personnel, we therefore place the principle of taking the “Spitze der Behörde,” as our neighbors call it, exclusively from the Mother Country, and of allowing the service to be managed under them by well-educated and strictly disciplined sons of the indigenous aristocracy. But then this line of our administration must also extend down to the ordinary dessah-head, that is, these village chiefs may no longer remain what they have so far been: a sheep-dog used by us to keep the herd tame; but they must become persons who, under supervision, exercise a well-defined authority and are paid on a fixed footing for their services.” Thus we expressed ourselves in 1878 on the civil service personnel. In the opinion then expressed, we feel obliged to persist. Only a brief word has been added, that more than hitherto will have to be done to increase the number of Christian confessors. The late Rev. Rudolph even called a special institution to life for the promotion of this. It is by no means intended hereby that less competent and less ready candidates should nevertheless be placed because they are orthodox. The government may never lower the standards to be set, but two things must apply. First, someone must no longer be placed behind because he is orthodox, and secondly, the government may no longer tolerate in its civil service circle a conception of personal and moral life that falls short of the honor of the Christian name. In particular, in the missionary residencies, it will be desirable to entrust men who are warm for the mission with higher authority.

[609]

MUNICIPAL COUNCILS

§15. Municipal Councils. The establishment of Municipal Councils deserves applause, provided that one also holds these in the Eastern manner, and does not also make the fundamental mistake in this establishment of wanting to transfer our Dutch Municipal Councils to Java. Even if this were possible, one could never imitate what is useful to us in the initial period. One would have to go back to our Municipal Councils of the 15th and 16th centuries, or even further back. We also did not start with what we now have, but rather gradually climbed up to this. So too in the Indies it will have to go, otherwise the institution will not take root in the peculiar soil of Indian life. It already follows from this that for the time being, only in the larger cities can there be talk of a real flourishing of the Municipal Council system. In places of lower order, there is not yet any question of this. And on the countryside, even the end of this century will not yet see its appearance. That in these Municipal Councils, as they now establish themselves in the larger cities, a blending of the two elements, the European and the Indian, must occur, goes without saying. With the population figure, no equal step can be kept. Even with inferiority in number, the European part of the population in places like Surabaya and Surakarta still commands the authoritative majority. So far, the establishment of these Municipal Councils has not disappointed. They bring more order to local administration. They promote hygienic interests in a very open manner. They bring institutions into being that are of undeniable significance. The paving and even the architecture improves. And financially, they proved to be equal to their task. Although one may not expect more from these Municipal Councils than they can give on such a weak footing, yet the influence that emanates from them can be quite desirable. They give, if only this, a new blow to the age-old overly centralized Bureaucracy.

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[610]

598 THE KINGDOM’S OVERSEAS POSSESSIONS

§16. The Suzerainty. The word “Suzerainty” has not been introduced into our Indian governmental parlance, but the thing itself we also possess. Suzerainty is distinguished from Sovereignty in this, that the Suzerain Authority holds the Sovereign in its power, yet allows a certain degree of autonomy under its rule. Especially in our circles, one knows the Princes of Yogyakarta and Solo. They still wear their Sultan’s diadem. They still maintain an Eastern court ceremonial. And they even dispose of a military honor guard that also serves as a police force. Especially also on the Outer Possessions, our custom has been followed to subject the existing Principalities to our supreme authority, but to do this in a contractual manner. In such a contract, the heads of such groups then acknowledged the supremacy of our authority, but on the other hand our Government also granted a certain degree of autonomy to these Principalities. As always in such engagements in Eastern lands, this autonomy soon proved to be little more than nominal, and it can hardly be denied that more than one Resident has thought to improve his reputation in Bogor by succeeding in further obscuring this autonomy. But even on this limited footing, such autonomy has nevertheless contributed to preventing the wings of the central Bureaucracy from unfolding too broadly. This was of less importance as long as in Bogor, upon closer inspection, there was nothing but very reprehensible indifference towards many of these Outer Possessions; a fate that especially on the small islands struck the population and even on Celebes worked through in the non-Christian regions of this island so rich in potential. In this shortcoming, however, a thorough change came about of itself, when gradually quite different, higher-ranking ideas took the lead in the Central Administration. The idea of guardianship naturally brought with it that one began to pay attention precisely to those regions that had been so neglected from of old. Especially General Van Heutz has the honor of having moved powerfully in this direction. Illegible traditions of yore have now emerged from their parchment.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.
[611]

Sphere Sovereignty - THE CHURCHES. Although the number of the baptized in our archipelago may still be relatively small when compared to the total population, nevertheless, the founding of all kinds of churches has already taken place to a not insignificant extent. First, there is the official Protestant church of the Government, with its governmental superintendents and its governmental financial support. Then there are the various missionary churches, established by the different missionary societies, each with their own character and their own organization. And alongside these, there are also the independent native churches, which have emerged from the bosom of the people themselves, without any foreign influence or support. All these churches, each in their own way, have a certain sovereignty within their own sphere. The government superintendents may exercise a certain supervision, but they do not have the power to dictate the internal affairs of the churches. The missionary churches are dependent on their home societies, but within their own circle they have a certain autonomy. And the independent native churches are completely sovereign within their own domain. This diversity of churches, each with their own character and organization, is a healthy phenomenon. It is the natural result of the principle of Sphere Sovereignty. The government should not try to force a single, uniform church organization upon the entire population. Rather, it should respect the autonomy of the various churches and allow them to develop freely within their own spheres. Only in this way can the religious life of the people truly flourish. Forced uniformity stifles the organic growth of the churches and leads to a lifeless formalism. But where there is freedom and diversity, the churches can blossom and bear fruit according to their own unique character and calling.

[612]

The administration of the overseas territories, their governmental salaries. There is also, on a not too large scale, a certain number of Roman Catholic parishes. The Reformed churches from the Netherlands also succeeded in establishing a few churches in common in our Archipelago. More than one mission flourishes in an ecclesiastical community of its own order; for example, the Rhenish Mission among the Bataks and others called a peculiar kind of semi-inland church into being. Mennonites settled on a small scale. The “Salvation Army” established institutions there. And in Batavia, a Lutheran church has even been incorporated into the official church. If we now add the many mission fields that have been established by the Mission up to New Guinea, then we get a scattered whole of all kinds of formations, so that there can be no question of a well-ordered state. Now all other churches are free and autonomous, and as a rule receive no financial support from the Government; something which we sincerely hope will remain so. For only what can thrive in freedom and live from its own, appreciating resources, blooms on the ecclesiastical terrain. What is still lacking for these free churches is that they are not recognized by the Government in their ecclesiastical character, and are still too often registered as a kind of associations or societies of moral order. The great difficulty, on the other hand, which the ecclesiastical life in India still poses too much for the Government, lies in the Protestant State Church. For this church is a governmental product of entirely unchurchly workmanship. It is an imitation and product of what King William I established here at home as the Netherlands Reformed Church, but in an even more unthinkable state. What was created here at home still nominally connected to the past and to the Formulas of Unity. But even this was allowed to slip away in India. For convenience, a new kind of more general confession was pushed under the churches established there, and thereby completely broke with what, in the heyday of the East India Company, had been called into being on Java and in the Moluccas, partly even on Celebes, in conformity with our ecclesiastical life here at home. One wanted to show a religious character as a government itself, too, over against Islam and Animism. This character had to bear the Christian stamp by itself, but that Christian name was then taken as generally as possible. It would be a

[613]

The Government Church of the officials and merchants from Europe has now become a church of a different kind. This was still possible when a certain degree of religiosity still lived among them. But as unbelief took on increasingly brutal forms, and the official personnel either became completely indifferent or even slipped into Theosophy and Buddhism, this Government Church soon fell apart entirely, with no more faithful preaching to be found, and the ministers were completely divided into the various official ranks, as if it were a purely civil society. Now Mr. Colijn, and Minister De Waal Malefijt, and Governor-General Idenburg, have made an attempt to put an end to this misery, and even a highly important report has been issued by a State Commission, but the difficulty is still far from resolved. At most, a mere handful of local churches have been fished out of this deluge, to which a certain degree of independence might be attributed. In all the small towns, whatever is called a church naturally falls away as soon as the Government lowers the flag for the Confession and withdraws from it. But even those five local churches had to draw an ecclesiastical life from their own roots, and this is extremely difficult. The historical line has been broken in the 19th century. And in the end, nothing else will remain but for the Government to withdraw completely, capitalize its salaries, and then await what new ecclesiastical formations may arise from the religious life of those who still live in faith.

§18. The Mission.

Analytical Summary: A Happy Turn.

In the Mission, which we are only discussing here from a political point of view, the Government has the highest interest. Our future as a colonial Power is increasingly at stake. That future appears so extremely dangerous precisely because we are increasingly dealing with an Asian action that is preparing to wrest itself free from European supremacy. In this action of the Asian instinct, there is unity. It works from Mukden and Port Arthur all the way to Singapore and Timor. Japan has taken the lead in this action. And if this struggle between the Asian and the European element continues, we know in advance that we too will become the child of the reckoning, and that we will only be able to maintain ourselves in the Archipelago with an effort that is almost beyond our strength. Even the innate antipathy of the Javanese towards the Japanese will ultimately not be able to protect us. The Asian motive is of a general nature, and all minor differences will disappear in this mighty contrast. Now there is, as everyone admits, only one bond that can even connect the inhabitants of our Archipelago to the Netherlands in the face of the Asian movement, and that is the unity of Christian confession, the Christianization of the population and the connection of the natives to the Christian Church. From Ambon and Minahasa we get our most loyal troops for the Indian army, and it is in those same regions that the greatest interest in what the Netherlands experiences in the House of Orange is still to be observed. From this it follows that the Mission has not weakened the position of the Government, but on the contrary has strengthened it in a completely unique way. If the Christianization of the entire Archipelago had continued, we would not have had the slightest concern for our colonial possessions. That is precisely why it is so short-sighted and politically unwise if the liberal statesmen transfer their anti-Christian motive from the Netherlands to the Indies, look askance at the Mission, and do what they can to break the influence of the Mission. We will never ask the Government for money for the Mission. That would be contrary to our principle. But in itself, the Christianization of the Archipelago lies so much in the surest preservation, that the Government could not do better than to throw its millions at the Mission, if by doing so the Christianization of the entire Archipelago could indeed be achieved. If a Government understands its own interest, it must see and acknowledge that it can never be friendly and favorable enough towards the Mission. If necessary, the Mission does not need the Government, but the day may come when the Government will have to thank the Mission for the only remaining prospect of its continued existence. There is no better friend and no mightier support for the power of the Government than the Mission, as soon as the Asian prudence awakens.

[614]

Page 615—-

THE FOUR POINTS

§19. The Four Points. If you now ask what the Government can do for the benefit of the Mission, I would point to the following points. First of all, the treatment of the Mission by the Government and its officials is always as favorable as it possibly can be. The missionary must, in all receptions, intercourse, and contact, be certain that the Government holds him in high esteem in the eyes of the population. Secondly, the Government takes care that a Mission does not have to struggle with finding land for its establishments, schools, and churches. Thirdly, the Government makes it clear that there is a bond between our Authorities and the Christian natives that goes even beyond the family bond. And in the fourth place, the Government does what is feasible to promote in every way the school education that emanates from the Mission. A missionary school, provided it is pedagogically on par, must be held in higher esteem by our Government than a neutral school of the same order. In no way may the missionary school, or in general a Christian school, lag behind in the eyes of the Administration compared to the neutral Government school. With a full hand, all Christian schools must be compensated for what they spend on the Christian upbringing of the natives. One evil must be guarded against. The Government must never promote the violation of the freedom of conscience of the natives. It must therefore be firmly stipulated that the Government will never financially assist any school where a child of non-Christian parents would be forced to participate in Christian religious services. A father or mother who rebels against this from their own inner drive must be absolutely certain that this coercion will be removed. If the complaining parents do not, the Government need not interfere; but if a protest comes from the parents, then the last penny must be returned from every school that would want to violate the freedom of conscience of the child and its parents, especially in the prayers and hymns. On the other hand, the Government leaves the Mission completely free in what education it provides, and the parents must know this. We would even prefer to see the Government consult with the various Missions to regulate the distribution of mission stations and to guard against the waste of missionary forces. Those who for the time being expect the first missionary blessing are the Animistic inhabitants of the Outer Possessions. It has always appeared that Christianization here progresses much more smoothly than in the land of Islam. Even our fathers understood this and profited from it. And now especially the Mission must devote itself to this terrain, because the faith in their own religion is declining even more strongly among the Animists than before. But for that reason the Mission must not withdraw from the Mohammedan regions. The Government must never, as in earlier times, obstruct the missionary men who operate among the Muslims. The Christian protest against Islam must be audible on Java itself, and the choice of Yogyakarta and Solo was therefore excellent on principled grounds. A mistake was made when, even bypassing the Animists, the side of the Reformed churches almost exclusively devoted its missionary work to the Muslims. I have also protested against this error on my part. It seems, however, that this mistake can no longer be remedied. The damage from it is incalculable. And that is why I continue to plead for the adoption of a vigorous, decisive mission also among the Animists. If one deems it necessary to continue undiminished on Java, so be it; but then redouble your efforts, and also extend the mission’s blessings to the Animistic regions.

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.
[616]

§20. Our Army. As for the defense of our colonial possessions, two things naturally come to the fore. First, the danger of rebellion, which is never imaginary in conquered territories, and secondly, the possible attack from the side of a foreign Power. The first danger has diminished. More than before, we are now masters over our entire colonial territory. What still causes difficulties now and then are the Chinese who have infiltrated our territory, both of old descent and of more recent arrival, and further the tendency that now and then even arises on Java to free themselves more; something that then mostly finds support among the Indo-Europeans. Even as recently as two and a half years ago, there was still a serious uprising on the island of Lombok, which was only suppressed with great difficulty. But these are exceptions. In general, the internal security of our colonial empire is now firmly established. The second danger, however, that of a possible attack from abroad, is still very real. The colonial army, therefore, remains indispensable. But here too, the Government must be on its guard. It must not allow the colonial army to become a state within the state, nor must it allow the colonial army to be used for purposes other than the defense of the colonial empire. The colonial army must remain strictly subordinate to the civil authority. And the Government must also ensure that the colonial army does not become a tool in the hands of certain political parties or factions. The colonial army must remain neutral in political matters and must not be allowed to be used for party purposes. The Government must also ensure that the colonial army does not become a state within the state, but remains strictly subordinate to the civil authority. And the Government must also ensure that the colonial army does not become a tool in the hands of certain political parties or factions, but remains neutral in political matters.

Analytical Summary: The three types not equally characteristic. But this does not mean that, in the course of time, each of these three types has in turn dominated the national condition: the Roman Catholics in the Middle Ages, the Calvinists in the 16th century, and the French Revolutionaries after 1789. It does not at all follow from this that they are equal in national significance. Undoubtedly, the Seven Provinces, once living a common life with Flanders and other southern regions, have enjoyed a high and flourishing position and have gradually taken an increasingly important position in Northern Europe; but it must not be forgotten here that the national energy was at the time much more strongly developed in what is now called Belgium than in the northern provinces. Neither Amsterdam nor Rotterdam could in those days be placed on a par with Ghent and Bruges. The wealth of medieval life had more stamped its imprint on the national character in the South than in the North. The reference to the rich medieval type therefore has much less significance for the present Kingdom of the Netherlands than for the western part of Belgium. And in the second place, it must be added that at that time the unity of our national life was much less consolidated. Under the Burgundians, the Dutch provinces did not occupy an independent position, but were united with what lay outside our later borders; and under Charles V and Philip, we even came, though only in a personal way, into a Union with entirely different states from Central and Southern Europe—something which could not but be detrimental to the confirmation of our own national character. Whoever investigates the historical significance of our lower lands may well find the period we lived through in the Middle Ages highly important, but nevertheless hesitates for a moment to acknowledge without reservation that our glorious period dates from April 1, 1572. Not in the Middle Ages, but in the period of the Reformation, lies the high point of our national history. It cannot be emphasized enough how much scrupulous fairness is required in such a comparison, and we shall beware of the assertion that from the Calvinist side the correct toll of reverence has always been paid to the medieval period in our history, to which they undoubtedly had a right. But it stands to reason that from the Roman Catholic side, too, we have not always been done justice. It cannot be valued highly enough that especially in the second half of the 19th century a more impartial judgment gained influence. Just with a view to this more unanimous judgment, it may now at least be established that not only the foreign historians, one may well say without any exception of importance, honor the period after 1572 as the highest and most glorious, but that also within the country the best interpreters of each of the three types increasingly come to the unanimous conclusion that the world significance of our small country came out most richly in the heyday of Calvinism. This in no way denies that our national life in those days of our glory also showed dark sides. The resistance to the monarchical state form, and even against the House of Orange, has almost become a source of our unhappy discord until 1789, and it is impossible to say how much higher our country would have soared if immediately after the separation from Philip II the crown had been offered to Prince William. It had to come to this in 1813; but who calculates what national suffering would have been spared us if we had taken this important step two centuries earlier. But even if one plays open cards and takes into account all the suffering, oppression and restriction of rights that the temporarily dominant group has inflicted on the other two groups, it is historically established that we were never before 1572 what our national life was in the period between those two dates, and never again after 1789. To top it off, the fact that we offered a refuge to the politically and religiously persecuted from all countries is proof that the actual life here was a life in freedom, and that the publications and ordinances that claimed to infringe on this freedom had more the effect of satisfying formal demands than of inflicting suffering on those who deviated.
[617]

OUR ARMY - ALREADY OBSERVED EARLIER. 605 The ebb and flow of this kind of movement in our Indian life on Java has been observed earlier; most recently again in Djambi, but through the wise policy, combined with the necessary resilience, of the then Governor-General, Mr. Idenburg, and likewise of his successor, it has been successful in putting this movement back on a better track, so that what can be beneficial to Indian life remained, while what could lead to discord was averted. Hence, although the general Asian resurgence weighing down the situation must also be pointed out here, it may still be stated with a fair degree of certainty that at this moment, at least, there is no serious danger of an Indian revolution against our Dutch authority as such. Throughout our Archipelago, it is known that both our army and our navy have all the necessary strength to repel any resistance and subdue it. There is as yet no talk of a self-emancipation by force of arms in our Archipelago. For the maintenance of our Dutch authority, a navy of five cruisers and an army force of around 30,000 men is quite sufficient. Quite differently, however, the question of Defense stands in our Archipelago, if one is intent on defending against military action from the side of other Powers. In “Our Program” of 1878, in this connection, the danger that could threaten us from the English side was exclusively pointed out. It is true that during the great war here at home, a pamphlet did appear from the Anti-Revolutionary side, in which it was suggested as if the political line, as we thus set out, should always align us with our sympathies on the side of England; but in this brochure of Mr. Koffijberg, it was entirely overlooked that the Netherlands has never yet been threatened or deprived of territory or disadvantaged by Germany, while conversely England has always been intent on diminishing our colonial possessions. It only needs to be pointed to the Cape and Ceylon to make clear how our decline in power, especially as a colonial Power, has been brought about solely from the English side.

§21. Already observed earlier. This is not just now, but has been observed by us from the very beginning.

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over
[618]

606 THE KINGDOM’S OVERSEAS POSSESSIONS. This was done in particular in §279 ff. of “Our Program,” from which we must quote what we saw regarding Defense. We wrote then: “And when one finally comes to the highly important question of the Defense of our overseas possessions, everyone agrees that only towards England is our vigilance constantly required. From other powers we have nothing to fear, since England would never allow our colonial possessions to fall into the hands of France or Germany. And the chimeras, nurtured four years ago before the mobilization of a Turkish fleet to Atjeh, will no longer be shared by anyone now that this Turkish fleet has itself shown its insignificance even against Russian ships of much smaller tonnage! But England is dangerous. Dangerous because of its almost unlimited assimilative power and its insatiable greed, which with the Transvaal and Afghanistan has by no means reached its last bite. But this influence would become especially dangerous if the antinomy between St. James and Berlin-Petersburg were to develop even more sharply, and we, lying between the two, sought cover of certain boundaries. Then England would well again want to render us the good services of the beginning of this century: “to take our colonies into custody.” But just this prospect also raises the question whether we would not then show more prudence by timely understanding with another Power to whom we could entrust our possessions in custody if necessary. Just by its own precedent, England has made our right to this, both as regards ourselves and as regards that other Power, indisputable. Which power would come into consideration here is easy to guess. And if, by treaty, for such good services (if it remained only a custodial task) in money, and, if war were on our account, in land, we were to pay a proper reward, one must not forget that England simply took the reward for its services, and would take it again at its own discretion; and one must not lose sight of the fact that the mere knowledge that such a treaty existed would easily deter England, just in order not to allow a third power to arise in the East Indies, from an attack on our colonial possessions.”

[619]

Even stronger now. It is worth noting how, some twenty years later, the tension arose here at home due to the attitude adopted by England towards the Boers in the Transvaal and the Orange Free State. It is hardly conceivable that the brilliance of the war fire in 1915 could have blinded some of our wise men, so that siding with England, at least as far as our sympathies were concerned, was demanded as a requirement.

§22. Even stronger now.

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the

This further explanation of the matter does not detract from what was written in 1878 regarding England; rather, it receives even stronger emphasis. And the striking difference with the position taken then is not at all that we can now count on England, but much more in the entirely different circumstance that, in addition to England, an adversary, perhaps even more worrying, has been sighted on the horizon. Not in Turkey, which in 1874 sent a fleet to Tandjong Priok to lend support to certain desires inspired from Cairo, nor in China, which under Li Hung Chang did indeed stand up for its countrymen, but as a government always held back, but from the side of Japan. As recently as February 1916, reports came from Japan in the press, testifying to an ever-increasing desire in Japan’s leading circles to enrich themselves through the occupation of the southern Archipelago. We knew about this, and had long been aware of the danger from that side, but we had never imagined that the danger from that side had already taken on such a serious character. And if one now knows the tension that has arisen over the waters of the South Seas, between America, with China, against Japan, then it is clear that the Netherlands would completely misunderstand its vocation if it did not ask itself in time how it could defend itself, if sooner or later trouble came to us from North Asia. Then, of course, a fleet of five smaller, unequal cruisers, and an army of 30,000 men, would be more of a farce than a defense. In this respect, something has already been gained, in that it is increasingly being considered to concentrate this small army in Java and to make garrison service in Bandung a high-altitude and healthy one. This path will have to be followed more and more, and the maintenance of authority on the other islands will have to take on an increasingly police character. But this cannot remain the case.

[620]

608 OUR OVERSEAS POSSESSIONS.

If an enemy force of 50,000 men were to land on Java, which at present is far from unthinkable, our army in Bandung would soon be surrounded, and if no provision were made for the necessary provisioning and ammunition production, surrender would soon have to follow. Three demands follow from this. Firstly, that the police force in Sumatra, Borneo, Celebes, the Moluccas and the Lesser Sunda Islands must be properly organized and equipped. Secondly, that the position in Bandung be properly reinforced and provided with what would be indispensable in a prolonged siege. But also, thirdly, that serious consideration should be given as to whether the plan already proposed from more than one side for the realization of a militia, recruited from the indigenous population, under European command and under European officers, would not be capable of providing us with a real military defense force in time of war, by placing two hundred thousand men at our disposal. If the core element from Ambon and Celebes were strongly enough represented in this militia’s cadre, such a militia of indigenous personnel would very well be able to organize a real force of defense. Provided, and this must be emphasized with the utmost emphasis, that everything necessary for the armament, leadership, provisioning and ammunition of this militia was made available in peacetime, not halfway but completely.

§23. Our Fleet

Analytical Summary: The fundamental tone. This is what Article I of Our Program means by speaking of the “fundamental tone” of our national character. By this, no single group is done any injustice. It is not said that our direction represents the only tone, the sole emerging trait of our national character; rather, a place is left open for every other group. Our national character is not monotonous. It is not a national character that is weary of rigid uniformity. Many tones resound in our national character. But not each of these tones is equal in weight. There is a distinction in the sounding weight of each type of tone. There is even among these varied tones one tone that may be considered the fundamental tone. And now it is stated that this fundamental tone is the Calvinistic one. Not as if our direction could boast of the most numerous following. We have already expressed that the opposite is the case. More than one-tenth of the principled adherents of our direction cannot be estimated either in the 16th century or now. But the fact remains that it was only when this fundamental tone began to sound powerfully that our Dutch people, in separation from the South and in distinction from the German element, came to full independent formation, and the national unity in anxious,

Much more difficult to answer is the question of how we will defend ourselves at sea if, sooner or later, a naval attack from North Asia should come. We might then find ourselves facing an enemy force that already has 19 Dreadnoughts at its disposal, while we have nothing comparable to oppose it. The only way to meet this threat would be to build a fleet of at least 10 Dreadnoughts, supported by a number of smaller cruisers and destroyers. But the cost of such a fleet would be so enormous that it would be impossible for the Netherlands to bear it alone. Here, too, the question arises as to whether the time has not come to seek cooperation with the other powers that have interests in the East Indies, in order to jointly provide for the defense of this region. This would not only lighten the financial burden, but would also give greater weight to our voice in the councils of the great powers.

[621]
