OUR FLEET. 609 It still has four Super-Dreadnoughts under construction, and moreover 22 cruisers totaling 136,000 tons, accompanied by torpedoes, airships, and patrol ships, and what not. A complete fleet with over 63,000 men on board, drilled by 2,500 officers, with a very extensive staff and armed with the best artillery. On the other hand, we are of course completely powerless with our insignificant European fleet. Compared to a serious foreign fleet, what we can still put under steam is like a mouse before a cat. We would be gone in no time. The rescue that could be brought would have to be sought in three ways. First, in the submarine. This new invention does not exceed our financial strength in terms of construction costs. The crew of the smaller submarines rarely exceeds 30 men. With three thousand men, a fleet of a hundred submarines could already be manned, and repeatedly it has been shown that “a valuable fleet of Dreadnoughts does not readily venture to be shot down from below.” In this, therefore, our salvation may lie. But then this submarine fleet must also be fully up to date, must be manned with excellent personnel, must have its stations on all islands and coasts, must be able to put out at least a hundred ships, and in the East Indies even possess all the shipyards and all the factories for the technical aids it needs. Counting on supplies from the Netherlands may not be the case in any case. Already now we saw that we had to avoid the Suez and had to sail around the Cape, but in wartime even that sailing around the Cape would once and for all be cut off for us. Therefore, it must also be demanded in the second place as a definite requirement that we fully establish ourselves in the East Indies, if possible even in the field of shipping, but in any case for the complete repair of the ships that need to go to the shipyards. What we now have in terms of shipyards is completely insufficient for this. What we need are at least three first-class shipyards, equipped with skilled personnel and all the necessary aids. Only in this way is the deployment of a hundred submarines possible, but also only then can one think of possessing in our East Indies a fleet of Dreadnoughts. However, the question arises here whether, in the event of war, this would not be entirely repeated what is now experienced with the English and German Dreadnoughts, namely, that on both sides these mighty monsters are locked up in a ready harbor and leave the fighting to the smaller ships.

[622]

610 OVERSEAS POSSESSIONS OF THE KINGDOM. This consideration would nevertheless be of very serious influence on the determination of the number of Dreadnoughts. One might well be satisfied with a half dozen. But in the third place, it is never lost sight of that our Archipelago does not lie isolated in the South Seas, and that therefore a not insignificant part of our defense will always have to be sought in diplomacy. Even if there need be no talk of an alliance with America, it would still be reckless to proceed as if the United States did not exist. Unofficial discussions could take place that, in case of need, could yield valuable prospects. If it is known in North Asia that the government in Washington would never tolerate an unrestrained Pan-Asianism, this would already provide a valuable support point for us. Even without an alliance, such a sympathy for our international aims can easily create a community of interests. Of course, this in no way relieves us of the very heavy task of arming ourselves independently; but precisely because it is known that we can bite off from ourselves if necessary, our diplomacy also gains in importance. We will not go into the costs of a serious armament on land and at sea any further. Any estimate is at this moment a puzzle. Only this is clearly stated, that, although the Netherlands is obliged to pay from its own funds what it does in the East Indies to maintain its authority, the much larger sums that will now be necessary for the defense of the East Indies against a foreign enemy will nevertheless fall for the greater part on the East Indies account.

§24. Suriname and Curaçao. Here we can repeat what we wrote in 1878 about our not so small colony in the northwest of South America: “Our past in Suriname was highly interesting. After all, Suriname belongs to our oldest acquisitions; it is not less than five times as large as the whole of the Netherlands; it had years of production to a total (at the present monetary value) of more than thirty million; it knew its time when it produced more staple products than all of Java.

Analytical Summary: Our People.
[623]

SURINAME AND CURAÇAO.

This colony may surpass the East Indies in political significance as an American colony; it is noticeably closer to us; it has in the stations of the Moravian Brethren a model of successful missions; and in the future, as the Archipelago comes under the suction of Australia, it may well become more important for our trade than the East Indies. And yet, how bitterly miserable and shockingly sad is the state of these magnificent colonies at present! Of the 2,800 square geographical miles that it occupies, there are scarcely more than ten—yes, ten—under cultivation; in a land five times as large as the whole of the Netherlands, there live no more people than in the single city of Utrecht; the death rate exceeds the birth rate; production has declined to one-tenth of what it once was; idolatry, according to the latest colonial report, is on the rise; plantation after plantation lies desolate and abandoned; the colonization of the Saramacca has failed miserably; the Netherlands must annually contribute some tons; and to go to the West Indies is already considered in our public opinion as such overwhelming proof of complete uselessness that the social conditions there are truly not shining either. The primary cause of this was undoubtedly the great prosperity that enervated and weakened the former colonists in the middle of the eighteenth century; their strength was robbed from them to withstand the adversities that repeated wars, through the obstruction of the slave markets and export production, brought with them, and they too willingly, by mortgaging their property to Amsterdam capitalists, placed themselves under the power of a foreign stock exchange. Especially in 1770, this taking of Amsterdam money became a frenzy that in a few weeks almost brought the entire colony to its independence and burdened the cultivated inheritance with a debt of 50 million (= now 150 million). This naturally broke the energy. One does not work for strangers. So the colonists thinned out and these masters came; thereby the pith was removed from industry and agriculture; and the result was that long before the emancipation of the slaves, the colonial luster had been extinguished. This emancipation, the proceeds of which naturally mostly benefited the Amsterdam gentlemen, then demolished what little was left standing; and for a time gave rise to the opinion that nothing more could be done with these colonies. More than once, their sale, especially by Indo-Malay, has even been considered! And yet, for such...

[624]

612 ‘SCOLONIAL POSSESSIONS OVERSEAS. There was no more despondent and unpatriotic judgment than this. Right next to Suriname lies Demerary, Berbice and Essequebo, which, formerly belonging to us, and now having come under England, shared the same fate as Suriname and are also in a far from flourishing condition, but are nevertheless noticeably better off. While Suriname has to beg from us, British Guiana comes out, and sometimes even has a surplus. In ‘71 it had only £338,000 in expenditure against £379,000 in revenue, and in ‘77 no more than £344,000 in costs, against £364,000 in receipts. With a population three times as large, it spends only four million a year, while our administration, with an equal ratio, would cost five. The exports amount to a total of about thirty million, against three to four million in Suriname, which, at an equal ratio, should have been ten million. Accordingly, the merchant also used 200,000 tons of shipping freight, compared to only 20,000 tons in our colony; which again, at an equal ratio of population, should have been 65,000. While we, thanks to the excellent services of the Moravian brothers, occupy an honorable position in the school system, with 8,000 students out of a population of 60,000 in Suriname, compared to 16,000 in British Guiana, in a population three times as large.

§25. Still the same. The criticism we gave at the time on the existing situation also retains its significance. You will find it in Our Program on pages 357 ff. What then applied, applies in essence still, and therefore need not be repeated. Only this may be added, that the later Minister of Colonies, Mr. van Asch van Wijck, who had first gone to Suriname himself as Governor, thoroughly investigated and studied the state of affairs and in a rare way won the love of the population, who also honored him in a quite unique way. As a minister, it was also his last concern to lift Suriname out of its decay. In this, he had especially in mind the possible...

Analytical Summary: Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit
[625]

STILL THE SAME. 613 The difficulty was to establish a railway connection from the lower terrain to the higher terrain on the first mountain ridge. He expected this would lead to the establishment of an entirely new colonization on this mountain plateau, so that an entirely new colonial-social life could work there. This plan has also been carried out, but it has not yet been able to meet the great expectations that were initially cherished. Colonists of European blood have not come to appear. We have indeed driven the former slave negroes partly into the mountains, but to become wild there. And in Paramaribo, and a few nearby places, the old life remained largely. The gold fishing, which initially seemed to hold out beautiful promises, has indeed been continued, but has soon fallen far short of expectations. The attempt to replace the former negroes with Javanese cannot be considered successful. And so it lies there that the precious land calls to be worked, to provide us with new treasures, but that the workers are lacking. What deserves notice in this state of affairs is that in this colony Christian confessors repeatedly appeared as governors—we only mention Messrs. Savorin Lohman, Van Asch van Wijck and Idenburg, and in connection with this it remains significant that the Moravian missionary has succeeded in bringing a not insignificant number of negroes to baptism. Although it is true that our Christians have not yet become what they should be morally in more than one respect with the negroes, it may nevertheless be gratefully acknowledged that what was seen in the United States also proved to apply here. Although the negroes, by race, stand on the lowest ethnological steps, yet it has also been seen here that they are eminently susceptible to Christianization, and that the call of Christ: “Come unto me, all ye that labor and are heavy laden,” also finds entrance with them.

[626]

614 THE KINGDOM’S POSSESSIONS OVERSEAS.

§26. The Opium. Our colonial administration still drives us to the 25 million guilders that the sale of Opium throws into the lap of the Government as net profit. This is all the more offensive now that in China itself a more serious action against the use of Opium has arisen, and international conferences have been held to put this intoxicating evil out of use. All the more so now that England has lent its cooperation to these conferences, one momentarily cherished the silent hope that the Opium problem was nearing its solution. Yet here too we were disappointed again. Practically nothing has been gained with these international conferences. Yet this does not relieve us of the obligation to continue the fight against Opium uninterruptedly and undiminished. The government monopoly that has now been applied to Opium has of course yielded some benefit. Much injustice in the opium dens has been averted. It was precisely with a view to this that it was disappointing that in the Outer Possessions the sale was nevertheless again left to the opium farmer. But apart from this, the system itself does not yield the desired result. Progress is undoubtedly being made, and there are now already areas where the use of opium can no longer thrive. But we must go further. If the use of the opium that is now sold were equally distributed over the 40 million in our Archipelago, there would hardly be reason to complain. But this is not the case. Masses do not use opium at all, and the main use comes down to a small group that is addicted to it. The Government knows this, and this is precisely what it should not tolerate as a Christian Power. Therefore, we do not abandon our resistance to the consumption of opium, even if we know that we are not total abstainers.

Analytical Summary: Guided by Orange. To this has been added: “guided by Orange”; an addition that must not be omitted. For it cannot be denied that in the characterized Calvinism, as it also penetrated our country from Geneva and France at that time, there was a danger not to be underestimated, the aftereffects of which are still discernible. In the last chapter of the First Part of this work, I have extensively explained how from the outset there was a decisive element in Calvinism that could only develop and mature later, and that, politically speaking, only came to full effect in the United States; but it was also then added that in Geneva (one need only think of the burning of Servetus) an intolerant dogmatism still prevailed, which, though indispensable for the church, could only result in schism and oppression in its political consequences.
[627]

ORGANIZATION AND POLICY

CHAPTER XXII
PARTY POLICY AT THE BALLOT BOX

§1. Organization and Policy

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.

Apart from the rights and the necessity of appearing as a separate and independent party, the party policy also requires further explanation, insofar as it must govern the advance to the ballot box. Here, too, the sharp distinction must be kept in view between the two different natures that come to expression in the appearance of parties at the ballot box. A party in the political field can either be no more than a variation of related parties, or it can stand in principle opposed to a series of parties based on entirely different foundations. If we take no account of any of the parties that have their roots in the revolutionary principles of 1789, and set against them the parties that cling to the old Christian starting point, then there can certainly be variations on both sides. Among the parties of the Revolution, whose year of birth is 1789, there can mainly be three formations, depending on whether one forms the rear guard, the middle corps, or the vanguard, with a certain degree of difference. But an equally pronounced degree of difference can just as well occur among the parties that do not greet their year of birth in 1789, but in the year one of Bethlehem. Although all political parties that owe their origin to the rise of Christianity go back to Bethlehem, this does not at all rule out the often so strongly pronounced difference between those who lead the way, form the middle, or bring up the rear. Upon closer inspection, there then runs a straight line that distinguishes the two triads of groups from each other, so that these move along the two sides, on the one hand the three Christian formations, and on the other hand the three Revolutionary formations. It is precisely this that so often gives occasion for impure mixing in the ballot box policy. The mixing is less pronounced among the parties that form the two sides of the dividing line, but the tendency towards it comes out repeatedly, both in the rear guard and in the vanguard of both, fundamentally different formations. The Conservatives of both groups easily close ranks, and often with such a preference for each other, to defend their backward position. And just as frequently, among the Democrats on both sides of the line, the urge to unite strongly appears, to defend themselves against those who favor a more backward attitude.

[628]

616 PARTY POLICY AT THE BALLOT BOX

§2. Not Always Excluded

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...

More serious attention must be paid to this at the ballot box policy, because it is by no means certain that a political party can and may always unconditionally lend itself to cooperation with a variation in the political field that shares the same proposal with it. It requires no further indication that in itself and as a rule, cooperation with what is closest to you in principle will be recommended. However, it does not at all follow from this that this must and will always be the rule, regardless of all the circumstances. There can even be temporary cases where clinging to what is fundamentally congenial to you would weaken and ultimately disarm you. Take as an example the emergence of the socio-economic question. This question came to the fore as a result of two historical causes. The first lay in the gradually much broader education of the youth, whereby even among the lower classes a development was gradually reached that of itself brought the right to have a say. And to this was added, almost simultaneously, the increase in material prosperity and a directing of human effort towards acquiring a more abundant life through that prosperity. Now it lay in the nature of the matter that on both sides of the great dividing line, the conservative groups felt this urge of social life less, or perceived it with reluctance. This phenomenon then also presented itself in the second half of the 19th century, both among Christian politicians and among the revolutionaries, and many unintentionally went over to opposing this movement, both...

[629]

NOT ENTIRELY EXCLUDED. - PRACTICALLY ALWAYS DIFFICULT.

617 on our side as well as on the other side. In the first attempt, this even went so far that the Conservative gentlemen, both to the right and left of the line of principle, also did not want to hear anything about the emergence of this new political issue. Not only that this issue was combated and abhorred on both sides in its socialist form, but initially most of them, and this on both sides, went so far that they themselves vehemently disputed, to whomever differed from them, even the right to raise such an issue. It was not a scientific or a political issue. It was nothing more than an invention of popular despotism, and indeed a despotism that was devised for the overthrow of the entire existing order of things. Did it not speak for itself that those among us who also very decisively felt the legitimacy in the emergence of this socio-economic issue, had to take a stand against their own more closely related fellow-thinkers, and could even be called upon to support those who were principally opposed to us?

§3. Practically always difficult. Yet experience taught that, however noble and honest the intention of such temporary support may be, the reality of the political situation could make the pursuit of such a course of action impossible. Elsewhere such an attempt has succeeded, but when it was also tried here in this country, it turned out that even the left was still too immature to be able to lend itself to it. We have already pointed out in the foregoing how such an attempt, undertaken by us out of conviction, has failed because the revolutionary democrats cast their vote much more willingly for a revolutionary middle man or even for a revolutionary-conservative, than for a democrat who presented himself as a Christian democrat. In other countries, especially in England, in Germany and in the United States of America, this did not go so far. Here in this country, on the other hand, the aversion in the revolutionary-democratic circles towards anyone who also confesses Christ in politics is so far-reaching that they would rather risk anything than allow the authority of Christ to be asserted in the political arena. In more than one State on foreign soil, where the Roman Catholic Church is all-controlling in the religious sphere, possible aversion may manifest itself in brutal anti-Papism, but then such an anti-Papist is not infrequently still found to have a warm inspiration for the honor of Christ. Here in this country, on the other hand, where the revolutionary aversion is not directed against Rome, but in general against the Christian element, it has become increasingly clear how almost impossible it was, to reach across the great principles, to lend a hand to the men on the other side, even temporarily. Of course, this promotes each other’s efforts and intentions well enough, and so the democratic element has also come to its right against the conservative-aristocratic element among us, but at the ballot box the effect of this disposition remained small. Our own party was in every way ripe for it, for from the Anti-Revolutionary side the courage did not seem to be lacking to join the opposing party for a particular election. In reality, however, one encountered two kinds of resistance, which soon proved to be a stumbling block for each. The rear guard and the middle group of the Christian side made our intention out to be betrayal, which temporarily cost us the support of many. And on the revolutionary side of the dividing line, the aversion to what they called our feigned piety proved so general that any desired result failed to materialize. §4. Material or spiritual.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.

From this misery a unmistakable danger was soon born. However highly one may conceive of the political ideals, it cannot be denied that both a material and a spiritual tendency underlie all political striving. In the ecclesiastical sphere this may have escaped notice, although even there it is never entirely absent, but it is certain that the political sphere of power exerts influence both on the material and on the spiritual well-being or misery. Politics moves in the tangible reality of our human life, and this human life, arising as it does from soul and body, and being constantly bound to it, naturally demands that attention be paid both to the needs of the bodily life and to the higher needs of the life to which the soul aspires. Now it needs no argument that, to this end,

[630]

618 PARTY POLICY AT THE BALLOT BOX.

the Roman Catholic Church is all-controlling in the religious sphere, possible aversion may manifest itself in brutal anti-Papism, but then such an anti-Papist is not infrequently still found to have a warm inspiration for the honor of Christ. Here in this country, on the other hand, where the revolutionary aversion is not directed against Rome, but in general against the Christian element, it has become increasingly clear how almost impossible it was, to reach across the great principles, to lend a hand to the men on the other side, even temporarily. Of course, this promotes each other’s efforts and intentions well enough, and so the democratic element has also come to its right against the conservative-aristocratic element among us, but at the ballot box the effect of this disposition remained small. Our own party was in every way ripe for it, for from the Anti-Revolutionary side the courage did not seem to be lacking to join the opposing party for a particular election. In reality, however, one encountered two kinds of resistance, which soon proved to be a stumbling block for each. The rear guard and the middle group of the Christian side made our intention out to be betrayal, which temporarily cost us the support of many. And on the revolutionary side of the dividing line, the aversion to what they called our feigned piety proved so general that any desired result failed to materialize.

§4. Material or spiritual.

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.

From this misery a unmistakable danger was soon born. However highly one may conceive of the political ideals, it cannot be denied that both a material and a spiritual tendency underlie all political striving. In the ecclesiastical sphere this may have escaped notice, although even there it is never entirely absent, but it is certain that the political sphere of power exerts influence both on the material and on the spiritual well-being or misery. Politics moves in the tangible reality of our human life, and this human life, arising as it does from soul and body, and being constantly bound to it, naturally demands that attention be paid both to the needs of the bodily life and to the higher needs of the life to which the soul aspires. Now it needs no argument that, to this end,

[631]

The distinction between the material and the spiritual is often quite marked. Some are so absorbed in the care of the body that they scarcely pay any attention to the significance of the soul, and then only when illness or adversity casts them down. Others, on the contrary, are so immersed in the spiritual that the care of earthly things is almost unknown to them. This naturally brings the danger that, wherever the influence of the left-wing political parties is strong, the urge to focus solely on the material will be so strong that those among us who also have a certain predominance in the material aspect of the intended purpose will, before they themselves suspect it, be inclined to feel more sympathy for the left-wing groups than for the more spiritually-minded in our own circle. Not that they would openly depart from Christ. Not at all. Personally, they will still highly value piety, but when it comes to upholding the honor of Him who said, “All power is given to me in heaven and on earth,” they do not give Christ, the God of the distant, the high place on the political and economic field that belongs to the Son of God by inheritance. Their political gaze is so one-sidedly focused on improving the material position of their fellow-members, that they gradually feel more sympathy for the deniers of Christ, who want to improve the social and economic position of their fellow-members, than for a zealot for the cause of Christ, who is less attached to the material and therefore feels less for the material interests of the least privileged. It then depends on the position of the balance. The ratio between the material and the spiritual must of course be for the Christian 51 as a minimum of the spiritual and 49 as the highest of the material. But soon one notices that with such people it becomes 75 for the material, while the spiritual has fallen to 25. And then, this can hardly be otherwise, the balance swings more and more towards the material, until in the end nothing but a semblance of the spiritual remains, and one jumps over to the Materialistic revolutionaries with bag and baggage.

[632]

620 PARTY POLICY AT THE BALLOT BOX.

§5. Predominance of the Minimum. Among those who remain loyal to Christ, not only as their Redeemer, but also as their King, a defect and a disproportion is all too often born at the ballot box, against which one cannot be warned seriously enough. This defect is born from the fact that, with the disappearance of not an insignificant democratic element among the voters, who are all inclined towards the Socialists, the sincere Christian Democracy is pressed, since in an election the three Christian factions need each other and cannot do without each other. The rear guard is only too likely to try to abuse this. One is so eager to cooperate, and immediately one calls out to you: Support our candidates, and you will not find us unwilling to pay you back in kind in any eventual districts. In itself there is nothing wrong with this. It is something quite natural and necessary. If at our elections there were no more than two parties, there would be no talk of alliances. In America one is either Republican or Democratic. Roosevelt did try for a short time to break through this rigid two-party system, but he did not succeed, and even in the latest elections it was only between Wilson and Hughes. A third choice was practically non-existent. With us, however, the constellation is not so sharply defined. Just as Robert Fruin estimated the Calvinists of the 16th century at a tenth of the population, so it still stands. We may now and then go somewhat beyond that, and even reach 2/10, but in any case an alliance is the only means for us to come out with the highest available strength. This has, especially since 1889, led to a combination of Anti-rev., Chr.-Hist. and Roman Catholics, and an alliance as it presented itself here recommends itself of its own accord. The difficulty lies only in the question of how to give such an alliance a certain reasonableness and a certain firmness. But when one came to that question, the unpleasant phenomenon soon presented itself that there were some gentlemen among the Chr.-Hist. who did not want to hear anything about a coalition agreement, in whatever form, and preferred to give the preference to...

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.
[633]

AGREEMENT ON EQUAL TERMS

To remain free and fish in troubled waters, while others, feeling certain fear that it went too far, well, wanted to come to an agreement, but then also demanded that the agreement would consist in this, that the other two parties would adopt their program.

§6. Agreement on equal terms. Now this was in conflict with all sense of fairness. Three factions that profess to possess a unity of political roots can only appear together and go to the ballot box successfully if it is met, and indeed by agreement met, with these three conditions: first, that the mutual strength of the numbers be firmly established through serious electoral organization; second, that the candidates of the three factions remain equal in number with the figure of this investigated and controlled numerical strength; third, that one accepts a program for which each makes a sacrifice, but for which also joint gain is held out in prospect. The first of these three conditions has, as far as possible, been met both by the Anti-Revolutionary and the Roman Catholic side, but not by the Christian Historical side. The Anti-Revolutionary party is able to meet this to a certain extent through its very extensive local organization in 600 electoral associations. The Roman Catholic party largely meets this requirement through its ecclesiastical organization; but the Christian Historical party falls short in this first condition. It goes almost entirely on guesswork, and all too often overestimates its demands. This has also been the reason why the second condition, which we mentioned, could not come to its right from that side. Not knowing its numerical strength, it estimated it far too high, and this was the cause that one constantly sought to trim down the number of its candidates, without being able to devise a means to settle the dispute that constantly arose from this.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.
[634]

622 PARTY POLICY AT THE BALLOT BOX. From this then arose not only unpleasant accusations in the press, but also locally, entirely at odds with the spirit and mental labor that was always demanded for a well-considered agreement. Yet this first disturbance was of lesser significance compared to the much more annoying disturbance that presented itself as soon as one came to the drafting of the common ballot box program. For then one did not go over to the co-signing of such a program except with reluctance, and insisted that no single point on this program would find a place that did not fully agree with the party program of one’s own faction, or was borrowed from it. Often the Anti-Revolutionaries and the Roman Catholics have then adapted themselves to this. The Anti-Revolutionaries could do this to a certain extent, because they could still work the voters with a more elaborated Ballot Box program, and the Roman Catholics were always more inclined to come over to our side. Yet everyone increasingly felt through the years that the mutual relationship in this way did not come into its own. Yet the Christian Historicals persisted in never wanting to recognize anything else as a condition than what was plucked from the branches of their own tree, from which it would follow that no other position was granted to us than that of an auxiliary service for the Christian Historicals. One also saw increasingly clearly that more and more mutual accommodation was an inexorable demand. Consultation was also indispensable here, but the outcome of this consultation would then have to be that the three factions of the Christian groups would take something from each other at each Ballot Box, in order to, better at least than hitherto, find their own interest in the agreement entered into. That this would always remain a delicate point, went without saying, but precisely for that reason the intensity of mutual sympathy had to come out, that such an agreement proved possible.

§7. Good tone. With the demand for the agreement, it went hand in hand that one and the other, in the press and at meetings, remained in the good tone as far as the mutual relationship was concerned. Of course, the convictions of the three factions pushed many a demand to the fore, in which a clear, sometimes even very characteristic difference of opinion was reflected. This could not be otherwise, and first of all the theory of the death in the pot has been advocated in this respect from the Anti-Revolutionary side. Above all, in politics, the expression of life is a condition of vigorous life. And so it went without saying that none of the factions should be hindered in the proper pursuit of what characterized it, and that this

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how
[635]

MAINTAINING A GOOD TONE - RESPECTING ONE’S OWN DOMAIN.623 This had to be accompanied by criticism on those points where the other two factions differed from her. On our side, it is not disputed - indeed, we have always insisted - that a vigorous life should also be applauded in other quarters. However, this freedom of movement in life can never excuse the mingling of bitterness and wounding scorn in one’s speech. On the contrary, it should be demanded that, in dealing with the groups of the Left, one should take them to one’s heart with warmth, and that in discussing the mutual disputes, this should always be done in a tone that speaks of attachment and appreciation. The example given in this regard from the Anti-rev. side can hardly be faulted. At least in the main organ of the Anti-rev. party, all wounding criticism has always been deliberately avoided, with only a single high-necessary exception, and all sharp treatment and every bitter attack has been avoided. When disputes arose, the editor of the Anti-rev. main organ hardly ever entered into them. And it has always been recognized, both by friend and foe, that dispute has never been dear to us, and that our batteries have never been aimed at the allied factions, but solely at the fortress of the Left. With this in view, it cannot but be regretted that the main organ of the Christian Historical faction has by no means always struck the right tone, and has not infrequently allowed itself to be tempted into taking a position and adopting an attitude as if its batteries were leaving the politics of the Left side unscathed, yes, sometimes even sparing them, and not infrequently, even coarsely, turning against the leader of the Anti-revolutionary party. Sometimes the tone even lacked something personal. The Anti-revolutionary party did not experience such treatment from the Roman Catholic party. Only here too it may not be concealed that now and then a tone was to be heard in North Brabant and South Gelderland that gave the impression of being more directed towards estrangement than towards rapprochement.

§8. Respecting one’s own domain.

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.

The most painful thing here was that the unfriendly treatment of the kindred factions, as a matter of course, also had an influence on the relationship within our own circle. For within our own bosom, everyone had been accustomed from the outset that every nuance should come into its own and express itself freely, provided that, as far as the affairs of the country were concerned, they kept step and did not break the ranks. Yet it could not be avoided that many nuances and insights came to light here that provoked disputes. More than once, use was then made of this with the other factions to intervene, and to condemn the opinion of the one in order to praise all the more strongly the conviction of the one who stood opposite him. One knows well what a difference of opinion, just think of the oath question and the reading rooms, sometimes arose in our circles because of this. But even if this did not take on a seriously serious character, the Left was always there like the chickens, to mix in with praise or blame, and not infrequently the Anti-rev. side then committed the weakness of mastering these expressions of adhesion and using them as a weapon of defense against the attacked. This was even worse in the days of Groen van Prinsterer, since then it has even become visibly calmer and more calm. Yet the world is by no means out of it. It is therefore so important to be on our guard in two respects. On the one hand, against the fear that among us there should be no differences and that we should not contradict each other, but also, on the other hand, against the evil that so often creeps in with this difference of opinion, to seek help from the Left in order to strengthen oneself in the struggle against the brothers. It has always been and in all lands the difficult art to find the right bond on the political field, which combines the freedom of the word with the mutual cohesion. In itself, it is therefore nothing strange that in the Anti-revolutionary party too, the one good rule has not infrequently been sinned against, and above all, one draws from the contrast to which we have pointed, not the consequence as if we wanted to assert that the writer of these lines would never have failed on his side either. All the more for this reason, he emphasizes here that the Christian parties may decisively condemn the evil mentioned here and may oppose it with pious power, because what may still seem pardonable elsewhere, among those who raise the Christian banner, may never be tolerated.

[636]

624 PARTY POLICY AT THE BALLOT BOX.

[637]

PROPORTIONAL REPRESENTATION

§9. Proportional Representation. Since 1870, when the writer became acquainted with Hodge’s ideas on Proportional Representation, he has consistently pointed out how the introduction of this system of proportionality could become the most decisive means to definitively end much of what was and remained improper in the political party struggle. A system of proportionality, as will soon, we may hope, also become our part in the Netherlands, offers the great advantage that it removes the fierce struggle from the ballot box and, if it must still continue, transfers it to the States General. But one is certainly mistaken who may have imagined that, if this new system were to go through, all party struggle would now be over for good. This, of course, cannot be, for the principles remain in their antithesis and the variations retain their color differences, but there is still this truth in the assertion, that the curse of dishonesty, which has burned on it for so long, can now finally be averted from the ballot box, and that the antithesis will continue to hold sway in the political field, but with this difference, that the antithesis will now leave the ballot box to concentrate in the clubs, in order to remain unchangeably in control, especially in our Second Chamber, but in part also in the First Chamber. The writer also considers himself fortunate to have been able to thank that from the outset his eye was opened to the so serious significance of the Proportional system, and that what had been withheld from us for too long by a party struggle that deserved to be condemned, will now finally be obtained. Whether the way in which it is now being introduced to our liking is the most desirable, is left aside here. The scissors must be extremely fine that can bring about the absolutely required and impeccably accurate division in this matter. But this can never condemn the principle. What has remained below perfection can now be elevated to the sphere of the perfect. Therefore, nothing may be deducted from what has now been granted to us. Compulsory voting and women’s suffrage may almost completely ruin it, and the maintenance of the individualistic suffrage makes many clear-sighted people doubt, but so much has now been achieved, that the immorality, which has almost completely ruined our ballot box struggle for so many years, is now being removed from the ballot box.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.
[638]

Already in this respect our people’s morality will gain. But let no one imagine that the character of the party struggle will lay aside its fierceness. The ballot box only gets a word once every four years, and the tense relations will have to continue to dwell in the States General.

§10. Article 21 remained.

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.

From the fact that even after the realization of the pending constitutional revision, an entirely modified form of the suffrage will govern the ballot box struggle, it in no way follows that any necessity has arisen to make changes in the fundamental idea of Article 21 of our Program. After serious consideration, it has been decided to leave this article substantively as it was. This article now determines, and in essence entirely as before, that the Anti-Revolutionary party “maintains the independence of the party to have its principles prevail; does not allow itself to be incorporated into any other party; and only accepts cooperation with other parties if this can be obtained through a previously well-defined accord, with unimpaired preservation of its independence.” On the other hand, a change has been made in the final clause of this article. This final clause previously read as follows: “Reason why it generally participates in the political elections with its own candidate in the first ballot, and reserves the right to act according to circumstances in the run-off.” In 1878 this could not be stated otherwise. At the time there were still not three but only two Righteous parties, for besides the Anti-Revolutionary, only the Roman Catholic state party was then active. The Christian-Historical party was not formed until 1894. If the Roman Catholic party had already been in 1874 what it later became, the attempt at cooperation in the ballot box would in all probability have found a first regulation already in the initial period. But it was not yet so at the time. The writer of this considers himself fortunate to have been able to thank that from the outset his eye was opened to the so serious significance of the Proportional system, and that what had been withheld from us for too long by a party struggle that deserved to be condemned, will now finally be obtained.

[639]

Article 21 remained. When he first obtained a seat in the Second Chamber, he immediately came into conflict with some of his Roman Catholic colleagues, and it was not until the Episcopate had declared its position in the education dispute that the idea of organized cooperation found more ready acceptance. It was especially Dr. Schaepman who had been pushing for this from the outset, but from the side of not a few of his fellow believers he incurred rather sharp criticism, especially in the beginning. The appearance of the Anti-Revolutionary party as an organized whole could therefore not immediately lead to a cordial coalition. The wording of Article 21, that it should be the rule for us to appear entirely independently, with our own candidates, and that it would only be “according to circumstances” to act otherwise, did not in the least refer to a certain coalition with kindred spirits, but without any further specification, to possible cooperation at the ballot box with whichever other party. As a result of the deception to which we had been exposed from the side of the Conservatives, one then began to turn against them more particularly, and a persistent tendency to dare to make an accord with the Radicals at times and occasions manifested itself more clearly. For the sake of caution, the word “generally” had been inserted in the last sentence of Article 21. The political relations differed so widely in the various parts of our country that setting an absolute rule seemed risky. The insertion of the word “generally” left open, with a view to this, the possibility of making a somewhat firmer accord, especially in the provinces of Utrecht and Gelderland. This was the reason that later, when the idea of an alliance gained more acceptance, the letter of Article 21 did not oppose this, and the article remained usable, at least temporarily, even without amendment. Now, however, in 1916, this could no longer remain so. We now faced the fact that the Roman Catholic political party had completely abandoned its former sympathies for Liberalism, which had quite understandably manifested themselves especially after 1853, that is, after the so-called April Movement, and was now taking the field against the fundamental ideas of 1789. And now that the separation of the Christian-Historicals was added to this, it soon became clear how in fact there were now three parties standing side by side.

[640]

628PARTY POLICY AT THE BALLOT BOX.stood, which, despite strong variation, undoubtedly had a certain principled idea as a starting point. Hence the urge for cooperation, which increasingly brought the alliance idea to the fore. With this in mind, the final text of Article 21 is now worded as follows: “Outside of an agreement, it (the party) fields its own candidates in the first round, and if a run-off election ensues, it acts according to the circumstances. At a Deputies’ meeting, to be held before each general election, it adopts the program and the agreement for that election by a majority of votes. All affiliated electoral associations are then obliged to adhere to this program and this agreement.”

§11. The current regulation. This newly formulated regulation is based on the fundamental idea that no attack on our independent character as a Coalitional party, with a view to our historical past from the 16th century, and no less with a view to our principle of loyalty to God’s Word, is permitted, under any pretext whatsoever. This lies in the explanation that no cooperation is deemed to arise of itself, but that it can only come about as the fruit of consultation, and indeed of such consultation as did not confine itself to a loose discussion here and there, but obtained a well-defined agreement as a result. If such an agreement had not been reached, one would have retained one’s full freedom, fielded one’s own candidates throughout the country in the first round, and reserved the right to act as circumstances dictated in the event of a run-off. By placing the formula “outside of an agreement” at the forefront here, it was nevertheless recognized and admitted that an agreement would not only be desirable, but would even be the rule. But precisely for that reason, it had to be laid down under what conditions such an agreement would be bound from the outset. As a matter of course, the power to enter into such an agreement was reserved for the Deputies’ meeting, but in such a way that the right of decision remained with the majority in the Deputies’ meeting, so that even if a different opinion was expressed in a minority, not only the election program, but also the election agreement for the impending election, could be determined.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.
[641]

THE CURRENT REGULATION. - BREACH OF THE AGREEMENT.629However, this could not suffice. As soon as one enters into an agreement with another party, the party agreeing with us must also be able to count on the fact that the votes that have been promised will actually be delivered. Hence the necessary addition at the end of Article 21: “All affiliated electoral associations are then obliged to adhere to this program and this agreement.” It hardly needs to be added that we derive the moral right to demand a firm and continuous delivery of the assistance agreed on our part, from this as well. A moral right that, by virtue of the agreement, passes informally into a legal right.

§12. Breach of the agreement. As became clear especially after 1913, there remained even then a curious lacuna in the very formally concluded agreement, to which it is advisable to draw attention for the future. Up to that point, the agreement entered into had only aimed at the major election campaign that preceded each general election. But if that general election campaign was once behind us, and an unexpected vacancy occurred in the meantime, then the one considered himself entirely free for the four years until the next general election to deviate from the agreement, while the other thought that he could not interpret it that way and therefore also insisted on the continued validity of the agreement even in such an interim election. This must not remain an open question, and therefore it will be a requirement in the future to stipulate in the agreement itself that it remains valid until such time as a new general election intervenes. But even this cannot suffice, and especially after 1913 a second lacuna has arisen that has put the alliance of the three Right-wing parties to a very serious test. There was

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.
[642]

PARTY POLICY AT THE BALLOT BOX. Agreed, but the accord we entered into in 1913 applied only to the case where the Right had remained in power. Now that the Right has lost and the Left has gained such a decisive majority in the Second Chamber, that we, as far as the handling of affairs is concerned, would be completely at their mercy, it soon became clear that the accord we entered into in 1913 hardly applied in any respect to the situation that has now arisen. The consequence of this was that the Roman Catholic State Party, without any consultation or consultation with us, entered into negotiations with the Cabinet of liberal ministers, and for itself, entirely without us, established a course of action that promised it golden mountains, but left us high and dry. The details of this particular case cannot be discussed here. It appears to us that the advantages that opened up for the Roman Catholic State Party in this case were of such a nature that it would have been unfaithful to itself if it had not seized the profit that presented itself with both hands. The question that remained was only this: does an allied party act according to duty and good order if it proceeds to follow such a course of action without informing its allies of it and continuing to regulate the further relationship? And then our answer is certainly negative, with this remark, that of the Roman Catholic party, when it acted in this way, it cannot be said that it had broken the accord, since a case position such as then presented itself was not mentioned in the accord at all. It seems to us that it should nevertheless have made a confidential communication of its intention, even though the accord did not oblige it to do so. And this now precisely points to the second defect to which we alluded. Is it not undeniable that an accord entered into with a view to a general election can hardly be anything other than a positive accord, determining what one will have to do if one is to support a Cabinet that is of the same spirit, then it follows from this that in the event of a setback at the ballot box, the accord entered into can no longer serve its purpose, and that one will then have to hastily proceed to conclude a new accord, in which the attitude to be taken towards the non-kindred Cabinet is determined. It should even be added that in such a position it would be desirable to appoint a joint standing committee...

[643]

DEFECTS IN THE ACCORD - IS ACCORD STILL NECESSARY?

Many expect that, if the Constitution is revised, and the party truce is maintained, as may be assumed, there will already be a next year, after the election campaign, no more talk of alliance or even of electoral consultation under the leadership of the organized parties. Each independent party, it is said, will then operate entirely on its own. Consultation between like-minded or even other parties will then only be possible in the Chamber and in no case during an election campaign. Everyone will go their own way. The voters will receive lists from their own circle or faction. They will fill them in in the most promising manner, and the result will show by itself how many votes, and consequently how many parliamentary seats, they can count on. Even on a superficial view, this is not entirely correct. Suppose that the common vote comes to 20,000, then it is quite conceivable that if there are three parties with mutual interests, party A retains 15,000 votes without benefit, party B 10,000 and party C 5,000. Without further regulation, in this way the allied parties of the Right could lose 30,000 votes. This damage, which would naturally accrue to the benefit of the opposing parties, could be averted if the related parties agreed among themselves as to whom they wish to place on their list as a collector of the other, lost votes. Naturally, the choice here would have to be fixed on a candidate of that party who could dispose of the highest unused figure. A measure, it is true, of small alloy, but as experience has taught, in the Netherlands the majority in the Second Chamber can hang on a single vote. To look sharply at the small things is therefore always advisable with us at the Ballot Box. But even if one disregards this, there can never be a question of any party, in exercising its electoral rights, abstaining from indicating its own tone.

[644]

632 PARTY POLICY AT THE BALLOT BOX.

It is therefore completely incorrect to assume that the like-minded parties, as far as the election campaign is concerned, could leave everything blue and could only proceed to political consultation in the Chamber. One may not even for a moment assume the appearance of this. Even after the amendment of the electoral law, it remains inexorably necessary that each party determine for itself what the task will be that the soon-to-be-elected Members of Parliament will have to take upon themselves. Each party must therefore, before the election, give an account of what it deems desirable and necessary in the interest of the country. It will therefore have to design, under this new star, a program of action, just as it does now, and when this program of action has been prepared and regulated by the Deputies’ meeting, then of course each electoral association that has joined will, just as it does now, be obliged to investigate to what extent the candidates it intends to place on the list can be regarded as the men from whom an action in the sense of such a program may be expected. If this is to lead to an effective result, then the like-minded or allied parties, for whatever reason, must be well aware of what is sought in the list candidates as attractive. An accord, not to deceive the voters, but to determine the conditions of the now isolated voters, will therefore remain entirely the same as it is now. The voters must know what they can expect from each other’s support; and in presenting their candidates on the list, they must be sufficiently assured that these candidates, if they become Members of Parliament, will respond in sympathy of purpose to what the voters expect. It is therefore incorrect to assume that the election campaign will be carried on by the various parties entirely separately from each other. Although it is true that the definitive agreement will only be able to be reached in the Chamber itself, yet even at the ballot box, not personnel, but a political accord must set the tone. A party that would neglect this would come out in a loss of a part of its political power.

[645]

FRAGMENTATION

§14. Fragmentation. A serious concern arises from the system that is about to be introduced—that, whether we like it or not, a daring “Sonder-bündlerei” could lead to a partial disintegration of the party. A group, however insignificant, could easily, through collaboration, manage to cast enough votes in a certain region of the country to ultimately secure the required quota and obtain a seat. The possibility of this is undeniable. And it is this possibility that so easily leads to attempting such a ballot box isolation. The danger threatens from two sides. In the first place, there may be an individual who would consider his highest ideal achieved if he could become a Member of Parliament. Such a case was always conceivable, but now even more so than before, because the financial position of a Member of Parliament has markedly improved. There was an allowance of 2,000 guilders, now it amounts to 3,000 guilders, and in addition, not only travel expenses, but also a possible pension that can amount to a certain amount, high enough to maintain one’s life in old age. Both the urge to enter Parliament for a special conviction and the desire to improve one’s financial position can thus work together, and so it could be imagined that a political eccentric would work his way into Parliament. This cannot be prevented with a single person, as it is certain that in the long run the chance of success for such eccentrics will become less and less, and the freedom of choice will decline rather than rise. But even if the outcome would soon cut off such isolation by itself, it is very conceivable that in the first onslaught a dozen or so of such individualists would attempt it. And if they then succeed, even if only a very few, they can still cause, by such action, a fragmentation to set in at the very first onset, which will cost us the loss of four or five seats. Not that all four or five would be elected. On the contrary, more than one or two such things rarely succeed. Belgium, right next to us, provides proof of this. But our party would then still lose a significant number of votes in total, and the consequence of this is that these eccentrics themselves will run against us and offer support to the left-wing groups. And now do not say that all parties are initially exposed to this danger, and that the loss of the one will compensate for the loss of the other—never forget that individualism speaks very strongly in our circles, so that comparatively the danger lurking here is greater for us.
