Later, it will disappear, just as it has almost completely ceased to work in Belgium in the end. Only at the beginning can such damage be done, and that is why it is important to be mindful of it.

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”
[646]

634 PARTY POLICY AT THE BALLOT BOX

§15. District division. Just as every party has an organization, so the Anti-Revolutionary party also has one that is directly related to the district division prescribed by the electoral law. Many have only one division for both the House and the States, but there are also fifteen in double form, one for the House and the other for the States. Now, however, these electoral districts will be abolished, and a much more limited division will take their place. This naturally leads to the question of whether it will be necessary for our party organization to bring it into line with this new legal division. Of course, this would be strictly necessary if the number of votes were rounded off and finalized per electoral district, according to the new division, in such a way that it would not remain inactive in any new district, in an absolute sense. However, this will not be the case in such a strict sense. A completely new district division for the Anti-Revolutionary party is therefore not strictly necessary. Maintaining our existing division would even provide the Anti-Revolutionary party with the not insignificant advantage that the proven leadership, which has been working regularly for about twenty years per district, could remain what it was. Yet it will be advisable for our party to also seriously consider this new emerging issue and consult the district administrations about it. If modification proves desirable and promises us gain, then nothing would prevent us from making the necessary changes. Perhaps it would even be better in any case to live in a division that has lost its legal basis.

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.
[647]

DISTRICT DIVISION - THE COMPELLED VOTERS

§16. The Compelled Voters. If the duty to vote, as may be presumed, also goes away, then our Members of Parliament will certainly bear no responsibility for this, for our Party has always strenuously opposed it. But now that this voting compulsion has come, we will also have to reckon with it. For this confronts us with the fact that likely 15 to 20% of the registered voters would rather stay home, since they have no interest in the electoral process, but now must come forward. Partly these are voters who do not care to take the time, and do not want to leave their affairs for a trip to the ballot box. Partly they are persons of weak health, who, if the weather is unfavorable, would rather not venture out. Partly they are the indifferent, who do not concern themselves with politics, even mocking it for years. And finally, there are also those persons who are driven by their own opinion and yet see no chance of succeeding with that opinion. Four categories, to which is finally added the larger quantity of those who feel that they are not up to par, and therefore are embarrassed about how they should vote, a conviction that then leads them to prefer to stay home. Yet all of these must now go to the ballot box. No more fetching is needed. They now come of their own accord. But now the point is that their vote does not slip away in the wrong direction. This now brings a very serious duty for our electoral associations, to make accurate lists of all such stay-at-homes, and not just in the election month, but one or two years in advance, to exert influence on them from afar, to enlighten them, to provide them with the necessary reading material, to visit them, and to win them over, so that at least they will no longer act without some awareness of what they are doing. A very serious task for our electoral associations, but really a task for our organization. It is precisely with a view to this that the necessity has already been pointed out from more than one side, to appoint two permanent agents, something for which a large part of the funds that will have to flow from the entire country to the common war chest will have to be used. Understand well, therefore, that from now on it will not be half the work that we have thus far defended, but on the contrary, a doubling of the task that awaits us.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.
[648]

PARTY POLICY IN THE ELECTIONS

§17. The Propaganda Clubs. Especially with a view to the compelled voters, who must now come forward on pain of punishment by the judge, it was excellent that the Propaganda Clubs are now officially recognized in our Statutes as one of the most desirable manifestations in the electoral field. The relationship to these clubs will naturally have to be regulated in more detail, but we have now already advanced so far that these clubs desire nothing more than to offer support to our party, and that the Anti-Revolutionary Party is now intent on having the most desirable forces of these clubs primarily benefit the former stay-at-homes, now compelled voters.

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the

§18. Holy Inspiration. We conclude this explanation, as regards the electoral action, with the prayer that the holy character that our political struggle has always borne may in no way be disrupted, but may, in the seriousness of the times, increasingly inspire our men in higher and lower circles. It has been the misfortune of such a mighty Conservative party that it has always counted politics apart from the Almighty. Hence its inglorious downfall. Insofar as the Anti-Revolutionary Party has also contributed to the collapse of their palaces and the rise of their huts, it thinks of the exactly opposite endeavor, to always begin with the God of our Fathers, and to beg from Him the watchword for our political conduct. Religion first, and politics derived from it! Let this remain our watchword. The Word of God in Holy Scripture remains above all political calculation, ever resounding above us. The “nil desperandum Deo” (never despair in God) was thus the ever-victorious leading power. May this remain so!

Analytical Summary: A Happy Turn.
[649]

APPENDICES (see Chapter XVI § 7 page 453).

[651]

The Antirevolutionary Party as a People’s Party

§1. Connection with the first part.

Analytical Summary: Connection with the first part. In the First Part, the principles from which we believe political science should proceed have been extensively explained. Now, in this Second Part, we come to the application of these principles, particularly in our country and in the present time. Part I gave the general; in this Part follows the specification. This is not to be understood as if the exposition of the Principles had been carried out entirely apart from the data that present themselves to us in our time. This would not even have been thinkable in a constitutional exposition. The absolutely abstract theory has never done anything but mislead, and Savigny’s historical school has above all cured us of the theoretical one-sidedness of the natural sciences. But even apart from this, it was not to be thought of, in a practical popular handbook like the one offered here, to exclude all specification from the exposition of the Principles. A public, such as I have in mind as readers, and which consists mainly of non-academic leaders of the people, would not easily be able to follow a purely theoretical exposition. Only by specification could the positions I advocate become clear to such people. As far as, in the pleading of these principles, a reference to the particular and to the practical data had to be made, only auxiliary service was sought, and there could be no question of a special application to the Antirevolutionary Party, as it presents itself for the various political issues in our country and in our time.

The first part of this work dealt with the principles of the Antirevolutionary Party. This second part will now consider the application of those principles.

§2. The beginning of our Program.

Analytical Summary: The opening of our Program. In proceeding to the discussion of the direct application of the advocated principles in our country, I choose as my starting point the first article of our Program, which reads as follows: “The Antirevolutionary or Christian-Historical direction represents, as far as our country is concerned, the fundamental tone of our national character, as it received its stamp, under the leadership of Orange, under the influence of the Reformation, around 1572, and wishes to develop this, in accordance with the modified state of the people, in a form that meets the needs of our time.” §3. Difference according to the country. The way in which one stands up for the profession and defense of one’s political principles cannot be the same everywhere. In St. Petersburg, or, as it is now called, in Petrograd, there has been for some time a not so small Dutch colony, which has continued to exist mainly from the north of our country. The Minister of the Navy Kruys, who was approached by me in 1901 to form a Cabinet, was descended from one of the families that have constantly nourished this circle in St. Petersburg. Now, because of this historical origin, it could be thought that the members of the circle in the capital of Russia, had remained faithful to the Calvinistic principles that their fathers professed. Suppose now that among these emigrated Dutchmen, especially since they also possess their own church there, a political club had formed that was devoted to the principles advocated in this work, then...

Our Program begins with the declaration that the Antirevolutionary Party is a people’s party. This is not merely a rhetorical flourish, but a fundamental characteristic that must be maintained.

§3. Difference according to the country.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.

The Antirevolutionary Party in the Netherlands has a different character than in other countries, due to the unique history and circumstances of our nation.

§4. The Calvinists here in this land.

Analytical Summary: THE CALVINISTS HERE IN THIS COUNTRY. As far as our country is concerned, the Calvinists in the 16th century formed little more than one-tenth of the population. This figure, indicated by Professor Fruin, was very sharply taken; but when it comes to very decisive principles of politics, one is always safer by staying below the imposing figure than by aiming above it. Now if we stick to this one-tenth, it may be presumed that we still stand in the same proportion to the entire population. Although it is true that the candidates put forward on our side for the Second Chamber have repeatedly reached a much higher proportion, this does not at all lead to the conclusion of a higher proportion figure. Only after the introduction of proportional representation would it be possible to draw a conclusion on this point. Not now, and the less so because the Anti-Revolutionary Party does not dominate a particular part of the country, but is scattered over all the provinces, with only North Brabant and partly Limburg excepted.

The Calvinists in the Netherlands form a distinct group within the people, with their own history, customs, and popular morality.

§5. Allies.

Analytical Summary: Allies. This state of affairs necessarily leads to the consequence that, while maintaining one’s independence as a political party, one must nevertheless be intent on forming alliances with other parties in order to succeed and achieve a common goal. And here, the uncertainty often arises by which motives the choice of your allies should be determined. For this choice will in France lead our kindred spirits to join the radical elements, and conversely here at home, that we seek support from the Roman Catholic part of the population. But that the latter should be our rule everywhere, does not at all follow from this. Just think of Spain and Portugal, and you will immediately perceive the profound difference. In Spain, the Curia has persistently insisted that the Papal Church alone be allowed to appear freely. The infiltration of Protestantism was not even to be tolerated. If there is now at least a milder view prevailing here, it is not the Curia that has conceded this, but rather a radical current that has pushed this through, against the will and intention of the Curia. As for how it went in France, we know. Pure indifference in name, but in fact enmity against all religion has become the leading idea. The consequence of this was that in France too the Réformés joined the Radicalism. For they knew that, if Rome regained its old, lost position, a revival of the spirit of the St. Bartholomew’s Night was to be feared. It is therefore never in an absolute sense to be determined with which tendencies, with which parties we, the Anti-Revolutionaries, should ally ourselves on the political field. This cannot be established equally for all lands and peoples. The choice is necessarily determined by the attitude of the Church of Rome in such a land. If it can live on a footing of equality with the Church of Rome, then it is obvious that we as Calvinists feel much more akin to Rome than to the parties of the Revolution. If, on the other hand, a Roman-clerical regime, as in Spain, even begrudges us the light in our eyes, then we are compelled to seek support where otherwise the principled opposition would forbid us all cooperation.

The Antirevolutionary Party has allies in other groups that share its fundamental principles, even if they differ in certain details.

§6. Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld.

Analytical Summary: Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld. It is from the failure to properly distinguish these two that Dr. Bronsveld, and always only his kindred spirits, have become the victim. Intercourse and association with the collectors of the Églises réformées in France led this otherwise so noble scholar to want to apply the relationship that was found there in France also to our country. However warmly he had initially welcomed my appearance in the political field, I soon lost his sympathy, and he tried to apply the relationship that had to be enforced in France also here at home. His alignment with the Liberals became increasingly decisive; that is to say, with the Conservative-Liberals; and he withdrew his support from me. In Kuyper especially, Dr. Bronsveld found his opponent on this point. This noble fighter too, just like Dr. Bronsveld, always harbored a certain fear that cooperation with Rome promised us bitter fruits, but far from leaning towards Liberal Conservatism or Conservative Liberalism, Kuyper always came out uncompromisingly for the independence of the Anti-Revolutionary principles, and allied himself more faithfully and decisively than any other party member with the Central Committee. Kuyper showed his superiority of spirit in this. Leaders of the second rank like Dr. Bronsveld allow themselves to be determined in their choice by incidental motives; a man of the first rank like Kuyper does not pluck the flower from the stem, but clings firmly to the root. This never letting go of the root could therefore allow much play in the unfolding of the sympathies in practical action. Kuyper had not closely followed the latest development of the Roman political ideas, as they had come to the fore especially under Leo X. The “rather Turkish than Popish” therefore continued to play through his mind.

The relationship between Abraham Kuyper and Dr. Bronsveld illustrates the unity and diversity within the Antirevolutionary movement.

§7. The Electoral Law Branch.

Analytical Summary: The Tak Electoral Law. Yet it always seemed to me that an alliance with Rome need not necessarily lead to a marriage. Undoubtedly, the Roman Catholic State Party, as it presented itself after the well-known episcopal mandate on the school question, was not only our neighbor, but was also the most congenial to us in spirit. This could not be otherwise, because the confession of the Christ governed the life of both of us; but even so, the question remained open whether the appreciation of freedom of movement and the maintenance of our independence as Calvinists would not naturally lead us, if a favorable opportunity presented itself in a particular emerging issue, to not necessarily disdain their eventual support on our side. We know how it went with Tak’s Electoral Law. Among the Roman Catholic politicians, the majority still leaned quite strongly towards the conservative side. We were not yet separated from them for much more than a quarter of a century since 1853. The time when Thorbecke was elected in Limburg was still fresh in many people’s memories, and the April movement was still reverberating. It is true that under the inspiration of the German Center Party, a completely different current had already arisen by then, but the old phalanx had not yet dissolved. We know what Aeneas Mackay has undergone and how

The Electoral Law Branch represents a specific application of Antirevolutionary principles within the political sphere.

§8. The schism of 1894.

Analytical Summary: The Schism of 1894.

The schism within the Antirevolutionary Party in 1894 was a significant event with lasting consequences.

§9. Mr. Jhr. A.F. De Savornin Lohman.

Analytical Summary: MR. JHR. A.F. DE SAVORNIN LOHMAN. Mr. De Savornin Lohman had his own standpoint, different from that of Mr. Van Idsinga, and likewise from that of Mr. De Geer. It was precisely in 1913 that it became evident in what political chaos the Union threatened to dissolve. A danger further aggravated by the fact that the Réveil abroad, especially in largely Roman Catholic countries, not infrequently allied itself, in order to ward off Clericalism, with the radical politicians, while on the other hand the Conservatives here at home, after the merger of their own party, sought shelter in this Union. Yet this did not take away that on our side we always had to reckon with cooperation with the Christian Historicals as well. Neither at the ballot box, nor in the States-General, can one take a position without their support. Not only from the Roman Catholic side, but also from the side of the Christian Historicals, the Anti-Revolutionary side had to suffer a certain pressure, from which we could never completely free ourselves. In an earlier period this pressure was less, because until 1889 we were nothing but a protest party. If protest is the sole purpose of your appearance, then pay no heed to any connection, and seek salvation alone in the clear and vigorous manifestation of your own conviction. Groen van Prinsterer especially derived immense strength from this protest position. For he could even go so far as to withdraw the confidence of his entire party in the Second Chamber, and stand in the country as a general without an army. But this becomes quite different when you, with an eye on an alliance, see the chance of becoming a majority in the Second Chamber, as this has already been the case for thirty years after Groen’s death. Just the growth of our party among the electorate, and the new influx of voters that the constitutional revision of 1889 brought us, completely changed our responsible position.

The role and influence of Mr. Jhr. A.F. De Savornin Lohman must be carefully considered.

§10. Protest party or Cabinet.

Analytical Summary: PROTEST PARTY OR CABINET. This brief overview makes it abundantly clear how much prudent leadership is required to keep our little ship on course. There is no prospect of an uninterrupted majority. In the 16th century this succeeded, because at that time the struggle was principally about the faith, and the entire national existence gathered around the matter of faith. Then it could not be otherwise, or the party of the Dim Vivant, from whose midst the martyrs arose, had to set the tone.

The Antirevolutionary Party must decide whether to function as a protest party or to participate in government.

§11. Election program.

Analytical Summary: Electoral Program. The appearance at the ballot box with its own Electoral Program finds its readier justification in this change of relationships. Already in itself, the pushing to the fore at the ballot box of a striking slogan, especially in England, has been warmly recommended and used. In America too, the same practice was followed in the Union. There it was what was called the good cry. Groen van Prinsterer also felt the significance of this and often urged it. Yet this is not what we intend here in connection with the argument made. Repeatedly, the Anti-Revolutionary party has also felt compelled to profess a much broader confession at the ballot box. Our Program of 1878 had a general character and was entirely calculated on the then still entirely principled position in which we found ourselves squeezed. Now, however, thanks to Kappeynes’ sharp Resolution, an alliance arose that promised a chance of success, the necessity arose to express itself as a party on issues and matters about which we had hitherto kept silent; or also to express themselves in a more stringent form on issues that we had indeed dealt with one-sidedly, but not with a view to allied cooperation, and thus to move from the general to the particular on a particular moment and under the particular circumstances that presented themselves at that moment. This appearance with its own Electoral Program has been repeatedly condemned, especially from the Christian-Historical side. Not from the side of the Roman Catholic State Party. This too saw the necessity of such an electoral agreement early on and was quite willing to be in our company in drawing up the electoral agreement. This is not to be misunderstood. There has been no attempt to appear at the ballot box with a program on both sides. One felt that from this, if it went too far, danger could arise for the independence of both. What was done, on the other hand, was to consult in order to agree on both sides which issues would be raised this time and in what sense the solution of these particular issues would be attempted. Each of the two parties remained entirely free to formulate what was desired on both sides in its own way and in connection with its own confession of principles, as well as to add to the commonly established one or two points that were of interest to its own party, but not to the ally. Think, for example, of the relationship between the government and the religious denominations. Finally, this has also been followed from the Christian-Historical side. A requested conference has been attended, and already in more than one election, an agreement has also been reached with this party. §12. Once more Mr. Lohman. This helped a little to achieve unity in the nomination of candidates, but it never bore rich fruit in the Chamber; and this could hardly be otherwise, since Mr. De Savornin Lohman, through his personal predominance actually representing the entire Union in the Second Chamber, not only kept himself completely outside the making of such an electoral agreement, but also constantly set his own price, clearly indicating that he did not want to be bound by any agreement.

The election program of the Antirevolutionary Party must reflect its principles and appeal to the people.

§12. Once more the Lohman.

Analytical Summary: Before documents are issued. Yet, in practical terms, a serious control over what will be issued, before it is issued, is not only desirable but absolutely necessary. Such control is indispensable, not only regarding the substance, but even the form. Before any document reaches the States-General or is published in the Official Gazette, it ought to have been examined by the government to see whether it is in harmony with the entire body of legislation, the Constitution foremost, and further whether it fits the existing modes of execution and administration. It is also important to consider whether the forms and language fit the whole of our existing legislative and administrative style. One can give a very different form to a draft law. The English, German, and French ways of framing a law of a certain scope are by no means the same. It often happens that in one and the same Cabinet the various Departments proceed according to different methods. This must not be. The Netherlands, too, must have its own conception of the theory of law-making, and it makes a very undesirable impression when one sees law after law appear, of which the one follows the German, the other the English method, to the utter absence of a national form of our own. This even extends to the language; something that deserves special mention, because it is especially the language in our legislation that has always left so much to be desired—a defect that is even aggravated if the language in one official document speaks so differently from another. Even the Constitution suffers from this, and anyone who takes the trouble to compare, for example, the Belgian Constitution with ours, immediately feels how in the French a perfection of style, and even of linguistic form, has been achieved that puts us to shame. Even the choice of words in our legislation often leaves much to be desired.

The position of Mr. Lohman continues to be an important factor.

§13. Firm starting point.

Analytical Summary: Fundamental Starting Point. But precisely this alternation between isolation and cooperation, and the change that, due to the intended cooperation, the attitude of the Anti-Revolutionary party could undergo, made it all the more necessary, as point one on its program, to express in words, not open to two interpretations, the fundamental starting point of its entire political thought. The Roman Catholic party possesses such a fixed starting point in its ecclesiastical confession and its ecclesiastical hierarchy. The parties of unbelief and revolution derive their fixed life principles from what was uttered in Paris from 1789–1795, only in a more radical degree.

The Antirevolutionary Party must maintain a firm starting point in its principles.

§14. One-sided sense of “Christian.”

Analytical Summary: The one-sided view of “Christian.” In these two lay the past, from these two it was hoped that a future of salvation would come; and so it was these two that one took a positive stance towards. One could hardly have formulated the antithesis otherwise. It was not against the Pope in Paris, but against Christ, and no more against a particular form of government, but against the whole that history had provided. The history with Christ had now come to an end; a new political creation without Christ would now take its beginning. And over against this triad, whoever held fast to the faith, set forth the Christian and the Historical element as in one breath. In the first onset, the word “Christian” here had a general, extra-ecclesiastical meaning. Hence it was used both in Roman Catholic and in Lutheran, in Reformed and in Greek-Orthodox lands. Through the rise of the then current Revival, however, the term Christian-Historical gradually acquired a more specific Protestant meaning. Especially from England came to us the usage to apply the word “Christian” one-sidedly to one’s own action and to exclude the Roman Catholic element. This still has an after-effect. Thus one speaks of the “Christian” school, which stands over against the neutral public school, but then reckons almost exclusively with what since 1878 has been called the “School with the Bible.” It is precisely against the incorrect, because one-sided, use of the expression “Christian School” that I then recommended the phrase “School with the Bible” in the great petition movement. At the time this found acceptance, but the new name of “Christian school” could no longer be banished, and the one-sided use of “Christian” for what arose from one’s own circle, unjustly persisted. So also, for example, in the phrase “Christian Press,” under which no one of ours included the Roman Catholic press. Christian philanthropy, Christian pedagogy, Christian tracts, Christian missions—it all retained the exclusive meaning that the English Revival had once given to the term “Christian.”

The term “Christian” must not be understood in a one-sided or exclusive manner.

§15. Not limited to our land.

Analytical Summary: Not limited to our country. In Article I of Our Program, therefore, it was not expressed that only our party had the right to the name Anti-revolutionary or Christian-Historical, but that among the many political parties that here at home as well as abroad rose up against the principles of the French Revolution, and sought to lead the political life back to the old Christian track, and this in its historical course, our party also took its place. Groen knew very well from the outset that it was not the Netherlands alone, and in the Netherlands only our group, that could develop the spiritual giant power required to erect a dam against the stream of Revolutionary principles. For that our strength was too small, our position in world politics had sunk too deeply from its zenith, and our national life was too characteristic. Our joining the world action against the Revolutionary principles could at most even take the lead; but there was no question of the leadership coming from us. Article I of Our Program, therefore, was not intended to express anything other than that, standing before the fact of a division of European politics into the revolutionary and the anti-revolutionary channel, our party also set itself against the former and moved forward in the latter.

The Antirevolutionary principles have relevance beyond the borders of the Netherlands.

§16. Emerging from the past.

Analytical Summary: Emerging from the Past. Yet the name Anti-Revolutionary must not lead to misunderstanding. It must be recognized that the resistance intended here against the principles of the Revolution is aimed specifically against the upheaval that unfolded in Paris in 1789; it must never be inferred from this that our political formation only began at the end of the eighteenth century. Even if one admits that what broke out in Paris in 1789 had already smoldered for more than a century, it still cannot be said that the birth certificate of our party dates back to such a young date. As far as our party is concerned, one must always go back to the first half of the 16th century. The rise of our party lay in the rise of the Reformation. Now if we assume that there is a principled kinship between the Reveil, which emerged against the Revolution of 1789, and the Reformation of the 16th century, then it is clear that the Reformation here acts as the mother, and that the Reveil can be nothing other than her daughter. It is therefore to be regretted that the clear insight into these historical relationships in the first decades of the 19th century was so lacking here as elsewhere, with the result that the movement emerging here and elsewhere could not delve deeply enough, lapsed too much into restoration, and often remained too superficial; the sad consequence of which was that in France, Switzerland, Italy, etc. the Reveil joined the radical aims of the Left party all too quickly. Even with Bilderdijk it did not come to a correct appreciation of the Reformation.

The Antirevolutionary Party arises from the historical development of the Dutch nation.

§17. Adrift.

Analytical Summary: Adrift. If Groen van Prinsterer had also been of Reformed descent, our party life would have immediately returned to the Calvinist stream. Now since this was not the case, and Groen van Prinsterer only gradually drew closer to Calvinism, until his life’s thread was about to be cut, to come to full confession, it could not be otherwise, or a certain gap remained, and two different elements came together. On the one hand, the fondness for the Geuzen and Calvinism lived on; while on the other hand, the sons of the Reveil, although spiritually akin, yet felt much warmer for the much later emerging aim that was inspired by the Reveil. The Calvinist group reached much further back into the past, therefore penetrated more to the root, and found its support mainly in the countryside and among the common people; while conversely the Reveil group recruited more from the higher social class; counted more with the English and Swiss way of life than with our own national character; and felt more at home in the salons of the aristocrats than in the peasant cottages. The literature of the Calvinist was mostly found in Dutch, the Reveil literature spoke more in French and English. There was even more that separated the two groups. The Geuzen and Calvinists of the 16th and early 17th century had been heroes who aroused the admiration of all Europe, not only in the theological writings of that time, but on the broad world stage. In every field they had almost worked wonders, and a barely imaginable life force had emanated from them. But this had not remained so. After Maurice’s death, the

There have been times when the Antirevolutionary Party has seemed adrift or lacking direction.

§18. Fortunate turn.

Analytical Summary: A Happy Turn.

Fortunately, the Antirevolutionary Party has made a positive turn in recent years.

§19. Three groups in the people.

Analytical Summary: Three Groups in the People.

Within the Dutch people, there are three distinct groups that the Antirevolutionary Party must address.

§20. The three groups not equally representative.

Analytical Summary: The three types not equally characteristic. But this does not mean that, in the course of time, each of these three types has in turn dominated the national condition: the Roman Catholics in the Middle Ages, the Calvinists in the 16th century, and the French Revolutionaries after 1789. It does not at all follow from this that they are equal in national significance. Undoubtedly, the Seven Provinces, once living a common life with Flanders and other southern regions, have enjoyed a high and flourishing position and have gradually taken an increasingly important position in Northern Europe; but it must not be forgotten here that the national energy was at the time much more strongly developed in what is now called Belgium than in the northern provinces. Neither Amsterdam nor Rotterdam could in those days be placed on a par with Ghent and Bruges. The wealth of medieval life had more stamped its imprint on the national character in the South than in the North. The reference to the rich medieval type therefore has much less significance for the present Kingdom of the Netherlands than for the western part of Belgium. And in the second place, it must be added that at that time the unity of our national life was much less consolidated. Under the Burgundians, the Dutch provinces did not occupy an independent position, but were united with what lay outside our later borders; and under Charles V and Philip, we even came, though only in a personal way, into a Union with entirely different states from Central and Southern Europe—something which could not but be detrimental to the confirmation of our own national character. Whoever investigates the historical significance of our lower lands may well find the period we lived through in the Middle Ages highly important, but nevertheless hesitates for a moment to acknowledge without reservation that our glorious period dates from April 1, 1572. Not in the Middle Ages, but in the period of the Reformation, lies the high point of our national history. It cannot be emphasized enough how much scrupulous fairness is required in such a comparison, and we shall beware of the assertion that from the Calvinist side the correct toll of reverence has always been paid to the medieval period in our history, to which they undoubtedly had a right. But it stands to reason that from the Roman Catholic side, too, we have not always been done justice. It cannot be valued highly enough that especially in the second half of the 19th century a more impartial judgment gained influence. Just with a view to this more unanimous judgment, it may now at least be established that not only the foreign historians, one may well say without any exception of importance, honor the period after 1572 as the highest and most glorious, but that also within the country the best interpreters of each of the three types increasingly come to the unanimous conclusion that the world significance of our small country came out most richly in the heyday of Calvinism. This in no way denies that our national life in those days of our glory also showed dark sides. The resistance to the monarchical state form, and even against the House of Orange, has almost become a source of our unhappy discord until 1789, and it is impossible to say how much higher our country would have soared if immediately after the separation from Philip II the crown had been offered to Prince William. It had to come to this in 1813; but who calculates what national suffering would have been spared us if we had taken this important step two centuries earlier. But even if one plays open cards and takes into account all the suffering, oppression and restriction of rights that the temporarily dominant group has inflicted on the other two groups, it is historically established that we were never before 1572 what our national life was in the period between those two dates, and never again after 1789. To top it off, the fact that we offered a refuge to the politically and religiously persecuted from all countries is proof that the actual life here was a life in freedom, and that the publications and ordinances that claimed to infringe on this freedom had more the effect of satisfying formal demands than of inflicting suffering on those who deviated.

These three groups do not equally represent the Dutch people as a whole.

§21. The third type the weakest.

Analytical Summary: The third type the weakest. The least fortunate impression of the three periods, with an over

The third type of group is the weakest in terms of its influence and significance.

§22. Psychological national mystery.

Analytical Summary: The Psychological National Mystery. Now it is certainly extremely difficult to establish historically how, in the multifarious variations of the mutually succeeding types, the

The dynamics within the Dutch people constitute a psychological national mystery.

[652]

The fundamental tone.

The Antirevolutionary Party must strike a fundamental tone that resonates with the Dutch people.

§24. Our people.

Analytical Summary: Our People.

The Antirevolutionary Party must understand and embrace the unique character of the Dutch people.

§25. Lasting influence. Calvinism.

Analytical Summary: Lasting Influence. Calvinism. That a place must remain in our national life for Calvinism as well, does not mean, however, that the freedom of confession is not to be honored, nor that the extermination of the group that even now, whether large or small, presents itself as Calvinist, should be tolerated. Just as the Beni-Israel alone are considered foreign to us in a national sense, although they contribute so much economically to the strength of our people, yet they remain alien to us in blood. The Calvinists, on the other hand, are not a group merely tolerated, as if our people’s whole could just as well be conceived of without them. Whoever presents it that way completely misses the historical truth. For the formation of what is now called the people of the Netherlands, the Calvinists have rather the oldest rights, and it is through the Calvinists that the deepest characteristic incisions have been made in the national being. Hence now it is and remains our calling; not at all solely for our own benefit, but very certainly also for the promotion of a fundamentally healthy development of our national life, to stand up for the Calvinist principle. Especially the theologians should see to it here that they do not estrange our national character. For it is precisely from their side that a very serious danger has threatened in this respect since the second half of the previous century. From the 16th century onward, we had at our national universities a native Dutch theology that even exerted influence abroad; and already the professor Van Hengel in Leiden was departing in many ways from the once-confessed truth, yet the honor should not be denied him that in his commentary on the Epistle to the Romans he still continued the peculiar Dutch traditions. To a certain extent, this can even be said of Professor Scholten, who was the only one among the professors of that time who still possessed a rich and ancient-national treasure in his library, and continually tried whether his modern sympathies could not find a connection to the beautiful work of our fathers. But it went quite differently in Groningen and in Utrecht. Here both the Ethical and the Vermittlungs-direction increasingly came under German-Lutheran influence. Schleiermacher’s mighty spirit

The lasting influence of Calvinism on the Dutch people is a crucial factor.

§26. Guided by Orange.

Analytical Summary: Guided by Orange. To this has been added: “guided by Orange”; an addition that must not be omitted. For it cannot be denied that in the characterized Calvinism, as it also penetrated our country from Geneva and France at that time, there was a danger not to be underestimated, the aftereffects of which are still discernible. In the last chapter of the First Part of this work, I have extensively explained how from the outset there was a decisive element in Calvinism that could only develop and mature later, and that, politically speaking, only came to full effect in the United States; but it was also then added that in Geneva (one need only think of the burning of Servetus) an intolerant dogmatism still prevailed, which, though indispensable for the church, could only result in schism and oppression in its political consequences.

The House of Orange has played a pivotal role in the history and development of the Dutch nation.

§27. The wrong done to us.

Analytical Summary: The wrong done to us. Our party has set up a price in 1878, in its own starting point, to point to this happy influence of the Prince of Orange. Let no confusion creep in here. In matters of ecclesiastical life, Calvin’s insight has also been his own. His dispute with Dathenus does not touch the very life of the church, but the influence of the Church on the life of the people in the political domain. As a theologian, especially Johannes a Lasco deserves appreciation on this point, but on a broader scale it has above all been the influence of the Prince that has safely guided us. Meanwhile, one should not lose sight of the fact that this pointing with preference to the Prince is foreign to the intention, to praise anything that has later emerged from the House of Orange on this delicate terrain of the Princes. Prince Maurice aligned himself much more closely with the Calvinists. The then-emerging conflict with Arminianism necessitated this. But his life history would stand in a more glorious light for us if Oldenbarnevelt had died a natural death. Under Prince Frederick

The Antirevolutionary Party must address the wrongs that have been done to the Dutch people.

§28. The people’s party.

Analytical Summary: Enduring Bond. No Calvinist, in any country, has ever thought of whitewashing any injustice for the benefit of a princely ruler. But apart from what criticism may later have become necessary, we still always honor in the appearance of Prince William the Silent a gift of God to our people in their then so distressed condition; we thank God for what the time has bestowed on our fatherland in the generation of the Prince; we now still refresh ourselves in the national glory that has emanated from this House of Princes over our people as a nation; and we now still take sides, not on ecclesiastical, but on political terrain, for what the Silent One, the first among all princes in Europe, as the consequence of the Reformation, brought us in the bestowal of full spiritual freedom. Precisely for this reason, protest is constantly lodged by us against any attempt to discredit Orange as an ornament, as Mr. S. van Houten has dared to do. Not, we hasten to add here, as if this Statesman wanted to detract from the glory of our House of Orange. What he spoke of as an “ornament” was only the formulation in a single word of his constitutional theory. But here lies the difference, that such a declining expression of “ornament” was more dear to him, while it strikes us as offensive to our love for Orange. Especially after the shameful treatment that the House of Orange has suffered through its expulsion and forced flight to England in 1797, and after the more than shameful threat with which in 1848 in the liberal press one even dared to speak of the scaffold to unsettle Orange itself, it betrayed a stiff lack of tenderness and self-control in a man who ultimately became an advisor to the Crown, to hear him speak of “ornament.” Not daring to criticize a Prince openly is a cringing cowardice that Orange demands less than it requires, and which is unthinkable especially in the Calvinist. But this does not detract from the firm demand that we remain thankful for honoring what our God has bestowed on us in Orange through his great Princes. A glory in the past, the happy echo of which was to be heard in our revered Queen, as the last scion of the ancient House of Orange.

The Antirevolutionary Party must truly be a people’s party, rooted in the history, customs, and popular morality of the Dutch nation.

[653]

The Antirevolutionary Party as a People’s Party (continued)

§29. Calvinism and the people.

Analytical Summary: According to the needs of our time.

Calvinism has shaped the character and identity of the Dutch people in profound ways.

§30. Calvinism and the state.

Analytical Summary: The Church and the State in Mutual Relation. One immediately feels this in the relationship between Church and State. In the 16th century, the struggle that the Reformation undertook against Rome’s overpowering authority naturally brought the ecclesiastical question to the fore in all political domains. The ecclesiastical question dominated the entire political sphere, both domestically and with a view to foreign relations, and here the foreign relations mostly stood...

The Antirevolutionary Party’s understanding of the relationship between Calvinism and the state is crucial.

§31. Calvinism and the social question.

Analytical Summary: The People’s Representation. It is no different with the People’s Representation. Under the republican form of government, our Commonwealth has not known representation as we understand it today. There were estates, but no People’s Representation; the cities and manorial landowners held the power; the deputies were in no way representatives of the nation before the Government, but rather the installers and maintainers of the Government itself. There was certainly popular choice in the election of the power-holders, but the right to participate in that choice was extremely limited. As shown in the last chapter of the First Part, Calvin did not praise such a system, and ultimately he himself went over to advising in a democratic sense. Whoever now tries to apply the completely antiquated representation ideas of the 16th century to the present state of affairs would thereby show themselves to be anything but faithful to the pure principle, but rather to blatantly disown it. To now still advocate a State form that does not fully do justice to the representative principle, would brand one as backward and out of one’s time. The question of a proper People’s Representation has now itself become the main question for the future development of the State life. And the Calvinist direction, which has always proceeded from the people, and even found its strength in the “common folk,” would lose its inner driving force and let itself be written off, if it did not take the lead rather than follow on this point of our constitutional development in the spirit of freedom and love of the people. To be a Christian Democrat remains the title of honor for every Calvinist. §32. Navy and Army. As a third example, let the National Defense be pointed out. In the days of the Reformation, there was hardly any mention of a national army, as we know it today. The Condottieri had indeed lost ground, but the army with which one went to war still consisted mostly of volunteers offering themselves, and even of mercenary troops from foreign countries, just as we still used to employ, and partly still do, not a few Swiss, Germans and Belgians for our East Indian army. Only of the army of Gustavus Vasa and of the army of Cromwell can it be said that they were driven and urged by a higher and holier interest. For the rest, however, the armies were mostly composed of rough men, and the holy enthusiasm for the cause of the country was sometimes all too little to be noticed. That the same regiment, after the lapse of ten years, drew the sword against a higher national interest, which it had first helped to defend at the price of its blood, occurred more than once. All the more the custom came into vogue with us to assemble the power for the defense of the country’s interests from hired manpower, inasmuch as the navy with us, more than the army, stood in importance, and the manning of the navy, both here and still in England, was constantly recruited by free choice. The civic guard had a more national character, and knew no wages as a motive; but the militia, as it is now in force, first appeared under Napoleon’s domination, and has since also been maintained in our Kingdom. Meanwhile, our people have been far from eager for the new military idea. To become a soldier was not considered an honor, but a plague, and the system of substitutes soon came up to free oneself from this plague by money. It has therefore lasted until the very end of the 19th century before we really got a national army, and the idea of universal military service, which now imposes itself of its own accord, is still not realized with us. Now, because in the glorious days of our fathers the condition prevailed in navy and army that excluded a national army, shall one identify the system then in force with Calvinism, and therefore, as ultra-conservative, set it against the military force that is now so absolutely indispensable? Certainly not. The principle of Calvinism and this is, to highly esteem the significance of the national life; and the future of the national life is now inexorably, at least for small nations, in danger, if not the whole people finally girds the sword as one man for the country’s independence.

The Antirevolutionary Party must address the social question from a Calvinist perspective.

§32. Calvinism and the arts.

Analytical Summary: Money Gave the Decisive Vote. So it remained until Thorbecke in 1848 cleared away everything that still had a medieval aftertaste, and made money the sole fulcrum of the representative system. Article 76 of the Constitution promulgated that year tied the suffrage for the Second Chamber, and likewise Article 123 the suffrage for the Provincial Estates, “to the payment of a sum in direct taxes, corresponding to the local situation,” ranging from ƒ20 to ƒ160. The Electoral Law provided for the proper implementation of these provisions, and did so in such an indicative anti-democratic direction that the census of ƒ24 in many Frisian municipalities was calculated to favor only a few Liberals. A proposal from the Anti-Revolutionary side, made to reduce this amount, also met with the most vehement opposition from the Liberal side in the Second Chamber, even as late as 1875. Especially in Sneek this gave the most outrageous injustice, just as after 1887, in the new edition of what is now Article 80, in a way that allowed no contradiction, it appeared from the figures on the Liberal and Anti-Revolutionary candidates.

The Antirevolutionary Party must also consider the role of Calvinism in the realm of the arts and culture.

§33. Calvinism and science.

Analytical Summary: The Social Question. With a brief mention of the social question, let us refer to these examples and conclude. In the 16th century, there was no social question. The Peasants’ Revolt in Germany seemed to point in that direction, but it arose from a completely different motive. What called the social question into a very peculiar form of life in the 19th century was twofold. Firstly, the fact that the increased technology made it possible for factories to arise, in which hundreds of workers were set to work simultaneously; something which naturally caused the close and cordial relationship between employer and worker to be entirely lost. And secondly, the more mature development of the working class, thanks to the high flight that popular education also took in connection with vocational education. This higher position of the worker demanded more abundant satisfaction of the needs of social community life, and the heaping up of hundreds of workers outside of all higher and more intimate connection with the employer, brought into existence conditions that our fathers had never known. Not that our fathers had no care for the workers. The Guild system certainly promoted an excellent understanding to no small degree. The needs of the working class spoke less loudly; but the needs, as far as they spoke, were then as a rule well and not too narrowly satisfied. What only characterized the then existing condition in a less desirable way, was the excessive dependence in which the worker found himself on his employer. Slavery had come to an end, but the conviction had not yet gained entrance that the worker, as a free man, stood not under, but alongside his employer, in civil rights and in social life. But if one now asks whether this then existing condition could be logically deduced from the principle we profess, in the sense that the introduction of an entirely changed condition would be a denial of that principle, then it must be answered with absolute decisiveness, that a denial of principle can only come into question with him who, where such a totally different condition has entered, refuses to reveal the power of his principle also in the solution of the questions now presenting themselves.

The Antirevolutionary Party must engage with the relationship between Calvinism and the pursuit of scientific knowledge.

§34. Calvinism and the family.

Analytical Summary: Keeping Pace with Our Time.

The Antirevolutionary Party must uphold the centrality of the family within Calvinist thought and practice.

§35. Calvinism and the church.

Analytical Summary: Dogmatic and Political.

The Antirevolutionary Party must navigate the complex relationship between Calvinism and the institutional church.

§36. Calvinism and the world.

Analytical Summary: The Shift in England. In the nearly forty years that have elapsed since the first publication of Our Program, yet another complete transformation has taken place, which must equally not be passed over in silence, namely, in regard to our connection with the Anti-revolutionary groups or circles in other countries. Since 1878, a wholly different relationship has also emerged outside our own circle, as far as the foreign countries are concerned, particularly in Great Britain. Gladstone’s death on May 19, 1898, and Chamberlain’s attack in 1900 on the South African Boer Republics are the two historical monuments that have been decisive here. Those who were Calvinists in the democratic sense had often turned their political sympathies towards England, and I too have allowed myself to do so. I did not forget how England had often done us highly unjust competition in the maritime and colonial sphere in the 17th century, nor how England had taken not only Ceylon but even the Cape Colony from us at the beginning of the 19th century. Yet our sympathies still seemed to have to lean towards England. In 1890, Gladstone had published his well-known work: The Impregnable Rock of the Holy Scripture, in which he had advocated more clearly than ever before his steadfast agreement with the Statesmanship that rests on God’s Word. He felt a deep aversion to the Radicalism that was then already creeping into England. He moved unconditionally in the line of the Old Whigs. And without hesitation it must be stated that all that was of Calvinistic origin, both in England and especially in Scotland, supported him. It is true that it already raised some doubts that a man like John Morley, who was Gladstone’s political confidant, moved entirely outside that rock of the holy Scripture, but here too Groen van Prinsterer found support in Allard Pierson, and Calvin was also rehabilitated here in our country by Allard Pierson. Forster’s school policy could serve as an example to us here, and the encounters I had with Forster and other English statesmen, through the mediation of my academic contemporary Lord Reay from Leiden, always warmed my sympathies anew. I felt again and again that, as an English citizen, I would be a Gladstonian, and how, conversely, Gladstone, whatever his ultimate fate may have been on our terrain, was undoubtedly a Groenian.

The Antirevolutionary Party must articulate a Calvinist perspective on the broader world and its place within it.

[654]

The Antirevolutionary Party as a People’s Party (continued)

§37. Calvinism and the nation.

Analytical Summary: The Antirevolutionaries in Germany. In Germany, there was no compensation to be found for this. Stöcker’s appearance did have my warm sympathy, but as I have told Stöcker himself more than once, he failed to take a political stance in a general sense. In the social sphere, he acted energetically and decisively, but his social policy hung too much in the air and lacked a general political foundation. That is why joining his efforts was not possible for us. The Junker parties, the Agrarians, who made up the core of the Conservatives, had increasingly disowned Stahl’s political testament. Von Bismarck’s political conduct moved almost entirely in the sphere of strengthening Germany’s global significance, and although he openly professed, “We Germans fear God and no one else,” the religious vein running through his politics was still strongly tinged. In the May Laws, he even dared to attack the freedom of the Church. With all the admiration for the mighty results he achieved, he could still not elicit any political affiliation from us Antirevolutionaries. The “Reformists,” who were reviving in Germany, did try to approach me once or twice to join their ranks, but their school tactics, their avoidance of all political involvement, and their sober Calvinism seemed to me to promise too little for an attempt at affiliation. In Barmen and Elberfeld, there certainly would have been favorable prospects; but even there, the conditions under which the action took place were too specifically German, and too little principled and comprehensive in a general sense, for me to engage in a closer understanding without suffering harm myself. The Protestant element in general, and the Reformed in particular, lacked entirely what made the Roman Catholic Center in Germany so strong, in that it did not have the courage to act as an independent party. It was therefore understandable that some more decisive men like Kleist-Retzow and Von Gerlach sought to be incorporated into the Center. But this too failed on its own. And so, alas, it must be admitted that the rich prospect that opened up for the powerful formation of an Antirevolutionary party in Germany after Stahl’s appearance has not been realized. Personally, there are thousands and thousands in Germany who are kindred spirits to us and envy our independent party position; but it has not come to an independent formation, and there is hardly any prospect of it happening in the future. Switzerland offers no hope for the future either. In the French cantons, what is outlined in France is repeated in a bioscopic way. The Italian cantons are too small in population to take an independent position. And in the German cantons, it is the Roman Catholic ones where the most emphasis is placed on the honor of one’s own principles, while in the Protestant ones, German theology often marries French political ideas, and thus here too a revival of Calvinism is excluded. One saw it at Calvin’s centenary. Glorification of a name in which part of Switzerland spoke of ancient glories, but of spiritual kinship with the traditions of the man of Geneva, not a trace in all that was manifested and celebrated. Religion and politics here too almost hermetically sealed off from each other. Not even the faintest indication of political action in Calvin’s footsteps. §38. In Roman Catholic lands. In predominantly Roman Catholic lands, there could naturally be no revival of the Antirevolutionary movement. The Roman Catholic Church, with its hierarchical structure and its centralized authority, does not allow the formation of independent political parties within its fold. The Ultramontane movement, which has spread from Rome, has indeed brought about a certain political mobilization, but this has been entirely in the service of the Roman Catholic Church and its interests. The Antirevolutionary principle, which seeks to maintain the independence of the various spheres of life, is diametrically opposed to this. The Roman Catholic Church, with its claim to sovereignty over all of life, cannot tolerate any independent political movement that would limit its power. And so, in Roman Catholic lands, the Antirevolutionary movement has found no foothold. The political activity of Roman Catholics has been entirely absorbed by the struggle for the interests of their Church. The Antirevolutionary principle, which seeks to maintain the independence of the various spheres of life, has found no echo there.

The Antirevolutionary Party must understand and champion the role of Calvinism in shaping the Dutch national identity.

§38. Calvinism and propriety.

Analytical Summary: In Roomsche landen. In overwegend Roomsche landen kon natuurlijk van een weer op-bloeien

The Antirevolutionary Party must uphold the Calvinist understanding of propriety and decency in public life.

§39. Calvinism and custom.

Analytical Summary: Russia.

The Antirevolutionary Party must respect and preserve the Calvinist customs and conventions that have become integral to Dutch society.

§40. Calvinism and the future.

Analytical Summary: State of Strength and Weakness. There are two phenomena in life: the one of full power, the other in weakness. This latter can have three causes: first, that the person is still a child and has not yet grown up to adulthood; the second, that the person is sick; and the third, that he lost his father before he came of age. In each of these three cases, the woman appears as the natural caretaker: as a mother with her child, as a nurse with the sick, and as a substitute father with the orphan; and in each of these three case-positions, the regiment is with her. On the other hand, when dealing with the adult person, with the normally healthy person, and with a household that still possesses its father, the regiment does not rest with the woman, but with the man, and this by virtue of the fact that the man, as a rule, is the stronger in physical strength, in mental power, in willpower and in readiness to act. According to this self-evident rule, it then also becomes clear in what and to what extent the leadership belongs to the woman, and where not. From this it follows that there can be no question of active or passive suffrage for the States-General for the woman; nor for the Provincial States; and as for municipal administration, she may well be called upon to perform various functions, but not in the government itself. This also remains the case in the ecclesiastical domain. The government of the Church is with the Elders, teaching or ruling, and the woman can only appear as a deaconess in the Diaconate, since the Diaconate is not part of the church government, but only of the ecclesiastical care. This does not mean, however, that the Anti-Revolutionary Party will always succeed in permanently opposing the introduction of active and passive suffrage.

The Antirevolutionary Party must articulate a Calvinist vision for the future of the Dutch nation.

§41. Calvinism and the world.

Analytical Summary: Radicalism in France. With France, our relationship could not be other than strained. Not because the Monarchy gave way to the Republic. We ourselves have been a Commonwealth, and our country has been able to fulfill the most glorious role in Europe’s history precisely in that Commonwealth form of government. The form of government decides nothing here in the least. It is precisely with the United States, who also chose the republican form of government, that we feel the closest political kinship. A Calvinist can never forget that in Geneva there was no talk of a Monarchy, and that Calvin himself lived in a Commonwealth, so to speak. What makes reconciliation with the French form of government unthinkable for us is solely this, that France still moves along the straight line for which Rousseau first indicated the two fixed points, and that line is precisely the line of Unbelief and Revolution. It is perfectly true that first Napoleon, and after him both the returned Bourbons and what came after them, have in many ways deviated from that line. But the fact remains that the basic direction of France’s political development has been and still is the direction of Unbelief and Revolution.

The Antirevolutionary Party must engage with the broader world from a Calvinist perspective, while maintaining its distinctive identity.

§42. Calvinism and the people.

Analytical Summary: Disappointment. The disappointment brought upon us by France was thus complete. As followers of Calvin, nothing could have been more congenial and dear to us than to incorporate, in tempering the one-sided Germanic element in our national character, the French variation as well. Already under the Burgundians, the first germination of this had manifested itself, and even in our language the admixture of a French element is unmistakable. They are not all Gallicisms that one wants to banish from our language as such. Words like: pleasure, umbrella, province, fruit and the like have, by introducing variations in meaning, enriched our language. The affinity between the Swiss-German and our Dutch also points to the fact that, in contrast to High German, Low Dutch prefers to maintain itself through this French admixture. Through Calvinism, had it been able to gain a foothold in France, this intrusion of a Romance variation into our people would likely have truly enriched us. Yet it was not to be. The Bartholomew’s Night has given us the Huguenots, which of itself brought a breach with France.

The Antirevolutionary Party must remain firmly rooted in the Calvinist character of the Dutch people.

[658]

Our Protest Against the Census. 351.

Analytical Summary: Weakening of significance. The fact that from a more revolutionary side, one was always intent on removing the Council of State, as having become altogether superfluous, leads to the same conclusion. In Belgium, this has already come about in 1830, and also among us, from the liberal side, attempt after attempt has been made to make the Council of State disappear from our institutions. And although this has not yet been achieved, it cannot be denied that the legal regulation given to this Council has not enhanced its prestige in the life of the State. What Article 45 assigns to the Council, in the event of the immediate disturbance of the Crown Bearer, or also what is assigned to the Council in its Administrative Division, is undoubtedly of very high importance, but these two tasks are of an incidental nature, and the main task of the Council of State remains always to lie in the advice it provides to the King, either according to the law, or at the King’s special request; and precisely as regards these opinions, the significance of the Council of State is no longer rated very highly. The reason for this is not far to seek. It lies in its composition and in its authority so severely limited by law. Its composition, it is true, proceeds from the King, but is in fact entirely dominated by the Minister of the Interior, under the control of the Cabinet. The time also lies so far behind us that the successive liberal Cabinets almost never presented to the King for membership anyone other than kindred spirits. Often, it is true, a limited number of Roman Catholic statesmen were also given a seat, but previously, at least, the choice for this was easily made from among statesmen who still felt entirely homogeneous with the Liberals.
[663]
